# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define MARPOL 73/78. List its six Annexes and state the type of pollution each Annex addresses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** MARPOL 73/78 stands for the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978**. It is the principal international convention under the International Maritime Organization (IMO) that aims to prevent pollution of the marine environment from operational and accidental sources.

**Six Annexes (2 marks):**

| Annex | Pollution Type |
|---|---|
| Annex I | Oil (from cargo, machinery, bilge) |
| Annex II | Noxious Liquid Substances (chemicals in bulk) |
| Annex III | Harmful Substances in Packaged Form |
| Annex IV | Sewage (domestic wastewater) |
| Annex V | Garbage (solid waste) |
| Annex VI | Air Pollution (SOₓ, NOₓ, CO₂, ODS, VOC) |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is an Emission Control Area (ECA) under MARPOL Annex VI? Name any two ECA zones and the sulphur cap applicable therein.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An Emission Control Area (ECA) is a designated sea area where stricter controls on **SOₓ (sulphur oxides), NOₓ (nitrogen oxides), and particulate matter (PM)** emissions from ships are enforced under MARPOL Annex VI.

**Two ECA zones (1 mark):**
1. **Baltic Sea ECA**
2. **North Sea ECA** (also: North American ECA, US Caribbean ECA)

**Sulphur cap in ECA (1 mark):** Fuel oil used in ECAs must contain **not more than 0.10% m/m (mass by mass) sulphur** content.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (MARPOL Questions):**
> - Students frequently **omit the "Protocol of 1978"** in the convention's full title. Always write "MARPOL 73/78" — never just "MARPOL 1973."
> - Many confuse **ECA** (Emission Control Area) with **PECA** (Particularly Sensitive Sea Area) under SOLAS, or with **Special Areas** under Annex I/V. **ECA** is **Annex VI-specific** and relates to **air emissions**, not operational discharges.
> - For Annex I oil discharge, students must specify **all three** conditions: 15 ppm oil content, ≥ 4 knots speed, and 30-metre hull discharge rule. Omitting any one costs 1 mark.
> - The **0.50% global sulphur cap effective from 1 January 2020** is a high-yield date. Forgetting the year loses 1 mark.
> - In Annex V questions, students often state "**plastic discharge is banned within 12 NM**" — this is **incorrect**. Plastics are **banned universally** at sea; the 12 NM rule applies only to food waste (uncommiuted) and other non-plastic items.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Annex I** regulations of MARPOL covering the prevention of pollution by oil. Include the discharge criteria for oil tankers and the special requirements for tankers operating in Special Areas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **six Annexes of MARPOL 73/78** with reference to the type of pollution each addresses, the categories of ships affected, and the main certificates or documents required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a)**

**1. Introduction to Annex I (1 mark):**
Annex I of MARPOL 73/78 deals with the prevention of **pollution by oil** from ships. It applies to all vessels but is most stringent for **oil tankers**. Annex I entered into force on 2 January 1983 along with the convention.

**2. Discharge Criteria for Oil Tankers (3 marks):**

According to **Regulation 34** (formerly Regulation 15), oily water discharged from the cargo area of an oil tanker must satisfy **all** the following conditions simultaneously:

- The oil content of the effluent must not exceed **15 parts per million (ppm)** instantaneously.
- The vessel must be **en route** (i.e., not at anchor or in port) and proceeding at a speed of **not less than 4 knots**.
- The discharge must be made **below the waterline through a port not exceeding 30 metres from the hull**.

In addition, the ODMCS (Oil Discharge Monitoring and Control System) must be operational and recording.

**3. Special Area Concept (1 mark):**
A "Special Area" is a sea area where the discharge standards are more stringent due to its particular oceanographic and ecological characteristics. Examples include the **Mediterranean Sea, Baltic Sea, Black Sea, Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, and Antarctic Area**.

**4. Special Requirements for Tankers in Special Areas (2 marks):**

- **Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT):** Crude oil tankers of **20,000 DWT and above** delivered after 1 June 1982 must be fitted with SBT to provide dedicated ballast capacity, eliminating the need to ballast contaminated cargo tanks.
- **Crude Oil Washing (COW):** Applicable to crude oil tankers of **20,000 DWT and above** — they must use COW to remove oil residues from cargo tanks using crude oil itself as a washing medium.
- **Product carriers of 30,000 DWT and above** must use SBT.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b)**

**1. Overview (1 mark):**
MARPOL 73/78 is composed of **six technical Annexes**, each addressing a distinct category of marine pollution. The full title is the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978**.

**2. Detailed Annex Breakdown (5 marks — approximately 0.85 marks per annex):**

| Annex | Pollution Type | Vessels Affected | Key Certificate / Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Oil | All ships; esp. oil tankers | **IOPP Certificate** (International Oil Pollution Prevention) |
| **II** | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in bulk | Chemical/Product tankers | **NLS Certificate** |
| **III** | Harmful substances in packaged form | All ships carrying packaged dangerous goods | Compliance with **IMDG Code** |
| **IV** | Sewage | Ships ≥ 400 GT or carrying > 15 persons | **ISPP Certificate** (International Sewage Pollution Prevention) |
| **V** | Garbage | All ships | **Garbage Management Plan + Garbage Record Book** |
| **VI** | Air Pollution (SOₓ, NOₓ, CO₂, etc.) | All ships | **IAPP Certificate** (International Air Pollution Prevention) + **EEDI** + **SEEMP** + **CII** rating |

**3. Closing Note (1 mark):**
Each annex has its own set of regulations, certificates, and entry-into-force dates. Compliance is enforced by the **flag state** and verified by **port state control** inspections.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the **MARPOL Annex V** regulations for the prevention of pollution by garbage from ships. Construct a clear table of discharge distances and explain the absolute ban on plastic discharge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **MARPOL Annex VI** regulations for the prevention of air pollution from ships. Detail the sulphur cap rules (global and ECA), the EEDI concept, and the CII rating system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a)**

**1. Introduction to Annex V (1 mark):**
Annex V addresses the prevention of **pollution by garbage from ships**. It applies to **all vessels**, including fixed and floating platforms, and prohibits the discharge of all ship-generated solid waste except under specific conditions.

**2. Plastic Discharge Ban — Absolute (2 marks):**
The discharge of **all forms of plastics** into the sea is **strictly prohibited at all times, in all zones, and under all conditions**. This is the most rigorous rule in Annex V. Plastics include:
- Plastic bags, sheets, containers
- Synthetic ropes and fishing nets
- Plastic-coated paper
- Plastic strapping bands

The rationale is that plastic persists for centuries in marine environments, entangles marine life, and breaks down into microplastics that enter the food chain.

**3. Discharge Distance Table (3 marks):**

| Garbage Type | Outside Special Area | Inside Special Area |
|---|---|---|
| Plastics (all types) | **PROHIBITED** | **PROHIBITED** |
| Food waste (comminuted/ground) | $\ge 3$ NM from nearest land | $\ge 12$ NM from nearest land |
| Food waste (not comminuted) | $\ge 12$ NM from nearest land | **PROHIBITED** |
| Dunnage, lining, packing materials | $\ge 25$ NM from nearest land | **PROHIBITED** |
| Cargo residues (non-HME) | $\ge 12$ NM from nearest land | **PROHIBITED** |
| Animal carcasses | As far as possible from land | **PROHIBITED** |

**4. Documentation Requirements (1 mark):**
- Every ship of **100 GT and above**, and every ship certified to carry **15 persons or more**, must carry a **Garbage Management Plan** (Regulation 3).
- A **Garbage Record Book** must be maintained (Regulation 4), with entries for every discharge, incineration, or landing of garbage.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b)**

**1. Introduction to Annex VI (1 mark):**
Annex VI addresses the prevention of **air pollution from ships** and entered into force on **19 May 2005**. It regulates emissions of **SOₓ, NOₓ, ODS (ozone-depleting substances), VOC, and CO₂**.

**2. Sulphur Cap Rules (3 marks):**

- **Global sulphur cap (since 1 January 2020):** The sulphur content of fuel oils used on board ships must not exceed **0.50% m/m** (mass by mass) outside Emission Control Areas.
- **ECA sulphur cap:** Within designated ECAs (Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA, US Caribbean ECA), the cap is reduced to **0.10% m/m**.
- **Compliance methods:** Use of low-sulphur fuels (ULSFO, VLSFO, MGO), installation of **Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS / scrubbers)**, or use of **LNG / alternative fuels**.

**3. EEDI — Energy Efficiency Design Index (1.5 marks):**
EEDI is a **design-based index** for newbuild ships. It measures the grams of CO₂ emitted per tonne-nautical mile of transport work. The EEDI must be **at or below a reference baseline** that becomes progressively stricter over time (reduction factor X increasing year by year).

$$\text{EEDI} = \frac{CO_2 \text{ emissions per voyage (g)}}{\text{Carrying capacity (DWT)} \times \text{Distance (nm)}}$$

**4. CII — Carbon Intensity Indicator (1.5 marks):**
CII is an **operational** measure that rates the annual carbon intensity of an existing ship on an **A–E scale**:
- **A:** Major Superior
- **B:** Minor Superior
- **C:** Moderate
- **D:** Minor Inferior
- **E:** Inferior (3 consecutive E ratings = corrective action plan required)

CII requires a yearly reduction factor $z$ on the attained CII relative to 2019 reference. The CII applies to ships ≥ 5,000 GT.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **MARPOL = International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973 as modified by the Protocol of 1978 (MARPOL 73/78).** Adopted under IMO, London. Entered into force **2 November 1983**.
- ✅ **Six Annexes:** I (Oil), II (NLS), III (Packaged), IV (Sewage), V (Garbage), VI (Air).
- ✅ **Annex I Key Number:** Oily water discharge $\le$ **15 ppm**, ship speed $\ge$ **4 knots**, discharge port $\le$ **30 m** from hull.
- ✅ **Annex I Special Equipment:** SBT (Segregated Ballast Tank), COW (Crude Oil Washing), ODMCS (Oil Discharge Monitoring and Control System), IOPP Certificate.
- ✅ **Annex II Categories:** X, Y, Z, OS — based on hazard level. X = strictest (no discharge).
- ✅ **Annex IV:** Sewage discharge (treated) allowed $\ge$ **4 NM**; (untreated) $\ge$ **12 NM** from nearest land. STP or holding tank mandatory for ships $\ge$ 400 GT.
- ✅ **Annex V:** **Plastics PROHIBITED** absolutely. Food waste (comminuted) $\ge$ **3 NM** normal, $\ge$ **12 NM** Special Area. Dunnage $\ge$ **25 NM** normal.
- ✅ **Annex VI:** Global sulphur cap = **0.50% m/m** (since **1 Jan 2020**); ECA sulphur cap = **0.10% m/m**.
- ✅ **ECA zones:** Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA, US Caribbean ECA.
- ✅ **EEDI** = design index; **SEEMP** = operational plan; **CII** = annual rating A–E.
- ✅ **Special Areas (Annex I):** Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, Gulf of Aden, North-West European, Oman, Arabian Seas, Antarctic.
- ✅ **Special Areas (Annex V):** Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, Gulf of Aden, North Sea, Wider Caribbean, Antarctic.
- ✅ **Key Certificates:** IOPP (Annex I), NLS (Annex II), ISPP (Annex IV), IAPP (Annex VI).
- ✅ **Enforcement:** By **flag state** (primary) and **port state control** (secondary).
- ✅ **Scrubbers (EGCS)** are an alternative to low-sulphur fuel for SOₓ compliance.
- ✅ **Plastic Discharge:** Always 0% — there is **no** minimum distance at sea that permits plastic discharge.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: STCW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** STCW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: STCW — Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STCW (International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Seafarers, 1978)** is the primary international maritime convention adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** that prescribes minimum qualification standards for **masters, officers, and watchkeeping personnel** on seagoing merchant ships. It was adopted on **7 July 1978** at a conference held at the IMO headquarters in London and entered into force on **28 April 1984**.

The convention was later substantially revised through the **1995 amendments** (entry into force: **1 February 1997**) and the major **Manila Amendments 2010** (entry into force: **1 January 2012**), which is the version currently in force globally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Regulatory Bodies Referenced:**
> - **IMO** — International Maritime Organization (UN specialized agency, HQ: London)
> - **ILO** — International Labour Organization (co-advisor on seafarer welfare)
> - **Flag State** — Country of ship registration
> - **Port State Control (PSC)** — Authority enforcing STCW on foreign ships calling at port
> - **Maritime Administrations** — National bodies (e.g., DG Shipping India, MCA UK, USCG USA)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **global highway system** (the seas) where vehicles (ships) from every country travel. Without a universal driving license, chaos would reign. STCW acts exactly like an **internationally agreed-upon "driver's license"** for seafarers:

- Just as a car driver must pass standardized tests, a **ship's captain must prove competence** in navigation, cargo handling, and emergency response.
- Just as traffic rules prevent accidents, **STCW watchkeeping rules** (Chapter VIII) prevent collisions by mandating standardized bridge procedures.
- Just as commercial truckers need special HazMat certification, STCW has **specialized training** for tankers, passenger ships, and high-speed craft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why STCW Matters in One Line:**
> STCW ensures that **whoever stands a watch on your ship** — be it an Indian officer, a Filipino rating, or a Greek master — has been trained, examined, and certified to a **common, legally binding global minimum standard**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Memorize These)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Year of adoption | **1978** |
| Original entry into force | **28 April 1984** |
| Manila Amendments entry into force | **1 January 2012** |
| Major revised Annex adopted | **2010 (Manila Conference)** |
| Minimum hours of rest per 24 hrs | **10 hours** |
| Minimum hours of rest per 7 days | **77 hours** |
| Maximum number of rest periods per 24 hrs | **2 (exceptionally 3)** |
| Minimum consecutive rest period | **6 hours** |
| Manila transitional period (existing certificates) | **Until 1 January 2017** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose and Objectives of STCW

The convention was born out of global concern that **lack of uniform training standards** was a major contributor to maritime casualties, oil spills, and loss of life. The key objectives are:

1. **Promote Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS-aligned):** Standardize competence of seafarers to reduce human error.
2. **Promote Safety of Property at Sea:** Ensure crew can handle cargo, machinery, and navigation equipment safely.
3. **Promote Protection of the Marine Environment:** Particularly through competence in pollution prevention (linked to MARPOL).
4. **Establish Internationally Agreed Minimum Standards:** For training, certification, and watchkeeping duties.
5. **Enable Port State Control:** Port authorities can verify foreign seafarers hold valid STCW-compliant certificates.
6. **Promote Continuous Professional Development (CPD):** Especially after Manila 2010 amendments.

---

## 2.2 Structural Breakdown of the Convention

The convention consists of **Articles** (the legal body) and an **Annex** (technical regulations), plus a **STCW Code** (detailed implementation rules). The hierarchy is critical for exam questions.

### 2.2.1 Articles (The Legal Framework)

- **Article I** — General obligations of Parties (member states).
- **Article II** — Definitions.
- **Article III** — Application of the Convention.
- **Article IV** — Communication of information (each Party must submit lists of certificates).
- **Article V–IX** — Special provisions for the Convention's operation.
- **Article X** — Signature, ratification, acceptance, approval, and accession.
- **Article XI** — Amendments (technical and non-technical procedures).
- **Article XII** — Entry into force.
- **Article XIII–XVI** — Reservations, denunciation, depositary functions, official languages.

### 2.2.2 Annex (Chapters) — The Technical Framework

The Annex contains **8 Chapters** of mandatory regulations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STCW Annex — 8 Chapters (KTU High-Yield List):**
>
> | Chapter | Title | Key Focus |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Chapter I** | General Provisions | Definitions, application, control, certificates, dispensation |
> | **Chapter II** | Master & Deck Department | Master, Chief Mate, OOW, ratings forming part of navigational watch |
> | **Chapter III** | Engine Department | Chief Engineer, 2nd Engineer, OOW (engine), ratings forming part of engine-room watch |
> | **Chapter IV** | Radiocommunication & Radio Personnel | GMDSS requirements, GOC, ROC, restricted operator's certificate |
> | **Chapter V** | Special Training for Specific Ship Types | Tankers (oil, chemical, gas), passenger ships, ro-ro, high-speed craft |
> | **Chapter VI** | Emergency, Occupational Safety, Security, Medical Care | Survival craft, fire-fighting, medical first aid, security awareness |
> | **Chapter VII** | Alternative Certification | Special provisions for personnel serving on board ships of limited size/tonnage |
> | **Chapter VIII** | Watchkeeping Arrangements | Bridge watchkeeping, engine-room watchkeeping, fitness for duty |

### 2.2.3 STCW Code (Implementation Rules)

The **STCW Code** is divided into **two parts** (A and B):

> [!NOTE]
> - **Part A — Mandatory Standards:** Specifies **minimum standards of competence** for each function (e.g., navigation, cargo handling, controlling the operation of the ship). These are **binding**.
> - **Part B — Recommended Guidance:** Provides **guidance on implementation** of the mandatory standards. Not binding but provides best practices.

The Code mirrors the Annex structure (Chapters A-I to A-VIII, B-I to B-VIII).

---

## 2.3 The Four Functions of Competence (Post-Manila 2010)

A core post-2010 concept is that seafarer competence is categorized into **four broad functions**. Examiners frequently ask students to list these.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Functions of Competence (STCW Code, Part A, Chapter II):**
>
> 1. **Function 1 — Navigation** (Voyage planning, position fixing, radar/ARPA, ECDIS, meteorology, etc.)
> 2. **Function 2 — Cargo Handling and Stowage** (Loading/discharging, stability, dangerous goods)
> 3. **Function 3 — Controlling the Operation of the Ship and Care for Persons on Board** (Safety, environmental protection, medical, crew management)
> 4. **Function 4 — Marine Engineering** (Main & auxiliary machinery, electrical, control systems)

For **Engine Department** officers, Functions 1, 2, 3, and **Function 4** are defined analogously with engineering emphasis (Function 4 is "Marine Engineering"; an additional function — **Function 5 — Electrical, Electronic and Control Engineering** — is added for engine officers, and **Function 6 — Maintenance and Repair** for engineering ratings).

---

## 2.4 Manila 2010 Amendments — Major Changes

The Manila Amendments represent the most comprehensive revision in STCW history. Examiners expect knowledge of these changes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Manila 2010 Changes (High-Yield for KTU):**
>
> 1. **Security Awareness & Training (Chapter VI, Section A-VI/1 to A-VI/4):** New mandatory security training for all seafarers.
> 2. **ECDIS Mandatory Competence:** Every navigation officer must be ECDIS competent (Section A-II/1).
> 3. **BRM & ERM (Bridge Resource Management / Engine-room Resource Management):** Leadership and teamworking skills made mandatory.
> 4. **Updated Tanker Training:** New specialized training for LNG/LPG, chemical, oil tankers.
> 5. **Updated GMDSS Requirements:** New GMDSS certificates (GOC/ROC) aligned with revised radio regulations.
> 6. **Hours of Work & Rest (Chapter VIII, Section A-VIII/1):** New explicit minimum rest hours (10/24, 77/7).
> 7. **Polar Code & High-Speed Craft training** (added via Section A-V/4).
> 8. **Continuous Professional Competence:** Periodic training requirements made more explicit.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — STCW Hours of Rest Calculation

This is the most "calculable" part of STCW. Use it for numerical questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **STCW Minimum Rest Requirements (Section A-VIII/1):**
>
> | Quantity | Required Value |
> |---|---|
> | $R_{24}$ — Rest hours per 24-hour period | $\geq \mathbf{10 \text{ hours}}$ |
> | $R_{7}$ — Rest hours per 7-day period | $\geq \mathbf{77 \text{ hours}}$ |
> | $N_{periods}$ — Number of rest periods per 24 hrs | $\leq \mathbf{2 \text{ periods}}$ |
> | $L_{max}$ — Longest consecutive rest period | $\geq \mathbf{6 \text{ hours}}$ |
> | $H_{work}^{24}$ — Maximum work hours per 24 hrs | $\leq \mathbf{14 \text{ hours}}$ |
> | $H_{work}^{7}$ — Maximum work hours per 7 days | $\leq \mathbf{91 \text{ hours}}$ |

**Mathematical relationships** (use these for any 14-mark problem involving rest hours):

$$
H_{work}^{24} + R_{24} = 24 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
H_{work}^{7} + R_{7} = 7 \times 24 = 168 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
H_{work}^{7} \le 168 - R_{7}^{min} = 168 - 77 = 91 \text{ hours}
$$

**Engineering Utility:** Used by **Flag State inspectors** to audit **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC 2006)** compliance and by **Port State Control (PSC)** officers to verify seafarer welfare. A ship failing this can be detained.

---

## 2.6 Certification Pathway — Hierarchy of Documents

The convention specifies the chain of documents a seafarer must hold:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Certification (from basic to highest):**
>
> 1. **Proof of Basic Training** (Section A-VI/1 — PST, PSCRB, EFA, FA, Security Awareness)
> 2. **Certificate of Competency (CoC)** — National, issued by Maritime Administration (e.g., MEO Class IV → Class I Master Mariner)
> 3. **Endorsement (Flag State Endorsement)** — Attests the CoC is recognized by another Flag State
> 4. **Certificate of Equivalent Competency (CEC)** — Issued in some countries (e.g., UK MCA) to foreign CoC holders
> 5. **Flag State CoC** — The operational certificate carried onboard

**Note:** A seafarer must hold a **CoC issued by a Party (country) to the STCW** and obtain an **endorsement** if serving on a ship of a different flag.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — STCW Hours of Rest Verification

This is a classic **14-mark Part B question**. The examiner typically gives a 7-day work/rest log and asks you to verify STCW compliance. We will work through it step-by-step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Model KTU Question Style):**
> The OOW of MV *Kerala Pioneer* recorded the following rest hours during a 7-day period:
>
> | Day | Total Rest (hours) | Number of Rest Periods | Longest Consecutive Rest |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 9 | 2 | 5 |
> | 2 | 10 | 1 | 10 |
> | 3 | 8 | 2 | 4 |
> | 4 | 11 | 2 | 6 |
> | 5 | 9 | 1 | 9 |
> | 6 | 7 | 2 | 3 |
> | 7 | 12 | 2 | 7 |
>
> **Verify whether the watchkeeper is STCW-compliant. State violations, if any.**

### Step 1 — Recall the Four Mandatory STCW Rules

For every 24-hour period:
- Rule (a): Total rest $\ge 10$ hours
- Rule (b): Rest split into $\le 2$ periods (exceptionally 3)
- Rule (c): At least one rest period $\ge 6$ hours consecutive

For every 7-day period:
- Rule (d): Total rest $\ge 77$ hours

### Step 2 — Verify Each Day Against Rules (a), (b), (c)

**Day 1:** Rest = 9 hrs; Periods = 2; Longest = 5 hrs.
- (a) 9 < 10 → ❌ **VIOLATION** (Rule a)
- (b) 2 ≤ 2 → ✓
- (c) 5 < 6 → ❌ **VIOLATION** (Rule c)

**Day 2:** Rest = 10 hrs; Periods = 1; Longest = 10 hrs.
- (a) 10 ≥ 10 → ✓
- (b) 1 ≤ 2 → ✓
- (c) 10 ≥ 6 → ✓
- **COMPLIANT** for all daily rules.

**Day 3:** Rest = 8 hrs; Periods = 2; Longest = 4 hrs.
- (a) 8 < 10 → ❌ **VIOLATION** (Rule a)
- (b) 2 ≤ 2 → ✓
- (c) 4 < 6 → ❌ **VIOLATION** (Rule c)

**Day 4:** Rest = 11 hrs; Periods = 2; Longest = 6 hrs.
- (a) 11 ≥ 10 → ✓
- (b) 2 ≤ 2 → ✓
- (c) 6 ≥ 6 → ✓
- **COMPLIANT** for all daily rules.

**Day 5:** Rest = 9 hrs; Periods = 1; Longest = 9 hrs.
- (a) 9 < 10 → ❌ **VIOLATION** (Rule a)
- (b) 1 ≤ 2 → ✓
- (c) 9 ≥ 6 → ✓

**Day 6:** Rest = 7 hrs; Periods = 2; Longest = 3 hrs.
- (a) 7 < 10 → ❌ **VIOLATION** (Rule a)
- (b) 2 ≤ 2 → ✓
- (c) 3 < 6 → ❌ **VIOLATION** (Rule c)

**Day 7:** Rest = 12 hrs; Periods = 2; Longest = 7 hrs.
- (a) 12 ≥ 10 → ✓
- (b) 2 ≤ 2 → ✓
- (c) 7 ≥ 6 → ✓
- **COMPLIANT** for all daily rules.

### Step 3 — Verify the 7-Day Rule (Rule d)

Sum the rest hours across the week:

$$
R_{7} = 9 + 10 + 8 + 11 + 9 + 7 + 12
$$

$$
R_{7} = 66 \text{ hours}
$$

**Comparison with the required minimum:**

$$
R_{7} = 66 \text{ hours} \;\;\; < \;\;\; R_{7}^{min} = 77 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad R_{7} - R_{7}^{min} = 66 - 77 = -11 \text{ hours deficit}
$$

**Result:** ❌ **VIOLATION** of Rule (d). The watchkeeper is short by **11 hours** of rest in the 7-day period.

### Step 4 — Compute Maximum Work Hours for the Week

By the complementary relationship:

$$
H_{work}^{7} = 168 - R_{7} = 168 - 66 = 102 \text{ hours}
$$

Compare with the maximum allowed:

$$
H_{work}^{7,max} = 168 - 77 = 91 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
102 > 91 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Excess work} = 102 - 91 = 11 \text{ hours}
$$

This matches the rest deficit. ✓

### Step 5 — Final Verdict and Action

> **Conclusion:** The watchkeeper is **NOT STCW-compliant**.
> - **Day 1:** Violation of Rules (a) and (c) — rest short by 1 hr; longest rest short by 1 hr.
> - **Day 3:** Violation of Rules (a) and (c) — rest short by 2 hrs; longest rest short by 2 hrs.
> - **Day 5:** Violation of Rule (a) — rest short by 1 hr.
> - **Day 6:** Violation of Rules (a) and (c) — rest short by 3 hrs; longest rest short by 3 hrs.
> - **Weekly:** Violation of Rule (d) — 11 hours rest deficit (66 vs 77 required).

**Corrective Action:** Rest periods must be **rearranged** so that on each of the violating days, at least **6 consecutive hours of rest** are obtained, and the weekly total exceeds **77 hours**. The Master should review **manning levels**, **port operation workload**, and **fatigue mitigation** measures.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping STCW Competence to Career Levels

> [!NOTE]
> **STCW Career-Level Competency Matrix (Deck Department):**
>
> | Function | Master (Unlimited) | Chief Mate (Unlimited) | OOW (Operational) | Rating (Watch) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Navigation (F1) | Full | Full | Watchkeeping level | Helmsman, lookout |
> | Cargo Handling (F2) | Full | Full | Basic | Basic |
> | Controlling Ship Operation (F3) | Full | Full | Basic | Crowd management |
> | Engineering (F4) | Basic | Basic | Basic | — |
> | Minimum sea service (months) | ≥ 36 (post-MEO Class I) | ≥ 18 | ≥ 12 | ≥ 6 (BST) |

For **Engine Department** — the analogous hierarchy is **Chief Engineer, 2nd Engineer, OOW (Engine), Motorman/Rating**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Architecture of the STCW Framework

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture** of STCW. It shows the relationship between the Convention body, the Annex, the Code (Parts A and B), and their interaction with shipboard personnel, Flag States, and Port State Control.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STCW CONVENTION 1978"] --> A2["ARTICLES I to XVI"]
    A1 --> A3["ANNEX with 8 CHAPTERS"]
    A3 --> B1["Chapter I General Provisions"]
    A3 --> B2["Chapter II Master and Deck Dept"]
    A3 --> B3["Chapter III Engine Department"]
    A3 --> B4["Chapter IV Radio Personnel"]
    A3 --> B5["Chapter V Special Ship Types"]
    A3 --> B6["Chapter VI Safety and Security"]
    A3 --> B7["Chapter VII Alternative Cert"]
    A3 --> B8["Chapter VIII Watchkeeping"]

    A1 --> A4["STCW CODE"]
    A4 --> C1["Part A MANDATORY STANDARDS"]
    A4 --> C2["Part B RECOMMENDED GUIDANCE"]
    C1 --> D1["Minimum competence criteria"]
    C2 --> D2["Best practice implementation"]

    B8 --> E1["Section A VIII 1 Hours of Rest"]
    B8 --> E2["Section A VIII 2 Bridge Watch"]
    B8 --> E3["Section A VIII 2 Engine Room Watch"]
    E1 --> F1["Min 10 hrs per 24 hrs"]
    E1 --> F2["Min 77 hrs per 7 days"]
    E1 --> F3["Longest period at least 6 hrs"]

    A1 --> G1["ENFORCEMENT MECHANISMS"]
    G1 --> H1["Flag State Administration"]
    G1 --> H2["Port State Control PSC"]
    G1 --> H3["Recognized Organizations ROs"]
    G1 --> H4["Maritime Labour Convention MLC 2006"]
```

---

## 4.2 The Four Functions of Competence — Mermaid Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["SEAFARER COMPETENCE FRAMEWORK"] --> F1["Function 1 Navigation"]
    S1 --> F2["Function 2 Cargo Handling and Stowage"]
    S1 --> F3["Function 3 Controlling the Ship and Care for Persons"]
    S1 --> F4["Function 4 Marine Engineering"]

    F1 --> S1a["Voyage planning"]
    F1 --> S1b["Radar and ARPA"]
    F1 --> S1c["ECDIS operation"]
    F1 --> S1d["Meteorology"]

    F2 --> S2a["Cargo loading and discharging"]
    F2 --> S2b["Ship stability"]
    F2 --> S2c["Dangerous goods IMDG"]

    F3 --> S3a["Safety management"]

    F3 --> S3c["Medical care"]

    F4 --> S4a["Main propulsion"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. What is the full form of STCW? When was the Manila Amendments version entered into force?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> STCW stands for the **International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Seafarers, 1978**. It was adopted on 7 July 1978 and originally entered into force on **28 April 1984**. The most recent major revision — the **Manila Amendments 2010** — entered into force on **1 January 2012** and is the version currently applicable. The convention is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and establishes minimum training, certification, and watchkeeping standards for masters, officers, and watchkeeping ratings on seagoing merchant ships.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q2. List the minimum hours of rest required under STCW for a watchkeeper.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> As per **STCW Section A-VIII/1**, the minimum rest requirements are:
> 1. **10 hours of rest in any 24-hour period**.
> 2. **77 hours of rest in any 7-day period**.
> 3. The rest may be divided into **no more than 2 periods** (exceptionally 3).
> 4. **At least one of the periods must be of 6 consecutive hours minimum**.
> Hours of rest should not be divided into more than three periods without valid documented reason, and any reduction of the 6-hour consecutive rule must be compensated by an equivalent increase in another rest period.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** Discuss the historical background, purpose, and major changes introduced by the **STCW Manila Amendments 2010**. (7 marks)
> **(b)** With the help of a suitable example, verify whether the following 7-day rest log is STCW-compliant:
> *Daily rest hours: 9, 10, 8, 11, 9, 7, 12; number of rest periods: 2, 1, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2; longest consecutive rest: 5, 10, 4, 6, 9, 3, 7 hours respectively.* (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Historical Background and Manila Amendments (7 marks)

**Historical Background:**
- Following major maritime disasters in the 1970s, a growing concern emerged that **inconsistent training standards** among seafarers worldwide contributed to **maritime casualties and pollution**.
- In **1978**, the IMO adopted the **STCW Convention** at a conference in London.
- It entered into force on **28 April 1984**, becoming the **first international convention to establish mandatory minimum qualification standards** for seafarers.
- Subsequent amendments:
  - **1989 amendments** — Small amendments on radio operators.
  - **1991 amendments** — Special requirements for tankers.
  - **1994/1995 amendments** — Major revision (entered into force 1 February 1997) including the **STCW Code**.
  - **2010 Manila Amendments** — The most comprehensive revision (entered into force 1 January 2012).
- The convention applies to **seagoing ships** of 200 GT or more, engaged on international voyages, but Parties may extend it to smaller ships and to their own flag vessels.

**Purpose of STCW:**
1. Promote **safety of life and property at sea**.
2. Promote **protection of the marine environment**.
3. Establish **internationally agreed minimum standards** of training, certification, and watchkeeping.
4. Enable **Port State Control (PSC)** to enforce uniform standards.

**Major Changes in Manila Amendments 2010 (write at least 5 for full marks):**
1. **Mandatory security training** for all seafarers (Ship Security Officer, Security Awareness, etc.).
2. **ECDIS competence** made mandatory for all navigation officers.
3. **Bridge Resource Management (BRM)** and **Engine-room Resource Management (ERM)** introduced.
4. **Revised GMDSS** requirements and updated radio operator certificates.
5. **New hours of rest** rules under Section A-VIII/1.
6. **Updated tanker training** for oil, chemical, LNG/LPG carriers.
7. **New training for passenger ships, high-speed craft, and polar waters** (Section A-V/4).
8. Strengthened **Port State Control** provisions and **fitness-for-duty** requirements.

#### Part (b) — Compliance Verification (7 marks)

*(Refer to the step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes for the full solution. Briefly: the rest log is **non-compliant** on Days 1, 3, 5, 6 and weekly total is 66 hours — 11 hours short of the required 77. Watchkeeper violates Rules (a), (c) on 4 days and Rule (d) weekly.)*

**Final Table for Examiner's Reference:**

| Day | Rest (hr) | Periods | Longest | (a) ≥10 | (b) ≤2 | (c) ≥6 | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 9 | 2 | 5 | ❌ | ✓ | ❌ | Violation |
| 2 | 10 | 1 | 10 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | OK |
| 3 | 8 | 2 | 4 | ❌ | ✓ | ❌ | Violation |
| 4 | 11 | 2 | 6 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | OK |
| 5 | 9 | 1 | 9 | ❌ | ✓ | ✓ | Violation |
| 6 | 7 | 2 | 3 | ❌ | ✓ | ❌ | Violation |
| 7 | 12 | 2 | 7 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | OK |
| **7-Day Total** | **66** | | | | | | **< 77 — Violation** |

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **(a)** Explain the **structure of the STCW Code** (Parts A and B) and describe in detail **Chapter VIII — Watchkeeping Arrangements and Principles**. (7 marks)
> **(b)** What are the **four functions of competence** under STCW Manila Amendments? List at least **three specific competencies** required for an **Officer in Charge of a Navigational Watch (OOW)** under **Function 1 — Navigation**. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — STCW Code Structure and Chapter VIII (7 marks)

**STCW Code Structure:**
- The **STCW Code** was introduced through the **1994/1995 amendments** and provides detailed implementation rules for the Annex.
- It is divided into **two parts**:
  - **Part A — Mandatory Standards** (binding on Parties): Specifies **minimum standards of competence** for each function, including the **knowledge, understanding, and proficiency (KUP)** required.
  - **Part B — Recommended Guidance** (non-binding): Provides **best practice guidance** on how to implement the mandatory standards, including training methods, assessment, and quality standards.
- Both Parts are organized in chapters (A-I to A-VIII and B-I to B-VIII) that mirror the Annex chapters.

**Chapter VIII — Watchkeeping Arrangements and Principles (Section A-VIII/2 and B-VIII/2):**

The watchkeeping principles are issued as:
- **Section A-VIII/1** — **Fitness for duty** (minimum hours of rest).
- **Section A-VIII/2** — **Principles governing watchkeeping arrangements** for:
  1. **Part 1 — General principles** (for all watchkeepers — applies to all officers and ratings).
  2. **Part 2 — Principles governing navigation watchkeeping** (bridge).
  3. **Part 3 — Principles governing engine-room watchkeeping**.
  4. **Part 4 — Principles governing watchkeeping by ratings**.

**Key principles for navigation watch (bridge):**
1. The Master must **arrange the watch** to maintain safety of navigation.
2. **Officer in Charge of a Navigational Watch (OOW)** must ensure continuous watchkeeping at all times.
3. OOW must not be assigned or undertake any duties which would interfere with navigation duties.
4. Proper **hand-over/take-over** procedure must be followed.
5. Use of **navigational equipment** (radar, ECDIS, GPS) must be in accordance with international regulations (COLREGs, SOLAS).
6. **Lookout** must be maintained at all times (Rule 5 of COLREGs).
7. Master must call OOW when in doubt, restricted visibility, heavy traffic, or any abnormal circumstance.

**Key principles for engine-room watch:**
1. Engineer officer must be on call when ship is at anchor or in port.
2. Continuous or periodic watch as per Master/Chief Engineer's standing orders.
3. Proper log-keeping and machinery condition monitoring.
4. Hand-over/take-over procedures similar to bridge.

#### Part (b) — Four Functions of Competence and OOW Navigation Competencies (7 marks)

**The Four Functions of Competence (STCW Code, Part A, Chapter II, Section A-II/1, A-II/2, A-II/3):**

> 1. **Function 1 — Navigation** (voyage planning, position fixing, meteorology, BRM)
> 2. **Function 2 — Cargo Handling and Stowage** (loading/discharging, stability)
> 3. **Function 3 — Controlling the Operation of the Ship and Care for Persons on Board** (safety, environmental, medical, crew)
> 4. **Function 4 — Marine Engineering** (basic engineering knowledge for deck officers; for engine officers, F1–F4 redefined analogously with F4 = Marine Engineering, F5 = Electrical/Electronic/Control, F6 = Maintenance and Repair for engine ratings)

**Three Specific Competencies Required for OOW under Function 1 — Navigation:**

1. **Voyage Planning and Navigation for Safe Passage:**
   - Knowledge of voyage planning principles, including routing per IMO Ships' Routeing, weather routing, ocean current, and use of publications (Sailing Directions, ALRS, Tide Tables, Routeing Charts).
   - Proficiency in calculating **Compass Error** by azimuth/amplitude, and **position fixing** by terrestrial, celestial, electronic, and astronomical means.

2. **Radar Navigation and Use of ARPA:**
   - Knowledge of **radar principles**, ability to operate radar, use of **ARPA** (Automatic Radar Plotting Aid) for target tracking, identification of targets, **CPA/TCPA** determination, trial maneuvers.

3. **Use of ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display and Information System):**
   - Proficiency in **ECDIS** operation, including route planning, route monitoring, alarm handling, system limitations, and use of **ENC (Electronic Navigational Charts)** as per **IHO S-57/S-100** standards.

4. *(Optional — for additional marks)* **Meteorology and Oceanography:** Knowledge of weather systems, synoptic chart interpretation, sea state, and tropical storm avoidance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse STCW with SOLAS** — STCW is for **seafarer competence**, SOLAS is for **ship construction and equipment**.
> 2. **Do NOT say "STCW is from 1984"** — It was **adopted in 1978**; 1984 is the year of **entry into force**. Examiners strictly differentiate.
> 3. **Rest hours question:** Remember the **6-hour consecutive rule** and the **2-period (exceptionally 3) rule**. Students often forget the **consecutive 6-hour rule** and lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **"Master Mariner" vs "Master Unlimited":** In the STCW context, "Master" is a competency, not a license; the relevant certificate is **Master Mariner (Unlimited)** under MEO Class I (or equivalent).
> 5. **Chapters IX and X:** Some students mistakenly cite **Chapter IX and X of STCW** — there are **only 8 chapters** in the current Annex. Watch out!
> 6. **Manila Amendments transitional period:** Existing certificates issued before 1 January 2012 had to be **revalidated by 1 January 2017** — this date is frequently asked in Part A.
> 7. **Section A-VIII/1 vs B-VIII/1:** Section A is **mandatory**, Section B is **recommended guidance** — don't mix them up when citing rest hours.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STCW — Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This for Last-Minute Review):**
>
> ✅ **STCW =** International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Seafarers, 1978
>
> ✅ **Adopted:** 7 July 1978 | **Original Entry into Force:** 28 April 1984 | **Manila Amendments Entry into Force:** 1 January 2012
>
> ✅ **Administered by:** International Maritime Organization (IMO), headquartered in London
>
> ✅ **Structure:** Articles (I–XVI) + Annex (8 Chapters) + STCW Code (Part A mandatory + Part B guidance)
>
> ✅ **Annex Chapters:** (1) General, (2) Master & Deck, (3) Engine, (4) Radio, (5) Special Ship Types, (6) Safety/Security/Medical, (7) Alternative Cert, (8) Watchkeeping
>
> ✅ **Four Functions of Competence (Deck):** Navigation, Cargo Handling & Stowage, Controlling Ship Operation, Marine Engineering
>
> ✅ **Four Functions of Competence (Engine):** Mechanical Engineering, Electrical/Electronic/Control, Maintenance & Repair, Controlling Operation
>
> ✅ **Hours of Rest Rules (A-VIII/1):** ≥10 hrs/24 hrs, ≥77 hrs/7 days, ≤2 periods (exceptionally 3), ≥6 hrs consecutive
>
> ✅ **Maximum Work Hours:** ≤14 hrs/24 hrs, ≤91 hrs/7 days
>
> ✅ **Key Manila 2010 Changes:** Security training, ECDIS, BRM/ERM, updated GMDSS, updated tanker training, hours of rest
>
> ✅ **Enforcement Bodies:** Flag State Administration, Port State Control (PSC), Recognized Organizations (ROs like Class NK, Lloyd's Register, DNV)
>
> ✅ **Certificate Types:** Certificate of Competency (CoC), Endorsement, GOC/ROC (GMDSS), BST/PST certificates
>
> ✅ **Cross-referenced Conventions:** SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC 2006, COLREGs
>
> ✅ **Transitional Deadline:** Existing certificates to be revalidated by **1 January 2017**
>
> ✅ **Number of Chapters in Annex = 8** (Do NOT say 9 or 10)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ISPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code** is a comprehensive set of mandatory measures developed by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) in response to the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks. It establishes an international framework involving cooperation between Contracting Governments, Government agencies, local administrations, and the shipping and port industries to detect and deter security threats affecting ships or port facilities used in international trade.

The ISPS Code was adopted at a Diplomatic Conference held at IMO Headquarters in London on **12 December 2002** and entered into force on **1 July 2004**. It is implemented under Chapter **XI-2** of the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), 1974**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statutory Anchors of ISPS:**
> - **SOLAS Chapter XI-2** – Special measures to enhance maritime safety and security.
> - **Part A** – Mandatory provisions (regulatory).
> - **Part B** – Guidance/Recommendation (advisory, but treated as mandatory in many jurisdictions).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the ISPS Code as the **"Airport-Style Security Checkpoint" applied to the entire maritime supply chain**.

Before ISPS existed, a port was like a small village where anyone with a hard hat and clipboard could walk onto a ship. After 9/11, the global community realized that ships carrying thousands of containers, hazardous cargo, and hundreds of passengers could be weaponized. ISPS is essentially a **layered security belt** that:

- **Identifies** who is responsible (governments, companies, port authorities, ships).
- **Assesses** what could go wrong (security risk assessment).
- **Implements** three escalating alert states (Security Levels 1, 2, 3).
- **Verifies** readiness through certified Ship Security Officers (SSO) and Port Facility Security Officers (PFSO).

### 1.3 Why ISPS Was Necessary

| Trigger Event | Consequence |
|---|---|
| USS Cole bombing (2000, Yemen) | Showed commercial vessels are vulnerable. |
| 9/11 attacks (2001) | Maritime assets could be terrorist targets. |
| Limburg tanker attack (2002) | Confirmed energy shipping is a target. |
| Global containerized trade growth | Container ships = potential weapons. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The ISPS Code applies to: (i) passenger ships and cargo ships of **500 GT and above** on international voyages, and (ii) the port facilities serving such ships.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Three-Tier Structural Architecture of ISPS

The ISPS framework is built on **three operational pillars**:

**Pillar A – The Ship (Vessel Side)**
- Every applicable vessel must carry a **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** approved by the Administration.
- Each ship must designate a **Ship Security Officer (SSO)**.
- Each vessel must have a **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)** that silently transmits a distress signal to the flag state if activated.

**Pillar B – The Port Facility (Shoreside)**
- Every applicable port facility must develop a **Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP)**.
- Each facility must designate a **Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO)**.
- Facilities must implement access control, surveillance, and restricted zones.

**Pillar C – The Government/Administration (Regulatory Side)**
- Flag states (Administration) issue **International Ship Security Certificates (ISSC)**.
- Contracting Governments set security levels, identify **Port Facility Security Officers**, and approve port facility plans.

### 2.2 The Three Security Levels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Security Levels define the **degree of risk** that a security incident will occur or is currently occurring. Higher levels = more stringent measures.

| Security Level | Color Code (Industry Standard) | Threat Condition | Required Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | Green | Normal – baseline security | Maintain routine preventive security measures. |
| **Level 2** | Yellow | Heightened risk – credible threat | Additional precautionary measures for limited period. |
| **Level 3** | Red | Imminent / actual security incident | Maximum protective measures; possible ship diversion. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Exam Fact:** Security Levels are set by the **Contracting Government** (the coastal state). The Master of the ship always has **overriding authority** to take emergency measures he deems necessary.

### 2.3 The Five Core Functional Requirements

The ISPS Code is structured around five responsibilities for Contracting Governments and the industry:

1. **Gather and assess security-related information** regarding threats.
2. **Maintain a communication link** between ships, port facilities, and authorities.
3. **Prevent unauthorized access** to ships, port facilities, and restricted areas.
4. **Prevent introduction of unauthorized weapons, incendiaries, or explosives**.
5. **Provide a means of raising the alarm** and communicating with authorities in case of threat.

### 2.4 Key Roles and Their Responsibilities

| Role | Full Form | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **SSO** | Ship Security Officer | Ship-side implementation of SSP; liaison with PFSO. |
| **PFSO** | Port Facility Security Officer | Port-side implementation of PFSP; controls access. |
| **CSO** | Company Security Officer | Coordinates fleet security; ensures SSP compliance. |
| **DDoS** | Designated Person Ashore | 24×7 contact between ship and company. |
| **RO** | Recognized Security Organization | Independent body authorized to act on Administration's behalf (audits/certifications). |

### 2.5 Ship Security Plan (SSP) — Mandatory Contents

> [!NOTE]
> **SSP must include the following (Part A, Section 9):**
> 1. Measures to prevent unauthorized weapons/dangerous substances.
> 2. Identification of restricted areas and access control measures.
> 3. Procedures for responding to security threats/breaches.
> 4. Procedures for responding to **Security Level 2 & 3** instructions.
> 5. Procedures for evacuation and **SSAS activation**.
> 6. Duties of crew members and SSO at each security level.
> 7. Procedures for **auditing** the SSP.
> 8. Training, drill, and exercise requirements.
> 9. Procedures for interfacing with port facility security.
> 10. Procedures for periodic review/update of plan.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula/Cheat Sheet

Since ISPS is regulatory rather than numerical, the "formula sheet" below captures all high-yield values, thresholds, and constants the examiner expects.

| Parameter | Value / Threshold | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Applicable ship size | **≥ 500 GT** | International voyage ships |
| ISPS adoption date | **12 December 2002** | Diplomatic Conference, London |
| ISPS entry into force | **1 July 2004** | Operational from this date |
| Statutory anchor | **SOLAS Chapter XI-2** | Part A = mandatory |
| Security Levels | **3 levels (1, 2, 3)** | Green → Yellow → Red |
| Master’s Override | **Always supreme** | Even over PFSO orders |
| ISSC validity | **5 years** | Continuous validity requires audits |
| Ship drills required | **Minimum 1 per quarter** | Security drills |
| Port drills | **Minimum 1 per year** | Combined with agencies |
| SSAS link | **Flag State authority only** | Silent alert; tamper-proof |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beyond compliance, ISPS introduced engineering and operational concepts that drive modern port design:
> - **Surveillance systems**: CCTV, AIS, LRIT (Long Range Identification and Tracking).
> - **Access control hardware**: Biometric turnstiles, biometric cards, ANPR (Automatic Number Plate Recognition).
> - **Restricted zone engineering**: Hardened perimeters, X-ray scanners, sniffer dogs.
> - **Cyber-physical integration**: SSAS transmits over satellite links integrated with ECDIS and bridge systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Risk Assessment & Plan Approval

> [!NOTE]
> While ISPS is not a numerically heavy topic, KTU examiners frequently test the **logical sequence** of how an SSP/PFSP is approved and how security levels escalate. The step-by-step "derivation" below explains the procedural logic that is the actual exam skill.

### 3.1 Logical Derivation: From Threat to Authorized SSP

**Step 1 — Risk Identification**
The Contracting Government continuously gathers intelligence on threats to ships, ports, and trade routes.

**Step 2 — Risk Assessment**
Using a structured risk matrix, threats are evaluated by:

$$
\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Consequence}
$$

**Step 3 — Security Level Setting**
Based on assessed risk, the Contracting Government declares Security Level **1, 2, or 3**.

**Step 4 — Ship-Side Plan Development**
The Company Security Officer (CSO), in consultation with the SSO, drafts the SSP conforming to Part A Section 9.

**Step 5 — Independent Verification**
A **Recognized Security Organization (RSO)** audits the draft SSP against Part A requirements.

**Step 6 — Plan Submission**
The Company submits the SSP to the **Flag Administration** for approval.

**Step 7 — Approval and Certification**
Once approved, the Administration issues the **International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)** valid for 5 years, subject to intermediate verification audits.

### 3.2 Logical Derivation: Security Level Escalation Decision Tree

The escalation logic at any port call is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial State} &= \text{Level 1 (Normal)} \\[4pt]
\text{If threat intel credible} &\Rightarrow \text{Level 2 (Heightened)} \\[4pt]
\text{If attack imminent/underway} &\Rightarrow \text{Level 3 (Maximum)} \\[4pt]
\text{End state} &= \text{Return to Level 1 after debrief}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Level 1 → Level 2: Activated by **Government or PFSO recommendation**; lasts hours to days.
- Level 2 → Level 3: Activated by **Government only**; ship may be ordered to leave port or proceed to sea.

### 3.3 Procedural Derivation: What Happens When SSAS Is Triggered

**Step 1** — SSO/Master activates SSAS (deliberate action; covert to attacker).

**Step 2** — Signal is transmitted via satellite to the **Flag State** designated authority.

**Step 3** — Flag State authorities coordinate with **coastal state** and **naval/law enforcement** assets.

**Step 4** — Master simultaneously raises shipboard alarm, ensures crew safety, and documents the threat.

**Step 5** — After resolution, the incident is reported to IMO via flag state investigation.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Responsibilities vs. Security Level

| Responsibility | At Level 1 | At Level 2 | At Level 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access control | Routine checks | Increased scrutiny | Total lockdown |
| Surveillance | Standard CCTV | Enhanced patrols | Continuous monitoring |
| Crew readiness | General training | Briefings & drills | Combat posture |
| Communication | Routine VHF/SatCom | Increased reporting | Real-time link |
| Cargo handling | Standard screening | Targeted inspections | Suspended/certified |
| Ship movement | Normal ops | Possible delay | Possible diversion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ISPS Architecture — Master Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    govA["Contracting Government"] -->|"Sets Security Levels, Approves PFSP"| pfsA
    govA -->|"Recognizes"| rsoA["Recognized Security Organization RSO"]
    rsoA -->|"Audits and Certifies"| sspA
    govB["Flag Administration"] -->|"Issues ISSC valid 5 yrs"| shipA
    pfsA["PFSO Port Facility Security Officer"] -->|"Implements"| pfspA["Port Facility Security Plan PFSP"]
    shipA["Ship Master and SSO"] -->|"Implements"| sspA["Ship Security Plan SSP"]
    csoA["CSO Company Security Officer"] -->|"Drafts and Maintains"| sspA
    ddosA["DDoS Designated Person Ashore"] -->|"24x7 Contact Link"| shipA
    shipA -->|"SSAS Silent Alert"| govB
    shipA -->|"Interfaces with"| pfsA
    govA -.->|"Security Level 1 2 3"| shipA
    govA -.->|"Security Level 1 2 3"| pfsA
```

### 4.2 Security Level Escalation Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> L1
    L1: Level 1 Normal Green
    L2: Level 2 Heightened Yellow
    L3: Level 3 Imminent Red
    L1 --> L2: Credible threat detected
    L2 --> L1: Threat neutralized
    L2 --> L3: Attack imminent underway
    L3 --> L2: Threat contained
    L3 --> [*]: Incident resolved debrief
    note right of L3: Master override authority applies at all levels
```

### 4.3 Ship Security Plan Approval Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Threat Identification"] --> stepB["Risk Assessment"]
    stepB --> stepC["Security Level Setting"]
    stepC --> stepD["Draft SSP by CSO and SSO"]
    stepD --> stepE["RSO Independent Audit"]
    stepE -->|"Pass"| stepF["Flag Admin Approval"]
    stepE -->|"Fail"| stepD
    stepF --> stepG["ISSC Issued 5 yr"]
    stepG --> stepH["Quarterly Drills"]
    stepH --> stepI["Intermediate Audit"]
    stepI -->|"Pass"| stepG
    stepI -->|"Fail"| stepD
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Crew-to-Authority Information Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    crewA["Ship Crew"] --> ssoA["Ship Security Officer SSO"]
    ssoA -->|"SSAS Alert"| adminA["Flag Administration"]
    ssoA -->|"Routine Info"| csoA["Company Security Officer CSO"]
    csoA --> ddosA["Designated Person Ashore DDoS"]
    ssoA -->|"Interface on Berthing"| pfsoA["Port Facility Security Officer PFSO"]
    pfsoA --> govA["Contracting Government"]
    adminA --> govA
    govA -->|"Orders and Directives"| adminA
    govA -->|"Directives"| pfsoA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**Define the ISPS Code. When did it enter into force?**

**Model Answer:**
The International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code is a mandatory IMO framework adopted on 12 December 2002 and entering into force on **1 July 2004**, under SOLAS Chapter XI-2. It establishes security obligations for ships (≥500 GT on international voyages), port facilities serving them, and Contracting Governments to detect and deter maritime security threats.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**List any three Security Levels under ISPS and their corresponding threat conditions.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Security Level 1** – Normal, baseline condition (Green).
- **Security Level 2** – Heightened risk due to credible threat (Yellow).
- **Security Level 3** – Imminent or actual security incident (Red).

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the responsibilities of the **Ship Security Officer (SSO)** and the **Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO)** under the ISPS Code.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Ship Security Officer (SSO) — Responsibilities:**
1. Conducts regular security inspections of the ship.
2. Ensures implementation of the approved **Ship Security Plan (SSP)**.
3. Maintains liaison with the PFSO and CSO.
4. Reports security deficiencies and incidents to the Master and CSO.
5. Coordinates security drills at the prescribed frequency.
6. Verifies SSAS functionality and trains crew on activation.

**Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO) — Responsibilities:**
1. Conducts an initial **Port Facility Security Assessment (PFSA)**.
2. Develops and implements the **Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP)**.
3. Controls access to the port facility and restricted zones.
4. Liaises with ship SSOs, RSO, and Contracting Government agencies.
5. Ensures adequate training and drills for port personnel.
6. Reports security incidents to the relevant authorities.

> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)** and its operational significance.

**Model Solution (b):**
The SSAS is a tamper-proof silent alarm system mandated by the ISPS Code. When activated, it sends a covert alert via satellite to the **flag state authority** without alerting the threat actor on board.

**Operational Significance:**
1. Enables rapid flag-state response without endangering crew.
2. Triggers naval/law enforcement mobilization.
3. Acts as a credible deterrent against hijack attempts.
4. Operates independently from standard GMDSS equipment.
5. Must be tested annually and after every maintenance.

#### **Question B — 14 Marks**

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **five core functional requirements** of the ISPS Code.

**Model Solution (a):**
1. Ensure relevant security information is gathered and assessed.
2. Maintain continuous communication between ships, port facilities, and authorities.
3. Prevent unauthorized access to ships, port facilities, and restricted areas.
4. Prevent introduction of unauthorized weapons, incendiaries, or explosives.
5. Provide a robust means of raising alarms and coordinating responses during threats.

> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)** — issuing authority, validity, and verification requirements.

**Model Solution (b):**
- **Issuing Authority:** Flag Administration (or a recognized RSO authorized by the Administration).
- **Validity:** Five years from the date of issue.
- **Continuous Validity:** Subject to:
  - At least one **intermediate verification audit** (between 2nd and 3rd year).
  - Annual compliance confirmation.
- **Issuance Condition:** Only after the SSP is approved and verified compliant with Part A of the Code.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse **"Part A"** (mandatory) and **"Part B"** (advisory) — remember that KTU questions award marks only for Part A compliance.
> 2. ISSC is valid for **5 years**, but many write "3 years" — that's wrong.
> 3. SSAS transmits to **flag state only**, not to coastal state or port — many students misstate this.
> 4. **Master's Override Authority** must always be mentioned when discussing Security Levels.
> 5. The 500 GT threshold applies to international voyages — do not omit this qualification.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ISPS Code** = IMO's mandatory maritime security framework, in force since **1 July 2004**.
- Statutory anchor: **SOLAS Chapter XI-2**, with Part A mandatory and Part B advisory.
- Applies to **ships ≥500 GT** on international voyages and their serving port facilities.
- **Three Security Levels:** Level 1 (Normal), Level 2 (Heightened), Level 3 (Imminent).
- Security Levels are set by the **Contracting Government**; **Master's override authority** always prevails.
- **Key Roles:** SSO (ship), PFSO (port), CSO (company), DDoS (ashore 24×7 contact).
- **SSAS:** Silent, tamper-proof satellite alert → flag state only.
- **ISSC:** Issued by flag administration → **valid 5 years** with intermediate audits.
- **SSP mandatory contents** include restricted area identification, level-specific procedures, SSAS operation, drill schedule, and audit/review mechanism.
- **Three-tier architecture:** Government (regulator) ↔ Port Facility (PFSO/PFSP) ↔ Ship (SSO/SSP).
- **Five functional requirements** form the exam backbone of any ISPS "explain" question.
- **Drills:** Quarterly on ship, annually at port facility.
- **Recognized Security Organization (RSO)** = independent body authorized to audit and certify on behalf of Administration.
- Failure to comply → detention, ISSC withdrawal, port-state control action.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** MLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of MLC

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006** — officially titled the *Maritime Labour Convention* and frequently referred to as the **"Fourth Pillar"** of international maritime regulation alongside SOLAS, MARPOL, and STCW — is an International Labour Organization (ILO) convention that establishes minimum working and living standards for all seafarers working on ships flying the flags of ratifying countries. It consolidates and updates over **68 prior ILO maritime conventions and recommendations** into a single, comprehensive, and enforceable legal instrument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"The Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006 is a comprehensive ILO convention that sets out seafarers' rights to decent conditions of work, covering a wide range of requirements including minimum age, employment conditions, hours of work, rest periods, repatriation, accommodation, recreational facilities, food and catering, health protection, medical care, welfare, and social security protection."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of the MLC as the **"Seafarers' Magna Carta"** — just like a country's labour laws protect factory workers by specifying minimum wages, working hours, safety, and rest, the MLC does exactly the same for people working on ships. If SOLAS protects the *ship* from sinking, and MARPOL protects the *sea* from pollution, then MLC protects the *seafarer* from exploitation.

Imagine a ship is like a **floating factory**. Inside this factory, thousands of miles from home, work for months on end. The MLC is essentially a **"labour inspector's rulebook"** that travels with every ship, ensuring the crew has:
- A proper bed (not a hammock in the engine room)
- Nutritious food (not just biscuits and stale water)
- Fair wages paid on time
- Limits on working hours (because exhaustion at sea causes accidents)
- A contract they can understand
- Medical care when sick

This convention is often called the **"single most important"** labour instrument for seafarers because for the first time in history, virtually every aspect of a seafarer's working life is covered under ONE binding international law.

### 1.3 Historical Context & Significance

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1919** | ILO founded (Part of Treaty of Versailles after WWI) |
| **1920** | First maritime labour conventions adopted |
| **2001** | ILO Director-General Juan Somavia proposes consolidation |
| **February 2006** | MLC adopted unanimously at 94th ILO Session in Geneva |
| **August 2012** | Entry into force (after 30 ratifications representing 33% of world tonnage) |
| **2022** | Amendments entered into force (e.g., repatriation, connectivity) |

### 1.4 Physical & Operational Constants

- **Threshold of entry into force:** **30 ratifications representing at least 33% of world gross tonnage**
- **Minimum age for seafarers:** **16 years** (with restrictions for hazardous work)
- **Minimum contractual notice period:** Industry standard referenced in **Regulation 2.3** = minimum **30 days** for indefinite contracts
- **Annual leave entitlement:** Minimum **2.5 calendar days per month of service** = **30 days/year**
- **Maximum hours of work:** **14 hours/day** and **72 hours/week** (over 7-day period)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Tripartite Structure of MLC (THE MOST IMPORTANT FRAMEWORK)

The MLC is unique because of its **three-tier architecture**, which is a key high-yield topic for KTU exams:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture:** **ARTICLES** → **REGULATIONS** → **CODE (Part A & Part B)**

**Tier 1 — ARTICLES (16 Articles):**
- Set out the **general obligations and principles** of ratifying Member States.
- Define scope, application, entry into force, and amendment procedures.
- Are binding on the Member State (country), not directly on the ship.

**Tier 2 — REGULATIONS (5 Regulations):**
- Set out the **core rights and principles** at a broad level.
- Are organized into **5 Titles** corresponding to major subject areas.
- Each Regulation has an associated **Code**.

**Tier 3 — THE CODE:**
- **Part A — Mandatory Standards** (binding detailed implementation)
- **Part B — Non-Mandatory Guidelines** (practical guidance for implementation)

> This structure is similar to how a country's constitution (Articles), Acts of Parliament (Regulations), and detailed Schedules/Rules (Code) work together.

### 2.2 The Five Titles of MLC

This is the **heart of the MLC** — every examiner question will revolve around these:

| Title | Title Name | Key Concern | Memory Keyword |
|-------|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Title 1** | Minimum Requirements for Seafarers to Work on a Ship | Age, medical fitness, training, recruitment | **"WHO can work"** |
| **Title 2** | Conditions of Employment | Contracts, wages, hours, leave, repatriation | **"HOW they work"** |
| **Title 3** | Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food & Catering | Living conditions onboard | **"WHERE they LIVE"** |
| **Title 4** | Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare & Social Security Protection | Healthcare, onboard safety, social security | **"STAYING HEALTHY"** |
| **Title 5** | Compliance & Enforcement | Flag State, Port State, labour-supply State duties | **"POLICING the system"** |

### 2.3 The "No More Favourable Treatment" Clause (NMFT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept:** Regulation 5 — Vessels of non-ratifying states cannot be given **"more favourable treatment"** than MLC-compliant vessels. This means: when a port state inspects a foreign ship from a country that has NOT ratified MLC, that ship cannot be treated better than an MLC-compliant one. This prevents "flags of convenience" from gaining competitive advantages by avoiding MLC compliance.

### 2.4 Certification System — The Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC)

| Certificate Type | Issued By | Purpose |
|-----------------|-----------|---------|
| **Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC)** | Flag State | Certifies ship complies with MLC standards |
| **Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC)** | Shipowner + Flag State | Lists national requirements and how ship meets them (Part I: national; Part II: ship-specific measures) |
| **Inspection Certificate** | Recognised Organisation (RO) | Issued after initial inspection |

The MLC certificate is valid for a **maximum of 5 years**, with intermediate inspections at intervals not exceeding **3 years**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Numerical/Logical Reference Table

| Parameter | Standard | KTU Reference |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| Minimum age (general) | **16 years** | Reg. 1.1 |
| Minimum age (hazardous work) | **18 years** | Reg. 1.1 |
| Max hours of work (24h) | **14 hours** | Reg. 2.3 |
| Max hours of work (7 days) | **72 hours** | Reg. 2.3 |
| Min hours of rest (24h) | **10 hours** | Reg. 2.3 |
| Min hours of rest (7 days) | **77 hours** | Reg. 2.3 |
| Min annual leave | **2.5 days/month** = **30 days/year** | Reg. 2.4 |
| Notice period | **≥ 30 days** (if indefinite contract) | Reg. 2.3 |
| Onboard repatriation right | Always available (cost borne by shipowner) | Reg. 2.5 |
| MLC validity | **5 years** (max) | Title 5 |
| Intermediate inspection | **≤ 3 years** | Title 5 |
| Entry-into-force threshold | 30 ratifications / 33% tonnage | Article VIII |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** *"14 - 72 - 10 - 77"* — Max work hours: **14/day, 72/week**; Min rest hours: **10/day, 77/week**. Examiners love to test these numerical limits!

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Design Impact:** Engineers designing accommodation must comply with MLC Title 3 (headroom, ventilation, lighting, sanitary facilities, insulation).
- **Ship Operation:** Crew schedules are now legally constrained — impacting vessel manning levels and rotation planning.
- **Port State Control (PSC):** Ships can be detained for MLC violations, making it economically vital for shipowners.
- **Insurance & Chartering:** Charterers now include MLC compliance clauses in charter parties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Calculating Maximum Work Hours with Rest Periods

A common KTU numerical problem tests whether a watchkeeper schedule complies with MLC limits. Let's derive the methodology:

**Problem Framework:** A ship's engine-room rating has been scheduled as follows:
- Work period: 6 hours ON / 6 hours OFF
- Daily schedule: 06:00–12:00 work; 12:00–18:00 rest; 18:00–24:00 work; 00:00–06:00 rest
- Verify MLC compliance.

**Step 1 — Identify the 24-hour work total:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Work in 24 hours} &= (12:00 - 06:00) + (24:00 - 18:00) \\
&= 6 \text{ hours} + 6 \text{ hours} \\
&= 12 \text{ hours of work per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the 24-hour rest total:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rest in 24 hours} &= (18:00 - 12:00) + (06:00 - 00:00) \\
&= 6 \text{ hours} + 6 \text{ hours} \\
&= 12 \text{ hours of rest per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with MLC limits:**

| Parameter | MLC Limit | Calculated | Status |
|-----------|-----------|------------|--------|
| Max work in 24h | **≤ 14 hours** | 12 hours | ✓ COMPLIANT |
| Min rest in 24h | **≥ 10 hours** | 12 hours | ✓ COMPLIANT |
| Max work in 7 days | **≤ 72 hours** | 7 × 12 = 84 hours | ✗ NON-COMPLIANT |

**Step 4 — Determine weekly compliance assuming same pattern:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Work in 7 days} &= 7 \times 12 = 84 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Max allowed in 7 days} &= 72 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Excess hours} &= 84 - 72 = 12 \text{ hours over the limit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Although daily limits are met, the **weekly work limit (72h) is exceeded by 12 hours**, making the schedule **NON-COMPLIANT** with MLC Regulation 2.3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** MLC sets TWO simultaneous limits — daily AND weekly. A schedule must satisfy BOTH conditions to be compliant. This dual-constraint framework prevents shipowners from simply averaging out hours to evade daily rest provisions.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping MLC Titles to Real Shipboard Functions

| MLC Title | Shipboard System Affected | Engineer/Designer Responsibility |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Title 1 — Minimum Requirements | Manning plan, certification records | HR + Operations verify STCW + medical fitness |
| Title 2 — Conditions of Employment | Crew rotation schedule, payroll system | Operations + accounts ensure timely wages |
| Title 3 — Accommodation | HVAC, electrical, plumbing, structural design | Naval architects + Marine engineers |
| Title 4 — Health Protection | Medical locker, hospital space, hygiene | Chief Officer + Chief Engineer |
| Title 5 — Compliance | Documentation, certification, inspections | Master + DPA (Designated Person Ashore) |

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Calculating Minimum Annual Leave Entitlement

A seafarer joined a vessel on 1st January and was repatriated on 31st December (12 months of service). What is the minimum annual leave due under MLC?

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$
\text{Minimum Annual Leave} = \text{months of service} \times 2.5 \text{ days/month}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum Annual Leave} &= 12 \text{ months} \times 2.5 \text{ days/month} \\
&= 30 \text{ calendar days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify against MLC Regulation 2.4:**

The MLC standard minimum is **30 days/year** (or **2.5 days/month** for incomplete years). Since **30 ≥ 30**, the seafarer is entitled to **at least 30 calendar days of paid annual leave**, which may be taken onshore or under controlled conditions onboard.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing "working days" with "calendar days." MLC entitlements are in **calendar days**, NOT working days. A 30-day calendar leave means 30 actual days off, irrespective of how many would have been working days.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MLC Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[MLC 2006 Convention] --> nodeB[ARTICLES - 16 Articles]
    nodeA --> nodeC[REGULATIONS - 5 Regulations]
    nodeA --> nodeD[CODE]
    
    nodeD --> nodeE[Part A - Mandatory Standards]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Part B - Non Mandatory Guidelines]
    
    nodeC --> nodeG[Title 1: Minimum Requirements]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Title 2: Conditions of Employment]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Title 3: Accommodation]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Title 4: Health Protection]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Title 5: Compliance and Enforcement]
    
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Min Age 16]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[Medical Fitness]
    nodeG --> nodeG3[Training Certificates]
    
    nodeH --> nodeH1[Seafarer Contracts]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[Wages and Pay]
    nodeH --> nodeH3[Hours of Work and Rest]
    nodeH --> nodeH4[Repatriation]
    nodeH --> nodeH5[Annual Leave 30 days]
    
    nodeI --> nodeI1[Bunk Size and Headroom]
    nodeI --> nodeI2[Ventilation and Heating]
    nodeI --> nodeI3[Food Quality and Catering]
    
    nodeJ --> nodeJ1[Medical Care Onboard]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ2[Social Security]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ3[Onboard Welfare]
    
    nodeK --> nodeK1[Flag State Duties]
    nodeK --> nodeK2[Port State Control]
    nodeK --> nodeK3[Maritime Labour Certificate]
    nodeK --> nodeK4[No More Favourable Treatment]
```

### 4.2 Certification Workflow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Shipowner prepares DMLC Part II] --> nodeS2[Flag State Review]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Recognised Organisation RO Inspection]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4{Compliant?}
    nodeS4 --> nodeY[Yes] --> nodeS5[Issue MLC Certificate]
    nodeS4 --> nodeN[No] --> nodeS6[Rectification Period]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS3
    nodeS5 --> nodeS7[Validity 5 Years Max]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[Intermediate Inspection at 3 Years]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9{Renewal Required?}
    nodeS9 --> nodeY2[Yes] --> nodeS3
    nodeS9 --> nodeN2[No] --> nodeS10[Continue Operating]
```

### 4.3 Compliance Triangle — Three Pillars of Enforcement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeFS[FLAG STATE - Country of Ship Registration] --> nodeE[MLC ENFORCEMENT]
    nodePS[PORT STATE - Country of Port Visited] --> nodeE
    nodeLS[LABOUR SUPPLY STATE - Crew Source Country] --> nodeE
    
    nodeFS --> nodeF1[Issues Maritime Labour Certificate]
    nodeFS --> nodeF2[Conducts Inspections]
    nodeFS --> nodeF3[Maintains DMLC Records]
    
    nodePS --> nodeP1[Port State Control Inspections]
    nodePS --> nodeP2[Can Detain Non-Compliant Ships]
    nodePS --> nodeP3[Applies No More Favourable Treatment]
    
    nodeLS --> nodeL1[Regulates Recruitment Agencies]
    nodeLS --> nodeL2[Protects Migrant Seafarers]
    nodeLS --> nodeL3[Certifies Crew Training]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*What is the Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006? Why is it called the "Fourth Pillar" of international maritime regulation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006 is a comprehensive ILO convention adopted on 23rd February 2006 that establishes minimum working and living standards for seafarers worldwide. It consolidates over 68 older ILO maritime conventions and recommendations into one single instrument.
