<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Maritime Safety** and list the three pillars on which the modern maritime safety framework is built.

**Model Answer:**

Maritime Safety is the integrated body of engineering design, operational practice, and regulatory compliance aimed at preserving life, protecting property, and preventing pollution in the marine environment. It is primarily governed by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** conventions, especially **SOLAS 1974 (as amended)**.

The three pillars are:

1. **Prevention** — proactive engineering: sound design, subdivision, stability, risk assessment.
2. **Protection** — mitigation systems: fire detection, fire-fighting systems, PPE, watertight integrity.
3. **Response** — emergency action: muster, abandon-ship, GMDSS communication, rescue.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only "SOLAS" without mentioning the **three pillars** — this loses 1 mark. Always mention **Prevention, Protection, Response**.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is the **SOLAS subdivision factor $F$** and what is its physical significance?

**Model Answer:**

The subdivision factor $F$ is a dimensionless number defined as:

$$F = \frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} p_i}$$

where $p_i$ is the probability of flooding of compartment $i$ and $s_i$ is the probability of survival after flooding.

**Physical significance:**
- $F$ measures the **attained probability of survival** of a ship after hull damage.
- It must satisfy $R \leq F$, where $R$ is the **required subdivision index** (a function of ship length).
- A higher $F$ means the ship is safer — the watertight subdivision is more effective.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **SOLAS concept of subdivision and damage stability** for cargo ships. Derive the subdivision factor formula with a clearly labelled illustration of damage cases. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following damage data. Determine whether it satisfies SOLAS damage-stability requirement. Length $L_s = 150\ m$. Use the formula $R = 0.0023 L_s^2 - 0.2205 L_s + 18.85$. **[7 Marks]**

| Compartment | $p_i$ | $s_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Forepeak | 0.05 | 1.00 |
| Hold 1 | 0.20 | 0.90 |
| Hold 2 | 0.20 | 0.85 |
| Engine Room | 0.15 | 0.60 |
| Hold 3 | 0.20 | 0.80 |
| Hold 4 | 0.15 | 0.75 |
| Aft Peak | 0.05 | 1.00 |

#### Model Solution for (a):

SOLAS Chapter II-1, Part B-1 mandates the subdivision of ships to ensure **residual stability** after hull damage. The principle is **progressive flooding control** — limiting flooding extent via watertight bulkheads so that the ship remains afloat and stable.

The **Subdivision Factor** $F$ is the ratio of the sum of weighted survival probabilities to the sum of damage probabilities:

$$F = \frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} p_i s_i}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} p_i}$$

The **Required Subdivision Index $R$** depends only on ship length $L_s$:
- For $131 \leq L_s < 200$ m: $R = 0.0023 L_s^2 - 0.2205 L_s + 18.85$
- For $200 \leq L_s \leq 300$ m: $R = 1 - \frac{128}{L_s + 152}$

Compliance rule: **$R \leq F$**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Sum of $p_i$:**
$$\sum p_i = 0.05 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.05 = 1.00$$

**Step 2 — Compute each $p_i s_i$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 s_1 &= 0.05 \times 1.00 = 0.0500 \\
p_2 s_2 &= 0.20 \times 0.90 = 0.1800 \\
p_3 s_3 &= 0.20 \times 0.85 = 0.1700 \\
p_4 s_4 &= 0.15 \times 0.60 = 0.0900 \\
p_5 s_5 &= 0.20 \times 0.80 = 0.1600 \\
p_6 s_6 &= 0.15 \times 0.75 = 0.1125 \\
p_7 s_7 &= 0.05 \times 1.00 = 0.0500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of $p_i s_i$:**
$$\sum p_i s_i = 0.0500 + 0.1800 + 0.1700 + 0.0900 + 0.1600 + 0.1125 + 0.0500 = 0.8125$$

**Step 4 — Compute $F$:**
$$F = \frac{0.8125}{1.00} = 0.8125$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.0023(150)^2 - 0.2205(150) + 18.85 \\
&= 0.0023(22500) - 33.075 + 18.85 \\
&= 51.75 - 33.075 + 18.85 \\
&= 37.525
\end{aligned}
$$

Normalized: $R_{norm} = 0.37525$

**Step 6 — Compare:**
Since $F = 0.8125 > R_{norm} = 0.37525$, the bulk carrier **satisfies** SOLAS damage-stability requirements with a large safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently compute $R$ but forget to **normalize** it (divide by 100) before comparing with $F$. This loses 1–2 marks. Always state: *"Final R normalized = R/100"*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **classification of fires** onboard ships as per SOLAS / ISGOTT and explain the **fire zones and structural fire protection** concept. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A passenger vessel carries $POB = 2400$ persons. Calculate the **minimum number of lifeboats** required if each lifeboat has SOLAS-certified capacity of 150 persons. State the relevant SOLAS regulation reference. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Fire Classification (SOLAS / ISGOTT):**

| Class | Fuel | Extinguishing Agent |
|---|---|---|
| A | Ordinary combustibles (wood, fabric) | Water, foam |
| B | Flammable liquids (oil, fuel) | Foam, CO₂, dry chemical |
| C | Flammable gases | Dry chemical, CO₂ |
| D | Combustible metals | Special dry powder |
| E / Electrical | Live equipment | CO₂ (non-conductive) |
| F | Cooking oils | Wet chemical |

**Structural Fire Protection (SOLAS Chapter II-2, Reg. 9):**

Ships are divided into **fire zones** separated by **fire-rated divisions**:
- **A-class divisions**: Steel bulkheads/decks with insulation. Sub-classes A-60, A-30, A-15, A-0 (number = minutes of fire resistance).
- **B-class divisions**: Non-combustible bulkheads with insulation. Sub-classes B-15, B-0.
- **C-class divisions**: Non-combustible but no insulation requirement.

**Main vertical fire zones** limit spread — typically 40 m maximum length per zone.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Relevant SOLAS Regulation:** SOLAS Chapter III, Regulation 21 — *Survival craft and rescue boats*.

**Step 1 — SOLAS III/21.1.1** requires survival craft capacity for **at least 100% of POB**.

**Step 2 — Compute number of lifeboats:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{craft} &= \left\lceil \frac{POB}{C_{craft}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{2400}{150} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 16.0 \rceil \\
&= 16\ \text{lifeboats}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify:** $16 \times 150 = 2400 \geq 2400$ POB. ✓

**Result:** The vessel must carry a minimum of **16 lifeboats** with a total capacity of **2400 persons**, plus additional liferafts as backup (typically 25% of POB on each side per SOLAS III/31).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often ignore the **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ and answer 15 when POB is not divisible. Also, do not forget to mention the **specific SOLAS regulation number** (e.g., III/21) — examiners award 1 mark for this citation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

- **Maritime Safety** rests on three pillars: **Prevention, Protection, Response**.
- **SOLAS 1974** is the primary international convention; **MARPOL**, **STCW**, **MLC 2006** are supplementary.
- **Subdivision Factor** $F = \frac{\sum p_i s_i}{\sum p_i}$; must satisfy $F \geq R$ where $R$ depends on $L_s$.
- For $131 \leq L_s < 200$ m: $R = 0.0023 L_s^2 - 0.2205 L_s + 18.85$ (then divide by 100).
- **Fire Classes**: A, B, C, D, E, F — know the extinguishing agents.
- **Structural Fire Protection**: A-class (A-60 highest) > B-class (B-15) > C-class (no insulation).
- **GM** $= KM - KG$; positive GM indicates positive initial stability.
- **Survival craft capacity** must cover **≥ 100% POB** (SOLAS III/21).
- **Fire pump flow** for cargo ships: $Q \geq 180\ m^3/h$ at $P \geq 0.25\ N/mm^2$.
- **Main vertical fire zones**: maximum **40 m** in length.
- **GMDSS** — Global Maritime Distress and Safety System — handles emergency communication.
- Always cite the **SOLAS regulation number** in exam answers (e.g., III/21.1.1, II-1/27).
- Three mandatory items to mention in any "General Maritime Safety" answer: **SOLAS, three pillars, life-saving appliances**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** For 14-mark questions, allocate ~1 page for theory (definitions + diagrams) and ~1 page for calculation steps with proper aligned equations. Use **Mermaid diagrams** or hand-drawn sketches of ship GA (general arrangement) wherever applicable — visual answers consistently score 1–2 marks higher.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** International Shore Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ INTERNATIONAL SHORE CONNECTION — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

An **International Shore Connection (ISC)** is a permanently fitted, universally standardized pipe coupling device on board every merchant ship of **500 Gross Tonnage (GT) and above**, designed in accordance with the **SOLAS Convention (Chapter II-2, Regulation 19)** of the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, to facilitate the cross-border transfer of fire-fighting water between a ship and an external source (shore fire-main, another ship, or a tug/supply vessel) during a fire emergency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OESFT727 — Module 2):**
> The International Shore Connection is governed by the **Fire Safety Systems Code (FSS Code)** Chapter 8 and **SOLAS Reg. II-2/19.3.2**. The device acts as a *universal adapter*, eliminating the incompatibility between fire-fighting systems of vessels from different nationalities when mutual aid is required.

## 1.2 Standardized Dimensions (As per SOLAS Reg. II-2/19.3.2.2)

The flange must comply **exactly** with the following universal dimensions, irrespective of the ship's flag state:

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Outer Diameter (OD) | **215 mm** |
| Inner Diameter (ID) | **110 mm** |
| Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD) | **170 mm** |
| Number of Bolt Holes | **4 (equally spaced at 90°)** |
| Bolt Hole Diameter | **19 mm** |
| Slot / Bolt Hole Shape | **Hole (not slot), 19 mm dia.** |
| Flange Thickness | Typically **25 mm – 35 mm** |
| Material | **Steel or equivalent marine-grade metal** |
| Working Pressure | Suitable for the ship's fire main (**min. 0.3 N/mm² = 3 bar**) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Power Socket" of the Sea

Imagine you are travelling across Europe with a laptop. Every country used to have different plug shapes (British 3-pin, Schuko, French, etc.). To solve this, the world adopted a *universal travel adapter* — a single standardized device that bridges any socket to your plug.

The **International Shore Connection is the maritime equivalent of this universal adapter for fire-fighting water**.

- A Greek bulk carrier docked at a Singapore port suffers an engine-room fire.
- The local shore fire brigade arrives with their hydrant hoses.
- Without the ISC, the shore hoses (say, 2.5″ instantaneous coupling) would **NOT** physically fit the ship's fire main.
- With the ISC, the ship's crew simply bolts the ISC flange to a matching flange on the shore-supplied hose, and water from the shore (or another vessel's pump) flows directly into the ship's fire main.

> [!NOTE]
> **One ship, one world, one standard flange — that is the essence of the ISC.**

## 1.4 Purpose & Operational Significance

1. **Mutual Aid in Port** — Enables shore fire services to feed water into a ship's fire main.
2. **Ship-to-Ship Fire Fighting (SSFF)** — Allows one vessel to pump water into another vessel on fire.
3. **Overcomes Coupling Incompatibility** — Bridges the gap between national fire-fighting standards (Instantaneous, Storz, BIC, etc.).
4. **Mandatory under SOLAS** — Required for all passenger ships and cargo ships ≥ 500 GT engaged in international voyages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Standard Flange? — The Engineering Rationale

The international maritime fleet comprises vessels from ~170+ maritime nations, each with domestic fire-fighting standards. In a port emergency, **time is the scarcest resource** — a ship fire doubles in intensity every 30 seconds. Therefore, IMO mandated a single, rigid, mechanically simple bolted flange that:

- Can be tightened with **standard wrenches** (no special tools).
- Is **self-aligning** (4-hole symmetric PCD).
- Provides a **leak-proof metal-to-metal seal** with a soft gasket.
- Has **no threaded mismatch** issues (bolted flanges, not threaded).

## 2.2 Component Anatomy of an ISC Assembly

A complete shipboard ISC outfit includes:

1. **Flanged Connection Piece** — The standardized 215/110/170 mm steel flange.
2. **Gasket** — Soft material (rubber/Klingerite) for water-tight sealing.
3. **Bolts, Nuts & Washers (4 sets)** — To clamp the joint.
4. **Adaptor Piece** — A short pipe with a national coupling (e.g., 2.5″ instantaneous male) on the other end, to connect the ISC flange to the shore hose.
5. **Hose or Pipe Spanner** — For tightening.
6. **Stowage Box** — Permanently marked, painted red, located near the ship's manifold / accommodation.

## 2.3 SOLAS Compliance — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

| Step | Regulatory Requirement | Practical Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | One ISC to be provided (Reg. II-2/19.3.2.1) | At least one physical ISC on board |
| 2 | Flange dimensions as per IMO Res. **A.170 (II)** | 215/110/170 mm, 4 × 19 mm holes |
| 3 | Accompanied by a gasket + 4 bolts + nuts | Tight joint ready for use |
| 4 | Stowed in a readily accessible position | Near fire station or deck manifold |
| 5 | Tested together with fire main | Hydrostatic test of the assembly |
| 6 | Permanent marking **"FOR INTERNATIONAL SHORE CONNECTION"** | Bilingual (English + national) |
| 7 | Inspected during **annual / renewal / dry-dock surveys** | By Class (IRS, DNV, LR, ABS, etc.) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Hydraulic Flow Through the ISC

> [!NOTE]
> Although an ISC is a passive fitting, the **fire-water flow** through it follows standard hydraulic equations. KTU examiners love testing this connection.

**Table 2.1 — High-Yield Formulas for ISC & Fire-Main Hydraulics**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, D^{2}$ | Cross-sectional area of ISC bore ($D = 0.110$ m) | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, (0.110)^{2} \approx 9.503 \times 10^{-3}$ | ISC bore area | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 4 | $H_{f} = \dfrac{4 f L v^{2}}{2 g D}$ | Darcy–Weisbach head loss across the ISC length $L$ | m |
| 5 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (laminar if $\text{Re} < 2300$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ | Friction factor (laminar regime) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f = \dfrac{0.0791}{\text{Re}^{0.25}}$ | Blasius equation (turbulent, smooth pipe) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta P = \rho \, g \, H_{f}$ | Pressure drop across ISC | $\text{N}/\text{m}^{2}$ (Pa) |
| 9 | $P_{\text{ship}} + \rho g h = P_{\text{shore}} + H_{f}$ | Pressure continuity across the ISC joint (Bernoulli-extended) | Pa |
| 10 | $v = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | Velocity in terms of flow rate (useful for inverse problems) | m/s |

> ⚠️ **LaTeX Safety Note:** Absolute values in any inline expression must use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the pipe `|` — to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Port Fire Brigades (Mumbai, Rotterdam, Singapore)** keep ISC-compatible adapters ready.
- **Ship-to-Ship (STS) transfers** during emergencies at anchorages use this coupling.
- **Salvage Operations (e.g., Smit Tak, Ardent)** rely on ISC to feed high-pressure seawater into a casualty's fire main.
- **Offshore Drilling Rigs** (MODUs) also carry ISCs under MODU Code.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cross-Sectional Bore Area of the ISC

The standard inner diameter of an ISC is **110 mm = 0.110 m**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{ISC}} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \, D^{2} \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \, (0.110)^{2} \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.01210 \\
&= 0.7854 \times 0.01210 \\
A_{\text{ISC}} &= 9.503 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^{2} \;\;\approx 9503 \;\text{mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Step 1: $D$ in SI metres ($0.110$).
- Step 2: Square $D$.
- Step 3: Multiply by $\pi/4 = 0.7854$.
- Final bore area $\approx 9.503 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^{2}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Theoretical Flow Through the ISC

A typical shore-supplied fire-fighting pump delivers water at **10 bar = $1.0 \times 10^{6} \; \text{N}/\text{m}^{2}$**. Assuming this pressure drives water through the ISC (with negligible losses for a maximum-flow estimate), apply Bernoulli's equation between the shore hydrant and the ship's manifold.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{shore}} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_{\text{shore}}^{2} + \rho g z_{\text{shore}} &= P_{\text{ship}} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_{\text{ship}}^{2} + \rho g z_{\text{ship}} + \Delta P_{\text{loss}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Simplified (assuming $v_{\text{shore}} \ll v_{\text{ship}}$, $\Delta P_{\text{loss}} \approx 0$, $z_{\text{shore}} = z_{\text{ship}}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{shore}} - P_{\text{ship}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_{\text{ship}}^{2} \\
v_{\text{ship}} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \, (P_{\text{shore}} - P_{\text{ship}})}{\rho}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.0 \times 10^{6} - 0)}{1000}} \\
&= \sqrt{2000} \\
v_{\text{ship}} &\approx 44.72 \;\; \text{m/s} \;\; \text{(theoretical, friction-less)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Take $\rho_{\text{sea water}} = 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$.
- The pressure difference is $1 \times 10^{6} \;\text{Pa}$.
- Substituting gives $v \approx 44.72 \;\text{m/s}$ (a frictionless upper bound).

**Realistic flow rate (with a typical ISC friction loss of $\sim 0.5$ bar and a back-pressure of 0.3 bar at the ship's hydrant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{net}} &= 1.0 - 0.3 - 0.5 = 0.2 \;\text{bar} = 2.0 \times 10^{4} \;\text{Pa} \\
v_{\text{realistic}} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2.0 \times 10^{4}}{1000}} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32 \;\text{m/s} \\
Q &= A_{\text{ISC}} \times v_{\text{realistic}} \\
&= (9.503 \times 10^{-3}) \times 6.32 \\
Q &\approx 6.007 \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \\
Q &\approx 60.07 \;\text{L/s} \;\;\approx 3604 \;\text{L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A properly pressurised shore supply through the ISC can deliver **~ 3.6 m³/min** of fire-fighting water into the ship's fire main — sufficient to power **2–3 fire hoses simultaneously**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reynolds Number Check (Flow Regime)

For $v = 6.32 \;\text{m/s}$, $D = 0.110 \;\text{m}$, kinematic viscosity of seawater $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Re} &= \frac{v \, D}{\nu} \\
&= \frac{6.32 \times 0.110}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{0.6952}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \\
\text{Re} &= 6.952 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\text{Re} \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent** — friction factor must be obtained from the **Blasius equation** or the **Colebrook-White equation** (not the laminar $f = 64/\text{Re}$ formula).

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{Blasius}} &= \frac{0.0791}{\text{Re}^{0.25}} \\
&= \frac{0.0791}{(6.952 \times 10^{5})^{0.25}} \\
&= \frac{0.0791}{(6.952)^{0.25} \times 10^{1.25}} \\
&= \frac{0.0791}{1.622 \times 17.78} \\
f_{\text{Blasius}} &\approx 2.74 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes & Mitigations

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Mitigation in KTU Exam Context |
|---|---|---|
| Leakage at flange joint | Worn gasket, uneven bolt torque | Replace gasket; torque bolts in **star pattern** to 60 Nm |
| Misalignment with shore flange | Damaged flange face | Re-machine or renew flange |
| Internal corrosion | Seawater ingress, poor drainage | Annual internal inspection + dry stowage |
| Bolts missing in stowage | Poor inventory control | ISM-Code periodic checks |
| Coupling incompatibility | Wrong national adaptor | Carry multiple adaptors (2.5″, Storz, BIC) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of an ISC Fire-Fighting Water Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shore Fire Brigade Pump] -->|Pressurised seawater| B[Shore Hose 2.5 inch Instantaneous Coupling]
    B --> C[Adaptor Piece National to ISC Flange]
    C --> D[International Shore Connection Flange 215 / 110 / 170 mm]
    D --> E[Shipboard Pipe Section]
    E --> F[Ship Fire Main Manifold]
    F --> G[Fire Hydrant Station 1]
    F --> H[Fire Hydrant Station 2]
    F --> I[Fixed Fire Extinguishing System e.g. Water Mist]
    J[Class Surveyor Annual Inspection] -.->|Compliance check| D
    K[Crew Weekly Check] -.->|Visual + stowage| D
```

**Node Identifier Alpha Rule Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`...) and **no reserved keyword** (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) is used as a node name.

**Label Formatting:** All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text. **No bold, italics, or HTML tables** inside the quoted labels.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — SOLAS Compliance Workflow for ISC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[SOLAS Reg II-2 / 19.3.2.1 triggered] --> S2[Design Standard 215 mm OD, 110 mm ID, 170 mm PCD]
    S2 --> S3[Fabricate or Procure ISC with Gasket and 4 Bolts]
    S3 --> S4[Permanent Marking FOR INTERNATIONAL SHORE CONNECTION]
    S4 --> S5[Stowage in Red Box near Fire Station]
    S5 --> S6[Hydrostatic Test with Fire Main]
    S6 --> S7{Class Survey Acceptance}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Operational Status OK]
    S7 -->|No| S9[Rework and Re-test]
    S9 --> S6
    S8 --> S10[Annual + Renewal Survey Continues]
```

## 4.3 Standard ISC Flange — Dimensional Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PCD["Pitch Circle Diameter 170 mm — 4 bolt holes equally spaced"]
    OD["Outer Diameter 215 mm"]
    ID["Inner Diameter 110 mm — water flow path"]
    BH["4 x Bolt Holes 19 mm diameter at 0, 90, 180, 270 degrees"]
    TH["Flange Thickness 25 to 35 mm"]
    MAT["Material Steel or equivalent marine grade"]
    SEAL["Gasket rubber Klingerite 110 mm ID"]
    PCD --> OD
    PCD --> ID
    PCD --> BH
    PCD --> TH
    PCD --> MAT
    PCD --> SEAL
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** representation of the ISC flange as mandated by SOLAS. Mermaid is being used here to convey the dimensional hierarchy; a physical engineering drawing with sectioned views would normally be supplied in the actual class notes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B, Q.2(a)]**
**Define an International Shore Connection (ISC). State the SOLAS regulation that governs it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An International Shore Connection is a **standardized bolted flange coupling** fitted on board every merchant ship of **500 GT and above**, used to connect the ship's fire main to an external source of fire-fighting water (shore hydrant or another ship). It is governed by **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 19.3.2** and conforms to the dimensions prescribed in **IMO Resolution A.170 (II)**. The standard dimensions are **OD 215 mm, ID 110 mm, PCD 170 mm, with 4 bolt holes of 19 mm diameter** equally spaced at 90°.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
> - 1 mark — Definition
> - 1 mark — SOLAS regulation reference
> - 1 mark — One dimensional parameter

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary, Q.1(b)]**
**List any four components that must accompany an International Shore Connection on board.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any 4 of the following):**
1. **Gasket** of soft material (rubber / Klingerite).
2. **Four sets of bolts, nuts and washers** of 19 mm diameter.
3. **Adaptor piece** with national coupling (e.g., 2.5″ instantaneous male).
4. **Pipe spanner / wrench** for tightening.
5. **Stowage box** painted red, permanently marked "FOR INTERNATIONAL SHORE CONNECTION".

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often quote **wrong dimensions** (e.g., 220/100/180 mm). **The only correct SOLAS dimensions are 215/110/170 mm.**
> 2. Many forget to mention that **one ISC is mandatory for ships ≥ 500 GT** engaged in international voyages.
> 3. Failing to state **SOLAS Reg. II-2/19.3.2** and **IMO Res. A.170 (II)** together leads to loss of 1 mark.
> 4. In numericals, students use $\rho = 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$ for sea water — **this is correct** (often confused with 1025).
> 5. In sketches, students draw a **threaded coupling** instead of a **bolted flange** — the ISC is *NOT* threaded.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### **(a)** [7 marks] — **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2, Q.4(a)]**
**With the help of a neat sketch, describe the design and dimensional standard of an International Shore Connection as per SOLAS. List the items that must be kept with it on board a merchant ship.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Design (3 marks):**
- Bolted flange type (not threaded).
- Made of steel or equivalent marine-grade material.
- Self-aligning, 4-hole symmetric pattern.

**2. Dimensions (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Outer Diameter | 215 mm |
| Inner Diameter | 110 mm |
| Pitch Circle Diameter | 170 mm |
| Bolt Holes | 4 × 19 mm dia. |

**3. Items kept with the ISC (1 mark):**
- Gasket, 4 bolts/nuts/washers, adaptor, spanner, stowage box.

---

#### **(b)** [7 marks] — **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2, Q.4(b)]**
**An international shore connection of 110 mm bore is being used to deliver water at a velocity of 5 m/s. Calculate (i) the volumetric flow rate in L/s, and (ii) verify the flow regime by computing the Reynolds number. Given: kinematic viscosity of seawater $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$.**

**Model Solution (step-by-step):**

**(i) Volumetric Flow Rate (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 110 \;\text{mm} = 0.110 \;\text{m} \\
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.110)^{2} = 9.503 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2} \\
Q &= A \times v \\
&= 9.503 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \\
&= 4.7515 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \\
Q &= 47.515 \;\text{L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Reynolds Number & Flow Regime (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Re} &= \frac{v \, D}{\nu} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 0.110}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{0.550}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} \\
\text{Re} &= 5.238 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\text{Re} \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

#### **(a)** [7 marks] — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, Module 2, Q.5(a)]**
**Explain the operational procedure for using an International Shore Connection during a shipboard fire emergency in port. Mention at least three safety checks to be performed before the connection is made live.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Operational Procedure (4 marks):**
- **Step 1** — Master / Chief Officer authorizes the operation and informs the port authority.
- **Step 2** — Fire team retrieves the ISC stowage box and brings it to the manifold station.
- **Step 3** — Bolts, gasket and adaptor are inspected; the flange face is cleaned.
- **Step 4** — The ISC flange is bolted (star pattern) to the shore-supplied flange with the gasket in place.
- **Step 5** — Air is bled from the joint; the shore pump is started gradually.
- **Step 6** — Pressure is raised to the design level of the ship's fire main and hydrants are opened as required.

**2. Safety Checks (3 marks — any 3):**
- Gasket is in good condition (no cuts, no hardening).
- Bolts, nuts and washers are complete and of correct size (19 mm).
- Shore-side pressure does not exceed the ship's fire main design pressure.
- Fire main isolation valves are correctly aligned.
- Communication is established with the shore fire brigade.

---

#### **(b)** [7 marks] — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, Module 2, Q.5(b)]**
**During a fire-fighting operation, sea water is supplied through an ISC at a net pressure differential of 0.4 bar. If the bore diameter is 110 mm, determine the velocity of water and the corresponding flow rate. State any two assumptions made. Given: $\rho = 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Steady, incompressible, frictionless flow through the ISC.
2. No elevation difference between shore and ship manifolds.

**Velocity (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 0.4 \;\text{bar} = 0.4 \times 10^{5} \;\text{Pa} = 4.0 \times 10^{4} \;\text{Pa} \\
v &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{4}}{1000}} \\
&= \sqrt{80} \\
v &\approx 8.944 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Flow Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.110)^{2} = 9.503 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2} \\
Q &= A \times v = 9.503 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.944 \\
Q &= 8.500 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 85.0 \;\text{L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> High-density revision summary — go through this **30 minutes before** the KTU exam.

- **ISC stands for International Shore Connection**, a SOLAS-mandated **bolted flange** device.
- Governing regulation: **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 19.3.2** + **IMO Resolution A.170 (II)**.
- Applicable to: **All cargo ships ≥ 500 GT and all passenger ships** on international voyages.
- **Memorize the dimensions verbatim:** **OD 215 mm, ID 110 mm, PCD 170 mm, 4 × 19 mm bolt holes**.
- The ISC is **not threaded** — it is a **bolted flange joint** with a soft gasket.
- The complete outfit must include: **flange + gasket + 4 bolts/nuts/washers + adaptor + spanner + stowage box**.
- Stowage box must be **red**, permanently marked **"FOR INTERNATIONAL SHORE CONNECTION"**, near the fire station.
- Material: **Steel or equivalent marine-grade metal**.
- Pressure rating: Suitable for the ship's fire main (typically **min. 0.3 N/mm² = 3 bar**).
- **Hydraulic shortcut for exams:** $A = \pi/4 \cdot D^{2} = 9.503 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2}$ for $D = 0.110 \;\text{m}$.
- **Bernoulli for ISC velocity:** $v = \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P / \rho}$ (frictionless assumption).
- **Realistic flow** through a well-pressurised ISC: **~ 50–85 L/s**.
- Reynolds number is **always > 4000** → **turbulent flow regime** → use **Blasius** or **Colebrook** for friction factor.
- **Class surveyor** inspects ISC during **annual, renewal and dry-dock surveys**.
- ISC enables **Mutual Aid (shore↔ship)** and **Ship-to-Ship Fire Fighting (SSFF)**.
- **Common pitfall:** Students write 220/100/180 mm — that is **WRONG**; only 215/110/170 mm is accepted.
- **Common pitfall:** ISC is a flange, **not** a thread, **not** a quick-coupling, **not** a Storz fitting.
- **Common pitfall:** One ISC is *minimum* — the regulation states "at least one".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Personnel Protection
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ PERSONNEL PROTECTION IN MARITIME OPERATIONS

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Personnel Protection

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personnel Protection** in maritime engineering refers to the integrated system of policies, equipment, training, and engineering controls designed to safeguard the **physical, chemical, biological, thermal, and radiological well-being of shipboard personnel** against occupational hazards inherent to marine operations, as mandated under **SOLAS Chapter III**, **ILO Maritime Labour Convention (MLC 2006)**, **ISM Code**, and **Flag State / Port State Control regulations**.

In the context of maritime safety engineering, personnel protection encompasses:
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — equipment worn to minimize exposure to specific occupational hazards.
- **Engineering Controls** — design features that isolate personnel from hazards (e.g., enclosed bridges, ventilation systems).
- **Administrative Controls** — procedural safeguards (permits-to-work, rotation of duties, drills).
- **Medical & Survival Provisions** — first aid, hypothermia prevention, occupational health monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Order of Controls (NIOSH / IMO Hierarchy):**
> **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE**
> PPE is the **last line of defense**, not the first.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Maritime Knight's Armor"

Imagine a seafarer as a **medieval knight** entering a hostile battlefield. The knight does not rely on a single piece of armor — instead, the knight wears a complete **integrated suit**: helmet (head protection), chainmail (body protection), gauntlets (hand protection), and boots (foot protection). Each piece addresses a *specific* threat.

In the same way, a modern seafarer on a chemical tanker, an oil rig supply vessel, or a fishing trawler must be equipped with a **tailored ensemble of PPE** matched precisely to the operational hazards:

| Maritime Hazard | "Knight's Armor" Equivalent | PPE Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Falling objects (cargo) | Helmet | Hard hat (ANSI Z89.1 / EN 397) |
| Chemical splash (oil, acids) | Chainmail | Chemical-resistant coveralls |
| Slippery decks | Boots | Non-slip safety footwear (EN ISO 20345) |
| Loud engine room noise | — | Ear defenders (EN 352) |
| Smoke / toxic gas | — | SCBA / EEBD (SOLAS Ch. II-2) |
| Cold ocean water | — | Immersion suit (SOLAS LSA Code) |

> **Key Standard Body:** **IMO** sets regulatory frameworks; **ISO**, **EN**, and **SOLAS LSA Code** provide technical specifications; **Classification Societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) verify compliance.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Maritime Personnel Protection Metrics:**
> - **Survival immersion water temperature threshold: ≤ 15 °C** (mandatory immersion suit provision)
> - **Audible alarm threshold: 85 dB(A)** (8-hour exposure — PPE mandatory above this)
> - **Glove cut resistance rating: EN 388 Level 5** (highest, for mooring operations)
> - **Survival craft air temperature after launch: ≥ 0 °C** for **24 hours minimum**
> - **EEBD (Emergency Escape Breathing Device) duration: ≥ 10 minutes** of usable air
> - **SCBA cylinder pressure: 200–300 bar** (15–30 minutes duration)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

## 2.1 Classification of Maritime PPE

Maritime PPE is systematically classified based on the **body region protected** and the **nature of hazard mitigated**.

### A. Head Protection
- **Hard Hats (Safety Helmets)** — Type I (top impact) and Type II (top + lateral)
  - Material: HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene), ABS, or fiberglass
  - Standards: **EN 397**, **ANSI Z89.1**, **IS 2925**
  - Marine variant includes **chin straps** for rough seas and **electrical insulation up to 440 V**

### B. Eye and Face Protection
- **Safety Goggles** — chemical splash, dust protection
- **Face Shields** — radiant heat, molten metal splash (foundry work)
- **Welding Helmets** — auto-darkening filter (ADF) with shade 9–13
- Standard: **EN 166** (optical class, mechanical strength)

### C. Hearing Protection
- **Earplugs** (NRR 25–33 dB)
- **Earmuffs** (SNR 25–35 dB)
- Standard: **EN 352**
- Threshold for mandatory provision: **85 dB(A) LEX,8h** per IMO / ILO

### D. Respiratory Protection
- **Dust Masks (FFP1, FFP2, FFP3)** — particulate filtration
- **Half/Full Face Respirators with cartridges** — gas/vapor (e.g., benzene, H₂S)
- **Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA)** — oxygen-deficient or IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) atmospheres
- **Emergency Escape Breathing Device (EEBD)** — minimum **10 minutes** air supply
  - Standard: **SOLAS Reg. II-2/13.3.4**, **ISO 23269**

### E. Hand Protection (Maritime Critical)
| Glove Type | Hazard | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Cotton/Leather | General handling | EN 388 |
| Cut-resistant (Kevlar) | Mooring ropes, wires | EN 388 Level 5 |
| Chemical-resistant (Nitrile) | Oil, fuel, chemicals | EN 374 |
| Cryogenic | LNG carrier operations | EN 511 |
| Electrically insulating | Electrical work | EN 60903 (Class 0–4) |

### F. Foot Protection
- **Steel-toe boots** (impact resistance 200 J, compression 15 kN) — **EN ISO 20345 S3**
- **Chemical-resistant rubber boots** — tankers, chemical carriers
- **Non-slip sole** — mandatory on all deck ratings

### G. Body Protection
- **Lifejackets (SOLAS LSA Code Ch. II)** — ≥ 150 N buoyancy
- **Immersion Suits** — survival in ≤ 15 °C water for ≥ 1 hour, donnable in ≤ 2 minutes
- **Anti-exposure (Constant Wear) Suits** — helicopter crews, fast rescue craft
- **Fire-fighter's Outfit** — proximity suit with breathing apparatus (SOLAS Reg. II-2/10.10)
- **Chemical Protective Coveralls (Type 3/4/5/6)** — per **EN 14605**, **EN ISO 13982**

### H. Fall Protection
- **Safety Harnesses** — full-body, EN 361
- **Lifelines and Lanyards** — EN 354, EN 355 (with energy absorber)
- **Fall Arresters** — retractable type
- **Safety Nets** — under overhead work areas

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind PPE Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the Hierarchy (Control Measures):**
> 1. **Elimination** — Remove the hazard entirely (e.g., switch to non-toxic cargo).
> 2. **Substitution** — Use a safer alternative (e.g., water-based paint instead of solvent-based).
> 3. **Engineering Controls** — Isolate people from the hazard (e.g., enclosed cargo control rooms).
> 4. **Administrative Controls** — Change work procedures (e.g., permit-to-work systems, training).
> 5. **PPE** — Protect the worker with personal equipment (last resort).

The deeper logic is **effectiveness and reliability**. PPE relies on **continuous human compliance**, while engineering controls operate **automatically and continuously**. KTU examiners often test this hierarchy because it is the foundation of modern maritime safety culture.

---

## 2.3 SOLAS & Regulatory Framework for Personnel Protection

| Regulation | Scope | PPE Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **SOLAS Chapter II-2** | Fire protection | Fire-fighter's outfit, SCBA, EEBD on every ship |
| **SOLAS Chapter III** | Life-saving appliances | Lifejackets, immersion suits, life rafts |
| **MARPOL Annex VI** | Air pollution | Respiratory protection during cargo operations |
| **MLC 2006** | Crew welfare | Adequate PPE, occupational health surveillance |
| **ISM Code** | Safety Management | PPE issuance, maintenance, training records |
| **STCW** | Training | Crew familiarization with PPE use |
| **ISGOTT** (Int'l Safety Guide for Oil Tankers) | Tanker-specific | Chemical PPE, gas measurement, PPE zoning |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personnel Protection — Quick Reference for Examinations**

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Permissible Noise Exposure (8 h)** | $L_{EX,8h} = L_A + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)$ | dB(A) | Hearing PPE trigger |
| **Half-value Doubling Rule** | Every +3 dB(A) → halve exposure time | dB(A) | Hearing protection |
| **Lifeline Anchorage Force** | $F_{anchor} = m \cdot g \cdot n$ (n = 2 safety factor) | N | Fall arrest design |
| **Impact Attenuation (Helmet)** | $\text{Force transmitted} \leq 5 \text{ kN}$ | kN | EN 397 test |
| **Respirator APF (Assigned Protection Factor)** | APF = $\frac{\text{Concentration outside}}{\text{Concentration inside mask}}$ | — | Selection of respirator |
| **Immersion Suit Buoyancy** | $F_b \geq 70 \text{ N}$ (with wearer) | N | SOLAS LSA Code |
| **EEBD Duration** | $\geq 10$ min | min | SOLAS II-2 |
| **Donning Time (Immersion Suit)** | $\leq 2$ min | min | SOLAS LSA Code |
| **Hard Hat Impact Energy** | $E = m g h = 5 \text{ kg} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1 \text{ m} = 49 \text{ J}$ | J | Penetration test |
| **Cut Resistance (Couptest)** | Index 1–5 | — | EN 388 |
| **Boots Toe Compression** | $\leq 15 \text{ kN}$ at $F = 200 \text{ kN}$ | kN | EN ISO 20345 |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Personnel protection principles are not confined to ships. They underpin safety design in:
- **Offshore oil & gas platforms** (drops, hydrocarbon fires)
- **Port crane operations** (fall arrest for riggers)
- **Ship-breaking yards** (heavy metal, asbestos)
- **Submarine escape systems** (Steinke hood — single-use escape device)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern "smart PPE" integrates IoT sensors (heart rate, gas exposure, fall detection) with the ship's Integrated Bridge System (IBS). This represents the convergence of personnel protection and **e-Navigation / Industry 4.0** — a likely topic in KTU 2024 advanced questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Permissible Noise Exposure Calculation

The IMO and ILO use the **equal-energy hypothesis** (3 dB exchange rate) to compute permissible exposure times at elevated noise levels.

### Step 1: Define the Standard Reference
A reference exposure of **85 dB(A)** for **8 hours** is the threshold.

### Step 2: Establish the Equal-Energy Relationship

$$L_{EX,8h} = L_A + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

Where:
- $L_A$ = measured sound level (dB(A))
- $T$ = actual exposure duration (hours)
- $T_0$ = 8 hours (reference)

### Step 3: Solve for Maximum Permissible Time $T_{max}$

Set $L_{EX,8h} = 85$ dB(A) and solve for $T$:

$$85 = L_A + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{T_{max}}{8}\right)$$

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{T_{max}}{8}\right) = \frac{85 - L_A}{10}$$

$$\frac{T_{max}}{8} = 10^{\left(\frac{85 - L_A}{10}\right)}$$

$$T_{max} = 8 \times 10^{\left(\frac{85 - L_A}{10}\right)} \text{ hours}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example
A seafarer works in an engine room with measured noise $L_A = 100$ dB(A). Calculate $T_{max}$:

$$T_{max} = 8 \times 10^{\left(\frac{85 - 100}{10}\right)} = 8 \times 10^{-1.5}$$

$$T_{max} = 8 \times 0.0316 = 0.253 \text{ hours} = 15.2 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** At 100 dB(A), the seafarer may be exposed for only **~15 minutes** without hearing protection. Above 85 dB(A), **hearing PPE is mandatory**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fall Arrest Energy and Lanyard Length

For a worker falling from height $h$ on a ship, the dynamic force on the anchorage must be limited to **F ≤ 6 kN** (per EN 363).

### Step 1: Free-Fall Velocity
At the end of an unprotected fall of height $h$:

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 2: Kinetic Energy at Fall Arrest
$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = mgh$$

### Step 3: Energy Absorbed by the Lanyard
If the lanyard stretches by distance $\Delta s$ under force $F$:

$$E_{absorbed} = \frac{1}{2} F \Delta s$$

### Step 4: Equate and Solve

$$mgh = \frac{1}{2} F \Delta s$$

$$F = \frac{2 m g h}{\Delta s}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example
- Mass $m = 100$ kg (seafarer + gear)
- Fall height $h = 4$ m (ship rail height)
- Lanyard stretch $\Delta s = 1.75$ m (typical energy absorber deflection)

$$F = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 9.81 \times 4}{1.75} = \frac{7848}{1.75}$$

$$F \approx 4485 \text{ N} = 4.49 \text{ kN}$$

Since $F = 4.49 \text{ kN} < 6 \text{ kN}$, the configuration is **safe per EN 363**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why energy-absorbing lanyards are mandatory in shipboard fall protection — a standard lanyard would transmit unacceptably high forces to the spine.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PPE Selection for Common Maritime Tasks

| Task | Primary Hazard | PPE Required | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mooring operations | Snap-back rope, crushing | Hard hat, gloves (cut-resistant), boots, high-visibility vest | EN 388, EN ISO 20345 |
| Cargo tank entry | Toxic gas, O₂ deficiency | SCBA, gas monitor, harness, rescue tripod | SOLAS II-2, EN 529 |
| Engine room maintenance | Hot surfaces, oil, noise | Heat-resistant gloves, safety goggles, ear defenders, apron | EN 407, EN 352 |
| Painting (enclosed space) | VOC vapors | Half-face respirator (organic vapor cartridge), goggles | EN 140 |
| Welding on deck | UV radiation, sparks | Welding helmet (Shade 11), leather gloves, fire-resistant clothing | EN 169, EN 407 |
| Lifeboat launching | Cold water, impact | Immersion suit, lifejacket, helmet | SOLAS LSA Code |
| Working aloft (mast, funnel) | Fall from height | Full-body harness, energy-absorbing lanyard, helmet | EN 361, EN 355 |
| Helicopter operation (offshore) | Ditching, impact | Aviation lifejacket, anti-exposure suit | CAP 437, ICAO Annex 6 |

---

## 3.4 PPE Failure Mode Analysis (FMEA)

A systematic failure mode analysis for lifejackets:

| Failure Mode | Cause | Effect | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bladder puncture | Sharp debris | Loss of buoyancy | Inspection before donning |
| CO₂ cylinder leak | Corrosion | Inadequate inflation | Annual servicing |
| Whistle missing | Lost | No rescue signaling | Pre-use checklist |
| Strap deterioration | UV, salt | Failure to secure | Replace every 5 yrs |

---

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

---

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and state the hierarchy of controls in occupational safety.**

**Model Answer:**
Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) refers to protective clothing, helmets, goggles, gloves, footwear, breathing apparatus, and other equipment worn by workers to shield them from occupational hazards. The hierarchy of controls, as mandated by NIOSH and adopted by maritime regulators, is:

**Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE**

PPE is the last line of defense because it depends on continuous human compliance. Higher-tier controls (elimination, engineering) are more reliable because they function automatically.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between SCBA and EEBD? State at least two distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SCBA | EEBD |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | 15–30 minutes | ≥ 10 minutes (SOLAS II-2) |
| Use | IDLH atmospheres, fire-fighting | Emergency escape only |
| Cylinder pressure | 200–300 bar | Lower pressure, single use |
| Air supply | Compressed breathing air | Compressed oxygen or air |
| Reusability | Refillable | Single-use (disposable) |

SCBA is used for **entry and fire-fighting**, while EEBD is used only for **escape** from a smoke-filled compartment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice 1]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the various types of Personal Protective Equipment used on board ships. State the relevant international standards for each category.**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Head Protection — Hard Hats (2 marks)**
- Provide protection from impact and penetration by falling objects.
- Materials: HDPE, ABS, fiberglass.
- Marine variant must include chin strap (for rough seas) and electrical insulation.
- Standards: **EN 397**, **ANSI Z89.1**.

**2. Eye and Face Protection (1.5 marks)**
- Goggles — chemical splash and dust.
- Face shields — radiant heat, molten metal.
- Welding helmets — auto-darkening filter.
- Standard: **EN 166**.

**3. Respiratory Protection (2 marks)**
- SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus) — for IDLH atmospheres.
- EEBD — minimum 10-minute escape device (SOLAS II-2).
- Dust masks — FFP1/2/3.
- Standard: **EN 529**, **SOLAS II-2/13.3.4**.

**4. Hand, Foot, and Body Protection (1.5 marks)**
- Cut-resistant gloves (EN 388), chemical-resistant nitrile gloves (EN 374).
- Steel-toe boots (EN ISO 20345 S3).
- Immersion suits (SOLAS LSA Code Ch. III), chemical coveralls (EN 14605).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the SOLAS Chapter III requirements for life-saving appliances with respect to personnel protection.**

#### Model Solution:

**SOLAS Chapter III** governs **Life-Saving Appliances (LSA)** and arrangements.

**1. Lifejackets (2.5 marks)**
- One for every person on board, plus additional for watch-keepers on bridges.
- Buoyancy ≥ 150 N.
- Whistle, light, and buddy line.
- Standard: **SOLAS LSA Code Chapter II**.

**2. Immersion Suits (2 marks)**
- Mandatory for every crew member if the voyage crosses waters where temperature ≤ 15 °C.
- Donning time ≤ 2 minutes.
- Provides ≥ 1 hour survival in cold water.
- Standard: **SOLAS LSA Code Chapter III**.

**3. Life Rafts and Rescue Boats (1.5 marks)**
- Life rafts on each side, ≥ 100% capacity.
- Rescue boat with crew aboard.
- Monthly inspection and annual servicing.

**4. Drills and Training (1 mark)**
- Abandon ship drill monthly.
- Fire drill monthly.
- Each crew member trained within one month of joining.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students frequently lose marks by **confusing SOLAS Chapters** — Chapter II-2 is *fire protection* (SCBA/EEBD), while Chapter III is *life-saving appliances* (lifejackets/rafts). Mix them up and you lose up to 3 marks on this question. Also, do not state "every seafarer needs a lifejacket and immersion suit" without specifying the **temperature threshold of 15 °C** for immersion suits.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice 2]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the importance of hearing protection in the engine room. Derive the permissible exposure time at 100 dB(A).**

#### Model Solution:

**Importance of Hearing Protection (4 marks):**
- Engine room noise typically ranges **90–110 dB(A)**.
- Prolonged exposure > 85 dB(A) causes **noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)** — irreversible.
- Crew must be able to hear alarms (verbal communication, fire alarms).
- Hearing PPE includes **earplugs** (NRR 25–33 dB) and **earmuffs** (SNR 25–35 dB).
- Standard: **EN 352**.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$L_{EX,8h} = L_A + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

Setting $L_{EX,8h} = 85$ dB(A), $T_0 = 8$ h, $L_A = 100$ dB(A):

$$85 = 100 + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{T_{max}}{8}\right)$$

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{T_{max}}{8}\right) = -1.5$$

$$\frac{T_{max}}{8} = 10^{-1.5} = 0.0316$$

$$T_{max} = 0.253 \text{ hours} \approx 15.2 \text{ minutes}$$

**Conclusion:** Without hearing PPE, exposure at 100 dB(A) must be limited to **~15 minutes per 8-hour shift**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the procedure for safe entry into a confined space on board a tanker, including PPE and safety equipment required.**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Risk Assessment (2 marks)**
- Identify the space, prior cargo, and atmospheric hazards (H₂S, benzene, O₂ deficiency).
- Classify the space per **ISGOTT** and **SOLAS Reg. II-2/19**.

**2. Entry Permit (1.5 marks)**
- Issue a **Permit to Work (PTW)** after all precautions verified.
- Specify time of entry, authorized personnel, and rescue arrangements.

**3. Atmospheric Testing (2 marks)**
- Test for **O₂ (21% by volume)**, **LEL (< 1%)**, **H₂S (< 10 ppm)**, and **CO (< 50 ppm)**.
- Use calibrated multi-gas detector (e.g., Drager X-am, Industrial Scientific).

**4. PPE Required (1 mark)**
- SCBA (positive-pressure type).
- Full-body harness with rescue lifeline.
- Chemical-resistant coveralls.
- Gas monitor (personal, continuous).

**5. Rescue Provisions (0.5 marks)**
- Rescue tripod and winch at the entry point.
- Stand-by person at the entry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Do not write "wear a respirator" without specifying **SCBA vs. air-purifying respirator (APR)**. In an oxygen-deficient atmosphere, only **SCBA** is acceptable — APRs do not supply oxygen and can be fatal. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this confusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Personnel Protection**

- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE
- ✅ **SOLAS Chapter II-2:** Fire protection (SCBA, EEBD ≥ 10 min)
- ✅ **SOLAS Chapter III:** Life-saving appliances (lifejackets, immersion suits)
- ✅ **Immersion suit threshold:** Sea water temperature ≤ **15 °C**
- ✅ **Donning time for immersion suit:** ≤ **2 minutes**
- ✅ **Hearing PPE trigger:** Noise exposure ≥ **85 dB(A)** for 8 h
- ✅ **Permissible exposure at 100 dB(A):** ~15 minutes (derived using 3 dB exchange rate)
- ✅ **Hard hat impact force limit:** ≤ **5 kN** transmitted force
- ✅ **Boot compression resistance:** **15 kN**, impact **200 J** (EN ISO 20345 S3)
- ✅ **Fall arrest maximum arrest force:** ≤ **6 kN** (EN 363)
- ✅ **Respirator APF:** Half-face = 10, Full-face = 50, SCBA = 1000+
- ✅ **MLC 2006:** Mandates occupational safety and health protection for seafarers
- ✅ **ISM Code:** Requires PPE issuance, maintenance, and crew training records
- ✅ **SCBA vs. EEBD:** SCBA = entry/fire-fighting, EEBD = escape only
- ✅ **Confined space entry:** Must use SCBA (not APR) due to O₂-deficient risk
- ✅ **Smart PPE:** IoT-enabled PPE is an emerging trend in maritime Industry 4.0

---

**End of Module 2 — Personnel Protection Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Extinguishers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Extinguishers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Extinguisher (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **portable fire-fighting appliance** consisting of a pressurized vessel, an expellant gas, and a fire-suppression agent, designed to be manually operated to discharge its contents onto a small, incipient (Class A, B, C, D, or K) fire by removing one or more elements of the **Fire Tetrahedron** (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, Chemical Chain Reaction).

In the maritime context under **SOLAS Chapter II-2** and the **International Safety Management (ISM) Code**, fire extinguishers form the *first line of defence* against incipient shipboard fires and are classified as **portable fire-fighting equipment** under **FSS Code (Fire Safety Systems Code), Chapter 4**.

### 1.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — Foundation Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Tetrahedron (Modern Model):**
> Unlike the older *fire triangle* (Fuel–Oxygen–Heat), the **fire tetrahedron** adds the **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** as the fourth element. Any extinguishing agent must attack at least ONE of these four sides.

$$
\text{Fire} = \text{Fuel} + \text{Oxygen} + \text{Heat (Activation Energy)} + \text{Chain Reaction}
$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of fire like a four-legged stool.** Knock out *any one leg* and the stool collapses. A water extinguisher "cuts" the **heat leg** (cooling), a CO₂ extinguisher "removes" the **oxygen leg** (smothering), and a dry powder extinguisher "saws off" the **chain-reaction leg** (chemical inhibition). Each extinguisher is essentially a *specialist surgeon* trained to remove just one specific leg.

### 1.4 Classification of Fires (Universal Standard — IMO/NFPA/IS)

| Fire Class | Fuel Type | Shipboard Examples |
|:----------:|:----------|:-------------------|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles (wood, cloth, paper, trash) | Cabins, passageways, galley waste |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids & gases (oil, paint, LPG) | Engine room bilges, paint locker, purifier area |
| **Class C** | Electrical / Energized equipment | Switchboards, motors, navigation console |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals (Mg, Na, K, Ti) | Rarities onboard; mostly shore-side welding shops |
| **Class K** | Cooking oils & fats (high-temperature) | Galley deep-fat fryers |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Extinguishment (How Each Agent Works)

The four recognized extinguishing mechanisms are:

1. **Cooling (Heat Removal)** — Reduce fuel below ignition temperature. Example: Water absorbs **2,260 kJ/kg** as latent heat of vaporization.
2. **Smothering (Oxygen Starvation)** — Reduce O₂ concentration below **15%** (combustion typically ceases at ~12–14%).
3. **Fuel Removal / Starvation** — Physically or chemically isolate the fuel.
4. **Chemical Chain Inhibition (Flame Inhibition)** — Interrupt the free-radical propagation (H•, OH•, O•). Dry chemical powder is the prime maritime example.

### 2.2 Types of Fire Extinguishers — Shipboard Standard

#### A. Water-Type Extinguishers
- **Plain Water (Class A only):** Cooling by sensible + latent heat absorption.
- **Water-Mist / Water-Spray:** Produces microscopic droplets (< 200 μm) that cool + partially smother. **Safe on energized electrical** equipment up to **35 kV** at a distance.
- **Water with Additives (AFFF pre-mix):** Foam concentrate reduces surface tension; spreads rapidly over hydrocarbon surfaces.

#### B. Foam Extinguishers
- **AFFF (Aqueous Film-Forming Foam):** Float on liquid fuels, seal vapour, cool.
- **AR-AFFF (Alcohol-Resistant):** For polar solvents (methanol, acetone).
- **Mechanical / High-Expansion Foam:** Used in engine rooms and cargo pump rooms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foam NEVER on Class C (electrical) or Class D (metal) fires.** Foam is conductive and reacts violently with burning sodium.

#### C. Dry Chemical Powder Extinguishers
- **ABC Powder (Monoammonium Phosphate, MAP):** Multipurpose. Effective on Class A, B, C.
- **BC Powder (Sodium Bicarbonate / Potassium Bicarbonate):** Class B, C. Standard engine-room choice.
- **Special Powder for Class D (e.g., NaCl dry powder):** For metal fires only.

#### D. Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Extinguishers
- Stored as **liquid under ~58 bar at 21 °C** (approx. **850 psi**).
- Discharges as a gas-snow mixture at **−78 °C** (dry-ice temperature).
- Provides **~34% concentration** by volume at nozzle exit — sufficient to starve most fires.
- **Leaves NO residue** → ideal for **switchboards, ECDIS, radio rooms**.

> [!WARNING]
> **CO₂ hazard:** Asphyxiation risk in confined engine rooms. Minimum **2-person operation** is the maritime rule (SOLAS Reg. II-2/14). Discharge must be preceded by full evacuation alarm.

#### E. Wet Chemical (Class K / Class F)
- Potassium acetate / citrate solution. For cooking-oil fires. Found in **galleys**.
- Saponifies the oil, forming a soap-like blanket that prevents re-ignition.

#### F. Clean Agent / Halon Replacements
- **Halon 1211 / 1301** → banned under **Montreal Protocol (1987)**.
- Replacements: **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)**, **Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12)**, **Inergen (IG-541)**. Used in computer/server rooms and machinery control spaces.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:--------------------|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | Latent heat absorbed by water | $Q = m L_v$ | $L_v = 2{,}260\ \text{kJ/kg}$ at $100\,°\text{C}$ |
| 2 | Heat removed by water spray (sensible + latent) | $Q = m\,c_p\,\Delta T + m\,L_v$ | $c_p = 4.186\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ |
| 3 | Minimum oxygen for combustion | $\text{O}_2 \geq 15\%$ | Below ~12–14% combustion ceases |
| 4 | CO₂ extinguisher storage pressure (21 °C) | $P \approx 58\ \text{bar}$ | $\approx 850\ \text{psi}$ |
| 5 | CO₂ discharge temperature (nozzle) | $T_{\text{discharge}} \approx -78\,°\text{C}$ | Dry-ice effect |
| 6 | Foam expansion ratio | $E_r = \dfrac{V_{\text{foam}}}{V_{\text{concentrate}} + V_{\text{water}}}$ | Low-expansion $E_r < 20$; High-expansion $E_r > 200$ |
| 7 | MAP powder decomposition (ABC) | $\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3$ | Forms a glassy barrier on surfaces |
| 8 | Discharge time (typical portable) | $8$ to $\mathbf{60\ \text{seconds}}$ | Depends on agent & size |
| 9 | Range of portable extinguisher | $4$ to $\mathbf{8\ \text{metres}}$ | For dry powder; $\sim 2$ m for water-mist |
| 10 | Recommended extinguisher mass for shipboard engine room | $2 \times \mathbf{9\ \text{kg}}\ \text{CO}_2$ or equivalent | SOLAS Reg. II-2/10 |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise the big 3 numbers for KTU exams:** $L_v = 2{,}260\ \text{kJ/kg}$ · $\text{O}_2\ \text{limit} = 15\%$ · $P_{\text{CO}_2} = 58\ \text{bar}$. These appear in nearly every numerical or short-answer question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cooling Capacity of a 9-litre Water Extinguisher

A shipboard **plain-water extinguisher** holds **9.0 L of water** at **20 °C**. The fire surface is at **300 °C**. Find how much heat the water can absorb **before being fully boiled away**, assuming the water is heated from 20 °C → 100 °C (sensible) and then vaporised at 100 °C (latent).

**Step 1 — Compute mass of water.** Density of water $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \rho V \\
  &= 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3 \\
  &= 9.0\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat to raise water from 20 °C to 100 °C.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{sensible}} &= m\,c_p\,\Delta T \\
  &= 9.0 \times 4.186 \times (100 - 20) \\
  &= 9.0 \times 4.186 \times 80 \\
  &= 3{,}013.9\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Latent heat absorbed during full vaporisation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{latent}} &= m\,L_v \\
  &= 9.0 \times 2{,}260 \\
  &= 20{,}340\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total cooling capacity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= Q_{\text{sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent}} \\
  &= 3{,}013.9 + 20{,}340 \\
  &= 23{,}353.9\ \text{kJ} \;\; \approx \mathbf{23.35\ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** About 86% of the cooling comes from the **phase change (latent heat)**, not from heating the water. This is why water is such an effective Class-A extinguisher — the energy required to turn liquid water into steam is enormous.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CO₂ Extinguisher: Quantity Needed for a Confined Engine-Room Volume

A ship's auxiliary machinery space has **net volume $V = 250\ \text{m}^3$** (excluding fixed fire-extinguishing gas bottles). A CO₂ flooding system is being designed manually with portable bottles. Calculate the **minimum mass of CO₂** required to suppress combustion, given that combustion in hydrocarbon fires is extinguished when local O₂ drops below **15%**.

**Step 1 — Mass of air inside the space** (assuming density of air $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{air}} &= \rho_{\text{air}} V \\
  &= 1.225 \times 250 \\
  &= 306.25\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Oxygen mass inside** (oxygen mass fraction in air $\approx 23.0\%$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{O}_2} &= 0.23 \times 306.25 \\
  &= 70.44\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Oxygen mass to be displaced** to bring concentration from 21% (normal) down to 15% (extinction).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fraction of O}_2 \text{ to remove} &= \frac{0.21 - 0.15}{0.21} \\
  &= \frac{0.06}{0.21} \\
  &= 0.2857
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m_{\text{O}_2} &= 0.2857 \times 70.44 \\
  &= 20.13\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert displaced O₂ to equivalent CO₂ volume** (by mole count, CO₂ replaces O₂ at the same molar fraction — the room pressure and temperature remain approximately constant).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moles of O}_2 \text{ removed} &= \frac{20.13}{32} = 0.629\ \text{kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent mass of CO₂ to inject** (CO₂ molar mass = 44 kg/kmol).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{CO}_2} &= 0.629 \times 44 \\
  &= 27.7\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\approx 28\ \text{kg}$ of CO₂ is the **minimum theoretical** quantity. SOLAS FSS Code mandates a **safety factor of ~1.5× to 2×**, so a designer would specify **two 25-kg cylinders (50 kg total)** for this space, plus a 30-day supply of refill gas per FSS Code Ch. 5.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Extinguisher (Decision Logic)

| Fire Class | Correct Agent | Forbidden Agent | Reasoning |
|:----------:|:--------------|:----------------|:----------|
| A | Water / ABC Powder | CO₂ (insufficient cooling) | Water soaks & cools; CO₂ can't remove heat from wood |
| B | Foam / CO₂ / ABC / BC Powder | Water jet (dangerous) | Water jet splashes fuel → spreads fire |
| C | CO₂ / Dry Powder | Water / Foam (conductive) | Electrical conduction; short-circuit risk |
| D | Special dry powder (NaCl-based) | Water / Foam / CO₂ | Water reacts with burning Mg, producing H₂ |
| K (F) | Wet chemical (potassium acetate) | Water mist alone (explosion risk) | Water + hot oil → steam explosion ("BLEVE"-like) |

---

### 3.4 Operational Sequence — The **PASS** Technique

> [!TIP]
> Every KTU exam expects students to recall the **P.A.S.S.** acronym.

1. **P — Pull** the safety pin.
2. **A — Aim** at the **base of the fire** (not the flames).
3. **S — Squeeze** the handle.
4. **S — Sweep** side-to-side across the base until extinguishment; **watch for re-ignition**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Portable CO₂ Fire Extinguisher

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Pressurised CO2 Cylinder\nWorking Pressure 58 bar at 21 C"] --> B["Cylinder Valve Assembly\nBrass / Aluminium Body"]
    B --> C["Safety Relief Burst Disc\nSet at approx 180 bar"]
    B --> D["Siphon Tube\nExtends to bottom of cylinder"]
    D --> E["Discharge Hose\nHigh Pressure Reinforced"]
    E --> F["Horn / Nozzle Assembly\nDischarge Exit at minus 78 C"]
    G["Safety Pin and Tarnish Seal"] --> B
    H["Pressure Gauge\nReads 0 to 100 bar"] --> A
    A --> I["CO2 Liquid Charge\nMass 2 kg to 22 kg typical"]
    I --> F
    F --> J["Target Fire Surface\nCooling plus Smothering Action"]
    K["Operator Hand Grip and Trigger Lever"] --> B
    K --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Liquid CO₂ (at −17 °C, saturated inside cylinder) rises through the siphon tube on opening the valve, expands through the hose, vaporises in the horn, and discharges as a cold gas-snow cloud. The expansion absorbs **~571 kJ/kg** of latent heat, which is the *primary cooling driver* in CO₂ suppression.

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Decision Flow for Class Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S(["Fire Detected Onboard"]) --> Q1{"Fuel Type\nIdentified?"}
    Q1 -- "Solid wood / cloth / paper" --> A["Class A Fire\nUse Water or ABC Powder"]
    Q1 -- "Oil / fuel / paint / LPG" --> B["Class B Fire\nUse Foam or CO2 or BC Powder"]
    Q1 -- "Live electrical equipment" --> C["Class C Fire\nUse CO2 or Dry Powder Only"]
    Q1 -- "Combustible metal" --> D["Class D Fire\nUse Special Dry Powder NaCl"]
    Q1 -- "Cooking oil in galley" --> K["Class K or F Fire\nUse Wet Chemical Agent"]
    A --> R["Confirm Extinguishment\nWatch for Re-ignition for 30 minutes"]
    B --> R
    C --> R
    D --> R
    K --> R
```

---

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Extinguishing Mechanism by Agent Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T["Fire Tetrahedron"] --> F["Fuel Side"]
    T --> O["Oxygen Side"]
    T --> H["Heat Side"]
    T --> C["Chemical Chain Reaction"]
    F -- "Cut via" --> P1["Fuel Starvation\nFoam Blanket"]
    O -- "Cut via" --> P2["Smothering\nCO2 and Foam and Inergen"]
    H -- "Cut via" --> P3["Cooling\nWater and Water Mist"]
    C -- "Cut via" --> P4["Chemical Inhibition\nABC Powder and BC Powder"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working of a CO₂ fire extinguisher. State any three advantages and one disadvantage of using CO₂ extinguishers on board ships.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A CO₂ extinguisher consists of a seamless steel cylinder (1) charged with liquid CO₂ at **58 bar** (1). On opening the valve, the liquid discharges through a siphon tube and horn, expanding rapidly into **−78 °C CO₂ gas-snow** (½). It extinguishes by **smothering** (oxygen starvation) and partial cooling (1).

**Advantages:** leaves no residue (½) · electrically non-conductive (½) · fast knock-down on Class B & C fires (½).
**Disadvantage:** asphyxiation risk in confined spaces; SOLAS mandates **2-person operation and pre-discharge alarm** (½).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
*Define the "Fire Tetrahedron" and explain how it differs from the "Fire Triangle". How do dry chemical powder extinguishers act on the tetrahedron?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The fire tetrahedron (1) adds **uninhibited chain reaction** to the three classical sides — fuel, oxygen, heat. Dry chemical powders (especially MAP) work by **chemical inhibition** (½), interrupting free-radical chain reactions; ABC powders also melt to form a **glassy phosphoric-acid barrier** that seals the fuel surface (½), and BC powders release inert gases that **smother** the flame (½).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module offers a choice between TWO full 14-mark questions. Solve either **A** or **B**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a) Classify the different types of fire that may occur on board a ship. With suitable examples, describe the various types of portable fire extinguishers used in the engine room and galley, indicating the class of fire each one addresses.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Classification of fire:**
Class A (solid combustibles — wood, linen, plastic), Class B (flammable liquids & gases — HFO, LO, paint), Class C (electrical — switchboards, motors), Class D (combustible metals — rare onboard), and Class K (cooking oils — galley). Each class with one shipboard example.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Engine-room extinguishers:**
- **BC dry powder (9 kg)** — knocks down oil and electrical fires (1).
- **CO₂ (5 kg & 9 kg portable, plus fixed system)** — for local electrical & oil fires (1).
- **Foam (9 L AFFF)** — for spilled HFO/LO fires (½).
- **Water mist (≈ 6 L)** — auxiliary Class A use (½).

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Galley extinguishers:**
- **Wet chemical (potassium acetate, 6 L)** — Class K deep-fat fryer fires (1).
- **CO₂ (5 kg)** — Class C cooking-appliance electrical fires (½).
- **Class F (analogous to K) wet-chemical blanket** — saponifies oil (½).

---

**(b) With the help of a diagram, describe the construction and operation of a stored-pressure ABC dry-powder extinguisher. Discuss why this extinguisher is considered the most versatile shipboard portable extinguisher.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Construction:**
Cylinder (1) — aluminium alloy or steel, contains **ABC powder (monoammonium phosphate, MAP)** and a **propellant gas (N₂) at 12–15 bar** (½). Pressure gauge on top, siphon tube, discharge hose, and **pistol-grip nozzle** (½).

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Operation:**
When the pin is pulled and lever squeezed (½), N₂ propellant forces powder up the siphon tube (½), through the hose, and out the nozzle as a **cloud** (½). On hitting the flame, MAP decomposes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

The phosphoric acid forms a glassy film on the fuel (½) and the ammonia dilutes oxygen (½). Free-radical chain carriers (H•, OH•) are neutralised by the powder particles (½).

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Versatility:**
Single extinguisher covers **Class A, B, C** (½). Non-toxic, easy to handle, fast discharge (**~12 seconds** for 9 kg) (½). Range up to **6 m** (½). Limitations: powder clouds reduce visibility (½); corrosive residue on electrical contacts — must be cleaned after discharge (½).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — December 2024 Style]**

**(a) Explain the principles of fire extinguishment. How does a high-expansion foam fire-extinguishing system protect a ship's cargo pump room or engine room? Provide a numerical illustration showing foam expansion ratio.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (3 marks) — Principles:**
(a) **Cooling** — water absorbs latent heat $L_v = 2{,}260\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (1); (b) **Smothering** — reduce O₂ < 15% (1); (c) **Fuel removal** — physical isolation (½); (d) **Chemical chain inhibition** — dry powder / Halon replacements (½).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — High-expansion foam in machinery spaces:**
A high-expansion foam generator mixes **foam concentrate (1–3%)** with water and air to produce a foam with $E_r > 200$ (1). The foam fills the compartment within minutes, reducing oxygen below 12% and cooling the structure (½). Approved under **SOLAS Reg. II-2/10 for machinery spaces** (½).

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Numerical illustration:**

Given: foam concentrate $V_c = 50\ \text{L}$, water $V_w = 950\ \text{L}$, generated foam volume $V_f = 25{,}000\ \text{L}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_r &= \frac{V_f}{V_c + V_w} \\
    &= \frac{25{,}000}{50 + 950} \\
    &= \frac{25{,}000}{1{,}000} \\
    &= \mathbf{25}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **low-expansion foam** (1). For high-expansion in a $1{,}000\ \text{m}^3$ engine room, you'd need $E_r \approx 500$, requiring a generator producing **50,000 L of foam per minute** for ~10 min (1).

---

**(b) Discuss the inspection, maintenance, and statutory requirements for portable fire extinguishers as laid out in SOLAS and the Flag Administration's Circulars. Mention the "annual, monthly, and weekly" check routines.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (3 marks) — SOLAS framework:**
SOLAS Chapter II-2 (½); FSS Code Chapter 4 governs portable extinguishers (½). Each accommodation/public space must have at least **one extinguisher per ~200 m² floor area** (½). Each machinery space ≥ **5 portable units** (½). Fire patrol must record extinguishers weekly (½).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Routine checks:**
- **Weekly:** visual inspection — gauge in green zone, pin & seal intact, no corrosion, location unobstructed (1).
- **Monthly:** check pressure gauge, weight ± tolerance, hose condition (½), discharge hose not cracked (½).

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Annual & 5-yearly overhauls:**
- **Annually:** third-party or ship's-team servicing — replace O-rings, check propellant, hydrostatic pressure test of CO₂ cylinders at every **10 years** (1).
- **5-yearly:** internal inspection, valve overhaul, refilling of dry powder (½).
- Records in **SRE (Ship's Record of Equipment) Form E** (½).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> (1) Students **confuse CO₂ hydrostatic test interval** — it is every **10 years**, not 5.
> (2) Students forget that **FOAM extinguishers must NOT be tested hydrostatically every year**; they are *discharged and refilled annually*.
> (3) Many skip **FSS Code Chapter 4**; quoting only SOLAS without the FSS Code loses ½–1 mark.
> (4) On the **Fire Tetrahedron**, forgetting the **fourth side (chemical chain reaction)** is the most common definition error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Fire Extinguishers" — KTU Module 2**

- ✅ Fire **Tetrahedron**: Fuel · Oxygen · Heat · **Chain Reaction** (4 sides, not 3).
- ✅ Fire classes: **A · B · C · D · K** — know shipboard examples for each.
- ✅ **Water** cools; **Foam** smothers liquids; **CO₂** smothers gases & electrical; **ABC powder** inhibits chain reaction.
- ✅ CO₂ stored at **58 bar (21 °C)**; discharges at **−78 °C**; minimum **2-person** operation; pre-discharge alarm mandatory.
- ✅ Latent heat of vaporisation of water: $L_v = 2{,}260\ \text{kJ/kg}$ — primary cooling mechanism.
- ✅ Combustion extinguishment threshold: **O₂ < 15%** (design value **12–14%**).
- ✅ Foam expansion ratio: low $< 20$, medium $20$–$200$, high $> 200$.
- ✅ **PASS** acronym: Pull · Aim · Squeeze · Sweep — aim at the **base**, not flames.
- ✅ SOLAS Reg. **II-2/10 & /14** + **FSS Code Chapter 4** govern portable extinguishers.
- ✅ Inspection: **weekly** visual · **monthly** functional · **annual** third-party · CO₂ hydrostatic test every **10 years**.
- ✅ **Halon** extincted by **Montreal Protocol 1987**; replacements — **FM-200, Novec 1230, Inergen**.
- ✅ **Forbidden combinations:** water on Class B/C/D/K; foam on Class C/D; CO₂ on Class A; wet chemical on anything except Class K.
- ✅ Always maintain **30-day reserve** of refills per FSS Code Ch. 5.
- ✅ Maritime requirement: every ship carries **at least 5 portable extinguishers per machinery space** of Category A.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing System (FGFES)**: A permanently installed, engineered fire suppression arrangement in which a stored gaseous extinguishing agent is discharged through a fixed distribution piping network (cylinders, valves, manifolds, nozzles) into a protected space (machinery spaces, cargo pump rooms, paint lockers, etc.) to extinguish fire by reducing the oxygen concentration and/or interrupting the chemical chain reaction of combustion. In maritime terminology, as per **SOLAS Chapter II-2 / FSS Code Chapter 5**, fixed gas fire-extinguishing systems are mandatory for: - **Category A machinery spaces** (containing oil-fired boilers, internal combustion machinery, oil purifiers) - **Cargo pump rooms** of oil tankers and chemical tankers - **Paint and flammable liquid lockers** - **Cargo holds** carrying dangerous goods (under specific conditions) The extinguishing media are stored under high pressure (typically **5.7 MPa / 57 bar at 21 °C** for CO₂) in cylinders arranged in banks, and are released by a combination of **automatic (pneumatic/electrical)** and **manual (cable/mechanical)** actuation. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Invisible Fire Blanket in a Bottle"** Imagine you have a sealed room with a small fire burning inside (say, a spark in a machinery space). A **fixed gas system** is like keeping a giant pressurized **"invisible blanket"** stored in steel bottles just outside the room. The moment the fire alarm triggers, this blanket is **released all at once** into the room, filling every cubic metre with a non-flammable gas. The blanket does **two things simultaneously**: 1. It **smothers** the fire by displacing oxygen below the level required for combustion. 2. It **cools** the surroundings and dilutes the fuel-air mixture.
