Unlike a sprinkler that *pours water*, or a foam system that *smothers with foam*, a gas system works by **changing the atmosphere** of the room itself — leaving no residue, no water damage, and no cleanup. This is why gas systems are the *gold standard* for engine rooms and control rooms. --- ## 1.3 Key Design Metrics & Standard Reference Values > [!IMPORTANT] > **The four critical design numbers a maritime safety officer must memorize:** | Parameter | Standard Value | Reference | |---|---|---| | **CO₂ design concentration (machinery spaces)** | **34% (minimum), 40% (preferred)** | FSS Code Ch. 5.2.1.1 | | **CO₂ design concentration (cargo pump rooms)** | **45% (minimum)** | FSS Code Ch. 5.2.1.2 | | **CO₂ discharge time (machinery spaces)** | ≤ **120 seconds** (2 minutes) | FSS Code Ch. 5.2.1.1 | | **CO₂ discharge time (pump rooms)** | ≤ **120 seconds** | FSS Code Ch. 5.2.1.2 | | **Minimum hold time** | **20 minutes** at design concentration | FSS Code Ch. 5.2.1.3 | | **CO₂ cylinder storage pressure** | **5.7 MPa (57 bar)** at 21 °C | Industry standard | | **Specific volume of CO₂ at 1 atm, 21 °C** | **0.5614 m³/kg** | NTP reference | | **Oxygen below which combustion stops** | **< 15% by volume** (varies with fuel) | Combustion physics | --- ## 1.4 Classification of Fixed Gas Systems > [!NOTE] > **Two fundamental operating philosophies — Board examiners love this classification:** ### A. Total Flooding Systems - The entire **protected enclosure** is filled with the extinguishing gas to a designed concentration. - Used for **enclosed spaces** (engine rooms, pump rooms, control rooms, paint lockers). - Relies on **room integrity** — doors, vents, and openings must be closable automatically. ### B. Local Application Systems - Gas is directed at a **specific hazard** (e.g., a burner front, a paint locker interior, a deep-fat fryer). - Used where **total flooding is impractical** (open deck, large spaces). - Nozzle is aimed at the fire surface; concentration is maintained locally. ### C. By Extinguishing Mechanism 1. **Inerting / Smothering agents** — CO₂, N₂, IG-541 (Inergen), IG-01 (Argon) 2. **Chemical inhibition agents** — Halon alternatives: FM-200 (HFC-227ea), NOVEC 1230 (FK-5-1-12) 3. **Hybrid systems** — water-mist + inert gas # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Major Gaseous Extinguishing Agents — Engineering Comparison ### A. Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) — *The Workhorse of Shipping* **Why CO₂ dominates maritime applications:** - Cheap, abundant, non-corrosive, electrically non-conductive. - Leaves **zero residue** — safe for electrical machinery. - Stored as a **liquid** under pressure → high agent density per cylinder. **Mechanism of action:** 1. **Displaces O₂** to a concentration below the flame's limiting oxygen index (LOI). 2. **Thermal cooling** via gas expansion (Joule-Thomson effect — CO₂ vaporizes and absorbs latent heat). 3. For CO₂ design concentration of 34–45%, the residual O₂ drops to roughly **14–12%**, which suffocates the flame. > [!IMPORTANT] > **CO₂ is the ONLY gas permitted by FSS Code for machinery spaces on merchant vessels.** Halons and HFCs are **prohibited** under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** and its **Kigali Amendment (2016)** due to Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) and Global Warming Potential (GWP). ### B. Halons (Historical — Now Banned) - **Halon 1301** (CBrF₃) and **Halon 1211** were highly effective but caused severe ozone depletion. - **ODP of Halon 1301 = 10.0** (reference: CFC-11 = 1.0). - All new installations on SOLAS vessels are forbidden. Existing halon systems must be **phased out**. ### C. Clean Agent Replacements (Inert Gas Class) - **IG-541 (Inergen)**: 52% N₂ + 40% Ar + 8% CO₂ → designed to reduce O₂ to **12.5%** (safe for humans for short exposure). - **IG-01 (Argon)**: 100% Argon → similar LOI effect. - **IG-100 (Nitrogen)**: 100% N₂. - These work by **oxygen displacement only** — no chemical action on the flame. ### D. Clean Agent Replacements (Chemical Class) - **HFC-227ea (FM-200)**: GWP = 3,220 → being phased down. - **FK-5-1-12 (NOVEC 1230)**: GWP = 1, ODP = 0 → modern preferred replacement. - These work by **chemical chain inhibition** (break flame-propagating radicals H•, OH•, O•). --- ## 2.2 System Components — Engineering Anatomy > [!NOTE] > **The CO₂ fixed system is the most heavily examined component in KTU ESE papers. Memorize the parts and the release sequence.** 1. **Cylinders / Bottles** — Seamless steel, typically 45 L or 67 L water capacity, filled with **42 kg liquid CO₂**. 2. **Cylinder valves** — Quick-opening, typically with a bursting disc safety (set to ~**18 MPa**). 3. **Master valve / manifold header** — Collects discharge from all cylinders. 4. **Distribution piping** — Schedule 40/80 steel, sized for the design flow rate. 5. **Nozzles** — Brass or stainless steel; sized for discharge area (typically **0.25–0.5 m² coverage** each). 6. **Pilot cylinder** — A small CO₂ cylinder that releases pneumatic pressure to open the master valves of the main bank. 7. **Release station** — Manual pull-cable mechanism on deck and at protected space entrance. 8. **Time delay** (usually **30–60 seconds**) — Allows personnel evacuation before discharge. 9. **Fire detection system** — Heat/smoke/flame detectors triggering automatic release. 10. **Pneumatic/electrical actuators** — Push or pull the master valves open. 11. **Pressure switches** — Confirm discharge (low-pressure alarm). 12. **Weighing scales / level gauges** — Verify cylinder contents during maintenance. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every numerical sub-question.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $M = \dfrac{V \cdot C}{(100 - C) \cdot S \cdot K}$ | Mass of CO₂ required (with safety factor) | kg | | 2 | $M_{min} = V \cdot k \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{100 - C}\right)$ | Minimum CO₂ mass (basic form) | kg | | 3 | $N = \dfrac{M_{total}}{M_{cylinder}}$ | Number of cylinders required | dimensionless | | 4 | $V = L \times B \times H$ | Gross volume of protected space | m³ | | 5 | $V_{net} = V - V_{free}$ | Net volume after subtracting permanent free space | m³ | | 6 | $S = 0.5614 \, \text{m}^3\text{/kg}$ | Specific volume of CO₂ at 21 °C, 1 atm | m³/kg | | 7 | $k = 0.79$ | Volumetric safety factor (free space correction) | dimensionless | | 8 | $K = 1.10$ to $1.30$ | Additional safety factor (class dependent) | dimensionless | | 9 | $Q = \dfrac{M}{\Delta t}$ | Required CO₂ mass flow rate | kg/s | | 10 | $O_2^{residual} = 21\% \times \left(1 - \dfrac{C}{100}\right)$ | Residual oxygen after flooding | % | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** > - $V$ = gross volume of space (m³) > - $C$ = design concentration (%) > - $S$ = specific volume of CO₂ (m³/kg) > - $k$ = correction factor for free air space (typically 0.79 for machinery spaces) > - $K$ = design safety factor > - $M_{cylinder}$ = mass per cylinder (typically 42 kg for standard shipboard cylinders) --- ## 2.4 Operational Release Sequence — The "Six-Second Logic" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Every examiner expects this sequence. Write it in this order for full marks.** 1. **t = 0 s** — Fire detected by heat/smoke/flame detector. 2. **t = 0–5 s** — Fire alarm sounded, pre-discharge alarm activated. 3. **t = 5–30 s** — **Time delay** (gives crew time to evacuate). Audible and visual "EVACUATE" alarms. 4. **t = 30 s** — Pilot cylinder discharges → pneumatic pressure to master valve actuators. 5. **t = 30–150 s** — All CO₂ cylinders discharge simultaneously into the protected space. 6. **t ≥ 150 s** — Hold time begins. Space must remain sealed for **20 minutes minimum**. --- ## 2.5 Why This System Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility - **Statistically**, CO₂ systems have extinguished **over 90% of machinery space fires** in early stages (per IMO casualty data). - **SOLAS** mandates fixed CO₂ systems equivalent to discharging the **entire machinery space volume** within **2 minutes** — making this a life-safety system, not merely a fire-loss mitigation system. - Modern **NOVEC 1230** systems are used in naval combat vessels and cruise ship control rooms where post-fire continuity of operations is critical (no damage to electronics). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solutions ## 3.1 Derivation: Quantity of CO₂ Required for a Protected Space ### Statement of the Problem > Compute the **mass of CO₂** required to flood a ship's machinery space of dimensions **Length = 25 m, Breadth = 12 m, Height = 8 m** to a **design concentration of 40%**, with a safety factor $K = 1.20$. --- ### Step 1: Calculate the Gross Volume of the Protected Space The volume of any rectangular space is: $$ V = L \times B \times H $$ Substituting the given dimensions: $$ V = 25 \, \text{m} \times 12 \, \text{m} \times 8 \, \text{m} $$ $$ V = 2400 \, \text{m}^3 $$ > **Interpretation:** This is the *total enclosed volume* of the machinery space, ignoring any internal structure (engines, tanks, foundations). --- ### Step 2: Subtract Permanent Free Space (Optional Refinement) Machinery space free air correction factor $k = 0.79$ accounts for engine blocks, oil tanks, foundations, and other permanent obstructions that displace the air (and therefore reduce the volume of gas required). $$ V_{net} = V \times k $$ $$ V_{net} = 2400 \, \text{m}^3 \times 0.79 = 1896 \, \text{m}^3 $$ > **Interpretation:** The *effective* volume that needs to be flooded with CO₂ is only about 79% of the gross volume because the remaining 21% is occupied by non-removable machinery. --- ### Step 3: Apply the Mass Formula The fundamental equation for CO₂ mass required to achieve concentration $C$ in volume $V$: $$ M = \frac{V_{net} \cdot C}{S \cdot (100 - C)} $$ Where: - $V_{net}$ = net volume (m³) - $C$ = design concentration (%) - $S$ = specific volume of CO₂ at 21 °C = **0.5614 m³/kg** Substituting: $$ M = \frac{1896 \times 40}{0.5614 \times (100 - 40)} $$ $$ M = \frac{75840}{0.5614 \times 60} $$ $$ M = \frac{75840}{33.684} $$ $$ M = 2251.45 \, \text{kg} $$ > **Interpretation:** At 40% concentration, the space requires about 2.25 tonnes of pure gaseous CO₂. However, the *stored* liquid CO₂ is much more compact (liquid density ~771 kg/m³ at 5.7 MPa), so this fits in a reasonable cylinder bank. --- ### Step 4: Apply the Engineering Safety Factor Designers add a safety margin (typically 10–30%) to ensure concentration is maintained even with minor leaks: $$ M_{design} = M \times K $$ With $K = 1.20$: $$ M_{design} = 2251.45 \times 1.20 $$ $$ M_{design} = 2701.74 \, \text{kg} $$ > **Interpretation:** The ship must carry at least 2702 kg of liquid CO₂ in the cylinder bank — always rounded up. --- ### Step 5: Calculate Number of Cylinders Standard maritime CO₂ cylinders hold **42 kg of liquid CO₂** each. Number of cylinders: $$ N = \left\lceil \dfrac{M_{design}}{42} \right\rceil $$ $$ N = \left\lceil \dfrac{2701.74}{42} \right\rceil $$ $$ N = \lceil 64.33 \rceil = 65 \, \text{cylinders} $$ > **Interpretation:** 65 cylinders (bank) must be installed, with **at least one spare** per SOLAS regulation — so **66 cylinders** total is the practical answer. --- ### Step 6: Verify the Discharge Time Total discharge time must be ≤ **120 s**. With 65 cylinders releasing in parallel: $$ \Delta t = 120 \, \text{s (max allowed)} $$ $$ Q = \frac{M_{design}}{\Delta t} = \frac{2701.74}{120} = 22.51 \, \text{kg/s} $$ > **Interpretation:** The system must be capable of a sustained mass flow rate of ~22.5 kg/s for 120 seconds. Pipe sizing and nozzle orifice calculations follow from this. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Templates for KTU > [!NOTE] > **Practice all four templates. The examiner will rotate the numerical inputs, not the method.** ### Template A: Mass Calculation $$ M = \frac{V \cdot C \cdot K}{S \cdot (100 - C)} $$ ### Template B: Number of Cylinders $$ N = \frac{M}{W_{cyl}} \quad \text{(rounded up, with spare)} $$ ### Template C: Discharge Flow Rate $$ Q = \frac{M}{\Delta t} \quad [\text{kg/s}] $$ ### Template D: Pipe Sizing (Hagen–Poiseuille approximation for gas flow) $$ D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot Q \cdot v}{\pi \cdot \dot{m}}} \quad \text{or by FSS Code chart (pressure drop method)} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Pump Room at 45% Concentration A cargo pump room has volume **V = 800 m³**. Free space factor $k = 0.80$. Design concentration $C = 45\%$. Cylinder size = **45 kg**. Safety factor $K = 1.15$. ### Step 1: Net volume $$ V_{net} = 800 \times 0.80 = 640 \, \text{m}^3 $$ ### Step 2: Mass required $$ M = \frac{640 \times 45}{0.5614 \times (100 - 45)} $$ $$ M = \frac{28800}{0.5614 \times 55} $$ $$ M = \frac{28800}{30.877} = 932.7 \, \text{kg} $$ ### Step 3: With safety factor $$ M_{design} = 932.7 \times 1.15 = 1072.6 \, \text{kg} $$ ### Step 4: Number of cylinders $$ N = \lceil 1072.6 / 45 \rceil = \lceil 23.84 \rceil = 24 \, \text{cylinders} $$ > **Interpretation:** Pump rooms at 45% concentration need a *larger cylinder bank per unit volume* than machinery spaces at 34% — because the higher concentration requires more gas per cubic metre of air. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fixed CO₂ System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fire Detection Sensors
Heat, Smoke, Flame Detectors] B[Fire Alarm Control Panel
FACU/Mimic Diagram] C{Automatic or
Manual Release} D[Time Delay Unit
30 to 60 Seconds] E[Pre-Discharge Alarm
Audible and Visual] F[Pilot Cylinder
Small CO2 Bottle] G[Pneumatic Control Line
High Pressure Tubing] H[Master Valve Actuators
Pneumatic Pistons] I[Main CO2 Cylinder Bank
42 kg per Cylinder] J[Distribution Manifold
High Pressure Header] K[Distribution Piping
Schedule 80 Steel] L[Nozzles
Discharge Orifices] M[Protected Space
Machinery Room / Pump Room] N[Fire Extinguished
O2 below LOI] O[Hold Time
20 Minutes Minimum] P[Pressure Switch
Discharge Confirmation] Q[Alarm and Logging System] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E D --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K K --> L L --> M M --> N M --> O M --> P P --> Q ``` --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cylinder Bank Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pilot Cylinder
2 kg] -->|Pneumatic| B[Master Valve 1] A -->|Pneumatic| C[Master Valve 2] A -->|Pneumatic| D[Master Valve N] B --> E[Cylinder 1
42 kg CO2] C --> F[Cylinder 2
42 kg CO2] D --> G[Cylinder N
42 kg CO2] E --> H[Common Manifold Header] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Main Discharge Pipe] I --> J[Nozzle Bank in Protected Space] ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Gas Selection on a Ship ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Fixed Gas System] --> B{Application Type} B -->|Enclosed Machinery Space| C[CO2 - FSS Code Mandated] B -->|Cargo Pump Room| D[CO2 - 45% Concentration] B -->|Control Room Electronics| E[NOVEC 1230 / Inergen] B -->|Paint Locker| F[CO2 or NOVEC 1230] B -->|Server Room| G[NOVEC 1230 Preferred] C --> H[Verify SOLAS Ch II-2 Compliance] D --> H E --> I[Verify NZS 4541 / NFPA 2001] F --> I G --> I H --> J{Crew Safety Check} I --> J J -->|O2 Drop Risk to Crew| K[Add Pre-Discharge Alarm and Time Delay] J -->|Safe for Occupied Spaces| L[Direct Release Permitted] ``` --- ## 4.4 Schematic — Release Sequence Timeline ```mermaid gantt title CO2 Fixed System Release Sequence dateFormat ss axisFormat %S sec section Detection Fire Detected and Alarm :a1, 00, 5s section Pre-Release Time Delay and Evacuation :a2, 05, 25s section Discharge Pilot Cylinder Activation :a3, 30, 2s Main Cylinder Bank Discharge :a4, 32, 118s section Hold Hold Time 20 Minutes Minimum :a5, 150, 1200s ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define a "Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing System" as applied on board ships. List any four locations where SOLAS mandates its installation.** > **[Model Answer — 3 Marks]** > > **Definition (1.5 Marks):** A Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing System is a permanently installed fire-fighting arrangement in which a gaseous extinguishing agent (most commonly CO₂) is stored under pressure in cylinders, and is released through a fixed piping and nozzle network into a protected space to extinguish fire by reducing oxygen concentration and/or interrupting the chemical chain reaction of combustion. > > **Four SOLAS-mandated locations (1.5 Marks — 0.5 each, any four):** > 1. Category A machinery spaces containing oil-fired boilers or internal combustion engines. > 2. Cargo pump rooms of oil and chemical tankers. > 3. Paint lockers and flammable liquid storage lockers. > 4. Ro-Ro spaces and vehicle decks (under specific conditions). > 5. Spaces containing oil purifiers, centrifuges, and heated fuel oil units. --- ### **Q2. State the FSS Code requirements for design concentration and discharge time of CO₂ in a machinery space.** > **[Model Answer — 3 Marks]** > > 1. **Design concentration (1.5 Marks):** The minimum CO₂ concentration in a machinery space shall be **34% by volume**, with a preferred design of **40%**. > 2. **Discharge time (1.5 Marks):** The required quantity of CO₂ shall be discharged into the protected space within **120 seconds (2 minutes)** to ensure rapid fire suppression. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Pattern** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Type Pattern]** **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **components and operational sequence** of a fixed CO₂ fire-extinguishing system installed in a ship's machinery space. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A ship's engine room has dimensions: **Length = 20 m, Breadth = 10 m, Height = 6 m**. Calculate **(i)** the mass of CO₂ required for **40% design concentration**, **(ii)** the number of cylinders required if each cylinder holds **42 kg**, and **(iii)** the mass flow rate if discharge must be completed in **120 s**. Use specific volume of CO₂ = **0.5614 m³/kg** and free space factor $k = 0.79$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **Components (4 Marks — 0.5 each for any 8):** 1. CO₂ cylinders (bank) — typically 42 kg liquid CO₂ per cylinder. 2. Cylinder valves — quick-opening type with bursting disc (set ~18 MPa). 3. Pilot cylinder — small CO₂ bottle for pneumatic actuation. 4. Master valve and manifold header — collects discharge. 5. Distribution piping — high-pressure steel pipes (Schedule 80). 6. Nozzles — sized to give uniform distribution. 7. Time delay unit (30–60 s) — allows evacuation. 8. Manual release station (on deck and at entrance). 9. Fire detection system — heat/smoke/flame detectors. 10. Pressure switches — confirm discharge and trigger alarms. **Operational Sequence (3 Marks):** 1. Fire detected → alarm sounded and pre-discharge alarm activated. 2. **30-second time delay** for evacuation. 3. Pilot cylinder discharges → pneumatic pressure to master valve actuators. 4. All main cylinders release CO₂ simultaneously into the protected space. 5. Discharge completed in **120 seconds** → space sealed for **20 minutes minimum** to maintain concentration. **Block Diagram (drawn or described):** Detection → Alarm → Time Delay → Pilot → Master Valves → Cylinders → Manifold → Piping → Nozzles → Protected Space. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** - $L = 20 \, \text{m}$, $B = 10 \, \text{m}$, $H = 6 \, \text{m}$ - $C = 40\%$, $k = 0.79$, $S = 0.5614 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, $W_{cyl} = 42 \, \text{kg}$, $\Delta t = 120 \, \text{s}$ **Step 1: Gross Volume (1 Mark)** $$ V = L \times B \times H = 20 \times 10 \times 6 = 1200 \, \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 2: Net Volume (1 Mark)** $$ V_{net} = V \times k = 1200 \times 0.79 = 948 \, \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 3: Mass of CO₂ required (1.5 Marks)** $$ M = \frac{V_{net} \cdot C}{S \cdot (100 - C)} = \frac{948 \times 40}{0.5614 \times 60} $$ $$ M = \frac{37920}{33.684} = 1125.75 \, \text{kg} $$ **Step 4: Number of cylinders (1.5 Marks)** $$ N = \left\lceil \frac{M}{42} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1125.75}{42} \right\rceil = \lceil 26.8 \rceil = 27 \, \text{cylinders} $$ Add **1 spare** → 28 cylinders total. **Step 5: Mass flow rate (2 Marks)** $$ Q = \frac{M}{\Delta t} = \frac{1125.75}{120} = 9.38 \, \text{kg/s} $$ **Final Answer:** $M = 1125.75 \, \text{kg}$, $N = 27$ cylinders (+1 spare), $Q = 9.38 \, \text{kg/s}$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Compare **CO₂, NOVEC 1230, and Inergen (IG-541)** as fixed gas extinguishing agents on board ships. Discuss their **mechanisms, advantages, limitations, and applicability** under SOLAS. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **safety precautions** that must be observed by ship's crew before and during the release of a fixed CO₂ system. Why is a **time delay** mandatory? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks | Parameter | CO₂ | NOVEC 1230 (FK-5-1-12) | Inergen (IG-541) | |---|---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | O₂ displacement + cooling | Chemical chain inhibition | O₂ displacement only | | **ODP** | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **GWP** | 1 | 1 | 0 | | **Design concentration** | 34–45% | 4–6% | ~37% (reduces O₂ to 12.5%) | | **Storage** | Liquid at 5.7 MPa | Liquid at low pressure | High-pressure gas (15–30 MPa) | | **Toxicity** | High (asphyxiant) | Low at design conc. | Safe for occupied spaces | | **SOLAS permitted** | **Yes — mandatory for machinery spaces** | Limited (special applications) | No (mostly land-based) | | **Residue** | None | None | None | | **Cost** | Low | High | High | | **Application** | Machinery spaces, pump rooms | Control rooms, electronics | Server rooms, archives | **Key Points (2 Marks — any four):** 1. CO₂ is the **only FSS Code-approved** gas for machinery spaces on merchant vessels. 2. NOVEC 1230 has near-zero GWP and is safe for electronics, but is expensive. 3. Inergen is safe for humans but is mostly used in **land-based** systems (NFPA 2001), not ships. 4. Halons (1301, 1211) are **banned** under the Montreal Protocol due to ODP. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Safety Precautions before release (3.5 Marks):** 1. **Total evacuation** of all personnel from the protected space. 2. **Closure of all doors, vents, skylights, and dampers** to ensure room integrity. 3. Stopping of **ventilation fans** and HVAC systems to prevent gas loss. 4. **All personnel accounted for** at the muster station. 5. **Engine room bilges** checked for hydrocarbon vapours (to prevent explosion post-discharge). **Safety Precautions during release (2 Marks):** 1. **No re-entry** into the protected space during discharge. 2. Breathing apparatus (SCBA) must be worn by any crew entering after discharge. 3. The space must remain **sealed for at least 20 minutes** to maintain concentration. **Why time delay is mandatory (1.5 Marks):** - The mandatory **30–60 second time delay** provides a safe window for crew evacuation before the asphyxiating CO₂ is released. - It also prevents accidental discharge during routine testing or false alarms. - Failure to evacuate can cause **loss of consciousness in seconds** and **death within minutes** — CO₂ concentrations above 10% cause rapid asphyxiation. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost 1–3 marks each in KTU ESE papers — avoid these at all costs:** > > 1. **Forgetting the free space factor $k$** — examiners frequently deduct **1 mark** for using gross volume instead of net volume in the mass equation. > 2. **Not rounding up the cylinder count** — never write 26.8 cylinders; you must write **27 cylinders + 1 spare = 28**. > 3. **Mixing up units** — remember specific volume $S$ has units of **m³/kg**, and using it incorrectly as kg/m³ will produce a mass off by a factor of ~1000. > 4. **Omitting the time delay** in the operational sequence — this is a **favourite sub-question**, and skipping it loses **2 marks** instantly. > 5. **Confusing concentration with oxygen percentage** — design concentration is the **CO₂ fraction**, not residual O₂. They are related but distinct. > 6. **Writing "Halon 1301"** in a modern design answer — this is a **dead agent**. Always state the modern replacement. > 7. **Forgetting to state the 20-minute hold time** after discharge. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-revision checklist — print this and read 5 minutes before the exam:** ### **Must-Memorize Definitions** - [x] **Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing System** — permanently installed system releasing gas to suppress fire by reducing O₂ and/or chemical inhibition. - [x] **Total Flooding** — fills the entire protected enclosure. - [x] **Local Application** — gas directed at a specific hazard. ### **Critical FSS Code Numbers** - [x] **CO₂ design concentration (machinery space)** = **34% min, 40% preferred** - [x] **CO₂ design concentration (pump room)** = **45% min** - [x] **Discharge time** = **≤ 120 seconds** - [x] **Hold time** = **≥ 20 minutes** - [x] **Cylinder pressure** = **5.7 MPa at 21 °C** - [x] **Specific volume of CO₂** = **0.5614 m³/kg** - [x] **Free space factor k** = **0.79 (machinery), 0.80 (pump rooms)** ### **Essential Formulas** - [x] Mass: $\;M = \dfrac{V_{net} \cdot C}{S \cdot (100 - C)} \times K$ - [x] Cylinders: $\;N = \lceil M / W_{cyl} \rceil + 1 \, \text{spare}$ - [x] Flow rate: $\;Q = M / \Delta t$ ### **Components — Remember 8 of These** - [x] Cylinders, valves, pilot cylinder, master valve, manifold, piping, nozzles, time delay, detection system, manual release station, pressure switch, weighing scale. ### **Operational Sequence — Write in this Order** - [x] **Detect → Alarm → Time Delay (30 s) → Pilot → Master Valves → Cylinders → Discharge (≤120 s) → Hold (20 min).** ### **Key Bans & Replacements** - [x] **Halons banned** (Montreal Protocol) → use CO₂, NOVEC 1230, Inergen. - [x] **CO₂ is the SOLAS-mandated agent** for machinery spaces on merchant ships. ### **Safety Facts** - [x] CO₂ at >10% is **rapidly asphyxiating**. - [x] Time delay exists **to save lives**, not as a design choice. - [x] Crew must wear **SCBA** for any post-discharge entry. - [x] **Zero residue** — gas systems are preferred for **electrical and electronics** protection.

topics.title: Fixed Foam Fire Extinguishing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed Foam Fire Extinguishing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fixed Foam Fire Extinguishing System** is a permanently installed, automatically or manually actuated fire suppression arrangement that uses a mechanically generated blanket of aqueous foam to extinguish fires by simultaneously cooling the fuel surface and excluding oxygen from the combustion zone. The system is governed internationally by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under **SOLAS Chapter II-2** and the **Fire Safety Systems (FSS) Code**, with detailed specifications issued in **MSC/Circ.1239** and **MSC.1/Circ.1503**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The system is a *fixed* installation meaning all major components — proportioner, piping, foam generators/discharge outlets, and concentrate storage — are permanently secured to the ship's structure. It is not portable and must be capable of operating without crew intervention in machinery spaces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **blow-up air mattress** floating on the surface of a swimming pool full of oil. The mattress (foam blanket) is inflated using a chemical "blower" (foam generator) and once it covers the oil, three things happen at once:
1. The **fuel is cooled** because the foam is mostly water.
2. The **oxygen is starved** because the foam bubbles are sealed.
3. The **fuel vapors are suppressed** because the blanket blocks them from rising.

This is exactly what a fixed foam system does on a marine fuel tank, bilge, or machinery space floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box**
> - **Foam Concentrate** — A chemical liquid (typically **1%, 3%, or 6%** concentration) that, when mixed with water and air, produces foam.
> - **Foam Solution** — The pre-mix of foam concentrate and water before air induction.
> - **Finished Foam** — The expanded, aerated bubble mass that is finally discharged on the fire.
> - **Expansion Ratio (ER)** — The ratio of finished foam volume to foam solution volume consumed.

## 1.3 Standard Marine Metrics (SOLAS Compliant)

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Minimum Application Rate (Machinery Spaces)** | **4.0 L/min per m²** (AFFF) |
| **Minimum Application Rate (Helideck)** | **6.5 L/min per m²** |
| **Minimum Discharge Time** | **10 minutes** (typical machinery space) |
| **Helideck Discharge Time** | **5 minutes minimum** |
| **Expansion Ratio (Low Expansion)** | **< 20 : 1** |
| **Expansion Ratio (Medium Expansion)** | **20 : 1 to 200 : 1** |
| **Expansion Ratio (High Expansion)** | **200 : 1 to 1000 : 1** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — Component Logic

A marine fixed foam system consists of **five mandatory subsystems** that operate sequentially:

1. **Foam Concentrate Storage Tank** — Holds the chemical liquid, sized per the protected area's demand.
2. **Water Supply Network** — The ship's existing fire main (typically **sea water** or **fresh water**) pressurized by the fire pump.
3. **Proportioner / Ratio Controller** — A **balanced pressure proportioner** or **line proportioner** that injects concentrate at a fixed ratio (**3%** or **6%**) into the water stream.
4. **Distribution Piping & Valves** — Branches the foam solution to protected zones, controlled by section valves and release stations.
5. **Foam Generators / Discharge Devices** — Aspirating devices (e.g., foam sprinklers, pourers, high-expansion generators) that mix air with the foam solution to produce finished foam.

## 2.2 Operational Sequence — The "Why" and "How"

- **Detection** → Heat/fire detector trips an alarm.
- **Release** → Solenoid valve opens; foam concentrate flows to the proportioner.
- **Mixing** → The proportioner maintains a **3%** or **6%** ratio using the Venturi principle.
- **Aspiration** → Air is drawn into the solution via venturi or foam maker at each discharge device.
- **Discharge** → The finished foam blanket flows over the fire surface.
- **Suppression** → Cooling + Smothering + Fuel vapor suppression occur simultaneously.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Foam Solution Flow Rate | $Q_s = A \times R$ | L/min | $A$ = Area in m², $R$ = Application rate in L/min/m² |
| 2 | Foam Concentrate Flow Rate | $Q_c = Q_s \times C$ | L/min | $C$ = Concentration fraction (**0.03** or **0.06**) |
| 3 | Foam Concentrate Tank Volume | $V_t = Q_c \times t$ | Litres | $t$ = Discharge time in minutes |
| 4 | Finished Foam Production Rate | $Q_f = Q_s \times ER$ | L/min | $ER$ = Expansion Ratio |
| 5 | Total Water Demand | $Q_w = Q_s - Q_c = Q_s(1 - C)$ | L/min | Water consumed in foam solution |
| 6 | Mass of Concentrate | $m = V_t \times \rho$ | kg | $\rho \approx$ **1.00 to 1.20 g/cm³** for protein foams |
| 7 | Velocity in Pipe (Hagen–Poiseuille Ref.) | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{pipe}}$ | m/s | For hydraulic design |
| 8 | Reynolds Number for Foam Solution | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | $\mu_{solution} \approx$ **1.0 to 1.5 cP** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tank Protection:** Deck foam systems protect **oil cargo tanks** and **bunker tanks** from rupture-fire scenarios.
- **Engine Room Protection:** Fixed high-expansion foam can flood a machinery space up to the **highest point** of the space.
- **Helideck Safety:** IMO mandates foam systems for all **helidecks** on ships and offshore platforms.
- **Chemical Carrier Safety:** Alcohol-resistant foams (AR-AFFF) are mandatory where **polar solvents** are carried.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 3% / 6% Standard?** These are the historical industry standards. **3% AFFF** uses less concentrate, reducing storage weight. **6%** is preferred for protein foams due to viscosity. Selection depends on the protected hazard and SOLAS approvals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Sample Problem (Worked Model for KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem (Typical KTU Module 2 Numerical):**
> A **fixed foam system** is to protect a **cargo oil tank deck area of 240 m²**. The system uses **AFFF at 3% concentration** with a **medium expansion ratio of 60:1** and an **application rate of 4.0 L/min/m²**. The discharge time is **10 minutes**. Calculate:
> (i) Foam solution flow rate
> (ii) Foam concentrate flow rate
> (iii) Foam concentrate tank capacity
> (iv) Finished foam production rate
> (v) Total water consumed

### Given Data
- $A = 240 \text{ m}^2$
- $C = 3\% = 0.03$
- $ER = 60 : 1 = 60$
- $R = 4.0 \text{ L/min/m}^2$
- $t = 10 \text{ min}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### (i) Foam Solution Flow Rate $Q_s$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= A \times R \\
&= 240 \text{ m}^2 \times 4.0 \text{ L/min/m}^2 \\
&= 960 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each square metre requires **4.0 L** of foam solution per minute, and the deck has **240 m²**, so the total solution required is their product.

#### (ii) Foam Concentrate Flow Rate $Q_c$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= Q_s \times C \\
&= 960 \text{ L/min} \times 0.03 \\
&= 28.8 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Since the solution is **3% concentrate by volume**, we multiply the solution rate by **0.03** to extract the concentrate portion.

#### (iii) Foam Concentrate Tank Capacity $V_t$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t &= Q_c \times t \\
&= 28.8 \text{ L/min} \times 10 \text{ min} \\
&= 288 \text{ Litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The tank must hold enough concentrate to sustain the flow for the entire discharge duration. A safety factor of **1.10 to 1.25** is normally added in actual design.

#### (iv) Finished Foam Production Rate $Q_f$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_f &= Q_s \times ER \\
&= 960 \text{ L/min} \times 60 \\
&= 57{,}600 \text{ L/min} \\
&= 57.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Every litre of foam solution expands into **60 litres** of finished foam (medium expansion), so the foam generator output is 60 times the solution rate.

#### (v) Total Water Consumed $Q_w$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_w &= Q_s - Q_c \\
&= 960 - 28.8 \\
&= 931.2 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Water is the **97%** portion of the solution. Subtracting concentrate from solution gives the pure water demand on the fire main.

### Analytical Matrix — Foam Type Selection (High-Yield for KTU)

| Foam Type | Expansion | Best Suited For | Concentration | Application Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AFFF (Low)** | 5:1 to 10:1 | Machinery spaces, oil fires | 3% / 6% | **4.0 L/min/m²** |
| **AFFF (Medium)** | 20:1 to 60:1 | Helidecks, deck areas | 3% / 6% | **6.5 L/min/m²** |
| **Protein** | 5:1 to 10:1 | Hydrocarbon fires | 3% / 6% | **4.0 L/min/m²** |
| **Fluoroprotein (FP)** | 5:1 to 10:1 | Polar solvents, tank farms | 3% / 6% | **4.0 L/min/m²** |
| **AR-AFFF** | 5:1 to 10:1 | Alcohols, ketones, polar liquids | 3% / 6% | **6.5 L/min/m²** |
| **High Expansion (HEX)** | 200:1 to 1000:1 | Machinery space flooding | 2% / 3% | **Submersion ≤ 4.0 m** |

### Numerical Justification (Helideck Example)

> **Problem:** Helideck of area **312 m²** protected by medium expansion AFFF (3%), $ER = 50:1$, $R = 6.5$ L/min/m², $t = 5$ min.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= 312 \times 6.5 = 2028 \text{ L/min} \\
Q_c &= 2028 \times 0.03 = 60.84 \text{ L/min} \\
V_t &= 60.84 \times 5 = 304.2 \text{ Litres} \\
Q_f &= 2028 \times 50 = 101{,}400 \text{ L/min} \\
Q_w &= 2028 - 60.84 = 1967.16 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Detection Loop] --> B[Control Panel and Release Station]
    B --> C[Solenoid Release Valve Opens]
    C --> D[Fire Pump Pressurizes Sea Water]
    D --> E[Balanced Pressure Proportioner 3 percent]
    E --> F[Distribution Manifold and Section Valves]
    F --> G1[AFFF Pourers in Engine Room]
    F --> G2[Medium Expansion Foam Generators on Helideck]
    F --> G3[Deck Foam Pourers over Cargo Tanks]
    G1 --> H1[Low Expansion Foam 5 to 10 Ratio]
    G2 --> H2[Medium Expansion Foam 20 to 60 Ratio]
    G3 --> H3[Medium Expansion Foam Blanket over Fuel]
    H1 --> I[Suppression Cooling and Oxygen Exclusion]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    J[Foam Concentrate Storage Tank] -->|Concentrate Line| E
    K[Compressed Air or Aspirated Air] --> G1
    K --> G2
    K --> G3
```

## 4.2 System Component Flow Diagram (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SupplySide[Water and Concentrate Supply]
        W1[Fire Main Sea Water]
        W2[Fire Pump]
        F1[Foam Concentrate Tank]
        F2[Concentrate Strainer]
    end
    subgraph MixingUnit[Proportioning Section]
        M1[Pressure Reducing Valve]
        M2[Balanced Pressure Proportioner]
        M3[Concentrate Control Valve]
    end
    subgraph Distribution[Distribution Network]
        D1[Main Manifold]
        D2[Section Valve 1 Engine Room]
        D3[Section Valve 2 Helideck]
        D4[Section Valve 3 Cargo Deck]
    end
    subgraph Discharge[Discharge Devices]
        P1[Low Expansion Foam Nozzles]
        P2[Medium Expansion Generators]
        P3[High Expansion Generators]
    end
    W1 --> W2
    W2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> M3
    M3 --> M2
    M2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> P1
    D3 --> P2
    D4 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Fire Suppression Mechanism Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[Finished Foam Blanket Layer] --> R2[Blocks Heat Transfer to Fuel]
    R1 --> R3[Excludes Atmospheric Oxygen]
    R1 --> R4[Prevents Fuel Vapor Release]
    R2 --> R5[Triple Action Fire Suppression]
    R3 --> R5
    R4 --> R5
    R5 --> R6[Fire Extinguished within 10 to 30 seconds]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define "Expansion Ratio" in the context of foam fire suppression. State the range for medium expansion foam.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Expansion Ratio (ER)** is the ratio of the volume of finished foam produced to the volume of foam solution (water + concentrate) consumed. It is expressed as:
$$ER = \frac{V_{foam}}{V_{solution}}$$

Medium expansion foam has an **Expansion Ratio between 20:1 and 200:1**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any four advantages of AFFF over standard protein foam.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Faster knockdown** — AFFF spreads rapidly across fuel surfaces (fluorinated surfactants reduce surface tension dramatically).
2. **Vapor sealing** — Forms an aqueous film that suppresses fuel vapors effectively.
3. **Lower storage weight** — 3% concentration requires less concentrate tank capacity.
4. **Compatible with non-aspirating devices** — Can be applied through water spray nozzles for dual-purpose systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **balanced pressure proportioner** in a fixed foam system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A helideck of area **400 m²** is protected by a medium expansion foam system. The application rate is **6.5 L/min/m²**, expansion ratio is **50:1**, concentration is **3%**, and discharge time is **5 minutes**. Calculate the **foam concentrate tank capacity** and the **water consumption**. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a)

A **Balanced Pressure Proportioner (BPP)** maintains a constant proportioning ratio regardless of fluctuations in water pressure or foam concentrate pressure. The working principle is as follows:

1. The **water line** passes through a pressure-reducing valve, while the **concentrate line** is connected to the concentrate tank (pressurized by its own nitrogen or pump).
2. A **diaphragm-actuated control valve** senses the differential pressure between the water and concentrate sides.
3. The diaphragm opens/closes the concentrate flow path so that the pressure difference is **nullified**.
4. When water pressure rises, the diaphragm lifts, restricting concentrate flow proportionally. When it falls, concentrate flow increases.
5. The result: a **stable 3% or 6% proportioning ratio** is maintained automatically.

> A **neat sketch** should show: Water inlet → Pressure reducing valve → Diaphragm chamber → Foam solution outlet, with concentrate inlet on the other side of the diaphragm, and a balance line connecting both chambers.

#### Solution to (b)

**Given:** $A = 400$ m², $R = 6.5$ L/min/m², $ER = 50$, $C = 0.03$, $t = 5$ min

**Step 1 — Foam Solution Flow Rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= A \times R \\
&= 400 \times 6.5 \\
&= 2600 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Foam Concentrate Flow Rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= Q_s \times C \\
&= 2600 \times 0.03 \\
&= 78 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Foam Concentrate Tank Capacity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t &= Q_c \times t \\
&= 78 \times 5 \\
&= 390 \text{ Litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Design Note:** With a **1.25 safety factor**, $V_{t,design} = 390 \times 1.25 = 487.5$ Litres.

**Step 4 — Water Consumption:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_w &= Q_s - Q_c \\
&= 2600 - 78 \\
&= 2522 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Water for 5 Minutes:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= 2522 \times 5 \\
&= 12{,}610 \text{ Litres} \\
&= 12.61 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $V_t = 390$ L, $Q_w = 2522$ L/min, $W_{total} = 12.61$ m³.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**(a)** Compare **Low Expansion, Medium Expansion, and High Expansion foam systems** with respect to expansion ratio, application area, and typical marine use. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machinery space of **floor area 80 m²** is to be protected by a high-expansion foam system with **ER = 500:1**, **application rate 4.0 L/min/m²**, **concentration 3%**, and **discharge time 10 minutes**. Calculate the **foam concentrate tank capacity** and the **finished foam volume produced per minute**. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a)

| Feature | Low Expansion | Medium Expansion | High Expansion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Expansion Ratio** | 5:1 to 20:1 | 20:1 to 200:1 | 200:1 to 1000:1 |
| **Application Rate** | **4.0 L/min/m²** | **6.5 L/min/m²** | Flooding volume basis |
| **Typical Marine Use** | Machinery spaces, oil tank deck | Helidecks, bunker stations | Engine room flooding, holds |
| **Discharge Device** | Foam pourers, sprinklers | Foam generators | Large blower-type generators |
| **Coverage Method** | Surface application | Surface application | Volume flooding |
| **AFFF Type** | Yes | Yes | Mostly synthetic HEX |

#### Solution to (b)

**Given:** $A = 80$ m², $R = 4.0$ L/min/m², $ER = 500$, $C = 0.03$, $t = 10$ min

**Step 1 — Foam Solution Flow Rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= 80 \times 4.0 \\
&= 320 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Foam Concentrate Flow Rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= 320 \times 0.03 \\
&= 9.6 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Foam Concentrate Tank Capacity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t &= 9.6 \times 10 \\
&= 96 \text{ Litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Design with safety factor (1.15):** $V_{t,design} = 96 \times 1.15 = 110.4$ Litres.

**Step 4 — Finished Foam Volume Produced per Minute:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_f &= Q_s \times ER \\
&= 320 \times 500 \\
&= 160{,}000 \text{ L/min} \\
&= 160 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $V_t = 96$ L, $Q_f = 160$ m³/min.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
> - **Forgetting the Safety Factor:** Real shipboard design requires **10% to 25% extra capacity** beyond the calculated tank size. Many students lose **1 to 2 marks** by quoting the bare minimum.
> - **Unit Confusion:** Expansion Ratio is **dimensionless**. Do not attach units to ER in your answer.
> - **Concentration Misinterpretation:** **3%** means 3% of solution **volume** is concentrate, not 3% of foam. State the assumption clearly.
> - **Wrong Discharge Time:** For helidecks, the SOLAS-mandated discharge is **5 min**, not 10. For machinery spaces, it is **10 min**. Mixing these loses marks.
> - **Skipping Final Units in Numerical:** Always write L/min or Litres after the final number — examiners will not assume units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fixed Foam System** = permanently installed, SOLAS Chapter II-2 compliant, governed by **FSS Code Chapter 14**.
- **Three Suppression Mechanisms** — Cooling, Smothering, and Fuel Vapor Suppression.
- **Application Rates (Critical KTU Numbers):**
  - AFFF Machinery space → **4.0 L/min/m²**
  - Helideck → **6.5 L/min/m²**
  - Discharge time → **5 min (helideck)**, **10 min (machinery space)**
- **Expansion Ratios** — Low: <20, Medium: 20-200, High: 200-1000.
- **Standard Concentrations** — **3%** and **6%** are the universally accepted industry ratios.
- **Proportioner Types** — Balanced Pressure Proportioner (BPP) is the gold standard; Line Proportioners are simpler alternatives.
- **Key Calculations to Master:**
  - $Q_s = A \times R$
  - $Q_c = Q_s \times C$
  - $V_t = Q_c \times t$
  - $Q_f = Q_s \times ER$
  - $Q_w = Q_s(1 - C)$
- **Always include safety factor (1.10 to 1.25)** in tank sizing.
- **AR-AFFF** is required for polar solvent fires; standard AFFF fails on alcohol fires.
- **High expansion foam** is used for total flooding of enclosed spaces, not for surface application.
- **Helideck foam systems** are governed by **MSC/Circ.1431** and **CAP 437** (offshore).
- **Foam concentrate shelf life** — **5 to 25 years** depending on type; protein foams are shortest.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low-Location Lighting Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Low-Location Lighting Systems**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Low-Location Lighting (LLL) System** is a mandatory emergency escape lighting arrangement installed on passenger ships (and certain other vessels) at a low height — typically **not more than 300 mm above the deck** — along all escape routes, corridors, stairways, and assembly stations. It is designed to provide continuous visual guidance to crew and passengers during emergency evacuation when the main, emergency, and supplementary lighting systems may have failed, particularly under smoke-filled conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** As per **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 13** and **IMO Resolution A.752(18)** / **ISO 15370**, every ro-ro passenger ship and every passenger ship ≥ 36 passengers must be fitted with an LLL system. This is an examinable regulatory specification.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the glowing green guide strips you see along the floor of an **aircraft cabin** leading to emergency exits — that is exactly the principle behind LLL. 

When a fire breaks out on a ship:
- **Hot smoke and toxic gases rise upward**, leaving a clearer breathable layer near the deck.
- A person crawling on the floor will survive far longer than one standing upright.
- Normal ceiling-mounted emergency lights become **useless**, because smoke obscures them first.

The LLL system is essentially a **"floor-level runway"** of light that crawls with you, pointing the way to the lifeboat stations. Imagine a marathon route at night marked with reflective tape along the road edges — LLL does the same job inside a smoke-filled ship corridor, but it actively *emits* light rather than just reflecting it.

### Core Specifications at a Glance
| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Installation Height | **≤ 300 mm** above finished deck |
| Minimum Visibility Distance | Typically **10 metres** |
| Color of Light | **Green** (universal "safety/way out") |
| Continuous Operation | Minimum **3 hours** (ro-ro: 6 hours critical paths) |
| Power Source | Emergency source of electrical power (battery) |
| Activation | Automatic on loss of main + emergency lighting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why GREEN?** Green is reserved internationally for safety/escape signage because the human eye is most sensitive to green wavelengths (~555 nm) under low-light, scotopic conditions, and it contrasts strongly against the typical warm (orange/red) tones of a ship interior fire.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of an LLL System — Stepwise Breakdown

1. **Normal Operation:** The LLL system is energized from the ship's main lighting distribution. It may be continuously lit or only activated on power loss (system-dependent).
2. **Loss of Main Power:** The Emergency Generator (or Emergency Battery Bank) takes over within **45 seconds** for SOLAS compliance.
3. **Total Blackout (Both Main & Emergency Fail):** The LLL system **must continue operating** because it is now drawing from a dedicated, independent battery source — typically sized for **at least 3 hours**.
4. **Smoke Penetration Event:** Because the luminaires sit at ≤ 300 mm, they remain visible below the smoke layer. Crew evacuating on hands and knees can follow the light path.
5. **Escape Route Tracing:** Each segment of the route is marked with directional arrow pictograms at decision points (junctions, stair landings, doors).
6. **Continuous Visual Cue:** The strip itself is unbroken, providing continuous linear guidance rather than point sources.

### 2.2 System Components — Functional Architecture

- **Light Source Strip** — Electroluminescent (EL) strip, photoluminescent (PL) strip, or LED-based strip encapsulated in marine-grade polymer.
- **Directional Pictograms** — Illuminated arrow/door symbols at intervals (commonly every 1 m to 2 m).
- **Junction Boxes & Connectors** — Marine-grade, IP56/IP67 rated, flame-retardant cabling.
- **Central Power Supply Unit (PSU)** — Transformer/rectifier fed from emergency switchboard.
- **Dedicated Battery Pack** — Ni-Cd or sealed lead-acid, sized for the autonomy duration.
- **Monitoring & Control Module** — Indicates lamp failure, battery health, mains presence.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum Autonomy Time | $T_{LLL} \ge 3\,\text{h}$ (SOLAS Reg. II-2/13) | Continuous operating duration | hours |
| Installation Height | $h_{LLL} \le 0.3\,\text{m}$ | From finished deck surface | metres |
| Spacing of Pictograms | $s \le 2.0\,\text{m}$ | Maximum gap between markers | metres |
| Battery Capacity | $C_{bat} = \dfrac{I_{load} \times T_{autonomy}}{k_{discharge}}$ | Required Amp-hour rating | Ah |
| Illumination Level (deck) | $E_{min} \ge 1\,\text{lux}$ (at deck level) | Photopic illuminance | lux |
| Visibility Range | $d_{vis} = \dfrac{I_{lumen}}{E_{min}}$ | Inverse square law reference | metres |
| Power Consumption | $P_{strip} = V \times I \times L_{total}$ | Total power demand | Watts |
| Luminous Flux Required | $\Phi = E_{min} \times A_{floor}$ | Area method | lumens |
| Energy Stored | $E_{stored} = V_{bat} \times C_{bat}$ | Battery energy reserve | Wh |
| Charge Time | $t_{charge} \le 10\,\text{h}$ for Ni-Cd | Recovery to 100% SOC | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Note:** Examiners frequently test the **3-hour autonomy rule** and the **300 mm height rule**. Memorize both as they are recurring high-yield questions in KTU Maritime Safety papers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond passenger vessels, the principles of LLL are applied in:
- **Aircraft cabin floor path lighting** (e.g., Boeing 787, Airbus A350).
- **Submarine internal escape routes** (where smoke can accumulate rapidly).
- **Offshore platform accommodation modules** (similar SOLAS-equivalent code).
- **Tunnel evacuation lighting** (road and rail tunnels worldwide).
- **High-rise building photoluminescent egress markings** (now mandated by NFPA 101 and IBC).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Battery Sizing Derivation for an LLL System

**Problem Statement:** A ro-ro passenger ferry has an LLL system consisting of LED strips drawing a total steady current of $I_{load} = 12\,\text{A}$ from a $24\,\text{V}$ DC battery bank. The system must operate for **$T = 3\,\text{h}$** continuously during blackout. Calculate the required battery capacity assuming a discharge efficiency factor of $k_{dis} = 0.80$.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing relation.**
The usable ampere-hours from a battery is less than its nominal rating because deep discharges are inefficient and damaging. We therefore apply the standard marine battery-sizing formula:

$$C_{bat} = \frac{I_{load} \times T_{autonomy}}{k_{discharge}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$C_{bat} = \frac{12\,\text{A} \times 3\,\text{h}}{0.80}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator.**

$$12 \times 3 = 36\,\text{Ah}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by efficiency.**

$$C_{bat} = \frac{36}{0.80} = 45\,\text{Ah}$$

**Step 5 — Apply engineering safety margin.**
KTU Board answers must include a margin (typically 1.25×) for battery ageing and temperature derating:

$$C_{design} = 1.25 \times 45 = 56.25\,\text{Ah}$$

**Step 6 — Round up to commercial battery size.**

$$C_{selected} = 60\,\text{Ah (commercial standard)}$$

**Final Answer:** A **24 V, 60 Ah** sealed lead-acid or Ni-Cd battery pack is required.

---

### 3.2 Visibility Range Derivation Using Inverse Square Law

**Problem Statement:** An LLL strip emits a luminous intensity of $I_v = 20\,\text{cd}$ (candela). Determine the maximum distance at which the illuminance on a crawling person's eye still meets the SOLAS minimum of $E_{min} = 1\,\text{lux}$.

**Step 1 — State the inverse square law for point source illumination.**

$$E = \frac{I_v}{d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for distance $d$.**

$$d^2 = \frac{I_v}{E_{min}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{I_v}{E_{min}}}$$

**Step 3 — Insert values.**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{20}{1}} = \sqrt{20}$$

**Step 4 — Compute square root.**

$$d = 4.472\,\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

A person crawling on the deck at deck-level can perceive the LLL strip from **approximately 4.47 m** away under clear conditions. In a smoke-filled corridor, this range decreases sharply — hence the **≤ 2 m pictogram spacing rule** is critical.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of LLL Technologies

| Property | Electroluminescent (EL) | Photoluminescent (PL) | LED Strip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Source | Mains + battery | Ambient light charging | Mains + battery |
| Lifetime | ~5,000 h | >10 years (no electricity needed) | >50,000 h |
| Brightness | Medium | Low–Medium (decays over hours) | High |
| Visibility in Smoke | Good | Moderate | Excellent |
| Maintenance | Moderate | Very Low | Low |
| Cost | Medium | Low initial, high lifecycle | High initial, low lifecycle |
| SOLAS Acceptance | Yes (with battery backup) | Yes (Type 1 / 2 approvals) | Yes |
| Typical Application | Older vessels retrofit | New-builds, secondary means | Modern new-builds |

### 3.4 Energy Reserve Calculation

Given the design above ($24\,\text{V}$, $60\,\text{Ah}$):

$$E_{stored} = V_{bat} \times C_{bat}$$

$$E_{stored} = 24 \times 60 = 1440\,\text{Wh} = 1.44\,\text{kWh}$$

This **1.44 kWh** reserve is the engineering justification for sizing the emergency switchboard feed to the LLL system — it must be sufficient to sustain all escape route segments simultaneously for the autonomy period.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LLL System Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Main Switchboard 440V AC] --> nodeB[Emergency Switchboard 220V AC]
    nodeB --> nodeC[LLL Power Supply Unit PSU]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Distribution Junction Boxes]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Corridor LLL Strip 300mm height]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Stairway LLL Strip]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Assembly Station LLL Marker]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Dedicated Battery Bank 24V DC NiCd]
    nodeH --> nodeC
    nodeI[Monitoring Panel Bridge] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeJ[Smoke Detector Loop] -.-> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeK[Pictogram Directional Arrows spaced 2m]
```

### 4.2 Escape Route with LLL Layout — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Cabin Berth] --> nodeB[Corridor LLL Strip Start]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Decision Junction LLL Arrow]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Stairway LLL Vertical Strip]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Landing LLL Strip]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Final Door LLL Arrow]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Assembly Station LLL Marker]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Lifeboat Embarkation Deck]
```

### 4.3 Failure Mode Hierarchy — Power Fallback Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Main Power Normal] --> nodeB{Step 2: Main Power Lost?}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeA
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[Step 3: Emergency Generator Starts within 45s]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Emergency Generator OK?}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[Step 4: LLL Powered from Emergency Board]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF[Step 5: LLL Runs on Dedicated Battery]
    nodeE --> nodeG{Catastrophic Blackout?}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeF
    nodeG -- No --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeH[Step 6: 3 hour autonomy for escape]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When asked in the exam to "sketch the LLL system arrangement," the **three-tier power fallback** (Main → Emergency → Battery) is what examiners want to see demonstrated. Always show all three sources feeding the LLL bus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OESFT727]**
*Define Low-Location Lighting (LLL) system. State the SOLAS regulation under which it is mandated.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Low-Location Lighting (LLL) system is an emergency escape lighting arrangement installed on passenger ships at a height of **not more than 300 mm above the deck**, along all escape routes, stairways, corridors and assembly stations. Its purpose is to provide continuous visual guidance to persons evacuating the ship during emergencies, particularly when main and emergency lighting has failed and the space is filled with smoke (since smoke rises, leaving the lower levels clearer). It is mandated under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 13** (Means of Escape) and is further detailed in **IMO Resolution A.752(18)** and the standard **ISO 15370**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, OESFT727]**
*Why is the colour GREEN chosen for Low-Location Lighting strips on ships?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The colour **green** is internationally reserved for safety, escape and "way out" signage because:
1. The human eye is most sensitive (peak photopic and mesopic sensitivity) to green wavelengths around **555 nm** under low-light, scotopic conditions, so green appears brightest when other colours fade.
2. Green contrasts sharply with the typical **orange-red flame and smoke** hues encountered in a shipboard fire, making the escape line highly visible.
3. Green is universally recognized (IMO, ISO, SOLAS) as the "safe condition / escape route" indicator, so even multilingual crews respond correctly. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> Choose **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, OESFT727 Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **components**, **installation requirements**, and **SOLAS/IMO standards** governing Low-Location Lighting systems on passenger ships. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ro-ro passenger vessel has an LLL load of **15 A at 24 V DC**. Calculate the **battery capacity** required for **3 hours** of autonomy assuming a discharge efficiency of **0.75**, and apply a **1.25× ageing margin**. State the commercial rating you would select. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Components, Installation & Standards (7 marks):**

The LLL system on a passenger ship consists of the following:
- **Light strips**: Linear strips of electroluminescent (EL), photoluminescent (PL), or LED-based material, encapsulated in flame-retardant marine polymer.
- **Directional pictograms**: Illuminated arrows and door symbols placed at intervals of not more than 2 m along the escape route.
- **Central power supply unit (PSU)**: Transformer/rectifier that conditions the emergency AC supply to DC for the strips.
- **Dedicated battery bank**: Sealed Ni-Cd or lead-acid, sized for the required autonomy.
- **Junction boxes and marine-grade cabling**: IP56/67 rated.
- **Monitoring panel**: Indicates lamp failure and battery status on the bridge.

**Installation Requirements:**
- Height: ≤ **300 mm above finished deck**.
- Continuous strip from cabin door to assembly station, no gaps.
- Spacing of arrows: ≤ **2 m**.
- Minimum deck-level illuminance: ≥ **1 lux**.
- Color: **Green** with white pictograms.
- Battery autonomy: minimum **3 hours**.

**Standards Governing LLL:**
- **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 13** — Means of escape.
- **IMO Resolution A.752(18)** — Guidelines for LLL on ro-ro vessels.
- **ISO 15370** — Ships and marine technology — Low-location lighting.
- **IEC 60092** — Electrical installations in ships. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Battery Sizing Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $I_{load} = 15\,\text{A}$, $T = 3\,\text{h}$, $k_{dis} = 0.75$, safety margin = $1.25$.

**Step 1 — Apply the battery sizing formula:**

$$C_{bat} = \frac{I_{load} \times T}{k_{dis}} = \frac{15 \times 3}{0.75} = \frac{45}{0.75} = 60\,\text{Ah}$$

**Step 2 — Apply ageing margin of 1.25×:**

$$C_{design} = 1.25 \times 60 = 75\,\text{Ah}$$

**Step 3 — Round up to nearest commercial rating:**

$$C_{selected} = 80\,\text{Ah (commercial standard)}$$

**Step 4 — Final specification:**
A **24 V, 80 Ah** sealed Ni-Cd or lead-acid battery bank is to be installed.

**Step 5 — Energy reserve check:**

$$E_{stored} = 24 \times 80 = 1920\,\text{Wh} = 1.92\,\text{kWh}$$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary, OESFT727 Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare the **three main technologies** used in LLL systems — Electroluminescent, Photoluminescent, and LED — in terms of working principle, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **three-tier power fallback logic** of an LLL system during progressive blackout conditions. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison of LLL Technologies (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Electroluminescent (EL) | Photoluminescent (PL) | LED |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Phosphor layer emits light when AC voltage is applied (electroluminescence). | Phosphors absorb ambient light and re-emit it over time (phosphorescence). | Semiconductor p–n junction emits photons under forward DC bias. |
| **Power Required** | Continuous AC supply. | None (passive, charges from ambient light). | Continuous DC supply. |
| **Brightness** | Medium, uniform glow. | Low, decays over 3–10 hours. | Very high, focused. |
| **Lifetime** | ~5,000 h. | >10 years (no electronics). | >50,000 h. |
| **Smoke Visibility** | Good. | Moderate. | Excellent. |
| **Limitation** | Needs inverter; degrades with humidity. | Needs charging light source; dim in pitch dark. | Higher cost; needs heat-sinking in enclosed strips. |

**Conclusion:** LED strips dominate modern new-builds; PL strips are used as supplementary passive markings. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Three-Tier Power Fallback Logic (7 marks):**

```
TIER 1 — Normal Operation:
   LLL is energized from the MAIN switchboard via the 
   emergency switchboard bus-tie. PSU supplies regulated 
   DC to all corridor/stairway strips.

TIER 2 — Main Power Failure:
   The Emergency Generator (diesel) auto-starts within 
   45 seconds and re-energises the Emergency Switchboard. 
   LLL remains lit without interruption because the PSU 
   is fed from the Emergency Switchboard.

TIER 3 — Catastrophic Blackout (both Main and Emergency 
   Generator fail, e.g., flooding of E/R):
   The DEDICATED BATTERY BANK automatically takes over 
   the LLL load via a changeover relay. The strips 
   continue to operate for the full autonomy period 
   (3 hours minimum) ensuring crew can still locate 
   and reach lifeboats.
```

**Diagram (text representation):**

```
[ Main Board ] ──┐
                 ├──► [ Emergency Switchboard ] ──► [ PSU ] ──► [ LLL Strips ]
[ Emerg. Gen ]  ─┘                                  ▲
                                                     │
                                          [ Battery Bank 24V DC ]
                                          (failover if both fail)
```

This three-tier design ensures that **no single failure** can extinguish the escape path, satisfying the SOLAS philosophy of "defence in depth" for life-saving systems. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** state the LLL height as "anywhere on the wall" — it must be **≤ 300 mm from deck**. Marks lost for wrong height.
> 2. **Do NOT** forget the 1.25× ageing margin in battery sizing — KTU Board examiners *expect* this safety factor.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse LLL with the supplementary emergency lighting; LLL is a **dedicated** system with its **own battery**.
> 4. **Do NOT** quote colour as "any colour"; **green is mandatory** under SOLAS / ISO 15370.
> 5. **Do NOT** draw diagrams without labelling all three power sources (Main / Emergency / Battery) — full 7 marks depend on this.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** LLL = low-height emergency escape lighting, installed **≤ 300 mm above deck**.
- ✅ **Regulation:** **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 13** + **IMO Res. A.752(18)** + **ISO 15370**.
- ✅ **Purpose:** Visible escape guidance under **smoke-filled** conditions (smoke rises, light stays low).
- ✅ **Color:** **Green** with white directional arrows/door symbols.
- ✅ **Autonomy:** Minimum **3 hours** continuous operation from dedicated battery.
- ✅ **Pictogram Spacing:** Every **≤ 2 metres** along escape route.
- ✅ **Minimum Illuminance:** ≥ **1 lux at deck level**.
- ✅ **Power Source Hierarchy:** Main → Emergency Generator → **Dedicated Battery Bank**.
- ✅ **Technologies:** **Electroluminescent (EL)**, **Photoluminescent (PL)**, and **LED** strips.
- ✅ **Mandatory For:** All passenger ships (especially ro-ro) under SOLAS.
- ✅ **Battery Sizing Formula:** $C_{bat} = \dfrac{I_{load} \times T}{k_{dis}} \times 1.25$ (always apply ageing margin).
- ✅ **Always mention** the three-tier fallback logic in any descriptive answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Emergency Fire Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed Emergency Fire Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fixed Emergency Fire Pump (FEFP)** is a SOLAS-mandated, independently powered, permanently installed centrifugal fire pump, located outside the main machinery space, designed to deliver seawater to the ship's fire main under emergency conditions when the main fire pumps and main power source are unavailable due to fire, flooding, or electrical failure.

Per **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 10.2.2.2**, the emergency fire pump forms the last line of defence in the fire-fighting chain on board a vessel and is governed by the International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) and the FSS Code (Fire Safety Systems Code), Chapter 5.

> [!NOTE]
> The pump is classified as "Fixed" because it is permanently piped to the fire main and is **not portable**. It is "Emergency" because it is reserved exclusively for fire-fighting use during casualty scenarios.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a large hospital complex where the **main water pumps (main fire pumps)** are located in the basement (engine room). If a major fire breaks out in the basement, all these pumps stop. To handle this, hospitals install a **separate outdoor water pump with its own diesel engine** that can be started from outside, even if the building loses all electricity. This is exactly what the **Emergency Fire Pump** is on a ship — the "outdoor backup pump with its own engine" sitting in a completely different fire zone.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Minimum Capacity (Ships ≥ 1000 GT) | **25 m³/h** |
| Minimum Capacity (Ships < 1000 GT) | **15 m³/h** |
| Minimum Hydrant Pressure | **0.25 N/mm² (2.5 bar)** |
| Density of Seawater (ρ) | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Acceleration due to gravity (g) | **9.81 m/s²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 SOLAS Operational Mandate — The Five Pillars

The SOLAS framework requires the emergency fire pump to satisfy five non-negotiable design pillars:

1. **Independence of Power** — Power source (diesel engine or emergency-electric motor) must be independent of the ship's main source of electrical power.
2. **Geographic Separation** — Located in a space **outside the main machinery space** (Category A machinery space) and not adjacent to it.
3. **Sea Connection Independence** — Sea suction must be from a sea inlet **separate from the main sea inlet** (where practicable), positioned below the load waterline.
4. **Rapid Starting** — Capable of being brought into operation within **30 minutes** from a dead-ship condition (cold start).
5. **Self-Priming Capability** — If located above the load waterline, must be self-priming or equipped with an approved priming system.

### 2.2 Pump Type and Driver Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pump Type:** Almost universally a **centrifugal pump** (single-stage, end-suction type).
> **Driver:** A **diesel engine** is the most common and preferred driver because it is independent of electrical power. Electric motor-driven emergency pumps are permitted only if powered from the **emergency switchboard**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | m³/h or m³/s | Capacity of pump |
| Pressure-Head Conversion | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$ | Pa (N/m²) | H in metres, ρ in kg/m³ |
| Hydrant Pressure Check | $P_{hydrant} \geq 2.5 \times 10^{5}$ | Pa | SOLAS minimum |
| Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Watts | Useful power delivered |
| Pump Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{P_{shaft}}$ | dimensionless | $\times 100$ for % |
| Velocity of Jet | $v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot H}$ | m/s | Torricelli's theorem |
| Discharge through Nozzle | $Q_{n} = C_{d} \cdot A_{n} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot H}$ | m³/s | $C_{d} \approx 0.97$ |
| SOLAS Min Capacity Rule | $Q_{min} = \max(25,\ 0.4 \times Q_{main})$ | m³/h | Whichever is greater for ≥ 1000 GT |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use SI base units. The hydrant-pressure check is the **only practical numerical test** during survey, converting pressure to equivalent head using $H = P / (\rho g)$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The emergency fire pump is the cornerstone of the **Casualty Control Concept** under SOLAS Chapter II-2. After catastrophic events such as the *MV Herald of Free Enterprise* and *RMS Titanic*, regulators mandated geometric and functional separation of emergency services from main machinery to ensure survivability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Converting SOLAS Pressure to Equivalent Head

The SOLAS rule states that any hydrant must deliver at least **0.25 N/mm² (= 2.5 bar)** at the required flow. To verify this using a manometer or tank-level analogy, we convert this pressure to metres of seawater head.

$$
P_{hydrant} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{head}
$$

Rearranging for head:

$$
H_{head} = \dfrac{P_{hydrant}}{\rho \cdot g}
$$

Substituting $P_{hydrant} = 0.25 \times 10^{6}\ \text{N/m}^2$, $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$:

$$
H_{head} = \dfrac{0.25 \times 10^{6}}{1025 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
H_{head} = \dfrac{250000}{10055.25}
$$

$$
H_{head} \approx 24.86\ \text{metres}
$$

> **Logic:** SOLAS 2.5 bar is therefore roughly equivalent to a 25 m water column — that is the source of the often-quoted "minimum 25 m head" rule.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Pump Discharge Pressure at the Pump Outlet

The hydrant is typically located at the **uppermost deck**, far above the pump. The pump must therefore overcome:

1. Static head ($H_{static}$) — vertical distance from pump to highest hydrant.
2. Friction losses ($H_{f}$) — pipe-wall and fitting losses.
3. Nozzle pressure head ($H_{nozzle}$).

Total dynamic head at pump outlet:

$$
H_{total} = H_{static} + H_{f} + H_{nozzle}
$$

Expressing total pump delivery pressure:

$$
P_{pump} = \rho \cdot g \cdot \left(H_{static} + H_{f} + \dfrac{P_{hydrant}}{\rho \cdot g}\right)
$$

Simplifying:

$$
P_{pump} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{static} + \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{f} + P_{hydrant}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A bulk carrier has its emergency fire pump installed **8 m below** the load waterline (LWL). The highest hydrant is on the bridge deck, **22 m above LWL**. Pipe length from pump to highest hydrant = **85 m**, with friction factor $f = 0.025$ and pipe diameter $D = 0.15\ \text{m}$. The pump must deliver **25 m³/h**. Compute the required pump delivery pressure.

**Step 1: Static head**
$$
H_{static} = 22 - (-8) = 30\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2: Velocity in pipe**
$$
Q = 25\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} = \dfrac{25}{3600} = 6.944 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
A_{pipe} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{6.944 \times 10^{-3}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 0.393\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Friction head loss (Darcy–Weisbach)**
$$
H_{f} = \dfrac{f \cdot L \cdot v^{2}}{2 \cdot g \cdot D}
$$

$$
H_{f} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 85 \times (0.393)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15}
$$

$$
H_{f} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 85 \times 0.1545}{2.943}
$$

$$
H_{f} \approx \dfrac{0.3283}{2.943} \approx 0.1116\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4: Nozzle/pressure head at hydrant (SOLAS min)**
$$
H_{nozzle} = 24.86\ \text{m} \quad \text{(from previous derivation)}
$$

**Step 5: Total head**
$$
H_{total} = 30 + 0.1116 + 24.86 \approx 54.97\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 6: Pump delivery pressure**
$$
P_{pump} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 54.97
$$

$$
P_{pump} \approx 552840\ \text{Pa} \approx 5.53\ \text{bar}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The pump must deliver approximately **5.53 bar** at its outlet to satisfy SOLAS at the highest hydrant.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Emergency vs Main Fire Pump

| Feature | Main Fire Pump | Emergency Fire Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Engine room | Outside main machinery space |
| Driver | Electric motor (main bus) | Diesel engine (independent) |
| Min Capacity | Per SOLAS formula | **25 m³/h** (≥1000 GT) |
| Sea Suction | Main sea chest | **Separate** sea inlet |
| Power Independence | NO | **YES** |
| Starting Time | Immediate (electrical) | ≤ 30 min from cold |
| Survey Test | Per Class rules | **Annual survey** by Class |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ship Fire Emergency] --> B{Catastrophic Event Detected}
    B --> C[Main Machinery Space Inaccessible]
    B --> D[Main Power Failure]
    C --> E[Activate Emergency Fire Pump]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Start Diesel Engine from Remote Location]
    F --> G[Self-Priming or Direct Sea Suction]
    G --> H[Sea Inlet Below LWL]
    H --> I[Centrifugal Pump Impeller]
    I --> J[Fire Main Ring System]
    J --> K1[Hydrant - Bridge Deck]
    J --> K2[Hydrant - Accommodation]
    J --> K3[Hydrant - Cargo Deck]
    K1 --> L[Fire Hose and Nozzle]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[Pressure Check >= 2.5 bar at Hydrant]
    M --> N[Fire Suppression Achieved]
    style E fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style I fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 System Topology — Power and Flow Independence

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        M1[Main Engine Room]

    end

    end
    M4 -. Power Loss .-> E2

    style EMER fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
```
