
### 4.3 Pump Suction and Discharge Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sea - Below LWL] --> B[Sea Strainer]
    B --> C[Emergency Sea Chest Valve]
    C --> D[Self-Priming Device or Foot Valve]
    D --> E[Centrifugal Pump Casing]
    E --> F[Discharge Check Valve]
    F --> G[Fire Main Isolation Valve]
    G --> H[Fire Main Ring Main]
    H --> I1[Hydrant 1 - Upper Deck]
    H --> I2[Hydrant 2 - Bridge]
    H --> I3[Hydrant 3 - Engine Room]
    E --> J[Pressure Gauge on Discharge]
    E --> K[Diesel Engine Drive]
    K --> L[Fuel Tank - Independent]
    L --> K
    style E fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#ff9f43,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#ee5253,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
*State the SOLAS-mandated minimum capacity and the minimum pressure at any hydrant for the fixed emergency fire pump on board a ship of 2000 GRT.*

**Model Answer:**
As per SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 10.2.2.2:

> **Minimum Capacity:** **25 m³/h** (since GRT ≥ 1000)
> **Minimum Hydrant Pressure:** **0.25 N/mm² (≈ 2.5 bar)**

This ensures simultaneous operation of at least two fire hoses while maintaining effective jet throw at the nozzle. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**
*List the three essential design requirements that make a fire pump an "emergency" fire pump under SOLAS.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Independent Power Source** — driven by a diesel engine or emergency-electric motor.
2. **Geographical Separation** — installed outside the main machinery space.
3. **Independent Sea Suction** — sea inlet separate from main sea chest.

These ensure survivability during main-space casualty. *(3 marks)*

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the complete operational layout of a fixed emergency fire pump system on a cargo ship, clearly showing the diesel driver, pump, sea suction, priming arrangement, fire main and hydrants. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the SOLAS-mandated starting and capacity requirements for the emergency fire pump. Why is a 30-minute starting limit imposed? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Operational Layout *(7 marks)*

The emergency fire pump system comprises the following functional chain:

1. **Sea Inlet (Emergency Sea Chest):** Positioned below the load waterline in the fore or aft region, geometrically separated from the main sea chest to ensure redundancy.

2. **Sea Strainer and Foot Valve:** A coarse strainer removes debris; a foot valve retains water in the suction line for re-priming.

3. **Priming Device:** A self-priming ejector or vacuum pump primes the pump when located above the waterline.

4. **Centrifugal Pump (end-suction type):** The pump is single-stage, constructed of corrosion-resistant bronze or stainless steel.

5. **Diesel Engine Driver:** A marine-grade diesel engine with its own independent fuel tank, batteries, cooling (sea or air), and exhaust system, capable of running on fuel oil.

6. **Discharge Check Valve and Isolation Valve:** Prevents backflow into the fire main.

7. **Fire Main Ring System:** Interconnected loop running fore-to-aft, supplying all hydrants.

8. **International Shore Connection (ISC):** Allows cross-connection with shore fire services.

9. **Remote Starting Point:** Located outside the machinery space boundary, allowing crew to start the pump without entering the casualty zone.

*(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.3 above)*

**(b)** Starting & Capacity Requirements *(7 marks)*

**Capacity:**
- ≥ **25 m³/h** for ships of 1000 GT and above.
- ≥ **15 m³/h** for ships below 1000 GT.
- Pressure ≥ 2.5 bar at the most remote hydrant.

**Starting Requirements:**
- The pump must be capable of being started from a **readily accessible position** outside the main machinery space boundary.
- Cold-start within **30 minutes** from a dead-ship condition (no steam, no power, no compressed air — only batteries and fuel).
- Pre-lubrication may use a hand pump or electric pre-lubricator from emergency power.

**Rationale for 30-Minute Limit:**
This limit originates from casualty analysis showing that fire escalation is manageable within 30 minutes if fire-fighting efforts begin promptly. The regulation balances engineering practicality (diesel engine warm-up, priming, hydraulic stabilisation) with the urgency of fire response. It also forces designers to specify a pump that is **reliably startable** without auxiliary services.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast the Fixed Emergency Fire Pump with the Main Fire Pump under the following headings: location, driver, sea suction, capacity, and survey interval. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A ship's emergency fire pump has its suction point 6 m below LWL and the highest hydrant is 24 m above LWL. The discharge pipe is 100 m long with diameter 150 mm and Darcy friction factor 0.025. Calculate the pump discharge pressure required to deliver 30 m³/h while maintaining 2.5 bar at the highest hydrant. Take seawater density = 1025 kg/m³. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Comparison Table *(7 marks)*

| Heading | Main Fire Pump | Emergency Fire Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Inside E/R (Category A space) | Outside main machinery space |
| Driver | Electric motor on main switchboard | Diesel engine (independent) |
| Sea Suction | Main sea chest | Separate sea inlet |
| Capacity | Per SOLAS formula (typically 2 × 50% rule) | 25 m³/h (≥ 1000 GT) |
| Survey Interval | Per Class routine | Annual survey + operational test |
| Starting | Automatic from power | Manual remote start within 30 min |

*(Marks distributed across the five comparison points plus an introductory and concluding remark)*

**(b)** Numerical Calculation *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $H_{static} = 24 + 6 = 30\ \text{m}$; $L = 100\ \text{m}$; $D = 0.15\ \text{m}$; $f = 0.025$; $Q = 30\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$.

**Step 1: Convert flow rate**
$$
Q = \dfrac{30}{3600} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Pipe cross-sectional area**
$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^{2} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Velocity**
$$
v = \dfrac{8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 0.4716\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Friction head loss**
$$
H_{f} = \dfrac{f \cdot L \cdot v^{2}}{2 \cdot g \cdot D} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 100 \times (0.4716)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15}
$$

$$
H_{f} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 100 \times 0.2224}{2.943} \approx \dfrac{0.5560}{2.943} \approx 0.189\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 5: Pressure head at hydrant**
$$
H_{nozzle} = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{0.25 \times 10^{6}}{1025 \times 9.81} \approx 24.86\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 6: Total head**
$$
H_{total} = 30 + 0.189 + 24.86 \approx 55.05\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 7: Required pump discharge pressure**
$$
P_{pump} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{total} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 55.05
$$

$$
P_{pump} \approx 553660\ \text{Pa} \approx 5.54\ \text{bar}
$$

**Answer: Required pump delivery pressure ≈ 5.54 bar**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Forgetting to add the static head $(H_{static})$ to the SOLAS nozzle head. Many students treat the 2.5 bar as the *pump* pressure, leading to massive underestimation.
> 2. Mixing units — using $Q$ in m³/h directly in SI formulas. Always convert to m³/s first.
> 3. Forgetting to include the **negative static head** when the pump is below the waterline. This actually *assists* the suction, but the discharge side always needs full static + friction + nozzle head.
> 4. Confusing the emergency fire pump capacity (25 m³/h) with the main fire pump capacity (often 50–180 m³/h).
> 5. Failing to mention the **30-minute cold-start** rule — this is the single most tested SOLAS fact.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** 📌

- **SOLAS basis:** Chapter II-2, Regulation 10.2.2.2.
- **Min Capacity:** **25 m³/h** (≥ 1000 GT); **15 m³/h** (< 1000 GT).
- **Min Pressure:** **0.25 N/mm² (2.5 bar)** at any hydrant while two hoses are running.
- **Driver:** Diesel engine preferred (power-independent).
- **Location:** **Outside** main machinery space; **separate** sea suction.
- **Starting:** Remotely startable from outside; cold-start within **30 minutes**.
- **Pump Type:** Centrifugal, end-suction, single-stage.
- **Test Frequency:** **Weekly** operational check; **Annual** Class survey.
- **Materials:** Bronze / stainless steel casings for seawater compatibility.
- **Head-pressure conversion:** $P(\text{Pa}) = 1025 \times 9.81 \times H(\text{m})$.
- **International Shore Connection (ISC)** is mandatory and forms part of the fixed emergency fire pump system architecture.
- **Fuel supply** for the diesel driver must be independent of main bunker lines — a dedicated day-tank or service-tank take-off with non-return valve.
- **Cooling** of the diesel driver can be sea-water or radiator (air-cooled) — both are acceptable if independent.
- **Surveyor trick question:** The pump must be capable of supplying water at the required pressure **simultaneously with** two fire hoses operating at the most remote hydrant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrangement of Means of Escape
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arrangement of Means of Escape

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (SOLAS-Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Means of Escape** refers to the continuous, unobstructed, and protected path of travel provided on a vessel from any occupied space (accommodation, service, machinery, or cargo space) to a place of safety, namely an **open deck**, an **embarkation station**, or a **lifeboat/liferaft embarkation deck**.

The regulatory backbone for this topic is **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 13** (Construction – Arrangements for Means of Escape). For **passenger ships**, the stricter provisions of **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 28** (Means of Escape from Accommodation Spaces, Service Spaces, and Machinery Spaces) apply, while **cargo ships** are governed by **Regulation 44** of the same chapter.

The arrangement must ensure:
1. At least **two (2) means of escape** from every occupied space or deck level.
2. Escape routes must be as **widely separated as practicable** to minimize the risk of a single incident blocking both.
3. Routes must be **fire-rated**, **smoke-free** where required, and lead to a **safe muster area**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Building Evacuation Plan:**
> Imagine a multi-storey office building. Every room must have **two exit doors** placed at opposite ends, so that if a fire blocks one, the other is still usable. These doors lead into protected corridors → staircases → ground floor → outside assembly point. **Ships follow the same logic**, but with added marine complications: rolling motion, darkness, smoke, and the "assembly point" being the **lifeboat deck** at the ship's side.

| Key Term | Plain English Meaning |
|----------|----------------------|
| Place of Safety | The **open deck** where lifeboats are launched |
| Embarkation Station | Where passengers/crew muster and board survival craft |
| Protected Route | An escape path shielded by **fire-resisting bulkheads and decks** |
| Vertical Escape | Moving **up or down** via stairways/ladders |
| Horizontal Escape | Moving **sideways** through corridors/halls |

## 1.3 Why This Matters in Maritime Safety

- **Smoke and toxic gases** spread rapidly on ships due to confined geometry and HVAC ducting.
- The **Ro-Ro ferry disasters** (e.g., *MV Estonia, 1994*) showed that poor escape arrangements cause catastrophic loss of life.
- Regulatory compliance is verified by **classification societies** (LR, DNV, ABS, BV) and port state control officers.
- **Standard Metric:** Escape corridor width minimum **900 mm**; door width minimum **650 mm** (clear opening).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Three-Tier" Escape Architecture

The arrangement of means of escape on a SOLAS-regulated vessel is organized into **three nested tiers**:

### Tier I – From the Compartment of Origin
- **At least two exits** from every accommodation space, service space, or control station.
- Exceptions: small spaces (e.g., private cabins < 10 m²) with a single exit, provided the escape distance to a stairway is short.

### Tier II – From the Deck Level
- Two escape routes **as widely separated as possible** (preferably fore-aft extremes).
- One route may be via a window/porthole to an adjacent deck **only if approved**.
- **Vertical escape** is provided by stairways enclosed in **A-class fire-rated trunks**.

### Tier III – From the Ship
- Routes converge on the **muster/embarkation deck**.
- **Embarkation stations** must be positioned so that lifeboats can be boarded safely even with a **15° list** and **10° trim**.

## 2.2 Number of Means of Escape – Decision Logic

```
STEP 1: Identify the space (accommodation, service, machinery, control).
STEP 2: Check passenger ship vs cargo ship applicability.
STEP 3: Verify area of space → triggers single vs double exit rule.
STEP 4: Verify distance to nearest stairway ≤ 40 m (or per SOLAS table).
STEP 5: Confirm vertical escape provision via A-class stairway.
STEP 6: Verify embarkation station access ≤ 45 m walking distance.
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metrics under SOLAS Reg. II-2/13 & Reg. II-2/28:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Notes |
|-----------|--------|----------------|-------|
| Minimum corridor width | $W_c$ | **900 mm** | Clear walkway |
| Minimum door clear opening | $W_d$ | **650 mm** | Single door |
| Minimum stairway width | $W_s$ | **900 mm** | For passenger ships |
| Maximum escape distance | $L_{max}$ | **45 m** | To stairway or embarkation |
| Fire rating of escape trunk | $FR$ | **A-60 / A-30** | Class A division |
| Allowed ship list at muster | $\theta_{list}$ | **15°** | Embarkation still possible |
| Allowed trim | $\theta_{trim}$ | **10°** | Embankation deck usable |

## 2.4 Escape from Machinery Spaces (Special Case)

For main propulsion machinery spaces of **Category A** (containing main propulsion boilers, internal combustion engines, etc.):

- **Two means of escape** must be provided.
- One escape must be a **vertical escape** through a protected trunk leading directly to an open deck.
- The second may be a **horizontal escape** through a watertight door leading to another machinery space with independent escape.
- **Hot surfaces** near ladders must be insulated (Class A-60).

## 2.5 Special Considerations for Ro-Ro / Vehicle Spaces

For Ro-Ro cargo spaces and special category spaces:

- **Two means of escape** must be provided, widely separated.
- One escape must lead to a stairway enclosed in **A-class divisions**.
- Must be accessible **at all times during voyage and port stay**.
- Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) stations must be provided nearby.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Safety Engineering**: Escape route analysis underpins CFD smoke modelling and evacuation simulation (e.g., Maritime Research Institute Netherlands – MARIN).
- **Human Factors**: Evacuation modelling uses parameters like walking speed $v = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$ on stairs (horizontal) and $v = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$ (vertical descent).
- **Regulatory Auditing**: Every newbuilding must undergo a **Means of Escape review** by the Class surveyor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Number of Escape Routes

The general rule for a passenger ship accommodation deck is derived as follows:

$$
N_{escape} \geq \max(2, \; \lceil N_{occupants} / N_{threshold} \rceil)
$$

Where:
- $N_{escape}$ = Number of independent escape routes.
- $N_{occupants}$ = Maximum number of persons normally accommodated in that deck.
- $N_{threshold}$ = Threshold value (typically **60 persons per stairway** for passenger ships).

**Step-by-step logical derivation:**

```
STEP 1: Start with the SOLAS baseline rule → "at least 2 means of escape".
STEP 2: If the deck accommodates > 60 persons → 1 stairway is insufficient.
STEP 3: Calculate additional stairways needed:
         N_extra = ceil( (N_occupants - 60) / 60 )
STEP 4: Total escapes:
         N_total = 2 + N_extra (rounded up)
STEP 5: Cross-check with REGULATION 28.1.4 (passenger ships) or REG. 44 (cargo ships).
```

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Escape Travel Distance

The travel distance $L$ from any point in an accommodation space to the nearest stairway is governed by:

$$
L \leq L_{max} = 45 \; \text{m}
$$

**Reasoning chain:**

```
STEP 1: Smoke filling time for a typical cabin corridor ≈ 2-3 minutes.
STEP 2: Average walking speed under reduced visibility = 0.7 m/s.
STEP 3: Maximum safe distance in smoke:  L = v × t = 0.7 × 60 ≈ 42 m.
STEP 4: Apply safety factor of ~1.07 → SOLAS-prescribed 45 m.
```

This gives the analytical basis for the **45 m rule** cited in SOLAS Reg. II-2/28.1.6.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Means of Escape by Ship Type and Space

| Ship Type | Space | Primary Escape | Secondary Escape | Governing Reg. |
|-----------|-------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Passenger Ship** | Cabin / Stateroom | Corridor → A-class stairway | Window / balcony to adjacent deck | II-2/28.1.5 |
| **Passenger Ship** | Public space (> 60 pax) | Two stairways at fore/aft ends | Direct door to open deck (if deck level) | II-2/28.1.4 |
| **Cargo Ship** | Crew cabin | Corridor → stairway | Adjacent cabin via watertight door | II-2/44 |
| **All Ships** | Machinery Space Cat. A | Vertical escape trunk (A-class) | Watertight door to adjacent space | II-2/13.4.1 |
| **Ro-Ro Ship** | Vehicle deck | Forward enclosed stairway | Aft enclosed stairway | II-2/28.1.10 |
| **All Ships** | Galley | Main corridor exit | Service corridor / back door | II-2/13 |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Escape Provision for a 3-Deck Accommodation Block

**Given:**
- Passenger ferry with 3 accommodation decks.
- Deck C: 150 persons, Deck B: 180 persons, Deck A: 90 persons.
- Stairway capacity $N_{threshold} = 60$ persons.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deck C: } N_{total} &= 2 + \lceil (150 - 60)/60 \rceil \\
&= 2 + \lceil 1.5 \rceil \\
&= 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ stairways}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deck B: } N_{total} &= 2 + \lceil (180 - 60)/60 \rceil \\
&= 2 + \lceil 2.0 \rceil \\
&= 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ stairways}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deck A: } N_{total} &= 2 + \lceil (90 - 60)/60 \rceil \\
&= 2 + \lceil 0.5 \rceil \\
&= 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ stairways}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The vessel must be fitted with **at least 4 stairways serving Decks B and C**, and **3 stairways serving Deck A**, arranged symmetrically about the midship line for maximum separation.

## 3.5 Time-Based Evacuation Estimate (TEE)

Approximate evacuation time:

$$
T_{evac} = \frac{L}{v_{horiz}} + \frac{H}{v_{vert}} + t_{muster}
$$

Where:
- $L$ = horizontal travel distance (m).
- $v_{horiz}$ = horizontal walking speed ≈ **1.0 m/s**.
- $H$ = vertical climb height (m).
- $v_{vert}$ = vertical climbing speed ≈ **0.5 m/s**.
- $t_{muster}$ = muster delay ≈ **5 min**.

**Example:** For $L = 30 \text{ m}$ and $H = 15 \text{ m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{evac} &= \frac{30}{1.0} + \frac{15}{0.5} + 300 \\
&= 30 + 30 + 300 \\
&= 360 \; \text{s} \; = \; 6 \; \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This must be less than the **fire endurance rating** of the escape trunk (typically A-60 = 60 min), providing a **10× safety margin**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Means of Escape – Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Occupied Space: Cabin Public Area Service Space] --> B{Space Type and Size}
    B -->|Single Cabin < 10 sqm| C1[Single Exit Acceptable]
    B -->|Public or Large Space| C2[Two Exits Required]
    C1 --> D1[Main Door to Corridor]
    C2 --> D2[Exit 1: Main Door]
    C2 --> D3[Exit 2: Alternate Door Window or Balcony]
    D1 --> E1[Corridor: Min Width 900 mm]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E2[Alternate Path to Adjacent Deck]
    E1 --> F{A Class Stairway Trunk}
    E2 --> F
    F -->|Fire Rated A60 A30| G[Vertical Travel to Embarkation Deck]
    G --> H[Embarkation Station: Muster Area]
    H --> I[Lifeboat Liferaft Embarkation]
    I --> J[Open Deck: Place of Safety]
    K[Machinery Space Cat A] --> L[Vertical Escape Trunk]
    K --> M[Watertight Door to Adjacent Space]
    L --> J
    M --> N[Independent Escape Route]
    N --> J
    P[Ro Ro Vehicle Deck] --> Q[Forward A Class Stairway]
    P --> R[Aft A Class Stayrway]
    Q --> J
    R --> J
```

## 4.2 SOLAS Regulation Mapping Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    SOLAS[SOLAS Chapter II 2] --> R13[Reg 13: Means of Escape General]
    SOLAS --> R28[Reg 28: Passenger Ship Detailed]
    SOLAS --> R44[Reg 44: Cargo Ship Detailed]
    R13 --> S1[Two Means of Escape Principle]
    R13 --> S2[Fire Integrity A Class]
    R28 --> S3[45 m Travel Distance Limit]
    R28 --> S4[Embarkation Station Provision]
    R44 --> S5[Crew Accommodation Standards]
    R44 --> S6[Machinery Space Cat A Rules]
    S1 --> SHIP[Ship Type Application]
    S2 --> SHIP
    S3 --> SHIP
    S4 --> SHIP
    S5 --> SHIP
    S6 --> SHIP
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description (Ship Cross-Section View)

```
            OPEN DECK / EMBARKATION DECK  (PLACE OF SAFETY)
            ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
            │   ▢ STBD Lifeboat       ▢ PORT Lifeboat   │
            │   ▢ Liferaft            ▢ Liferaft        │
            └─────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ▲   ▲   ▲   ▲
                          │   │   │   │
            ┌─────────────┴───┴───┴───┴─────────────┐
            │       A-CLASS STAIRWAY TRUNK (×2)     │  ← VERTICAL ESCAPE
            │  ┌──────────┐         ┌──────────┐    │
            │  │ STAIR A  │         │ STAIR B  │    │
            │  │  (FWD)   │         │ (AFT)    │    │
            │  └──────────┘         └──────────┘    │
            ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
            │   ACCOMMODATION DECK – 45 m MAX        │
            │   ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐    │  ← HORIZONTAL ESCAPE
            │   │CAB01│ │CAB02│ │HALL │ │CAB03│    │     (corridor width 900 mm)
            │   └──┬──┘ └──┬──┘ └──┬──┘ └──┬──┘    │
            │      └───────┴───────┴────────┘       │
            └──────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ▲   ▲   ▲   ▲
                          │   │   │   │
            ┌─────────────┴───┴───┴───┴─────────────┐
            │  MACHINERY SPACE CAT. A               │
            │  E1: Vertical Escape Trunk            │  ← TWO ESCAPES
            │  E2: Watertight Door to Adjacent Space│
            └──────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*Define the term "Means of Escape" as applied under SOLAS Chapter II-2.*

**Model Answer:**
Means of Escape refers to the continuous, protected path of travel provided on a vessel from any occupied space (accommodation, service, machinery or cargo space) to a **Place of Safety**, which is typically the **open deck** or **lifeboat embarkation deck**. Under SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 13 (general) and Regulation 28 (passenger ships), at least **two widely separated means of escape** must be provided from every occupied space or deck level, leading through fire-rated corridors and A-class stairways to the muster/embarkation deck. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*State the minimum width of an escape corridor and the maximum permitted travel distance to a stairway on a passenger ship.*

**Model Answer:**
- Minimum corridor clear width: **900 mm** (1 mark).
- Maximum travel distance from any point in an accommodation space to the nearest stairway: **45 m** (1 mark).
- Stairway minimum width: **900 mm**; door clear opening: **650 mm** (1 mark).

These values are derived from SOLAS Reg. II-2/28 and validated by evacuation modelling assuming smoke visibility limits.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

#### (a) Explain the general principles governing the arrangement of means of escape on board ships as per SOLAS Chapter II-2 Regulation 13. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The general principles of SOLAS Reg. II-2/13 are:

1. **At Least Two Means of Escape (3 marks):**
   Every occupied space (accommodation, service, control station, machinery space) must have **at least two** independent escape routes. This prevents a single fire incident from trapping occupants.

2. **Wide Separation of Routes (2 marks):**
   The two escape routes must be positioned at the **widely separated extremities** of the space (e.g., fore and aft stairways), so that a localised fire cannot block both simultaneously.

3. **Fire-Rated Construction (1 mark):**
   Escape stairways and trunks must be enclosed by **A-class fire divisions** (typically A-30 or A-60), ensuring integrity for 30–60 minutes under standard fire test conditions.

4. **Embarkation Stations (1 mark):**
   Escape routes terminate at embarkation stations which must remain accessible at a ship list of up to **15°** and trim of **10°**.

#### (b) Discuss the special requirements for means of escape from Category A Machinery Spaces and Ro-Ro Vehicle Spaces. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Category A Machinery Spaces (4 marks):**
1. Two independent means of escape must be provided.
2. **One escape must be vertical** – a protected escape trunk leading directly to an open deck. Hot surfaces on the ladder route must be insulated.
3. The **second escape** may be a horizontal watertight door leading to an adjacent machinery space from which a separate escape route is available.
4. The escape trunk must have a fire integrity of **A-60** and self-closing fire doors.

**Ro-Ro Vehicle Spaces (3 marks):**
1. Two widely separated stairways enclosed in A-class divisions must be provided.
2. Stairways must be **accessible at all times** during voyage, including during vehicle movement and loading.
3. **SCBA stations** and fireman's outfits must be located adjacent to escape routes.
4. Markings and emergency lighting (powered by the **emergency generator**) must illuminate escape routes continuously for at least **3 hours** after loss of main power.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the concept of "Place of Safety" and "Embarkation Station" with reference to SOLAS. Describe the marking and lighting requirements for escape routes. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Place of Safety (2 marks):**
A Place of Safety is the open deck area from which survival craft (lifeboats/liferafts) can be safely boarded and launched. It must be:
- Open to the atmosphere.
- Accessible even at **15° list** and **10° trim**.
- Free from fire and smoke hazards.

**Embarkation Station (2 marks):**
An Embarkation Station is the muster area adjacent to the survival craft. It must:
- Be wide enough to accommodate the muster of all assigned persons.
- Provide direct, unobstructed access to the lifeboat deck.
- Be marked with the **muster list** and assembly signals.

**Marking and Lighting (3 marks):**
1. **Photoluminescent (PL) signs** along escape routes – minimum height **100 mm**, running continuously from cabin to stairway to embarkation deck.
2. **Emergency lighting** powered by the emergency source of power, providing at least **1 lux** illumination on escape routes for **≥ 3 hours**.
3. Directional arrows showing the shortest path to safety; bilingual signage (English + national language) on passenger ships.

#### (b) An accommodation deck on a passenger ferry has 200 occupants. Using the SOLAS stairway capacity rule of 60 persons per stairway, calculate the minimum number of stairways required. Also, for a horizontal travel distance of 30 m and vertical climb of 12 m, estimate the total evacuation time. Take horizontal speed = 1.0 m/s, vertical speed = 0.5 m/s, muster delay = 5 min. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 – Number of Stairways (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 2 + \lceil (N_{occupants} - 60)/60 \rceil \\
&= 2 + \lceil (200 - 60)/60 \rceil \\
&= 2 + \lceil 140/60 \rceil \\
&= 2 + \lceil 2.333 \rceil \\
&= 2 + 3 = 5 \; \text{stairways}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum stairways required = 5**, arranged fore-aft symmetrically.

**Step 2 – Evacuation Time (4 marks):**

Horizontal component:

$$
T_h = \frac{L}{v_{horiz}} = \frac{30}{1.0} = 30 \; \text{s}
$$

Vertical component:

$$
T_v = \frac{H}{v_{vert}} = \frac{12}{0.5} = 24 \; \text{s}
$$

Muster delay:

$$
t_{muster} = 5 \; \text{min} = 300 \; \text{s}
$$

Total evacuation time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{evac} &= T_h + T_v + t_{muster} \\
&= 30 + 24 + 300 \\
&= 354 \; \text{s} \approx 5.9 \; \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The estimated total evacuation time is approximately **5 minutes 54 seconds**, which is well within the **A-60 fire integrity rating (60 minutes)**, satisfying SOLAS with a wide safety margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Skipping the "wide separation" principle** – mentioning only the *number* of escapes but not the *spatial separation*. This loses **2 marks** on a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing SOLAS Chapter II-2 Regulation 13 with Regulation 28** – students often quote the wrong regulation for passenger ships. *Rule of thumb*: Reg. 13 = General; Reg. 28 = Passenger Ship Detail; Reg. 44 = Cargo Ship Detail.
> 3. **Forgetting muster delay** in evacuation calculations – the $t_{muster} = 5 \text{ min}$ term is **mandatory**; omitting it results in loss of 1 mark.
> 4. **Not citing units** in numerical answers – always state **mm, m, seconds, or minutes** to gain full marks.
> 5. **Vague terminology** – "stairway" is acceptable; "stairs" or "steps" is not professional for SOLAS documentation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Two means of escape** is the universal SOLAS baseline.
- ✅ Routes must be **widely separated** (fore vs. aft).
- ✅ **A-class fire integrity** (A-30/A-60) for stairway trunks.
- ✅ **Corridor min width = 900 mm**, door min = **650 mm**.
- ✅ **Max travel distance = 45 m** to nearest stairway.
- ✅ **Machinery spaces Cat. A**: one vertical + one horizontal escape.
- ✅ **Ro-Ro decks**: two enclosed stairways accessible at all times.
- ✅ **Embarkation deck** must remain accessible at **15° list, 10° trim**.
- ✅ **Emergency lighting** must last **≥ 3 hours** at **≥ 1 lux**.
- ✅ **Stairway rule**: 1 stairway per **60 persons**, minimum 2.
- ✅ **Evacuation time formula**: $T_{evac} = L/v_h + H/v_v + t_{muster}$.
- ✅ Key regulations: **SOLAS II-2/13, II-2/28, II-2/44**.

> **End of Notes – Module 2: Arrangement of Means of Escape**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Deck Foam Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed Deck Foam Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fixed Deck Foam System** is a permanently installed fire-fighting installation on board ships and offshore platforms, designed to deliver a controlled quantity of foam solution onto the exposed weather deck (especially the cargo tank deck of oil/chemical tankers and the helicopter deck) to suppress, control, and ultimately extinguish fires involving flammable liquids (Class B fires). The system operates automatically or via remote manual activation and is governed by **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 10.5.6** and **IMO MSC.1/Circ.798 / MSC.1/Circ.1432** guidelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-style):** "A fixed foam installation on the weather deck comprises foam concentrate storage, proportioning equipment, fixed foam monitors, and deck foam applicators (troughs/sprayers) capable of delivering foam at a specified application rate over the entire deck area simultaneously."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine the deck of an oil tanker as a giant **circular pizza tray** filled with hot oil (representing the cargo). If the oil catches fire, throwing a blanket of dough over it is impractical. Instead, imagine a **ring of showerheads mounted along the rim of the tray**, each connected through a **metered mixing tap** to a giant soap-bubble reservoir. When fire breaks out, a single valve opens, and a **fluffy, oxygen-excluding blanket of foam** is gently poured over the entire burning surface, starving the flames of oxygen and cooling them simultaneously.

The "metered mixing tap" is the **foam proportioner**, the "showerheads" are the **deck foam monitors/applicators**, and the "soap-bubble reservoir" is the **foam concentrate tank**. The system is *fixed* because all these components are permanently plumbed into the ship's structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from CO₂ / Water Mist:**
> Foam does not *smother* fire by oxygen displacement like CO₂ alone. Instead, it forms a **persistent aqueous film** that (a) excludes oxygen, (b) cools the fuel surface, and (c) prevents reignition by sealing fuel vapours — a **triple-action** mechanism uniquely suited to liquid hydrocarbon fires on open decks.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Foam application rate (hydrocarbon cargo deck) | **6.5 L/min per m²** of deck area |
| Foam application rate (chemical/polar solvent deck, AR-AFFF) | **10–12 L/min per m²** |
| Minimum foam discharge duration | **20 minutes** (SOLAS minimum), often **30 min** |
| Foam expansion ratio | **Low (3:1 to 20:1)** for deck monitors |
| Proportioning ratio (AFFF) | **1%, 3%, or 6%** foam concentrate to water |
| Sea water supply temperature (operational) | **0 °C to 45 °C** |
| Design pressure at most remote applicator | **5 bar minimum** |

> [!TIP]
> Always memorize the **6.5 L/min/m² application rate** — this is the single most-tested numerical value in KTU board examinations on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — Operational Components

A fixed deck foam system is composed of five functionally distinct sub-systems:

1. **Water Supply Subsystem** — Sea chest → ballast/cooling pumps → foam system branch line. Provides the *continuous water carrier* for the foam solution.
2. **Foam Concentrate Storage** — Tanks of stainless steel, mild steel (coated), or GRP. Capacity sized for the worst-case scenario (largest protected area × duration).
3. **Proportioning Subsystem** — Inductors / bladder tanks / around-the-pump proportioners / balanced pressure proportioners. Mixes concentrate with water at a fixed ratio (1%, 3%, or 6%).
4. **Distribution Network** — Piping, valves, section valves, and deck foam fire main (typically stainless steel to resist corrosion).
5. **Discharge Subsystem** — Deck foam monitors (jet/applicator type), fixed foam applicators (deck troughs, pourers, spray nozzles), and foam pourers for cargo manifolds.

## 2.2 Operating Logic — Why and How Each Step Works

- **Step 1: Detection & Activation** — The fire detection panel (often flame/heat detectors in the cargo deck area) or manual call point triggers the foam system control valve.
- **Step 2: Pump Start** — The dedicated foam pump (or the fire main pump via diversion) starts automatically, supplying sea water at design pressure.
- **Step 3: Proportioning** — Water flows through the proportioner, creating a pressure differential that draws foam concentrate from the storage tank at a calibrated rate (e.g., 3 parts per 100 parts water for 3% AFFF).
- **Step 4: Mixing & Transport** — The foam solution (water + concentrate) travels through the deck foam fire main to the discharge devices.
- **Step 5: Aeration** — At the foam pourers/monitors, ambient air is entrained into the solution, expanding it into finished foam (typically 5:1 to 10:1 for deck applications).
- **Step 6: Application** — Foam is gently poured onto the burning surface, forming a coherent blanket.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "gentle application"?** High-velocity foam jets rupture foam bubbles, breaking the blanket's integrity. Deck monitors therefore use **non-air-aspirating pourers** or **low-expansion foam pourers** to lay foam softly — this is a *favourite examiner question*.

## 2.3 Classification of Foam Systems by Expansion Ratio

| Foam Type | Expansion Ratio | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Low Expansion | 3:1 – 20:1 | Deck fires, tank fires, jet fires |
| Medium Expansion | 20:1 – 200:1 | Engine rooms, pump rooms (flooding) |
| High Expansion | 200:1 – 1000:1 | Confined spaces, hold flooding |

**Deck applications almost universally use low-expansion foam** because:
- Higher density gives greater throw distance from monitors.
- Lower drift loss in wind on open decks.
- Faster knockdown on large vertical surfaces (e.g., a tanker's manifold tower).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Foam solution flow rate | $Q_s = A_d \times R_a$ | L/min | $A_d$ = deck area (m²), $R_a$ = application rate (L/min/m²) |
| 2 | Total foam solution for duration | $Q_{tot} = A_d \times R_a \times t$ | Litres | Multiply by time in minutes |
| 3 | Foam concentrate volume | $Q_c = Q_{tot} \times p_c$ | Litres | $p_c$ = proportioning ratio (e.g., 0.03 for 3%) |
| 4 | Foam concentrate mass | $m_c = Q_c \times \rho_c$ | kg | $\rho_c \approx 1.02$ kg/L for AFFF |
| 5 | Pump capacity | $Q_{pump} = Q_s \times SF$ | L/min | $SF$ = safety factor (1.15 – 1.25) |
| 6 | Discharge duration | $t = \dfrac{V_c}{Q_c \text{ per minute}}$ | minutes | $V_c$ = concentrate tank volume |
| 7 | Expansion ratio | $ER = \dfrac{V_{foam}}{V_{solution}}$ | dimensionless | Low: 3–20; Medium: 20–200; High: 200–1000 |
| 8 | Finished foam volume | $V_f = Q_{tot} \times ER$ | Litres | For blanketing thickness check |
| 9 | Deck area (rectangular tanker) | $A_d = L \times B$ | m² | $L$ = length, $B$ = breadth of cargo deck |
| 10 | Deck area (circular) | $A_d = \pi \times r^2$ | m² | For helideck foam systems |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil Tankers (OBOs, VLCCs, Product Carriers)** — Mandatory under SOLAS Reg. II-2/10.5.6 to protect the entire cargo deck.
- **Chemical Tankers** — Use **Alcohol-Resistant (AR-AFFF)** foam because polar solvents (methanol, acetone) destroy regular foam.
- **FPSOs & Offshore Platforms** — Helideck foam systems use fixed monitors plus ring-main pourers to deliver foam at **6.5 L/min/m² for 5 minutes minimum** (CAP 437 standard).
- **LNG / Gas Carriers** — Deck foam complements the dry chemical powder system as a secondary line of defence for jet fires.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Foam Concentrate Tank Sizing

The following derivation shows the complete chain from regulatory input → final tank capacity, as expected in KTU 14-mark problems.

### Given Data (Representative KTU Problem)

- Vessel: Product Tanker, length between perpendiculars $L = 180$ m, breadth $B = 32$ m.
- Foam concentrate: **AFFF 3%** type.
- Application rate (per SOLAS): $R_a = 6.5$ L/min/m².
- Discharge duration (chosen for margin): $t = 30$ min.
- Safety factor on pump: $SF = 1.20$.
- Density of AFFF: $\rho_c = 1.02$ kg/L.
- Expansion ratio to verify: $ER = 8{:}1$.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Protected Deck Area

The cargo deck area is taken as the rectangular footprint of the cargo tank region:

$$
A_d = L \times B
$$

$$
A_d = 180 \text{ m} \times 32 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_d = 5760 \text{ m}^2}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the gross area over which foam must be applied simultaneously to comply with SOLAS II-2/10.5.6 ("protect the entire cargo deck").

### Step 2 — Foam Solution Flow Rate

$$
Q_s = A_d \times R_a
$$

$$
Q_s = 5760 \text{ m}^2 \times 6.5 \text{ L/min/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_s = 37440 \text{ L/min} = 37.44 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the rate at which the system must produce *finished foam solution* (water + concentrate mixture) to satisfy the regulatory application rate.

### Step 3 — Total Foam Solution Volume Required

$$
Q_{tot} = A_d \times R_a \times t
$$

$$
Q_{tot} = 5760 \times 6.5 \times 30
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{tot} = 1{,}123{,}200 \text{ L} \approx 1123.2 \text{ m}^3}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the cumulative volume needed for 30 minutes of continuous operation at full regulatory rate.

### Step 4 — Foam Concentrate Volume (3% AFFF)

$$
Q_c = Q_{tot} \times p_c
$$

$$
Q_c = 1{,}123{,}200 \times 0.03
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_c = 33{,}696 \text{ L} \approx 33.7 \text{ m}^3}
$$

*Explanation:* For every 100 L of foam solution, 3 L is concentrate. This is the *minimum* tank capacity; shipyards typically round up by 10 % for unusable bottom volume.

### Step 5 — Foam Concentrate Mass

$$
m_c = Q_c \times \rho_c
$$

$$
m_c = 33{,}696 \text{ L} \times 1.02 \text{ kg/L}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_c = 34{,}369.92 \text{ kg} \approx 34.37 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

*Explanation:* Required for procurement, tank loading calculations, and stability/trim corrections.

### Step 6 — Pump Sizing (Including Safety Factor)

$$
Q_{pump} = Q_s \times SF
$$

$$
Q_{pump} = 37440 \times 1.20
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{pump} = 44928 \text{ L/min} \approx 44.93 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}}
$$

*Explanation:* Real pumps must overcome piping losses, leaks, and simultaneous dual-zone operations. A 20 % margin is standard maritime practice.

### Step 7 — Verification of Finished Foam Volume

$$
V_f = Q_{tot} \times ER
$$

$$
V_f = 1{,}123{,}200 \times 8
$$

$$
\boxed{V_f = 8{,}985{,}600 \text{ L} \approx 8985.6 \text{ m}^3}
$$

*Explanation:* A 1 cm thick blanket over the entire deck would require $5760 \times 0.01 = 57.6$ m³, so even with 8:1 expansion the produced foam volume vastly exceeds minimum blanket requirements — confirming that the application *rate* (not blanket *thickness*) governs the design.

### Summary of Numerical Outputs

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deck area | $A_d$ | 5760 | m² |
| Foam solution rate | $Q_s$ | 37440 | L/min |
| Total solution volume | $Q_{tot}$ | 1.123 × 10⁶ | L |
| Concentrate volume | $Q_c$ | 33696 | L |
| Concentrate mass | $m_c$ | 34370 | kg |
| Pump capacity | $Q_{pump}$ | 44928 | L/min |
| Finished foam volume | $V_f$ | 8.986 × 10⁶ | L |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Foam Type

| Hazard Scenario | Foam Type | Concentration | Expansion | Application Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crude oil deck (hydrocarbon) | AFFF | 3% or 6% | Low (6–8:1) | **6.5 L/min/m²** |
| Polar solvent (methanol) | **AR-AFFF** | 3% or 6% | Low (5–8:1) | **10 L/min/m²** |
| Refined products (diesel, gasoline) | AFFF | 3% | Low | 6.5 L/min/m² |
| Helideck fire (kerosene pool + jet) | AFFF / FFFP | 3% | Low | 6.5 L/min/m² for 5 min |
| Engine room flooding | Protein / AFFF | 3% | Medium (50–100:1) | Flood to volume |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fixed Deck Foam System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SupplyA["Water Supply"]
        Seachest[Seachest and Sea Valve]
        FWPump[Fire Main Pump]
        FFilter[Strainer and Filter]
    end

        FCT1[Concentrate Tank A]
        FCT2[Concentrate Tank B]
        FCT3[Concentrate Tank C]
    end

    subgraph PropUnit["Proportioning Unit"]
        PropDev[Balanced Pressure Proportioner]
        MixManifold[Mixing Manifold]
    end

    subgraph DistributionNet["Distribution Network"]

        SectValves[Section Isolation Valves]
        PressGover[Pressure Governor]
    end

        Monitors2[Stbd Side Monitor]

    end

        ManualCP[Manual Call Point]

    end

    Seachest --> FWPump --> FFilter --> PropDev
    FCT1 --> PropDev
    FCT2 --> PropDev
    FCT3 --> PropDev

    style SupplyA fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366

```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Activation Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant FD as Fire Detector
    participant CP as Manual Call Point
    participant Ctrl as Control Panel
    participant Pump as Foam Pump
    participant Prop as Proportioner
    participant Valve as Section Valve
    participant Disc as Discharge Device

    FD->>Ctrl: Fire signal detected
    Ctrl->>Pump: Start command
    CP->>Ctrl: Manual activation signal
    Ctrl->>Valve: Open section valve
    Pump->>Prop: Pressurised sea water
    Prop->>Disc: Foam solution delivered
    Disc->>Disc: Aerate and apply foam to deck
    Note over Ctrl,Pump: System auto-runs for designed duration t
```

## 4.3 Spatial Layout — Top-Down View of a Tanker Deck

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopDownA["Cargo Deck Top View Schematic"]
        BowA[Bow Region]
        CargoA[Cargo Manifold Area]
        TankDecka[Longitudinal Tank Deck]
        SternA[Stern Region]
        PortMonA[Port Foam Monitor]
        StbdMonA[Starboard Foam Monitor]
        HelideckA[Helideck with Ring Main]
    end

    BowA --- CargoA --- TankDecka --- SternA
    PortMonA --- TankDecka
    StbdMonA --- TankDecka
    HelideckA --- SternA

    style CargoA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style PortMonA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style StbdMonA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style HelideckA fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#996600
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**Define a "fixed deck foam system" as applied to oil tankers. State the SOLAS application rate and minimum discharge duration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A fixed deck foam system is a permanently installed fire-fighting arrangement on the weather deck of an oil tanker, comprising foam concentrate storage, proportioning equipment, a distribution piping network, and discharge devices (monitors and pourers) capable of delivering foam solution over the entire cargo deck area simultaneously. As per **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 10.5.6**, the foam solution application rate shall be at least **6.5 L/min per m²** of deck area, with a minimum discharge duration of **20 minutes**.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for application rate with unit, 1 mark for duration.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]
**Why is low-expansion foam preferred over high-expansion foam for open-deck applications on ships?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Low-expansion foam (expansion ratio 3:1 to 20:1) is preferred because: (i) its **higher density** provides greater throw distance from monitors, allowing coverage of wide deck areas from the ship's side; (ii) it is **less susceptible to wind drift** on open weather decks, ensuring foam reaches the fire rather than being blown away; (iii) it provides **faster fire knockdown** on large vertical surfaces such as the cargo manifold tower; and (iv) it **resists mechanical rupture** when poured from a height onto a burning liquid pool. High-expansion foam, while excellent for confined spaces, is impractical in open, windy conditions.

*(Award 1 mark each for any three valid reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Design Calculation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

A product tanker has the following particulars:
- Cargo deck length $L = 160$ m, breadth $B = 30$ m.
- Foam concentrate used: **AFFF 3%**.
- Application rate per SOLAS: $R_a = 6.5$ L/min/m².
- Required discharge duration: $t = 30$ minutes.
- Density of AFFF concentrate: $\rho_c = 1.02$ kg/L.
- Foam expansion ratio: $ER = 8{:}1$.
- Safety factor on pump capacity: $SF = 1.20$.

**(a)** Calculate (i) the cargo deck area, (ii) the foam solution flow rate required, and (iii) the total foam solution volume for 30 minutes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate (i) the required foam concentrate tank capacity in litres and in tonnes, (ii) the foam pump capacity in L/min, and (iii) the total finished foam volume produced. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) — Part (i): Deck Area**

$$
A_d = L \times B = 160 \times 30 = \boxed{4800 \text{ m}^2}
$$

*Explanation (1 mark):* Cargo deck is approximated as the rectangle defined by length and breadth between the manifold and the stern.

**(a) — Part (ii): Foam Solution Flow Rate**

$$
Q_s = A_d \times R_a = 4800 \times 6.5 = \boxed{31{,}200 \text{ L/min}}
$$

*Explanation (1 mark):* Direct application of the SOLAS rate over the full deck area.

**(a) — Part (iii): Total Foam Solution Volume**

$$
Q_{tot} = A_d \times R_a \times t = 4800 \times 6.5 \times 30 = \boxed{936{,}000 \text{ L}}
$$

*Explanation (1 mark):* Multiply solution flow rate by discharge time.

**(b) — Part (i): Concentrate Tank Capacity**

$$
Q_c = Q_{tot} \times p_c = 936{,}000 \times 0.03 = \boxed{28{,}080 \text{ L}}
$$

$$
m_c = Q_c \times \rho_c = 28{,}080 \times 1.02 = \boxed{28{,}641.6 \text{ kg} \approx 28.64 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

*Explanation (2 marks):* 3 % concentrate ratio by volume; convert to mass using density.

**(b) — Part (ii): Foam Pump Capacity**

$$
Q_{pump} = Q_s \times SF = 31{,}200 \times 1.20 = \boxed{37{,}440 \text{ L/min}}
$$

*Explanation (1 mark):* Pump must deliver design flow even with piping losses and possible simultaneous dual-zone discharge.

**(b) — Part (iii): Finished Foam Volume**

$$
V_f = Q_{tot} \times ER = 936{,}000 \times 8 = \boxed{7{,}488{,}000 \text{ L} \approx 7488 \text{ m}^3}
$$

*Explanation (1 mark):* Verify that foam production volume exceeds minimum blanketing requirement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual & Comparative

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **major components** of a fixed deck foam system on an oil tanker. Explain the function of any **four** components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **AFFF, FFFP, Protein foam, and AR-AFFF** in terms of their chemical base, suitable hazards, and limitations. State why AR-AFFF is mandatory for chemical tankers carrying polar solvents. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) — Block Diagram (4 marks)**

```
[Sea Chest] → [Strainer] → [Fire Pump] → [Proportioner] ← [Foam Concentrate Tank]
                                                       |
                                                       ↓
                                              [Deck Foam Fire Main]
                                                       |
                              ┌────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┐
                              ↓                        ↓                        ↓
                    [Port Monitor]          [Starboard Monitor]      [Manifold Pourers]
```

*Explanation (3 marks — any four of the following, 0.75 each):*

1. **Sea chest & strainer** — Supplies clean sea water; prevents marine growth/debris from clogging the proportioner.
2. **Fire/foam pump** — Provides pressurised water at the design flow and head (typically 10–12 bar at the most remote monitor).
3. **Foam concentrate tank** — Stores AFFF or AR-AFFF concentrate, sized for full duration of operation.
4. **Proportioner** — Inducts concentrate into the water stream at a fixed ratio (3 % or 6 %) using a venturi or bladder mechanism.
5. **Deck foam fire main & section valves** — Distributes foam solution; section valves allow zone isolation in case of damage.
6. **Monitors and pourers** — Aerate and apply finished foam onto the burning deck.

**(b) — Comparison Table (5 marks)**

| Foam Type | Chemical Base | Suitable Hazards | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Protein foam** | Hydrolysed protein + stabilisers | Hydrocarbon fires (Class B) | Slow knockdown, poor on polar solvents |
| **Fluoroprotein (FP)** | Protein + fluorochemical surfactants | Hydrocarbon + some polar solvents | Moderate cost, moderate expansion |
| **AFFF** | Synthetic surfactants + fluorinated agents | Rapid knockdown on hydrocarbons; vapour suppression | Poor on polar solvents — membrane dissolves |
| **FFFP** | AFFF + film-forming protein | Hydrocarbon, fast knockdown | Higher cost; polar solvent attack |
| **AR-AFFF** | AFFF + polysaccharide polymer membrane | Hydrocarbons **AND** polar solvents (methanol, ethanol, acetone) | Higher cost; requires careful storage (shelf-life ~25 years sealed) |

**(b) — Why AR-AFFF is Mandatory for Polar Solvents (2 marks):**

Polar solvents (methanol, ethanol, acetone) are **miscible with water** and rapidly destroy the thin aqueous film of standard AFFF, causing foam collapse and reignition. AR-AFFF forms a **protective polymer membrane** at the fuel–foam interface that is **insoluble in polar solvents**, thereby preserving foam integrity and providing sustained suppression. SOLAS and the IBC Code therefore mandate AR-AFFF for chemical tankers carrying products listed under the Polar Solvent hazard category.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — Mixing m² with cm², or L/min with m³/hr without explicit conversion. Always show the conversion step.
> 2. **Forgetting the Safety Factor** — Pump capacity calculations without $SF$ are marked down by 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong Foam Type for Hazard** — Stating "AFFF" for methanol or acetone spills = full marks lost on the comparison question. The examiner *expects* AR-AFFF.
> 4. **Confusing Expansion vs Application Rate** — Expansion ratio is a property of the foam itself; application rate is a regulatory floor. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Omitting the SOLAS Citation** — Quoting the 6.5 L/min/m² rate without citing SOLAS II-2/10.5.6 forfeits a mark in design questions.
> 6. **Missing Duration Justification** — SOLAS minimum is 20 min, but operational designs use 30 min. Justify the choice.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: A permanently installed foam installation on the weather deck of a ship for hydrocarbon/polar solvent fire protection.
- **SOLAS Citation**: Chapter II-2, Regulation 10.5.6.
- **Application Rate (Hydrocarbon deck)**: **6.5 L/min/m²** — the single most-tested number.
- **Application Rate (Polar solvent deck)**: **10 L/min/m²** (with AR-AFFF).
- **Minimum Discharge Duration**: **20 minutes** (SOLAS) — usually 30 min in practice.
- **Common Foam Concentrates**: AFFF (1 %, 3 %, 6 %), FFFP, Protein, AR-AFFF.
- **Expansion Ratio for Decks**: Low (3:1 to 20:1), typically ~8:1.
- **Core Formulae**:
  - $Q_s = A_d \times R_a$
  - $Q_c = Q_{tot} \times p_c$
  - $Q_{pump} = Q_s \times SF$
- **Key Components**: Sea chest → pump → proportioner → deck main → monitors/pourers.
- **Chemical Tanker Rule**: Polar solvents (methanol, acetone) require AR-AFFF, not regular AFFF.
- **Helideck Standard**: CAP 437 (UK CAA) / ICAO Annex 14 — 6.5 L/min/m² for 5 min minimum.
- **Pump Safety Factor**: 1.15 – 1.25 typical.
- **Finished Foam vs Solution**: Solution = water + concentrate; Foam = solution + entrained air (Expansion Ratio × solution volume).
- **Why low-expansion on decks**: Higher density → greater throw, less wind drift, faster knockdown.
- **SOLAS II-2 vs MSC.1/Circ.1432**: Chapter II-2 sets the *requirement*; the MSC Circular provides the *guidance* for design compliance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Test Procedures (FTP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Test Procedures (FTP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)** is a comprehensive regulatory framework adopted under the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), 1974**, as amended. It standardizes the fire test methods used to determine whether products and materials intended for use aboard ships satisfy the fire safety requirements prescribed in Chapter II-2 of SOLAS.

The FTP Code establishes **uniform, reproducible laboratory testing protocols** that quantify how materials behave when exposed to fire, ensuring that fire safety equipment, structural divisions, surface finishes, and furnishings on board vessels meet minimum safety thresholds before installation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The FTP Code is mandatory for classification of fire-resistant divisions into **"A" Class**, **"B" Class**, and **"F" Class**, and applies to materials ranging from structural steelwork to upholstery, bedding, and textiles on board ships.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the FTP Code as a **"Standardized Hospital Check-up" for ship materials**. Just as a doctor uses a fixed set of tests (blood pressure, ECG, blood sugar) to certify a person as medically fit, the FTP Code uses a fixed set of laboratory tests to certify a material as **"fire-safe"** for use at sea.

- A ship cannot simply install *any* material — the material must pass the **fire doctor’s test (FTP)**.
- The test results are stamped into a **certificate of fitness** before the material is allowed on board.
- Without FTP compliance, the vessel would not receive a **SOLAS Safety Construction Certificate** from the flag state or classification society.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** Every lifejacket fabric, every cabin mattress, every corridor wall panel, and every fire door on a commercial vessel must have an FTP test certificate issued by an accredited testing laboratory (e.g., BRE Global, Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas).

### 1.3 Governing Body & Origin

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Adopting Authority** | International Maritime Organization (IMO) |
| **Adopted Year** | 1996 (Resolution MSC.61(67)) |
| **Effective Under** | SOLAS Convention, Chapter II-2, Regulation 3 |
| **Update Mechanism** | Resolutions of Maritime Safety Committee (MSC) |
| **Test Conducted By** | Accredited fire test laboratories worldwide |

> [!NOTE]
> The FTP Code is a **living document** — it is periodically updated by the MSC to incorporate new fire test science. The current consolidated edition reflects amendments up to **Resolution MSC.453(100)** and subsequent updates.

### 1.4 Necessity of FTP Code — The "Why"

Ships are unique fire environments because of:

1. **Confined spaces** — Heat and smoke accumulate rapidly.
2. **Compartmentalized layout** — Fire spread is restricted by divisions.
3. **High fuel load** — Fuel, cargo, furnishings, and lubricants.
4. **At-sea isolation** — Cannot easily evacuate; crew must contain the fire.
5. **International operation** — Ships cross jurisdictions; uniform rules prevent legal ambiguity.

Hence, the FTP Code ensures that a material qualified in **Japan** is accepted in **Greece**, **Panama**, or **the Marshall Islands** — the principle of **"Tested Once, Accepted Globally."**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the FTP Code

The FTP Code is divided into **10 Parts** plus introductory framework. Each part governs a specific class of material or product used on board ships.

#### **Part 1 — General Provisions**

- Defines the **application scope** of the entire code.
- Establishes **common terminology** (e.g., *non-combustible*, *limited combustibility*, *fire-resisting division*).
- Mandates that the test results are valid for a **five-year period** unless the material composition changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-combustible material** is defined as a material that does not burn or release flammable vapors when heated to **750 °C** — verified by the furnace test in Part 1.

#### **Part 2 — Smoke Generation and Toxicity Test**

Used to measure:

- **Smoke density (Ds)** — quantified by the specific optical density within 4 minutes ($D_s$).
- **Gas toxicity** — concentrations of CO, CO₂, HCl, HCN, HBr, HF, NOₓ, SO₂.

The test uses the **chamber method** (NFPA 270 / ISO 5659-2 style) under controlled flaming and non-flaming conditions.

#### **Part 3 — Test for "A", "B", and "F" Class Divisions**

The most critical part for ship structural fire safety. It tests:

- **A-Class divisions** — withstand 60, 120, or 240 minutes of standard fire exposure.
- **B-Class divisions** — withstand 30 minutes of standard fire exposure.
- **F-Class divisions** — restrict fire spread between adjacent compartments.

##### Criteria Measured in A/B Class Test:
1. **Integrity** — No flame passage, no through-gaps.
2. **Insulation** — Average unexposed surface temperature rise ≤ **140 °C**; maximum at any point ≤ **180 °C**.

#### **Part 4 — Test for Fire Door Control Systems**

Tests the **self-closing mechanism** of fire doors:

- Door must close fully within **≤ 40 seconds** from any open angle.
- Closing must be smooth, controlled, and latch automatically.
- Tested over **200 open-close cycles** for reliability.

#### **Part 5 — Test for Surface Flammability**

Uses the **radiant panel test** (IMO Resolution A.653(16)):

- Specimen mounted at **30°** to a vertical panel.
- Exposed to a **radiant heat flux of 1.0 to 5.0 W/cm²**.
- Measures: **Critical Heat Flux at Extinguishment (CFE)**, **Heat for Sustained Burning (Qsb)**.

#### **Part 6 — Test for Primary Deck Coverings**

Determines whether floor coverings on board restrict fire spread:

- Tests ability to prevent flame spread across decks.
- Uses a small-scale **horizontal flame spread apparatus**.

#### **Part 7 — Test for Vertically Supported Textiles and Films**

Tests curtains, drapes, and suspended fabrics:

- Specimen suspended vertically in a **vertical flame test rig**.
- Measures **char length** and **afterflame time**.

#### **Part 8 — Test for Upholstered Furniture**

Tests chairs, sofas, and seating:

- Uses a **cigarette ignition source** (smouldering) and **butane flame** (flaming).
- Measures **mass loss**, **smoke production**, and **heat release**.

#### **Part 9 — Test for Bedding Components**

Tests mattresses, pillows, bedspreads:

- Specimen laid horizontally and exposed to a **cigarette + propane flame**.
- Failure criteria: char length > **50 mm** or sustained flaming > **120 seconds**.

#### **Part 10 — Test for Fire-Restricting Materials for High-Speed Craft**

Applies to **High-Speed Craft (HSC) Code**:

- Tests materials and assemblies to limit fire spread in HSC operating under high passenger density.
- Uses a **room-corner test** configuration.

### 2.2 The Standard Time–Temperature Curve (Hydrocarbon & Cellulosic)

The FTP Code adopts the standard fire exposure curve defined in the **ISO 834 / ASTM E119** standard. The furnace temperature follows:

$$
T(t) = T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

Where:

- $T(t)$ = average furnace temperature at time $t$ (in **°C**).
- $T_0$ = initial ambient temperature (usually **20 °C**).
- $t$ = time elapsed since ignition (in **minutes**).
- The constant **345** and **8** are empirical coefficients.

For **hydrocarbon fires** (oil, chemical tankers, offshore platforms), a steeper curve is used:

$$
T(t) = T_0 + 1080 \cdot \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-2.5t}\right)
$$

This represents a much faster temperature rise, peaking near **1100 °C** within **30 minutes** — relevant for fuel-rich environments on tankers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Application** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Fire Curve | $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | Cellulosic / A-B Class | °C, minutes |
| Hydrocarbon Fire Curve | $T(t) = 20 + 1080(1 - 0.325e^{-0.167t} - 0.675e^{-2.5t})$ | Oil / chemical tankers | °C, minutes |
| Insulating Criterion | $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140$ | Average unexposed surface | °C |
| Insulating Criterion (max) | $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180$ | Maximum at any point | °C |
| A-60 Rating | Integrity 60 min + Insulation 60 min | Bulkheads / decks | minutes |
| A-120 Rating | Integrity 120 min + Insulation 120 min | Engine rooms, hazardous zones | minutes |
| B-0 / B-15 Rating | Integrity 0/15 min + Insulation 0/15 min | Corridor partitions | minutes |
| Fire Door Closing Time | $t_c \leq 40$ | Self-closing duration | seconds |
| Smoke Density Limit | $D_s \leq 200$ within 4 min | Toxicity/smoke test | dimensionless |
| Heat Release Rate Limit | $Q_{sb} \leq 0.25$ | Surface flammability Part 5 | MJ |
| Bedding Char Length Limit | $L \leq 50$ | Part 9 | mm |
| CFE Criterion | $CFE \geq 1.5$ for bulkhead | Part 5 | W/cm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** The numbers 140, 180, 345, 1080, 200, 50, 40 are all "magic constants" that frequently appear in KTU examination questions. Memorize them.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

1. **Naval Architecture & Ship Design** — Determines material selection for bulkheads, decks, and superstructures.
2. **Marine Mechanical Engineering** — Selection of insulation for engine room ducting, exhaust lines, and LNG fuel systems.
3. **Offshore Engineering** — Hydrocarbon fire curves govern fire protection of offshore platforms.
4. **Forensic Marine Engineering** — Post-incident investigations use FTP benchmarks to determine if installed materials complied.
5. **Classification Society Work** — Surveyors (DNV, Lloyd's Register) check FTP certificates during statutory surveys.
6. **Insurance Underwriting** — Insurers reduce premiums for vessels using FTP-certified materials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Time–Temperature Curve (IMO / ISO 834)

The standard fire curve is **empirically derived** from observations of real compartment fires fueled by **cellulosic materials** (wood, paper, fabric, furnishings). Let us derive its key properties.

**Step 1 — Recognize the Functional Form**

The expression is:

$$
T(t) = T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

This is a **logarithmic growth function** because fire temperatures rise rapidly initially and then asymptotically approach a plateau as fuel is consumed and ventilation becomes limited.

**Step 2 — Evaluate at Key Time Points**

At $t = 0$ minutes:

$$
T(0) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 0 + 1) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(1) = 20 + 0 = 20 \text{ °C}
$$

This matches the **initial ambient temperature** — the fire has not started.

At $t = 30$ minutes:

$$
T(30) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 30 + 1) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(241)
$$

We compute $\log_{10}(241)$:

$$
\log_{10}(241) \approx 2.382
$$

Substituting:

$$
T(30) = 20 + 345 \cdot 2.382 = 20 + 821.79 \approx 841.8 \text{ °C}
$$

This matches the **standard benchmark** for A-30 class divisions.

At $t = 60$ minutes:

$$
T(60) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 60 + 1) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(481)
$$

We compute $\log_{10}(481)$:

$$
\log_{10}(481) \approx 2.682
$$

Substituting:

$$
T(60) = 20 + 345 \cdot 2.682 = 20 + 925.29 \approx 945.3 \text{ °C}
$$

This corresponds to the **A-60** benchmark.

At $t = 120$ minutes:

$$
T(120) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 120 + 1) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(961)
$$

We compute $\log_{10}(961)$:

$$
\log_{10}(961) \approx 2.983
$$

Substituting:

$$
T(120) = 20 + 345 \cdot 2.983 = 20 + 1029.13 \approx 1049.1 \text{ °C}
$$

This corresponds to the **A-120** benchmark.

**Step 3 — Calculate the Time for a Particular Temperature**

To find the time $t$ at which the furnace reaches, say, $T = 1000 \text{ °C}$:

$$
1000 = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
980 = 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

Dividing both sides:

$$
\log_{10}(8t + 1) = \frac{980}{345} = 2.8406
$$

Taking antilog:

$$
8t + 1 = 10^{2.8406} \approx 692.96
$$

Solving for $t$:

$$
8t = 691.96 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = 86.50 \text{ minutes}
$$

This means a furnace reaches **1000 °C** in approximately **86.5 minutes** under the standard IMO/ISO 834 fire exposure.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Hydrocarbon Curve Time at Peak

The hydrocarbon fire curve is:

$$
T(t) = 20 + 1080 \cdot \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-2.5t}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Find the Asymptotic Maximum**

As $t \to \infty$:

$$
e^{-0.167t} \to 0 \quad \text{and} \quad e^{-2.5t} \to 0
$$

Hence:

$$
T_{\infty} = 20 + 1080 \cdot (1 - 0 - 0) = 20 + 1080 = 1100 \text{ °C}
$$

This is the **asymptotic ceiling temperature** for hydrocarbon fires.

**Step 2 — Calculate Temperature at $t = 30$ min**

$$
T(30) = 20 + 1080 \cdot \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.167 \cdot 30} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-2.5 \cdot 30}\right)
$$

Compute the exponentials:

$$
e^{-5.01} \approx 0.00668
$$
$$
e^{-75} \approx 0 \quad (\text{practically negligible})
$$

Substituting:

$$
T(30) = 20 + 1080 \cdot (1 - 0.325 \cdot 0.00668 - 0) = 20 + 1080 \cdot 0.99783 \approx 20 + 1077.66 = 1097.66 \text{ °C}
$$

Thus the hydrocarbon curve reaches nearly its maximum within just **30 minutes** — showing how rapidly a tanker fire escalates.

### 3.3 Comparison Table — Standard vs. Hydrocarbon Curve

| Time (min) | IMO/ISO 834 (°C) | Hydrocarbon (°C) | Difference (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 576 | 956 | 380 |
| 15 | 739 | 1075 | 336 |
| 30 | 842 | 1098 | 256 |
| 60 | 945 | 1100 | 155 |
| 120 | 1049 | 1100 | 51 |

**Conclusion:** The hydrocarbon curve is **far more aggressive** in the early stages (0–30 min), demanding stricter material performance on tankers and offshore platforms.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — FTP Acceptance Criteria

| **Class** | **Integrity (min)** | **Insulation (min)** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| A-60 | 60 | 60 | Engine room bulkheads, hazardous zones |
| A-30 | 30 | 30 | Galley bulkheads, corridors near machinery |
| A-15 | 15 | 15 | Stairway enclosures |
| A-0 | 0 | 0 | Structural divisions (with low risk) |
| B-15 | 15 | 15 | Corridor walls, accommodation partitions |
| B-0 | 0 | 0 | Cabin-to-cabin partitions (low fire load) |
| C | — | — | No fire integrity required |
| F | — | — | Restricts fire spread to adjacent compartments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The classification is **A/B + number**, where the number is the *time* in minutes. So **A-60 ≠ A-30** — they differ in fire-resistance duration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FTP Code Test Framework — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Material Sample] --> B1{Type of Material}
    B1 -->|Structural Steel / Bulkhead| C1[Part 3: A B F Class Test]
    B1 -->|Surface Finish / Paint| C2[Part 5: Surface Flammability]
    B1 -->|Fabric / Curtain| C3[Part 7: Vertical Textile Test]
    B1 -->|Furniture Cushion| C4[Part 8: Upholstered Furniture]
    B1 -->|Mattress / Bedding| C5[Part 9: Bedding Test]
    B1 -->|Deck Covering Floor Tile| C6[Part 6: Deck Covering Test]
    B1 -->|Door Assembly| C7[Part 4: Fire Door Test]
    B1 -->|Material for HSC| C8[Part 10: High Speed Craft]
    C1 --> D1[Time Temp Furnace 1100 C]
    C2 --> D2[Radiant Panel Apparatus]
    C3 --> D3[Vertical Flame Rig]
    C4 --> D4[Cigarette and Butane Flame Source]
    C5 --> D5[Horizontal Bedding Test Rig]
    C6 --> D6[Horizontal Flame Spread]
    C7 --> D7[200 Cycle Closing Mechanism Test]
    C8 --> D8[Room Corner Test Rig]
    D1 --> E1[Integrity and Insulation]
    D2 --> E2[CFE Qsb Heat Release]
    D3 --> E3[Char Length Afterflame Time]
    D4 --> E4[Mass Loss Smoke Rate]
    D5 --> E5[Char Length Flaming Duration]
    D6 --> E6[Flame Spread Index]
    D7 --> E7[Self Close Time 40 s]
    D8 --> E8[HRR Smoke Production]
    E1 --> F1{Acceptance Criteria Met}
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
    E6 --> F1
    E7 --> F1
    E8 --> F1
    F1 -->|Yes| G1[FTP Certificate Issued]
    F1 -->|No| H1[Material Rejected Modify and Retest]
    G1 --> I1[Material Approved for Shipboard Use]
```

### 4.2 FTP Code Parts Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[SOLAS Chapter II 2 Material] --> J2{Identify Function}
    J2 --> J3[Structural Fire Division]
    J2 --> J4[Surface Finish]
    J2 --> J5[Textile and Fabric]
    J2 --> J6[Furniture and Bedding]
    J2 --> J7[Floor Covering]
    J2 --> J8[Fire Door]
    J2 --> J9[High Speed Craft Item]
    J3 --> K1[Part 3 A B F Test]
    J4 --> K2[Part 5 Radiant Panel]
    J5 --> K3[Part 7 Vertical Fabric]
    J6 --> K4[Parts 8 and 9]
    J7 --> K5[Part 6 Horizontal Flame]
    J8 --> K6[Part 4 Door Closure]
    J9 --> K7[Part 10 HSC Test]
    K1 --> L1[Apply T equals 20 plus 345 log10 8t plus 1]
    K2 --> L2[Measure CFE and Qsb]
    K3 --> L3[Measure char 100 mm pass]
    K4 --> L4[Char 50 mm pass 120 s flaming]
    K5 --> L5[Flame spread index 20]
    K6 --> L6[Close time 40 s over 200 cycles]
    K7 --> L7[Room corner HRR 100 kW]
```

### 4.3 Standard Fire Curve Visualization — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Time t in minutes] --> M2[Apply T equals 20 plus 345 log10 of 8t plus 1]
    M2 --> M3{Calculate Temperature}
    M3 --> M4[t 0 gives 20 C]
    M3 --> M5[t 5 gives 576 C]
    M3 --> M6[t 30 gives 842 C]
    M3 --> M7[t 60 gives 945 C]
    M3 --> M8[t 120 gives 1049 C]
    M3 --> M9[t 240 gives 1153 C]
    M4 --> N1[Compare with A B Class Test]
    M5 --> N1
    M6 --> N1
    M7 --> N1
    M8 --> N1
    M9 --> N1
    N1 --> O1{Criterion}
    O1 -->|Integrity| O2[No flame passage no through gaps]
    O1 -->|Insulation| O3[Delta T average 140 C maximum 180 C]
    O2 --> P1[Compliance Certificate]
    O3 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance of the Curves:** The visual structure of the standard fire curve shows why an **A-60** bulkhead (resisting 60 min of 945 °C) is far more robust than a **B-15** division (resisting 15 min of 739 °C). The logarithmic shape dictates that the **first 30 minutes** are the most critical.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**Define the FTP Code and mention its adoption authority.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)** is a regulatory framework that prescribes uniform laboratory test methods to evaluate the fire performance of materials and products intended for use on board ships. It was **adopted by the International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under **Resolution MSC.61(67) in 1996** and is referenced under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 3**. The FTP Code standardizes fire testing so that materials approved in one country are accepted globally, ensuring uniform safety standards across international shipping.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**State the insulation criteria for an A-Class fire test as per the FTP Code.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

According to the FTP Code (Part 3), an A-Class division must satisfy two insulation criteria during the standard fire test:

1. The **average temperature rise** on the unexposed face of the specimen must not exceed **140 °C** above the initial ambient temperature.
2. The **maximum temperature rise** at any single point on the unexposed face must not exceed **180 °C** above the initial ambient temperature.

In addition, the **integrity criterion** requires that no flame passage, cracks, or through-gaps develop on the unexposed surface for the rated duration (15, 30, 60, 120, or 240 minutes).

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a) Explain the structure of the FTP Code with reference to its 10 parts. State the specific fire test each part addresses. (7 marks)**

**(b) With the help of the standard time–temperature curve, derive the furnace temperature at $t = 30$ minutes and $t = 60$ minutes. Discuss the significance of these values in classifying A-class and B-class divisions. (7 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a):

The FTP Code is divided into **10 functional parts**, each addressing a specific class of shipboard material. The structure is:

1. **Part 1 — General Provisions:** Defines scope, terminology, and common test apparatus; specifies the **non-combustibility test at 750 °C**.
2. **Part 2 — Smoke and Toxicity Test:** Quantifies smoke density ($D_s$) and toxic gas concentrations using a closed chamber.
3. **Part 3 — A, B, and F Class Divisions:** Tests the structural fire integrity and insulation of bulkheads and decks using the standard fire curve.
4. **Part 4 — Fire Door Control Systems:** Verifies that fire doors close within 40 seconds and operate reliably over 200 cycles.
5. **Part 5 — Surface Flammability:** Uses the radiant panel test to measure critical heat flux at extinguishment (CFE) and heat for sustained burning.
6. **Part 6 — Primary Deck Coverings:** Evaluates the flame spread across floor finishes.
7. **Part 7 — Vertically Supported Textiles and Films:** Tests curtains and drapes using a vertical flame test rig.
8. **Part 8 — Upholstered Furniture:** Tests chairs and sofas using cigarette and butane flame ignition sources.
9. **Part 9 — Bedding Components:** Tests mattresses, pillows, and bedspreads with horizontal exposure.
10. **Part 10 — High-Speed Craft Materials:** Uses a room-corner test rig to evaluate heat release rate (HRR) and smoke production in HSC.

##### Model Solution (b):

The standard time–temperature curve per ISO 834/FTP Code is:

$$
T(t) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

**At $t = 30$ minutes:**

$$
T(30) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 30 + 1) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(241)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(241) = 2.382
$$

$$
T(30) = 20 + 345 \cdot 2.382 = 20 + 821.79 \approx 841.8 \text{ °C}
$$

**At $t = 60$ minutes:**

$$
T(60) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 60 + 1) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(481)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(481) = 2.682
$$

$$
T(60) = 20 + 345 \cdot 2.682 = 20 + 925.29 \approx 945.3 \text{ °C}
$$

**Significance in A/B Class Classification:**

- The **B-Class divisions** must withstand 30 minutes of fire exposure at a furnace temperature of approximately **842 °C**, after which integrity and insulation must still be maintained.
