- The **A-30 class** also withstands 30 minutes, but A-30 bulkheads are tested for structural integrity at a much higher standard (same temperature but more demanding load-bearing criteria).
- The **A-60 class** must withstand 60 minutes at approximately **945 °C**, used in engine rooms and high-risk areas.
- This curve quantifies what we mean by "30 minutes" or "60 minutes" of fire resistance — the structure must remain intact at these extreme temperatures.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) Compare the standard cellulosic fire curve with the hydrocarbon fire curve. State the equation for each and explain why the hydrocarbon curve is used in oil tanker fire protection design. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe in detail the FTP Part 5 test for surface flammability. What is the significance of the Critical Heat Flux at Extinguishment (CFE) and the Heat for Sustained Burning (Qsb) parameters? (7 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a):

**Standard Cellulosic Fire Curve (ISO 834 / IMO FTP):**

$$
T(t) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

**Hydrocarbon Fire Curve:**

$$
T(t) = 20 + 1080 \cdot \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-2.5t}\right)
$$

**Comparison:**

| Time (min) | Cellulosic Curve (°C) | Hydrocarbon Curve (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 576 | 956 |
| 15 | 739 | 1075 |
| 30 | 842 | 1098 |
| 60 | 945 | 1100 |

The **hydrocarbon curve rises much faster** in the initial 5–15 minutes, reaching over **1000 °C** within 15 minutes, while the cellulosic curve takes 60 minutes to reach a similar temperature.

**Why Hydrocarbon Curve for Tankers:**

Oil tankers and chemical carriers carry hydrocarbon fuels (crude oil, LNG, methanol). If ignited, these fuels produce a much more **rapid, high-intensity fire** than cellulosic materials. The hydrocarbon curve simulates this scenario, ensuring that bulkheads, decks, and structural divisions on tankers can withstand the **extreme heat flux** for the required period — typically 60 or 120 minutes. The cellulosic curve would be insufficient and understate the actual thermal load.

##### Model Solution (b):

**FTP Part 5 — Surface Flammability Test (Radiant Panel Test):**

- **Specimen mounting:** Test sample of size **155 mm × 800 mm** is mounted at an angle of **30°** from the vertical.
- **Heat source:** A **porous refractory panel** (the "radiant panel") is heated by a gas burner. The panel emits a controlled **radiant heat flux** ranging from **1.0 to 5.0 W/cm²** across the specimen.
- **Pilot flame:** A small pilot flame is applied to the top of the specimen to ignite any combustible gases released.
- **Duration:** The test is conducted for a fixed period, and the flame front progression is observed.

**Parameters Measured:**

1. **Critical Heat Flux at Extinguishment (CFE):** This is the **lowest heat flux** at which a material will continue to burn. A higher CFE indicates a more flame-resistant material. The FTP Code requires that for **bulkheads and decks**, the CFE must be **≥ 1.5 W/cm²**.
2. **Heat for Sustained Burning (Qsb):** This is the **minimum total heat energy** required per unit area of the specimen to sustain combustion. The FTP Code requires **Qsb ≤ 0.25 MJ** for surface materials.

**Significance:**

- The **CFE** parameter determines how easily a surface will propagate flame. Materials with low CFE (like untreated plywood) are hazardous; high CFE materials (like mineral wool composites) are preferred for shipboard use.
- The **Qsb** parameter quantifies the **fire load contribution** of the surface material — important in fire growth modeling and evacuation time calculations.
- Together, these two parameters provide a **quantitative risk assessment** of surface finishes on board, replacing older "pass/fail" tests with measurable engineering properties.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the standard fire curve with the hydrocarbon curve** — they are different equations, and applying the wrong one will cost full marks.
> 2. **Insulation vs. Integrity confusion:** Students often write "fire resistance = A-60" without separating the two criteria. Always mention BOTH **integrity** and **insulation** separately.
> 3. **Wrong units in the answer:** Temperature must always be in **°C**, heat flux in **W/cm²**, char length in **mm**, and time in **minutes/seconds** as appropriate.
> 4. **Memorize 140 °C and 180 °C** as the insulation thresholds. Forgetting these constants is a common loss of 2–3 marks.
> 5. **CFE units** are **W/cm²** (not kW/m²). One common student mistake is mixing units and losing a mark.
> 6. **For Part 9 (Bedding)**: The test uses a **horizontal** specimen, not vertical. The char length limit is **50 mm** — not the 100 mm used in Part 7.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **FTP Code = IMO Resolution MSC.61(67) (1996),** governs fire testing under SOLAS Chapter II-2.
- **The Code has 10 Parts**, each addressing a specific material type.
- **Standard Fire Curve:** $T(t) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t+1)$ — for cellulosic/shipboard A and B class tests.
- **Hydrocarbon Fire Curve:** $T(t) = 20 + 1080 \cdot (1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5t})$ — for oil tankers and offshore.
- **A-60 rating** ≈ 945 °C sustained for 60 min; **A-120 rating** ≈ 1049 °C for 120 min.
- **Insulation criteria:** Average ΔT ≤ 140 °C, Maximum ΔT ≤ 180 °C.
- **Integrity criterion:** No flame passage, cracks, or through-gaps on the unexposed side.
- **Fire door self-closing time:** Must close within **40 seconds**, tested over **200 cycles**.
- **Smoke density limit:** $D_s \leq 200$ within the first 4 minutes of exposure.
- **Surface Flammability (Part 5):** CFE ≥ 1.5 W/cm² for bulkheads; Qsb ≤ 0.25 MJ.
- **Bedding Test (Part 9):** Char length ≤ 50 mm; flaming duration ≤ 120 s.
- **High-Speed Craft (Part 10):** Uses room-corner test with HRR ≤ 100 kW threshold.
- **FTP certificate validity:** Generally **5 years**, unless material composition changes.
- **"Tested Once, Accepted Globally"** is the cornerstone principle of the FTP Code.
- **Non-combustible material definition:** A material that does not burn or release flammable vapors when heated to **750 °C** (Part 1).
- **Key laboratories issuing FTP certificates:** BRE Global (UK), DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, RINA.
- **The classification A/B + number = time in minutes** (e.g., A-30 = 30 minutes of fire resistance).
- **Difference between A-class and B-class:** A-class has structural integrity + insulation; B-class has lower thermal load resistance (no structural load).
- **F-class:** Restricts fire spread to adjacent compartments, used in specialized applications.
- **C-class:** No fire integrity requirement (e.g., ceilings that are not load-bearing).
- **Real-world applications:** Engine room bulkheads, cabin walls, corridor partitions, upholstery, mattresses, deck tiles, curtains, fire-rated doors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Fire Test Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Fire Test Procedures # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fire Test Procedures** in the maritime domain refer to the standardised, scientifically controlled laboratory and full-scale experiments conducted on materials, products, and structural assemblies to evaluate their **reaction-to-fire** (surface flammability, ignitability, heat release, smoke production, toxic gas emission) and **fire-resistance** (ability to withstand a standardised fire exposure for a defined period). The International Maritime Organization (IMO) codified these procedures under the **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)**, which is mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter II-2** for all ships engaged on international voyages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **FTP Code – Official Definition (IMO Resolution MSC.307(88)):** > The FTP Code is the international standard that prescribes the fire test procedures for determining the fire properties of materials, components, and structures used in ships. It is referenced in **SOLAS 1974, Chapter II-2, Regulation 3**, making compliance legally binding on all flag states. The principal objectives of fire testing in the maritime context are: 1. **Quantifying the fire hazard** of materials (heat release rate, flame spread index). 2. **Certifying fire-resisting divisions** (A, B, and F Class divisions under SOLAS). 3. **Validating fixed fire-extinguishing systems** (water mist, gas, foam). 4. **Ensuring life safety** by limiting smoke opacity and toxic gas concentration. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **"Passport Control"** at an international airport. Every traveller (material/component) must present a valid "visa" (fire test certificate) before boarding the ship. Just as no passenger is allowed in without a passport, no material is allowed onboard a SOLAS-regulated vessel without a valid fire test report recognised by the Flag Administration and Classification Society. A second analogy is a **medical check-up**: - **Blood test** → Small-scale fire test (reaction to fire). - **MRI / Stress test** → Full-scale fire test (fire resistance). - The **doctor's report** → Type Approval Certificate issued by the certifying body. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Glossary) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Reaction to Fire (RTF)** | The response of a material in contributing to fire growth, measured in terms of ignitability, flame spread, heat release, and smoke/toxicity. | | **Fire Resistance (FR)** | The ability of a structural element to maintain its stability, integrity, and insulation for a specified period under standard fire exposure. | | **FTP Code** | Fire Test Procedures Code (IMO Res. MSC.307(88)). | | **A-Class Division** | A division formed by steel or equivalent, capable of preventing the passage of smoke and flame for **60 minutes**. | | **B-Class Division** | A division formed by non-combustible materials, with fire resistance up to **30 minutes**. | | **C-Class Division** | A division of non-combustible materials with no fire-resistance rating required. | | **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** | The rate at which thermal energy is generated by combustion, measured in **kW/m²** or **MW**. | | **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** | The minimum incident heat flux required to ignite a material under sustained flaming combustion, measured in **kW/m²**. | | **Lateral Flame Spread** | Propagation of flame along a horizontal surface, governed by conduction through the substrate and convective pre-heating. | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Temperature-Time Curve (ISO 834 / ASTM E119):** > The cellulosic fire curve is the cornerstone of all fire-resistance tests. Its mathematical form is: > $$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$ > where *T* is the furnace temperature in °C, *T₀* is the ambient (typically 20°C), and *t* is the time in minutes. This curve models a fully developed **compartment fire** and is the benchmark used in IMO FTP Part 3 and equivalent naval/marine standards. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Framework of Fire Test Procedures The IMO FTP Code is structured into **11 Parts** (Part 0 – Part 10), each addressing a specific aspect of material or system performance: ### 2.1.1 Structural Logic of the FTP Code 1. **Part 0** – General requirements (test specimen preparation, laboratory conditions, classification principles). 2. **Part 1** – Non-combustibility test (small-scale furnace at **750°C**, single specimen). 3. **Part 2** – Smoke and toxicity test (chamber method, ISO 5659-2 equivalence). 4. **Part 3** – Test for "A", "B", and "F" Class divisions (full-scale furnace exposure, 60-min / 30-min ratings). 5. **Part 4** – Test for fire door control systems (self-closing mechanisms). 6. **Part 5** – Test for surface flammability (radiant panel, IMO spread index ≤ 20). 7. **Part 6** – Test for primary deck coverings (critical radiant flux test). 8. **Part 7** – Test for vertically supported textiles and films. 9. **Part 8** – Test for upholstered furniture (cigarette + butane flame ignition). 10. **Part 9** – Test for bedding components. 11. **Part 10** – Test for fire-extinguishing media concentration in fire-protected containers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" behind the FTP Code:** A fire on a vessel is fundamentally different from a terrestrial fire because of the **confined geometry**, **high fuel density**, **rapid ventilation-controlled growth**, and **evacuation constraints**. The FTP Code addresses these unique conditions by harmonising test methods globally, eliminating flag-state variation, and providing designers a **performance-based acceptance matrix**. ## 2.2 Categories of Fire Tests (High-Yield Summary) | Category | Test Type | Scale | Standard Reference | Acceptance Criteria | |---|---|---|---|---| | Reaction to Fire | Non-combustibility | Small | FTP Part 1 (ISO 1182) | Furnace temp. rise ≤ 30°C; specimen mass loss ≤ 50% | | Reaction to Fire | Surface Flammability | Small/Medium | FTP Part 5 (ISO 5658-2) | Spread Index (CFE) ≤ 20 | | Reaction to Fire | Smoke Density & Toxicity | Small | FTP Part 2 (ISO 5659-2) | Ds,max ≤ 250; CIT ≤ 0.4 (4-min); ≤ 1.5 (8-min) | | Reaction to Fire | Primary Deck Covering | Small | FTP Part 6 (ASTM E648) | CHF ≥ 1.1 kW/m² (Class 1) | | Fire Resistance | A/B Class Division | Full-scale | FTP Part 3 (ISO 834) | Integrity & Insulation: 60/30 min | | Fire Resistance | Fire Door | Full-scale | FTP Part 4 / EN 1634 | Self-closing within ≤ 1 s; passage gap ≤ 6 mm | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready) | Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Standard Fire Curve | $T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | $T$ in °C, $t$ in minutes | Furnace temperature profile | | Heat Release Rate (Cone Calorimeter) | $\dot{Q} = \chi \cdot \dot{m} \cdot h_c$ | $\dot{Q}$ = kW/m²; $\chi$ = combustion efficiency (–); $\dot{m}$ = mass loss rate (kg/s); $h_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg) | Quantifying fire load | | Total Heat Released (THR) | $\mathrm{THR} = \int_0^{t_f} \dot{Q}(t)\,dt$ | $t_f$ = test duration (s) | Cumulative fire energy | | Specific Optical Density | $D_s = \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | $D_s$ = dimensionless; $V$ = chamber volume (m³); $A$ = exposed area (m²); $L$ = beam path (m); $I_0/I$ = transmittance ratio | Smoke obscuration | | Conventional Index of Toxicity (CIT) | $\mathrm{CIT} = \frac{0.5\,[\mathrm{CO}] + [\mathrm{CO}_2] + [\mathrm{HCN}]}{30}$ | Concentrations in ppm (×10⁻⁴ in 30 m³) | Toxic hazard assessment | | Mass Loss Fraction | $\Delta m_f = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_i}$ | $m_i, m_f$ = initial and final mass (g) | Non-combustibility test | | Critical Heat Flux (CHF) | $\mathrm{CHF} = q''_{\mathrm{rad}} + q''_{\mathrm{conv}}$ | $q''$ in kW/m² | Deck covering classification | | Flame Spread Index (FSI) | $\mathrm{FSI} = 1.85 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot (Q_{sb} - 5.7)$ | $Q_{sb}$ = total heat flux sustained in kW/m² (ASTM E162) | Surface flammability | > [!NOTE] > **Why "$\log_{10}$" and not "$\ln$" in the fire curve?** > The ISO 834 curve is empirical, derived from experimental data on fully developed **cellulosic fires** in compartments. The factor **345** is a fitted coefficient, and the **$\log_{10}$** form produces a fire that grows rapidly in the first 10 minutes and then plateaus — matching real compartment behaviour where the fire is ventilation-controlled after flashover. Using natural log would distort the early-time fire growth and is therefore **not permitted** in the FTP Code. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Ship design offices** use FTP Part 3 data to specify bulkhead thickness and insulation. - **Yards and outfitting contractors** procure materials with valid **Type Approval Certificates** (TAC). - **Port State Control (PSC) officers** verify that installed materials match the approved test reports during inspections. - **Classification Societies (LR, DNV, BV, ABS, KR)** conduct or witness fire tests and issue **Certificates of Fitness for Purpose**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Temperature-Time Curve The **ISO 834** curve is the cornerstone of all **A-class and B-class division** testing. Below is a step-by-step derivation grounded in heat transfer fundamentals. ### 3.1.1 Assumed Fire Physics A fully developed compartment fire is governed by the **energy balance** of the enclosure: $$\dot{Q}_{\mathrm{comb}} = \dot{Q}_{\mathrm{wall}} + \dot{Q}_{\mathrm{vent}} + \dot{Q}_{\mathrm{rad}}$$ For a ventilation-controlled fire, the wall heat transfer dominates and is approximately proportional to the temperature difference between the hot gases and the wall: $$q''_{\mathrm{wall}} = h_c\,(T_g - T_w)$$ Since the wall is thick (thermal mass $\rho c_p L$ is large), the rise in wall temperature is slow, and $T_g$ can be assumed to follow a logarithmic growth pattern fitted to experimental data on cellulosic fuels (wood, paper, textiles). ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Empirical starting point.** Experimental data by the British Standards Institution (BS 476, Part 20) and ISO TC 92 showed that the gas temperature in a post-flashover compartment fire follows a logarithmic growth. A curve of the form: $$T(t) = T_0 + K \log_{10}(at + 1)$$ was fitted, with $T_0$ the ambient and $K$, $a$ constants. **Step 2 — Calibration at $t = 0$ (time zero).** At $t = 0$, we must have $T = T_0$ (ambient). Substituting $t = 0$: $$T(0) = T_0 + K \log_{10}(1) = T_0 + 0 = T_0 \quad \checkmark$$ The condition is automatically satisfied for any $K$ because $\log_{10}(1) = 0$. **Step 3 — Calibration at $t = 30$ min.** The experimental benchmark is that after 30 minutes, the furnace should reach approximately $842^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. Therefore: $$842 = 20 + K \log_{10}(30a + 1)$$ **Step 4 — Calibration at $t = 60$ min.** At one hour, the curve must reach approximately $945^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$: $$945 = 20 + K \log_{10}(60a + 1)$$ **Step 5 — Solving the system.** Subtracting the two equations: $$103 = K \bigl[\log_{10}(60a + 1) - \log_{10}(30a + 1)\bigr]$$ Using the logarithmic identity $\log p - \log q = \log(p/q)$: $$103 = K \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{60a + 1}{30a + 1}\right)$$ By iterative solution, the fitted constants are $K = 345$ and $a = 8$ (these are the **IMO/ISO canonical values** that best match compartment fire data across multiple experimental studies). **Step 6 — Final canonical form.** Therefore: $$\boxed{T(t) = T_0 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8t + 1)}$$ This is the equation codified in **IMO FTP Code Part 3, Appendix 1** and **ISO 834-1**. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Heat Release Rate from a Cable Tray A **15 m²** section of shipboard cable insulation is tested in a cone calorimeter at an incident heat flux of **50 kW/m²**. The measured mass loss rate is **0.012 kg/s**, and the effective heat of combustion is **22 MJ/kg** with a combustion efficiency of **0.85**. **Step 1:** Convert heat of combustion to kJ/kg: $$h_c = 22 \, \mathrm{MJ/kg} = 22\,000 \, \mathrm{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the HRR equation: $$\dot{Q} = \chi \cdot \dot{m} \cdot h_c = 0.85 \times 0.012 \times 22\,000$$ **Step 3:** Compute intermediate result: $$0.85 \times 0.012 = 0.0102$$ **Step 4:** Final multiplication: $$\dot{Q} = 0.0102 \times 22\,000 = 224.4 \, \mathrm{kW}$$ **Step 5:** Express per unit area (if test area = 0.01 m²): $$\dot{q}'' = \frac{224.4}{0.01} = 22\,440 \, \mathrm{kW/m²} = 22.44 \, \mathrm{MW/m²}$$ **Interpretation:** This HRR far exceeds the threshold for **flashover potential in ship compartments** (typically 1 MW total). The material would be classified as a **high fire load** and would not qualify for unrestricted use under FTP Part 1 unless properly protected. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Material Acceptance under FTP Code | Material Use | Applicable FTP Part | Test Method | Key Threshold | Decision if Failed | |---|---|---|---|---| | Bulkhead (load-bearing) | Part 3 | ISO 834 furnace | 60 min integrity & insulation | Re-design or add insulation | | Corridor ceiling | Part 5 | Surface flammability | Spread Index ≤ 20 | Substitute with compliant product | | Cabin mattress | Part 9 | Cigarette + match test | No progressive smouldering; < 5 cm char | Quarantine entire batch | | Cable insulation | Part 1 + Part 2 | Non-combustibility + smoke | ΔT ≤ 30°C; Ds,max ≤ 250 | Replace with low-smoke halogen-free (LSHF) type | | Primary deck covering | Part 6 | ASTM E648 radiant floor | CHF ≥ 1.1 kW/m² | Add protective finish | | Fire door (A-60) | Part 4 | Hose stream + furnace | Self-close < 1 s; no passage of flame 60 min | Adjust closer mechanism | ## 3.4 Analogy Check — Why Both "Small" and "Full-Scale" Tests? - **Small-scale tests** (e.g., non-combustibility at 750°C, cone calorimeter) are **screening** tools: cheap, fast, reproducible. They classify materials by intrinsic property. - **Full-scale tests** (e.g., A-60 furnace, room corner test) are **performance** tests: they evaluate the material **as installed**, including joints, fixings, and adjacent materials. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** A material passing small-scale tests does **not** automatically pass full-scale tests. Both layers of testing are mandatory under SOLAS for structural divisions. This is a frequent Part-A question. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart — The FTP Code Test Selection Process The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the decision logic a naval architect or certifying engineer follows to determine the **applicable FTP test** for a given shipboard material. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Material or Component to be Used Onboard] --> B{Is it a structural
division A, B, or F class?} B -- Yes --> C[FTP Part 3
Full-Scale Furnace Test
ISO 834 Curve] B -- No --> D{Is it a fire door
or shutter?} D -- Yes --> E[FTP Part 4
Self-Closing Test] D -- No --> F{Is it a surface
finish?} F -- Yes --> G{Is it a deck
or bulkhead lining?} G -- Bulkhead or Ceiling --> H[FTP Part 5
Surface Flammability
Spread Index <= 20] G -- Floor or Deck --> I[FTP Part 6
Critical Radiant Flux
CHF >= 1.1 kW/m2] F -- No --> J{Is it furniture
or textile?} J -- Upholstered Furniture --> K[FTP Part 8
Cigarette + Butane Flame] J -- Bedding --> L[FTP Part 9
Bedding Test] J -- Vertical Textile --> M[FTP Part 7
Vertical Textile Test] F -- No --> N{Is it a non-combustible
material like insulation?} N -- Yes --> O[FTP Part 1
Non-Combustibility Furnace
750 deg C] N -- No --> P[All materials require
FTP Part 2
Smoke and Toxicity] C --> Q[Issue Type Approval Certificate] E --> Q H --> Q I --> Q K --> Q L --> Q M --> Q O --> Q P --> Q Q --> R[Install on Ship
Subject to PSC Verification] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Anatomy of an A-60 Division Fire Test Setup This block diagram shows the **experimental arrangement** used in FTP Part 3 for fire-resistance testing of bulkheads and decks. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FurnaceChamber[FURNACE CHAMBER] Burners[Gas Burners
4 to 8 Nozzles
Calibrated for ISO 834] Thermocouples1[Furnace Thermocouples
Plate Thermometers
9 locations] end TestSpecimen[TEST SPECIMEN
Bulkhead or Deck
Typical 3m x 3m] --> FurnaceChamber FurnaceChamber --> HeatTransfer[Heat Transfer Through
Steel, Insulation, Plating] HeatTransfer --> UnexposedSide[UNEXPOSED SIDE
Disc Thermocouples
Cotton Wool Pads] UnexposedSide --> IntegrityCheck[Integrity Check
No flame passage for 60 min] UnexposedSide --> InsulationCheck[Insulation Check
Avg temp rise <= 140 C
Max single point <= 180 C] Burners --> FurnaceChamber Thermocouples1 --> ControlUnit[Data Acquisition
and Furnace Control] UnexposedSide --> ControlUnit ControlUnit --> Report[Fire Test Report
Tested by Notified Body
Witnessed by Class] Report --> TAC[Type Approval Certificate
Issued to Manufacturer] ``` ## 4.3 Flowchart — Material Approval Workflow on a Shipyard This diagram tracks the **end-to-end workflow** from material procurement to final installation under the FTP Code. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Material Specification
by Ship Design Office] --> B[Selection of Manufacturer
with Valid Type Approval] B --> C[Receipt of Material
at Shipyard] C --> D{Documents Complete?
Test Report, TAC, DoC} D -- No --> E[Reject Material
Quarantine and Return] D -- Yes --> F[In-House QC
Density, Thickness, Visual] F --> G{QC Pass?} G -- No --> E G -- Yes --> H[Store in Designated Area
with Traceability Tag] H --> I[Installation by Yard
Per Approved Drawing] I --> J[Site Inspection by
Classification Surveyor] J --> K{Compliance Verified?} K -- No --> L[Rework or Replace] K -- Yes --> M[Final Fit-Out Sign-Off
Flag State Acceptance] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:** In board questions, schematic questions often ask you to "label the components of a fire test furnace" or "explain the workflow for material approval". The Mermaid block diagrams above can be redrawn by hand in the answer script, focusing on the **five critical nodes**: Test Specimen, Furnace, Unexposed Side Instrumentation, Control Unit, and Final Report. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A] **"Define the FTP Code. List any four parts of the FTP Code and state their purpose."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code**, adopted by the International Maritime Organization under **Resolution MSC.307(88)**, is the international standard for evaluating the fire properties of materials, components, and structures used on ships. It is referenced under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 3**, making it mandatory for all SOLAS-regulated vessels. Four parts of the FTP Code: 1. **Part 1** – Non-combustibility test (determines if a material is non-combustible at 750°C). 2. **Part 2** – Smoke and toxicity test (measures smoke density and toxic gas emission). 3. **Part 3** – Fire test for "A", "B", and "F" class divisions (full-scale fire resistance). 4. **Part 5** – Surface flammability test (determines flame spread index for surface finishes). ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B] **"Distinguish between 'reaction to fire' and 'fire resistance' with one example each."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Reaction to Fire | Fire Resistance | |---|---|---| | Definition | How a material contributes to the growth and spread of fire when exposed to an ignition source. | Ability of a structural assembly to maintain stability, integrity, and insulation under standard fire conditions. | | Test Scale | Small or medium (cone calorimeter, radiant panel). | Full-scale (furnace, room corner). | | Outcome | Material classification (e.g., non-combustible, low flame spread). | Time rating (e.g., A-60, B-30). | | Example | Cone calorimeter test on cable insulation (FTP Part 2). | A-60 bulkhead furnace test (FTP Part 3, 60 min). | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the procedure for the **non-combustibility test** as per FTP Code Part 1. State the pass/fail criteria clearly. **(b) [7 Marks]** A specimen of mass **80 g** is subjected to the non-combustibility test. The maximum furnace temperature rise is recorded as **28°C**, and the final mass of the specimen is **46 g**. Determine whether the specimen passes the non-combustibility criteria. Justify with calculations. #### Model Solution **(a) Procedure for FTP Part 1 Non-Combustibility Test (7 Marks):** 1. **Apparatus:** A vertical cylindrical furnace with an internal diameter of **75 mm** and a height of **150 mm**, capable of maintaining a uniform temperature of **750°C ± 5°C** for the duration of the test. 2. **Specimen preparation:** Cylindrical specimens of diameter **45 mm** and height **50 mm** are conditioned at **60°C for 20 hours** and then cooled in a desiccator to ambient temperature. 3. **Specimen placement:** The specimen is suspended in the furnace on a wire mesh holder with thermocouples embedded at the geometric centre and on the surface. 4. **Test duration:** The specimen is exposed for a total of **30 minutes**. 5. **Measurements recorded:** - Furnace temperature (maintained at 750°C ± 5°C). - Surface temperature of the specimen. - Centre temperature of the specimen. - Duration of any sustained flaming. - Mass loss of the specimen. **Pass/Fail Criteria (3 of 3 must be met):** - Average furnace temperature rise due to the specimen: **≤ 30°C**. - Average specimen surface temperature rise: **≤ 30°C**. - Mass loss of the specimen: **≤ 50%** of original mass. - No sustained flaming lasting more than **10 seconds**. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** Given: - Initial mass $m_i = 80$ g - Final mass $m_f = 46$ g - Maximum furnace temperature rise $\Delta T_f = 28^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ **Step 1 — Check mass loss criterion:** $$\Delta m_f = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} = \frac{80 - 46}{80} = \frac{34}{80} = 0.425 = 42.5\%$$ **Step 2 — Compare with acceptance limit:** $$42.5\% \le 50\% \quad \checkmark \text{ (Passes)}$$ **Step 3 — Check furnace temperature rise criterion:** $$\Delta T_f = 28^{\circ}\mathrm{C} \le 30^{\circ}\mathrm{C} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Passes)}$$ **Step 4 — Conclusion:** Since both the mass loss (42.5%) and the furnace temperature rise (28°C) satisfy the FTP Part 1 limits, **the specimen passes the non-combustibility test** and can be classified as a **non-combustible material** under the FTP Code, subject to confirmation that the surface temperature and flaming criteria are also met. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **standard temperature-time curve** (ISO 834) used in fire-resistance testing of A-class divisions. State its significance and key numerical values at 0, 30, and 60 minutes. **(b) [7 Marks]** A steel bulkhead is protected with **50 mm** of mineral wool insulation. The thermal conductivity of the insulation is $k = 0.04 \, \mathrm{W/(m \cdot K)}$. The unexposed-side maximum permissible temperature rise is **180°C** above ambient. If the bulkhead is exposed to the ISO 834 fire for 60 minutes, estimate whether the insulation thickness is adequate using a one-dimensional steady-state heat conduction approximation. Use furnace temperature at 60 min = **945°C** and ambient = **20°C**. #### Model Solution **(a) ISO 834 Standard Temperature-Time Curve (7 Marks):** **Sketch description (verbal, to be drawn in script):** - X-axis: Time in minutes (0 to 120). - Y-axis: Furnace temperature in °C (0 to 1200). - Curve: A logarithmically rising curve starting at 20°C, rising steeply in the first 10–15 minutes, then gradually flattening. **Equation:** $$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$ **Key numerical values:** - At $t = 0$ min: $T(0) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(1) = \mathbf{20^{\circ}C}$ - At $t = 30$ min: $T(30) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(241) = 20 + 345 \times 2.382 = \mathbf{842^{\circ}C}$ - At $t = 60$ min: $T(60) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(481) = 20 + 345 \times 2.682 = \mathbf{945^{\circ}C}$ - At $t = 120$ min: $T(120) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(961) = 20 + 345 \times 2.983 = \mathbf{1049^{\circ}C}$ **Significance:** - Simulates a fully developed cellulosic compartment fire. - Used universally for testing A-class and B-class divisions in FTP Part 3. - Provides a **common reference fire severity** across all laboratories and testing bodies. **(b) Insulation Adequacy Check (7 Marks):** Given: - $L = 0.050$ m - $k = 0.04 \, \mathrm{W/(m \cdot K)}$ - $\Delta T_{\max} = 180^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ on unexposed side - $T_{\mathrm{furnace}}(60) = 945^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ - $T_0 = 20^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ **Step 1 — Compute the temperature difference across the insulation:** $$\Delta T = T_{\mathrm{furnace}} - T_{\mathrm{unexposed,max}} = 945 - (20 + 180) = 945 - 200 = 745^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$$ **Step 2 — Use Fourier's law of heat conduction (steady-state 1-D):** $$q'' = \frac{k \cdot \Delta T}{L}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$q'' = \frac{0.04 \times 745}{0.050} = \frac{29.8}{0.050} = 596 \, \mathrm{W/m^2} = 0.596 \, \mathrm{kW/m^2}$$ **Step 4 — Apply criterion (heat flux through the wall must be low enough that the unexposed-side temperature rise is bounded by the bulkhead's thermal mass).** For an A-60 division, the typical acceptance requires that **heat flux through the wall is below ~5 kW/m²** to prevent piloted ignition on the unexposed side. $$q'' = 0.596 \, \mathrm{kW/m^2} \ll 5 \, \mathrm{kW/m^2} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** The 50 mm mineral wool insulation is **adequate** for an A-60 rating under the steady-state approximation. In reality, the transient response and the steel bulkhead's thermal mass would further enhance the insulation performance. However, the **one-dimensional steady-state** analysis is conservative; the actual system would perform even better. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the FTP Code name in full** on first reference — always write "International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code), Resolution MSC.307(88)". > 2. **Mixing up A, B, C, and F class** ratings: A = 60 min, B = 30 min, C = no rating required, F = fire door with self-closing. > 3. **Using the wrong base for logs in the ISO 834 curve** — it is $\log_{10}$ (base 10), **never** $\ln$ (natural log). Examiners award zero marks for natural log in this formula. > 4. **Skipping units** in numerical problems — always show $T$ in °C, $t$ in minutes, $q''$ in kW/m². > 5. **Not stating the acceptance limit explicitly** in numerical solutions — just calculating is not enough; you must compare with the FTP limit and write "Passes" or "Fails" with the threshold value. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **FTP Code = IMO Res. MSC.307(88)** — referenced in **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 3**, mandatory for all SOLAS ships. - ✅ The Code has **11 Parts** (Part 0 to Part 10); Part 0 gives general rules, Parts 1–10 cover specific tests. - ✅ **Part 1** = Non-combustibility at **750°C**; pass criteria: ΔT ≤ 30°C, mass loss ≤ 50%, no sustained flaming. - ✅ **Part 2** = Smoke & toxicity (Ds,max ≤ 250; CIT ≤ 0.4 at 4 min). - ✅ **Part 3** = A/B/F class divisions tested against the **ISO 834 fire curve**. - ✅ **Part 5** = Surface flammability with a **spread index ≤ 20**. - ✅ **Part 6** = Deck covering test, **CHF ≥ 1.1 kW/m²** for Class 1. - ✅ **Standard fire curve equation:** $T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$. - ✅ Key numerical values: $T(0) = 20^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$; $T(30) = 842^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$; $T(60) = 945^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$; $T(120) = 1049^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. - ✅ **Reaction to fire** = small/medium scale material property tests; **fire resistance** = full-scale structural performance test. - ✅ **Type Approval Certificate (TAC)** is the legal document proving material compliance; it is verified by **Classification Society surveyors** and **Port State Control (PSC)** officers. - ✅ Both small-scale AND full-scale tests are mandatory for structural materials — small-scale success does NOT waive full-scale testing. - ✅ The HRR formula is $\dot{Q} = \chi \cdot \dot{m} \cdot h_c$, used to quantify fire load in ship compartments. - ✅ Shipboard fire hazards are amplified by **confined geometry, restricted ventilation, and limited escape routes** — the FTP Code is specifically engineered to address these marine conditions.

topics.title: Purpose of FTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Purpose of FTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)** is a unified international regulatory framework adopted by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) that standardizes the fire test procedures, fire safety engineering procedures, and verification methodologies for materials, structural elements, and equipment used aboard ships, ensuring a globally harmonized benchmark for fire safety compliance under the SOLAS Convention (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea, 1974).

In KTU 2024 scheme maritime safety parlance, the FTP Code is officially referred to as **Resolution MSC.307(88)** — adopted by the Maritime Safety Committee (MSC) of the IMO at its 88th session in November 2010, and made mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter II-2 (Construction – Fire protection, fire detection, and fire extinction)** through amendments adopted by MSC.339(91).

The acronym breakdown is as follows:
- **F** — Fire
- **T** — Test
- **P** — Procedures (Fire Test Procedures)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you walk into a pharmacy to buy medicine. You don't just trust the label — you trust that the medicine has been **tested in a certified lab** following standardized protocols. The FTP Code is the "lab testing protocol" of the maritime world. Every piece of insulation, every door, every cable, and every wall panel on a ship must pass a strict laboratory fire test before it can be legally fitted onto a vessel. Without FTP, every country would test materials differently — like having different driving rules in every state. FTP unifies this.

Think of a ship as a floating city. A fire on a ship at sea is catastrophic — there is no fire brigade coming in 5 minutes. So before any material ever touches a ship's deck, it must be **scientifically proven** in a furnace or test chamber that it will not accelerate fire, produce lethal smoke, or fail prematurely. The FTP Code defines exactly how that proof must be obtained.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Metrics Under FTP Code:**
> - **Standard Fire Test Duration:** **60 minutes** for "A" Class divisions, **30 minutes** for "B" Class divisions.
> - **Standard Furnace Temperature Rise (ISO 834 Curve):** $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20$, where $T$ is in °C and $t$ is time in minutes.
> - **Heat Flux Standard for Surface Flammability:** **50 kW/m²** (critical radiant flux testing).
> - **Smoke & Toxicity Index Limits:** $\text{CO} \leq 1450 \text{ ppm·min}$, $\text{CO}_2 \leq 90000 \text{ ppm·min}$, $\text{HCl} \leq 600 \text{ ppm·min}$, $\text{HBr} \leq 600 \text{ ppm·min}$, $\text{HCN} \leq 140 \text{ ppm·min}$, $\text{HF} \leq 240 \text{ ppm·min}$, $\text{SO}_2 \leq 1200 \text{ ppm·min}$.
> - **Non-combustibility Furnace Temperature:** **750 °C** ± **5 °C** (for materials to qualify as non-combustible per FTP Part 1).

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of FTP

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose of FTP Code rests on three foundational pillars:**
> 1. **Standardization** — One test, one global benchmark, applicable at any shipyard worldwide.
> 2. **Safety Assurance** — Empirical, repeatable proof of fire performance of materials and assemblies.
> 3. **Regulatory Mandate** — Mandatory under SOLAS, enforceable by Port State Control (PSC) and Flag State Administrations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of FTP — Why It Exists

The FTP Code was conceived to address a **catastrophic historical problem** in maritime safety: before its adoption, fire test procedures were scattered across **multiple FTP Resolutions** (FTP/Res.1 through FTP/Res.6), each with different testing rigs, methodologies, and acceptance criteria. This fragmented regulatory landscape resulted in:

- **Non-reciprocal recognition** of test certificates between flag states.
- **Material re-testing** for every ship delivered to a different nationality of buyer.
- **Loopholes** where substandard materials could pass lenient national tests.
- **Higher compliance costs** for shipbuilders due to repeated certification.

The IMO consolidated all six resolutions into a **single, integrated, performance-based code** — the modern FTP Code. The **purpose**, therefore, is multilayered:

### 2.1.1 Purpose 1: Uniformity of Fire Test Procedures Worldwide

A fire test certificate issued under the FTP Code in **South Korea** is valid for a vessel flagged in **Panama, Marshall Islands, Liberia, Singapore, or Malta** — without re-testing. This is the principle of **"test once, accept everywhere."**

### 2.1.2 Purpose 2: Performance-Based Rather Than Prescriptive Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FTP moves away from telling manufacturers *"use asbestos"* or *"use steel of 4 mm"* (prescriptive). Instead, it tells them: *"Whatever material you use, it MUST survive this furnace test for X minutes, must not exceed Y smoke opacity, and must not emit Z toxic gases."* This encourages **innovation** while maintaining safety.

### 2.1.3 Purpose 3: Integration with SOLAS Chapter II-2

The FTP Code is the **technical annex** that gives SOLAS Chapter II-2 its teeth. SOLAS says *"use non-combustible materials"* — FTP defines **what "non-combustible" actually means scientifically**.

### 2.1.4 Purpose 4: Protection of Life, Property, and the Marine Environment

By enforcing strict fire testing:
- **Crew and passenger lives** are protected (reduced smoke toxicity).
- **Vessel asset value** is preserved (structural fire integrity).
- **Marine pollution** is minimized (preventing ship loss that leads to oil spills).

## 2.2 Structure of the FTP Code — Seven Parts

The FTP Code consists of **7 operative parts**, each addressing a specific fire test scenario:

| Part | Subject | Test Standard Basis | Purpose |
|:-----|:--------|:--------------------|:--------|
| **Part 1** | Non-combustibility Test | ISO 1182 | Defines "non-combustible material" — materials that do not burn at **750 °C**. |
| **Part 2** | Smoke & Toxicity Test | ISO 5659-2 | Quantifies smoke density ($\text{D}_s$) and toxic gas emission limits. |
| **Part 3** | Fire Resistance Test | ISO 834 / IMO A.754(18) | Determines structural fire endurance of "A", "B", and "F" class divisions. |
| **Part 4** | Surface Flammability Test | IMO Resolution A.653(16) | Measures critical radiant flux of floor coverings. |
| **Part 5** | Test for "A", "B", and "F" Class Divisions | ISO 834 / IMO A.754(18) | Verifies integrity (E) and insulation (I) of bulkheads and decks. |
| **Part 6** | Test for Fire Door Control Systems | — | Ensures self-closing fire doors function under fire exposure. |
| **Part 7** | Equivalent Fire Test Procedures | ISO 9705 / Room Corner Test | Alternative full-scale room-corner fire tests. |
| **Annex** | Fire Safety Engineering (FSE) Procedures | Performance-based design | Allows engineering analysis as an alternative to prescriptive testing. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE — memorize these formulas and their variables.**

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|:-:|:------------------|:------------------|:-------------------|:------------|
| 1 | Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834) | $T(t) = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20$ | $T$ = furnace temperature (°C), $t$ = time (min) | Defines the time-temperature exposure for fire resistance tests. |
| 2 | Time-Temperature Equivalence | $\Delta T = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | $\Delta T$ = temperature rise above ambient (°C) | Used to compare fire exposure severity. |
| 3 | Heat Release Rate (HRR) | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c$ | $\dot{Q}$ = HRR (kW), $\dot{m}$ = mass loss rate (kg/s), $\Delta h_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg) | Quantifies fire severity in surface flammability tests. |
| 4 | Specific Optical Density (Smoke) | $D_s = \dfrac{V}{\Lambda \cdot A} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | $D_s$ = specific optical density, $V$ = chamber volume (m³), $A$ = exposed area (m²), $I_0/I$ = light transmission ratio | Measures smoke obscuration per FTP Part 2. |
| 5 | Critical Radiant Flux (CRF) | $q''_{cr} = q''_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot x}$ | $q''_{cr}$ = critical heat flux (kW/m²), $k$ = attenuation coefficient (mm⁻¹), $x$ = depth (mm) | Determines floor covering fire spread potential. |
| 6 | Toxic Gas Index | $\text{Index} = \sum_{i} \left(c_i \cdot t\right)_{\text{measured}} \div \left(c_i \cdot t\right)_{\text{limit}}$ | $c_i$ = gas concentration (ppm), $t$ = time (min) | Aggregated toxicity metric per FTP Part 2 limits. |
| 7 | Insulation Criterion (I) | $T_{\text{avg,unexposed}} \leq T_{\text{ambient}} + 140\,°\text{C}$ | $T$ = temperature (°C) | Pass/fail criterion for "A" and "B" class divisions. |
| 8 | Integrity Criterion (E) | No flame/gas passage for full test duration | — | Pass/fail for openings, cracks, hot spots. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **"A Class"** with **"B Class"** divisions. *A-60* means **60 minutes** fire resistance; *B-15* means **15 minutes**. The letter (A/B/F) defines the *type* (structural/window/door), and the *number* defines *minutes*.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Maritime Engineering

The FTP Code is invoked daily in:

- **Shipyard Material Selection** — Insulation manufacturers (e.g., Rockwool, Armacell) certify every batch.
- **Classification Society Surveying** — DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, ABS audit FTP compliance.
- **Port State Control (PSC) Inspections** — Random ships are detained for non-compliant materials.
- **New-Build Design Approval** — Naval architects must submit FTP-compliant test certificates.
- **Reflagging & Repairs** — When a 20-year-old ship changes flag, FTP standards determine if older materials are grandfathered or must be replaced.

## 2.5 Why FTP Is Mandatory — The Regulatory Chain

The chain of legal enforcement is:

$$\boxed{\text{IMO MSC Resolution} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SOLAS Chapter II-2} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{FTP Code} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Flag State Rules} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Classification Society Certification} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Onboard Compliance}}$$

This ensures that **a single missing FTP certificate can legally prevent a ship from sailing**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834)

The FTP Code's fire resistance test exposes specimens to a **standardized time-temperature curve** defined by ISO 834. The derivation of this curve follows from a logarithmic heat-transfer model:

### Step 1: Define the Heat Transfer Mechanism
The furnace applies **convective and radiative heating** to the test specimen. The empirical model assumes that the **logarithmic temperature rise** corresponds to the thermal inertia of a semi-infinite solid.

### Step 2: Empirical Equation (ISO 834)
The standard furnace temperature $T(t)$ in degrees Celsius at time $t$ minutes is given by:

$$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

### Step 3: Justify the Constants
- $T_0 = 20\,°\text{C}$ (standard ambient reference temperature per FTP Part 3).
- $345$ is a constant derived from the **Stefan-Boltzmann law** integration over the standard fuel (gasoline/diesel) combustion profile, calibrated against historical compartment fire data.
- The factor $8$ originates from the **time-temperature ratio** typical of hydrocarbon pool fires.

### Step 4: Sample Numerical Calculation
At $t = 30$ minutes:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(30) &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 30 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(241) \\
&= 20 + 345 \times 2.382 \\
&= 20 + 821.79 \\
&= 841.79\,°\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** This matches the ISO 834 reference table (840 °C – 842 °C range), confirming correctness.

### Step 5: Application to "A-60" Division
For an A-60 division, the specimen must withstand **60 minutes** of this curve:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(60) &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 60 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(481) \\
&= 20 + 345 \times 2.682 \\
&= 20 + 925.30 \\
&= 945.30\,°\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** After 60 minutes, the furnace temperature reaches ~945 °C. The unexposed side of the division must remain below $T_{\text{ambient}} + 140\,°\text{C} = 160\,°\text{C}$ (insulation criterion).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Smoke Specific Optical Density (FTP Part 2)

The smoke density in FTP Part 2 is measured using a **closed chamber** with a photometric light source. The Beer-Lambert law governs optical absorption:

### Step 1: Beer-Lambert Law
$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity (lux).
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity (lux).
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient (m⁻¹).
- $L$ = optical path length (m).

### Step 2: Convert to Optical Density
The dimensionless optical density is:

$$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \log_{10}(e)$$

### Step 3: Specific Optical Density ($D_s$)
To normalize for chamber geometry and specimen area:

$$D_s = \dfrac{V}{\Lambda \cdot A} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

Where:
- $V$ = chamber volume ($\text{m}^3$).
- $\Lambda$ = optical path length (m).
- $A$ = exposed specimen area ($\text{m}^2$).

### Step 4: Sample Numerical Evaluation
Suppose $V = 0.5\,\text{m}^3$, $\Lambda = 0.5\,\text{m}$, $A = 0.01\,\text{m}^2$ (typical chamber), and $I/I_0 = 0.01$ (99% obscuration):

$$\begin{aligned}
D_s &= \dfrac{0.5}{0.5 \times 0.01} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{0.01}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{0.5}{0.005} \cdot \log_{10}(100) \\
&= 100 \cdot 2 \\
&= 200
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: FTP Part 2 Acceptance Limit
Per FTP Part 2, $D_s \leq 200$ at 4 minutes into the test (smoldering mode). If $D_s > 200$, the material **fails** the smoke test.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Radiant Flux (FTP Part 4)

For floor coverings, the critical radiant flux (CRF) determines the **radiant heat flux at which flame spread becomes self-sustaining**.

### Step 1: Heat Flux Decay Model
At depth $x$ from the heated surface:

$$q''(x) = q''_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot x}$$

### Step 2: Critical Condition
Self-sustained flame spread occurs when surface temperature reaches the **ignition temperature** $T_{\text{ig}}$, giving:

$$q''_{cr} = q''_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot x_c}$$

Where $x_c$ is the critical depth.

### Step 3: Numerical Example
Let $q''_0 = 50\,\text{kW/m}^2$, $k = 0.5\,\text{mm}^{-1}$, $x_c = 1.5\,\text{mm}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
q''_{cr} &= 50 \cdot e^{-0.5 \times 1.5} \\
&= 50 \cdot e^{-0.75} \\
&= 50 \times 0.4724 \\
&= 23.62\,\text{kW/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: FTP Part 4 Limit
For floor coverings on passenger ships, $q''_{cr} \geq 7.8\,\text{kW/m}^2$ (low flame spread). Our example exceeds this comfortably — material **passes**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — FTP Test Outcomes Summary

| FTP Part | Test Name | Pass Criterion | Typical Failure Reason |
|:---------|:----------|:---------------|:-----------------------|
| **Part 1** | Non-combustibility | Temperature rise $\leq 30\,°\text{C}$; mass loss $\leq 50\%$ | Organic materials (e.g., untreated plywood). |
| **Part 2** | Smoke & Toxicity | $D_s \leq 200$; toxic gas indices within limits | PVC, polyurethane foams. |
| **Part 3** | Fire Resistance | E + I criteria for full duration | Inadequate insulation thickness. |
| **Part 4** | Surface Flammability | CRF meets category requirement | Carpets with high flame spread. |
| **Part 5** | A/B/F Class Divisions | Integrity + Insulation maintained | Penetration by cables, pipes. |
| **Part 6** | Fire Door Control | Self-closing within $\leq 25$ sec | Failed door closers, warped frames. |
| **Part 7** | Room Corner Test | HRR $\leq 1$ MW peak | Highly combustible wall linings. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FTP Code's Position Within the Maritime Regulatory Framework

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the FTP Code sits as the **technical backbone** of SOLAS fire safety regulations, connecting international law to onboard material testing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["UN Specialized Agency"] --> nodeB["International Maritime Organization IMO"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Maritime Safety Committee MSC"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Resolution MSC.307 88 - FTP Code"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["SOLAS Chapter II-2 Fire Safety"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["FTP Code 7 Parts"]
    nodeF --> nodeF1["Part 1 Non-combustibility"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["Part 2 Smoke Toxicity"]
    nodeF --> nodeF3["Part 3 Fire Resistance"]
    nodeF --> nodeF4["Part 4 Surface Flammability"]
    nodeF --> nodeF5["Part 5 A B F Class Divisions"]
    nodeF --> nodeF6["Part 6 Fire Door Systems"]
    nodeF --> nodeF7["Part 7 Room Corner Test"]
    nodeF --> nodeF8["Annex FSE Procedures"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG["Approved Testing Laboratories"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG
    nodeF3 --> nodeG
    nodeF4 --> nodeG
    nodeF5 --> nodeG
    nodeF6 --> nodeG
    nodeF7 --> nodeG
    nodeF8 --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Material Test Certificates"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Classification Society Approval"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Flag State Endorsement"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Shipboard Installation"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Port State Control PSC Inspection"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of an FTP Compliance Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Material Manufacturer Submits Sample"] --> stepB["FTP Test Method Selection"]
    stepB --> stepC["Sample Conditioning at 23C 50 percent RH"]
    stepC --> stepD["Laboratory Furnace Exposure"]
    stepD --> stepE["Continuous Data Acquisition"]
    stepE --> stepF{"Pass Criteria Met"}
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG["Issue Type Approval Certificate"]
    stepF -- No --> stepH["Reject Material"]
    stepG --> stepI["Annual Audit Testing"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Maintain Validity of Certificate"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: FTP Test Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["Material Type"] --> p2{"Structural or Decorative"}
    p2 -- Structural --> p3["Apply FTP Part 3 5"]
    p2 -- Decorative --> p4["Apply FTP Part 2 4"]
    p3 --> p5["Conduct ISO 834 Furnace Test"]
    p4 --> p6["Conduct Smoke Chamber Test"]
    p5 --> p7{"Fire Resistance >= Required Class"}
    p6 --> p8{"Ds less than 200 and Toxicity within limits"}
    p7 -- Yes --> p9["Compliant Material"]
    p7 -- No --> p10["Non compliant reject"]
    p8 -- Yes --> p9
    p8 -- No --> p10
    p9 --> p11["Install onboard ship"]
    p10 --> p12["Return to manufacturer redesign"]
```

## 4.4 Classification of Fire Divisions by FTP Code

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    fd["Fire Divisions Classification"]
    fd --> fda["A Class Divisions"]
    fd --> fdb["B Class Divisions"]
    fd --> fdc["F Class Divisions"]
    fda --> fda1["A 0 minimum"]
    fda --> fda2["A 15"]
    fda --> fda3["A 30"]
    fda --> fda4["A 60"]
    fda --> fda5["A 120"]
    fdb --> fdb1["B 0"]
    fdb --> fdb2["B 15"]
    fdb --> fdb3["B 30"]
    fdc --> fdc1["F Class Fire Doors Windows"]
    fdc --> fdc2["Tested per FTP Part 6"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the FTP Code and state its primary purpose.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**
>
> The **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)** is an IMO resolution (MSC.307(88)) that standardizes fire testing methods for materials and equipment used on ships. Its **primary purpose** is to provide a **uniform, performance-based, and globally accepted** set of fire test procedures that ensure materials meet SOLAS Chapter II-2 fire safety requirements regardless of where they are tested, thereby protecting **life, property, and the marine environment** from fire risks at sea.

### Question 2: List any three parts of the FTP Code with their respective tests.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks, ~60 words):**
>
> 1. **Part 1** — *Non-combustibility Test* (ISO 1182; furnace at 750 °C).
> 2. **Part 2** — *Smoke and Toxicity Test* (ISO 5659-2; $D_s \leq 200$, toxic gas limits).
> 3. **Part 4** — *Surface Flammability Test* (IMO A.653(16); critical radiant flux for floor coverings).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): The Purpose of FTP Code in Maritime Fire Safety

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the historical background and the main purposes of the FTP Code adopted by the IMO.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **Background:**
> - Before 2010, fire test procedures were scattered across six separate FTP Resolutions (FTP/Res.1 to FTP/Res.6).
> - These resolutions were adopted between 1968 and 1996, each covering different aspects of fire testing.
> - The fragmented nature caused confusion, duplication, and inconsistent acceptance between flag states.
>
> **Adoption:**
> - In **November 2010**, the IMO Maritime Safety Committee at its **88th session** adopted **Resolution MSC.307(88)** — the consolidated FTP Code.
> - It became **mandatory** under SOLAS Chapter II-2 via amendments in **Resolution MSC.339(91)** (2012).
> - The new code entered into force on **1 July 2012**.
>
> **Main Purposes (enumerate 7 purposes for full marks):**
>
> 1. **Standardization** — Single global benchmark replacing multiple national procedures.
> 2. **Mutual Recognition** — Test certificates accepted worldwide without re-testing.
> 3. **Performance-Based Approach** — Encourages innovation while ensuring safety.
> 4. **Integration with SOLAS** — Provides technical annex to Chapter II-2.
> 5. **Protection of Life** — Reduces smoke toxicity and ensures structural integrity.
> 6. **Environmental Protection** — Prevents ship loss and pollution from fire.
> 7. **Cost Reduction** — Eliminates redundant testing for shipbuilders.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the structure of the FTP Code, listing its seven parts and explaining the purpose of Part 1 (Non-combustibility) and Part 2 (Smoke and Toxicity) tests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **Structure of FTP Code (7 Parts + Annex):**
>
> | Part | Title |
> |:-----|:------|
> | Part 1 | Non-combustibility Test |
> | Part 2 | Smoke and Toxicity Test |
> | Part 3 | Fire Resistance Test |
> | Part 4 | Surface Flammability Test |
> | Part 5 | Test for A/B/F Class Divisions |
> | Part 6 | Test for Fire Door Control Systems |
> | Part 7 | Equivalent Fire Test Procedures (Room Corner Test) |
> | Annex | Fire Safety Engineering (FSE) Procedures |
>
> **Part 1 — Non-combustibility Test:**
> - **Standard:** ISO 1182.
> - **Procedure:** Material specimen is placed in a furnace preheated to **750 °C ± 5 °C** for 30 minutes.
> - **Pass criteria:** Furnace temperature rise $\leq 30\,°\text{C}$, mean specimen temperature rise $\leq 50\,°\text{C}$, mass loss $\leq 50\%$.
> - **Purpose:** Defines which materials qualify as "non-combustible" for use in machinery spaces, control stations, and high-risk areas.
>
> **Part 2 — Smoke and Toxicity Test:**
> - **Standard:** ISO 5659-2 (smoke chamber).
> - **Procedure:** Specimen is exposed to a **25 kW/m²** heat flux in a closed chamber; smoke density measured photometrically; combustion gases analyzed.
> - **Pass criteria:** Specific optical density $D_s \leq 200$ at 4 minutes (smoldering mode); toxic gas indices within IMO limits (CO, HCl, HCN, HF, SO₂, NOₓ).
> - **Purpose:** Ensures that burning materials do not produce optically dense smoke or lethal toxic gases that could incapacitate crew/passengers during evacuation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): FTP Code — Application and Testing Methodology

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain with a neat sketch (or functional flow) how a material is tested and certified under the FTP Code for use on a ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **Stage 1: Submission by Manufacturer**
> - Material manufacturer submits representative samples to an **approved testing laboratory** (e.g., IMO-recognized labs like UK HSE, Germanischer Lloyd).
>
> **Stage 2: Sample Preparation**
> - Specimens conditioned at **23 °C ± 2 °C** and **50% ± 5% RH** for 24–48 hours.
>
> **Stage 3: Test Selection**
> - Based on intended application (structural, decorative, insulation), the appropriate FTP Part is selected:
>   - Structural bulkheads → Parts 3 & 5.
>   - Insulation → Part 1 & 2.
>   - Floor coverings → Part 4.
>   - Fire doors → Part 6.
>
> **Stage 4: Fire Testing**
> - Specimen mounted in **standard furnace** or chamber.
> - ISO 834 time-temperature curve applied: $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$.
> - Temperature, smoke, and toxic gases continuously monitored.
>
> **Stage 5: Pass/Fail Determination**
> - Compare results against acceptance criteria (insulation, integrity, $D_s$, toxicity indices).
>
> **Stage 6: Type Approval Certificate Issued**
> - If pass → **Type Approval Certificate (TAC)** issued by the certifying body.
>
> **Stage 7: Onboard Verification**
> - Class surveyor verifies the TAC during ship construction.
> - Annual audit testing ensures continued compliance.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Calculate the standard furnace temperature at $t = 45$ minutes in the ISO 834 fire curve and explain the implications for an "A-60" class division.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **Given:**
> - Standard ISO 834 fire curve: $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$.
> - Time $t = 45$ minutes.
>
> **Calculation:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T(45) &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 45 + 1) \\
> &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(361) \\
> &= 20 + 345 \times 2.5575 \\
> &= 20 + 882.34 \\
> &= 902.34\,°\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$
>
> **Implications for A-60 Class Division:**
>
> 1. **Fire Exposure Duration:** The "60" in A-60 means the division must withstand the ISO 834 curve for **60 minutes**, not just 45 minutes.
>
> 2. **Total Exposure:** At 60 minutes, furnace temperature reaches:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T(60) &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(481) \\
> &= 20 + 345 \times 2.6821 \\
> &= 945.33\,°\text{C}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> 3. **Insulation Criterion:** The unexposed surface temperature must remain $\leq 160\,°\text{C}$ (average) and $\leq 180\,°\text{C}$ (any single point).
>
> 4. **Integrity Criterion:** No passage of flame or hot gases through the division for the full 60 minutes.
>
> 5. **Material Choice:** A-60 divisions typically use **steel bulkheads with 75–100 mm of mineral wool insulation** (e.g., Rockwool SeaRox) to meet these criteria.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in FTP Code Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing A, B, F classes:** Students often interchange "A-60" with "B-60" — they are **different** (steel bulkhead vs. lightweight partition).
> 2. **Forgetting the time component:** A-60 means **60 minutes**, not a generic "high-class" rating.
> 3. **Missing the year of FTP Code adoption:** Examiners love to ask when the FTP Code was adopted (**2010, MSC.307(88)**) — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question.
> 4. **Not mentioning MSC.339(91):** Forgetting that the FTP Code became mandatory through MSC.339(91) loses a mark.
> 5. **Ignoring smoke density limits:** $D_s \leq 200$ is a frequently tested numerical.
> 6. **Forgetting the Annex:** The FTP Code has an Annex on **Fire Safety Engineering (FSE)** — students who skip this lose 1 mark.
> 7. **Mixing units:** Furnace temperature is in **°C**, heat flux in **kW/m²**, smoke density is dimensionless — wrong units = wrong marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Purpose of FTP Code:**
>
> - ✅ FTP Code = **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures**.
> - ✅ Adopted by IMO in **2010** as **Resolution MSC.307(88)**.
> - ✅ Became mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter II-2** via **MSC.339(91)**.
> - ✅ Entered into force on **1 July 2012**.
> - ✅ Replaced **6 older FTP Resolutions** (FTP/Res.1 to FTP/Res.6).
> - ✅ Contains **7 Parts + 1 Annex (FSE)**.
> - ✅ **Part 1** tests **non-combustibility** at **750 °C** (ISO 1182).
> - ✅ **Part 2** tests **smoke & toxicity** ($D_s \leq 200$, ISO 5659-2).
> - ✅ **Part 3** uses **ISO 834 curve** for fire resistance.
> - ✅ **Standard fire curve equation:** $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$.
> - ✅ **A-60** = 60 minutes fire resistance for structural divisions.
> - ✅ **B-15** = 15 minutes fire resistance for lightweight partitions.
> - ✅ Purpose: **standardization, mutual recognition, life safety, environmental protection**.
> - ✅ **Toxic gas limits:** CO ≤ 1450, HCl ≤ 600, HCN ≤ 140 ppm·min.
> - ✅ Integrated with **Classification Societies** (DNV, LR, BV, ABS).
> - ✅ Enforced via **Port State Control (PSC)** inspections worldwide.
> - ✅ **Anne** allows **Fire Safety Engineering** as alternative to prescriptive testing.
> - ✅ Critical for **passenger ships** (especially SOLAS Reg. II-2/Reg.20 for ro-ro).
> - ✅ Tests performed in **approved laboratories** with continuous data logging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to Remember the 7 Parts:** **"No Smoke Fire Surfaced Divisions Doors Equally"**
> → **N**on-combustibility, **S**moke & Toxicity, **F**ire Resistance, **S**urface Flammability, **D**ivisions (A/B/F), **D**oors, **E**quivalent (Room Corner).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relevant SOLAS Regulations and Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relevant SOLAS Regulations and Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is SOLAS?

The **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** is the most important international treaty concerning the **safety of merchant ships**. It is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, a specialized agency of the United Nations headquartered in **London, United Kingdom**.

The first version of SOLAS was adopted in **1914**, in direct response to the **Titanic disaster of 1912**. The convention has been revised multiple times — most notably in **1960** and **1974**. The **SOLAS 1974 Convention**, as amended, is the version currently in force and is considered the **"Constitution of Maritime Safety."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS 1974 (as amended)** is universally regarded as the single most important international convention on maritime safety. As of today, it has **169 Contracting States**, representing approximately **99% of the world gross tonnage** of merchant shipping.

## 1.2 Formal Definition

> **SOLAS Convention:** A multilateral international maritime treaty that lays down minimum standards for the construction, equipment, and operation of ships, compatible with their safety. Flag States are responsible for ensuring that ships under their flag comply with the requirements, and a number of certificates are prescribed in the Convention as proof of such compliance.

*(Source: IMO Official SOLAS Text)*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — SOLAS as the "Maritime Highway Code"

Imagine SOLAS as the **traffic rules, vehicle fitness rules, and emergency protocols combined**, but for ships sailing the world's oceans:

| Highway Code Equivalent | SOLAS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Speed limits | Structural stability rules (Chapter II-1) |
| Brake & light standards | Fire protection systems (Chapter II-2) |
| Seatbelts & airbags | Life-saving appliances (Chapter III) |
| Radio communication | Radio communications (Chapter IV) |
| Road signs & navigation | Safety of navigation (Chapter V) |
| Cargo securing rules | Carriage of cargo (Chapter VI/ VII) |
| Driving license / fleet operator rules | ISM Code (Chapter IX) |
| Anti-terrorism border security | ISPS Code (Chapter XI-2) |

In simple terms: **SOLAS tells shipbuilders how to build a ship, tells shipowners how to run a ship, and tells seafarers how to survive when things go wrong at sea.**

## 1.4 Why SOLAS Was Created — The Genesis

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Trigger — RMS Titanic (15 April 1912):**
> - **1,517 lives lost** out of 2,223 aboard.
> - Inquiry revealed: insufficient lifeboats, no standardized radio watch, weak hull plating.
> - Result: First **SOLAS Convention adopted on 20 January 1914**.

The **Titanic disaster** demonstrated that without international rules, shipping disasters would keep recurring. SOLAS essentially "standardized survival" across all nations.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

- **Flag State:** The country where the ship is registered. Responsible for enforcing SOLAS on its vessels.
- **Port State Control (PSC):** Inspection of foreign ships in national ports to verify compliance.
- **Recognized Organization (RO):** Classification societies (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas, ABS) authorized by Flag States to conduct surveys and issue certificates.
- **Contracting Government:** A nation that has ratified and is legally bound by SOLAS.
- **Certificate of Compliance:** A document issued to confirm a ship meets specific SOLAS requirements.
- **TAC (Type Approval Certificate):** Issued for equipment approved to SOLAS performance standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & SOLAS Structure

## 2.1 Architecture of SOLAS 1974

SOLAS is structured hierarchically into:

$$\text{SOLAS 1974} = \underbrace{\text{Articles}}_{\text{Legal Framework}} + \underbrace{\text{Annex (Chapters I–XII)}}_{\text{Technical Regulations}} + \underbrace{\text{Codes}}_{\text{Mandatory/Optional Appendices}}$$

There are **12 Chapters** and **several associated Codes** (some mandatory, some recommendatory).

## 2.2 Chapter-Wise Breakdown (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Questions are frequently asked on Chapter II-2 (Fire), Chapter III (LSA), Chapter V (Navigation), and the ISM/ISPS Codes. Master these.

### **Chapter I — General Provisions**

- Definitions, survey requirements, certification.
- Mandates regular **surveys**: Annual, Periodical (5-yearly), and Renewal.
- Issuance of key certificates:
  - Passenger Ship Safety Certificate
  - Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate
  - Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate
  - Cargo Ship Safety Radio Certificate

### **Chapter II-1 — Construction: Subdivision & Stability**

- Watertight subdivision of the hull.
- **Damage stability** requirements: ship must survive flooding of any one compartment.
- Machinery and electrical installations standards.
- Steering gear requirements.

### **Chapter II-2 — Fire Protection, Fire Detection, Fire Extinction** ⭐ *Most Exam-Relevant*

- **FSS Code** (Fire Safety Systems Code) — **mandatory under Chapter II-2**.
- **FTP Code** (Fire Test Procedures Code) — defines test methods for fire-resistant materials.
- Structural fire protection: division of ship into **Main Vertical Zones (MVZs)**.
- Means of escape, fire pumps, fixed fire-extinguishing systems (CO₂, foam, water mist, inert gas).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Test Codes (FTP Code):** Defines how materials are tested for **non-combustibility, fire resistance, surface flammability, and smoke generation**.

### **Chapter III — Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements** ⭐

- **LSA Code** (Life-Saving Appliances Code) — **mandatory under Chapter III**.
- Lifeboats, liferafts, lifejackets, immersion suits, EPIRB, SART.
- **Muster list, abandon ship drills, training requirements.**
- Survival craft capacity rules:
$$\text{Number of Lifeboats} \geq \frac{\text{Total Persons on Board}}{\text{Lifeboat Rated Capacity}}$$

### **Chapter IV — Radiocommunications**

- **GMDSS (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System)** — fully implemented under SOLAS Chapter IV since **1 February 1999**.
- Sea areas A1, A2, A3, A4 — each defines mandatory radio equipment.
- **EPIRB, NAVTEX, INMARSAT, DSC** — key components.

### **Chapter V — Safety of Navigation** ⭐

- Bridge procedures, watchkeeping.
- **AIS (Automatic Identification System)** — mandatory.
- **VDR (Voyage Data Recorder)** — equivalent to "black box."
- **ECDIS** (Electronic Chart Display and Information System).
- Passage planning, navigation warnings.

### **Chapter VI — Carriage of Cargoes and Oil Fuels**

- Stowage and securing of cargo.
- Grain Code (loading of grain).
- Loading/unloading of solid bulk cargoes.

### **Chapter VII — Carriage of Dangerous Goods**

- **IMDG Code (International Maritime Dangerous Goods Code)** — mandatory.
- Classification of dangerous goods into **9 classes**.

### **Chapter VIII — Nuclear Ships**

- Specific safety rules for nuclear-powered merchant ships (rare).

### **Chapter IX — Management for the Safe Operation of Ships** ⭐

- **ISM Code (International Safety Management Code)** — **mandatory**.
- Establishes **Safety Management System (SMS)**.
- Roles: Master, Designated Person Ashore (DPA), Company.

### **Chapter X — Safety Measures for High-Speed Craft (HSC)**

- **HSC Code** (1994/2000).
- Distinct rules for craft exceeding **37 km/h (20 knots)**.

### **Chapter XI-1 — Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety**

- Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR).
- Enhanced surveys for bulk carriers and oil tankers.
- Ship identification number (IMO Number).

### **Chapter XI-2 — Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Security** ⭐

- **ISPS Code (International Ship and Port Facility Security Code)** — mandatory.
- Adopted after **9/11 attacks (2001)**, effective **1 July 2004**.
- **SSO (Ship Security Officer)** and **CSO (Company Security Officer)** mandatory.
- **Ship Security Levels 1, 2, 3.**

### **Chapter XII — Additional Safety Measures for Bulk Carriers**

- Added after bulk carrier casualties (e.g., **MV Derbyshire, 1980**).
- Strengthened hatch covers, double-skin construction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Standards Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lifeboat capacity per person | $\geq 9.15 \,\text{L}$ per person | Litres | LSA Code Reg. 4.4.1.4 |
| Liferaft capacity | $25$ persons max per raft | Persons | LSA Code |
| EPIRB battery life | $\geq 5$ years | Years | SOLAS Ch. IV |
| Damage stability flooding length | $\text{L}_{\text{flood}} \leq 0.03\,\text{L}$ | Metres | SOLAS Ch. II-1 |
| Bulk carrier hatch cover strength | $1.75 \times \text{shear force}$ | kN/m | SOLAS Ch. XII |
| ISPS effective date | $1^{\text{st}}$ July $2004$ | Date | SOLAS Ch. XI-2 |
| GMDSS full implementation date | $1^{\text{st}}$ February $1999$ | Date | SOLAS Ch. IV |
| ISM Code mandatory from | $1^{\text{st}}$ July $1998$ | Date | SOLAS Ch. IX |
| Net tonnage threshold for many regs | $\geq 500\,\text{GT}$ | GT | Most SOLAS regs |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Design Engineers:** Use SOLAS damage stability curves to size compartments.
- **Naval Architects:** Apply SOLAS II-1 subdivision rules for bulkhead placement.
- **Marine Electrical Engineers:** Comply with SOLAS II-1 electrical standards.
- **Marine Operations Managers:** Implement ISM SMS systems.
- **Port Authorities:** Apply ISPS Code for port facility security.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown — SOLAS Compliance Process

## 3.1 The SOLAS Compliance Lifecycle

The **SOLAS compliance process** for any ship follows a strict sequence. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step analytical breakdown:

### **Step 1: Design Approval (New Ship)**

The ship designer ensures the vessel's plans comply with all relevant chapters:
- Hull subdivision (Ch. II-1)
- Fire protection plans (Ch. II-2)
- LSA arrangements (Ch. III)
- Radio installation (Ch. IV)

### **Step 2: Classification Society Survey**

A Recognized Organization (RO) like **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV, ClassNK** verifies compliance and issues the **Class Certificate**.

### **Step 3: Flag State Survey & Certification**

Flag State inspectors (or delegated RO surveyors) conduct:
- **Initial Survey** → before ship enters service.
- **Annual Survey** → every year.
- **Periodical Survey** → within 5 years.
- **Renewal Survey** → every 5 years.

### **Step 4: Issuance of Statutory Certificates**

The Flag State issues the appropriate **SOLAS Certificates** (listed in Section 2.2).

### **Step 5: Continuous Operational Compliance**

- Master and crew follow **ISM Code SMS** procedures.
- Drills are conducted as per Ch. III.
- Navigation follows Ch. V rules.

### **Step 6: Port State Control (PSC) Inspections**

Foreign port authorities may inspect the ship to verify compliance with SOLAS. PSC detention rates drive continuous compliance.

## 3.2 SOLAS Amendment Procedure

SOLAS uses a **Tacit Acceptance Procedure** — amendments enter into force unless a majority of Contracting States object.

$$\text{Adoption (MSC)} \xrightarrow{\text{notification}} \text{Tacit Acceptance Period (24 months default)} \xrightarrow{\text{no objections}} \text{Entry into Force}$$

### **Step-by-Step Logic of the Tacit Amendment Process:**

1. The **Maritime Safety Committee (MSC)** of IMO adopts the amendment.
2. Amendments are communicated to all Contracting States.
3. A default **24-month acceptance period** begins (some amendments specify 12 or 18 months).
4. If **less than one-third** of Contracting States object (and they represent less than 50% of gross tonnage), the amendment is deemed accepted.
5. The amendment enters into force on the specified date.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Tacit Acceptance Procedure** dramatically speeds up maritime regulation adoption. Without it, ratifying amendments could take decades (as it did historically before the 1974 Convention).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Codes Under SOLAS

| Code | Full Name | Mandatory Under | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| ISM Code | International Safety Management Code | SOLAS Chapter IX | Safe operation of ships, SMS |
| ISPS Code | International Ship & Port Facility Security Code | SOLAS Chapter XI-2 | Maritime & port security |
| FSS Code | Fire Safety Systems Code | SOLAS Chapter II-2 | Engineering specifications for fire systems |
| FTP Code | Fire Test Procedures Code | SOLAS Chapter II-2 | Testing methods for fire materials |
| LSA Code | Life-Saving Appliances Code | SOLAS Chapter III | Detailed specs for lifeboats, liferafts, etc. |
| IMDG Code | International Maritime Dangerous Goods Code | SOLAS Chapter VII | Safe carriage of dangerous goods |
| Grain Code | International Code for the Safe Carriage of Grain in Bulk | SOLAS Chapter VI | Grain loading stability |
| HSC Code | International Code of Safety for High-Speed Craft | SOLAS Chapter X | HSC safety standards |
| SPS Code | Code of Safety for Special Purpose Ships | Not mandatory under SOLAS | Special purpose vessels |
| IBC Code | International Bulk Chemical Code | SOLAS + MARPOL | Chemical tanker safety |
| IGC Code | International Gas Carrier Code | SOLAS + MARPOL | LNG/LPG carrier safety |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SOLAS Convention Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[SOLAS Convention 1974] --> nodeB[Articles 1-12 Legal Basis]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Annex with 12 Chapters]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Associated Codes]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Flag State Obligations]
```
