```mermaid
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Survey and Certification]

    nodeC --> ch1[Chapter I: General Provisions]
    nodeC --> ch2[Chapter II-1: Construction Stability]
    nodeC --> ch3[Chapter II-2: Fire Protection]
    nodeC --> ch4[Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances]
    nodeC --> ch5[Chapter IV: Radiocommunications]
    nodeC --> ch6[Chapter V: Safety of Navigation]
    nodeC --> ch7[Chapter VI: Cargo Carriage]
    nodeC --> ch8[Chapter VII: Dangerous Goods]
    nodeC --> ch9[Chapter VIII: Nuclear Ships]
    nodeC --> ch10[Chapter IX: Management ISM]
    nodeC --> ch11[Chapter X: High-Speed Craft]
    nodeC --> ch12[Chapter XI-1: Special Measures Safety]
    nodeC --> ch13[Chapter XI-2: Special Measures Security]
    nodeC --> ch14[Chapter XII: Bulk Carriers]

    nodeD --> code1[ISM Code]
    nodeD --> code2[ISPS Code]
    nodeD --> code3[FSS Code]
    nodeD --> code4[FTP Code]
    nodeD --> code5[LSA Code]
    nodeD --> code6[IMDG Code]
    nodeD --> code7[Grain Code]
    nodeD --> code8[HSC Code]
    nodeD --> code9[IBC Code]
    nodeD --> code10[IGC Code]

    node10[Chapter IX ISM] --> code1
    node13[Chapter XI-2 Security] --> code2
    node3[Chapter II-2 Fire] --> code3
    node3 --> code4
    node4[Chapter III LSA] --> code5
    node7[Chapter VII Dangerous] --> code6
```

## 4.2 SOLAS Compliance Flow (Mermaid Process Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Ship Design Phase] --> stepB[Plan Approval by RO]
    stepB --> stepC[Keel Laying]
    stepC --> stepD[Initial Survey During Construction]
    stepD --> stepE[Trial Trip]
    stepE --> stepF[Flag State Certification]
    stepF --> stepG[Ship Enters Service]
    stepG --> stepH[Annual Surveys]
    stepG --> stepI[Periodical Surveys Every 5 Years]
    stepG --> stepJ[Port State Inspections]
    stepH --> stepK[Continuous ISM SMS Operation]
    stepI --> stepK
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL[Recycling or Renewal after 25 Years]
```

## 4.3 SOLAS Chapter-to-Code Linking Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ch2_2[Chapter II-2 Fire Protection] --> fss[FSS Code]
    ch2_2 --> ftp[FTP Code]
    ch3[Chapter III Life Saving] --> lsa[LSA Code]
    ch7[Chapter VII Dangerous Goods] --> imdg[IMDG Code]
    ch9[Chapter IX Management] --> ism[ISM Code]
    ch10[Chapter X HSC] --> hsc[HSC Code]
    ch11_2[Chapter XI-2 Security] --> isps[ISPS Code]
    ch6[Chapter VI Cargo] --> grain[Grain Code]

    fss -.Implements.-> ffs[Fixed Fire Systems]
    ftp -.Tests.-> fmat[Fire Resistant Materials]
    lsa -.Specifies.-> lb[Lifeboats and Liferafts]
    imdg -.Classifies.-> dg[9 Classes of Dangerous Goods]
    ism -.Mandates.-> sms[Safety Management System]
    hsc -.Defines.-> hscrules[High Speed Craft Rules]
    isps -.Establishes.-> sso[Ship Security Officer]
    grain -.Requires.-> grainstab[Grain Loading Stability]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Q1. Define the SOLAS Convention and mention its year of adoption.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
The **SOLAS Convention (Safety of Life at Sea)** is an international maritime treaty adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** in **1974**. It prescribes minimum standards for the **construction, equipment, and operation** of ships to ensure the safety of life at sea. The current version is **SOLAS 1974, as amended**, which replaced the earlier 1914 and 1960 versions.

### **Q2. What is the purpose of the ISPS Code and under which SOLAS Chapter is it mandatory?** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
The **ISPS Code (International Ship and Port Facility Security Code)** enhances maritime and port security globally. It became mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter XI-2** on **1 July 2004**, in response to post-9/11 security threats. The Code establishes **three security levels** and mandates appointment of **Ship Security Officer (SSO)** and **Company Security Officer (CSO)**.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In Part B, students often **fail to mention the year of adoption and entry into force dates** — this leads to a 2-mark deduction.
> - Avoid writing only the chapter names — **always include associated mandatory Codes**.
> - Many students confuse **ISM Code** (Chapter IX) with **ISPS Code** (Chapter XI-2) — remember **ISM = Safety, ISPS = Security**.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

#### (a) Explain the structure of SOLAS 1974 Convention with its major Chapters. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **SOLAS 1974 Convention** consists of:
1. **Articles** — legal framework (definitions, obligations, amendment procedures).
2. **Annex** — technical regulations divided into **12 Chapters**.
3. **Codes** — detailed technical specifications (mandatory or voluntary).

**Key Chapters:**

| Chapter | Title |
|---|---|
| I | General Provisions |
| II-1 | Construction - Subdivision and Stability |
| II-2 | Fire Protection, Detection, Extinction |
| III | Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements |
| IV | Radiocommunications (GMDSS) |
| V | Safety of Navigation |
| VI | Carriage of Cargoes |
| VII | Carriage of Dangerous Goods |
| VIII | Nuclear Ships |
| IX | Management for Safe Operation (ISM) |
| X | High-Speed Craft |
| XI-1 | Special Measures for Maritime Safety |
| XI-2 | Special Measures for Maritime Security (ISPS) |
| XII | Bulk Carriers |

#### (b) Discuss the ISM Code and ISPS Code in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**ISM Code (International Safety Management Code):**
- Mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter IX**.
- Effective from **1 July 1998**.
- Requires every shipping company to establish a **Safety Management System (SMS)**.
- Defines roles: **Master, Designated Person Ashore (DPA), Company**.
- Aims at **preventing human error**, which causes most maritime accidents.

**ISPS Code (International Ship and Port Facility Security Code):**
- Mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter XI-2**.
- Effective from **1 July 2004**.
- Adopted after 9/11 terror threats.
- Establishes **Ship Security Levels 1, 2, and 3**.
- Mandates appointment of **SSO** and **CSO**.
- Requires **Ship Security Assessment (SSA)** and **Ship Security Plan (SSP)**.

**Key Difference:** ISM = **Safety** (accident prevention). ISPS = **Security** (intentional threats).

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

#### (a) Explain in detail the Fire Protection requirements under SOLAS Chapter II-2. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

SOLAS Chapter II-2 governs **fire protection, detection, and extinction**. Key requirements:

1. **Structural Fire Protection:** Ship is divided into **Main Vertical Zones (MVZs)** by fire-resistant bulkheads and decks.
2. **FSS Code (Fire Safety Systems Code):** Specifies fire pumps, fixed CO₂ systems, foam systems, water mist, and sprinkler systems.
3. **FTP Code (Fire Test Procedures Code):** Defines test methods for non-combustibility and surface flammability of materials.
4. **Means of Escape:** At least **two means of escape** from every accommodation space.
5. **Fire Detection:** Smoke and heat detectors in all accommodation and machinery spaces.
6. **Fire-fighting equipment:** Portable extinguishers, fire hoses, breathing apparatus.

All materials used in ships must pass **FTP Code tests** to be classed as non-combustible or fire-resistant.

#### (b) Describe the GMDSS and its implementation under SOLAS Chapter IV. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**GMDSS (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System)** is the international system for maritime distress communication, mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter IV**, and **fully implemented on 1 February 1999**.

**Four Sea Areas:**

| Sea Area | Coverage | Equipment |
|---|---|---|
| A1 | Within VHF range of shore (~20–30 nm) | VHF with DSC |
| A2 | Within MF range of shore (~100 nm) | MF with DSC |
| A3 | INMARSAT coverage (~70°N to 70°S) | INMARSAT or HF DSC |
| A4 | Polar regions (above 70°N/S) | HF DSC + satellite |

**Key Equipment:**
- **EPIRB** (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon)
- **SART** (Search and Rescue Transponder)
- **NAVTEX** (Navigational Telex)
- **DSC** (Digital Selective Calling)
- **VHF/MF/HF Radios**

GMDSS ensures that any ship in distress can be located and assisted quickly — fixing the failures that caused the Titanic tragedy.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 SOLAS:**

- SOLAS = **Safety of Life at Sea**, adopted **1974**, administered by **IMO (London)**.
- Current SOLAS has **12 Chapters** + multiple Codes.
- **Chapter II-2** = Fire → uses **FSS Code** and **FTP Code**.
- **Chapter III** = Life-Saving → uses **LSA Code**.
- **Chapter IV** = GMDSS — implemented **1 February 1999**.
- **Chapter V** = Navigation → AIS, VDR, ECDIS mandatory.
- **Chapter VII** = Dangerous Goods → uses **IMDG Code**.
- **Chapter IX** = ISM Code — mandatory from **1 July 1998**.
- **Chapter XI-2** = ISPS Code — mandatory from **1 July 2004**.
- **Chapter XII** = Bulk Carrier safety.
- **Flag State** enforces; **Port State** verifies through inspections.
- **Recognized Organizations (ROs)** like DNV, LR, BV, ABS conduct surveys.
- **Tacit Acceptance** procedure for amendments (24-month default).
- **Titanic 1912** → triggered first SOLAS 1914.
- **9/11 2001** → triggered ISPS Code 2004.
- **MV Derbyshire 1980** → triggered bulk carrier rules (Chapter XII).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type Approval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Type Approval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Type Approval** is a formal, independent third-party certification procedure by which a **Recognised Organisation (RO)** — typically a Classification Society member of the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** or a Flag State Administration — verifies that a specific *design, model, or series* of marine equipment, machinery, or component conforms to a published set of technical requirements, international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, STCW), and the society's own rules.

Once granted, the Type Approval certificate confirms that **every unit produced of that identical design** is presumed to comply with the relevant regulations, **without the need to re-test each individual item** before installation on board a vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Type Approval is a generic certification granted to a product or equipment family demonstrating that the design meets specified performance, safety, and environmental standards through prototype testing, factory audit, and quality assurance assessment, thereby permitting subsequent series production under controlled conditions."*

---

## 1.2 Key Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Type** | The specific design, model, or family of equipment submitted for approval. |
| **Approval** | Formal written certification confirming regulatory compliance. |
| **IACS** | International Association of Classification Societies (e.g., DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV, KR, ClassNK). |
| **Flag State** | The country under whose laws the vessel is registered. |
| **Recognised Organisation (RO)** | Body authorised by a Flag State to act on its behalf (Delegated Authority). |
| **Certificate of Type Approval (CTA)** | The document proving approval has been granted. |
| **Product Quality Assurance (PQA)** | Auditing of the manufacturer's production line to ensure ongoing conformity. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Licence of Equipment"

Think of **Type Approval** exactly like the **type approval given to a car model by a government testing authority** (such as Bharat NCAP or Euro NCAP):

- **A single prototype car is crash-tested once** — if it passes, the *entire design family* (every car of that model rolling off the production line) is considered safe.
- The **manufacturer must continue producing cars to the same exact design** — any modification voids the approval.
- **Periodic factory audits** ensure the manufacturer isn't cutting corners in production.
- **Individual cars don't need to be re-crash-tested** — they carry the approval of their "type."

In maritime terms: a manufacturer of, say, a **lifejacket light** or a **fire detector** sends **one prototype** to a classification society. If the prototype passes SOLAS performance tests, **every lifejacket light of that model** gets permission to be carried on ships worldwide — provided the factory keeps making them identically.

---

## 1.4 Why Type Approval Matters in Maritime Safety

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — The Three Pillars of Type Approval**
> 1. **Safety** — Confirms the equipment will not fail catastrophically in maritime conditions (saltwater corrosion, vibration, fire, low visibility).
> 2. **Interoperability** — Ensures equipment fits into existing shipboard systems and rescue coordination globally.
> 3. **Traceability** — Provides a single, auditable chain of proof linking the equipment's design to a known certified standard.

The critical importance arises because **lives depend on this equipment** during emergencies at sea — where rescue is hours or days away, and replacement parts are unavailable. Failure of an unapproved life raft, EPIRB, or fire alarm can mean the difference between rescue and tragedy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Type Approval Process — Structured Logic

The Type Approval procedure is governed by the **IACS Unified Requirements (UR)** and individual society rules (e.g., DNV-CG-0339, LR Type Approval System, ABS Type Approval Program). It follows a strict sequential framework:

### Step 1: Application & Design Documentation Review
- The **manufacturer** submits a formal application to the chosen Classification Society.
- Required documents include:
  - Detailed design drawings
  - Bill of Materials (BoM)
  - Material certificates
  - Functional specifications
  - Quality Management System (QMS) — usually **ISO 9001** certification
- The society performs a **paper-based design review** against applicable rules (SOLAS Chapter II-2 for fire equipment, Chapter III for life-saving appliances, Chapter IV for radio, etc.).

### Step 2: Prototype Testing (Type Testing)
- **Two or more prototypes** are typically required to perform destructive and environmental tests.
- Tests are conducted at the society's own laboratory, an independent accredited lab, or in the presence of a **Surveyor**.
- **The "Why"** behind each test ensures the equipment withstands maritime realities.

### Step 3: Manufacturing Assessment (Initial Audit)
- The society's surveyor visits the **factory premises**.
- Verifies that the manufacturer has the technical capability, calibration records, and process control to **consistently replicate** the tested prototype.
- Checks welding, assembly, quality control, and traceability procedures.

### Step 4: Certificate Issuance
- Upon successful completion of Steps 1–3, a **Certificate of Type Approval (CTA)** is issued.
- The CTA is **valid for 5 years** (typical) and includes:
  - Approved model designation
  - Applicable rules and standards
  - Approved variants and accessories
  - Production surveillance requirements

### Step 5: Continuous Production Surveillance
- **Annual or biennial factory audits** are conducted during the CTA's validity.
- Any **design change**, supplier change, or material change requires **re-assessment** or **modification approval**.
- Non-conformity can lead to **suspension** or **withdrawal** of the CTA.

---

## 2.2 Equipment Typically Subject to Type Approval

Marine equipment requiring Type Approval (as per SOLAS Reg. II-1/3-1, II-2/4, III/4, IV/14-1) includes:

- **Life-Saving Appliances (LSA):** Life jackets, life rafts, immersion suits, EPIRBs, SARTs, line-throwing appliances, pyrotechnics.
- **Fire-Fighting Equipment:** Portable extinguishers, fixed CO₂ systems, foam systems, fire hoses, nozzles, fireman's outfits, breathing apparatus.
- **Navigation & Radio Equipment:** Echosounders, gyrocompasses, radar, AIS, VHF, GMDSS terminals.
- **Engineered Systems:** Steering gear, governors, bilge level alarms, oily-water separators (OWS), sewage treatment plants (STPs).
- **Electrical Equipment:** Cables, switchboards, emergency generators, batteries (mandatory under SOLAS Reg. II-1/40-45).

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Type Approval — Engineering Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Engineering Pillars Tested in Type Approval**

| Pillar | What It Tests | Real-World Failure It Prevents |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Performance** | Does the equipment perform its intended task? | EPIRB failing to transmit distress signal |
| **Environmental Resistance** | Survives heat, cold, humidity, salt spray, vibration, impact | Life jacket light dying in Arctic waters |
| **Long-Term Reliability** | Performs identically after 5+ years of storage | Fire extinguisher not discharging when needed |

---

## 2.4 Relationship Between Type Approval and Other Certifications

| Concept | Scope | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Type Approval** | Equipment design & production quality | Once per design family (renewed every 5 yrs) |
| **Unit Certification** | A specific individual product | Every unit produced |
| **Annual Survey** | Condition of equipment onboard | Yearly |
| **Port State Control (PSC)** | Operational compliance by foreign port | Per port call |
| **Flag State Inspection** | Compliance with national regulations | Periodic |

Type Approval is the **"design-side certification."** It does NOT replace onboard surveys — it complements them.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Type Approval Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> Maritime Type Approval is largely procedural, not computational. However, the following performance and testing benchmarks are essential.

| Parameter / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Applicable Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drop test height (lifejacket light) | $h$ | $\mathbf{4.5 \text{ m}}$ | Life jacket lights |
| Salt spray test duration | $t_{ss}$ | $\mathbf{28 \text{ days}}$ | Most marine electronics |
| Operational temperature range | $T_{op}$ | $-30^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+65^{\circ}\text{C}$ | EPIRB, SART |
| Survival temperature range | $T_{surv}$ | $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+70^{\circ}\text{C}$ | EPIRB, SART |
| Vibration test acceleration | $a_{vib}$ | $\mathbf{2.0 \, g}$ (5–500 Hz sweep) | All marine equipment |
| Watertight integrity (IP rating) | $IP$ | $\mathbf{IP67}$ minimum | Portable electronics |
| Fire test temperature | $T_{fire}$ | $\mathbf{800^{\circ}C}$ for 30 min | Cables, structural fire stops |
| Oil resistance immersion | $t_{oil}$ | $\mathbf{24 \text{ hours}}$ in diesel | Hose assemblies |
| Wind pressure (rope launcher) | $P_{w}$ | $\mathbf{30 \text{ m/s}}$ | Line throwing appliances |
| Test fire size (extinguisher) | $V_{fire}$ | $\mathbf{2B}$ to $\mathbf{3A}$ rating | Portable extinguishers |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Type Approval

- **Procurement Departments** of shipowners cannot buy non-Type Approved safety equipment — it would be rejected by the surveyor during class survey.
- **Insurance underwriters** (P&I Clubs) often demand Type Approved equipment as a precondition of cover.
- **Charter parties** (Shell, BP, etc.) frequently require *specific* Class Society Type Approvals as a condition of vessel employment.
- **Solas/Marpol Surveyors** use Type Approval marks as a *fast-track trust signal* during vessel inspection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Type Approval Validity Window (Analytical Framework)

Although Type Approval has no classical physics formula, its **validity lifecycle** can be expressed as a deterministic timeline derivation — useful for KTU board numerical / scheduling questions.

Let:

- $T_0$ = Date of Certificate Issuance (Day 0).
- $T_5$ = Date of Validity Expiry = $T_0 + 5 \text{ years}$ (per IACS UR Z17).
- $T_{audit,i}$ = Scheduled audit date for the $i$-th year of validity.
- $T_{suspension}$ = Date approval is suspended (if applicable).

The **Type Approval Validity Window Equation**:

$$
T_{valid} \;=\; T_5 \;-\; T_0 \;=\; 5 \times 365.25 \;=\; \mathbf{1826.25 \text{ days}}
$$

The **mandatory audit schedule** is given by:

$$
T_{audit,i} \;=\; T_0 + (i \times 365.25) \quad \text{for} \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

After 5 years, **re-certification** requires the manufacturer to either:

1. Re-submit prototypes for fresh type testing (full re-approval), OR
2. Provide documented **traceability of design consistency** + audit continuity.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Audit Calendar Calculation

**Problem (KTU Practice Style):**
A manufacturer receives a Certificate of Type Approval on **15 March 2022**. According to IACS UR Z17, on which dates must the annual factory audits be performed?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the issuance date.

$$
T_0 = 15 \text{ March } 2022
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the audit schedule formula.

$$
T_{audit,i} = T_0 + (i \times 365.25) \text{ days} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

**Step 3:** Compute each audit.

For $i = 1$:

$$
T_{audit,1} = 15 \text{ March } 2022 + 365.25 \text{ days}
$$

Accounting for leap year 2024:

$$
\begin{aligned}
15\text{ Mar } 2022 &\to 15\text{ Mar } 2023 \quad (365 \text{ days, year } 1) \\
&\to 15\text{ Mar } 2024 \quad (366 \text{ days, leap year 2024}) \\
&\approx 14\text{ Mar } 2024 \pm 1\text{ day tolerance}
\end{aligned}
$$

The classification society allows a **tolerance window of ± 3 months** for annual audits, so the first audit window is:

$$
T_{audit,1} \in [15 \text{ Dec } 2023,\; 15 \text{ Jun } 2024]
$$

For $i = 2$:

$$
T_{audit,2} \in [15 \text{ Dec } 2024,\; 15 \text{ Jun } 2025]
$$

For $i = 3$:

$$
T_{audit,3} \in [15 \text{ Dec } 2025,\; 15 \text{ Jun } 2026]
$$

For $i = 4$:

$$
T_{audit,4} \in [15 \text{ Dec } 2026,\; 15 \text{ Jun } 2027]
$$

The **certificate expires** at:

$$
T_5 = 15 \text{ March } 2027
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "When is Type Approval Required?"

This matrix is **high-yield for KTU Part A questions** (3 marks) and viva voce.

| Equipment Type | Convention Reference | Type Approval Required? | Flag State Acceptance Test Needed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lifeboat release hook | SOLAS Reg. III/1, III/34 | **Yes** | Yes |
| Portable fire extinguisher | SOLAS Reg. II-2/4 | **Yes** | Yes |
| EPIRB | SOLAS Reg. IV/14-1 | **Yes** | Yes (coding) |
| Fixed CO₂ system | SOLAS Reg. II-2/10 | **Yes** | Yes |
| Bridge navigation radar | SOLAS Reg. V/19 | **Yes** | Yes |
| Bilge alarm (15 ppm) | MARPOL Annex I | **Yes** | Yes |
| Magnetic compass | SOLAS Reg. V/19 | **Yes** | Yes |
| Lifeboat oar | SOLAS Reg. III/34 | **No** (consumable) | No |
| Ship's bell | COLREGs Annex IV | **No** (passive) | No |
| Wooden fender | Industry practice | **No** | No |

---

## 3.4 Derivative Computation — Test Failure Probability (Conceptual)

While not a formal probability formula, the conceptual derivation of Type Approval reliability rests on:

$$
P(\text{ship survives emergency}) \;=\; P(\text{equipment design approved}) \times P(\text{unit built correctly}) \times P(\text{equipment maintained})
$$

If we let:

- $P(\text{design approved}) = 1.00$ (by definition of Type Approval)
- $P(\text{unit built correctly}) = P_1$ (controlled by PQA audits)
- $P(\text{equipment maintained}) = P_2$ (controlled by onboard surveys)

Then Type Approval **only guarantees the first term**. The remaining terms rely on **production audits and onboard surveys**, hence their criticality.

$$
P(\text{ship survives}) \;\approx\; 1.00 \times P_1 \times P_2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Type Approval pushes $P(\text{design approved})$ to unity. The classification society's ongoing factory audits push $P_1$ close to unity. The onboard survey regime maintains $P_2$. Together, these layered defences create the maritime safety ecosystem.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The Type Approval Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturer Submits Application] --> B[Design Documentation Review]
    B --> C{Design Acceptable?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject / Request Modifications]
    D --> A
    C -- Yes --> E[Prototype Manufacturing]
    E --> F[Type Testing in Independent Lab]
    F --> G{All Tests Passed?}
    G -- No --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Factory Audit at Production Site]
    H --> I{QMS ISO 9001 Compliant?}
    I -- No --> J[Conditional Approval or Reject]
    I -- Yes --> K[Certificate of Type Approval Issued]
    K --> L[Annual Factory Audits]
    L --> M{Design Unchanged?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Continue Production Surveillance]
    M -- No --> O[Re-Assessment Required]
    O --> B
    N --> P{5-Year Re-Certification Due?}
    P -- No --> L
    P -- Yes --> Q[Full Re-Test or Documentary Renewal]
    Q --> K
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Type Approval System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Regulatory[Regulatory Framework]
        R1[SOLAS Convention]
        R2[MARPOL Convention]
        R3[MLC 2006]
        R4[Flag State Rules]
    end

    subgraph Society[Recognised Organisation]
        S1[Classification Society - IACS Member]
        S2[Surveyors and Laboratories]
        S3[Audit Teams]
    end

    subgraph Manufacturer[Manufacturer]
        M1[Design Engineering]
        M2[Prototype Production]
        M3[Quality Management System]
        M4[Mass Production Line]
    end

    subgraph Shipowner[Shipowner / Operator]
        O1[Procurement]
        O2[Class Survey]
        O3[Onboard Maintenance]
    end

    R1 --> S1
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    R4 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    M1 --> S2
    M2 --> S2
    M3 --> S3
    S2 --> M4
    M4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    S3 -.Annual Audits.-> M4
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Type Approval Decision Tree (Equipment Categorisation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Marine Equipment] --> Q1{SOLAS or MARPOL Applicable?}
    Q1 -- No --> NotReq[Type Approval NOT Required]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Life-Saving or Safety Critical?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> FullTest[FULL Type Test Required]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Pollution Control Equipment?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> FullTest
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Navigational or Radio Equipment?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> FullTest
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Manufacturer Has Existing TA?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Modify[Modification Assessment Only]
    Q5 -- No --> FullTest
    FullTest --> IssueCert[Issue Certificate of Type Approval]
    Modify --> IssueCert
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Interpretation Notes

- **Arrows** represent flow of authority, documentation, or audit interactions.
- **Boxes** represent functional entities — regulatory bodies, manufacturers, operators.
- **Diamond nodes** represent decision points where the process branches.
- The **dashed arrow** in the architecture diagram represents the **continuous feedback loop** of annual audits ensuring production conformity.
- The **subgraphs** (Regulatory, Society, Manufacturer, Shipowner) help visualise the **interdisciplinary nature** of Type Approval — it is *not* a single-organisation activity but a collaborative certification chain.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define Type Approval. Why is it important in maritime safety?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Type Approval is a formal certification procedure conducted by a Classification Society (IACS member) or Flag State Administration confirming that a specific *design* of marine equipment complies with relevant international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL) and the society's rules.

**Importance (1.5 Marks):**
1. It guarantees that *every unit* of an approved design family meets safety standards without individual re-testing.
2. It provides regulatory traceability — surveyors can verify compliance quickly during class and flag surveys.
3. It ensures that critical life-saving and pollution-prevention equipment has been tested for marine environmental extremes (salt, heat, vibration).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Model]`
**List any three marine equipment that require Type Approval under SOLAS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Life jackets and immersion suits** — covered under SOLAS Chapter III (Life-Saving Appliances).
2. **Fire extinguishers and fixed CO₂ systems** — covered under SOLAS Chapter II-2 (Fire Protection).
3. **EPIRBs (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacons)** — covered under SOLAS Chapter IV (Radio Communications).

*(Alternative valid answers: SART, line-throwing appliances, oil discharge monitors, magnetic compasses, radar, lifeboat release hooks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme provides **internal choice** in Part B. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a) Explain in detail the Type Approval procedure followed by Classification Societies. Include the role of IACS. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Type Approval procedure is a structured, multi-stage certification process governed by the **IACS Unified Requirements (UR Z17)** and the individual rules of member societies.

**Stages of the Procedure (5 Marks — 1 mark per major stage):**

1. **Application & Document Review:**
The manufacturer submits design drawings, material specifications, functional description, and ISO 9001 QMS certificate. The society reviews compliance against SOLAS, MARPOL, and its own rules.

2. **Prototype Type Testing:**
Two or more prototypes are tested at the society's laboratory or an accredited test house. Tests include environmental (temperature, humidity, salt spray), mechanical (vibration, impact, drop), and functional performance. For example, lifejacket lights undergo a **4.5 m drop test** and a **28-day salt spray test**.

3. **Initial Factory Audit:**
A society surveyor inspects the manufacturer's production facility, verifying process control, calibration, welding quality, and traceability of materials. This ensures the manufacturer can replicate the tested prototype consistently.

4. **Certificate Issuance:**
A Certificate of Type Approval (CTA) is issued, valid for **5 years**. It specifies the approved model, applicable rules, approved variants, and surveillance obligations.

5. **Annual Surveillance Audits:**
During the CTA's validity, the society conducts annual audits. Any design change requires modification approval. Non-conformity may lead to suspension or withdrawal.

**Role of IACS (1 Mark):**
The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** harmonises Type Approval standards across its member societies through Unified Requirements (URs). This prevents duplication of testing — a manufacturer who gets Type Approval from one IACS member receives recognition from others under the **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** and the **IACS Type Approval Mutual Recognition Agreement**.

**(b) Differentiate between Type Approval and Unit Certification. Why is Type Approval alone insufficient for onboard equipment compliance? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | Type Approval | Unit Certification |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Design and production quality of an equipment *family* | Individual *unit* verification |
| Frequency | Once every 5 years (renewable) | Every unit produced |
| Performed By | Classification Society (e.g., DNV, LR) | Same society or authorised surveyor |
| Output | Certificate of Type Approval (CTA) | Unit certificate / conformity stamp |
| Focus | Design-level compliance | Build-level compliance |

**Why Type Approval Alone Is Insufficient (3 Marks):**

1. **Time Degradation:** Equipment may deteriorate during storage. Type Approval only certifies the design, not the *condition* over years.
2. **Installation Variability:** Onboard installation differs — wiring, mounting, and ambient conditions can affect performance.
3. **Operational Wear:** Mechanical components (e.g., lifeboat release gear) wear out and require regular onboard inspection and maintenance.
4. **Modifications on Board:** Shipboard repairs may introduce non-original parts. Type Approval does not cover these.
5. **Periodic Surveys Required:** SOLAS requires Annual, Intermediate, and Renewal surveys that verify the *actual condition* of installed equipment, irrespective of its design approval.

Hence, Type Approval is a *prerequisite*, not a *substitute*, for ongoing compliance.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Model]`

**(a) Discuss the testing requirements for Type Approval of marine life-saving appliances. Mention at least five specific tests with their parameters. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Life-saving appliances (LSAs) are among the most rigorously Type Approved marine equipment because they are deployed in life-critical emergencies. Tests are conducted per SOLAS Reg. III/34 and the LSA Code.

**Five Specific Tests (6 Marks — approx. 1.2 marks each):**

1. **Drop Test:**
   - Lifejacket lights are dropped from **4.5 m** onto a hard surface.
   - Verifies structural integrity after accidental falls during embarkation.
   - Parameter: $h = \mathbf{4.5 \text{ m}}$.

2. **Salt Spray / Corrosion Test:**
   - Duration: **28 days** continuous salt fog exposure (per ASTM B117 or equivalent).
   - Ensures the equipment survives prolonged exposure to marine atmosphere.
   - Parameter: $t_{ss} = \mathbf{28 \text{ days}}$.

3. **Temperature Cycling Test:**
   - Operational range: $-30^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+65^{\circ}\text{C}$.
   - Survival range: $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+70^{\circ}\text{C}$.
   - Confirms functioning in polar and tropical waters.

4. **Vibration Test:**
   - Sweep of **5 Hz to 500 Hz** at **2.0 g** acceleration.
   - Simulates engine room vibration and rough seas.
   - Parameter: $a_{vib} = \mathbf{2.0 \, g}$.

5. **Watertightness / IP Test:**
   - Minimum **IP67** rating (immersion up to 1 m for 30 minutes).
   - Confirms operability after accidental dunking.

6. **Fire-Resistance Test (for line-throwing appliances):**
   - Line must not support combustion beyond specified length.

7. **Functional Operational Test:**
   - EPIRB must transmit distress signal for **at least 48 hours** continuously after activation.

**(b) Explain the significance of IACS in global maritime Type Approval. How does it prevent duplicate testing? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Significance of IACS (4 Marks):**

1. **Harmonisation:** IACS develops **Unified Requirements (URs)** that all member societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV, ClassNK, KR, RINA, etc.) must adopt. This means a manufacturer's design is judged against the *same* standard globally.

2. **Mutual Recognition:** Through the **IACS Type Approval Mutual Recognition Agreement**, an approval issued by one member society is recognised by others. The manufacturer does not need to apply repeatedly.

3. **Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS):** IACS audits member societies themselves, ensuring they all maintain consistent certification rigor.

4. **Global Industry Confidence:** Major charterers, insurance underwriters, and port state authorities trust IACS-recognised Type Approvals, making them a de facto international benchmark.

**Prevention of Duplicate Testing (3 Marks):**

1. A manufacturer obtaining Type Approval from, say, **DNV** can apply to **Lloyd's Register** for acceptance based on DNV's test reports.
2. IACS member societies share test data via the **IACS Quality Database**, eliminating redundant lab testing.
3. The QSCS framework ensures each society's test reports are of equivalent technical rigor, so cross-acceptance is technically defensible.
4. This **reduces cost**, **shortens time-to-market**, and **removes trade barriers** in the global marine equipment supply chain.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on Type Approval Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Type Approval with Class Survey.** Type Approval certifies the *design*, not the vessel. Writing "Type Approval is done on the ship" will lose 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Naming only one testing standard.** Always mention SOLAS Chapter reference *and* the specific regulation (e.g., "SOLAS Reg. III/34" not just "SOLAS").
> 3. **Forgetting the 5-year validity.** A complete answer must mention the validity period and renewal procedure.
> 4. **Omitting the production surveillance aspect.** Type Approval is *not* a one-time activity — annual audits are part of the process.
> 5. **Writing vague answers like "improves safety."** Use engineering-specific language: "ensures traceability," "guarantees design conformity," "provides regulatory audit chain."
> 6. **Ignoring IACS.** IACS is almost always required in KTU questions — at least one mark is reserved for naming its role.
> 7. **Mixing up EPIRB testing with life jacket testing.** Each LSA has distinct tests; generic answers lose precision marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Type Approval**

- **Definition:** Type Approval is *design-level* certification granted by an IACS Classification Society after prototype testing and factory audit.

- **Governing Authority:** Primarily **IACS Unified Requirements** (especially **UR Z17**) plus SOLAS, MARPOL, and individual society rules.

- **Mandatory Equipment Categories:**
  - Life-Saving Appliances (SOLAS III)
  - Fire-Fighting Equipment (SOLAS II-2)
  - Navigation & Radio Equipment (SOLAS V and IV)
  - Pollution Control Equipment (MARPOL)

- **Process Steps (Mnemonic: "DAF-CAS"):**
  - **D**ocumentation Review
  - **A**pplication & Prototype Manufacturing
  - **F**actory Audit
  - **C**ertificate Issuance
  - **A**nnual Surveillance Audits
  - **S**uspension or Re-certification at 5 years

- **Validity:** **5 years**, renewable; subject to **annual factory audits**.

- **Key Tests:** Drop test (4.5 m), salt spray (28 days), vibration (2.0 g), temperature extremes (-30°C to +65°C), IP67 waterproofing.

- **IACS Role:** Harmonises standards, enables mutual recognition, prevents duplicate testing across 12+ classification societies.

- **Difference from Unit Certification:**
  - Type Approval → *Design family*
  - Unit Certification → *Individual unit*
  - Both needed; one does not replace the other.

- **Why Type Approval Alone is Insufficient:** Onboard wear, installation variability, and degradation require **periodic class surveys** (Annual, Intermediate, Renewal).

- **Common KTU Exam Triggers:** "Explain IACS role," "List type-approved equipment," "Differentiate Type Approval and Unit Certification," "Discuss testing parameters for LSA."

- **Mnemonic for LSA Tests:** "**D**rop, **S**alt, **T**emperature, **V**ibration, **W**ater, **F**ire" — **DSTVWF**.

---

**End of Module 2 — Type Approval Notes**

*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme examination standards and Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fire Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Fire Tests # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Fire Tests** are standardized, controlled laboratory procedures used to determine the reaction of materials, products, and assemblies (such as bulkheads, decks, doors, furnishings, and cables) when exposed to specific fire conditions simulating onboard ship emergencies. Under the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code**, every material approved for marine use must undergo a series of prescribed fire tests to measure its **ignitability, flame spread, heat release, smoke production, and toxicity**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The syllabus strictly maps to the **IMO FTP Code (International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures)** and the **SOLAS Chapter II-2** (Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection, and Fire Extinction). Examiners expect the student to correlate test type → governing FTP Code Part → shipboard application. The complete FTP Code consists of **multiple Parts (Part 1 to Part 11+)**, each specifying a different fire test methodology for a different category of shipboard material. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Stress Test"** > Imagine a human heart being subjected to a treadmill stress test in a hospital. The doctor doesn't care that the heart "looks" strong; it must *demonstrate* controlled performance under standardized stress. Similarly, fire tests do not rely on a material *appearing* fire-resistant — the material is **actually burned, heated, or exposed to flame** in a controlled furnace or chamber, and its measurable response is recorded. The "heart" of ship safety is the structural material; the "treadmill" is the test furnace; and the "ECG printout" is the time-temperature curve, smoke density index, or flame spread rating. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The fire-testing universe relies on these universal benchmarks (must be memorized for KTU): - **Standard Fire Exposure Curve (ISO 834 / IMO Standard Time-Temp Curve):** $$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$ where $T_0 = 20\,°C$ (initial furnace temperature) and $t$ is time in minutes. - **Critical reference points on this curve:** - At $t = 5\,\text{min}$ → $T \approx 576\,°C$ - At $t = 30\,\text{min}$ → $T \approx 842\,°C$ - At $t = 60\,\text{min}$ → $T \approx 945\,°C$ - At $t = 120\,\text{min}$ → $T \approx 1049\,°C$ - **Heat Flux Standard:** $50\,\text{kW/m}^2$ is the **standard imposed heat flux** for many surface flammability tests. - **Critical Oxygen Index (LOI):** A material with **LOI > 27%** is generally considered self-extinguishing in air; **LOI > 35%** is fire-resistant. The non-combustibility test (Part 1) sets the furnace temperature to **750 °C**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Self-Combustion Threshold (Recap):** For a material to pass the **Non-Combustibility Test**, the average furnace temperature rise must not exceed **30 °C** above the baseline of 750 °C, and the average specimen surface temperature rise must not exceed **30 °C**. ## 1.4 The Governing Authority — IMO FTP Code The **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)** was adopted under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 3** and made mandatory on **1 July 1998** under the **1997 amendments to SOLAS** for new ships. > [!IMPORTANT] > The FTP Code applies to materials used in **structure, machinery, electrical, and fire safety systems**. It is the single most important reference document for any KTU Maritime Safety question on fire testing. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Philosophy of "Why Test?" Marine fires are uniquely lethal due to: 1. **Confined spaces** → rapid heat accumulation. 2. **Toxic smoke + plastics** → incapacitation before flame contact. 3. **Wind/tunneling effect** in corridors and stairways. 4. **Heel and trim of ship** → flame and smoke travel unpredictably. Hence, every material installed onboard must satisfy **minimum three risk attributes**: - **Reaction-to-Fire** (ignitability, flame spread, heat release) - **Fire Resistance** (ability to contain fire for a defined period) - **Smoke & Toxicity** (smoke density and emitted gases) ## 2.2 Classification of Fire Tests (Master Taxonomy) The IMO FTP Code categorizes fire tests into **two broad pillars** plus the **non-combustibility corner-stone**: | **Pillar** | **Purpose** | **Typical FTP Part** | |---|---|---| | **Reaction-to-Fire Tests** | Measure how *easily* a material ignites and propagates flame. | Parts 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 | | **Fire Resistance Tests** | Measure how *long* an assembly (bulkhead, deck, door) contains fire. | Parts 3, 4, 8, 9, 11 | | **Non-Combustibility Test** | The fundamental "is it even allowed onboard?" gatekeeper. | Part 1 | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the table below — questions directly pick one row and ask for explanation. | **Test Name** | **FTP Part** | **Sample Size / Specimen** | **Key Criterion** | **Application Onboard** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Non-Combustibility Test | Part 1 | Cylinder, $\phi 45\,\text{mm} \times 50\,\text{mm}$ height | Furnace $\Delta T \le 30\,°C$; surface $\Delta T \le 30\,°C$ | Bulkhead insulation cores, structural steel encasement | | Smoke Density Test | Part 2 | $75\,\text{mm} \times 75\,\text{mm}$ square, $\le 25\,\text{mm}$ thick | $D_m \le 200$ (weighted average specific optical density) | Insulation, ceiling, wall linings, ducts | | Test for "A", "B", and "F" Class Divisions | Part 3 | Full-scale bulkhead / deck panel | Passes standard 60 min or 30 min or 15 min fire exposure | Watertight and fire-rated bulkheads/decks | | Test for Fire Door Control Systems | Part 4 | Full door + frame | Integrity, insulation, self-closing | Fire-rated doors | | Test for Surface Flammability (Flooring) | Part 5 | Floor-covering sample | Critical Radiant Flux $\ge 1.1\,\text{kW/m}^2$ (for low flame spread) | Floor coverings (primary deck coverings) | | Test for Primary Deck Coverings | Part 6 | Sub-floor + deck covering | Passes Part 5 + smoke | Floor finishes with adhesive | | Test for Vertically Supported Textiles & Films | Part 7 | Vertical textile | Surface flammability criteria | Curtains, draperies, free-hanging fabrics | | Test for Upholstered Furniture | Part 8 | Chair mock-up | Cigarette + butane flame ignition; no progressive smouldering | Sofas, chairs in cabins, public spaces | | Test for Bedding Components | Part 9 | Mattress | Same as Part 8 | Mattresses, pillows, bedspreads | | Test for Fire-Restricting Materials (High Speed Craft) | Part 10 | Composite sample | Surface flammability + toxicity | HSC craft interior linings | | Test for Fire-Resisting Divisions for High Speed Craft | Part 11 | Full panel | A-60 / A-30 / A-0 / B-15 / B-0 | HSC bulkheads | | Cable Fire Test | IEC 60332 (referenced in FTP) | Cable bundle | Flame spread $\le 2.5\,\text{m}$ on vertical ladder | All marine electrical cables | ## 2.4 Mathematical Description of the Standard Fire Curve The **Standard Time-Temperature Curve** (ISO 834, used globally for fire resistance testing) is the foundational equation: $$T(t) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1) \quad [°C]$$ - $T(t)$ = mean furnace temperature at time $t$ (°C) - $t$ = time elapsed since ignition of the furnace (minutes) - The 20 °C baseline = ambient room temperature. - The logarithmic form is **empirically derived** from cellulose-fueled fires (wood, paper, textiles — the dominant fuel in ship fires). > [!NOTE] > **Why logarithmic?** The first few minutes see a very steep temperature rise (the fire is "growing"), but as fuel is consumed and ventilation becomes limited, the temperature growth slows. A logarithmic curve captures this "fast start, slow finish" physics beautifully. For hydrocarbon pool fires (e.g., bunker fuel, oil spills), a **steeper curve** is used: $$T_{HC}(t) = 1080 \cdot (1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.167t}) - 0.325 \cdot e^{-2.5t} \quad [°C]$$ This is the **HCM (Hydrocarbon Modified) Curve**, used in **offshore and tanker** fire tests where the fuel is liquid hydrocarbons rather than cellulosic solids. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Shipyard procurement** — No insulation, cable, or panel is allowed to be installed without a **Type Approval Certificate** (issued after FTP testing). - **Classification Societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV) act as **third-party verifiers** ensuring the test reports comply with FTP Code. - **Port State Control (PSC)** inspectors may demand the **test certificates** during inspection under SOLAS Reg. II-2/3.4. - **Forensic fire investigation** — when a ship fire occurs, investigators compare the **fire pattern** with test data to identify the material of origin. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Time-Temperature Curve The ISO 834 curve is empirically defined, but the underlying physics can be motivated as follows. > **Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Furnace:** > The heat added to the furnace walls ($Q_{in}$) is supplied by a controlled gas burner. The energy absorbed by the specimen ($Q_{specimen}$) plus the energy lost through furnace walls ($Q_{loss}$) must equal $Q_{in}$. $$\frac{dQ_{in}}{dt} = \frac{dQ_{specimen}}{dt} + \frac{dQ_{loss}}{dt}$$ > **Step 2 — The empirical fit (1940s, championed by British Standards Institution):** > After collecting thousands of cellulosic compartment fire data points, the engineers fitted a curve of the form $T - T_0 = a \log_{10}(bt + 1)$. Using boundary conditions $T(0) = T_0 = 20\,°C$ and matching the temperature at $t = 60$ min to 945 °C, the constants were determined: $$a = 345, \quad b = 8$$ > **Step 3 — Verification of coefficients:** > At $t = 60$ min: > $$T(60) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 60 + 1)$$ > $$= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(481)$$ > $$= 20 + 345 \cdot 2.6821$$ > $$= 20 + 925.3 \approx 945\,°C \quad \checkmark$$ > **Step 4 — At $t = 30$ min:** > $$T(30) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(241) = 20 + 345 \cdot 2.3820 \approx 842\,°C \quad \checkmark$$ > **Step 5 — Slope at any time (Rate of Temperature Rise):** > Differentiating with respect to $t$: > $$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{345}{\ln(10)} \cdot \frac{8}{8t + 1} = \frac{345 \cdot 8}{2.3026 \cdot (8t + 1)} = \frac{1198.7}{8t + 1} \quad [°C/\text{min}]$$ > At $t = 0$: $\frac{dT}{dt}\Big|_{t=0} = \frac{1198.7}{1} \approx 1199\,°C/\text{min}$ — an extremely rapid initial rise. > > At $t = 60$: $\frac{dT}{dt}\Big|_{t=60} = \frac{1198.7}{481} \approx 2.49\,°C/\text{min}$ — a slow rise late in the test. This confirms the **"fast start, slow finish"** physics. ## 3.2 Non-Combustibility Test (FTP Part 1) — Analytical Logic > **Step 1 — Specimen conditioning:** The cylindrical specimen is dried in an oven at **60 °C for 20–24 hours** to remove moisture. > **Step 2 — Furnace setup:** A vertical-tube furnace is pre-heated to a steady state of **750 °C ± 5 °C** for at least 30 minutes using a **nichrome heating element**. > **Step 3 — Specimen insertion:** The specimen is lowered into the furnace tube, suspended in a **specimen holder** with thermocouples embedded. > **Step 4 — Monitoring period:** Furnace temperature $T_f(t)$ and specimen surface temperature $T_s(t)$ are recorded continuously for **20 minutes**. > **Step 5 — Pass criteria (BOTH must be satisfied):** > $$\Delta T_f = \overline{T_f(t)} - 750 \le 30\,°C$$ > $$\Delta T_s = \overline{T_s(t)} - T_0 \le 30\,°C$$ > Additionally, no **flaming combustion lasting more than 10 seconds** may occur. > **Step 6 — Interpretation:** > - If the material neither **ignites sustainably** nor **causes significant furnace temperature rise**, it is classified as **Non-Combustible** — the gold standard for marine structural insulation. > - Materials failing this test are restricted to **decorative use only** (with quantity limits per SOLAS Reg. II-2/5.3). ## 3.3 Surface Flammability Test (FTP Part 5) — Analytical Logic > **Step 1 — Specimen:** A floor covering sample of size **$105\,\text{mm} \times 800\,\text{mm}$** is mounted horizontally on a sliding platform. > **Step 2 — Heat source:** A **gas-fired radiant panel** at $90°$ angle imposes a heat flux gradient along the specimen from **$11\,\text{kW/m}^2$ at one end to $1\,\text{kW/m}^2$ at the other**. > **Step 3 — Pilot flame:** A small **pilot flame** is positioned to ignite any combustible vapours at the hot end. > **Step 4 — Flame-front tracking:** The flame-front position is recorded as a function of time; from this, the **Critical Radiant Flux (CRF)** is determined — the heat flux below which the flame cannot propagate. > **Step 5 — Pass criterion (per IMO):** > $$\text{CRF} \ge 1.1\,\text{kW/m}^2 \text{ for low flame spread (Class 1)}$$ > $$\text{CRF} \ge 0.45\,\text{kW/m}^2 \text{ for medium flame spread (Class 2)}$$ ## 3.4 Smoke Density Test (FTP Part 2) — Analytical Logic > **Step 1 — Specimen exposure:** A $75 \times 75$ mm specimen is exposed to a **flame from a propane burner** at $25\,\text{kW/m}^2$ heat flux in a **closed chamber** of volume **$\approx 0.5\,\text{m}^3$**. > **Step 2 — Photometric measurement:** A **vertical light beam + photomultiplier** measures the attenuation of transmitted light $I(t)$ relative to the initial intensity $I_0$. > **Step 3 — Specific Optical Density calculation:** > $$D_s(t) = \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I(t)}\right) \times 10$$ > where $V$ = chamber volume, $A$ = specimen exposed area, $L$ = light path length. > **Step 4 — Pass criterion:** > $$D_m \le 200 \quad \text{(maximum specific optical density over the 20-min test)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **$D_s$ is dimensionless** but specifically optical — and **$D_m$ must not exceed 200** for any material used as lining, ceiling, or bulkhead facing. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table for KTU | **Test** | **Failure Consequence** | **Most-Critical Equipment Used** | **Time Required** | |---|---|---|---| | Non-Combustibility (Part 1) | Material removed from ship | Vertical-tube furnace, 750 °C | ~30 min | | Smoke Density (Part 2) | Restricted quantity onboard | Smoke chamber + photometer | 20 min test + 10 min cooling | | Bulkhead Fire Resistance (Part 3) | Bulkhead downgraded class | Large-scale furnace + loading jacks | 60 min (Class A-60) or 30 min (A-30) | | Fire Door (Part 4) | Door downgraded to Class C | Vertical furnace with door frame | 30–60 min | | Surface Flammability Floor (Part 5) | Material downgraded for cabins | Radiant panel + sliding specimen | 10–30 min | | Furniture (Part 8) | Material downgraded or banned | Chair mock-up + gas burner | 30 min | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid — Hierarchical Classification of Fire Tests under IMO FTP Code ```mermaid flowchart TD A["IMO FTP Code
Fire Test Procedures"]:::root A --> B["Reaction to Fire Tests"]:::branch A --> C["Fire Resistance Tests"]:::branch A --> D["Non Combustibility"]:::branch B --> E["Part 2
Smoke Density"]:::leaf B --> F["Part 5
Surface Flammability Floor"]:::leaf B --> G["Part 6
Primary Deck Coverings"]:::leaf B --> H["Part 7
Vertically Supported Textiles"]:::leaf B --> I["Part 8
Upholstered Furniture"]:::leaf B --> J["Part 9
Bedding Components"]:::leaf C --> K["Part 3
A B F Class Divisions"]:::leaf C --> L["Part 4
Fire Door Control"]:::leaf C --> M["Part 11
HSC Fire Resisting Divisions"]:::leaf D --> N["Part 1
Non Combustibility 750C"]:::leaf E --> E1["Smoke Dm less than 200"]:::detail F --> F1["CRF above 1.1 kW per m2"]:::detail K --> K1["Integrity 60 min
Insulation 60 min
Load bearing 60 min"]:::detail L --> L1["Self closing
Integrity criteria"]:::detail N --> N1["Delta T furnace below 30C
Delta T surface below 30C"]:::detail classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef detail fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fire Test Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Specimen
Mounted on
Holder"]:::io --> B["Furnace or
Heat Source
750 to 1050 C"]:::proc B --> C["Sensor Suite
Thermocouples
Photometers
Gas Analysers"]:::sensor C --> D["Data Acquisition
System"]:::proc D --> E["Criteria Check
Delta T and Dm
and CRF"]:::check E --> F["Pass or
Fail
Decision"]:::output F --> G["Type Approval
Certificate Issued"]:::output G --> H["Material Permitted
Onboard Ship"]:::output classDef io fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000 classDef proc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 classDef sensor fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000 classDef check fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000 classDef output fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid — The "A-60 / A-30 / A-0" Fire Resistance Tier Concept ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["A Class Division
Made of Steel or Equivalent"]:::top A0 --> A60["A 60
Withstands 60 min
Standard Fire"]:::mid A0 --> A30["A 30
Withstands 30 min"]:::mid A0 --> A0b["A 0
No Time Requirement
But Non Combustible"]:::mid A60 --> A60a["Integrity"]:::leaf A60 --> A60b["Insulation"]:::leaf A60 --> A60c["Load Bearing"]:::leaf classDef top fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef mid fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Description of Non-Combustibility Furnace (since this is the most-tested topic) - A **vertical silica tube** of inner diameter ~75 mm and length ~150 mm is heated externally by a **nichrome-wound resistance coil**. - The specimen is suspended inside the tube by a **refractory specimen holder**. - **Two thermocouples** (Type K or R) measure **furnace temperature** (just below the specimen) and **specimen surface temperature**. - A **water-cooled shield** can be slid in/out to expose the specimen to the furnace for exactly 20 minutes. - Bottom observation window allows the operator to monitor for **flaming combustion lasting > 10 seconds**. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question 1] **State the standard conditions and pass criteria for the Non-Combustibility Test as per IMO FTP Code Part 1.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Furnace temperature:** Maintained at **750 °C ± 5 °C** during the 20-minute exposure (1 mark). - **Specimen size:** Cylindrical, $45\,\text{mm}$ diameter × $50\,\text{mm}$ height, dried at 60 °C for 20–24 hours (1 mark). - **Pass criteria:** (i) Mean furnace temperature rise $\Delta T_f \le 30\,°C$; (ii) Mean specimen surface temperature rise $\Delta T_s \le 30\,°C$; (iii) No flaming combustion lasting more than 10 seconds (1 mark). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question 2] **Differentiate between "Reaction to Fire" tests and "Fire Resistance" tests with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Reaction to Fire | Fire Resistance | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Measures ease of ignition, flame spread, heat release, smoke | Measures ability of an assembly to **contain** fire for a defined time | | **Examples** | (i) Smoke Density Test (FTP Part 2), (ii) Surface Flammability Test (FTP Part 5) | (i) Test for A/B/F Class Divisions (FTP Part 3), (ii) Test for Fire Door Control Systems (FTP Part 4) | (1 mark for definition each, 1 mark for examples) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A — Choice 1 [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 3]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Surface Flammability Test (Floor Covering)** as per IMO FTP Code Part 5, with specimen, apparatus, procedure, and pass criteria. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the **Smoke Density Test (FTP Part 2)** with the formula for Specific Optical Density and the pass criterion. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks - **Specimen size and orientation (1 mark):** Floor covering sample of size approximately $105\,\text{mm} \times 800\,\text{mm}$ mounted horizontally on a movable platform. - **Apparatus (2 marks):** Gas-fired **radiant panel** inclined at 90° to the specimen, imposing a heat flux gradient from ~$11\,\text{kW/m}^2$ at the hot end to ~$1\,\text{kW/m}^2$ at the cool end. A small **pilot flame** ignites the specimen. A travelling thermocouple measures surface temperature. - **Procedure (2 marks):** Pilot flame ignites specimen; flame front position is recorded as a function of time. From this, the **Critical Radiant Flux (CRF)** is determined — the heat flux at which the flame front stops propagating. - **Pass criteria (2 marks):** For low flame spread materials (used in accommodation spaces), **CRF $\ge 1.1\,\text{kW/m}^2$**; for medium flame spread, CRF $\ge 0.45\,\text{kW/m}^2$. #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks - **Purpose and application (1 mark):** Measures the **amount of smoke** generated by a material when exposed to fire. Used for insulation, ceiling, wall linings, and duct materials. - **Specimen and apparatus (2 marks):** $75\,\text{mm} \times 75\,\text{mm}$ square specimen (thickness $\le 25\,\text{mm}$). A closed chamber of volume $V \approx 0.5\,\text{m}^3$. Propane burner at $25\,\text{kW/m}^2$ heat flux. Vertical light beam + photomultiplier detector. - **Formula (2 marks):** $$D_s(t) = \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I(t)}\right) \times 10$$ where $V$ = chamber volume, $A$ = exposed specimen area, $L$ = light path length, $I_0$ = initial light intensity, $I(t)$ = transmitted intensity. - **Pass criterion (1 mark):** **Maximum specific optical density $D_m \le 200$** throughout the 20-minute test. - **Significance (1 mark):** Limits visibility loss and inhalation hazard during evacuation. --- ### Question B — Choice 2 [14 Marks] (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 4]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Test for "A" Class Divisions** as per FTP Code Part 3. List the three criteria evaluated. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the **Standard Time-Temperature Curve (ISO 834)** used in fire resistance testing. Compute the furnace temperature at $t = 90$ min and the rate of temperature rise at $t = 0$ and $t = 60$ min. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks - **Purpose (1 mark):** Verifies that bulkheads, decks, and divisions satisfy the **A-60, A-30, A-15, A-0** fire resistance standards. - **Specimen and apparatus (2 marks):** Full-scale panel mounted in a **large vertical or horizontal furnace** with gas burners, fitted with thermocouples, pressure sensors, and cotton-wool pads for integrity check. - **Three criteria (3 marks):** - **Integrity:** No flames, hot gases, or passage of flame on the unexposed side (verified by cotton-wool pad ignition, gap gauges). - **Insulation:** Mean temperature rise on unexposed surface $\le 140\,°C$; any single-point rise $\le 180\,°C$. - **Load-bearing (for decks):** No collapse under design load. - **Time classification (1 mark):** A-60 = 60 min, A-30 = 30 min, A-15 = 15 min, A-0 = no time requirement (but non-combustible construction). #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks - **Curve equation (2 marks):** $$T(t) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1) \quad [°C]$$ - **Physical significance (1 mark):** Represents a fully developed cellulosic compartment fire, used to standardize the severity of fire exposure for all marine structural tests. - **Computation at $t = 90$ min (2 marks):** $$T(90) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 90 + 1) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(721)$$ $$\log_{10}(721) = 2.8579$$ $$T(90) = 20 + 345 \cdot 2.8579 = 20 + 985.97 \approx 1006\,°C$$ - **Rate of temperature rise (2 marks):** Using $\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{1198.7}{8t + 1}$: - At $t = 0$: $\frac{dT}{dt} = 1198.7\,°C/\text{min}$ - At $t = 60$: $\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{1198.7}{481} = 2.49\,°C/\text{min}$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "Non-combustibility" with "Fire Resistance."** Non-combustibility (Part 1) is about the *material itself*; fire resistance (Part 3) is about the *assembled panel under load.* A non-combustible material can still fail fire resistance if the assembly is poorly designed! > 2. **A-60 ≠ 60 min for a 60 min test.** The classification is based on **three simultaneous criteria** (integrity, insulation, load-bearing). Students who write only "60 minutes" lose 1 mark per criterion missed. > 3. **Smoke density uses $D_m$ (maximum), not $D_s$ (specific at one time).** Students who interchange the two lose 2 marks. > 4. **The 750 °C number is for the non-combustibility test, not the smoke test.** Students who write "smoke test furnace is 750 °C" lose 1 mark. > 5. **Never use absolute-value bars `|...|` inside the markdown table** — examiners parsing the script often miss rows; use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{vert}\,x$ in LaTeX instead. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!** - ✅ **FTP Code** = International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures; mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter II-2** since **1 July 1998**. - ✅ **Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834):** $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ — the "heartbeat" of all fire resistance tests. - ✅ **Non-Combustibility Test (Part 1):** Furnace at **750 °C**; pass if $\Delta T_f \le 30\,°C$, $\Delta T_s \le 30\,°C$, no flame > 10 s. - ✅ **Smoke Density Test (Part 2):** $D_s = \frac{V}{A L} \log_{10}(I_0/I) \times 10$; pass if $D_m \le 200$. - ✅ **Surface Flammability Test (Part 5):** CRF $\ge 1.1\,\text{kW/m}^2$ for low flame spread flooring. - ✅ **A-60 Division (Part 3):** Integrity + Insulation + Load-bearing all satisfied for 60 minutes. - ✅ **Furniture (Part 8) and Bedding (Part 9):** Must resist cigarette + butane flame ignition; no progressive smouldering. - ✅ **High-Speed Craft:** Part 10 (fire-restricting materials) and Part 11 (HSC divisions) in addition. - ✅ **HCM Curve (Hydrocarbon):** $T_{HC}(t) = 1080(1 - 0.325e^{-0.167t}) - 0.325 e^{-2.5t}$ — used for tankers and offshore. - ✅ **Class Society Role:** DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV act as third-party verifiers of FTP test reports. - ✅ **Type Approval Certificate:** Issued post-test, must be presented during Port State Control inspections. - ✅ **Remember the magic numbers:** 750 °C (Part 1 furnace); 25 kW/m² (Part 2 heat flux); 1.1 kW/m² (Part 5 CRF); $D_m$ = 200; 60 min (A-60); 140 °C mean / 180 °C single-point (insulation criterion). --- **End of Module 2 — Types of Fire Tests — KTU 2024 Premium Notes**

topics.title: Non-Combustibility Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Combustibility Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Non-Combustibility Test** is a standardised fire reaction test prescribed by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under the **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code), Part 1, Appendix 1**, and technically aligned with **ISO 1182:2020** — *Reaction to fire tests for products — Non-combustibility test*. It is the principal test used onboard ships to classify whether a material is **"non-combustible"** as required by **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 3.40** and related chapters concerning ship construction, fire safety, and material approvals.

A material is deemed **non-combustible** when it does not contribute to fire growth through heat release, mass loss, or flaming combustion under the prescribed furnace conditions of **750 °C ± 5 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 scheme, this test is a *high-weightage* topic in Module 2 (Fire Test Procedures for Ship Materials). Examiners expect familiarity with the apparatus geometry, the three pass/fail criteria, and the rationale behind the FTP Code classifying materials as "non-combustible."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine placing a small piece of material into a pre-heated furnace at **750 °C** (roughly the temperature of a wood fire at its peak) and watching it for **20 minutes**. 

- If the material **does not catch fire, does not produce flames, does not smoke, and barely loses any weight**, it has "passed" — it is *non-combustible*.
- If it **burns, smokes heavily, or crumbles away with significant mass loss**, it has "failed" — meaning it *contributes* to a fire.

Think of it like testing whether a sponge can survive in an oven: a metal sponge (e.g., **mineral wool**) will survive; an organic sponge (e.g., **cotton insulation**) will burn. The Non-Combustibility Test is essentially the "oven test" — a binary, pass/fail qualification check for shipboard materials used in structural fire protection, ducting, ceilings, and bulkheads.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Source Standard |
|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| Furnace set temperature | **750 °C ± 5 °C** | ISO 1182 / FTP Code Part 1 |
| Test duration | **30 min** (or until equilibrium) | ISO 1182 / FTP Code Part 1 |
| Specimen geometry | **Cylindrical: ⌀45 mm × 50 mm height** | ISO 1182 |
| Number of specimens | **3 minimum** | ISO 1182 |
| Specimen mass (typical) | **50 ± 5 g** | Lab practice |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant:** The test is *isothermal* in principle — the furnace is pre-stabilised at 750 °C, and any **temperature rise (ΔT)** observed inside the furnace or on the specimen surface is attributed to the **combustion of the specimen itself**, not to the furnace heater.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle (Structured Logic)

The Non-Combustibility Test operates on a **thermal balance principle** in a closed-tube furnace:

1. **Pre-conditioning:** The specimen is conditioned in an oven at **60 °C** for 20–24 h, then cooled in a desiccator to constant mass.
2. **Furnace stabilization:** The tubular electric furnace is heated to a uniform **750 °C ± 5 °C**, and a reference thermocouple at the centre records a stable baseline (≈ 30 min stabilisation).
3. **Specimen insertion:** The specimen is lowered into the furnace via a specimen holder along the central axis.
4. **Continuous measurement:** Three thermocouples continuously record:
   - **T<sub>f</sub>** — furnace temperature
   - **T<sub>s</sub>** — specimen surface temperature
   - **T<sub>c</sub>** — centre (or wall) reference temperature
5. **Observation:** Visual records are kept for **duration of sustained flaming (t<sub>f</sub>)** — any continuous flaming lasting ≥ 5 s.
6. **Post-test mass loss:** The specimen is reweighed after cooling to compute **Δm**.
7. **Evaluation:** Average values across 3 specimens are compared against the **four acceptance criteria**.

## 2.2 The Four Pass/Fail Criteria (FTP Code)

A material is classified as **non-combustible** if **ALL four** of the following criteria are met for the average of three specimens:

| # | Parameter | Pass Criterion | Symbol |
|---|-----------|----------------|--------|
| 1 | Average furnace temperature rise | **≤ 30 °C** | ΔT<sub>f</sub> |
| 2 | Average specimen surface temperature rise | **≤ 30 °C** | ΔT<sub>s</sub> |
| 3 | Average duration of sustained flaming | **≤ 10 s** | t<sub>f</sub> |
| 4 | Average mass loss | **≤ 50 %** of original mass | Δm% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** These thresholds are intentionally conservative because in a *real* shipboard fire, temperatures can easily exceed 1000 °C. A material that only "barely passes" the 750 °C test is generally unsuitable for high-risk zones (e.g., engine rooms, galleys). Materials are therefore chosen for **safety margins** beyond the test threshold.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|----------------------|---------|-------|
| $\Delta T_f = T_{f,\max} - T_{f,\text{baseline}}$ | Furnace temperature rise | °C |
| $\Delta T_s = T_{s,\max} - T_{s,\text{baseline}}$ | Specimen surface temperature rise | °C |
| $\Delta m = m_i - m_f$ | Absolute mass loss | g |
| $\Delta m \% = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ | Percentage mass loss | % |
| $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Average across *n* specimens (n = 3) | variable |
| $t_f$ | Total duration of sustained flaming (≥ 5 s continuous) | s |
| $Q_{\text{comb}} \approx m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_f$ | Approximate heat released by combustion (engineering estimate) | J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule Reminder:** In any markdown table, never use the bare pipe symbol `|`. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value expressions — this has been pre-applied above.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In ship design, materials declared as **"non-combustible"** under this test may be used in:

- **A-class and B-class bulkheads** (per SOLAS Reg. II-2/3) without further fire-resisting treatment.
- **Ducting, ceiling linings, and thermal insulation** in machinery spaces.
- **Cable penetrations and pipe insulation** where SOLAS forbids combustible linings.
- **High-speed craft and offshore installations** under the HSC Code and MODU Code.

Conversely, materials that **fail** the test must be treated with **fire-retardant coatings** or used only where SOLAS allows combustible materials (e.g., restricted furniture items in accommodation areas).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> The Non-Combustibility Test is procedural rather than purely mathematical. The "derivations" here are the **explicit computational steps** an examiner expects a student to demonstrate when given raw test data for evaluation.

## 3.1 Worked Example: Evaluation of Test Results

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A material was subjected to the IMO Non-Combustibility Test on three cylindrical specimens (⌀45 mm × 50 mm). The following observations were recorded. Determine whether the material is classified as *non-combustible* under the FTP Code.

| Specimen | Initial Mass $m_i$ (g) | Final Mass $m_f$ (g) | $\Delta T_f$ (°C) | $\Delta T_s$ (°C) | Sustained Flaming (s) |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| S1       | 52.0                   | 48.2                 | 18                  | 22                  | 0                      |
| S2       | 51.5                   | 47.0                 | 25                  | 28                  | 3                      |
| S3       | 52.5                   | 46.5                 | 22                  | 27                  | 2                      |

### Step 1 — Average Furnace Temperature Rise

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\Delta T_f} &= \frac{\Delta T_{f1} + \Delta T_{f2} + \Delta T_{f3}}{3} \\
&= \frac{18 + 25 + 22}{3} \\
&= \frac{65}{3} \\
&\approx 21.67 \;^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with limit (≤ 30 °C):** $21.67 \le 30$ → **PASS**

### Step 2 — Average Specimen Surface Temperature Rise

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\Delta T_s} &= \frac{\Delta T_{s1} + \Delta T_{s2} + \Delta T_{s3}}{3} \\
&= \frac{22 + 28 + 27}{3} \\
&= \frac{77}{3} \\
&\approx 25.67 \;^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with limit (≤ 30 °C):** $25.67 \le 30$ → **PASS**

### Step 3 — Average Duration of Sustained Flaming

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{t_f} &= \frac{t_{f1} + t_{f2} + t_{f3}}{3} \\
&= \frac{0 + 3 + 2}{3} \\
&= \frac{5}{3} \\
&\approx 1.67 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with limit (≤ 10 s):** $1.67 \le 10$ → **PASS**

### Step 4 — Average Percentage Mass Loss

For **Specimen 1**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m_1 \% &= \frac{m_{i1} - m_{f1}}{m_{i1}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{52.0 - 48.2}{52.0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3.8}{52.0} \times 100 \\
&\approx 7.31 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Specimen 2**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m_2 \% &= \frac{51.5 - 47.0}{51.5} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4.5}{51.5} \times 100 \\
&\approx 8.74 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Specimen 3**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m_3 \% &= \frac{52.5 - 46.5}{52.5} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6.0}{52.5} \times 100 \\
&\approx 11.43 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average mass loss:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\Delta m \%} &= \frac{7.31 + 8.74 + 11.43}{3} \\
&= \frac{27.48}{3} \\
&\approx 9.16 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with limit (≤ 50 %):** $9.16 \le 50$ → **PASS**

### Step 5 — Overall Verdict

All four acceptance criteria are satisfied. Hence, the material is classified as **NON-COMBUSTIBLE** under the FTP Code, Annex 1, Part 1 (Non-Combustibility Test).

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Measured Avg. | Limit | Result |
|-----------|---------------|-------|--------|
| $\overline{\Delta T_f}$ | 21.67 °C | ≤ 30 °C | ✓ Pass |
| $\overline{\Delta T_s}$ | 25.67 °C | ≤ 30 °C | ✓ Pass |
| $\overline{t_f}$ | 1.67 s | ≤ 10 s | ✓ Pass |
| $\overline{\Delta m \%}$ | 9.16 % | ≤ 50 % | ✓ Pass |

**Final Classification:** ✅ **NON-COMBUSTIBLE MATERIAL**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Non-Combustibility Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Preparation] --> A1[Oven dry 60 C 20 to 24 h]
    A1 --> A2[Cool in desiccator to constant mass]
    A2 --> B[Furnace Stabilization]
    B --> B1[Heat tubular furnace to 750 C]
    B1 --> B2[Wait 30 min for thermal stability]
    B2 --> C[Specimen Insertion]
    C --> C1[Place cylindrical specimen in holder]
    C1 --> C2[Lower into furnace centre axis]
    C2 --> D[Data Acquisition 30 min]
    D --> D1[Record T furnace vs time]
    D --> D2[Record T specimen surface vs time]
    D --> D3[Record duration of sustained flaming]
    D3 --> E[Post Test]
    E --> E1[Remove specimen and cool in desiccator]
    E1 --> E2[Weigh final mass]
    E2 --> F[Evaluation Module]
    F --> F1[Compute average delta Tf]
    F --> F2[Compute average delta Ts]
    F --> F3[Compute average flaming time]
    F --> F4[Compute average mass loss percent]
    F1 --> G{All four pass criteria met?}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Classify as NON COMBUSTIBLE]
    G -->|No| I[Classify as COMBUSTIBLE]
    H --> J[Permitted for SOLAS non combustible applications]
    I --> K[Requires fire retardant treatment or restricted use]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Description of the Apparatus

The Non-Combustibility Test apparatus consists of:

- **A refractory tubular furnace** with an internal diameter of **75 ± 5 mm** and an internal height of **150 ± 5 mm**, capable of reaching **750 °C ± 5 °C**.
- **A specimen holder** made of nickel-chromium wire mesh, suspended along the central vertical axis of the furnace tube.
- **A specimen insertion device** to lower the holder without disturbing the thermal field.
- **Three thermocouples** (typically Type K, sheath diameter ≤ 1.5 mm):
  - One at the **furnace centre** (T<sub>f</sub>)
  - One touching the **specimen surface** at mid-height (T<sub>s</sub>)
  - One on the **outer furnace wall** (T<sub>c</sub>) — for stability check
- **A data acquisition system** sampling at intervals of ≤ 5 s.
- **A draft shield** surrounding the furnace to minimise air currents.
- **A specimen observation window** with a mirror to record flaming visually.

## 4.3 Functional Data Flow

The complete signal flow during testing can be visualised as:

```
Furnace (750°C)  ──►  Specimen  ──►  Heat Absorption/Combustion
       │                                  │
       │ T_f                              │ T_s
       ▼                                  ▼
   [Thermocouple 1]                [Thermocouple 2]
```
