```mermaid
    h1["FLAG STATE ANNUAL SURVEY"] --> h2["CLASS PERIODICAL SURVEY"]
    h2 --> h3["CLASS RENEWAL SURVEY EVERY 5 YEARS"]
    h3 --> h4["FACTORY ACCEPTANCE TEST FAT AT YARD"]
    h4 --> h5["WEEKLY SHIPBOARD STEWARD ROUTINE"]
    h5 --> h6["MONTHLY CHIEF OFFICER DRILL"]
    h6 --> h1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

> *List any **three** test categories performed on a ship's fire door control system and state one acceptance criterion for each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Functional Release Test (FRT):** Door must close and latch within **$t_c \leq 60$ seconds** for self-closing type, and within **$t_c \leq 40$ seconds** for power-operated sliding type.
2. **Alarm Pre-warning Test:** Audible/visual alarm must sound for **$t_a \geq 5$ seconds** before the door begins closure.
3. **Power-Failure Simulation Test (PFST):** Door must close **by gravity alone** when all electrical power (main + emergency) is removed, demonstrating fail-safe operation.

*(1 mark per test category + acceptance criterion; 3 marks total.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**

> *What does the designation **"A-60"** signify on a marine fire door, and under which standard test curve is it evaluated?*

**Model Answer:**

The "A-60" classification (per **FSS Code Ch. 3 & SOLAS Reg. II-2/3**) denotes:

- **A = Integrity criterion** (no flame / hot gas passage) for **60 minutes**.
- **Insulation criterion** (unexposed face temperature rise ≤ 140 °C average / 180 °C single-point) for **60 minutes**.

The door is evaluated under the **ISO 834** standard time-temperature curve, given by:

$$T(t) = 20 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8t + 1) \;\; ^\circ\text{C}$$

At $t = 60$ min, the fire-side temperature reaches approximately **945 °C**.

*(2 marks for explanation of A-60; 1 mark for ISO 834 reference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Paper, Module 2, Q8 (a)]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat block diagram the architecture of a typical marine Fire Door Control System. Label all field devices, local panels, and the central station.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the procedure for the **Power-Failure Simulation Test (PFST)** of a sliding fire door. State the acceptance criteria and the statutory reference.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

The FDCS comprises **three tiers**:

**Tier 1 — Field Devices (at each door):**

- Door leaf with intumescent seals (rated A-0 to A-60)
- Magnetic holdback coil (24 V DC)
- Local mushroom-head release pushbutton
- Limit switches (door-open and door-closed contacts)
- Local indicator lamps (red/green)

**Tier 2 — Zone Mimic Panels:**

- One per fire zone (typically 3–8 doors per zone)
- Group release button
- Audible buzzer + flashing beacon

**Tier 3 — Central Fire Control Station (Bridge):**

- Master mimic panel showing all doors ship-wide
- Selective or All-Doors release
- Event logger with 24-hour black-box memory
- Powered from main + emergency + battery (24 V DC, ≥ 18 h endurance)

**Interconnections:** Addressable loop from detection panel → FCS → zone panels → field devices. Fail-safe design: loss of signal = door release.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for tier listing; 3 marks for component naming; 2 marks for interconnect explanation.)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**Pre-conditions:** Door fully open, holdback energized, mimic panel normal. Test team of 2 (one at door, one at FCS).

**Step 1 — Trip main power supply** at the main switchboard feeder for the fire door loop.

**Expected:** Door releases from magnetic holdback, alarm sounds for ≥ 5 s, door closes by gravity within ≤ 60 s. Mimic panel may go dark.

**Step 2 — Trip emergency supply** as well. All electrical power is now OFF.

**Expected:** Door **must still close** by gravity/spring alone. This is the critical test — it proves the *fail-safe* design.

**Step 3 — Verify latching** by visual inspection at the door.

**Step 4 — Restore supplies in correct sequence** (main → emergency → confirm holdback re-energizes with audible click).

**Step 5 — Record results** in the Fire Drill Logbook, signed by Master and Chief Engineer.

**Acceptance Criterion:** Door closes & latches with all power OFF within ≤ 60 s.

**Statutory Reference:** **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 9.4.1** and **FSS Code Chapter 3, Paragraph 3.2** (Self-closing fire doors shall be capable of closure against a 3° list).

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks pre-conditions; 2 marks procedure; 1 mark acceptance; 1 mark statutory reference; 1 mark record-keeping.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Main Paper, Module 2, Q9 (b)]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Hinged self-closing** and **Power-operated sliding** fire doors. State at least three points of difference and explain the additional test requirements for power-operated types.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A passenger ship's fire door takes **35 seconds** to close after a **6-second** alarm pre-warning. The door is rated A-30. Verify whether the door passes FSS Code requirements. Also calculate the fire-side temperature at the end of the A-30 rating using the ISO 834 curve.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

| S.No. | Hinged Self-Closing Door | Power-Operated Sliding Door |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spring-loaded hinges provide closure force | Electric motor + clutch provides drive |
| 2 | No alarm pre-warning required (silent closure) | **Mandatory ≥ 5 s** alarm + flashing beacon |
| 3 | Closure time ≤ 60 s permitted | **Tighter ≤ 40 s** limit per FSS Code |
| 4 | No backup power needed (passive) | Requires battery backup ≥ 18 h |
| 5 | Lower cost, simpler test | Higher cost, additional PFST on motor |
| 6 | No remote release from FCS typical | **Remote release from Bridge FCS mandatory** |

**Additional test requirements for power-operated doors:**

- Remote group-release test from Fire Control Station
- Battery backup endurance test (≥ 18 h)
- Pre-warning alarm verification with stopwatch
- Motor stall / clutch slip test under load

*(Mark distribution: 3 marks for table; 2 marks for additional tests; 2 marks for explanation.)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**Given:** $t_c = 35$ s, $t_a = 6$ s, Rating = A-30.

**Step 1 — Verify closure time:**

$$t_c = 35 \, \text{s} \quad \text{vs} \quad t_{c,\max} = 40 \, \text{s}$$

$$35 \leq 40 \;\;\checkmark \text{ PASS}$$

**Step 2 — Verify alarm pre-warning:**

$$t_a = 6 \, \text{s} \quad \text{vs} \quad t_{a,\min} = 5 \, \text{s}$$

$$6 \geq 5 \;\;\checkmark \text{ PASS}$$

**Conclusion:** Door **PASSES** FSS Code timing requirements.

**Step 3 — Fire-side temperature at end of A-30 rating (i.e., at $t = 30$ min):**

Apply ISO 834 equation:

$$T(t) = 20 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

Substitute $t = 30$ min:

$$T(30) = 20 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8 \times 30 + 1)$$

$$T(30) = 20 + 345 \, \log_{10}(241)$$

$$T(30) = 20 + 345 \times 2.3820$$

$$T(30) = 20 + 821.79$$

$$T(30) \approx 842 \, ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Conclusion:** The door must withstand approximately **842 °C** on the exposed face for 30 minutes while keeping the unexposed face below 140 °C average temperature rise.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks verification; 3 marks ISO 834 substitution; 1 mark final answer; 1 mark interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Fire Door Control System questions:**
> 1. **Confusing A-0, A-15, A-30, A-60 ratings** with H-class (H-60, H-120) — they are different! A = Integrity + Insulation; H = Integrity only (for deck penetrations).
> 2. **Forgetting the "3° list" requirement** — every fire door must be tested for closure against a 3° list. Examiners love this.
> 3. **Mixing up closure times** — 60 s for self-closing, 40 s for power-operated. Easy to swap.
> 4. **Omitting the alarm pre-warning** ($\geq 5$ s) — a 7-mark question becomes a 4-mark answer instantly.
> 5. **Not citing SOLAS Reg. II-2/9** or **FSS Code Ch. 3** — every Part B answer should include at least one statutory reference.
> 6. **Ignoring the Power-Failure Simulation Test (PFST)** — Class surveyors reject ships on PFST failure alone. Always mention it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-density 60-second revision dump — read this before every Fire Door question.**

- **Statute:** SOLAS Reg. II-2/9 + FSS Code Ch. 3 are the twin pillars.
- **Door ratings:** A-0, A-15, A-30, A-60 (integrity + insulation in minutes).
- **Test curve:** ISO 834 — $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ °C.
- **Closure time (self-closing):** ≤ 60 s.
- **Closure time (power-operated sliding):** ≤ 40 s.
- **Alarm pre-warning:** ≥ 5 s before closure begins.
- **List tolerance:** Door must close against **3° list** of ship.
- **Voltage:** Holdback operates on **24 V DC** (ship standard).
- **Battery backup:** ≥ **18 hours** endurance.
- **Critical test:** Power-Failure Simulation Test (PFST) — door must close with ALL power OFF.
- **Test interval:** Weekly (steward), Monthly (Chief Officer), Annually (Class Surveyor).
- **Visual inspection:** Intact seals, legible class label, no chocks/wedges.
- **Mimic panel:** RED = open, GREEN = closed (memorize this!).
- **Event logger:** Must record door number, time, date for 24 hours minimum.
- **Common mistake to AVOID:** Never wedge open a fire door — it is a **criminal offence** under SOLAS and may lead to imprisonment of the Master.
- **Examiner's favourite quote:** *"A fire door open is a fire door useless."* — Remember this for viva voce.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Surface Flammability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Surface Flammability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Test for Surface Flammability** (also known as the **Radiant Panel Flame Spread Test** or **IMO Surface Flammability Test (SFT)**) is a standardized fire-test procedure prescribed under the **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)**, specifically **Part 5 — Test for Surface Flammability** (formerly **IMO Resolution A.653(16)**).

It is used to evaluate the **flame-spread characteristics of exposed surface materials** (ceilings, wall linings, floor coverings, bulkhead insulations, and decorative veneers) used in **ships regulated under SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Convention**. The test quantifies how rapidly flame propagates along a material surface when exposed to a **graded radiant heat flux** profile and a small **pilot ignition source**.

The governing regulatory parameters established by this test are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Critical Flux at Extinguishment | **CFE** | Heat flux below which flame does not sustain propagation |
| Heat for Sustained Burning | **Qsb** | Total heat input required to sustain burning |
| Surface Flammability Index | **Is** | Composite non-dimensional index grading surface fire performance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 5.3**, all surface materials (including paints, laminates, veneers, textiles, and foam-backed composites) installed on **ships built on or after 1 July 1998** must be tested and certified per the **FTP Code Part 5** methodology. Compliance is evidenced by a **Type Approval Certificate** issued by a flag-state recognized laboratory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are lighting a row of candles by tilting only the **first candle** under a heat lamp:

- The heat lamp radiates different intensities across the row — very strong near the lamp, weak at the far end.
- If the **wax melts and vaporizes easily**, the flame will "creep" along the row of candles (bad surface).
- If the wax is hard and resists melting, the flame dies out before reaching the end (good surface).

In the Surface Flammability Test, a **material sample is the "row of candles"** and a **radiant heat panel is the "heat lamp"**. The **pilot flame** acts as the "match" that ignites the hot end. Engineers then measure **how far the flame spreads** and **how much energy it consumed** — the smaller both numbers, the safer the material is for a ship interior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students**: A material with a **low CFE** burns easily even under weak heat. A material with a **high CFE** is hard to ignite and stops spreading flame quickly. **High CFE + low Qsb = best fire performance for ship interiors.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and reference values must be memorized for KTU examination:

- **Standard specimen dimensions:** **800 mm × 155 mm** (length × width)
- **Specimen holder angle:** **30°** from vertical (i.e., inclined facing the radiant panel)
- **Pilot flame:** small **Tirril burner** positioned 8 mm above the specimen surface
- **Heat flux distribution:** graded from **≈ 50 kW/m²** at the hot end to **≈ 1.4 kW/m²** at the cool end
- **Heat flux at "no-burn" reference distance:** **0** kW/m² (at the far edge, beyond specimen)
- **Number of specimens tested:** typically **3** at base-line flux levels
- **Test result criteria for ships (IMO):** **CFE ≥ 20 kW/m²** for general ship surfaces (permitted lower values for small areas)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why a Heat-Flux Gradient?

A real ship corridor fire does not expose all surfaces to a **uniform** heat intensity. Near the seat of the fire, surfaces see high heat flux; far away, only weak flux. To simulate this realistically, the FTP Code mandates a **graded (linearly decreasing) heat-flux profile** along the specimen rather than a single uniform level.

This is achieved by:
1. A **porous refractory radiant panel** (≈ 300 mm × 450 mm) backed by an electric heater and a calibrated gas–air mixture.
2. The panel is **inclined at 15° from the vertical**, while the specimen is **inclined at 30° from the vertical**, producing a precise flux gradient.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The test procedure is executed in the following structured sequence:

1. **Specimen conditioning:** The sample is conditioned at **23 °C ± 2 °C** and **50 % ± 5 % relative humidity** for **24 hours** minimum, per ISO 554.
2. **Mounting:** The specimen is placed in the holder, with the **exposed face** toward the radiant panel.
3. **Pilot flame application:** A small pilot flame is applied at the **upper, hot end** of the specimen.
4. **Heat flux calibration:** A **water-cooled Schmidt-Boelter heat-flux transducer** measures flux at 50 mm intervals along the proposed specimen length.
5. **Ignition observation:** Time of ignition, flame spread front position, and time to extinguish are recorded.
6. **Quenching distances:** Two key positions are noted:
   - **ΔL₁**: distance from hot end to the **leading edge of sustained burning** (front of the luminous flame).
   - **ΔL₂**: distance from hot end to the **trailing edge of sustained burning** (where flame self-extinguishes).
7. **Sample scanning:** A **radiometer** or **pyrometer** (sometimes a data-acquisition radiometer) is moved along the length, mapping instantaneous heat flux at the burn-front location.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two distances (ΔL₁ and ΔL₂)?** — A material can have a long "glowing" zone with a short flame zone, or vice-versa. ΔL₁ captures the **flame front propagation**, while ΔL₂ captures the **residual heat-affected zone**. The difference **(ΔL₂ − ΔL₁)** is a measure of the **post-flame smouldering zone**.

## 2.3 Governing Equations

### 2.3.1 Critical Flux at Extinguishment (CFE)

The CFE is the radiant heat flux at the position where the **leading edge of the burning zone** is just self-extinguished:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFE} &= q_0 \, e^{-k \, \Delta L_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **$q_0$** = heat flux at the **hot end** of the specimen (typically calibrated to **50.6 kW/m²**)
- **$k$** = **flux-gradient decay constant** (≈ **0.0466 mm⁻¹**, derived from calibration per FTP Code Annex 1)
- **$\Delta L_2$** = distance (in mm) from the hot end to the **trailing edge** of sustained burning

> **Mnemonic:** "CFE = **q-zero times e to the minus k-L-2**" — the flux at the flame-out location, projected through the exponential decay of the panel.

### 2.3.2 Heat for Sustained Burning (Qsb)

The total integrated heat per unit width that must be delivered to the specimen to sustain burning across the flame-spread zone:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sb} &= \dfrac{q_0}{k} \, \left( e^{-k \, \Delta L_1} - e^{-k \, \Delta L_2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **$\Delta L_1$** = distance from hot end to the **leading edge** of the burning zone
- Other terms as defined above

**Units:** **kJ/m²** (since flux in kW/m² divided by k in mm⁻¹ and multiplied by mm gives kJ/m²)

### 2.3.3 Surface Flammability Index (Is)

The composite index combining both CFE (resistance to ignition) and Qsb (sustainability of burning). A **higher $I_s$ = better fire performance**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s &= 0 \, (\text{if CFE} \le 0) \\
I_s &= 1 + \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{0.04 \, Q_{sb}} \quad (\text{for CFE} > 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **constant 0.04** in the denominator normalizes $Q_{sb}$ such that **Is = 0** corresponds to a **non-fire-resistant material** (essentially a fuel) and **Is ≈ 100+** corresponds to a **highly fire-resistant material** (e.g., incombustible mineral wool).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol/Variable | Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Flux decay along panel | $q(x) = q_0 \, e^{-k x}$ | $q_0$ initial flux, $k$ decay constant, $x$ distance from hot end | kW/m² | Heat intensity at any point along specimen |
| **CFE** | $q_0 \, e^{-k \, \Delta L_2}$ | $\Delta L_2$ = trailing edge distance | kW/m² | Lowest flux to sustain burning |
| **Qsb** | $\dfrac{q_0}{k} \left( e^{-k\Delta L_1} - e^{-k\Delta L_2} \right)$ | $\Delta L_1$ = leading edge distance | kJ/m² | Total heat to sustain burning per unit width |
| **Is (Surface Flammability Index)** | $1 + \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{0.04 \, Q_{sb}}$ | — | dimensionless | Composite safety grading |
| Calibration constant | $q_0 = 50.6$ kW/m², $k = 0.0466$ mm⁻¹ | — | — | Apparatus-specific values from FTP Code Annex 1 |
| Specimen geometry | $L \times W = 800 \times 155$ mm at 30° from vertical | — | mm | Standard test sample |
| Pass criterion (general) | $\text{CFE} \ge 20$ kW/m² | — | kW/m² | SOLAS II-2/5.3 minimum for ship surfaces |

> **Avoid Table-Breaking Symbols:** Note that for **absolute value** type expressions in any extended derivation, students should use $\vert \Delta L \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ rather than `|x|` to maintain KTU-formatted table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Surface Flammability Test

- **Shipbuilding industry:** Mandatory for selecting **interior linings, ceiling panels, and bulkhead insulation** in passenger and cargo ships. Compliance is verified by a **Marine Equipment Directive (MED)** wheel-mark certificate.
- **Offshore platforms:** Many operators adopt FTP Code Part 5 as best practice for accommodation modules.
- **Tunnel and metro design:** The **ASTM E162** radiant-panel test is a U.S. analogue; results correlate with $I_s$ trends.
- **Architectural fire safety:** Used to grade **wall and ceiling finishes** in high-rise buildings, hospitals, and airports.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Surface Flammability Index ($I_s$)

The goal is to derive how the **$I_s$** expression naturally emerges from the combination of CFE and Qsb.

### Step 1 — Establish the dimensionally meaningful parameter

A material is "safe" when:
- It **ignites only under high heat flux** (large CFE), AND
- It **does not release much energy** during sustained burning (small $Q_{sb}$).

Therefore, the **ratio** $\dfrac{\text{CFE}}{Q_{sb}}$ intuitively captures the "safety quotient."

### Step 2 — Assign scaling constants

From empirical IMO calibration studies on reference materials:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reference worst-case material: } \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{Q_{sb}} \approx 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reference excellent material: } \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{Q_{sb}} \approx 3.96 \text{ (dimensionless in kW·s/m⁴)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: CFE is in kW/m² and Qsb is in kJ/m² (= kW·s/m²). Their ratio has units of **(kW/m²) / (kW·s/m²) = 1/s**. To make the index dimensionless, a **time-scale constant** of **25 seconds** is embedded, equivalent to dividing Qsb by **0.04** (since 1/25 = 0.04).

### Step 3 — Construct the composite index

Adding "1" shifts the worst-case value from 0 to 1, ensuring all materials have **$I_s \ge 1$** when $I_s$ is defined:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s &= 1 + \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{0.04 \, Q_{sb}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a shipboard **melamine-faced particle board** specimen yields:
- $q_0 = 50.6$ kW/m²
- $k = 0.0466$ mm⁻¹
- $\Delta L_1 = 150$ mm
- $\Delta L_2 = 250$ mm

**Step (a) — Calculate CFE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFE} &= 50.6 \times e^{-(0.0466)(250)} \\
&= 50.6 \times e^{-11.65} \\
&= 50.6 \times 8.71 \times 10^{-6} \\
&\approx 4.41 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kW/m²}
\end{aligned}
$$

This extremely low value indicates the material continues to burn even where the heat flux is very weak — **fire-hazardous**.

**Step (b) — Calculate Qsb:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sb} &= \dfrac{50.6}{0.0466} \left( e^{-(0.0466)(150)} - e^{-(0.0466)(250)} \right) \\
&= 1085.84 \left( e^{-6.99} - e^{-11.65} \right) \\
&= 1085.84 \left( 9.22 \times 10^{-4} - 8.71 \times 10^{-6} \right) \\
&= 1085.84 \times 9.13 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\approx 0.992 \text{ kJ/m²}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Calculate $I_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s &= 1 + \dfrac{4.41 \times 10^{-4}}{0.04 \times 0.992} \\
&= 1 + \dfrac{4.41 \times 10^{-4}}{0.03968} \\
&= 1 + 0.0111 \\
&\approx 1.01
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of worked example:** An $I_s$ value near **1** confirms the material is **highly flammable** and **fails SOLAS** requirements (typically requiring $I_s \gg 20$ for ship approval). This result would mandate replacement with a fire-retardant-treated board or a non-combustible alternative.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of Results

| $I_s$ Range | Material Behaviour | Example Material | SOLAS Compliant? |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 – 5 | Highly combustible | Untreated plywood, polyurethane foam | **No** |
| 5 – 20 | Moderately combustible | FR-treated particleboard | Limited use (small areas) |
| 20 – 50 | Low flame spread | Phenolic laminates, fire-rated plasterboard | **Yes** |
| 50 – 100+ | Negligible flame spread | Mineral wool, ceramic-fibre composites, calcium silicate board | **Yes** (preferred) |

## 3.3 Sample Length and Flux Constant — Origin of $k = 0.0466$ mm⁻¹

From the IMO Annex 1 calibration procedure, the panel flux follows an exponential decay fitted to five flux measurement points at $x = 50, 150, 250, 350, 450$ mm. Linearization gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\left( \dfrac{q(x)}{q_0} \right) &= -k \, x
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $q(50) = 48.5$ kW/m² and $q(450) = 1.4$ kW/m²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \dfrac{\ln(48.5) - \ln(1.4)}{450 - 50} \\
&= \dfrac{3.882 - 0.336}{400} \\
&= \dfrac{3.546}{400} \\
&\approx 0.00887 \text{ mm}^{-1} \text{ (apparatus-specific; varies by panel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note to KTU Students:** The exact value of $k$ is **laboratory-calibrated** and **must be cited from the test certificate**. The textbook representative value is **$k \approx 0.0466$ mm⁻¹** for the standard IMO apparatus. **Always use the certificate value in numerical problems.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture Flow of the Surface Flammability Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Conditioning 23C 50%RH 24h] --> B[Mount Specimen in Holder at 30 degrees]
    B --> C[Calibrate Radiant Panel with Heat Flux Transducer]
    C --> D[Apply Pilot Flame at Hot End]
    D --> E[Record Ignition Time t-ign]
    E --> F[Monitor Flame Front Propagation]
    F --> G[Measure Leading Edge Distance Delta L1]
    F --> H[Measure Trailing Edge Distance Delta L2]
    G --> I[Compute CFE using q0 e to the power of minus k Delta L2]
    H --> J[Compute Qsb using q0 over k times e to the minus k Delta L1 minus e to the minus k Delta L2]
    I --> K[Compute Surface Flammability Index Is]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Is Compliance Check versus SOLAS FTP Code Part 5}
    L -- Meets Criterion --> M[Issue MED Type Approval Certificate]
    L -- Fails Criterion --> N[Reject Material for Ship Use]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Apparatus Layout (Schematic Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Gas Air Supply Unit] --> P2[Radiant Panel 300x450 mm]
    P3[Electric Heater] --> P2
    P2 --> P4[Specimen at 30 deg Inclination]
    P5[Pilot Flame Tirril Burner] --> P4
    P4 --> P6[Flame Spread Observation Window]
    P7[Heat Flux Transducer Schmidt Boelter] --> P8[Data Acquisition System]
    P4 --> P7
    P8 --> P9[Compute CFE Qsb Is]
    P9 --> P10[Type Approval Report]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Matrix for Material Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Material Sample Received] --> S2{Is it Non Combustible ISO 1182}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Auto Approve for All Ship Areas]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Conduct Surface Flammability Test FTP Part 5]
    S4 --> S5{Is value Greater than 20}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Approve for General Use]
    S5 -- No --> S7{Area less than 20 sqm and Low Fire Load}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Restricted Approval with Risk Assessment]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Reject and Reject Use]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above (A, B, C, …, S1, S2 …) are **alphanumeric**, with no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone IDs. All node labels are **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** to prevent Mermaid-parser conflicts. This block-level functional flow replaces the physical apparatus drawing that cannot be rendered in plain markdown.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**Define the term "Surface Flammability Index ($I_s$)" as per IMO FTP Code Part 5. State its physical significance and the typical pass-criterion value for general ship surfaces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Surface Flammability Index ($I_s$)** is a composite, non-dimensional numerical index defined in **IMO FTP Code Part 5** to grade the fire performance of surface materials. It is calculated as:
> $$I_s = 1 + \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{0.04 \, Q_{sb}}$$
> where **CFE** is the **Critical Flux at Extinguishment** (kW/m²) and **$Q_{sb}$** is the **Heat for Sustained Burning** (kJ/m²).
>
> **Physical significance:** A higher $I_s$ indicates a **safer** material that is harder to ignite (high CFE) and releases less energy when burning (low Qsb). A value of $I_s = 1$ represents the worst-case combustible material.
>
> **Pass criterion:** For general ship surfaces under **SOLAS Regulation II-2/5.3**, materials must typically achieve **$I_s$ ≥ 20** (corresponding to CFE ≥ 20 kW/m²) to receive type-approval certification. **(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for pass criterion)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023, Model]

**List the three standard parameters reported from the Surface Flammability Test and explain the meaning of the "heat-flux gradient" used in the test apparatus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The three standard parameters are:
> 1. **Critical Flux at Extinguishment (CFE)** — the minimum heat flux needed to sustain flame propagation on the specimen. **(1 mark)**
> 2. **Heat for Sustained Burning ($Q_{sb}$)** — the integrated heat energy per unit width required to sustain the burning zone. **(1 mark)**
> 3. **Surface Flammability Index ($I_s$)** — a composite dimensionless index grading overall fire performance.
>
> **Heat-flux gradient meaning:** A real ship fire produces non-uniform heat exposure on surfaces, with higher flux near the fire seat. The test apparatus uses a **porous refractory radiant panel** that delivers a **graded flux profile** — typically **50.6 kW/m²** at the hot end, decaying exponentially to **≈ 1.4 kW/m²** at the cool end, modeling real-world fire conditions. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — December 2022, Model, with internal choice]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **apparatus and procedure** of the IMO Surface Flammability Test as specified in **FTP Code Part 5**. Highlight the function of the **pilot flame**, **radiant panel**, and **heat-flux transducer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **Surface Flammability Index $I_s$** starting from the exponential heat-flux profile of the radiant panel. A bulkhead-lining specimen is tested in a calibrated apparatus with $q_0 = 50.6$ kW/m², $k = 0.0466$ mm⁻¹, $\Delta L_1 = 80$ mm, and $\Delta L_2 = 180$ mm. Compute the values of **CFE, $Q_{sb}$, and $I_s$** and comment on the suitability of the material for ship use. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Apparatus and Procedure (7 marks):**

- **Apparatus (4 marks):** The IMO Surface Flammability Test rig consists of:
  - A **porous refractory radiant panel** (≈ 300 mm × 450 mm) backed by a **gas–air premix burner** and an **electric heater**, capable of delivering graded flux from 50.6 kW/m² to ≈ 1.4 kW/m². **(1 mark)**
  - A **specimen holder** that mounts the test sample at **30° from vertical**, facing the radiant panel. Standard sample size: **800 mm × 155 mm**. **(1 mark)**
  - A **pilot flame** (Tirril-type gas burner) positioned 8 mm above the surface at the **hot end** to ignite combustible gases. **(1 mark)**
  - A **water-cooled Schmidt-Boelter heat-flux transducer** mounted on a movable carriage, used to calibrate and verify the flux gradient along the specimen length. Output is logged via a **data-acquisition system**. **(1 mark)**

- **Procedure (3 marks):**
  1. **Conditioning:** Specimen kept at 23 °C ± 2 °C and 50 % ± 5 % RH for 24 h. **(1 mark)**
  2. **Calibration:** Heat-flux transducer measures flux at five positions; constants $q_0$ and $k$ are recorded. **(1 mark)**
  3. **Testing:** Pilot flame is applied; flame-spread front is observed and $\Delta L_1$, $\Delta L_2$ are measured; from these, CFE, $Q_{sb}$, and $I_s$ are calculated. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) — Derivation and Computation (7 marks):**

- **Derivation (3 marks):** The flux profile is $q(x) = q_0 e^{-k x}$. The flux at the trailing edge position $\Delta L_2$ is the CFE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFE} &= q(\Delta L_2) = q_0 \, e^{-k \Delta L_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total heat to sustain burning across the flame zone is the area under the flux curve from $\Delta L_1$ to $\Delta L_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sb} &= \int_{\Delta L_1}^{\Delta L_2} q(x) \, dx = \int_{\Delta L_1}^{\Delta L_2} q_0 e^{-kx} dx \\
&= \dfrac{q_0}{k} \left[ e^{-k \Delta L_1} - e^{-k \Delta L_2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The composite safety index normalizes this to a dimensionless form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s &= 1 + \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{0.04 \, Q_{sb}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFE} &= 50.6 \times e^{-(0.0466)(180)} \\
&= 50.6 \times e^{-8.388} \\
&= 50.6 \times 2.27 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\approx 0.0115 \text{ kW/m²}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sb} &= \dfrac{50.6}{0.0466} \left( e^{-(0.0466)(80)} - e^{-(0.0466)(180)} \right) \\
&= 1085.84 \left( e^{-3.728} - e^{-8.388} \right) \\
&= 1085.84 \left( 0.02405 - 2.27 \times 10^{-4} \right) \\
&= 1085.84 \times 0.02382 \\
&\approx 25.86 \text{ kJ/m²}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s &= 1 + \dfrac{0.0115}{0.04 \times 25.86} \\
&= 1 + \dfrac{0.0115}{1.0344} \\
&= 1 + 0.0111 \\
&\approx 1.01
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Comment (1 mark):** With **$I_s \approx 1$** and **CFE ≈ 0.01 kW/m²**, the material is **highly combustible**, fails SOLAS requirements, and **must be rejected** for use as ship bulkhead lining. A fire-retardant-treated or non-combustible alternative (e.g., mineral wool, calcium silicate board) should be used.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model, alternative]**

**(a)** Define the terms **CFE** and **$Q_{sb}$**. Explain their physical meanings and the relationship between them in the context of surface flame spread. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship ceiling panel is tested and yields $q_0 = 50.6$ kW/m², $k = 0.0466$ mm⁻¹, $\Delta L_1 = 120$ mm, $\Delta L_2 = 300$ mm. Compute **CFE, $Q_{sb}$, and $I_s$** and state whether the panel satisfies **SOLAS II-2/5.3** for unrestricted use. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Definitions and Relationship (7 marks):**

- **CFE — Critical Flux at Extinguishment (3 marks):**
  CFE is the **minimum radiant heat flux** (in kW/m²) that must be incident on a material surface to **sustain** flame propagation. Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFE} = q_0 \, e^{-k \Delta L_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

  A high CFE means the material requires intense heating to keep burning — a desirable fire property.

- **$Q_{sb}$ — Heat for Sustained Burning (3 marks):**
  $Q_{sb}$ is the **integrated heat energy per unit specimen width** (in kJ/m²) that the material must absorb to maintain a steady burning front. It is the area under the heat-flux curve between the leading and trailing edges of the burning zone:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sb} = \dfrac{q_0}{k} \left( e^{-k \Delta L_1} - e^{-k \Delta L_2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

  A low $Q_{sb}$ indicates the material has limited thermal energy available for sustaining combustion — also desirable.

- **Relationship (1 mark):** Both quantities are derived from the same exponential flux profile. CFE captures **ignition resistance**, $Q_{sb}$ captures **burning sustainability**, and together they yield the composite $I_s$ that grades overall surface fire performance.

---

**(b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFE} &= 50.6 \times e^{-(0.0466)(300)} \\
&= 50.6 \times e^{-13.98} \\
&= 50.6 \times 8.39 \times 10^{-7} \\
&\approx 4.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kW/m²}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sb} &= \dfrac{50.6}{0.0466} \left( e^{-(0.0466)(120)} - e^{-(0.0466)(300)} \right) \\
&= 1085.84 \left( e^{-5.592} - e^{-13.98} \right) \\
&= 1085.84 \left( 3.72 \times 10^{-3} - 8.39 \times 10^{-7} \right) \\
&= 1085.84 \times 3.719 \times 10^{-3} \\
&\approx 4.04 \text{ kJ/m²}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s &= 1 + \dfrac{4.25 \times 10^{-5}}{0.04 \times 4.04} \\
&= 1 + \dfrac{4.25 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1616} \\
&= 1 + 2.63 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\approx 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The panel has an **$I_s$ ≈ 1.00** and **CFE ≈ 0 kW/m²**, indicating **extreme flammability**. The panel **fails SOLAS II-2/5.3** and is **not approved** for unrestricted use. It may be considered only for **non-critical concealed areas** with formal risk-assessment approval.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Resulting in Mark Deductions):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1"** in the $I_s$ formula. The full expression is $I_s = 1 + \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{0.04 Q_{sb}}$, **not** $I_s = \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{0.04 Q_{sb}}$. Losing this gives an index of essentially 0, which the examiner will mark wrong. **(–1 mark)**
> 2. **Mixing up ΔL₁ and ΔL₂** — ΔL₁ is the **leading edge** (smaller distance, hotter location), ΔL₂ is the **trailing edge** (larger distance, cooler location). Reversing them in Qsb gives a wrong sign inside the parentheses. **(–1 mark)**
> 3. **Using inconsistent units** — k must be in **mm⁻¹** (not cm⁻¹ or m⁻¹) when ΔL is in mm. The product $k \Delta L$ must be **dimensionless**. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Stating the wrong regulatory threshold** — Some students write $I_s \ge 1$ as a pass criterion. The correct SOLAS pass criterion is **$I_s \ge 20$** (or **CFE ≥ 20 kW/m²**) for general unrestricted ship use. **(–2 marks)**
> 5. **Citing ASTM E84 or ISO 9705** instead of **FTP Code Part 5** as the regulatory standard for ship surfaces. The IMO standard is **FTP Code Part 5 / Resolution A.653(16)**. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Surface Flammability Test:**
>
> - **Regulatory basis:** **IMO FTP Code Part 5** (formerly **Resolution A.653(16)**); enforced under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 5.3**.
> - **Apparatus key components:** porous refractory **radiant panel**, **specimen holder at 30°**, **Tirril pilot flame**, **Schmidt-Boelter heat-flux transducer**.
> - **Standard specimen:** **800 mm × 155 mm**, conditioned at **23 °C / 50 % RH for 24 h**.
> - **Three reported parameters:** **CFE** (kW/m²), **$Q_{sb}$** (kJ/m²), and **$I_s$** (dimensionless).
> - **Exponential flux profile:** $q(x) = q_0 e^{-k x}$ with $q_0 = 50.6$ kW/m² and $k \approx 0.0466$ mm⁻¹.
> - **CFE formula:** $\text{CFE} = q_0 e^{-k \Delta L_2}$ — flux at the trailing edge of burning.
> - **Qsb formula:** $Q_{sb} = \dfrac{q_0}{k}\left(e^{-k \Delta L_1} - e^{-k \Delta L_2}\right)$ — area under flux curve in burn zone.
> - **$I_s$ formula:** $I_s = 1 + \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{0.04 \, Q_{sb}}$ — composite safety grading.
> - **Pass criterion (general ship surfaces):** **$I_s \ge 20$** and **CFE ≥ 20 kW/m²**.
> - **Higher $I_s$ = safer material**; "$I_s = 1$" corresponds to a worst-case combustible material.
> - **ΔL₁ < ΔL₂** (leading edge distance is always smaller than trailing edge distance along the flux gradient).
> - **Calibration:** the values of $q_0$ and $k$ must be obtained from the **apparatus calibration certificate**, not assumed from textbooks, in exam numerical problems.
> - **Compliance evidence:** a **Marine Equipment Directive (MED) wheel-mark** certificate or **flag-state Type Approval Certificate**.
> - **Common application areas:** ship bulkhead linings, ceiling panels, floor coverings, pipe insulation, and decorative veneers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Vertically Supported Textiles and Films
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Vertically Supported Textiles and Films

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Test for Vertically Supported Textiles and Films** is a standardised small-flame ignitability test prescribed under **Part 7 of the IMO International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code 2010)**, adopted under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 3.40**. It determines the propensity of a textile or film, when mounted in a vertical orientation, to propagate flame upward and to produce flaming droplets that could ignite adjacent combustible materials in ship accommodations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This test governs the acceptance of curtains, drapes, upholstery coverings, decorative cloths, suspended textile assemblies, plastic films, and any combustible surface material used vertically in passenger and crew spaces of seagoing vessels.

The test specimen (typically **220 mm wide × 1550 mm long**) is mounted on a vertical steel frame, and a calibrated **40 mm high propane gas flame** is applied to the lower surface of the specimen for **5 seconds** at a distance of **20 mm** from the lower edge.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Theatre Curtain Principle"

Imagine a heavy velvet theatre curtain hanging vertically. When a small match is held briefly to the bottom hem, what happens next determines the safety of an entire auditorium. If the flame climbs rapidly, scorching the entire curtain, dripping flaming bits onto seats, the material is dangerous. If the flame simply chars a small patch and dies out within seconds, the material is safe.

The IMO Part 7 test formalises this real-world concern. Instead of a match, a **calibrated 40 mm flame** is used. Instead of a 4-metre theatre curtain, a **standardised 220 mm × 1550 mm specimen** is used. The "dangerous theatre curtain" is the **fail case**; the "self-extinguishing laboratory-tested fabric" is the **pass case**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Insight:** A fabric that resists vertical flame spread when a small flame is briefly applied to its bottom edge is unlikely to contribute to catastrophic fire spread in a ship corridor or cabin during the critical first 60–120 seconds of a fire incident.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics Used in the Test

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specimen width | $W_s$ | **220** | mm |
| Specimen length | $L_s$ | **1550** | mm |
| Flame height | $h_f$ | **40** | mm |
| Burner–specimen distance | $d_b$ | **20** | mm |
| Flame application time | $t_a$ | **5** | s |
| Conditioning temperature | $T_c$ | **23** | °C |
| Conditioning humidity | $RH_c$ | **50** | % |
| Max permissible damaged length | $L_{d,\max}$ | **150** | mm |
| Max permissible afterflame time | $t_{af,\max}$ | **5** | s |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis of the Test

The test is founded on three interlocking physical principles of fire science:

1. **Surface flame spread on thermally thin solids** — for a vertically oriented fabric, the upward flame-spread velocity $v_f$ is governed by convective and radiative heat transfer from the flame to the unburnt fabric ahead of the pyrolysis front.
2. **Self-sustained combustion criterion** — propagation continues only if the heat flux received by the unburnt fabric exceeds the **critical heat flux for ignition**, $\dot{q}''_{ig}$.
3. **Drop transfer of fire** — flaming drips transfer combustion to lower combustible surfaces (e.g., a bunk mattress ignited by curtain drips).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Specimen Preparation**
- Three specimens are cut, each **220 mm × 1550 mm**, in the warp and weft directions (for woven textiles).
- Specimens are conditioned for **24 hours** at $23\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and **50 % RH**.

**Step 2 — Mounting**
- Specimen is clamped to a vertical stainless-steel frame so the lower edge is exposed.
- The exposed face area is approximately $220\,\text{mm} \times 1500\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 3 — Flame Application**
- A propane burner producing a **40 mm high** yellow-tipped blue flame is positioned such that the flame impinges on the lower face of the specimen, **20 mm** from the lower edge.
- Flame is held in contact for **5 seconds**, then withdrawn smoothly.

**Step 4 — Observation and Measurement**
- **Afterflame time** $t_{af}$ — duration of flaming after burner removal.
- **Afterglow time** $t_{ag}$ — duration of smouldering/glowing.
- **Damaged length** $L_d$ — measured as the maximum distance between the original lower edge and the farthest point of damage (charring, melting, or combustion).
- **Flaming droplets** — observed during and 5 s after flame application.

**Step 5 — Pass/Fail Determination**
- Mean values from 3 specimens compared against the acceptance criteria.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{d,\text{mean}} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{d,i}$ | Mean damaged length across $n$ specimens (typically $n=3$) | mm |
| 2 | $t_{af,\text{mean}} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{af,i}$ | Mean afterflame time | s |
| 3 | $L_{d,\text{mean}} \le 150\,\text{mm}$ | Pass criterion for damaged length | — |
| 4 | $t_{af,\text{mean}} \le 5\,\text{s}$ | Pass criterion for afterflame time | — |
| 5 | $t_{ag,\max} \le 5\,\text{s}$ | Pass criterion for afterglow time | — |
| 6 | $\dot{q}''_{ig} = h_c(T_{ig} - T_s) + \varepsilon \sigma (T_{ig}^{4} - T_s^{4})$ | Critical heat flux for ignition of the fabric surface | kW/m² |
| 7 | $v_f \propto \dfrac{\dot{q}''_{\text{flame}} - \dot{q}''_{ig}}{\rho_s c_s \delta (T_{ig} - T_s)}$ | Quasi-steady upward flame-spread velocity on a thin solid | mm/s |
| 8 | $B = \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{ig}}$ | Burning number (ratio of combustion enthalpy to gasification enthalpy) | — |
| 9 | $LOI = \dfrac{[O_2]}{[O_2] + [N_2]} \times 100\,\%$ | Limiting Oxygen Index (correlates with vertical flame spread) | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why formula (7) matters in this test:** A fabric with a low $\dot{q}''_{ig}$ (easy to ignite) and a small $T_{ig}$ will have a higher $v_f$, hence a larger $L_d$ during the 5-second flame application. The IMO Part 7 test empirically quantifies this physical tendency.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship outfitting compliance**: Curtains in passenger cabins, decorative fabric panels in lounges, suspended textile screens in stairways — all must pass Part 7.
- **Life-saving appliances**: Bunk covers, mattress tickings, and personal effects lockers' textile linings.
- **Hospital and high-risk zones**: Materials with $L_d < 50$ mm and $t_{af} < 1$ s are preferred for areas with high evacuee density.
- **Insurance and class society certification**: Failure to comply leads to detention of the vessel by Port State Control under SOLAS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Sample Calculation — Determination of Pass/Fail Status

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A vertically hung decorative textile is tested under IMO FTP Code Part 7 using three specimens. The recorded observations are tabulated below. Determine whether the textile passes the acceptance criteria.

| Specimen | Damaged Length $L_d$ (mm) | Afterflame Time $t_{af}$ (s) | Afterglow Time $t_{ag}$ (s) | Flaming Droplets |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | 105 | 2.0 | 4.0 | None |
| S2 | 125 | 3.0 | 6.0 | None |
| S3 | 145 | 1.0 | 3.0 | None |

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Compute the Mean Damaged Length

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{d,\text{mean}} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{d,i} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{3}\bigl(L_{d,1} + L_{d,2} + L_{d,3}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{3}\bigl(105 + 125 + 145\bigr)\,\text{mm} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{3} \times 375\,\text{mm} \\[4pt]
&= 125\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with criterion:**

$$
L_{d,\text{mean}} = 125\,\text{mm} \;\le\; 150\,\text{mm} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{PASS}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute the Mean Afterflame Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{af,\text{mean}} &= \frac{1}{3}\bigl(t_{af,1} + t_{af,2} + t_{af,3}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{3}\bigl(2.0 + 3.0 + 1.0\bigr)\,\text{s} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{6.0}{3}\,\text{s} = 2.0\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
t_{af,\text{mean}} = 2.0\,\text{s} \;\le\; 5\,\text{s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{PASS}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute the Maximum Afterglow Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ag,\max} &= \max\bigl(t_{ag,1},\, t_{ag,2},\, t_{ag,3}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \max(4.0,\, 6.0,\, 3.0)\,\text{s} \\[4pt]
&= 6.0\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
t_{ag,\max} = 6.0\,\text{s} \;\;>\;\; 5\,\text{s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{FAIL}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Flaming Droplet Check

$$
\text{Flaming droplets observed} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{PASS}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Final Decision

$$
\text{Overall Result} = L_{d}\;\text{PASS} \;\wedge\; t_{af}\;\text{PASS} \;\wedge\; t_{ag}\;\text{FAIL} \;\wedge\; \text{Droplets}\;\text{PASS}
$$

$$
\therefore \;\;\; \boxed{\text{TEXTILE FAILS THE TEST} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Not approved for shipboard use}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "AND" Rule:** A specimen passes **only if ALL four criteria** are simultaneously satisfied. A single failure in afterglow time is sufficient to reject the entire material batch.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why the Test Predicts Real-Ship Fire Behaviour

| Physical Phenomenon in a Ship Fire | Laboratory Simulation in Part 7 | Measured Output |
|---|---|---|
| Curtain catching fire from a small ignition source (e.g., electrical short) | 5-second 40 mm flame on lower edge | Afterflame time $t_{af}$ |
| Upward flame climb on a corridor drape | Vertical flame spread over 1500 mm length | Damaged length $L_d$ |
| Burning drips igniting mattresses below | Flaming droplet observation | Pass/Fail on drips |
| Smouldering reignition in concealed spaces | Continuing glow after flame extinction | Afterglow time $t_{ag}$ |

## 3.3 Worked Calculation — Flame-Spread Velocity Estimate

Using the de Ris-type correlation for thermally thin vertical surfaces:

$$
v_f \;=\; \frac{\lambda_g \, (T_f - T_{ig})}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, (T_{ig} - T_s)}
$$

Let:
- $\lambda_g = 0.025\,\text{W/m·K}$ (thermal conductivity of hot gas)
- $T_f = 1200\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 1473\,\text{K}$ (flame temperature)
- $T_{ig} = 350\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 623\,\text{K}$ (fabric surface ignition temperature)
- $T_s = 23\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 296\,\text{K}$ (ambient)
- $\rho_s = 300\,\text{kg/m}^3$ (woven polyester fabric)
- $c_s = 1500\,\text{J/kg·K}$ (specific heat of polyester)
- $\delta = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$ (thermal penetration depth, ~fabric thickness)

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_f &= \frac{0.025 \times (1473 - 623)}{300 \times 1500 \times 1.0\times 10^{-3} \times (623 - 296)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.025 \times 850}{300 \times 1500 \times 10^{-3} \times 327} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{21.25}{147.15} \\[4pt]
&= 0.144\,\text{m/s} \;\;=\; 144\,\text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

In a 5-second flame application, the predicted flame-front travel is:

$$
L_{f,\text{pred}} = v_f \times t_a = 144 \times 5 = 720\,\text{mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If $L_{f,\text{pred}} \gg 150\,\text{mm}$, the fabric will likely FAIL the test unless its actual $T_{ig}$ is higher than the assumed $350\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (e.g., flame-retardant-treated cotton, aramid, or inherently FR fibres).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Test Setup Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Textile or Film Sample] --> B[Cut 3 Specimens 220mm x 1550mm]
    B --> C[Condition 24h at 23C and 50 percent RH]
    C --> D[Mount Vertically on Steel Frame]
    D --> E[Position Propane Burner 20mm Below Lower Edge]
    E --> F[Apply 40mm Flame for 5 seconds]
    F --> G[Withdraw Burner]
    G --> H[Record Afterflame Time t_af]
    H --> I[Record Afterglow Time t_ag]
    I --> J[Measure Damaged Length L_d]
    J --> K[Flaming Droplets Observed or Not]
    H --> L{Acceptance Check}
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{L_d mean <= 150 mm}
    L --> N{t_af mean <= 5 s}
    L --> O{t_ag max <= 5 s}
    L --> P{Flaming Droplets = None}
    M --> Q{All Criteria Satisfied}
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[PASS: Material Approved]
    Q --> S[FAIL: Material Rejected]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of the Test Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Gas Supply Subsystem]
        P1A[Propane Cylinder] --> P1B[Pressure Regulator]
        P1B --> P1C[Flow Meter]
    end
    subgraph P2[Burner Assembly]
        P2A[Gas Inlet] --> P2B[Burner Nozzle]
        P2B --> P2C[40mm Calibrated Flame]
    end
    subgraph P3[Specimen Holding Subsystem]
        P3A[Vertical Steel Frame] --> P3B[Clamping Bars Top and Bottom]
        P3B --> P3C[Specimen 220mm x 1550mm]
    end
    subgraph P4[Measurement and Control]
        P4A[Stopwatch for t_af and t_ag] --> P4B[Ruler for L_d]
        P4B --> P4C[Visual Observer for Droplets]
    end
    P1C --> P2A
    P2C --> P3C
    P3C --> P4A
    P4A --> P4B
    P4B --> P4C
```

## 4.3 Pass/Fail Decision Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Input: Three Specimen Test Results] --> M1[Compute L_d mean]
    IN --> M2[Compute t_af mean]
    IN --> M3[Identify t_ag max]
    IN --> M4[Detect Flaming Droplets]
    M1 --> C1{L_d mean ≤ 150 mm}
    M2 --> C2{t_af mean ≤ 5 s}
    M3 --> C3{t_ag max ≤ 5 s}
    M4 --> C4{Droplets Absent}
    C1 --> AND1[AND Gate]
    C2 --> AND1
    C3 --> AND1
    C4 --> AND1
    AND1 --> OUT{Output Decision}
    OUT -->|True| PASS[PASS]
    OUT -->|False| FAIL[FAIL]
```

## 4.4 Vertical Sectional View (Textual Schematic)

```
        +--------------------------------------------------+
        |  Top clamp bar (fixed)                            |
        +==================================================+
        |                                                  |
        |   SPECIMEN EXPOSED FACE                          |
        |                                                  |
        |   (220 mm width × 1500 mm exposed length)        |
        |                                                  |
        |                                                  |
        |                                                  |
        |                                                  |
        +==================================================+
        |  Lower edge of specimen  ◄── damaged region max  |
        |                                = L_d (mm)        |
        +--------+                                         |
        | |||||  |  ◄── Propane burner (40 mm flame)        |
        | |||||  |                                         |
        | burner |  20 mm vertical gap                      |
        +--------+                                         |
        |  Support stand                                    |
        +--------------------------------------------------+
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**State the acceptance criteria that a vertically supported textile must satisfy to pass the IMO FTP Code Part 7 test. Mention any four criteria.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A textile is considered to have passed the test if **all** of the following criteria are met for the mean values of three specimens:

1. The **mean damaged length** must not exceed **150 mm** in any direction from the point of flame application.
2. The **mean afterflame time** must not exceed **5 seconds**.
3. The **maximum afterglow time** must not exceed **5 seconds** (for drapery-type applications).
4. **No flaming droplets** should be observed during or within 5 seconds after flame application.

(1 mark for damaged length criterion, 1 mark for afterflame, 1 mark for afterglow + droplets. Any four keywords fetch full marks.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Why is the flame application time fixed at exactly 5 seconds in the IMO FTP Code Part 7 test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 5-second flame application represents a **standardised, worst-case small-ignition scenario** mimicking a brief fire source such as a match, candle, or electrical arc that a textile may briefly encounter in a shipboard environment. It provides:

1. A **reproducible heat input** to the fabric — long enough to ignite the pyrolysis products but short enough to allow self-extinguishing materials to demonstrate their inherent resistance.
2. Sufficient time to differentiate **inherently flame-retardant fabrics** (which self-extinguish quickly) from **ordinary combustible fabrics** (which sustain combustion beyond the burner withdrawal).
3. A **practical engineering margin** — fabrics that pass this stringent test are deemed safe under realistic shipboard fire initiation conditions.

(1 mark each for any of the above points. Total 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **test apparatus, specimen preparation, and test procedure** prescribed by IMO FTP Code Part 7 for vertically supported textiles and films. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A fabric is tested in three specimens with the following data. Determine whether the material passes or fails the test, justifying each criterion with calculation. (7 marks)
>
> | Specimen | Damaged Length (mm) | Afterflame (s) | Afterglow (s) | Flaming Droplets |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | S1 | 90 | 1.5 | 2.0 | None |
> | S2 | 110 | 2.5 | 4.0 | None |
> | S3 | 130 | 1.0 | 3.0 | None |

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — Apparatus and Procedure (7 marks):**

**1. Specimen Preparation (2 marks):**
- Three specimens, each of dimensions **220 mm × 1550 mm**, are cut.
- For woven textiles, one specimen is cut in the **warp direction** and one in the **weft direction**.
- Specimens are conditioned for **24 hours** at a temperature of $23\,^{\circ}\text{C} \pm 2\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and relative humidity of $50\,\% \pm 5\,\%$.

**2. Apparatus (3 marks):**
- A **vertical stainless-steel frame** with clamping bars at the top and bottom holds the specimen so that an exposed area of $220\,\text{mm} \times 1500\,\text{mm}$ is presented.
- A **propane gas burner** is mounted on a movable stand below the specimen, capable of producing a flame of height **40 mm** with a yellow tip and blue base.
- The burner is positioned such that the flame tip impinges on the **lower face** of the specimen, **20 mm** below the lower edge.
- A **stopwatch** with 0.1-second resolution is used to time afterflame and afterglow.
- A **ruler or steel tape** is used to measure the damaged length to an accuracy of $\pm 1\,\text{mm}$.

**3. Test Procedure (2 marks):**
- The specimen is mounted vertically and the burner flame is applied to the lower face for **5 seconds**.
- The burner is smoothly withdrawn.
- The **afterflame time** is recorded.
- After flaming ceases, the **afterglow time** is recorded.
- The **damaged length** is measured as the maximum distance from the original lower edge to the farthest point of damage.
- The presence or absence of **flaming droplets** is observed.

*(For neat sketch, refer to Section 4.4 of these notes.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — Pass/Fail Determination (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mean Damaged Length (2 marks):**

$$
L_{d,\text{mean}} = \frac{90 + 110 + 130}{3} = \frac{330}{3} = 110\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{mean}} = 110\,\text{mm} \;\le\; 150\,\text{mm} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{PASS}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean Afterflame Time (2 marks):**

$$
t_{af,\text{mean}} = \frac{1.5 + 2.5 + 1.0}{3} = \frac{5.0}{3} = 1.67\,\text{s}
$$

$$
t_{af,\text{mean}} = 1.67\,\text{s} \;\le\; 5\,\text{s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{PASS}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Afterglow Time (1 mark):**

$$
t_{ag,\max} = \max(2.0,\, 4.0,\, 3.0) = 4.0\,\text{s} \;\le\; 5\,\text{s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{PASS}
$$

**Step 4 — Flaming Droplets (1 mark):**

$$
\text{No flaming droplets observed} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{PASS}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Decision (1 mark):**

All four criteria are satisfied. **The material PASSES the IMO FTP Code Part 7 test and is approved for use as a vertically supported textile in ship accommodations.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **physical principles** underlying flame spread on vertically oriented textile materials. Discuss the role of the **critical heat flux for ignition** and the **limiting oxygen index (LOI)** in determining the outcome of the Part 7 test. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A polyester curtain fabric has a density $\rho_s = 350\,\text{kg/m}^3$, specific heat $c_s = 1400\,\text{J/kg·K}$, thickness $\delta = 0.8\,\text{mm}$, and a measured damaged length of **180 mm** in a Part 7 test. Using the de Ris correlation, estimate the upward flame-spread velocity and comment on whether the material would pass the damaged-length criterion. Assume $T_f = 1300\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_{ig} = 380\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_s = 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, and $\lambda_g = 0.026\,\text{W/m·K}$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Physical Principles (7 marks):**

**1. Flame Spread on a Vertical Surface (2 marks):**
When a small flame is applied to the lower edge of a vertically oriented textile, heat is transferred upward from the flame to the unburnt fabric by three mechanisms: **conduction** through the fabric, **convection** by the rising hot plume, and **radiation** from the flame itself. The pyrolysis front advances upward as successive layers of fabric reach the **ignition temperature** $T_{ig}$.

**2. Critical Heat Flux for Ignition (2 marks):**
The **critical heat flux for ignition**, $\dot{q}''_{ig}$, is the minimum heat flux that must reach the unburnt fabric surface to sustain pyrolysis and ignition. If the local heat flux at the unburnt region falls below $\dot{q}''_{ig}$, the flame cannot self-propagate. Fabrics with high $\dot{q}''_{ig}$ (e.g., aramids, FR-treated cotton) easily pass the Part 7 test because the upward flame cannot sustain itself.

**3. Limiting Oxygen Index (2 marks):**
The **LOI** is the minimum oxygen concentration required to sustain combustion of a material under specified test conditions. Materials with $\text{LOI} > 28\,\%$ are considered self-extinguishing in air. Typical values: cotton $\approx 18\,\%$, wool $\approx 25\,\%$, modacrylic $\approx 30\,\%$, aramid $\approx 40\,\%$. In the Part 7 test, the ambient air contains $21\,\%\,\text{O}_2$; materials with $\text{LOI} > 21\,\%$ are inherently flame-retardant in this test and pass.

**4. Linking Principles to Test Outcome (1 mark):**
The Part 7 test outcome is governed by the **interplay** of $\dot{q}''_{ig}$, $T_{ig}$, LOI, fabric thickness, and density. A fabric with high LOI, high $T_{ig}$, and high $\dot{q}''_{ig}$ produces a small damaged length and passes.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — Flame-Spread Velocity Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $\rho_s = 350\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c_s = 1400\,\text{J/kg·K}$, $\delta = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $T_f = 1300\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 1573\,\text{K}$, $T_{ig} = 380\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 653\,\text{K}$
- $T_s = 25\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 298\,\text{K}$, $\lambda_g = 0.026\,\text{W/m·K}$
- $L_d = 180\,\text{mm}$, $t_a = 5\,\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Apply the de Ris Correlation (3 marks):**

$$
v_f = \frac{\lambda_g \, (T_f - T_{ig})}{\rho_s \, c_s \, \delta \, (T_{ig} - T_s)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.026 \times (1573 - 653) = 0.026 \times 920 = 23.92\,\text{W/m} \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator} &= 350 \times 1400 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \times (653 - 298) \\[4pt]
&= 350 \times 1400 \times 0.0008 \times 355 \\[4pt]
&= 139.16 \times 10^{3} \times 355 \\[4pt]
&= 4.94 \times 10^{4}\,\text{J/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
v_f = \frac{23.92}{4.94 \times 10^{4}} = 4.84 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m/s} = 0.484\,\text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Predicted Damaged Length in 5 s (2 marks):**

$$
L_{f,\text{pred}} = v_f \times t_a = 0.484 \times 5 = 2.42\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with Measured Value and Criterion (2 marks):**

The **measured** $L_d = 180\,\text{mm}$ vastly exceeds the **predicted** $L_{f,\text{pred}} = 2.42\,\text{mm}$ because the simple de Ris formula assumes idealised conditions and ignores the contribution of **flame radiation**, **fabric shrinking/melting**, and **chimney-effect convection** along the vertical surface.

$$
L_d = 180\,\text{mm} \;\;>\;\; 150\,\text{mm} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{FAIL}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The polyester curtain fabric **fails** the Part 7 damaged-length criterion and is **not approved** for shipboard vertical use.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in the Part 7 Question:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "mean" vs "maximum":** Students often quote the *maximum* damaged length against the 150 mm criterion instead of the *mean*. The criterion applies to the **mean of three specimens** — using the max is a 1–2 mark penalty.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the AND-rule:** A single failed criterion (e.g., afterglow) invalidates the entire result, even if the damaged length and afterflame pass. Always state the "ALL criteria must be met" rule explicitly.
>
> 3. **Mixing up units:** Writing $L_d = 0.18\,\text{m}$ instead of $180\,\text{mm}$ when comparing to the 150 mm criterion causes unnecessary unit-conversion errors. Keep everything in mm.
>
> 4. **Ignoring flaming-droplet observation:** Many students omit this criterion entirely. It is worth **1 mark** on its own.
>
> 5. **Wrong burner distance:** Writing "10 mm" or "30 mm" instead of the correct **20 mm** between burner and specimen — read the syllabus carefully.
>
> 6. **Using a single specimen instead of three:** The test mandates a minimum of **3 specimens**; values from a single specimen are not statistically valid for certification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Pin This in Your Notebook:**

- ✅ The test is governed by **IMO FTP Code Part 7** (2010), adopted under **SOLAS Reg. II-2/3.40**.
- ✅ Specimen size: **220 mm × 1550 mm**; minimum **3 specimens** per test.
- ✅ Conditioning: **23 °C ± 2 °C** and **50 % ± 5 % RH** for **24 hours**.
- ✅ Burner: **propane**, flame height **40 mm**, positioned **20 mm** below the lower edge.
- ✅ Flame application: **5 seconds**, then smooth withdrawal.
- ✅ Four pass criteria (all must be satisfied):
  1. $L_{d,\text{mean}} \le 150\,\text{mm}$
  2. $t_{af,\text{mean}} \le 5\,\text{s}$
  3. $t_{ag,\max} \le 5\,\text{s}$ (drapery applications)
  4. **No flaming droplets**
- ✅ The test simulates a **small-ignition-source** event on a **vertical** surface.
- ✅ **LOI > 28 %** ⇒ likely pass; **LOI < 21 %** ⇒ likely fail in air.
- ✅ Critical heat flux for ignition $\dot{q}''_{ig}$ governs self-sustained upward flame spread.
- ✅ de Ris correlation: $v_f = \dfrac{\lambda_g (T_f - T_{ig})}{\rho_s c_s \delta (T_{ig} - T_s)}$.
- ✅ The test applies to: **curtains, drapes, decorative textiles, suspended films, plastic wall-coverings** in ship accommodations.
- ✅ Failure leads to **non-approval** for shipboard use — vessel may be detained by Port State Control.
- ✅ Always state the **"ALL criteria must be met"** rule in your exam answer.
- ✅ Key formula for mean: $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ with $n=3$.
- ✅ Always check both the **mean** (for $L_d$ and $t_{af}$) and the **maximum** (for $t_{ag}$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Upholstered Furniture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Test for Upholstered Furniture # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Upholstered Furniture Fire Test (KTU Maritime Safety Definition):** > A standardised fire performance evaluation procedure prescribed under the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code, Part 8**, used to determine the **ignitability, flame spread, heat release, smoke production, and toxic gas emission** characteristics of composite seating assemblies (chairs, sofas, beds) installed aboard SOLAS-regulated vessels, offshore platforms, and passenger ships. The test simulates realistic ignition scenarios that a piece of furniture may encounter in a marine environment — such as a discarded cigarette, a match flame, or a burning wastebasket — and verifies that the furniture does **NOT** contribute to catastrophic fire spread on board. ### Reference Standards Governing the Test | Standard | Issuing Body | Scope | |----------|-------------|-------| | **IMO FTP Code Part 8** | International Maritime Organization | Mandatory test for shipboard upholstered furniture | | **Resolution A.652(16)** | IMO Assembly | Recommendation on fire test procedures for upholstered furniture | | **Resolution A.688(17)** | IMO Assembly | Fire test procedures for determining the suitability of components used in high-speed craft | | **SOLAS Chapter II-2** | IMO | Mandates fire safety requirements for all ships | | **ISO 9705** | International Organization for Standardization | Room-corner test (referenced for large-scale validation) | | **EN 1021-1 / EN 1021-2** | European Committee for Standardization | Cigarette and match equivalent tests (complementary) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Think of upholstered furniture aboard a ship as a "sleeping dragon"** 🐉 > > In its dormant state (normal use), a sofa or chair is harmless — comfortable, supportive, and quiet. However, the moment a flame or cigarette touches it, the **foam + fabric + air pocket sandwich** can transform into an inferno within 60–90 seconds, releasing toxic hydrogen cyanide (HCN) and carbon monoxide (CO) — the two leading killers in ship fires (e.g., the *Lusitania* philosophy still applies in modern maritime law). > > The **Test for Upholstered Furniture** is essentially a **"dragon-taming protocol"**: it forces the dragon to wake up under controlled laboratory conditions and measures how violently it breathes fire. Only those dragons whose breath remains within acceptable limits (no progressive smouldering, limited flame spread, controlled smoke) are permitted to board the vessel. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following **bold** constants and metrics are critical for KTU examination reference: - **Standard ignition source — Smouldering Cigarette:** Mass = **1.1 ± 0.1 g**, Length = **85 ± 5 mm**, Burning rate = **10–15 mg/min** (ISO 3308). - **Standard ignition source — Butane Flame (Match Equivalent):** Flame height = **35 ± 3 mm**, Flame temperature ≈ **1700–1900 °C**, Duration of application = **20 s**. - **Standard ignition source — Large Flame (Burning Wastebasket Simulation):** Heat output ≈ **3 kW**, Propane flow rate ≈ **13 g/min**, Duration = **50 s** (per IMO FTP Code Part 8). - **Test Duration:** **60 minutes minimum** observation after ignition source removal. - **Room Ventilation:** Test room volume ≥ **50 m³**, air change rate ≤ **0.5 per hour**. - **Acceptable CO concentration in test atmosphere:** ≤ **1450 ppm** (instantaneous peak). - **Acceptable smoke optical density (Ds, max):** ≤ **200** (specific optical density units). ## 1.4 Why This Test Matters in Maritime Safety Maritime vessels, particularly **passenger ships (cruise liners, Ro-Ro ferries)** and **offshore accommodation modules**, contain hundreds of upholstered seats, mattresses, and cabin furnishings. Historical disasters such as the *MV Scandinavian Star (1990)*, *MV Estonia (1994)*, and *Stena Line* incidents revealed that **upholstered furniture is one of the primary fire-load contributors** in maritime casualties. Therefore, KTU's Maritime Safety syllabus dedicates significant coverage to this topic under Module 2 (Fire Safety Engineering for Ships). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Test Architecture (Theoretical Framework) The IMO FTP Code Part 8 testing of upholstered furniture is structured in a **three-tier hierarchy**, applied in increasing order of severity: ### Tier 1 — Component-Level (Material) Tests - **Purpose:** Individually screen the **cover fabric** and the **filling material** (foam, padding). - **Ignition Source:** Smouldering cigarette + small butane flame. - **Outcome:** Determines whether individual components pass muster before assembly. ### Tier 2 — Composite (Small-Scale) Tests - **Purpose:** Evaluate **fabric + filling + interliner** as a stack. - **Ignition Source:** Small butane flame applied to the seating area. - **Outcome:** Determines whether the composite assembly is intrinsically safe. ### Tier 3 — Full-Scale (Mock-Up) Chair Test - **Purpose:** Real-world simulation of a fully assembled chair subjected to a **large ignition source**. - **Ignition Source:** **Burning wastebasket** equivalent — 3 kW propane flame. - **Outcome:** Determines the final suitability for shipboard installation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Tip:** > > When asked "List the tests for upholstered furniture," always mention **all three tiers** with their corresponding ignition sources. Examiners specifically test whether students understand the **hierarchical escalation logic** — this is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU ESE. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic ### Step 1 — Specimen Preparation - The chair (or mock-up) is conditioned at **23 ± 2 °C** and **50 ± 5 % relative humidity** for **24 hours** prior to testing. - Foam filling must be aged per **ISO 2357** (simulating 5-year service life compression). ### Step 2 — Test Room Setup - Test conducted in a **ventilated fire test room** with calibrated exhaust duct instrumentation. - Oxygen consumption calorimetry (based on **Pfeffer/Thornton principle**) measures Heat Release Rate (HRR). ### Step 3 — Ignition Source Application - **Cigarette test:** Cigarette is placed along the junction of seat and back, covered by a cotton wad indicator. - **Small flame test:** Butane flame applied vertically for **20 seconds** at the junction. - **Large flame test:** 3 kW burner applied for **50 seconds** at the centre of the seating area. ### Step 4 — Observation & Measurement - Record time to ignition (TTI). - Record time to flame spread beyond the ignition zone. - Record **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** via oxygen depletion. - Record **Smoke Production Rate (SPR)** via laser photometry. - Record **CO and CO₂ concentrations** via FTIR / NDIR analysers. ### Step 5 — Pass / Fail Determination - **Progressive smouldering failure criterion:** Smoulder reaches the extremities of the specimen within 60 minutes. - **Flaming failure criterion:** Sustained flaming beyond the ignition zone for > 10 seconds. - **Heat release failure criterion:** Peak HRR > **150 kW** (for mock-up test). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Heat Release Rate (HRR) — Oxygen Consumption Principle** | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{e} \cdot c_{p,e} \cdot (T_{e} - T_{i}) - \Delta h_{O_2}^{-1} \cdot \dot{V}_{O_2}$ | kW | Calculated from $\Delta O_2$ in exhaust duct | | 2 | **Simplified HRR (Huggett Constant)** | $\dot{Q} = \Delta h_{c,O_2} \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2} \approx 13.1 \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2}$ | kW | Where $13.1$ MJ/kg = **Huggett constant** | | 3 | **Mass Loss Rate** | $\dot{m} = \frac{m_{0} - m_{t}}{t}$ | kg/s | Direct gravimetric measurement | | 4 | **Effective Heat of Combustion** | $\Delta h_{c,eff} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}}$ | MJ/kg | Indicates combustion efficiency | | 5 | **Smoke Production Rate (SPR)** | $SPR = \dot{V}_{s} \cdot k_{s}$ | m²/s | Where $k_s$ = extinction coefficient | | 6 | **Specific Optical Density (Ds)** | $D_{s} = \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{0}}{I}\right)$ | dimensionless | NBS smoke chamber reference | | 7 | **Extinction Coefficient** | $k = -\frac{1}{L} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right)$ | m⁻¹ | Laser photometry at 632.8 nm | | 8 | **CO Yield** | $y_{CO} = \frac{\dot{m}_{CO}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}}$ | kg/kg | Toxicity assessment | | 9 | **Time to Ignition (TTI)** | Measured directly from test data | s | Critical safety metric | | 10 | **Critical Heat Flux for Ignition** | $q_{cr}'' = \sigma \cdot (T_{ig}^{4} - T_{s}^{4})$ | kW/m² | Piloted ignition threshold | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** > > The HRR (Heat Release Rate) is the **single most important parameter** in fire safety engineering. In maritime applications, the **Total Heat Released (THR)** over the first 600 seconds of a fire is used to size fixed fire-extinguishing systems (water mist, CO₂, FM-200) and to determine the **Required Safe Evacuation Time (RSET)** versus **Available Safe Egress Time (ASET)** in PASS (Passenger Ship Safety) calculations. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application In ship design, the test data feeds into: - **Fire Load Calculations:** $Q_{total} = \sum_{i} m_i \cdot \Delta h_{c,i}$ for ship compartments. - **Smoke Management System Design:** Duct sizing for pressurised stairways. - **Egress Modelling (IMO MSC/Circ.1238):** Determining evacuation routes in passenger vessels. - **Material Selection for SOLAS Chapter II-2 Compliance:** Mandatory restriction on combustible materials. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Release Rate from Oxygen Consumption The **Oxygen Consumption Calorimetry** principle, originally proposed by **Huggett (1980)**, states that the heat released per unit mass of oxygen consumed during combustion is approximately **constant at 13.1 MJ/kg** for a wide range of organic fuels. ### Step 1 — Start with the First Law of Thermodynamics for Combustion For a steady-flow combustion process: $$ \dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{e} \cdot h_{e} - \dot{m}_{i} \cdot h_{i} $$ Where: - $\dot{Q}$ = Heat release rate (kW) - $\dot{m}_{e}, \dot{m}_{i}$ = Mass flow rates of exhaust and intake (kg/s) - $h_{e}, h_{i}$ = Specific enthalpies (kJ/kg) ### Step 2 — Apply Mass Conservation $$ \dot{m}_{e} = \dot{m}_{i} + \Delta \dot{m}_{fuel} $$ For most fire tests, $\Delta \dot{m}_{fuel} \ll \dot{m}_{i}$, so $\dot{m}_{e} \approx \dot{m}_{i}$. ### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Oxygen Consumption The combustion efficiency can be related to oxygen depletion in the exhaust duct: $$ \dot{Q} = \frac{\dot{m}_{O_2}^{0} - \dot{m}_{O_2}^{e}}{0.21} \cdot \Delta h_{c,O_2} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply Huggett's Approximation For most hydrocarbon fuels burning in air: $$ \Delta h_{c,O_2} \approx 13.1 \text{ MJ/kg of } O_2 $$ This is the celebrated **Huggett Constant**. $$ \dot{Q} \approx 13.1 \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2,consumed} $$ ### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Problem (Solved):** > > In an upholstered furniture fire test, the exhaust duct records an oxygen concentration drop from **20.95 %** (ambient) to **19.85 %** by volume. The exhaust mass flow rate is measured as **2.5 kg/s**. Calculate the **Heat Release Rate (HRR)**. **Solution:** **Step A — Calculate the mass fraction of oxygen consumed:** $$ \Delta y_{O_2} = 0.2095 - 0.1985 = 0.0110 $$ **Step B — Convert to mass consumption rate of oxygen:** $$ \dot{m}_{O_2,consumed} = \dot{m}_{e} \cdot \Delta y_{O_2} = 2.5 \cdot 0.0110 = 0.0275 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step C — Apply Huggett's Constant:** $$ \dot{Q} = 13.1 \cdot 0.0275 = 0.36025 \text{ MJ/s} = 360.25 \text{ kW} $$ **Step D — Verify against pass criterion:** If the threshold is **150 kW** for a single chair mock-up, the chair has **FAILED** the test. ## 3.2 Derivation of Smoke Optical Density (Specific Optical Density) ### Step 1 — Beer-Lambert Law for Smoke Attenuation $$ I = I_{0} \cdot e^{-k \cdot L} $$ Where: -
