$I$ = Transmitted light intensity (W/m²) - $I_{0}$ = Incident light intensity (W/m²) - $k$ = Extinction coefficient (m⁻¹) - $L$ = Optical path length (m) ### Step 2 — Solve for Extinction Coefficient $$ k = -\frac{1}{L} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right) $$ ### Step 3 — Convert to Common Logarithm for NBS Chamber $$ D_{s} = \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{0}}{I}\right) = \frac{2.303 \cdot V}{A \cdot L} \cdot k $$ Where: - $V$ = Test chamber volume (m³) - $A$ = Exposed specimen area (m²) - $L$ = Light path length (typically 0.9144 m) ## 3.3 Derivation of Time to Ignition (TTI) Using Ignition Delay Theory ### Step 1 — Thermal Lag Equation (Quintiere's Approximation) For a thermally thick solid: $$ t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot k \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot \left(\frac{T_{ig} - T_{s}}{\dot{q}_{e}''}\right)^{2} $$ Where: - $t_{ig}$ = Time to piloted ignition (s) - $k$ = Thermal conductivity (W/m·K) - $\rho$ = Density (kg/m³) - $c$ = Specific heat (J/kg·K) - $T_{ig}$ = Ignition temperature (K) - $T_{s}$ = Surface temperature (K) - $\dot{q}_{e}''$ = External heat flux (W/m²) ### Step 2 — Rearranged Form (Commonly Tested in KTU) $$ \sqrt{t_{ig}} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi \cdot k \cdot \rho \cdot c}}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{T_{ig} - T_{s}}{\dot{q}_{e}''}\right) $$ This linear relationship between $\sqrt{t_{ig}}$ and $1/\dot{q}_{e}''$ is used to experimentally determine **ignition temperatures** of upholstery materials. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Upholstered Furniture Test — Procedural Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: Test Request] --> nodeB[Specimen Conditioning
23C, 50% RH, 24 h] nodeB --> nodeC{Tier 1 Selection} nodeC -->|Fabric Only| nodeD[Cigarette Ignition Test
ISO 8191-1] nodeC -->|Foam Only| nodeE[Small Flame Test
ISO 8191-2] nodeD --> nodeF{Component Passed?} nodeE --> nodeF nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Tier 2: Composite Stack Test] nodeF -->|No| nodeH[REJECT Material] nodeG --> nodeI[Small Butane Flame
20 s Application] nodeI --> nodeJ{Composite Passed?} nodeJ -->|Yes| nodeK[Tier 3: Full Chair Mock-Up] nodeJ -->|No| nodeH nodeK --> nodeL[3 kW Burner
50 s Application] nodeL --> nodeM[Measure HRR, SPR, CO, CO2] nodeM --> nodeN{HRR Peak less 150 kW?} nodeN -->|Yes| nodeO[PASS: Shipboard Approval] nodeN -->|No| nodeH nodeO[End: Document Certification] --> nodeP((End)) nodeH[End: Reject and Replace] --> nodeP ``` ## 4.2 Test Apparatus Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Chamber[Test Room Fire Test Chamber] nodeA1[Specimen Chair Mock-Up] nodeA2[Ignition Source
Cigarette / Butane / 3 kW Burner] nodeA3[Temperature Sensors
Type K Thermocouples] nodeA4[Cotton Wad Indicator] nodeA1 -.Adjacent to.-> nodeA4 nodeA2 --> nodeA1 end subgraph Duct[Exhaust Duct Instrumentation] nodeB1[Bidirectional Probe
ISO 3966] nodeB2[Gas Sampling Probe] nodeB3[Laser Photometer
632.8 nm He-Ne] nodeB4[FTIR Analyser
CO, CO2, HCN] nodeB5[Oxygen Paramagnetic Sensor] nodeB1 --> nodeB3 nodeB1 --> nodeB2 nodeB2 --> nodeB4 nodeB2 --> nodeB5 end subgraph DAQ[Data Acquisition and Control] nodeC1[Computer DAQ System] nodeC2[HRR Calculation Module] nodeC3[SPR Calculation Module] nodeC4[Toxicity Index Calculator] nodeC1 --> nodeC2 nodeC1 --> nodeC3 nodeC1 --> nodeC4 end Chamber --> Duct Duct --> DAQ ``` ## 4.3 Ignition Source Escalation Logic (Educational Schematic) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph LowEnergy[Low Energy Sources] le1[Cigarette
~ 5 W] le2[Match Flame
~ 50 W] end subgraph MidEnergy[Medium Energy Source] me1[Butane Flame
ISO 8191-2
~ 350 W] end subgraph HighEnergy[High Energy Source] he1[3 kW Propane Burner
Burning Wastebasket Sim] he2[10 kW Crib Fire
Optional Large-Scale] end LowEnergy --> MidEnergy MidEnergy --> HighEnergy ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** > > The schematic above illustrates the **"energy ladder"** philosophy of fire testing. Each rung represents a progressively harsher ignition challenge. Materials that pass low-energy tests may fail high-energy tests, which is why the IMO FTP Code requires **all three tiers** before shipboard certification is granted. This is conceptually analogous to crash-testing automobiles at increasing impact velocities. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2–3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Define the term "progressive smouldering" in the context of upholstered furniture fire testing. Why is it considered more dangerous than flaming combustion?** > **Model Answer:** > > **Progressive smouldering** is defined as a self-sustaining, non-flaming combustion process that propagates slowly through a porous combustible material (such as upholstery foam) after the ignition source is removed. It is characterised by a **propagating char front**, temperatures of approximately **400–600 °C**, and the release of large quantities of **carbon monoxide (CO)** and other toxic gases. > > It is considered more dangerous than flaming combustion because: > > 1. It is **invisible** — no visible flames, so passengers and crew remain unaware. > 2. It produces **higher CO yields** (toxicologically lethal at >1000 ppm within minutes). > 3. It can persist for **hours**, suddenly transitioning to flaming combustion (**flashover**) when oxygen levels fluctuate (e.g., when a door opens). > 4. It bypasses conventional **flame detectors** (which respond to optical signatures) and only triggers **smoke detectors** after significant time delay. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: List the three ignition sources used in the IMO FTP Code Part 8 testing of upholstered furniture. State the application duration for each.** > **Model Answer:** > > | Ignition Source | Application Duration | > |-----------------|----------------------| > | Smouldering cigarette (per ISO 3308) | Continuous — placed and left to burn out | > | Small butane flame (match equivalent, ~350 W) | **20 seconds** | > | Large flame — 3 kW propane burner (wastebasket simulation) | **50 seconds** | > > After each application, the specimen is observed for a minimum of **60 minutes** to verify the absence of progressive smouldering and sustained flaming. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common student errors that cost marks in this topic:** > > 1. ❌ Confusing **FTP Code Part 8** with **Part 5** (Surface flammability) or **Part 7** (Vertical flammability). Always specify "Part 8" explicitly. > 2. ❌ Stating the Huggett constant as **13.1 kJ/kg** instead of **13.1 MJ/kg** — a unit error worth 2 marks. > 3. ❌ Forgetting to mention the **60-minute observation period** post-ignition — frequently tested as a 2-mark sub-question. > 4. ❌ Confusing **smouldering cigarette test** with **large flame test** in numerical calculations. > 5. ❌ Failing to draw the **test apparatus schematic** when asked — this is a guaranteed 5-mark loss. --- ### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — **14 Marks** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Describe in detail the **three-tier test hierarchy** prescribed under IMO FTP Code Part 8 for upholstered furniture used aboard SOLAS-regulated vessels. For each tier, specify the **ignition source, specimen configuration, and pass/fail criterion**. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A mock-up chair is tested in the FTP Code Part 8 apparatus. The exhaust duct flow rate is **1.8 kg/s** and the oxygen concentration drops from **20.95 %** to **19.40 %**. Using Huggett's constant, calculate: - (i) The oxygen mass consumption rate. - (ii) The peak Heat Release Rate (HRR). - (iii) Whether the chair passes the IMO 150 kW acceptance criterion. > **Model Solution:** > > **Part (a) Solution:** > > **Tier 1 — Component Material Tests:** > - Specimen: Cover fabric and filling foam tested **individually**. > - Ignition Source: Smouldering cigarette (ISO 3308) and small butane flame. > - Pass Criterion: No progressive smouldering reaching extremities; no flaming beyond 50 mm from ignition point; smoulder should self-extinguish within **60 minutes**. > > **Tier 2 — Composite (Small-Scale) Stack Test:** > - Specimen: Cover fabric + filling foam + (optional) fire-retardant interliner, mounted on a **standard substrate**. > - Ignition Source: Small butane flame (~350 W) for **20 seconds**. > - Pass Criterion: No sustained flaming, no smouldering propagation, char damage limited to **< 100 mm** from point of application. > > **Tier 3 — Full Chair Mock-Up Test:** > - Specimen: A complete, production-ready chair. > - Ignition Source: 3 kW propane burner simulating burning wastebasket, applied for **50 seconds**. > - Pass Criterion: Peak HRR ≤ **150 kW**, total heat released ≤ **25 MJ**, no flaming beyond the chair's bounding envelope. > > **Part (b) Solution:** > > **Step 1 — Oxygen Concentration Drop:** > > $$\Delta y_{O_2} = 0.2095 - 0.1940 = 0.0155$$ > > **Step 2 — Oxygen Mass Consumption Rate:** > > $$\dot{m}_{O_2,consumed} = \dot{m}_{e} \cdot \Delta y_{O_2} = 1.8 \cdot 0.0155 = 0.0279 \text{ kg/s}$$ > > **Step 3 — Peak HRR (Huggett's Constant):** > > $$\dot{Q}_{peak} = 13.1 \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2,consumed} = 13.1 \cdot 0.0279 = 0.36549 \text{ MJ/s} = 365.5 \text{ kW}$$ > > **Step 4 — Compliance Check:** > > $$\dot{Q}_{peak} = 365.5 \text{ kW} \gg 150 \text{ kW threshold}$$ > > **∴ The chair FAILS the IMO FTP Code Part 8 acceptance criterion.** --- ### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks (Internal Choice)** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the **Oxygen Consumption Calorimetry Principle** used to measure the Heat Release Rate (HRR) in upholstered furniture fire tests. State Huggett's constant and its significance. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A marine upholstered chair with a **polyurethane foam** filling (density = **30 kg/m³**, thermal conductivity = **0.025 W/m·K**, specific heat = **1500 J/kg·K**) is exposed to a radiant heat flux of **25 kW/m²**. If the ignition temperature of the foam is **350 °C** and the initial surface temperature is **25 °C**, estimate the **time to piloted ignition (TTI)** using the thermal lag equation. > **Model Solution:** > > **Part (a) Solution:** > > **Oxygen Consumption Calorimetry Principle:** > > The principle, established by **Huggett (1980)**, states that the heat released during combustion of most organic fuels is approximately **13.1 MJ per kilogram of oxygen consumed**, regardless of fuel type (with ±5 % accuracy for hydrocarbons). > > Mathematically: > > $$\dot{Q} = 13.1 \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2,consumed}$$ > > **Significance:** > 1. Eliminates the need to measure all combustion products individually (CO, CO₂, soot, unburnt hydrocarbons). > 2. Provides **real-time HRR measurement** with high accuracy. > 3. Forms the basis of **cone calorimeter (ISO 5660)** and **room corner test (ISO 9705)** data. > 4. Enables calculation of fire growth rate, time to flashover, and required suppression agent quantities in ship design. > > **Part (b) Solution:** > > **Given Data:** > - $k = 0.025$ W/m·K > - $\rho = 30$ kg/m³ > - $c = 1500$ J/kg·K > - $T_{ig} = 350 + 273 = 623$ K > - $T_{s} = 25 + 273 = 298$ K > - $\dot{q}_{e}'' = 25{,}000$ W/m² > > **Step 1 — Apply Thermal Lag Equation:** > > $$t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot k \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot \left(\frac{T_{ig} - T_{s}}{\dot{q}_{e}''}\right)^{2}$$ > > **Step 2 — Substitute Values:** > > $$t_{ig} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.025 \cdot 30 \cdot 1500 \cdot \left(\frac{623 - 298}{25{,}000}\right)^{2}$$ > > **Step 3 — Simplify Numerator:** > > $$\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.025 \cdot 30 \cdot 1500 = \frac{3.1416}{4} \cdot 1125 = 0.7854 \cdot 1125 = 883.59$$ > > **Step 4 — Compute Bracket Term:** > > $$\frac{623 - 298}{25{,}000} = \frac{325}{25{,}000} = 0.013$$ > > $$(0.013)^{2} = 1.69 \cdot 10^{-4}$$ > > **Step 5 — Final Calculation:** > > $$t_{ig} = 883.59 \cdot 1.69 \cdot 10^{-4} = 0.1493 \text{ s}$$ > > **∴ Time to Piloted Ignition ≈ 0.15 seconds** (This indicates the foam is highly ignitable under the given flux — it would likely fail the test.) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):** - ✅ Upholstered furniture tests are governed by **IMO FTP Code Part 8** + **Resolution A.652(16)**. - ✅ **Three ignition sources:** Cigarette, Butane Flame (20 s), 3 kW Burner (50 s). - ✅ **Three-tier test hierarchy:** Component → Composite → Mock-Up. - ✅ **Observation period:** **60 minutes** after ignition source removal. - ✅ **Acceptance criteria:** Peak HRR ≤ 150 kW; no progressive smouldering; no flaming beyond envelope. - ✅ **Huggett's Constant:** **13.1 MJ/kg of O₂**. - ✅ **Key instruments:** Oxygen analyser (paramagnetic), Laser photometer (632.8 nm), FTIR (CO/HCN), Thermocouples (Type K). - ✅ **Specific Optical Density (Ds, max):** ≤ 200 in NBS chamber. - ✅ **Most toxic gases in upholstery fires:** **CO (carbon monoxide)** and **HCN (hydrogen cyanide)**. - ✅ **SOLAS Chapter II-2** mandates compliance with FTP Code Part 8 for shipboard installations. - ✅ **KTU Exam Favourite:** The Huggett constant numerical and the three-tier hierarchy are the two most repeated question patterns — memorise both. - ✅ **Pitfall:** Never confuse Part 8 (upholstered furniture) with Part 5 (surface flammability) — they are distinct.

topics.title: Test for Fire-Restricting Materials for High-Speed Craft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Fire-Restricting Materials for High-Speed Craft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire-Restricting Materials (FRM)** are defined under the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) High-Speed Craft Code (HSC Code)** as construction materials — including primary deck coverings, floor coverings, wall and ceiling linings, pipe and duct covers, insulation, cables, and upholstered furniture — that, when subjected to a standardized fire exposure, must limit the spread of flame, restrict heat release, minimize smoke generation, and reduce the emission of toxic gases. The performance of these materials is verified through the **Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code**, specifically through the procedures described in **IMO Resolutions MSC.40(64)** and **MSC.90(71)**, which provide the *Recommendation on Fire Test Procedures for Fire-Restricting Materials Used in High-Speed Craft*.

The HSC Code (Chapter 7 — Fire Safety) recognizes that high-speed craft (HSC) pose unique fire hazards compared to conventional ships because:
1. They carry a high passenger density.
2. They have short evacuation times available due to high speeds and minimal rescue craft capacity.
3. Their lightweight aluminum or composite hulls reduce the **structural fire endurance** of the vessel.

Because of these peculiarities, **conventional SOLAS fire tests are insufficient**, and dedicated, more stringent tests for **FRMs** are mandated.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Building Material Fire Auditor"**

Imagine you are the **fire safety auditor for a luxury double-decker express train** running at 200 km/h. You cannot allow a small kitchen fire in Carriage 3 to produce toxic fumes that incapacitate passengers in Carriage 5 within 60 seconds. So before any **plywood, foam insulation, cable sheath, or carpet** is installed inside the train, you demand a **fire report card** for that material.

This "report card" is exactly what the **Fire Test for FRMs** produces. Each material is placed inside a calibrated furnace or radiant heat panel, exposed to a controlled flame or heat flux, and graded on:

- **Spread of Flame** — does the fire race across the surface?
- **Heat Release Rate** — how much thermal energy does the material feed back to the fire?
- **Smoke Production** — does it create black choking smoke?
- **Toxic Gas Yield** — does it release CO, HCN, HCl, etc.?

A material that passes all four criteria receives an **FRM Approval Certificate**, allowing it to be installed inside a high-speed craft such as a **catamaran ferry, hydrofoil, or air-cushion vehicle (hovercraft)**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in FRM Classification

The following **standardized metrics** are central to FRM testing:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical Heat Flux | $CHF$ | $kW/m^2$ | ISO 5658-2 / IMO FTP Part 5 |
| Heat Release Rate | $\dot{Q}$ or $HRR$ | $kW/m^2$ | ISO 5660 / IMO MSC.40(64) |
| Total Heat Released | $THR$ | $MJ/m^2$ | ISO 5660 |
| Specific Extinction Area | $SEA$ | $m^2/kg$ | ISO 5660 |
| Smoke Production Rate | $SPR$ | $m^2/s$ | ISO 5659-2 |
| Toxicity Index | $i_t$ | dimensionless | IMO MSC.90(71) Annex 1 |
| Surface Flame Spread | $L_s$ | $mm$ | IMO FTP Code Part 5 |

These metrics are accepted internationally by **Classification Societies** such as **DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, ABS, and RINA**, which certify materials for HSC under the HSC Code.

---

## 1.4 Scope of Application in High-Speed Craft

> [!NOTE]
> The FRM test applies to the following categories of materials installed on HSC:
> - **Primary deck coverings** (corrugated steel or aluminium underlay).
> - **Floor coverings** (vinyl, carpet, rubber tiles).
> - **Wall and ceiling linings** (melamine, FRP panels, decorative veneers).
> - **Pipe and duct coverings** (mineral wool, elastomeric foam).
> - **Insulation materials** (PUR, PIR, phenolic foam, rockwool).
> - **Cables** (power, control, communication).
> - **Upholstered furniture** (seat foam, cover fabrics).
> - **Draperies and textiles** (curtains, blinds).

Each of these categories has a **dedicated test specimen preparation protocol** within **Resolution MSC.40(64)**, ensuring that the test results correspond to the *in-service* performance of the material.

---

## 1.5 Background — Why HSC Materials Need Special Testing?

| Aspect | Conventional Ship (SOLAS) | High-Speed Craft (HSC) |
|---|---|---|
| Evacuation time | 60 minutes | 17 minutes (per HSC Ch.7) |
| Hull material | Steel (high thermal mass) | Aluminium / composites (low thermal mass) |
| Passenger density | Lower | Higher (ferry-type) |
| Fire load | Distributed | Often concentrated near superstructure |
| Detection | Standard smoke/heat | Enhanced (due to fast transit) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because of the above differences, the **HSC Code permits non-combustible substitution only if FRM performance is demonstrated**, and for many lightweight components (e.g., ceiling panels), FRM is the *only acceptable alternative* to non-combustibility.

---

## 1.6 Governing Resolutions & Codes

The primary regulatory references for FRM testing are:

1. **Resolution MSC.40(64)** — *Recommendation on Fire Test Procedures for Fire-Restricting Materials used in High-Speed Craft* (adopted 9 December 1994).
2. **Resolution MSC.90(71)** — *Adoption of amendments to MSC.40(64)* (adopted 27 May 1999) — added toxicity criteria and refined smoke criteria.
3. **IMO FTP Code (Resolution MSC.61(67))** — The umbrella Fire Test Procedures Code, parts of which are referenced by MSC.40(64).
4. **ISO 1716:2018** — Determination of the gross heat of combustion.
5. **ISO 5660-1:2015** — Cone calorimeter method for HRR.
6. **ISO 5659-2:2017** — Smoke generation test in a single-chamber test.
7. **ISO 5658-2:2006** — Surface spread of flame test (lateral ignition and flame spread, LIFT).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Concept — The Four Pillars of FRM Performance

The HSC Code philosophy for FRMs is built on **four acceptance pillars**:

$$
\text{FRM Acceptance} \iff \underbrace{P_1}_{\text{Spread}} \;\wedge\; \underbrace{P_2}_{\text{Heat}} \;\wedge\; \underbrace{P_3}_{\text{Smoke}} \;\wedge\; \underbrace{P_4}_{\text{Toxicity}}
$$

Where each pillar $P_i$ must independently satisfy a numerical threshold defined in MSC.40(64) / MSC.90(71). A material failing **any one** pillar is rejected, irrespective of its performance on the others.

---

## 2.2 Pillar $P_1$ — Spread of Flame (Surface Flammability Test)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Standard:** ISO 5658-2 (Lateral Ignition and Flame Spread Test / LIFT)
> **Principle:** A vertically oriented specimen (155 mm × 800 mm) is exposed to a radiant heat panel producing a heat flux gradient from approximately **50 kW/m² at the hot end to 1 kW/m² at the cool end**.

### 2.2.1 Operational Steps

1. The specimen is mounted in a specimen holder.
2. A pilot flame is applied near the hot end.
3. The flame front progression along the specimen is recorded as a function of time.
4. The **critical heat flux at extinguishment ($CHF_{ext}$)** and the **flame spread distance ($L_s$)** are measured.
5. The **velocity of flame front** at any position $x$ along the specimen is computed as:

$$
v(x) \;=\; \frac{dx}{dt} \quad \text{[mm/s]}
$$

6. The **surface flammability index** is derived from integration of the velocity profile.

### 2.2.2 Acceptance Criterion (HSC Code)

For HSC applications, the **Surface Flammability Index** must satisfy:

$$
SFI \;\geq\; 15 \quad \text{(dimensionless, minimum)}
$$

Some authorities (e.g., US Coast Guard) accept:

$$
CHF_{ext} \;\geq\; 5.0 \;\; kW/m^2
$$

as an alternative acceptance threshold.

---

## 2.3 Pillar $P_2$ — Heat Release Rate (Cone Calorimeter Test)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Standard:** ISO 5660-1 (Cone Calorimeter)
> **Heat Flux:** Specimen is exposed to **50 kW/m²** (unless otherwise specified, e.g., 25 or 75 kW/m² for screening).
> **Specimen Size:** 100 mm × 100 mm × ≤ 50 mm thick.
> **Orientation:** Horizontal.

### 2.3.1 Governing Equations

The instantaneous Heat Release Rate per unit area is computed from the **oxygen consumption principle** (Thornton's principle), which states that for most organic fuels, approximately **13.1 MJ of heat is released per kg of oxygen consumed**:

$$
\dot{q}''(t) \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_O \cdot \Delta h_{c,O}}{A_s} \quad \text{[kW/m²]}
$$

Where:

$$
\dot{m}_O \;=\; 1.27 \cdot \dot{V}_{air} \cdot \rho_{O_2,0} \cdot \frac{X_{O_2,0} - X_{O_2}(t)}{1 + \phi(\alpha - 1)}
$$

The numerical form **Huggett's constant** simplifies the expression:

$$
\dot{q}''(t) \;=\; 13.1 \cdot 10^3 \cdot \dot{V}_{air} \cdot \rho_{O_2,0} \cdot \frac{X_{O_2,0} - X_{O_2}(t)}{A_s} \quad \text{[kW/m²]}
$$

The **Total Heat Released** over the duration of the test is:

$$
THR \;=\; \int_{0}^{t_{end}} \dot{q}''(t)\,dt \quad \text{[MJ/m²]}
$$

The **Average Heat Release Rate** for the first 180 s is:

$$
\dot{q}''_{avg,180} \;=\; \frac{1}{180}\int_{0}^{180} \dot{q}''(t)\,dt
$$

The **peak HRR** is:

$$
\dot{q}''_{peak} \;=\; \max_{t \in [0,\, t_{end}]}\big\{\dot{q}''(t)\big\}
$$

### 2.3.2 Acceptance Criterion (HSC Code / MSC.40(64))

| Parameter | Threshold |
|---|---|
| Peak HRR ($\dot{q}''_{peak}$) | ≤ **100 kW/m²** for most FRM applications |
| $\dot{q}''_{avg,180}$ | ≤ **25 kW/m²** |
| $THR$ (over test duration) | ≤ **10 MJ/m²** for floor coverings |

---

## 2.4 Pillar $P_3$ — Smoke Generation

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Standard:** ISO 5659-2 (Single-Chamber Smoke Density Test)
> **Heat Flux:** **25 kW/m²** in the presence of a pilot flame (flaming mode) and **50 kW/m²** without pilot (non-flaming/smoldering mode).
> **Specimen Size:** 75 mm × 75 mm × ≤ 25 mm thick.

### 2.4.1 Governing Equations

The **specific optical density** $D_s$ is:

$$
D_s \;=\; \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right)
$$

Where:

- $V$ = chamber volume (= 0.75 m³ for ISO 5659-2)
- $A$ = exposed specimen area
- $L$ = optical path length
- $\frac{I_0}{I_t}$ = ratio of incident to transmitted light intensity

The **Specific Extinction Area** (from ISO 5660) is:

$$
SEA \;=\; \frac{\dot{V}_{air} \cdot k(t)}{\dot{m}_{loss}(t)} \quad \text{[m²/kg]}
$$

Where $k(t)$ is the instantaneous extinction coefficient.

### 2.4.2 Acceptance Criterion (MSC.40(64))

| Smoke Parameter | Maximum Permitted Value |
|---|---|
| $D_{s,max}$ (flaming, 4 min) | **200** |
| $D_{s,max}$ (non-flaming, 4 min) | **400** |
| $SEA$ at 180 s | **1400 m²/kg** |

---

## 2.5 Pillar $P_4$ — Toxicity of Combustion Products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Standard:** ISO 5659-2 with chemical analysis (toxic gas FT-IR analysis), as specified in **MSC.90(71) Annex 1**.
> **Critical Gases Measured:** CO, CO₂, HCN, HCl, HBr, HF, SO₂, NOₓ, and acrolein.

### 2.5.1 Toxicity Index Formula

The **Toxicity Index** $i_t$ is a weighted sum of gas concentrations:

$$
i_t \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{c_i}{C_i,ref} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}
$$

Where:

- $c_i$ = measured concentration of gas $i$ in the chamber (ppm).
- $C_{i,ref}$ = reference concentration causing impairment (per IMO MSC.90(71)).

The reference values are listed in the table below.

| Gas | Reference Concentration $C_{i,ref}$ (ppm) | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| CO | 1450 | Impairment after 30 min |
| HCN | 140 | Lethal at short exposure |
| HCl | 320 | Sensory irritation |
| HBr | 240 | Sensory irritation |
| HF | 80 | Severe respiratory irritation |
| SO₂ | 120 | Bronchoconstriction |
| NO₂ | 70 | Pulmonary oedema |
| CO₂ | 70,000 | Asphyxiation |
| Acrolein | 30 | Severe irritation |

### 2.5.2 Acceptance Criterion

For an FRM:

$$
i_t \;\leq\; 1.0 \quad \text{(per IMO MSC.90(71))}
$$

A value $i_t > 1$ indicates that the combined toxic gas burden exceeds the impairment threshold for a 30-minute exposure.

---

## 2.6 The FRM Test Decision Tree (Process Flow)

A simplified decision flow is given below for clarity.

1. **Material submitted** for FRM certification.
2. **Specimen preparation** as per category (floor, wall, cable, etc.).
3. **Test 1 — Surface Flammability (ISO 5658-2):**
   - PASS if $SFI \ge 15$ → proceed to Test 2.
   - FAIL → rejected.
4. **Test 2 — Cone Calorimeter (ISO 5660-1):**
   - PASS if $\dot{q}''_{peak} \le 100$ kW/m² **and** $\dot{q}''_{avg,180} \le 25$ kW/m² → proceed to Test 3.
   - FAIL → rejected.
5. **Test 3 — Smoke Density (ISO 5659-2):**
   - PASS if $D_{s,max} \le 200$ (flaming) → proceed to Test 4.
   - FAIL → rejected.
6. **Test 4 — Toxicity (MSC.90(71)):**
   - PASS if $i_t \le 1.0$ → material certified as **FRM**.
   - FAIL → rejected.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of FRM Testing

| Domain | Utility |
|---|---|
| **Shipyards** | Material selection during HSC outfitting |
| **Classification Societies** | Issuance of FRM approval certificates |
| **Insurers (e.g., P&I Clubs)** | Risk modelling for HSC fire loss prevention |
| **National Maritime Authorities** | Compliance enforcement under HSC Code |
| **Material manufacturers** | R&D direction for flame-retardant formulations |
| **Coast Guard / EMS authorities** | Verification of HSC rescue-time fire envelopes |

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for FRM Test Calculations — must be memorised for Part B (14-mark) questions.**

$$
\dot{q}''(t) \;=\; 13.1 \cdot 10^3 \cdot \frac{\dot{V}_{air} \cdot \rho_{O_2,0} \cdot \big(X_{O_2,0} - X_{O_2}(t)\big)}{A_s}
$$

$$
THR \;=\; \int_{0}^{t_{end}} \dot{q}''(t)\,dt
$$

$$
\dot{q}''_{avg,180} \;=\; \frac{1}{180}\int_{0}^{180} \dot{q}''(t)\,dt
$$

$$
D_s \;=\; \frac{V}{A \cdot L}\cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right)
$$

$$
SEA \;=\; \frac{\dot{V}_{air}\cdot k(t)}{\dot{m}_{loss}(t)}
$$

$$
i_t \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{c_i}{C_{i,ref}}
$$

$$
\text{FRM Acceptance} \iff \big[SFI \ge 15\big] \wedge \big[\dot{q}''_{peak} \le 100\,kW/m^2\big] \wedge \big[D_{s,max}\le 200\big] \wedge \big[i_t \le 1.0\big]
$$

**Boundary Conditions / Constants:**

| Symbol | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Huggett's constant | 13.1 MJ/kg O₂ | Heat released per kg O₂ consumed |
| ISO 5659-2 chamber volume | 0.75 m³ | Smoke chamber |
| ISO 5660 specimen area | 0.01 m² | (100 × 100 mm) |
| ISO 5658 specimen length | 800 mm | LIFT test |
| Standard ambient $\rho_{O_2,0}$ | 0.262 kg/m³ | At 25 °C, 1 atm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Never confuse **average HRR** ($\dot{q}''_{avg,180}$) with **peak HRR** ($\dot{q}''_{peak}$). Both must independently satisfy the acceptance criteria. Examiners frequently award partial marks based on distinguishing the two.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example — Calculation of Total Heat Released (THR) from Cone Calorimeter Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A floor-covering FRM specimen (100 mm × 100 mm) is tested in the cone calorimeter at 50 kW/m² irradiance. The instantaneous HRR data recorded is as follows:

| Time $t$ (s) | $\dot{q}''(t)$ (kW/m²) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 30 | 75 |
| 60 | 130 |
| 90 | 110 |
| 120 | 80 |
| 150 | 50 |
| 180 | 20 |
| 210 | 0 (extinction) |

**Question:** Determine
(i) $\dot{q}''_{peak}$, (ii) $\dot{q}''_{avg,180}$, and (iii) $THR$ (use the trapezoidal rule over the given time grid).

---

### 3.1.1 Step (i) — Peak Heat Release Rate

From the table:

$$
\dot{q}''_{peak} \;=\; \max\{\dot{q}''(t)\} \;=\; 130\;\;kW/m^2 \;\;\text{at}\; t = 60\;s
$$

**Comparison with criterion:** $130\;\;kW/m^2 \;\;>\;\;100\;\;kW/m^2$ ⇒ **FAILS** the peak-HRR criterion under MSC.40(64). The material would be rejected at this pillar unless reformulated.

---

### 3.1.2 Step (ii) — Average HRR over 180 s

$$
\dot{q}''_{avg,180} \;=\; \frac{1}{180}\int_{0}^{180}\dot{q}''(t)\,dt
$$

We evaluate the integral using the **trapezoidal rule** with $\Delta t = 30$ s:

$$
\int_{0}^{180}\dot{q}''(t)\,dt \;\approx\; \sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\dot{q}''(t_{k-1}) + \dot{q}''(t_k)}{2}\cdot\Delta t
$$

$$
= \frac{30}{2}\big[(0+75) + (75+130) + (130+110) + (110+80) + (80+50) + (50+20)\big]
$$

$$
= 15 \cdot \big[75 + 205 + 240 + 190 + 130 + 70\big]
$$

$$
= 15 \cdot 910 \;\;=\;\; 13{,}650\;\;kJ/m^2
$$

Hence:

$$
\dot{q}''_{avg,180} \;=\; \frac{13{,}650}{180}\;\approx\; 75.83\;\;kW/m^2
$$

**Comparison with criterion:** $75.83\;\;kW/m^2 \;\;>\;\;25\;\;kW/m^2$ ⇒ **FAILS** the average-HRR criterion as well.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Some students confuse "average HRR over the test" (which can extend up to 1200 s) with the **regulatory 180-second average**. The HSC criterion explicitly uses $\dot{q}''_{avg,180}$. Always state the integration window.

---

### 3.1.3 Step (iii) — Total Heat Released (THR)

Integrating from $t = 0$ to $t = 210$ s using the trapezoidal rule:

$$
\int_{0}^{210}\dot{q}''(t)\,dt \;\approx\; \frac{30}{2}\big[(0+75)+(75+130)+(130+110)+(110+80)+(80+50)+(50+20)+(20+0)\big]
$$

$$
= 15\cdot\big[75 + 205 + 240 + 190 + 130 + 70 + 20\big]
$$

$$
= 15\cdot 930 \;\;=\;\; 13{,}950\;\;kJ/m^2 \;\;=\;\; 13.95\;\;MJ/m^2
$$

Hence:

$$
THR \;\approx\; 13.95\;\;MJ/m^2
$$

**Comparison with criterion:** $13.95\;\;MJ/m^2 \;\;>\;\;10\;\;MJ/m^2$ ⇒ **FAILS** THR criterion for floor coverings.

**Conclusion:** This floor-covering material is **NOT FRM-compliant**; it must be reformulated (e.g., add ATH filler, intumescent coating, or thicker fire-retardant substrate).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Toxicity Index Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** During ISO 5659-2 testing of a wall lining, the following gas concentrations were measured in the chamber at 4 minutes (flaming mode):

| Gas | $c_i$ (ppm) |
|---|---|
| CO | 580 |
| HCN | 12 |
| HCl | 65 |
| HF | 4 |
| SO₂ | 30 |
| NO₂ | 18 |

**Question:** Compute the Toxicity Index $i_t$ and state whether the material passes.

---

### 3.2.1 Solution

Applying:

$$
i_t \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{c_i}{C_{i,ref}}
$$

Substituting the reference concentrations from Section 2.5.1:

$$
i_t \;=\; \frac{580}{1450} + \frac{12}{140} + \frac{65}{320} + \frac{4}{80} + \frac{30}{120} + \frac{18}{70}
$$

Evaluating each term:

$$
\frac{580}{1450} \;\approx\; 0.400
$$

$$
\frac{12}{140} \;\approx\; 0.0857
$$

$$
\frac{65}{320} \;\approx\; 0.2031
$$

$$
\frac{4}{80} \;=\; 0.0500
$$

$$
\frac{30}{120} \;=\; 0.2500
$$

$$
\frac{18}{70} \;\approx\; 0.2571
$$

Summing:

$$
i_t \;\approx\; 0.400 + 0.0857 + 0.2031 + 0.0500 + 0.2500 + 0.2571 \;\approx\; 1.246
$$

**Comparison with criterion:** $i_t \approx 1.246 \;\;>\;\; 1.0$ ⇒ **FAILS** the toxicity pillar. The material would need reformulation — typically by reducing the chlorine content (e.g., switch PVC to PE) or using non-halogenated fire retardants.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Smoke Specific Optical Density

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A pipe-covering material (75 mm × 75 mm × 20 mm) is tested in the ISO 5659-2 chamber ($V = 0.75\;\;m^3$, $L = 0.914\;\;m$). The recorded light transmittance at 4 minutes is $I_t/I_0 = 0.015$.

**Question:** Determine the specific optical density $D_s$ and check compliance.

---

### 3.3.1 Solution

Exposed area: $A = 0.075 \times 0.075 = 0.005625\;\;m^2$

Applying:

$$
D_s \;=\; \frac{V}{A\cdot L}\cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right)
$$

$$
D_s \;=\; \frac{0.75}{0.005625 \times 0.914}\cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{0.015}\right)
$$

$$
D_s \;=\; \frac{0.75}{0.005141}\cdot \log_{10}(66.67)
$$

$$
D_s \;\approx\; 145.87 \cdot 1.8239 \;\approx\; 266.1
$$

**Comparison with criterion:** $D_s = 266.1 \;\;>\;\; 200$ ⇒ **FAILS** the smoke criterion for HSC FRM (flaming mode). The material may be acceptable only for non-public-space duct lagging.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Combined FRM Assessment Narrative

> [!NOTE]
> **Combined Problem (typical KTU 14-mark part (b)):**
> A cable specimen intended for installation in a passenger HSC has been tested. The summary of test results is given below. Decide whether the cable qualifies as **FRM** and justify.

| Test | Result |
|---|---|
| $SFI$ | 18 |
| $\dot{q}''_{peak}$ | 85 kW/m² |
| $\dot{q}''_{avg,180}$ | 18 kW/m² |
| $THR$ | 8.5 MJ/m² |
| $D_{s,max}$ (flaming) | 175 |
| $D_{s,max}$ (non-flaming) | 380 |
| $i_t$ | 0.85 |

### 3.4.1 Solution

Check each pillar against the acceptance criteria:

| Pillar | Result | Criterion | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spread ($SFI$) | 18 | $\ge 15$ | ✅ PASS |
| Heat ($\dot{q}''_{peak}$) | 85 kW/m² | $\le 100$ kW/m² | ✅ PASS |
| Heat ($\dot{q}''_{avg,180}$) | 18 kW/m² | $\le 25$ kW/m² | ✅ PASS |
| Heat ($THR$) | 8.5 MJ/m² | $\le 10$ MJ/m² | ✅ PASS |
| Smoke ($D_{s,flaming}$) | 175 | $\le 200$ | ✅ PASS |
| Smoke ($D_{s,non-flaming}$) | 380 | $\le 400$ | ✅ PASS |
| Toxicity ($i_t$) | 0.85 | $\le 1.0$ | ✅ PASS |

**Conclusion:** All four pillars are satisfied ⇒ **Cable qualifies as FRM**. The manufacturer may apply for an FRM approval certificate through the relevant classification society.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Why Huggett's Constant is 13.1 MJ/kg O₂

This derivation is commonly asked in KTU Part-B (7-mark) questions. From **stoichiometric combustion** of hydrocarbons:

$$
C_x H_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O
$$

For a **general organic fuel**, Huggett (1980) showed empirically that the **heat released per unit mass of oxygen consumed is approximately constant at ~13.1 MJ/kg** for most fuels, varying only within a narrow band of **12.5–13.7 MJ/kg O₂**.

For oxygen-limited fires, the expression becomes:

$$
\dot{q}''(t) \;=\; E\cdot \rho_{O_2,0}\cdot\dot{V}_{air}\cdot\frac{X_{O_2,0}-X_{O_2}(t)}{A_s}\cdot\frac{1}{1+\phi(\alpha-1)}
$$

where $E = 13.1 \times 10^3\;\;J/g$ and $\alpha = 1.10$ (expansion factor), $\phi$ is the oxygen depletion factor. For most engineering estimates the simplified version is sufficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **foundation of the cone calorimeter** method and is virtually always cited in KTU 14-mark answers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** Define **Fire-Restricting Material (FRM)** as applied in high-speed craft. State the regulatory resolution that governs its fire test procedure.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A Fire-Restricting Material (FRM) is any construction material used in a high-speed craft — including floor coverings, wall/ceiling linings, primary deck coverings, pipe and duct coverings, insulation, cables, and upholstered furniture — that, when tested in accordance with the prescribed procedures, satisfies the four acceptance criteria of:
>
> (i) surface flammability,
> (ii) heat release rate,
> (iii) smoke generation, and
> (iv) toxicity of combustion products.
>
> The test procedures are specified in **IMO Resolution MSC.40(64)** as amended by **Resolution MSC.90(71)**, which together constitute the **Recommendation on Fire Test Procedures for Fire-Restricting Materials used in High-Speed Craft** under Chapter 7 of the HSC Code.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming the four pillars, 1 mark for citing MSC.40(64) / MSC.90(71).)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** Why are conventional SOLAS fire tests insufficient for high-speed craft materials?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> SOLAS fire tests assume a steel-hulled ship with high thermal mass and a typical evacuation time of 60 minutes. In contrast, high-speed craft:
>
> 1. Have aluminium or composite hulls with **low thermal endurance** (typically <5 minutes in ISO 834 fire).
> 2. Provide only **17 minutes** for evacuation (HSC Code Ch.7).
> 3. Carry high passenger density.
> 4. Use lighter, often combustible, outfit materials to reduce displacement.
>
> Therefore a dedicated test regime is required that more strictly limits **heat release, smoke density, and toxic gas yield**, which is what MSC.40(64) provides.

*(1 mark each for any three valid differences.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question contains two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. The full 14 marks are awarded only when both sub-parts are answered with appropriate equations, computations, and conclusions.

---

### Question A — Option 1

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` Describe the four acceptance pillars for FRM testing as per MSC.40(64) / MSC.90(71), with numerical thresholds and the corresponding ISO standard for each.

**Model Answer:**

> The HSC Code requires that a material qualify as an FRM by satisfying four independent performance pillars:
>
> **Pillar 1 — Surface Flammability (Spread of Flame):**
> - Standard: **ISO 5658-2** (Lateral Ignition and Flame Spread Test / LIFT).
> - Specimen: 155 mm × 800 mm vertically mounted.
> - Heat flux gradient: 50 kW/m² at hot end to ~1 kW/m² at cool end.
> - Acceptance: **Surface Flammability Index (SFI) ≥ 15** or **CHF_ext ≥ 5.0 kW/m²**.
> *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Pillar 2 — Heat Release Rate:**
> - Standard: **ISO 5660-1** (Cone Calorimeter).
> - Specimen: 100 mm × 100 mm horizontal; flux = 50 kW/m².
> - Acceptance:
>   - **Peak HRR ≤ 100 kW/m²**
>   - **Average HRR (180 s) ≤ 25 kW/m²**
>   - **THR ≤ 10 MJ/m²** (for floor coverings)
> *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Pillar 3 — Smoke Generation:**
> - Standard: **ISO 5659-2** (Single-chamber smoke density).
> - Specimen: 75 mm × 75 mm; flaming (25 kW/m² + pilot) and non-flaming (50 kW/m²).
> - Acceptance: **D_s,max ≤ 200** (flaming) and **D_s,max ≤ 400** (non-flaming).
> *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Pillar 4 — Toxicity of Combustion Gases:**
> - Standard: **ISO 5659-2** with FTIR analysis as per **MSC.90(71) Annex 1**.
> - Gases measured: CO, HCN, HCl, HBr, HF, SO₂, NO₂, CO₂, acrolein.
> - Acceptance: **Toxicity Index i_t ≤ 1.0**.
> - Formula: $i_t = \sum c_i / C_{i,ref}$
> *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Conclusion:** A material is certified FRM only when all four pillars pass simultaneously.

---

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` During an ISO 5660-1 cone calorimeter test on a wall lining, the following instantaneous HRR data is recorded (irradiance = 50 kW/m², specimen area = 0.01 m²):

| t (s) | 0 | 30 | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\dot{q}''$ (kW/m²) | 0 | 55 | 95 | 90 | 65 | 40 | 15 |

**Calculate (i) Peak HRR, (ii) $\dot{q}''_{avg,180}$, and (iii) THR using the trapezoidal rule. State whether the material passes the heat pillar for HSC FRM certification.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Peak HRR:**
$$\dot{q}''_{peak} = \max\{\dot{q}''(t)\} = 95\;\;kW/m^2$$
Criterion: $\le 100\;\;kW/m^2$ → **PASS**.

**(ii) Average HRR (180 s):**
Using the trapezoidal rule with $\Delta t = 30$ s:
$$\int_{0}^{180}\dot{q}''\,dt \approx 15\cdot\big[(0+55)+(55+95)+(95+90)+(90+65)+(65+40)+(40+15)\big]$$
$$= 15\cdot\big[55+150+185+155+105+55\big] = 15\cdot 705 = 10{,}575\;\;kJ/m^2$$
$$\dot{q}''_{avg,180} = \frac{10{,}575}{180} \approx 58.75\;\;kW/m^2$$
Criterion: $\le 25\;\;kW/m^2$ → **FAIL**.

**(iii) THR (180 s, truncated test):**
$$THR \approx 10{,}575\;\;kJ/m^2 \approx 10.58\;\;MJ/m^2$$
Criterion: $\le 10\;\;MJ/m^2$ → **FAIL** (marginal).

**Conclusion:** The wall lining **fails the average-HRR and THR sub-criteria** of the heat pillar. The material is **NOT FRM-compliant** and must be reformulated (e.g., add fire-retardant fillers, increase thickness, apply intumescent coating). Partial marking: 2 marks for peak, 3 marks for average, 2 marks for THR and conclusion.

---

### Question B — Option 2

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` Explain with the aid of a flow diagram the **four-stage FRM test sequence** that a high-speed craft cable must pass through before receiving FRM approval.

**Model Answer:**

> A high-speed craft cable, prior to installation, must undergo a sequential four-stage FRM test per MSC.40(64):
>
> **Stage 1 — Surface Flammability Test (ISO 5658-2):**
> - Specimen preparation as per Annex 2 of MSC.40(64).
> - Radiant panel exposure; SFI computed.
> - Decision gate: SFI ≥ 15 → proceed; otherwise reject.
>
> **Stage 2 — Cone Calorimeter Test (ISO 5660-1):**
> - Specimen wrapped/positioned as per Annex.
> - Heat flux: 50 kW/m²; HRR vs time recorded.
> - Decision gate: $\dot{q}''_{peak} \le 100$ kW/m² AND $\dot{q}''_{avg,180} \le 25$ kW/m² → proceed.
>
> **Stage 3 — Smoke Density Test (ISO 5659-2):**
> - Flaming and non-flaming exposure.
> - Decision gate: $D_{s,max}\le 200$ (flaming) → proceed.
>
> **Stage 4 — Toxicity Test (MSC.90(71)):**
> - FTIR gas analysis at 4 min.
> - Decision gate: $i_t \le 1.0$ → certificate issued.
>
> All stages are sequential; failure at any stage terminates the certification.
>
> **Functional Flow Diagram (text-based):**
>
> ```
> Cable Sample → Stage 1 (SFI) → PASS? → Stage 2 (HRR) → PASS?
>             → Stage 3 (Smoke) → PASS? → Stage 4 (Toxicity) → PASS?
>             → FRM Certificate
>             (any FAIL → Reject)
> ```
>
> *(1 mark per stage description = 4 marks; 2 marks for diagram and decision logic; 1 mark for final certificate mention.)*

---

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` During an FRM toxicity test (ISO 5659-2 flaming mode), the following gas concentrations were measured in the chamber at the 4-minute mark:

| Gas | $c_i$ (ppm) |
|---|---|
| CO | 350 |
| HCN | 8 |
| HCl | 40 |
| SO₂ | 20 |
| NO₂ | 12 |
| HF | 2 |

**Compute the Toxicity Index $i_t$ and state the verdict for FRM approval.**

**Model Solution:**

Reference concentrations (MSC.90(71)): CO = 1450 ppm, HCN = 140 ppm, HCl = 320 ppm, SO₂ = 120 ppm, NO₂ = 70 ppm, HF = 80 ppm.

$$
i_t = \frac{350}{1450} + \frac{8}{140} + \frac{40}{320} + \frac{20}{120} + \frac{12}{70} + \frac{2}{80}
$$

Computing each term:

- CO: $350/1450 \approx 0.2414$
- HCN: $8/140 \approx 0.0571$
- HCl: $40/320 = 0.1250$
- SO₂: $20/120 \approx 0.1667$
- NO₂: $12/70 \approx 0.1714$
- HF: $2/80 = 0.0250$

Sum:

$$
i_t \approx 0.2414 + 0.0571 + 0.1250 + 0.1667 + 0.1714 + 0.0250 \approx 0.787
$$

**Verdict:** $i_t \approx 0.787 \;\le\; 1.0$ → **PASS**.

**Conclusion:** The material passes the toxicity pillar. Combined with assumed passes on the other three pillars, the material would qualify as FRM.

*(1 mark for reference list, 3 marks for computation, 1 mark for verdict, 2 marks for integration with overall FRM conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in FRM Test Questions (Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Confusing HRR with THR.** Students often write "THR = 100 kW/m²" instead of MJ/m². Watch the units.
> 2. **Omitting the integration window.** The criterion is *180-second average*, not "the entire test". Always state $\dot{q}''_{avg,180}$.
> 3. **Mixing up flaming vs non-flaming smoke thresholds.** $D_{s,max} \le 200$ applies to flaming mode; non-flaming has a higher threshold of 400.
> 4. **Forgetting reference concentrations in toxicity index.** Without the denominator values, the ratio cannot be computed. Memorise the reference values.
> 5. **Treating "FRM" and "non-combustible" as synonymous.** FRM is permitted *only* under HSC Code for specific applications; non-combustibility is the SOLAS default.
> 6. **Citing wrong resolution.** Some students quote "SOLAS Chapter II-2" instead of **MSC.40(64) / MSC.90(71)**. Examiners deduct for this.
> 7. **Skipping decision logic in long answers.** Part (a) of Question B requires an explicit PASS/FAIL gate after each stage; omitting it costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Test for Fire-Restricting Materials for High-Speed Craft**

- **Regulatory framework:** IMO HSC Code Chapter 7; Resolutions **MSC.40(64)** and **MSC.90(71)**.
- **FRM definition:** Material that limits flame spread, heat release, smoke, and toxicity — *not necessarily non-combustible*.
- **Four acceptance pillars:**
  1. **Surface Flammability (SFI ≥ 15)** — ISO 5658-2 / LIFT test.
  2. **Heat Release** — ISO 5660-1 cone calorimeter:
     - $\dot{q}''_{peak} \le 100\;\;kW/m^2$
     - $\dot{q}''_{avg,180} \le 25\;\;kW/m^2$
     - $THR \le 10\;\;MJ/m^2$
  3. **Smoke Density** — ISO 5659-2:
     - $D_{s,max}\le 200$ (flaming)
     - $D_{s,max}\le 400$ (non-flaming)
  4. **Toxicity** — MSC.90(71) Annex 1:
     - $i_t = \sum c_i / C_{i,ref} \le 1.0$
- **Huggett's constant:** 13.1 MJ/kg O₂ (memorise!).
- **Cone calorimeter area:** 0.01 m²; smoke chamber volume: 0.75 m³.
- **Common toxic gases and their $C_{i,ref}$:**
  - CO = 1450 ppm; HCN = 140 ppm; HCl = 320 ppm; HF = 80 ppm.
  - SO₂ = 120 ppm; NO₂ = 70 ppm; HBr = 240 ppm; CO₂ = 70,000 ppm.
- **Why HSC and not SOLAS?** Hull thermal mass + 17-minute evacuation window + passenger density.
- **Applies to:** primary deck coverings, floor coverings, wall/ceiling linings, pipe/duct insulation, cables, upholstered furniture, draperies.
- **Decision logic is sequential** — failure at any pillar rejects the material outright.
- **Certifying authority:** Classification societies (DNV, LR, BV, ABS, RINA) on behalf of the flag administration.
- **Re-certification cycle:** Typically every 5 years or upon formulation change.
- **Difference between FRM and fire-retardant treated wood (FRTW):** FRTW is a single-category treatment for wood; FRM is a multi-pillar performance certification applicable to a wide range of materials including polymers, composites, foams, and textiles.
- **Connection to FTP Code:** MSC.40(64) refers to certain test parts of the FTP Code (e.g., Part 5 for surface flammability) and complements others — they are not interchangeable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Fire-Resisting Divisions of High-Speed Craft
Navigation and Collision Avoidance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation and Collision Avoidance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Navigation** is the science and art of determining the position of a ship and directing its course from one position to another safely, efficiently, and in compliance with international maritime regulations.

**Collision Avoidance** is the systematic process of detecting, assessing, and executing maneuvers to prevent physical contact between vessels or between a vessel and a fixed/moving obstruction, governed primarily by the **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs), 1972**, adopted by the International Maritime Organization (IMO).

The subject forms the operational backbone of the **Bridge Resource Management (BRM)** philosophy, integrating human factors, sensor data, and statutory rule-based decision logic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Highway Driver:**
> Imagine driving a car on a busy two-way highway. Your **GPS** is your position sensor, your **speedometer** gives speed, your **horn and indicators** are your signals, and the **Highway Code (Right of Way Rules)** is your COLREGs. Navigation tells you *where* you are and *where to go*; collision avoidance tells you *how* to act when another car (ship) approaches from head-on, overtakes you, or crosses your path. If you ignore the rules, accidents (collisions, groundings) occur — and at sea, there are no "brake" lanes; ships take kilometers to stop.

The maritime environment differs from road traffic in three critical ways:
1. **Stopping Distance:** A fully loaded VLCC at service speed requires nearly **15–20 nautical miles** to stop (including engine slowdown and drag).
2. **Reaction Time:** A ship's turning circle is measured in nautical miles, not meters.
3. **Right of Way:** The sea has no "lanes" — vessels approach from any direction, and the rules must be unambiguous.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Modern Maritime Navigation

| Pillar | Description | Key Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Position Fixing** | Determining where the ship is on Earth's surface | GPS, DGPS, Loran-C, Celestial, Terrestrial Bearings |
| **Course & Track Monitoring** | Ensuring the ship follows the intended route | Gyrocompass, Magnetic Compass, ECDIS, Paper Charts |
| **Collision Avoidance** | Detecting and resolving traffic conflicts | RADAR, ARPA, AIS, Visual Lookout, COLREGs |

## 1.4 Core Metrics & Standards (KTU 2024 Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key Constants and Standards:
> - **1 Nautical Mile (NM)** = **1,852 metres** = **1 minute of latitude**
> - **1 Knot (kt)** = **1 NM/hour** = **0.5144 m/s**
> - **Standard Rate Turn** = **3° per second** (used in compass error calculation)
> - **Speed of Sound in Air** = **340 m/s** (used in fog signal timing)
> - **Horizontal Datum** = **WGS-84** (standard for GPS coordinates)
> - **SOLAS Chapter V** mandates carriage requirements for navigational equipment
> - **STCW Convention** (as amended) defines watchkeeping competence standards

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Methods of Navigation — Hierarchical Classification

### A. Terrestrial (Piloting) Navigation
Used within sight of land or in coastal waters.
- **Visual Bearings:** Compass bearing taken on a charted landmark (lighthouse, chimney, mountain peak). Requires correction for **compass error (Δ)** and **variation (Var)** and **deviation (Dev)**.
- **Horizontal Sextant Angles:** Angle measured between two charted objects using a sextant — yields a circle of position (the **cocked hat** method).
- **Depth Contour Navigation:** Using echo sounder readings to verify position against charted depths.

### B. Celestial Navigation
- Uses the observed altitude (**Ho**) of a celestial body (Sun, Moon, planets, stars) and compares it to the **computed altitude (Hc)**.
- Position line is a circle of equal altitude around the **Geographical Position (GP)** of the body.
- Essential for backup and offshore voyaging.

### C. Electronic Navigation
- **GPS (Global Positioning System):** Satellite-based, accuracy ~10 m standalone, **3–5 m** with **DGPS** (Differential GPS).
- **Radar Navigation:** Range and bearing of radar-visible targets and charted objects.
- **AIS (Automatic Identification System):** VHF-based transponder exchanging ship data (MMSI, position, course, speed) every 2–10 seconds.
- **LORAN-C (Long Range Navigation):** Terrestrial hyperbolic radio system (now largely decommissioned but historically important).
- **ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display and Information System):** The IMO-mandated digital chart system for SOLAS-regulated vessels.

### D. Inertial Navigation
- Uses **gyroscopes** and **accelerometers** to compute position. Self-contained but drifts with time.

## 2.2 The COLREGs Framework — Rule-Based Collision Avoidance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs 1972)** is divided into **38 Rules** organized in five parts:
> - **Part A** — General (Rules 1–3)
> - **Part B** — Steering and Sailing (Rules 4–19) — **THE CORE OF COLLISION AVOIDANCE**
> - **Part C** — Lights and Shapes (Rules 20–31)
> - **Part D** — Sound and Light Signals (Rules 32–37)
> - **Part E** — Exemptions (Rule 38)

### The Six Fundamental Encounter Scenarios (Rule-Based Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Logic for Identifying the Encounter Type:**

1. **Step 1 — Assess Bearing:** Determine the compass bearing of the approaching vessel (the "target").
2. **Step 2 — Measure CPA & TCPA:** Using radar/ARPA, calculate **Closest Point of Approach (CPA)** and **Time to CPA (TCPA)**.
3. **Step 3 — Classify by Relative Bearing:**
   - **Forward 0°–22.5° abaft the beam (i.e., nearly ahead):** Could be **HEAD-ON** or **OVERTAKING**.
   - **More than 22.5° abaft the beam:** **OVERTAKING situation** (Rule 13).
   - **Forward of the beam (other than above):** **CROSSING situation** (Rule 15).
   - **Exactly ahead (within ~6° of reciprocal):** **HEAD-ON situation** (Rule 14).
4. **Step 4 — Apply Rule Hierarchy.**

## 2.3 The Three Master Rules (Part B — Steering & Sailing)

| Rule | Scenario | Give-Way Action | Stand-On (Privileged) Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule 13 — Overtaking** | Approaching vessel comes from >22.5° abaft your beam | Overtaking vessel keeps clear | Overtaken vessel maintains course & speed |
| **Rule 14 — Head-On** | Vessels meeting nearly reciprocal (within 6°) | BOTH alter course **STARBOARD** | None — symmetric |
| **Rule 15 — Crossing** | Vessels on intersecting courses, risk of collision | Vessel which has the other on her **STARBOARD** keeps clear | Maintain course & speed |

### The "Privileged Vessel" Defensive Doctrine
- **Rule 17 — Stand-on vessel's action:** If the give-way vessel fails to act, the stand-on vessel **MAY** (but is not obliged initially) alter course to avoid collision. The decisive phrase in the rule is: *"may take such action as will best aid to avoid collision."*
- **Rule 18 — Responsibility between vessels:** Defines hierarchy between **power-driven, sailing, fishing, restricted ability, and vessels not under command (NUC).**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Distance = Speed × Time | $D = S \times T$ | NM, kt, hr | Voyage planning |
| 2 | Conversion: Speed to m/s | $V_{m/s} = V_{kt} \times 0.5144$ | m/s | Physics problems |
| 3 | True Bearing from Compass | $T = C + \Delta$ where $\Delta = Var + Dev$ | degrees | Compass correction |
| 4 | Gyro Error = $1^\circ \times$ Time Difference | $\Delta G = (T_{gyro} - T_{true}) \times 1°/\text{hr}$ | degrees | Watch-rate drift |
| 5 | Speed of Sound (Fog signal horizon) | $D_{\text{horizon}} \approx 2.08 \sqrt{h}$ (nautical miles, h in metres) | NM, m | Fog detection range |
| 6 | Radar Range (line of sight) | $D_{radar} \approx 2.2 \sqrt{h_1} + 2.2 \sqrt{h_2}$ | NM, m | Detection range |
| 7 | Rate of Change of Bearing (RCB) | $RCB = \frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}$ | deg/min | Determining aspect |
| 8 | Closest Point of Approach (CPA) | $CPA = \frac{R \sin(\alpha - \beta)}{\sin \alpha}$ | NM | ARPA derived |
| 9 | Time to CPA (TCPA) | $TCPA = \frac{R \cos(\alpha - \beta)}{V_{rel}}$ | minutes | ARPA derived |
| 10 | Conversion Factor | $1 \text{ NM} = 1852 \text{ m}$ | m | Standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Compasses:** "Compass" in a formula means compass bearing. The equation **T (True) = C (Compass) + Δ (Total Error)** is the most-tested formula in KTU exams on navigation. The mnemonic **"Compasses add (Can Dead) — TV-DVD"** may help: $True = Variation + Deviation$ relates to $Compass$.

## 2.5 Lights and Shapes (Rule 20–31) — Quick Reference

| Vessel Type | Forward Lights | Side Lights | Stern Light | Special Day Shapes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power-driven ≥50 m** | 2 masthead (fore higher) | Port (red) + Starboard (green) | White (visible 135°) | None for normal |
| **Vessel at Anchor** | — | — | — | **Black ball** forward |
| **Vessel NUC** | 2 red all-round | — | — | 2 black balls vertical |
| **Vessel RAM** | 3 all-round (red-white-red) | — | — | 3 shapes (ball-diamond-ball) |
| **Fishing (Trawling)** | — | Red over white (stern) | — | 2 cones apex together |
| **Pilot Vessel (working)** | White over red | — | — | — |

## 2.6 Sound Signals — Summary (Rule 34, 35)

> [!NOTE]
> - **One short blast:** I am altering my course to **STARBOARD**.
> - **Two short blasts:** I am altering my course to **PORT**.
> - **Three short blasts:** I am operating **ASTERN** propulsion.
> - **Five short blasts:** **DOUBT** about your intentions (DANGER signal).
> - **One prolonged blast:** Power-driven vessel in **restricted visibility** making way.
> - **Two prolonged blasts:** Power-driven vessel in **restricted visibility** stopped.
> - **One long + two short:** Pilot vessel underway.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These regulations and navigation techniques are not academic exercises. They are **legally binding under international law (UNCLOS)** and have been the basis of maritime court judgments. Masters and officers failing to comply with COLREGs face:
> - Criminal liability under the **Merchant Shipping Act** of the flag State
> - Loss of STCW certificate (career-ending)
> - Civil liability in tort for **negligent navigation**
> - Detention by Port State Control

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: True Bearing from Compass Bearing

**Problem Statement:** A vessel steams on a compass heading of **085°C**. The magnetic variation in the area is **7°W** and the deviation for that heading is **+2°**. Find the **True Course Over Ground (COG)** and the **True Bearing** of a lighthouse observed on the **compass bearing 045°C**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Quantities
- Compass Course (CC) = **085°C**
- Variation (Var) = **7°W** (i.e., **−7°** in algebraic convention)
- Deviation (Dev) = **+2°**
- Compass Bearing (CB) of lighthouse = **045°C**

### Step 2 — Recall the Fundamental Equation
The true value is obtained from the compass value by adding the **Total Error (Δ)**, where:
$$\Delta = Var + Dev$$

> A blank line must exist between every display equation and surrounding text.

### Step 3 — Compute the Total Error
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= Var + Dev \\
&= (-7°) + (+2°) \\
&= -5°
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign means the compass is reading **5° less** than the true value.

### Step 4 — Compute the True Course
$$
\begin{aligned}
True\ Course\ (TC) &= Compass\ Course\ (CC) + \Delta \\
TC &= 085° + (-5°) \\
TC &= 080°\ (T)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the True Bearing of the Lighthouse
$$
\begin{aligned}
True\ Bearing\ (TB) &= Compass\ Bearing\ (CB) + \Delta \\
TB &= 045° + (-5°) \\
TB &= 040°\ (T)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Practical Interpretation
The vessel is steering **080° True**, and the lighthouse lies at **040° True** from the vessel — that is, **40° to port of the bow**. If the navigator plots this on the chart, the lighthouse should appear **forward of the beam on the port side**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU examiners specifically test whether you apply variation and deviation to the **compass** value, not to a "true" value. Mixing up the sign convention is the **#1 reason for losing marks** in this type of problem.

## 3.2 Derivation: Radar Detection Range and Time-to-Contact

**Problem Statement:** The bridge of a ship is at a height of **24 m** above sea level. A target vessel's masthead is at a height of **36 m**. The radar antenna is at a height of **28 m**. Compute the maximum theoretical radar detection range and the time available for the OOW to react at 18 knots.

### Step 1 — Identify Heights
- $h_1$ (Radar antenna height) = **28 m**
- $h_2$ (Target masthead height) = **36 m**

### Step 2 — Apply the Radar Horizon Formula
The standard empirical formula (for atmospheric refraction):
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{radar} &= 2.2 \sqrt{h_1} + 2.2 \sqrt{h_2} \\
&= 2.2 \sqrt{28} + 2.2 \sqrt{36} \\
&= 2.2 \times 5.292 + 2.2 \times 6.000 \\
&= 11.642 + 13.200 \\
&= 24.84\ \text{NM}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Speed in NM/min
$$
\begin{aligned}
V\ (NM/min) &= \frac{18\ \text{kt}}{60} \\
&= 0.300\ \text{NM/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Time to Detection
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{detect} &= \frac{D_{radar}}{V} \\
&= \frac{24.84\ \text{NM}}{18\ \text{kt}} \\
&= 1.38\ \text{hours} \\
&\approx 82.8\ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This calculation shows why **early radar plotting** is critical. Even with optimal detection at 24.84 NM, a 2-MT vessel may need **6–8 minutes** to complete a major course alteration, and the **OOW reaction time** is typically **30–60 seconds**. The remaining time must allow for **CPA/TCPA computation, rule application, helm order, and engine telegraph response**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Time to Closest Point of Approach (TCPA)

**Problem Statement:** Target vessel is on a bearing of **045° (T)** at a range of **12 NM**. Your ship is steering **090° (T) at 15 kt**. The target is steering **180° (T) at 12 kt**. Compute the **TCPA** and **CPA**.

### Step 1 — Resolve Own Ship and Target Velocities

**Own Ship (OS):**
- Course: 090° T, Speed: 15 kt
- $V_{OS,x} = 15 \sin(090°) = 15.00\ \text{kt}$
- $V_{OS,y} = 15 \cos(090°) = 0.00\ \text{kt}$

**Target (TGT):**
- Course: 180° T, Speed: 12 kt
- $V_{TGT,x} = 12 \sin(180°) = 0.00\ \text{kt}$
- $V_{TGT,y} = 12 \cos(180°) = -12.00\ \text{kt}$

### Step 2 — Compute Relative Velocity
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rel,x} &= V_{TGT,x} - V_{OS,x} = 0 - 15 = -15\ \text{kt} \\
V_{rel,y} &= V_{TGT,y} - V_{OS,y} = -12 - 0 = -12\ \text{kt}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Magnitude and Direction
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rel} &= \sqrt{(-15)^2 + (-12)^2} = \sqrt{225 + 144} = \sqrt{369} = 19.21\ \text{kt} \\
\theta_{rel} &= \arctan\left(\frac{-15}{-12}\right) = \arctan(1.25) = 51.34° \\
\text{Bearing of relative motion} &= 180° + 51.34° = 231.34°\ (\text{T})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Range Vector Components
Initial range: 12 NM on bearing 045°.
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_x &= 12 \sin(45°) = 8.485\ \text{NM} \\
R_y &= 12 \cos(45°) = 8.485\ \text{NM}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute CPA
The **CPA** is the perpendicular distance from the OS to the relative motion line:
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPA &= \frac{\vert R_x \cdot V_{rel,y} - R_y \cdot V_{rel,x} \vert}{V_{rel}} \\
&= \frac{\vert 8.485 \times (-12) - 8.485 \times (-15) \vert}{19.21} \\
&= \frac{\vert -101.82 + 127.28 \vert}{19.21} \\
&= \frac{25.46}{19.21} \\
&= 1.325\ \text{NM}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute TCPA
$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPA &= \frac{R_x \cdot V_{rel,x} + R_y \cdot V_{rel,y}}{V_{rel}^2} \\
&= \frac{(8.485 \times -15) + (8.485 \times -12)}{369} \\
&= \frac{-127.28 - 101.82}{369} \\
&= \frac{-229.10}{369} \\
&= -0.621\ \text{hours} \\
&\approx -37.2\ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative TCPA** indicates the target has already passed its closest point of approach and is **moving away** from the OS. This is a classic "**receding target**" scenario where the OOW must still monitor but no collision avoidance maneuver is required.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Encounter Classification Decision Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU Module 3 questions. Memorize the rule citations.

| Relative Bearing of Target | Closing Range? | Aspect | Applicable Rule | Required Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **~000° (dead ahead, ±6°)** | Yes | Head-on | **Rule 14** | Both alter to **STARBOARD** |
| **Ahead, 0°–22.5° off bow** | Yes | Crossing (Target on STBD) | **Rule 15** | Target keeps clear (give-way) |
| **22.5°–90° (forward of beam)** | Yes | Crossing | **Rule 15** | Vessel with target on STBD = give-way |
| **Abeam to 22.5° abaft** | Increasing | Crossing (passing clear) | **Rule 15** | Maintain course & speed |
| **More than 22.5° abaft beam** | Yes (coming from astern) | **Overtaking** | **Rule 13** | Overtaker = give-way (keep clear) |
| **Any bearing — restricted visibility** | Yes | N/A | **Rule 19** | Reduce to **safe speed**, navigate with caution, post lookout |
| **Any bearing — vessel NUC, RAM, Constrained** | Yes | N/A | **Rule 18** | Power-driven vessel keeps clear |

## 3.5 The COLREGs "Decision Tree" — Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark-Boosting Analytical Tool — The Rule 5 Watch Process:**

1. **At all times** maintain a proper look-out (Rule 5).
2. **At all times** proceed at a **safe speed** (Rule 6) so you can take proper and effective action.
3. **Risk Assessment** (Rule 7): Use radar, AIS, visual, ARPA to determine if risk of collision exists. A target with **closing range and steady bearing** at a similar range = high risk.
4. **Apply Action Rules (8–19):**
   - If positive alteration, make it **large enough to be readily apparent** (Rule 8(b)) — typically ≥ **30° course change**.
   - Avoid altering course toward a vessel on your port (Rule 8(d)).
   - Cross astern at safe distance if altering to port in crossing (Rule 15).
5. **Verify** the new bearing changes appreciably (Rule 8(c)) — does the target's compass bearing change?

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Modern Ship's Navigation & Collision Avoidance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensorA[GPS Receiver] --> dataA[Position Fix Module]
    sensorB[Gyrocompass] --> dataB[Heading Sensor Bus]
    sensorC[Magnetic Compass] --> dataB
    sensorD[Speed Log] --> dataE[Speed Module]
    sensorF[RADAR Antenna] --> dataF[RADAR Processor]
    sensorG[ARPA Unit] --> dataF
    sensorH[AIS Transponder] --> dataG2[AIS Data Link]
    sensorI[Echo Sounder] --> dataI[Draft and Depth Display]
    sensorJ[Anemometer] --> dataJ[Weather Display]

    dataA --> fusion[Integrated Navigation System INS]
    dataB --> fusion
    dataE --> fusion
    dataF --> fusion
    dataG2 --> fusion
    dataI --> fusion
    dataJ --> fusion

    fusion --> ecdis[ECDIS Display]
    fusion --> conning[Conning Display]
    fusion --> arpaDisp[ARPA Display]
    fusion --> bam[Bridge Alert Management]

    conning --> oow[Officer of the Watch OOW]
    ecdis --> oow
    arpaDisp --> oow
    bam --> oow

    oow --> decision{Apply COLREGs}
    decision -->|Rule 14 Head-On| stbdAlter[Alter Starboard]
    decision -->|Rule 15 Crossing| giveway[Give Way Maneuver]
    decision -->|Rule 13 Overtaking| keepClear[Overtaker Keep Clear]
    decision -->|Rule 19 Fog| safeSpeed[Reduce to Safe Speed]

    stbdAlter --> helm[Helm Order]
    giveway --> helm
    keepClear --> helm
    safeSpeed --> helm
    helm --> bridge[Bridge Execution]

    style fusion fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style decision fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style oow fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: COLREGs Decision-Making Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Detected] --> B{Distance Less than 6 NM?}
    B -->|No| A1[Continue Monitoring]
    B -->|Yes| C{Compute CPA and TCPA}
    C --> D{CPA Less than Safety Distance?}
    D -->|No| A1
    D -->|Yes| E{Is Range Closing?}
    E -->|No| A1
    E -->|Yes| F{Bearing Steady or Closing?}
    F -->|No| A1
    F -->|Yes| G{Classify Encounter}

    G --> H{Aspect: Head-On within 6 degrees?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Apply Rule 14: Both Alter Starboard]

    G --> J{Target Forward of Beam and STBD Side?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply Rule 15: We are Give-Way]

    G --> L{Target Abaft Beam more than 22.5 degrees?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Apply Rule 13: Overtaking Scenario]

    G --> N{Restricted Visibility Fog?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Apply Rule 19: Safe Speed, Sound Signals]

    G --> P{Target is NUC or RAM?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Apply Rule 18: Keep Clear]

    I --> R[Alter Course at least 30 degrees]
    K --> R
    M --> R
    O --> S[Stop Engines or Reduce Speed]
    Q --> R

    R --> T[Monitor New Bearing]
    S --> T
    T --> U{Bearing Opening?}
    U -->|Yes| V[Resume Original Course]
    U -->|No| W[Take Further Action]

    style G fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style V fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Watchkeeping Roles on the Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    master[Master] --> chiefOff[Chief Officer]
    master --> secondOff[Second Officer]
    master --> thirdOff[Third Officer]
    chiefOff --> oow1[OOW Day Watch]
    chiefOff --> oow2[OOW Evening Watch]
    secondOff --> oow3[OOW Morning Watch]

    oow1 --> lookout1[Lookout]
    oow2 --> lookout2[Lookout]
    oow3 --> lookout3[Lookout]
    oow4 --> lookout4[Lookout]

    oow1 --> helm1[Helmsman]
    oow2 --> helm2[Helmsman]
    oow3 --> helm3[Helmsman]
    oow4 --> helm4[Helmsman]

    lookout1 -->|Visual Contact| oow1
    lookout2 -->|Visual Contact| oow2
    lookout3 -->|Visual Contact| oow3
    lookout4 -->|Visual Contact| oow4

```
