# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define "Stand-on Vessel" and "Give-way Vessel" as per COLREGs. Which vessel has the duty to act first in a crossing situation when the give-way vessel fails to take appropriate action?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Give-way Vessel:** The vessel required to keep out of the way of another vessel (COLREGs Rule 2(d)). In a crossing situation, this is the vessel that has the other on her **starboard side** (Rule 15).
- **Stand-on Vessel:** The vessel that should maintain her course and speed (Rule 2(e)). The vessel with the other on her **port side** is stand-on.
- **Duty Hierarchy (Rule 17):** The give-way vessel has the **primary** duty to act. However, when the give-way vessel fails to act, the stand-on vessel may (and should) take action to avoid collision by altering course or speed. This is the "**privileged vessel's defensive responsibility**."
- **(1 mark for definitions × 2 = 2 marks; 1 mark for hierarchy explanation)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List the three fundamental types of navigation and state the standard units used in maritime navigation for distance and speed.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Three Types of Navigation:**
  1. **Terrestrial / Piloting Navigation** — within sight of land
  2. **Electronic Navigation** — GPS, Radar, AIS, ECDIS
  3. **Celestial Navigation** — using Sun, Moon, planets, stars
- **Standard Units:**
  - **Distance:** **Nautical Mile (NM)** = 1,852 metres
  - **Speed:** **Knot (kt)** = 1 NM per hour
- **(1 mark per type × 3 = exceeds 3, so use 2 marks for the 3 types and 1 mark for units; condensed form acceptable)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Navigation Focus
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3 Variant]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A ship is on a compass course of **163°C**. The variation is **9°E** and deviation for that heading is **3°W**. A lighthouse bears **085°C** by compass. Compute the **True Course** and the **True Bearing** of the lighthouse. State the type of encounter the lighthouse would represent if it were another vessel approaching from that bearing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **three methods of position fixing in terrestrial navigation** with a labeled diagram. State the principle behind each method and its practical limitation.

#### MODEL SOLUTION

##### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the Total Error:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= Var + Dev \\
&= +9° + (-3°) \\
&= +6°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the True Course:
$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= CC + \Delta \\
TC &= 163° + 6° \\
TC &= 169°\ (T)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the True Bearing of the Lighthouse:
$$
\begin{aligned}
TB &= CB + \Delta \\
TB &= 085° + 6° \\
TB &= 091°\ (T)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Determine the Encounter Type:
- The lighthouse True Bearing is **091°**, while the ship's True Heading is **169°**.
- The relative bearing of the target from own ship = $091° - 169° = -78°$ → equivalent to **360° − 78° = 282°**.
- A bearing of **282°** is **78° on the starboard bow** (i.e., 78° forward of the starboard beam).
- This places the target **forward of the beam and on the starboard side** → **CROSSING SITUATION (Rule 15)**.
- The target has the ship on its port bow; by Rule 15, the **target vessel would be the GIVE-WAY vessel**.
- Our vessel is the **STAND-ON vessel** and must maintain course and speed (Rule 15) unless the give-way vessel fails to act (Rule 17).

**(Mark Distribution: 1 for Δ, 1 for TC, 1 for TB, 1 for relative bearing, 2 for encounter classification, 1 for rule identification)**

##### Part (b) — Solution

**The Three Methods of Terrestrial Position Fixing:**

**1. Visual Bearing (Single Bearing Method) — [2 marks]**
- A compass bearing is taken on a single charted object using the pelorus or azimuth circle.
- The reciprocal bearing is plotted from the object to obtain a **position line (PL)**.
- **Principle:** A bearing is a straight line through the object; the vessel must lie on this line.
- **Limitation:** A single bearing gives only a line, not a point. A second observation is needed for a fix, and the vessel moves between observations unless a running fix is plotted.

**2. Horizontal Sextant Angles (Cocked-Hat Method) — [2 marks]**
- A sextant is used to measure the **horizontal angle** between two charted objects.
- The angle is converted to a circle of equal angle on the chart (using a station pointer or protractor).
- Three such angles between three objects yield three circles intersecting at the **cocked hat triangle**.
- **Principle:** Inscribed Angle Theorem — all points on the circle see the baseline under the same angle.
- **Limitation:** Requires three well-defined objects, accurate timing, and a well-calibrated sextant. Errors produce a "cocked hat" whose center is the assumed position.

**3. Cross Bearings (Two-Bearing Fix) — [2 marks]**
- Two compass bearings are taken simultaneously (or as close as possible) on two charted objects.
- The position lines are plotted and their intersection is the **fix**.
- **Principle:** Intersection of two lines yields a unique point.
- **Limitation:** Bearings must be simultaneous; otherwise a running fix with course and speed advance is required. Compass error directly affects accuracy.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Each method has its place: visual bearing for quick checks, cross bearings for definitive coastal fixes, and sextant angles for surveying and rescue plotting.

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Collision Avoidance Focus
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3 Variant]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **COLREGs Rules for Head-On, Crossing, and Overtaking** situations with the help of suitable diagrams. State the required sound signal and maneuvering action in each case.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A target vessel is observed on radar at a range of **9.0 NM**, bearing **045° (T)** from your ship. Your ship is heading **270° (T)** at **18 knots**. The target is heading **000° (T)** at **12 knots**. Compute the **CPA, TCPA**, and decide the appropriate collision avoidance maneuver. State which COLREGs rule applies.

#### MODEL SOLUTION

##### Part (a) — Solution

**Rule 14 — Head-On Situation [2 marks]**
- Applies when two power-driven vessels are meeting on **nearly reciprocal courses** (within 6° of being bow-to-bow).
- **Action:** Each vessel must alter course to **STARBOARD** so that they pass on the port side of each other.
- **Sound Signal:** **One short blast** indicating the starboard alteration.
- **No stand-on vessel** — the rule is symmetric.

**Rule 15 — Crossing Situation [2.5 marks]**
- Applies when two power-driven vessels are on **intersecting courses** with risk of collision, and the situation is neither head-on nor overtaking.
- **Action:** The vessel that has the **other on her STARBOARD side** is the **GIVE-WAY** vessel and must keep clear. The vessel with the other on her **port** is the **stand-on** vessel and must maintain course and speed.
- **Sound Signal:** **One short blast** (altering starboard) or **two short blasts** (altering port) by the give-way vessel.

**Rule 13 — Overtaking Situation [2 marks]**
- Applies when a vessel approaches another from **more than 22.5° abaft her beam** (i.e., from the stern direction).
- The overtaking vessel is the **GIVE-WAY** vessel regardless of which side she is on.
- The overtaken vessel may maintain course and speed.
- **Sound Signal:** Overtaking vessel signals her intention with **Morse "D" (−··) or light flashes**.

**Note:** A vessel coming up with another from a direction **more than 22.5° abaft the beam** is deemed an overtaking vessel — this is a definite situation that continues even if the angle decreases.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** All three rules reflect the foundational principle of **early, large, and obvious action** to prevent confusion.

##### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1 — Establish Geometry**
- **Own Ship (OS):** Heading **270° T**, Speed **18 kt**
- **Target (TGT):** Heading **000° T**, Speed **12 kt**
- **Initial Range:** **9.0 NM** on bearing **045° T**

**Step 2 — Compute Velocity Components**

**Own Ship:**
- $V_{OS,x} = 18 \sin(270°) = -18.00\ \text{kt}$
- $V_{OS,y} = 18 \cos(270°) = 0.00\ \text{kt}$

**Target:**
- $V_{TGT,x} = 12 \sin(000°) = 0.00\ \text{kt}$
- $V_{TGT,y} = 12 \cos(000°) = 12.00\ \text{kt}$

**Step 3 — Compute Relative Velocity**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rel,x} &= V_{TGT,x} - V_{OS,x} = 0 - (-18) = +18\ \text{kt} \\
V_{rel,y} &= V_{TGT,y} - V_{OS,y} = 12 - 0 = +12\ \text{kt}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rel} &= \sqrt{18^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{324 + 144} = \sqrt{468} = 21.63\ \text{kt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Range Vector Components**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_x &= 9.0 \times \sin(45°) = 9.0 \times 0.7071 = 6.364\ \text{NM} \\
R_y &= 9.0 \times \cos(45°) = 9.0 \times 0.7071 = 6.364\ \text{NM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute CPA**
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPA &= \frac{\vert R_x V_{rel,y} - R_y V_{rel,x} \vert}{V_{rel}} \\
&= \frac{\vert 6.364 \times 12 - 6.364 \times 18 \vert}{21.63} \\
&= \frac{\vert 76.37 - 114.55 \vert}{21.63} \\
&= \frac{38.18}{21.63} \\
&= 1.765\ \text{NM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute TCPA**
$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPA &= \frac{R_x V_{rel,x} + R_y V_{rel,y}}{V_{rel}^2} \\
&= \frac{(6.364 \times 18) + (6.364 \times 12)}{468} \\
&= \frac{114.55 + 76.37}{468} \\
&= \frac{190.92}{468} \\
&= 0.408\ \text{hours} \\
&\approx 24.5\ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the Encounter**
- **CPA = 1.77 NM** — moderately close; **safety threshold is typically 2 NM** in open sea, so this is **at the edge of safe**.
- **TCPA = +24.5 minutes** — target is **closing** (positive TCPA).
- **Encounter Geometry:** Target bearing 045° from own ship heading 270° → Target is on the **starboard bow** of own ship.
- Target is forward of the beam, on the starboard side → **CROSSING SITUATION (Rule 15)**.
- The **target has our ship on her PORT bow** → Our ship is the **GIVE-WAY vessel**.

**Step 8 — Decide the Maneuver (Rule 15, Rule 8)**
- We are the give-way vessel; we must **alter course to PORT** (away from the target which is on our starboard).
- The alteration must be **large enough to be readily apparent** (Rule 8(b)) — typically **≥30°**.
- Suggested action: **Alter course to port by 30°–60°** and/or **reduce speed**.
- **Sound signal:** **Two short blasts** (I am altering to PORT).
- Verify: New bearing of target should be **opening** (steadily increasing) after the maneuver.

**Mark Distribution (Part b):**
- Velocity resolution: 1 mark
- Relative velocity: 1 mark
- CPA: 2 marks
- TCPA: 2 marks
- Rule identification and maneuver decision: 1 mark

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention for Variation/Deviation:** East is **positive**, West is **negative** in the KTU convention. Reversing this loses 1–2 marks silently.
> 2. **Mixing up CPA and TCPA formulas:** The CPA formula uses the **cross product** in the numerator; the TCPA uses the **dot product** divided by the squared magnitude. Students often swap them.
> 3. **Forgetting the unit conversion** for speed (kt vs. NM/min) when TCPA is in hours.
> 4. **Confusing Rule 14 with Rule 15:** Head-on requires **both** vessels to alter starboard; crossing gives the duty to **one vessel** only. Misclassifying the encounter by even a few degrees loses full marks.
> 5. **Neglecting the Rule 17 Stand-on Defensive Action:** When the give-way vessel fails to act, the stand-on vessel **must not** passively continue — that is the famous "**Titanic principle**." Examiners often test this in 2-mark sub-questions.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention Safe Speed (Rule 6):** A near-miss maneuver that ignores the safe-speed rule can be marked down.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — Read Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **COLREGs 1972** is the **bedrock of collision avoidance** — 38 Rules, 5 Parts.
- ✅ **Rule 5 — Look-out** is **continuous** and applies to **all vessels at all times** (Rule 5, "every vessel shall at all times maintain a proper look-out").
- ✅ **Rule 6 — Safe Speed** depends on visibility, traffic density, maneuverability, weather, and proximity to navigational hazards.
- ✅ **Rule 7 — Risk of Collision:** Determined by systematic observation; **closing range with steady bearing** = high risk.
- ✅ **Rule 8 — Action to Avoid Collision:** Alterations must be **large**, **early**, and **not toward a vessel on your port**.
- ✅ **Rule 13 — Overtaking:** Triggered at **>22.5° abaft the beam**; overtaker is the give-way vessel.
- ✅ **Rule 14 — Head-On:** Both alter to **STARBOARD**; symmetric duty.
- ✅ **Rule 15 — Crossing:** Vessel with target on **STBD** = give-way; on **PORT** = stand-on.
- ✅ **Rule 17 — Stand-on Defensive Action:** If give-way fails, stand-on may maneuver to avoid; **never cross ahead**.
- ✅ **Rule 18 — Responsibility Hierarchy:** Sailing > Power; NUC/RAM have priority; fishing (with nets) > others.
- ✅ **Rule 19 — Restricted Visibility:** Reduce to safe speed, post lookout, **switch on navigation lights**, sound fog signals.
- ✅ **Lights:** Port = **Red**, Starboard = **Green**, Stern = **White** (135° arc).
- ✅ **Shapes:** Anchor = **Black Ball**; NUC = **2 Red Balls**; RAM = **Ball-Diamond-Ball**.
- ✅ **Sound Signals:** 1 blast = STBD; 2 = PORT; 3 = ASTERN; 5 = **DOUBT (Danger)**.
- ✅ **Fog:** 1 long blast (making way); 2 long blasts (stopped); bell/gong for anchored vessels.
- ✅ **True = Compass + Variation + Deviation** (T = C + Var + Dev).
- ✅ **1 NM = 1,852 m = 1 minute of latitude**.
- ✅ **1 Knot = 1 NM/hr = 0.5144 m/s**.
- ✅ **Radar Range ≈ 2.2√h₁ + 2.2√h₂ (NM, h in m)**.
- ✅ **CPA** = perpendicular distance; **TCPA** = time to that point.
- ✅ **ARPA (Automatic Radar Plotting Aid)** computes CPA, TCPA, and trial maneuvers automatically.
- ✅ **AIS** transmits and receives ship data every **2–10 seconds** (Class A).
- ✅ **ECDIS** is the modern chart display; the **WGS-84 datum** is the standard.
- ✅ **Watchkeeping** is governed by **STCW Chapter VIII**; minimum bridge manning rules apply.
- ✅ **Bridge Resource Management (BRM)** integrates human factors, communication, and leadership for safety.
- ✅ **Voyage Planning** follows the **IALA/IMO Sailing Directions** and uses the **Tidal Diamond** and **Course Over Ground (COG)** analysis.
- ✅ **"If in doubt, alter course to starboard"** is a universally safe default in open sea.
- ✅ **Masters remain responsible** for the safe navigation of the ship at all times — even when a Pilot is embarked (subject to navigational exceptions in pilotage waters).
- ✅ The five types of vessels in priority (highest to lowest): **Vessel not under command > RAM > Constrained by draft > Fishing (engaged) > Sailing > Power** (Rule 18 + Rule 9 narrow channels).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigation** is the science, art, and practice of determining the position, course, and distance traveled of a vessel, aircraft, or vehicle, and directing its movement from one point to another safely and efficiently across the Earth's surface or through space.

In the **maritime context**, navigation encompasses the continuous process of:
1. **Fixing the vessel's position** (latitude and longitude).
2. **Plotting a safe track** to the destination.
3. **Monitoring progress** against the planned track.
4. **Adjusting course and speed** to compensate for drift, current, wind, and environmental conditions.

The three pillars of traditional maritime navigation are:

| Pillar | Primary Function | Key Tools |
|--------|------------------|-----------|
| **Piloting** | Near-shore navigation using visual references | Charts, buoys, landmarks, depth soundings |
| **Dead Reckoning (DR)** | Projecting position from last known fix using course & speed | Compass, log, stopwatch, plotting tools |
| **Celestial Navigation** | Position fixing using celestial bodies | Sextant, chronometer, Nautical Almanac |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern navigation** integrates **Electronic Navigation Systems** (ENS) such as **GPS (Global Positioning System), RADAR (Radio Detection and Ranging), ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display and Information System), AIS (Automatic Identification System), and Gyro/Fluxgate Compasses** as mandated by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under the **SOLAS Convention (Safety of Life at Sea), Chapter V**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car from Kochi to Delhi** with no Google Maps and no road signs:
- You check your **odometer** (analogous to a ship's **log** measuring distance).
- You check a **handheld compass** (analogous to a ship's **gyrocompass**).
- Every hour, you record where you think you are based on direction and distance (this is **Dead Reckoning**).
- You cross-check using landmarks like a river bend or a mountain peak (this is **Piloting**).
- At night, if the car had a periscope, you could measure the angle to a star (this is **Celestial Navigation**).
- Today, your phone uses satellites to pinpoint you exactly (this is **GPS-based electronic navigation**).

**A ship at sea faces identical challenges but with NO roads, NO signs, and often NO visible landmarks for thousands of kilometers** — making navigation not a convenience but a **life-critical discipline**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle — The Navigational Triangle (PZX Triangle):**
> The fundamental relationship in navigation is:
> $$\text{Distance} = \text{Speed} \times \text{Time}$$
> This simple linear relationship, combined with direction (bearing/heading), forms the basis of every navigational computation in maritime history.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Standard Nautical Mile (nm):** **1,852 meters** (≈ 1.15 statute miles; 1 minute of arc of the Earth's meridian).
- **Knot (kn):** **1 nautical mile per hour** (≈ 0.5144 m/s).
- **Standard Rate of Turn (ROT):** **3° per minute** (turn diameter ≈ 1% of speed in nm/h).
- **Earth's Radius (Mean):** **3,440 nm** (≈ 6,371 km).
- **Magnetic Declination:** Varies geographically; the angle between **True North (TN)** and **Magnetic North (MN)**.
- **Variation = Declination** (used interchangeably on charts).
- **Deviation:** Compass error caused by the ship's own magnetic field (compensated by **compass adjusters** using **Flinders bar** and **quadrantal correctors**).
- **CAVE mnemonic:** **C**ourse (ordered), **A**llocation, **V**ariation, **E**rror (Deviation).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Navigational Process — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Course Setting
- The **True Course (TC)** is the direction of the intended path measured **clockwise from True North (000°–360°)**.
- The **Magnetic Course (MC)** is obtained by applying **variation**:
  $$\text{True Course} = \text{Magnetic Course} + \text{Variation (East)}$$
  $$\text{True Course} = \text{Magnetic Course} - \text{Variation (West)}$$
- The **Compass Course (CC)** is the bearing read on the **compass**, obtained by applying **deviation**:
  $$\text{Magnetic Course} = \text{Compass Course} + \text{Deviation (East)}$$
  $$\text{Magnetic Course} = \text{Compass Course} - \text{Deviation (West)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cadet's Mental Rule — "Add West Going Out":** Add Westerly errors and subtract Easterly errors when converting True → Magnetic → Compass.

### Step 2: Distance Measurement
- **Speed over Ground (SOG)** — actual speed relative to seabed.
- **Speed through Water (STW)** — speed relative to water (affected by current).
- Distance is measured via:
  - **Chip log (historical):** Wooden chip thrown overboard, knotted rope counts knots in 30 seconds.
  - **Impeller log / EM log (modern):** Rotating impeller or electromagnetic sensor.
  - **GPS (modern):** Direct distance calculation from position fixes.

### Step 3: Position Fixing
| Method | Accuracy | Limitation |
|--------|----------|------------|
| **Visual bearing (compass + landmark)** | ±1°–2° | Requires visibility, range-limited |
| **Radar range & bearing** | ±10 m / ±0.5° | Subject to sea clutter, blind in shadow zones |
| **GPS fix** | ±5–10 m (SPS), ±1 m (DGPS) | Vulnerable to jamming, spoofing, outages |
| **Celestial fix (Sun/Moon/Stars)** | ±1–2 nm | Weather-dependent, time-critical |
| **Loran-C** | ±0.25 nm (legacy) | Limited coverage, largely decommissioned |

### Step 4: Course Correction — The Compass Error Triangle
- **Variation (Var):** Known from **isogonic lines** on the nautical chart.
- **Deviation (Dev):** Found in the **Deviation Card** (a table on the bridge calibrated during compass adjustment).
- **Leeway:** Drift due to wind, measured as angle between heading and course-made-good.
- **Set & Drift of Current:** Direction the current flows (set) and its speed (drift, in knots).

### Step 5: Chart Work & Plotting
- **Mercator Projection** (for coastal/ocean sailing) — straight lines = **rhumb lines (loxodromes)**, constant compass bearing.
- **Lambert Conformal Conic / Gnomonic Projection** (for polar/special routing).
- **Position notation:** **Latitude (N/S)** measured from Equator; **Longitude (E/W)** measured from Greenwich Prime Meridian.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** tested in KTU ESE for OESFT727 Module 3.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Distance | $D = S \times T$ | $D$ in nm, $S$ in knots, $T$ in hours |
| 2 | Speed in knots from chip log | $\text{Knots} = \frac{\text{Knots counted}}{28 \times \text{seconds}}$ | Standard 28-second chip log |
| 3 | True Course to Compass | $CC = TC - \text{Var} - \text{Dev}$ (using "Add West") | All angles in degrees |
| 4 | Compass Error (CE) | $CE = \text{Var} + \text{Dev}$ | Combined magnetic disturbance |
| 5 | Conversion: nm to km | $1\ \text{nm} = 1.852\ \text{km}$ | Exact SI definition |
| 6 | Conversion: km/h to knots | $1\ \text{km/h} = 0.539957\ \text{kn}$ | $\approx 0.54$ for quick mental math |
| 7 | Departure (Mercator sailing) | $\text{Dep} = D \times \sin(C)$ | $C$ = True Course, $D$ = Distance in nm |
| 8 | Difference in Latitude (DLat) | $\text{DLat} = D \times \cos(C)$ | In minutes of latitude |
| 9 | DLat / DLo in minutes | $1^\circ\ \text{latitude} = 60\ \text{nm}$ | Exactly on the meridian |
| 10 | DLo in minutes (any latitude) | $\text{DLo (min)} = \text{Dep} \times \sec(\text{Mean Lat})$ | At higher latitudes, 1° longitude < 60 nm |
| 11 | DLo in minutes (small angle) | $\text{DLo (min)} = \text{Dep} \times \frac{1}{\cos(\text{Mean Lat})}$ | Same as above, $\sec = 1/\cos$ |
| 12 | Gyrocompass settling time | $\approx 4$ hours (typical) | Spinning-mass north-seeking device |
| 13 | Rate of Turn diameter | $D_{\text{turn}} = \frac{S}{10} \times \text{Speed (kn)} \times ?$ | For 3°/min: $\text{Dia} \approx 0.01 \times V^2$ (V in knots) |
| 14 | Rate of Turn diameter (formula) | $\text{Diameter (nm)} = \frac{V^2}{57.3 \times \text{ROT}}$ | $V$ in knots, ROT in °/min |
| 15 | Maximum rate of turn ship | $\text{ROT} = 3^\circ/\text{min}$ (large vessels) | IMO recommendation for maneuvering |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pitfall on Table Rows:** When using DLo formulas, remember that **sec(90°) is undefined** (division by zero at the poles) — the **Gnomonic projection** is required near-polar navigation, not Mercator.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Maritime Engineering

- **Collision Avoidance:** RADAR + ARPA (Automatic Radar Plotting Aid) integration enables **CPA (Closest Point of Approach)** and **TCPA (Time to CPA)** calculations in the **COLREGs (International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea)** framework.
- **Search and Rescue (SAR):** IMO/IAMSAR Manual uses **Datum**, **Search patterns (expanding square, sector search, parallel sweep)**, and **drift calculations** to optimize rescue aircraft/vessel deployment.
- **E-Navigation Strategy (IMO 2014+):** Integrates all electronic data into a single bridge display to reduce human error — the centerpiece of the **e-Navigation concept** for the future smart ship.
- **Autonomous Shipping (MASS — Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships):** IMO Regulatory Scoping Exercise (2021+) relies entirely on robust navigation principles for remote and autonomous operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conversion of True Course to Compass Course

**Problem Statement:** A vessel intends to steer a True Course of **115°** in a region where the **Variation is 8° East**. The Deviation Card shows **Deviation = 3° West** for that heading. Find the **Compass Course (CC)** to be steered.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the given quantities.
$$\text{True Course (TC)} = 115^\circ$$
$$\text{Variation (Var)} = 8^\circ\ \text{East}$$
$$\text{Deviation (Dev)} = 3^\circ\ \text{West}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the "Add West" rule. When converting **True → Magnetic**, add Westerly errors.
$$\text{True Course} = \text{Magnetic Course} + \text{Easterly Variation}$$
$$115^\circ = \text{Magnetic Course} + 8^\circ$$

**Step 3:** Solve for Magnetic Course (MC).
$$\text{Magnetic Course} = 115^\circ - 8^\circ = 107^\circ$$

**Step 4:** Now convert Magnetic Course → Compass Course. **Apply Deviation using the same rule.** Deviation is 3° West, so we **add it going from Compass to Magnetic**, meaning we **subtract it going from Magnetic to Compass**? Let's apply the formula carefully.

The general relations are:
$$\text{Magnetic} = \text{Compass} \pm \text{Deviation (East +, West −)}$$

Substituting:
$$107^\circ = \text{Compass} - 3^\circ$$
$$\text{Compass} = 107^\circ + 3^\circ = 110^\circ$$

**Step 5:** Verification using the master conversion formula:
$$CC = TC - \text{Var (E)} - \text{Dev (W)}$$
$$CC = 115^\circ - 8^\circ - 3^\circ = 104^\circ \quad ❌ \text{(recheck)}$$

Let me re-derive the master formula systematically:

**Step 5 (Corrected):** The cleanest derivation is:
- True heading = Magnetic heading + East Variation
- Magnetic heading = Compass heading + East Deviation
- Therefore: $TC = CC + \text{Var} + \text{Dev}$ (using signed values, + for East, − for West)

Substituting with sign convention (East = +, West = −):
$$TC = CC + (+8) + (-3)$$
$$115 = CC + 5$$
$$\boxed{CC = 110^\circ}$$

**Step 6:** Final sanity check.
- Magnetic heading: $110 - 3 = 107^\circ$ ✓
- True heading: $107 + 8 = 115^\circ$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember — "Can Dead Men Vote Twice at Elections" (CDMVTE)**: Compass → Deviation → Magnetic → Variation → True (each step: East is positive / "Add", West is negative / "Subtract"). The mnemonic reads **C-D-M-V-T-E** for the chain of conversion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Distance and Time Problem (Chip Log & Modern Log)

**Problem Statement:** A ship steams at a speed of **18 knots**. Calculate the time to cover **162 nautical miles**, and verify the chip-log result for a 28-second run.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Use the fundamental relation.
$$D = S \times T \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{D}{S}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.
$$T = \frac{162\ \text{nm}}{18\ \text{kn}} = 9\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 3:** Express the result in hours and minutes.
$$9\ \text{hours} = 9\ \text{h}\ 0\ \text{min}$$

**Step 4:** Now verify the chip-log method. The chip log counts knots on a rope over 28 seconds. The number of knots counted equals the speed in nautical miles per hour:
$$\text{Knots counted} = S = 18$$

**Step 5:** Confirm using the formula:
$$S = \frac{\text{Knots counted}}{28 \times 28} \times 28 \quad \text{is incorrect; correct formula:}$$
$$S = \frac{\text{Knots counted}}{28\ \text{seconds}} \times 3600 = \text{Knots counted} \times 2.86...$$

For a properly designed chip log with 28-second timing, the number of knots passing equals the speed:
$$S_{\text{knots}} = \text{Knots counted over 28 s} = 18\ \text{knots}\ ✓$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mercator Sailing — Finding the Course and Distance

**Problem Statement:** A ship at position **A: 22° 30′ N, 060° 45′ E** is bound for **B: 18° 00′ N, 065° 30′ E**. Calculate the **True Course** and **Distance** using Mercator sailing.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute the Difference in Latitude (DLat).
$$\text{DLat} = L_B - L_A = 18^\circ 00' - 22^\circ 30' = -4^\circ 30'$$

DLat in minutes of latitude:
$$\text{DLat} = -270'\ \text{N (i.e., 270' S)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Difference in Longitude (DLo).
$$\text{DLo} = \text{Lo}_B - \text{Lo}_A = 65^\circ 30' - 60^\circ 45' = +4^\circ 45'$$

DLo in minutes:
$$\text{DLo} = +285' \quad (\text{East})$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Mean Latitude (M. Lat).
$$\text{M. Lat} = \frac{22^\circ 30' + 18^\circ 00'}{2} = 20^\circ 15'\ \text{N}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the Departure.
Departure is the **east-west distance in nautical miles**, calculated as:
$$\text{Dep} = \text{DLo (min)} \times \cos(\text{M. Lat})$$

$$\text{Dep} = 285' \times \cos(20^\circ 15')$$

Using a calculator or tables:
$$\cos(20^\circ 15') \approx 0.9379$$

$$\text{Dep} = 285 \times 0.9379 \approx 267.3\ \text{nm}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the True Course.
The Mercator course formula:
$$\tan(C) = \frac{\text{Dep}}{\text{DLat}} = \frac{267.3}{-270}$$

$$\tan(C) = -0.990$$

**Step 6:** Determine the quadrant.
- DLat is **negative (South)** and Dep is **positive (East)** → the course is in the **SE quadrant** (i.e., between 90° and 180°).
$$C = \arctan(-0.990) = -44.7^\circ \text{ (reference angle)}$$

In the SE quadrant:
$$C = 180^\circ - 44.7^\circ = 135.3^\circ \approx 135^\circ 18'$$

**Step 7:** Compute the Distance.
$$D = \sqrt{\text{DLat}^2 + \text{Dep}^2}$$

$$D = \sqrt{(-270)^2 + (267.3)^2}$$

$$D = \sqrt{72900 + 71449.3}$$

$$D = \sqrt{144349.3}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 380\ \text{nm}}$$

**Step 8:** Verification using the alternative formula:
$$D = \frac{\text{DLat}}{\cos(C)} = \frac{270}{\cos(135.3^\circ)} = \frac{270}{0.7121} \approx 379.2\ \text{nm}\ ✓$$

(±0.2% error due to rounding)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Navigation Methods

| Criterion | Dead Reckoning | Piloting | Celestial | GPS / Electronic |
|-----------|---------------|----------|-----------|------------------|
| **Accuracy** | ±2–5% of distance run | ±0.1–0.5 nm | ±1–2 nm | ±5–10 m (SPS) |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low (compass + log) | Low (charts) | Moderate (sextant) | High (receivers, ECDIS) |
| **Skill Required** | Moderate | High (judgment) | High (math) | Low (operator) |
| **Weather Dependence** | None (uses DR) | High (visibility) | Partial (clouds) | None (radio signals) |
| **Sea State Effect** | None (log can be affected) | High (rough seas) | High (platform) | Minimal |
| **Vulnerability** | Compounding errors | Limited range | Almanac needed | Jamming, solar storms |
| **Backup Status** | Always | Coastal only | Optional | Primary modern |

---

## 4.2 Flowchart: The Navigational Decision Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["FIX POSITION OBSERVATION"]
    stepB["PLOT POSITION ON CHART"]
    stepC["COMPARE WITH PLAN COURSE"]
    stepD["DEVIATION DETECTED"]
    stepE["COMPUTE CORRECTION COURSE"]
    stepF["ADJUST HELM OR AUTOPILOT"]
    stepG["RECORD IN LOG BOOK"]
    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepA
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: PZX Triangle of Navigation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["VESSEL DEPARTS LAST KNOWN POSITION P"]
    polaris["KNOWN VARIABLES: COURSE Z AND DISTANCE X"]
    target["CALCULATE NEXT FIXED POSITION Z"]
    expand["EXPANSION BY DEAD RECKONING DR OR BY OBSERVATION RUNNING FIX"]

    start --> polaris
    polaris --> target
    target --> expand
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The PZX Triangle (sometimes called the "Clock-and-Rose" diagram):**
> - **P** = Last known position (e.g., morning star fix)
> - **Z** = New observed position
> - **X** = A "running fix" — the position extrapolated forward from P by sailing time
> - The **azimuth of the observed body** and the **course and distance steamed** create two intersecting lines whose intersection = Z.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define "Dead Reckoning" and list any two inherent errors associated with it.*

**Model Answer:**

**Dead Reckoning (DR)** is the process of determining a vessel's present position by applying the **course steered** and **distance run** to a **last known position (fix)**, without reference to external observations. It is the most fundamental and continuously-used form of navigation, providing a real-time estimate of the vessel's position between external fixes.

**Two inherent errors:**
1. **Compass Error (CE):** Inaccurate steering due to uncorrected variation and deviation.
2. **Leeway:** Sideways drift caused by wind, causing the vessel to make good a course different from the one steered.
3. **Current (Set & Drift):** Unknown or uncompensated water movement.
4. **Log Error:** The distance run indicator may be miscalibrated.

*(Any two well-explained errors fetch full 3 marks; mentioning four provides cushion.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *What is the difference between a Magnetic Compass and a Gyrocompass? List any two advantages of the Gyrocompass.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Magnetic Compass | Gyrocompass |
|--------|------------------|-------------|
| **Principle** | Aligns with Earth's magnetic field | Spinning rotor seeks True North via Earth's rotation |
| **Reference** | Magnetic North | True North |
| **Errors** | Variation + Deviation | Settling time, latitude error, speed error |
| **Output** | Bearing of Magnetic North | Bearing of True North (high accuracy) |

**Two advantages of Gyrocompass:**
1. **No magnetic deviation** — it does not depend on Earth's magnetism and is unaffected by ferrous cargo.
2. **Higher accuracy and stability** — used as the master heading reference for all bridge systems (autopilot, radar, ECDIS).
3. **Provides stable signal** for repeaters and electronic systems (multi-output, no oscillation).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

> **Question A(a). [7 Marks]**
> A vessel steams **150 nautical miles** at a speed of **15 knots** on a True Course of **225°**. Variation is **6° West** and Deviation is **2° East** for that heading. Find the:
> (i) Time taken
> (ii) Compass Course to be steered
> (iii) Compass Error

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (i): Time taken**
$$T = \frac{D}{S} = \frac{150\ \text{nm}}{15\ \text{kn}} = 10\ \text{hours}$$

**Part (ii): Compass Course to be steered**

Using the **CDMVTE** chain (Compass → Deviation → Magnetic → Variation → True):
- True Course = Compass Course + Var (E+) + Dev (E+) [signed convention]
- $225 = CC + (-6) + (+2)$
- $225 = CC - 4$
- $\boxed{CC = 229^\circ}$

**Part (iii): Compass Error**
$$\text{CE} = \text{Var} + \text{Dev} = (-6^\circ) + (+2^\circ) = -4^\circ\ (\text{i.e., 4° West})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the sign convention for East/West. The correct approach is to assign **East = +ve, West = −ve** consistently. Writing "W" instead of "−6" in the formula loses **2 marks** in KTU valuation.

---

> **Question A(b). [7 Marks]**
> Explain the **principle, construction, and operation of a Gyrocompass**. Why is it preferred over a magnetic compass on modern vessels?

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Principle:** A **spinning gyroscope** (high-speed rotor) exhibits two fundamental properties:
1. **Rigidity in space** — the spin axis tends to point in a fixed direction.
2. **Precession** — when torque is applied, the axis moves at 90° to the applied force.

**Construction:**
- **Rotor:** Symmetrical wheel spinning at ~6,000–15,000 rpm in vacuum.
- **Spherical casing:** Houses the rotor in a low-friction environment.
- **Mercury ballistic or pendulous** arrangement providing the precession torque.
- **Follow-up system:** Detects tilt and applies torques to maintain North-seeking.
- **Phantom ring / outer ring** with damping mechanisms.

**Operation:**
- The rotor is spun to high speed (electric motor drive).
- The Earth's rotation creates an apparent torque on the gyroscope, causing its spin axis to **precess** toward the meridian.
- The system is **damped** (using a pendulous weight below the center of gravity) to settle at True North without oscillation.
- Settling time: typically **3–4 hours**.
- Output: **Gyro heading (True)** is sent to repeaters, autopilot, radar, and ECDIS.

**Advantages over Magnetic Compass:**
| Feature | Gyrocompass | Magnetic Compass |
|---------|-------------|------------------|
| **Points to** | True North | Magnetic North |
| **Variation** | None | Requires correction |
| **Deviation** | None | Affected by ship's magnetism |
| **Stability for repeaters** | Excellent | Limited |
| **Use with electronics** | Direct integration | Requires conversion |

*(A neat labeled diagram of the gyroscope rotor + Earth rotation is expected for full marks.)*

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

> **Question B(a). [7 Marks]**
> The position of a ship at **0800 hrs** is **20° 12′ N, 070° 30′ E**. At **1200 hrs**, the position is **19° 00′ N, 071° 18′ E**. Calculate the **Set and Drift** experienced by the ship if the **course steered was 150° (T)** at **12 knots**.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Course Made Good (CMG) and Speed Made Good (SMG).**

**DLat:**
$$\text{DLat} = 19^\circ 00' - 20^\circ 12' = -1^\circ 12' = -72'\ \text{N}$$

**DLo:**
$$\text{DLo} = 71^\circ 18' - 70^\circ 30' = +48'\ \text{E}$$

**Mean Latitude:**
$$\text{M. Lat} = \frac{20^\circ 12' + 19^\circ 00'}{2} = 19^\circ 36'\ \text{N}$$

**Departure:**
$$\text{Dep} = \text{DLo} \times \cos(\text{M. Lat}) = 48 \times \cos(19^\circ 36') = 48 \times 0.9421 \approx 45.2\ \text{nm}$$

**CMG:**
$$\tan(\text{CMG}) = \frac{\text{Dep}}{\text{DLat}} = \frac{45.2}{-72} = -0.628$$
$$\text{CMG} = \arctan(-0.628) = -32.1^\circ\ (\text{ref. angle})$$

DLat is negative (S), Dep is positive (E) → **SE quadrant**:
$$\boxed{\text{CMG} = 180^\circ - 32.1^\circ = 147.9^\circ \approx 148^\circ}$$

**SMG (Distance steamed in 4 hours):**
$$\text{Distance} = \sqrt{(-72)^2 + (45.2)^2} = \sqrt{5184 + 2043} = \sqrt{7227} \approx 85.0\ \text{nm}$$
$$\text{SMG} = \frac{85.0}{4} = 21.25\ \text{knots}$$

**Step 2: Course Steered = 150°(T), Course Made Good = 148°(T).**
The vessel has been **pushed south of the course** (and slightly faster than 12 knots — the surplus 9.25 knots).

**Step 3: Leeway + Current effects.**

*Leeway*: The course steered is **150°**, and the course made good is **148°**. The difference of **2°** is the **leeway** if no current is assumed, but here we have a speed differential (21.25 vs 12), so a current must be present.

**Step 4: Compute Set and Drift of the current.**

The current's effect is the **vector difference** between the **course & speed made good (CMG/SMG)** and the **course & speed through water (CT/ST)**.

**Current vector = SMG vector − STW vector**
- SMG vector: $85.0\ \text{nm at } 148^\circ$ → components: $(+45.2\ \text{E},\ -72\ \text{S})$
- STW vector: $48\ \text{nm at } 150^\circ$ in 4 hours → components: $(+24\ \text{E},\ -41.6\ \text{S})$
- Current vector: $((+45.2 - 24),\ (-72 - (-41.6))) = (+21.2\ \text{E},\ -30.4\ \text{S})$
- Current magnitude: $\sqrt{21.2^2 + 30.4^2} = \sqrt{449 + 924} = \sqrt{1373} \approx 37.1\ \text{nm over 4 hours}$
- Drift: $\frac{37.1}{4} = 9.3\ \text{knots}$
- Set: $\arctan(21.2 / 30.4) = \arctan(0.697) \approx 34.9^\circ$ (south of east, i.e., SE)
- $\boxed{\text{Set} = 145^\circ(\text{T}),\ \text{Drift} = 9.3\ \text{knots}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **"course steered"** with **"course made good"** and apply the difference to **leeway** alone. The question asks for **Set and Drift of current**, which must be calculated as a **vector difference between the DR and observed track**, not as a simple angular difference. Forgetting the **speed difference** (21.25 vs 12 knots) leads to **incorrect drift** and costs **3–4 marks**.

---

> **Question B(b). [7 Marks]**
> Explain the working principle of **GPS (Global Positioning System)** for maritime navigation. Mention its **three segments** and the **errors** that affect its accuracy.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Principle of GPS:** GPS is a **satellite-based radio-navigation system** that provides 24/7, all-weather, global 3D position (latitude, longitude, altitude) and time information.

**Working principle:** **Trilateration** — a GPS receiver measures the **distance to at least 4 satellites** (3 for position + 1 for time) and computes its own position by intersection of spheres.

**Distance measurement:** Using **Time of Arrival (TOA)** of signals.
$$\text{Distance} = c \times \Delta t$$
where $c$ = speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$) and $\Delta t$ = time taken for signal to travel from satellite to receiver.

The receiver computes pseudoranges using codes on **L1 (1575.42 MHz)** and **L2 (1227.60 MHz)** signals, then corrects them using ephemeris data.

**Three Segments of GPS:**

| Segment | Function | Components |
|---------|----------|------------|
| **Space Segment** | Broadcasting signals from orbit | 24+ MEO satellites at ~20,200 km altitude, in 6 orbital planes |
| **Control Segment** | Monitoring and maintaining the constellation | Master Control Station (Colorado), Monitor Stations, Ground Antennas for upload |
| **User Segment** | Receiving and processing signals | GPS receivers (handheld, bridge-mounted, integrated into ECDIS) |

**Errors affecting accuracy:**
1. **Ionospheric delay** — signal slows in ionized upper atmosphere (~5–10 m).
2. **Tropospheric delay** — water vapor causes signal delay (~2–3 m).
3. **Multipath errors** — signal reflection from sea, structures (~1–2 m).
4. **Satellite clock error** — atomic clock drift (~1 m).
5. **Ephemeris error** — predicted satellite position inaccuracy (~1 m).
6. **Selective Availability (SA)** — intentionally degraded (turned off in 2000).
7. **Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP)** — poor satellite geometry amplifies errors.
8. **Receiver noise.**

**Augmentation systems** (to improve accuracy):
- **DGPS (Differential GPS):** ±1–3 m accuracy.
- **WAAS, EGNOS, GAGAN, MSAS:** Satellite-based augmentation systems.
- **RTK GPS:** ±1 cm accuracy (used in hydrography and dynamic positioning).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 3 (Navigation):**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Forgetting to convert East = +, West = − in compass corrections. Always state the convention at the start.
> 2. **Wrong Quadrant Selection:** When $\tan(C)$ is negative, students pick the wrong quadrant (NE vs SE, SW vs NW). Use the **DLat and Dep sign rule** to determine the quadrant.
> 3. **Confusing Nautical Miles with Statute Miles:** 1 nm = 1.852 km, NOT 1.609 km. The nautical mile is the **standard** in maritime.
> 4. **Misapplying the "Add West" Rule:** The rule is asymmetric — adding West goes **outward** (from True to Compass) and **inward** (Compass to True) requires subtraction.
> 5. **Forgetting Time Conversion:** 1 knot = 1 nm/h. Speed readings in km/h must be divided by 1.852 to convert to knots. Marks lost in unit mismatches.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Principles of Navigation**

- ✅ **Navigation** = determining position + course + distance + directing movement safely.
- ✅ **1 Nautical Mile (nm) = 1,852 meters**; **1 Knot = 1 nm/h**; **1° of latitude = 60 nm** (everywhere).
- ✅ **Three traditional methods:** Piloting, Dead Reckoning, Celestial Navigation.
- ✅ **Modern methods:** GPS, RADAR/ARPA, ECDIS, AIS, Gyrocompass, e-Navigation.
- ✅ **CDMVTE mnemonic:** Compass → Deviation → Magnetic → Variation → True (East is +, West is −).
- ✅ **"Add West" rule:** When converting **outward** (True → Magnetic → Compass), **add West, subtract East**.
- ✅ **Dead Reckoning (DR)** = position projected from last fix using course and speed; always used between external fixes.
- ✅ **Course Made Good (CMG)** = the actual track of the vessel over the ground (from fix to fix).
- ✅ **Set & Drift** = the current's direction and speed (vector difference between DR and observed track).
- ✅ **Leeway** = sideways drift due to wind; **Drift** = current-induced motion (do NOT confuse them).
- ✅ **Compass Error (CE) = Variation + Deviation** (signed, with East = +).
- ✅ **PZX Triangle** is the geometric basis of fixing and running fixes.
- ✅ **Mercator sailing** uses $\tan(C) = \text{Dep}/\text{DLat}$ — straight rhumb line on a Mercator chart.
- ✅ **GPS works on trilateration** — needs **4 satellites minimum** (3 for position, 1 for time).
- ✅ **Gyrocompass** points to **True North**; **Magnetic compass** points to **Magnetic North**.
- ✅ **CAVE** mnemonic for True/Compass conversion: **C**ourse **A**llocated + **V**ariation + **E**rror.
- ✅ **Standard Rate of Turn = 3°/min**; turning diameter (nm) = $V^2 / (57.3 \times \text{ROT})$.
- ✅ **ECDIS** is now the **primary** navigation system (SOLAS Carriage Requirement, Chapter V).
- ✅ **e-Navigation (IMO)** is the future — integrates all bridge data for safer, error-reduced navigation.
- ✅ **SOLAS Chapter V** mandates carriage of: Gyrocompass, Radar, ARPA, ECDIS, AIS, BNWAS, VDR, Echo Sounder, Magnetic Compass, and EPIRB.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Celestial Navigation** is the science and art of determining one's geographic position on the surface of the Earth by observing the apparent positions (Altitudes and Azimuths) of celestial bodies — such as the Sun, Moon, Planets, and selected 57 navigational Stars — and reducing those observations to a **Line of Position (LOP)** through mathematical or tabular methods based on the **Astronomical Triangle (PZX)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Celestial Navigation is treated as the **primary backup / fail-safe positioning method** onboard vessels when electronic navigation systems (GNSS, GPS, AIS, RADAR) become unavailable due to power failure, jamming, spoofing, or equipment malfunction. The navigator measures the **Altitude (Ho)** of a body above the visible sea horizon, applies corrections, and uses the **Sight Reduction tables (HO 229 / HO 249 / NAO Tables)** to compute an Intercept and Azimuth, ultimately plotting a **Line of Position (LOP)** on a **Mercator chart** or **Universal Plotting Sheet**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sky as a Giant Lighthouse"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine the celestial sphere as a giant hollow sphere**, with the Earth at its centre and the Sun, Moon, and stars painted on its inner surface like a planetarium ceiling.

- Each star is like a **fixed lighthouse beam** shining straight down onto a single spot on the Earth's surface called the **Geographic Position (GP)** — the point where the star is directly overhead (at the zenith).
- Just as a mariner can locate himself by knowing where he is relative to a known lighthouse, he can locate himself by knowing where he is relative to the **GP of a star**.
- By measuring **how high** the star is above the horizon (Altitude, $h$), the observer computes **how far away** he is from the GP.
- By measuring **the compass bearing** of the star (Azimuth, $Zn$), the observer computes **which direction** the GP lies from his position.

**Together → Distance + Direction → Line of Position → Fix.**

This is precisely why Celestial Navigation is mathematically elegant: it reduces the infinite 3-D universe into a **single triangle on paper** — the **PZX Astronomical Triangle**.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following ten terms form the vocabulary backbone of Module 3. Memorise them precisely.**

| Term | Symbol | Plain-English Meaning |
|------|:------:|-----------------------|
| **Zenith** | Z | The point on the celestial sphere **directly above** the observer's head |
| **Nadir** | Na | The point on the celestial sphere **directly below** the observer (anti-zenith) |
| **Celestial Equator** | — | The projection of Earth's equator onto the celestial sphere |
| **Pole (Elevated/Depressed)** | P / P' | The projection of Earth's N/S pole onto the celestial sphere |
| **Horizon** | — | The plane perpendicular to the observer's zenith; the visible sea horizon is *slightly* below true horizon (Depression ≈ $2.1'$) |
| **Altitude** | $h$ | The angular distance of a body **above the horizon** ($0°$ to $90°$) |
| **Zenith Distance** | $z$ | $z = 90° - h$ (angular distance from body to zenith) |
| **Azimuth** | $Zn$ | The true compass bearing of the body measured from **North (000°) clockwise** |
| **Declination** | Dec / $\delta$ | Angular distance of the body **North (+) or South (−) of the celestial equator** (like latitude for stars) |
| **Hour Angle** | LHA / GHA | Angular measure of the body's meridian **West of the observer's/ Greenwich meridian** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Three physical constants** you must commit to memory:
> - **Standard Horizon Depression for height of eye 5 m ≈ 2.1′ (arc-minutes)**
> - **1 Nautical Mile = 1 minute of arc on Earth's surface (1852 m)**
> - **Earth's axial tilt (Obliquity of Ecliptic) = 23° 26′ 21″**

---

## 1.4 Why Celestial Navigation Still Matters in the 21st Century

| Electronic System | Failure Mode | Celestial Backup Status |
|-------------------|--------------|-------------------------|
| GPS / GNSS | Signal jamming, spoofing | **Fully operational** |
| AIS | Cyber-attack | **Independent reference** |
| RADAR | Power loss | **Standalone** |
| ECDIS | Software failure | **Manual plotting possible** |

It is a **STCW Manila 2010 amended** requirement (Table A-II/1) that every **Officer in Charge of a Navigational Watch (OICNW)** be proficient in celestial sight reduction. Hence the KTU 2024 Scheme places this module as a **mandatory competency** for Marine/Offshore Engineers and Nautical Science streams.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PZX Astronomical Triangle — The Heart of Celestial Navigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every celestial sight reduction problem** — whether Sun, Moon, Planet, or Star — **is solved by solving the same triangle:** the **PZX Astronomical Triangle** drawn on the celestial sphere.

The three vertices of this triangle are:

| Vertex | Represents | Located at |
|--------|------------|-----------|
| **P** | The **Elevated Pole** (N. Pole for Northern observers) | On celestial sphere over Earth's axis |
| **Z** | The **Observer's Zenith** | Directly above the observer |
| **X** | The **Geographical Position (GP)** of the celestial body | Where the body is at zenith on Earth |

The three **sides** (arcs on celestial sphere, measured in degrees):

| Side | Symbol | Length | Geometric Meaning |
|------|:------:|:------:|-------------------|
| PZ | Co-Latitude | $90° - L$ | From Pole to Observer's Zenith |
| PX | Polar Distance | $90° - \delta$ | From Pole to Body's GP |
| ZX | Zenith Distance | $z = 90° - h$ | From Observer's Zenith to Body |

The three **angles** at the vertices:

| Angle | Symbol | Meaning |
|-------|:------:|---------|
| At Z | — | Azimuth angle (Z = $Zn$) |
| At P | **LHA** | Local Hour Angle = time-arc from observer's meridian to body's meridian |
| At X | — | Hour angle at GP (not directly used in standard reduction) |

---

## 2.2 The Cosine-Haversine & ABC Tables

> [!NOTE]
> **Sight Reduction = Solving the PZX triangle** for $h$ (computed altitude, $Hc$) and $Z$ (azimuth angle).

The fundamental equation used in HO 229 / HO 249 is the **cosine formula**:

$$\cos(z) = \sin(L)\sin(\delta) + \cos(L)\cos(\delta)\cos(LHA)$$

In practice, this is decomposed into **pre-tabulated functions** to allow quick look-up:

| Quantity | Definition | Where Obtained |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| $A$ | $\tan^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\sin(LHA)}{\cos(L)\tan(\delta) - \sin(L)\cos(LHA)}\right]$ (intercept) | Nautical Almanac |
| $B$ | $\tan^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\sin(L)}{\cos(L)\cos(LHA)}\right]$ (azimuth component) | Nautical Almanac |
| $C$ | $\cos(LHA)\cos(\delta)\sin(LHA)$ (intercept factor) | Derived |

For the modern **Compact Sight Reduction Table (NAO Pub. NS 1)**, the formula set is:

$$A = \tan^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\tan(\delta)}{\cos(LHA)}\right] - L$$
$$B = \tan^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\sin(A)}{\cos(A)\cos(LHA)}\right]$$
$$Hc = B + \delta \quad (\text{when } L \text{ and } \delta \text{ same name})$$
$$Hc = B - \delta \quad (\text{when opposite names})$$

This will be **derived in full** in Section 3.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the consolidated cheat sheet.** Every value, every boundary, every unit is **exam-grade**.

| # | Formula / Identity | Variable Key | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | $z = 90° - h$ | Zenith distance ↔ Altitude | Degrees (°) |
| 2 | $\text{LHA} = \text{GHA} - \lambda_W$ or $\text{LHA} = \text{GHA} + \lambda_E$ | Local vs Greenwich Hour Angle | **Degrees**, West Longitudes subtracted, East added |
| 3 | $\text{SHA}_{\text{Star}} + \text{GHA}_{\odot} = \text{GHA}_{\text{Star}}$ | Star Hour Angle + Sun's GHA = Star's GHA | Nautical Almanac daily page |
| 4 | $\text{LAT}_{\text{Noon}} = \text{Dec}_{\text{meridian passage}} \pm z_D$ | Noon latitude | **+** when Dec and Lat same name; **−** when opposite |
| 5 | $\text{LAT}_{\text{Polaris}} = -1° + \text{Ho}_{\text{Polaris}} + a_0 + a_1 + a_2$ | Polaris latitude | $a_0, a_1, a_2$ are Almanac corrections |
| 6 | $\text{Intercept} = Ho - Hc$ | Distance of GP from observer | **+ Toward** (GP closer), **− Away** (GP further) |
| 7 | $Zn = Z + (\text{correct for N/S latitude})$ | True azimuth | See derivation in §3.3 |
| 8 | $\text{Amplitude} = \sin^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\sin(\delta)}{\cos(L)}\right]$ | Sunrise/sunset bearing | Approx. value at horizon |
| 9 | $\text{GMT} = \text{LMT} + \dfrac{\lambda_W}{15°}$ or $\text{GMT} = \text{LMT} - \dfrac{\lambda_E}{15°}$ | Local Mean Time → GMT | $\lambda$ in degrees, hours result |
| 10 | $\text{Altitude correction (Sun lower limb)} = Ho_{\text{sex}} + SD - 3'$ | Dip + Refraction | Standard KTU corrections |
| 11 | $\text{Dec correction (Sun)} = \text{Dec}_{\text{tab}} \pm d \cdot \Delta t$ | $d$ = hourly diff from Almanac | Per-hour rate |
| 12 | $\text{Time Zone} = \text{GMT} \pm \text{Zone Number}$ | UTC ↔ Ship's zone | East +, West − |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Astronomical Refraction at horizon ≈ 34′** (subtracted from sextant altitude when using the *natural* horizon reference). For marine sextant work, the **observed (apparent) altitude** $Ho$ is the *corrected* altitude after applying: **Index Error ± Dip − Refraction ± Semi-diameter ± Parallax − 34′**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This section derives the four core equations** that examiners most commonly test. No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is annotated.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between LHA, GHA, and Longitude

**Given:**
- A celestial body is observed.
- Its **Greenwich Hour Angle (GHA)** is read from the Nautical Almanac for a particular GMT.
- The **observer's longitude** is $\lambda$.

**Find:** The Local Hour Angle (LHA) at the observer's meridian.

**Geometric Setup:**
- The Greenwich meridian is the **Prime Meridian (0°)** by definition.
- The observer's meridian is at longitude $\lambda$ measured **East or West**.
- The body's celestial meridian (passing through its GP) rotates **westward** at $15°$ per hour due to Earth's rotation.

**Step 1 — Define each quantity:**
- $\text{GHA}$ = angular distance from Greenwich meridian, measured **Westward**, to the body's meridian.
- $\text{LHA}$ = angular distance from the observer's meridian, measured **Westward**, to the body's meridian.
- $\lambda$ = observer's longitude (West positive by convention in NA).

**Step 2 — Express LHA geometrically:**
The body's meridian is reached from Greenwich by rotating West through GHA degrees.
The observer's meridian is reached from Greenwich by rotating West through $\lambda$ degrees.
Therefore, the body's meridian is **further West** than the observer's meridian by:

$$\text{LHA} = \text{GHA} - \lambda_W \quad \text{(West longitude)}$$

$$\text{LHA} = \text{GHA} + \lambda_E \quad \text{(East longitude)}$$

**Step 3 — Unify using signed convention** (East negative, West positive as used in almanac tables):

$$\text{LHA} = \text{GHA} - \lambda_{West+}$$

**Step 4 — Modulus operation for 360° wrap-around:**
If $\text{LHA} > 360°$, subtract $360°$.
If $\text{LHA} < 0°$, add $360°$.

$$\text{LHA} = \left(\text{GHA} - \lambda\right) \mod 360°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Example:** Sun's GHA = 045° 22.3′ at 09h GMT. Ship's longitude = 060° 30′ W.
> $\text{LHA} = 045° 22.3' - 060° 30' = -015° 07.7'$. Add 360° → **LHA = 344° 52.3′** (Sun east of observer).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Latitude by Meridian Passage (Noon Sight)

**Given:**
- The observed altitude of the Sun at the moment it crosses the observer's meridian (upper transit).
- The Sun's declination at that instant.

**Find:** The observer's latitude.

**Geometric Setup:**
At meridian passage, the Sun, observer's zenith, and the pole lie on the **same great circle** (the observer's meridian). The PZX triangle **collapses** into a single straight line:

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Pole P | At one extreme |
| Observer's Zenith Z | At the observer's latitude (Co-latitude $90° - L$ from Pole) |
| Sun's GP X | At declination $\delta$ from Equator, i.e., at Co-declination $90° - \delta$ from Pole |

**Step 1 — Express the position of Z from Pole:**
$\text{Arc PZ} = 90° - L$ (Co-latitude)

**Step 2 — Express the position of X from Pole:**
$\text{Arc PX} = 90° - \delta$ (Co-declination = Polar distance)

**Step 3 — Express the position of X from Z:**
$\text{Arc ZX} = z = 90° - h$ (Zenith distance)

**Step 4 — Assemble into a single line** (since all lie on the same meridian):
$$\text{PX} = \text{PZ} \pm \text{ZX}$$

The sign depends on whether the Sun and observer are on the **same side or opposite sides** of the zenith:

$$90° - \delta = (90° - L) \pm z$$

**Step 5 — Solve for L:**

$$\boxed{L = \delta \pm z \;\; (\text{same name as } \delta \text{ if } +)}$$

More precisely (KTU standard form):

$$\text{Latitude} = \text{Declination} \pm \text{Zenith Distance}$$

| Case | Sign | Latitude equals |
|------|------|-----------------|
| Dec North, observer North of Sun (Sun **south** of zenith at transit) | $\delta + z$ | Larger value |
| Dec North, observer South of Sun (Sun **north** of zenith at transit) | $\delta - z$ | Smaller value |
| Dec South, observer South of Sun (Sun **north** of zenith) | $-\delta + z$ | (with N/S reversal) |

**Step 6 — Final compact rule used at sea:**

$$\text{LAT} = \text{Dec} \pm \text{ZD}$$

> **Same name** (Lat and Dec both North, or both South) → **add ZD**.
> **Contrary name** → **subtract ZD**, then label result opposite to Dec.

**Step 7 — Worked Example:**
At LAN (Local Apparent Noon), Sun's observed altitude = 64° 12.6′. Sun's declination = 22° 18.4′ N. Find latitude.

$z = 90° - h = 90° - 64° 12.6' = 25° 47.4'$.

Apply rule: Lat and Dec both North → ADD.

$$\text{Lat} = 22° 18.4' + 25° 47.4' = 47° 65.8' = 48° 05.8' \text{ N}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Latitude by Polaris (North Star)

**Given:**
- Observed altitude of Polaris, $Ho$.
- LHA Aries (the First Point of Aries, $\Upsilon$) at time of observation.

**Find:** Latitude (Northern Hemisphere only).

**Geometric Setup:**
Polaris lies within $\approx 0.7°$ of the True North Celestial Pole. Therefore, the altitude of Polaris **approximately equals** the observer's latitude (a classical rule of thumb used for centuries).

**Step 1 — Approximation:**
$$\text{Lat}_{\text{approx}} = Ho_{\text{Polaris}}$$

**Step 2 — Refinement using Almanac corrections:**
The Nautical Almanac provides three correction terms:

| Term | Function | Source |
|------|----------|--------|
| $a_0$ | Base correction ≈ $-0° 59'$ | Almanac Polaris table |
| $a_1$ | LHA Aries correction | Almanac |
| $a_2$ | Latitude correction | Almanac |

**Step 3 — Assemble:**
$$\boxed{\text{Latitude} = -1° + Ho + a_0 + a_1 + a_2}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**
$Ho = 38° 24'$. LHA Aries = 245°. Assumed latitude = 38° N.

From almanac:
- $a_0 = 0° 58.8'$
- $a_1 = 0.7'$ (for LHA Aries 245°)
- $a_2 = 0.6'$ (for assumed lat 38°)

$$\text{Lat} = -1° + 38° 24.0' + 0° 58.8' + 0° 0.7' + 0° 0.6'$$
$$= 38° 23.1' \;\; \text{N}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Azimuth Rule (True Bearing of Celestial Body)

**Given:**
- Computed altitude $Hc$, declination $\delta$, latitude $L$, and LHA.

**Find:** True Azimuth $Zn$ of the body.

**Geometric Setup:**
Using the Time-Azimuth formula derived from the PZX triangle (cosine rule for the angle at Z):

$$\sin(Z) = \dfrac{\sin(\text{LHA}) \cos(\delta)}{\cos(h)}$$

But for tabular methods (HO 229 / NAO Tables), the rule reduces to a **suffix correction**:

| Observer's Latitude | Declination of Body | Azimuth Rule |
|---------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| **N** | **N** | $Zn = 360° - Z$ (use Suffix N) |
| **N** | **S** | $Zn = 180° + Z$ (use Suffix S) |
| **S** | **S** | $Zn = 180° - Z$ (use Suffix S) |
| **S** | **N** | $Zn = Z$ (use Suffix N) |

Where $Z$ is the **Azimuth Angle** (always between 0° and 180°) taken from the tables.

**Step 1 — Look up $Z$** from HO 229 / HO 249 for the assumed lat, declination, and LHA.

**Step 2 — Apply rule** from the table above to convert $Z$ → $Zn$.

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**
Lat = 40° N, Dec = 20° S, $Z$ from table = 128°.

Rule: Lat N + Dec S → $Zn = 180° + Z$ = $180° + 128° = 308°$.

---

## 3.5 The Intercept Method — Marcq St Hilaire (Most Tested Procedure)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 14-mark procedure is the single most-tested topic** in KTU ESE for Module 3.

**Steps:**
1. **Measure altitude** of body with sextant → $hs$ (sextant altitude).
2. **Apply altitude corrections** (dip, refraction, SD, parallax) → $Ho$ (observed altitude).
3. **Note GMT** of observation to nearest second.
4. **Look up GHA & Dec** of body from Nautical Almanac for that GMT.
5. **Calculate LHA** = GHA − Longitude (W) [apply Section 3.1 rule].
6. **Assume a position (AP)** — round off Lat to nearest whole degree, Long to nearest whole degree.
7. **Use HO 229 / NAO Tables** with AP-Latitude, AP-Longitude (= LHA), and Dec to find:
   - $Hc$ (computed altitude)
   - $Z$ (azimuth angle) → convert to $Zn$
8. **Compute Intercept:**
$$a = Ho - Hc$$
- If $a > 0$ → intercept is **Toward** (GP is closer than assumed; plot intercept *toward* azimuth).
- If $a < 0$ → intercept is **Away** (GP is further; plot intercept *away* from azimuth).
9. **Plot the LOP** on the Mercator chart: from AP, measure intercept distance along $Zn$; draw perpendicular line.
10. **Repeat for another body** → cross the two LOPs → **Running Fix**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse $Ho$ and $Hc$. Memorise: **O**bserved = **O**utside (what you saw); **C**omputed = **C**alculated (what the tables said). Intercept **Ho − Hc**. Sign convention: Toward (T) means positive; Away (A) means negative. Drawing the LOP on the **wrong side** loses **3 marks** instantly.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the term *Line of Position (LOP)*. Explain how two celestial observations taken at different times can give a *running fix*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Line of Position (LOP)** is a locus on the surface of the Earth on which the observer's position lies, derived from a single observation of a celestial body's altitude. It is **not a single point** but an infinite line of possible positions.

When **two observations** are made — one at time $T_1$ and another at time $T_2$ — two LOPs are obtained. Because the observer's vessel has moved between the two observations by a known **run (distance)** and **course**, the first LOP must be **advanced** along the course by the run distance using the *Running Fix* technique. The advanced LOP and the second LOP intersect to produce a **fix** — a single point representing the observer's position at time $T_2$.

(2 marks for LOP definition + diagram; 1 mark for running fix explanation.)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between *Local Hour Angle (LHA)* and *Greenwich Hour Angle (GHA)*. State the conversion formula.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | GHA | LHA |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Reference Meridian | Greenwich (0°) | Observer's Meridian |
| Symbol | GHA | LHA |
| Almanac Source | Direct daily page value | Derived |
| Range | 0° to 360° | 0° to 360° |

**Conversion formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{LHA} = \text{GHA} - \lambda_W \quad \text{or} \quad \text{LHA} = \text{GHA} + \lambda_E}$$

where $\lambda_W$ is West longitude and $\lambda_E$ is East longitude. Apply modulus 360°.

(1 mark each for difference + 1 mark for formula.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Choice Format (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Astronomical Triangle (PZX)** used in celestial navigation. Draw a labelled diagram and state the cosine formula used to solve it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ship on voyage observes the Sun at Upper Transit (LAN). The sextant altitude is $58° 12.6'$, Index Error = $+1.4'$, Height of Eye = 12 m. Sun's declination at LAN = $22° 18.4'$ N. Find the ship's latitude.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The **Astronomical Triangle** is a spherical triangle drawn on the celestial sphere whose three vertices are:

1. **P** — The Elevated Pole (N for N. Hemisphere observer)
2. **Z** — The Observer's Zenith
3. **X** — The Geographical Position (GP) of the celestial body

**Sides of the triangle:**

| Side | Length | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| PZ | $90° - L$ | Co-latitude |
| PX | $90° - \delta$ | Polar distance |
| ZX | $z = 90° - h$ | Zenith distance |

**Angles:**
- At P = **LHA** (Local Hour Angle)
- At Z = **Azimuth Angle (Z)**
- At X (not directly used)

**Cosine Formula (fundamental):**

$$\cos(z) = \sin(L)\sin(\delta) + \cos(L)\cos(\delta)\cos(\text{LHA})$$

From this, **Altitude** is recovered: $h = 90° - z$.

[3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for sides definition + 2 marks for cosine formula statement.]

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Correct Sextant Altitude for Index Error:**

$hs_{\text{corr}} = 58° 12.6' - (+1.4') = 58° 11.2'$

**Step 2 — Apply Dip Correction** (Height of eye 12 m → Dip ≈ 6.5'):

$ha_{\text{app}} = 58° 11.2' - 6.5' = 58° 04.7'$

**Step 3 — Apply Refraction Correction (Sun lower limb assumed)** ≈ − 0.8':

$ho \approx 58° 03.9'$

(Sun upper/lower limb not explicitly stated → assume lower limb, add Semi-diameter ≈ +15.9' if needed.)

For brevity, take final $Ho \approx 58° 04'$.

**Step 4 — Compute Zenith Distance:**

$$z = 90° - Ho = 90° - 58° 04' = 31° 56'$$

**Step 5 — Apply Latitude Formula (Meridian Passage):**

Since Dec is North and the vessel is in the Northern Hemisphere (assumed from altitude value):

$$\boxed{\text{Latitude} = \text{Declination} + \text{Zenith Distance}}$$

$$\text{Lat} = 22° 18.4' + 31° 56' = 54° 14.4' \text{ N}$$

[1 mark IE correction + 1 mark Dip + 1 mark Refn + 2 marks ZD calculation + 2 marks final latitude — awarding partial marks for working shown.]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **Marcq St Hilaire Intercept Method** for finding a ship's position by celestial observation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ship in DR position 40° 12' N, 060° 18' W observes the Sun at GMT 09h 14m 32s. Sextant altitude of Sun (lower limb) = 38° 24.0', Index Error = − 2.0', Height of Eye = 8 m. The Nautical Almanac gives for 09h GMT: Sun's GHA = 045° 22.3', Dec = 22° 18.4' N (d = +0.6'/h). Find the **Intercept and True Azimuth** using HO 229 (Assumed Position: Lat 40° N, Long 060° W).

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The **Marcq St Hilaire Method** (also called the *Intercept Method*) is the modern, universal procedure used by navigators worldwide. Steps:

1. **Observe altitude** with marine sextant → $hs$.
2. **Apply corrections** (IE, Dip, Refraction, SD, Parallax) → obtain **observed altitude $Ho$**.
3. **Note GMT** accurately.
4. **Extract from Nautical Almanac**: GHA and Dec of body.
5. **Compute LHA** = GHA − λ(W) [+ 360° if negative].
6. **Assume a Position (AP)** — round off to whole degrees nearest DR position.
7. **Enter Sight Reduction Tables (HO 229)** with AP-Lat, AP-Long (= LHA), Dec → read $Hc$ and $Z$.
8. **Compute Intercept** $a = Ho - Hc$.
   - If $a > 0$ → **Toward** (plot from AP toward body's azimuth).
   - If $a < 0$ → **Away** (plot from AP away from body's azimuth).
9. **Convert $Z$ to $Zn$** using latitude/dec name rule.
10. **Plot LOP** — from AP, lay off intercept distance along $Zn$; draw perpendicular line — this is the LOP.

[3 marks diagram + 4 marks step-by-step procedure.]

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Index Error & Dip Correction:**

$hs = 38° 24.0'$, IE = − 2.0' (subtract, since IE is "− on arc" → add). Wait: IE convention. With IE = − 2.0', the sextant reads **less** than true → **add** 2.0'.

$hs_{\text{corr}} = 38° 24.0' + 2.0' = 38° 26.0'$

**Step 2 — Dip (Height of Eye 8 m → Dip ≈ 5.3'):**

$ha = 38° 26.0' - 5.3' = 38° 20.7'$

**Step 3 — Sun's Lower Limb — Refraction (~ − 1.4') + SD (~ + 15.9'):**

$Ho = 38° 20.7' - 1.4' + 15.9' = 38° 35.2'$

**Step 4 — Adjust Dec for time (d = +0.6'/h, Δt = 14m 32s ≈ 0.24 h):**

$\Delta\delta = 0.6 \times 0.24 \approx +0.1'$

$\text{Dec} = 22° 18.4' + 0.1' = 22° 18.5'$ N

**Step 5 — Compute LHA:**

$\text{LHA} = \text{GHA} - \lambda_W = 045° 22.3' - 060° 18' = -014° 55.7'$

Add 360°: $\text{LHA} = 345° 04.3'$

**Step 6 — From HO 229** with Lat = 40° N, LHA = 345°, Dec = 22° N (same name):

- $Hc = 38° 12'$
- $Z = 128.0°$ (Suffix S, since Lat N + Dec N)

**Step 7 — Intercept:**

$$a = Ho - Hc = 38° 35.2' - 38° 12.0' = +23.2'$$

$a > 0$ → **Toward**. Convert 23.2' → 23.2 nautical miles.

**Step 8 — Convert Z to Zn:**

Lat N, Dec N → rule: $Zn = 360° - Z$:

$$Zn = 360° - 128.0° = 232.0°$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\text{Intercept} = 23.2' \text{ TOWARD, True Azimuth} = 232.0°}$$

Plot from AP (40° N, 060° W) along bearing 232° for 23.2 nm, draw perpendicular LOP. Repeat for another body to cross-fix.

[1 mark IE+1 mark Dip+1 mark Refn+SD+1 mark LHA+1 mark HO 229 read+1 mark Intercept+1 mark Zn — partial marks for working shown.]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Ho and Hc** — students write $Hc - Ho$ instead of $Ho - Hc$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to add 360°** when LHA goes negative. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Wrong Sign of Index Error** — IE "+" subtracts; IE "−" adds. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Plotting intercept on wrong side** (Away when Toward was correct). **Loss: 3 marks — heaviest penalty.**
> 5. **Dec name reversal in Noon Sight** — students fail to flip N↔S when Dec and Lat are contrary. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 6. **Time conversion slip** — Zone Time vs GMT confusion. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — must be memorised before walking into the exam hall:**

- ☑ **PZX Triangle** is the master triangle; every sight reduction solves it.
- ☑ Three sides: **PZ = 90° − L**, **PX = 90° − δ**, **ZX = 90° − h**.
- ☑ Angle at P is **LHA**, angle at Z is **Azimuth Angle Z**, angle at X is unused directly.
- ☑ **LHA = GHA − λ(W)**, with **+360°** wrap-around.
- ☑ **Intercept a = Ho − Hc**; sign of $a$ → Toward (+ve) or Away (−ve).
- ☑ **Noon Latitude: Lat = Dec ± ZD** — same name add, contrary name subtract.
- ☑ **Polaris Latitude: Lat = −1° + Ho + a₀ + a₁ + a₂.**
- ☑ **Azimuth Rule:** Lat N + Dec N → Zn = 360° − Z; Lat N + Dec S → Zn = 180° + Z; Lat S + Dec S → Zn = 180° − Z; Lat S + Dec N → Zn = Z.
- ☑ **Standard corrections** to sextant altitude: IE, Dip, Refraction (always − 34′ at horizon), Semi-diameter, Parallax.
- ☑ **GMT ↔ Zone Time**: Zone Time = GMT ± Zone Number (East +, West −).
- ☑ **GMT ↔ LMT**: LMT = GMT ± λ/15 (East +, West −).
- ☑ **Equation of Time**: difference between apparent Sun and mean Sun (max ≈ +16 min in Nov).
- ☑ **Stars' GHA** = GHA Aries + SHA of Star (read from almanac daily page).
- ☑ **Amplitude** (sunrise/sunset bearing) used for compass error correction; formula: $\sin A = \sin\delta / \cos L$.
- ☑ **Time-Azimuth** used when body is near meridian (where altitude method fails): $Z = \tan^{-1}[\sin\text{LHA} / (\cos L \tan \delta - \sin L \cos\text{LHA})]$.
- ☑ Always quote latitude and longitude with correct hemisphere suffix **N/S/E/W** in the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Navigation Systems (ECDIS, Radar)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electronic Navigation Systems (ECDIS, Radar)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electronic Navigation Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Electronic Navigation Systems* are computer-based, sensor-integrated aids that determine the position, course, speed, and surrounding environment of a vessel using electromagnetic (radio/microwave) and electronic data processing techniques. In the maritime domain, the two principal IMO-mandated electronic navigation systems are **ECDIS** (Electronic Chart Display and Information System) and **Marine Radar** (Radio Detection and Ranging).

For KTU 2024 Scheme Maritime Safety (OESFT727), Module 3 focuses on:

- **ECDIS** — vector chart-based situational awareness under **SOLAS Chapter V, Regulation 19.2.10**
- **Marine Radar (X-Band & S-Band)** — under **SOLAS Chapter V, Regulation 19.2.3**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Eyes of the Ship"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine driving a car at night in a city you have never visited. You have **two essential tools**: (1) a **GPS map on a tablet** that shows where you are, where the roads go, and warns about one-way streets (this is **ECDIS**), and (2) a **headlight beam** that reveals obstacles, other cars, and the edge of the road in real time, even around blind corners (this is **RADAR**). Neither alone is sufficient — the map tells you *where danger is supposed to be*, while the radar tells you *where danger actually is right now*.

**Working Analogy Mapping:**

| Real World | Maritime Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| GPS map (Google Maps) | ECDIS | Static & dynamic charted info |
| Headlights | Radar pulse (magnetron) | Real-time object detection |
| Dashboard camera | ARPA (Automatic Radar Plotting Aid) | Tracking targets |
| Street signs (charted) | ENC (Electronic Navigational Chart) | Official vector data |
| Roadside warnings | AIS overlay | Cooperative identification |

---

### 1.3 IMO Regulatory Framework & Key Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key IMO/IALA Standards (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **SOLAS Ch. V Reg. 19** — Carriage requirements for shipborne navigational systems
> - **IMO Resolution A.817(19)** — Performance standards for ECDIS (updated by MSC.232(82))
> - **IMO Resolution A.477(XII)** — Performance standards for radar (updated by MSC.192(79))
> - **IEC 61174** — ECDIS operational/performance testing
> - **IEC 62388** — Marine radar performance standard
> - **IHO S-57** — ENC (Electronic Navigational Chart) data format
> - **IHO S-52** — Chart presentation library
> - **IHO S-63** — ENC data protection scheme

**Physical & Operational Constants (Memorize for Numerical Problems):**

- Speed of electromagnetic wave in free space: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Speed of EM wave in air (seawater surface): **v ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Radar frequency bands: **X-Band = 9.2 – 9.5 GHz** (λ ≈ **3 cm**); **S-Band = 2.9 – 3.1 GHz** (λ ≈ **10 cm**)
- RADAR Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF): typically **500 – 4000 Hz**
- Standard radar range scales (NM): **0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.5, 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, 96**
- Mandatory radar update rate: **every 2.5 seconds** for relative motion, **every 3 seconds** for true motion (MSC.192(79))

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Marine Radar — Operational Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (IMO MSC.192(79)):**
> A marine radar is a **pulse-echo, primary surveillance system** that transmits microwave electromagnetic energy and detects the echoes returned from targets (other vessels, landmasses, buoys, precipitation). It provides range, bearing, and relative motion information independent of cooperative transmissions from other vessels.

#### 2.1.1 Working Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1 — Pulse Generation:** The **magnetron** (transmitter) generates a high-power microwave pulse (typically **25 kW** peak power for X-band, **30 kW** for S-band) lasting **0.05 – 2 μs**.
- **Step 2 — Transmission:** The pulse travels through the **waveguide** to the **slotted waveguide array antenna**, which rotates at **24 RPM** (or **48 RPM** for high-speed craft).
- **Step 3 — Propagation:** The EM wave travels at the speed of light (**c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**) until it strikes a target.
- **Step 4 — Reflection:** A small portion of the energy is backscattered toward the antenna (radar cross-section dependent).
- **Step 5 — Reception:** The duplexer (TR switch) routes the returning echo to the **low-noise receiver**.
- **Step 6 — Display:** The echo is processed, digitized, and displayed on a **PPI (Plan Position Indicator)** with the ship at the center.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Bands?**
> - **X-Band (3 cm, ~9.4 GHz):** Higher resolution, better for **small targets in calm weather**, but heavily attenuated by **heavy rain** (rain clutter).
> - **S-Band (10 cm, ~3.05 GHz):** Penetrates rain/haze; better for **long-range detection** and in **tropical squalls**, but poorer resolution.

#### 2.1.2 Radar Performance Factors

The **Maximum Range** of a marine radar is governed by:

- **Line-of-Sight (geometrical horizon)** — dominant on a curved Earth
- **Radar horizon** — adjusted for atmospheric refraction (4/3 Earth radius model)
