- **Pulse power and antenna gain**
- **Target Radar Cross-Section (RCS, σ)** measured in m²
- **Receiver sensitivity (minimum discernible signal)**
- **Clutter and sea state**

---

### 2.2 ECDIS — Operational Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (IMO MSC.232(82)):**
> ECDIS is a **computer-based navigation information system** that displays integrated information from **Electronic Navigational Charts (ENC)** and **position, heading, and speed sensors** to assist the mariner in route planning, route monitoring, and position fixing. It may be accepted as **up-to-date paper chart equivalent** when fully compliant.

#### 2.2.1 Working Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1 — Data Input:** ECDIS receives real-time inputs from **GNSS (GPS/GLONASS)**, **Gyrocompass**, **LOG (speed)**, **AIS**, and **Echo Sounder**.
- **Step 2 — ENC Loading:** Official vector charts (IHO S-57 format) are loaded from a **DVD, USB, or satellite/terrestrial update service** (e.g., ChartCo, Admiralty Vector Chart Service — AVCS).
- **Step 3 — Chart Display:** The IHO S-52 presentation library renders ENC objects (depth contours, buoys, wrecks) with correct symbology.
- **Step 4 — Position Plotting:** The own-ship position is overlaid on the chart at the **WGS-84 datum**.
- **Step 5 — Alarm Generation:** ECDIS continuously checks vessel position against **Safety Depth, Safety Contour, and Prohibited Areas**, triggering **IMO-mandated alarms**.
- **Step 6 — Route Monitoring:** Predicted position is computed and displayed at user-set intervals (1 min, 5 min).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ECDIS Is a Legal Chart Equivalent:**
> When operating on **up-to-date ENCs** with a **Type-approved ECDIS** (backed up by a second independent ECDIS or paper charts), the system legally satisfies the SOLAS chart carriage requirement.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Radar & ECDIS Key Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Numerical Problems — Memorize All**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{round trip}} &= \frac{2R}{c} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R$ = target range (m), $c$ = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range (NM)} &= \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2 \times 1852} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta t$ is pulse round-trip time in seconds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\max, \text{geometric}} &= 2.23 \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $h_1, h_2$ are antenna heights in **meters**, $R_{\max}$ in **nautical miles (NM)**. (This is the standard 4/3 Earth radius model.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PRF}_{\max} &= \frac{c}{2 \cdot R_{\max, \text{desired}}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pulse repetition interval (PRI)} &= \frac{1}{\text{PRF}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Duty cycle (DC)} &= \text{Pulse width (\tau)} \times \text{PRF} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average power } P_{\text{avg}} &= P_{\text{peak}} \times \text{DC} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Beamwidth (deg)} &\approx \frac{70 \cdot \lambda}{D} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda$ = wavelength, $D$ = antenna horizontal aperture (meters).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Longitudinal resolution } \Delta R &= \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bearing resolution} &\approx \frac{70 \cdot \lambda}{D_{\text{azimuth}}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mandatory Alarms from IMO MSC.232(82) — ECDIS Alarm Table:**

| Alarm | Trigger Condition | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| Cross Safety Contour | Predicted position crosses Safety Contour | Alarm |
| Deviation from Route | Cross-track distance > user set limit | Warning |
| Position Fix Lost | No position update for > 30 s (default) | Alarm |
| Approach to Critical Point | Time/distance to next waypoint below limit | Warning |
| Chart Datum Mismatch | Datum of ENC ≠ system datum (WGS-84) | Alarm |
| ENC Not Up-to-Date | Permit expired beyond threshold | Alarm |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Range Equation for Pulse Radar

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A marine X-band radar transmits a pulse of width $\tau = 0.5\,\mu s$ and detects an echo after $\Delta t = 123.4\,\mu s$. Compute (a) target range in meters, (b) range in nautical miles, (c) verify against the maximum unambiguous range of the radar whose PRF is 1200 Hz.

#### Step 1 — Recall the round-trip time equation

The EM wave travels outward from the antenna, reflects off the target, and returns. The total path is $2R$.

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2R}{c}
$$

#### Step 2 — Solve algebraically for R

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$
R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \,\text{m/s} \times 123.4 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{s}}{2}
$$

$$
R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 123.4 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \,\text{m}
$$

**Explanation of conversion logic:** The factor $10^{-6}$ comes from microseconds ($\mu$s), converting to seconds for dimensional consistency with m/s. The factor of 2 in the denominator accounts for the round-trip path (outbound + return).

#### Step 4 — Simplify

$$
R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 1.234 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = \frac{3.702 \times 10^{4}}{2}
$$

$$
R = 18{,}510 \,\text{m} = 18.51 \,\text{km}
$$

#### Step 5 — Convert to nautical miles (KTU convention)

Since 1 NM = 1852 m:

$$
R_{\text{NM}} = \frac{18{,}510}{1852} \approx 9.99 \,\text{NM} \approx 10 \,\text{NM}
$$

#### Step 6 — Compute Maximum Unambiguous Range from PRF

The maximum unambiguous range is reached when a new pulse is transmitted just as the previous echo returns:

$$
R_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1200} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2400}
$$

$$
R_{\max} = 1.25 \times 10^5 \,\text{m} = 125 \,\text{km} = 67.5 \,\text{NM}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since 10 NM $\ll$ 67.5 NM, the detected target is unambiguous (no second-time-around echo).

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Radar Horizon

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A radar antenna is mounted at a height of $h_1 = 25$ m above sea level. A target ship's mast is at height $h_2 = 16$ m. Determine the maximum radar detection range assuming standard atmospheric refraction (4/3 Earth radius model).

#### Step 1 — Apply the empirical radar horizon equation

$$
R_{\max, \text{NM}} = 2.23 \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right)
$$

where $h_1, h_2$ are in **meters**.

#### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
R_{\max} = 2.23 \left(\sqrt{25} + \sqrt{16}\right)
$$

#### Step 3 — Evaluate the square roots

$$
\sqrt{25} = 5, \quad \sqrt{16} = 4
$$

#### Step 4 — Sum and multiply

$$
R_{\max} = 2.23 \times (5 + 4) = 2.23 \times 9
$$

$$
R_{\max} = 20.07 \,\text{NM}
$$

#### Step 5 — Compare with the optical horizon (no refraction)

For verification, the optical horizon (straight-line, no refraction) is $R \approx 2.08 \sqrt{h}$ NM:

$$
R_{\text{optical}} = 2.08(\sqrt{25} + \sqrt{16}) = 2.08 \times 9 = 18.72 \,\text{NM}
$$

The 4/3 Earth model extends detection by $\approx 7.3\%$ — a significant navigational margin.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Longitudinal (Range) Resolution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A marine radar uses a pulse width of $\tau = 0.08\,\mu s$. Determine the minimum range at which two targets along the same bearing can be resolved.

#### Step 1 — Apply the longitudinal resolution equation

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}
$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
\Delta R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \,\text{m/s} \times 0.08 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{s}}{2}
$$

#### Step 3 — Simplify

$$
\Delta R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 8 \times 10^{-8}}{2} = \frac{24}{2} = 12 \,\text{m}
$$

**Engineering implication:** Two targets along the same bearing separated by less than 12 m will merge into a single echo on the PPI. For navigational targets (ships hundreds of meters long), this is more than sufficient.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Radar vs ECDIS Comparison (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is heavily tested in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions — memorize the distinctions.**

| Parameter | Marine Radar | ECDIS |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Principle | Active pulse-echo (microwave) | Computer-based data integration |
| Sensor Type | Primary sensor (own transmission) | Secondary sensor (data fusion) |
| Data Source | Real-time backscatter | ENC (vector charts) + GNSS |
| Coverage Range | Line-of-sight (~24 NM practical) | Unlimited (depends on ENC coverage) |
| Detects | Targets not on chart | Charted hazards + own position |
| Legal Status | Mandatory under SOLAS V/19.2.3 | Mandatory under SOLAS V/19.2.10 |
| IMO Standard | MSC.192(79) | MSC.232(82) |
| IHO Standard | N/A | S-57, S-52, S-63 |
| Display Type | PPI (Plan Position Indicator) | Vector chart overlay |
| Cooperative Identification | Yes (via AIS overlay) | No (uses AIS data only) |
| Works in Fog/Darkness | Yes | Yes (it's a chart, not a sensor) |
| Failure Mode | Clutter, sea return, shadow zones | GPS outage, ENC absence |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Marine Radar System — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Timing Pulse Generator] --> node2[Magnetron Transmitter]
    node2 --> node3[Duplexer TR Switch]
    node3 --> node4[Slotted Waveguide Antenna]
    node4 --> node5[Free Space Microwave Propagation]
    node5 --> node6[Target Ship Land Buoy]
    node6 --> node5
    node5 --> node4
    node4 --> node3
    node3 --> node7[Low Noise RF Receiver]
    node7 --> node8[Signal Processor MTI]
    node8 --> node9[ARPA Tracking]
    node9 --> node10[PPI Display 30 cm CRT or LCD]
    node1 --> node11[Antenna Rotation Drive 24 RPM]
    node11 --> node4
    node10 --> node12[Bridge Watch Officer]
    node9 --> node13[AIS Interface]
    node13 --> node10
```

### 4.2 ECDIS System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[GNSS GPS Receiver] --> nodeD[ECDIS Central Processor]
    nodeB[Gyrocompass Heading] --> nodeD
    nodeC[Speed Log] --> nodeD
    nodeE[Echo Sounder] --> nodeD
    nodeF[AIS Transponder] --> nodeD
    nodeG[ENC Database IHO S57] --> nodeD
    nodeH[Update Service AVCS or Primar] --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeI[Chart Display IHO S52]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Route Planning Module]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Route Monitoring Module]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Alarm Management]
    nodeI --> nodeM[Integrated Bridge Display]
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeN[Bridge Watch Officer Alarm Panel]
```

### 4.3 Radar Pulse Train — Timing Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[TX Pulse width tau] --> nodeP2[Receiver Listening Period]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Next TX Pulse]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Receiver Listening Period]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Echo Return at time delta t]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[Next TX Pulse]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP7[Range R equals c delta t divided by 2]
```

### 4.4 ECDIS Data Flow — Acquisition to Display

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Hydrographic Office Source Data] --> nodeS2[IHO S57 ENC Production]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[ENC Distribution with S63 Protection]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Onboard ECDIS Loading]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[S52 Presentation Library Rendering]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Vector Chart Display on Bridge]
    nodeS7[Sensor Fusion GPS Gyro Log] --> nodeS6
    nodeS8[AIS Targets Overlay] --> nodeS6
    nodeS9[Periodic Updates Weekly] --> nodeS4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes:** All node identifiers follow the alphanumeric-prefix rule (node1, nodeA, etc.). All labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes. No reserved keywords or markdown formatting inside labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

---

**Q1. [3 Marks] — Define ECDIS. State the IMO and IHO performance standards that govern it.**

**Model Answer:**

ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display and Information System) is defined under **IMO Resolution MSC.232(82)** as a computer-based navigation information system that displays integrated information from electronic navigational charts (ENC) and position, heading and speed sensors to assist the mariner in route planning, route monitoring, and position fixing.

**Governing Standards:**
- **IMO MSC.232(82)** — Performance standards for ECDIS
- **IHO S-57** — ENC (Electronic Navigational Chart) data transfer standard
- **IHO S-52** — Chart content and display aspects (presentation library)
- **IHO S-63** — ENC data protection scheme (security)
- **IEC 61174** — Type-approval testing standard
- **SOLAS Chapter V, Regulation 19.2.10** — Carriage requirement

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] — Differentiate between X-band and S-band marine radars. State one application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | X-Band | S-Band |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 9.2 – 9.5 GHz | 2.9 – 3.1 GHz |
| Wavelength | 3 cm | 10 cm |
| Beamwidth | Narrow (good resolution) | Wider (poorer resolution) |
| Rain Penetration | Poor (heavy rain clutter) | Excellent |
| Target Detection | Small targets, buoys | Large targets in rain |
| Preferred Use | Calm weather, pilotage, harbor | Tropical squalls, offshore |

**Application:** X-band is preferred during **harbor pilotage and coastal navigation in clear weather** due to superior target resolution. S-band is preferred during **ocean passages in heavy rain or fog** due to better wave penetration.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, KTU Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 3, ESE Pattern with Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled block diagram, explain the working principle of a marine pulse radar system. State the function of each block.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** A marine radar operates on the **pulse-echo principle**. The transmitter emits a short, high-power microwave pulse. The pulse travels to the target, is partially reflected, and returns as a weak echo. The time delay between transmission and reception is used to compute target range.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1) — Functional Description:**

- **Timing Pulse Generator / Modulator:** Generates the trigger pulses that synchronize the transmitter and define the Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF). Also determines the pulse width $\tau$ (typically 0.05 – 2 μs).
- **Magnetron Transmitter:** Produces high-power microwave RF pulses, typically 25 kW (X-band) or 30 kW (S-band) peak power, at the carrier frequency.
- **Duplexer (TR Switch):** A gas-filled or ferrite device that routes the transmitted pulse to the antenna while protecting the sensitive receiver from the high-power transmit pulse. During reception, it routes the echo to the receiver.
- **Slotted Waveguide Antenna:** A horizontally-oriented array that produces a narrow azimuth beam (~1° for a 2 m antenna at X-band). Rotates at 24 RPM.
- **Free Space Propagation:** Microwave energy travels at $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Target Backscatter:** The target reflects a fraction of the incident energy proportional to its Radar Cross-Section (RCS).
- **Low-Noise Receiver (LNA + Mixer):** Amplifies the weak return echo and downconverts to IF for processing.
- **Signal Processor (MTI / CFAR):** Moving Target Indication cancels stationary sea/rain clutter; Constant False Alarm Rate maintains consistent detection in noise.
- **ARPA Tracker:** Automatically acquires and tracks up to 40 targets, computing CPA (Closest Point of Approach), TCPA (Time to CPA), course, and speed.
- **PPI Display:** A circular cathode-ray or LCD display with the ship at center. Range rings and bearing scale allow direct reading.

**Range Equation Derivation:**

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

For $\Delta t = 200\,\mu s$, $R = 30$ km = 16.2 NM.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A marine radar has a magnetron with peak power 25 kW, pulse width 0.4 μs, and PRF 1500 Hz. Compute (i) duty cycle, (ii) average power, (iii) maximum unambiguous range, (iv) longitudinal resolution. Assume c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Duty Cycle:**

$$
DC = \tau \times \text{PRF} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 1500 = 6 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Equivalently, $DC = 0.0006$ or **0.06%**.

**(ii) Average Power:**

$$
P_{\text{avg}} = P_{\text{peak}} \times DC = 25{,}000 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 15 \,\text{W}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Unambiguous Range:**

$$
R_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1500} = 1 \times 10^5 \,\text{m} = 100 \,\text{km} \approx 54 \,\text{NM}
$$

**(iv) Longitudinal Resolution:**

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 60 \,\text{m}
$$

**Conclusion:** The radar resolves two targets on the same bearing only if separated by **more than 60 m** in range.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of an ECDIS system. List six IMO-mandated alarms that ECDIS must generate.

**Model Solution:**

**ECDIS Architecture (refer Section 4.2):**

ECDIS consists of **four major subsystems**:

- **Data Acquisition Subsystem:** Receives inputs from GNSS, gyrocompass, speed log, echo sounder, and AIS.
- **Chart Database Subsystem:** Holds ENCs (IHO S-57 format) and updates (IHO S-63 protected).
- **Processing Subsystem:** A dual-redundant computer running type-approved ECDIS software; applies the IHO S-52 presentation library.
- **Display & Human-Machine Interface Subsystem:** Bridge-mounted color display with trackball/mouse, keyboard, and alarm panel.

**Six IMO-Mandated Alarms (MSC.232(82)):**

1. **Cross Safety Contour** — predicted position crosses the user-set Safety Contour.
2. **Approach to Critical Point** — vessel is within the user-defined time/distance of the next wheelover or waypoint.
3. **Position Fix Lost** — no position update from GNSS for more than 30 seconds (default).
4. **Crossing a Safety Depth Area** — vessel is about to enter a depth below Safety Depth.
5. **Deviation from Route** — cross-track error exceeds user limit.
6. **Chart Datum Mismatch / ENC Not Up-to-Date** — chart datum is not WGS-84 or the ENC permit has expired.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** The radar antenna of a ship is mounted at 28 m above sea level. A target is detected at a range of 22 NM. Calculate (i) the minimum height of the target's reflecting surface above sea level, and (ii) the round-trip time of the radar pulse to this target.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Minimum Target Height:**

Using the radar horizon equation:

$$
R_{\max} = 2.23 \left(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}\right)
$$

Substitute $R_{\max} = 22$ NM, $h_1 = 28$ m:

$$
22 = 2.23 \left(\sqrt{28} + \sqrt{h_2}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{22}{2.23} = \sqrt{28} + \sqrt{h_2}
$$

$$
9.865 = 5.292 + \sqrt{h_2}
$$

$$
\sqrt{h_2} = 9.865 - 5.292 = 4.573
$$

$$
h_2 = (4.573)^2 = 20.91 \,\text{m}
$$

**Therefore, the minimum target height ≈ 20.9 m.**

**(ii) Round-Trip Time:**

Convert range to meters: $R = 22 \times 1852 = 40{,}744$ m.

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{2 \times 40{,}744}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{81{,}488}{3 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
\Delta t = 2.716 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{s} = 271.6 \,\mu s
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the range equation $\Delta t = 2R/c$. Many students write $R = c \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$. This loses **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up NM and km.** The radar horizon equation expects NM and meters directly. Do not convert $h$ to km.
> 3. **Confusing X-band and S-band** in advantages/disadvantages questions. Memorize wavelength, rain penetration, and resolution.
> 4. **Missing ECDIS alarms.** KTU often asks "List six alarms" — students list only 3–4. Memorize all six from MSC.232(82).
> 5. **Confusing IHO S-57 with S-52.** S-57 = data format; S-52 = presentation; S-63 = protection. This is a **favourite 2-mark trap**.
> 6. **Forgetting to state the SOLAS regulation** when asked about ECDIS/Radar carriage. Always cite **SOLAS V/19**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Electronic Navigation Systems**

**Radar — Must-Know:**

- Radar = **R**adio **D**etection **A**nd **R**anging
- Pulse-echo principle; range from $\Delta t = 2R/c$
- Magnetron = transmitter; Duplexer = TR switch
- X-band: 9.4 GHz, λ = 3 cm, better resolution, poor in rain
- S-band: 3.05 GHz, λ = 10 cm, penetrates rain, used offshore
- Radar horizon: $R_{\text{NM}} = 2.23(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2})$
- PRF determines $R_{\max}$; range resolution from pulse width
- ARPA tracks targets, computes CPA/TCPA
- SOLAS V/19.2.3 carriage; IMO MSC.192(79) standard
- Antenna rotation: 24 RPM standard, 48 RPM HSC

**ECDIS — Must-Know:**

- ECDIS = Electronic Chart Display and Information System
- Three layered IHO standards: **S-57 (data), S-52 (display), S-63 (security)**
- Type-approved ECDIS = legal chart equivalent
- Mandatory dual system: Primary + Backup (second ECDIS or paper charts)
- Position source: DGPS/DGNSS; Datum: WGS-84
- Route planning + Route monitoring = core functions
- Six mandatory alarms (MSC.232(82)): Safety Contour, Critical Point, Position Lost, Safety Depth, XTE, Datum/Update
- AIS overlay shows cooperative targets; radar overlay shows raw targets
- SOLAS V/19.2.10 carriage; IMO MSC.232(82) standard

**Numerical Constants (Always Carry in Memory):**

- c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s
- 1 NM = 1852 m
- Radar horizon constant = 2.23 (4/3 Earth radius)
- Optical horizon constant = 2.08 (geometric)
- Beamwidth ≈ 70λ / D (degrees)

**Integration Note for KTU:**

Radar and ECDIS are **complementary, not redundant**. ECDIS answers *"Where am I and where is charted danger?"* Radar answers *"What is around me right now?"* KTU often frames this as a 6–8 mark discussion question — emphasize the **fusion** in modern Integrated Bridge Systems (IBS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chartwork and Publications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chartwork and Publications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chartwork** is the professional discipline within nautical navigation involving the systematic selection, interpretation, correction, and utilization of nautical charts and associated marine publications to plan, execute, and monitor a vessel's safe passage from one position to another across the world's oceans and waterways.

A **Nautical Chart** is a specialized graphical representation of a maritime area and adjacent coastal regions, depicting depths of water (bathymetry), coastal outlines, navigational hazards, aids to navigation (buoys, beacons, lighthouses), magnetic variation, and tidal information. It serves as the mariner's primary legal and operational document for voyage planning under the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** Chapter V Regulation 27.

**Navigational Publications** are the official printed and digital reference documents issued by national hydrographic authorities (such as the UKHO - United Kingdom Hydrographic Office, NOAA, NHO India) that supplement the chart by providing textual, tabular, and descriptive information impossible to include graphically on a chart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Chartwork and Publications is treated as a foundational pillar of the Maritime Safety course. Mastery of Mercator sailing, parallel sailing, Mercator projection mathematics, and publication cross-referencing is mandatory for both Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Delhi in an unfamiliar territory. What would you do?
- You would buy a **road atlas** that shows every highway, lane, junction, and landmark.
- You would refer to a **rule book** listing speed limits, no-entry zones, and toll procedures.
- You would check a **distance-time calculator** to estimate fuel and arrival.

**Chartwork and Publications is the maritime equivalent of these three tools combined:**

| Terrestrial Driving Analogy | Maritime Equivalent |
|------------------------------|----------------------|
| Road atlas / Google Maps | **Nautical Chart** |
| Highway Code (rule book) | **Sailing Directions / Light Lists** |
| Distance-time calculator | **Tide Tables / Almanac** |

The key difference is **gravity of consequence**: a wrong turn on land costs 30 minutes; a wrong chart interpretation at sea can result in **grounding, collision, or total loss of vessel and crew**. This is why every chart feature, every symbol, and every publication is governed by international standards set by the **International Hydrographic Organization (IHO)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values are universally referenced in chartwork:

- **Mean Radius of the Earth (R) = 3,440 Nautical Miles (NM)** ≈ 6,371 km
- **1 Nautical Mile (NM)** = 1 minute of latitude = 1,852 metres (international standard)
- **1 Nautical Mile = 1.852 km**
- **1 Knot** = 1 NM per hour (vessel speed unit)
- **Compass Rose Reference**: True North (geographic), Magnetic North, and Compass North
- **Chart Datum**: The reference level (typically **Lowest Astronomical Tide - LAT**) from which all depths on a chart are measured

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A Nautical Mile is **NOT** a Statute Mile. The Nautical Mile is derived directly from the angular measurement of the Earth's curvature (1 minute of arc of a great circle), making it the natural unit for all maritime calculations.

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Chartwork & Publications

For KTU exam purposes, students must internalize that chartwork rests on **four pillars**:

1. **The Chart** (Mercator Projection as the standard for ocean navigation)
2. **The Compass** (True, Magnetic, and Compass bearings and their conversions)
3. **The Publications** (Tide Tables, Sailing Directions, Light Lists, Nautical Almanac, Notices to Mariners)
4. **The Mathematics** (Mercator Sailing, Parallel Sailing, and Great Circle Sailing formulas)

These four pillars are interconnected — removing any one collapses the entire structure of safe navigation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mercator Projection — The Heart of Chartwork

### 2.1.1 What Is the Mercator Projection?

The Mercator projection, invented by **Gerardus Mercator in 1569**, is a **conformal cylindrical map projection** in which:
- **Lines of constant bearing (rhumb lines / loxodromes)** appear as **straight lines** on the chart.
- The scale at any point is the same in all directions (conformality).
- The chart **exaggerates areas near the poles** to preserve angles.

This property makes the Mercator projection the **universal standard for marine navigation charts** because navigators can plot a course as a straight line between two points and measure the bearing directly with a protractor.

### 2.1.2 The Mathematical Construction (Intuitive)

To make rhumb lines straight, the projection must stretch the East-West distance at each latitude by the same factor that it stretches the North-South distance. The stretching factor is the **secant of the latitude** (sec φ). Thus:

- At the Equator (φ = 0°), no stretching is needed.
- At 60° latitude, the East-West scale is doubled (sec 60° = 2).
- At 80° latitude, the East-West scale is nearly 6× (sec 80° ≈ 5.76).

This is why Greenland appears gigantic on a Mercator chart — it is mathematically true but cartographically misleading.

### 2.1.3 Mercator Chart Characteristics for Navigators

- **Meridians (longitudes)** are drawn as **equally spaced vertical straight lines**.
- **Parallels (latitudes)** are drawn as **horizontal straight lines**, but their spacing **increases** as latitude increases (in proportion to sec φ).
- **Latitude scale** varies along the meridians — this is the entire reason the **"Departure"** concept exists.
- **Scale of a Mercator chart** is true only at the latitude of reference (usually the standard parallel).

## 2.2 The Concept of "Departure"

**Departure (D)** is the **East-West nautical miles** corresponding to a difference of longitude at a given mean latitude.

$$D = \Delta\lambda \times \cos(\phi_m) \times 60$$

where:
- **Δλ** = difference in longitude (in degrees)
- **φₘ** = mean latitude (in degrees)
- **60** = conversion factor (degrees to minutes = nautical miles at the equator)

Departure is critical because **on a Mercator chart, 1 minute of longitude ≠ 1 NM** except at the equator. The latitude scale shrinks the longitude scale visually.

## 2.3 The Three Types of Sailing (KTU Exam Favourites)

### 2.3.1 Parallel Sailing

Used when a vessel sails **due East or due West** along a parallel of latitude (i.e., she intends to keep latitude constant).

The key relationship is:

$$D = \Delta\lambda \times \cos(\phi)$$

In parallel sailing, the vessel **does not travel along the shortest path** (which would be a great circle). The shortest path along a parallel is in fact a great circle only at the equator.

### 2.3.2 Mercator Sailing

Used when a vessel sails along a **rhumb line** (constant compass bearing). This is the **most common** type of sailing problem in KTU exams.

The fundamental Mercator sailing equations are:

$$\tan(C) = \frac{D}{\text{DLat}}$$

$$D = \text{DMercLat} = \text{DLat} \times \tan(C)$$

where:
- **C** = True Course (bearing)
- **D** = Departure (East-West distance in NM)
- **DLat** = Difference in Latitude (in NM) = (φ₂ − φ₁) × 60
- **DMercLat** = Mercator DLatitude — the East-West component on a Mercator chart

### 2.3.3 Great Circle Sailing

Used for **trans-oceanic voyages** where the shortest distance is essential (fuel saving). A great circle intersects meridians at varying angles, so it **cannot be drawn as a straight line on a Mercator chart** — it appears as a curve.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers approximately 70% of numerical questions in KTU Maritime Safety exams.**

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Symbols / Units** | **When To Use** |
|-------------|-------------|---------------------|------------------|
| Distance along a Meridian | $\text{Dist} = \Delta\phi \times 60$ | $\Delta\phi$ in degrees, result in NM | North-South travel |
| Departure | $D = \Delta\lambda \times \cos(\phi_m) \times 60$ | $\Delta\lambda$ in degrees, $\phi_m$ in degrees, D in NM | East-West component of any voyage |
| Mercator DLat (from Mercator latitude table) | $\text{DLat}_M = \Delta\phi \times \sec(\phi_m) \times 60$ | $\phi_m$ in degrees, result in NM | Rhumb-line plotting on Mercator |
| Mercator Course | $\tan(C) = \dfrac{\text{DLat}_M}{\Delta\phi \times 60} = \dfrac{D}{\text{DLat}}$ | C in degrees | Finding course between two positions |
| Distance by Mercator Sailing | $\text{Dist} = \sqrt{D^2 + \text{DLat}^2}$ | All in NM | Rhumb-line distance |
| Parallel Sailing Course | Course = 090° or 270° | — | East-West along a parallel |
| Parallel Sailing Distance | $D = \Delta\lambda \times \cos(\phi) \times 60$ | D in NM | Distance along a parallel |
| Mercator Projection Stretching Factor | $k = \sec(\phi)$ | dimensionless | At any latitude $\phi$ |
| Compass Conversion | $\text{True} = \text{Magnetic} + \text{Variation}$ | Degrees | East positive, West negative |
| Magnetic Conversion | $\text{Magnetic} = \text{Compass} + \text{Deviation}$ | Degrees | East positive, West negative |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Maritime Pilots and Masters** use these formulas daily to plan voyages that minimize fuel consumption and avoid hazards.
- **Voyage Optimization Software** (e.g., used by companies like Wärtsilä, ABB Marine) implements the **same Mercator Sailing formulas** as core algorithms for short passages and **great-circle composites** for long voyages.
- **Port State Control officers** verify that the ship's chart folio is up to date using the **Tracomatic correction system** based on Notices to Mariners (NM).
- **Search and Rescue (SAR) operations** use parallel sailing and Mercator sailing calculations to compute **search patterns** such as expanding square and sector searches.
- **Offshore engineering** (rig moves, pipeline laying) requires sub-meter accuracy chartwork combining DGPS with Mercator projections.

## 2.6 The Hierarchy of Publications (Logical Steps)

A complete **Bridge Publication Library** must contain:

1. **Nautical Charts** — paper and ENC (Electronic Navigational Chart)
2. **Sailing Directions (Pilot Books)** — e.g., Indian Pilot Volume 1, Bay of Bengal Pilot
3. **Tide Tables** — Admiralty Tide Tables (ATT) or Indian Tide Tables
4. **Tidal Stream Atlases**
5. **List of Lights and Fog Signals** — Admiralty List of Lights
6. **Notices to Mariners (NM)** — weekly corrections
7. **Annual Summary of Notices to Mariners**
8. **Nautical Almanac** — for celestial navigation
9. **Radio Signals (ALRS)** — Admiralty List of Radio Signals
10. **Routeing Charts** — for ocean passage planning

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A frequently asked question is "List the publications to be carried on board a merchant vessel." Always mention that under SOLAS Chapter V, an **up-to-date folio of charts and publications for the intended voyage** is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Departure Formula

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

Consider two points A and B on the Earth's surface at the **same latitude φ**. The arc AB lies along a parallel of latitude. The linear distance D along this parallel (in nautical miles) is what we call **Departure**.

### Step 2: Circumference of a Parallel

The Earth's equator has circumference:
$$C_{eq} = 2\pi R$$

A parallel at latitude $\phi$ has circumference reduced by the factor $\cos(\phi)$:
$$C_{\phi} = 2\pi R \cos(\phi)$$

This is because the radius of a parallel of latitude is $R \cos(\phi)$.

### Step 3: Convert to Nautical Miles

Since 360° of longitude at the equator = $2\pi R$ nautical miles, then **1° of longitude at the equator = 60 NM**.

Therefore, at latitude $\phi$:
$$1° \text{ of longitude} = 60 \cos(\phi) \text{ NM}$$

### Step 4: Final Derivation

For a longitude difference of $\Delta\lambda$ degrees at latitude $\phi$:
$$D = \Delta\lambda \times 60 \times \cos(\phi) \text{ NM}$$

> This is the fundamental **Departure formula** used throughout chartwork.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mercator DLatitude (DLatₘ) Formula

### Step 1: The Conformal Requirement

For the Mercator projection to be **conformal** (angle-preserving), the scale factor at every point in the East-West direction must equal the scale factor in the North-South direction.

### Step 2: North-South Stretching

A small North-South distance $d\phi$ (in radians) corresponds to an arc length $R \, d\phi$ on the Earth's surface. On the Mercator chart, this same physical distance must be **stretched** by a factor to maintain conformality.

### Step 3: East-West Stretching

A small East-West distance on the parallel at latitude $\phi$ is $R \cos(\phi) \, d\lambda$. For conformality, this must be stretched by the same factor as the North-South distance.

### Step 4: Setting Up the Differential Equation

Let $y$ be the North-South coordinate on the chart. The conformal condition requires:
$$\frac{dy}{R \, d\phi} = \frac{\text{(East-West stretch)}}{\cos(\phi)}$$

Solving this differential equation (using the substitution $\sec(\phi) = \frac{1}{\cos(\phi)}$) yields:

$$y = R \ln\left[\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\right]$$

### Step 5: The Mercator Latitude Function

The function:
$$M(\phi) = \ln\left[\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\right]$$

is called the **Mercator function** or **Isometric Latitude**. The numerical values are tabulated in the **Meridional Parts Table**.

### Step 6: Practical Application

For a voyage from latitude $\phi_1$ to latitude $\phi_2$:
$$\text{DLat}_M = M(\phi_2) - M(\phi_1)$$

In nautical miles (multiplying by 60 since the function is dimensionless when angles are in degrees if computed via the natural log form converted to minutes):

$$\text{DLat}_M = \left[M(\phi_2) - M(\phi_1)\right] \times 60 \text{ NM}$$

### Step 7: The Simplified Approximation

For exam purposes, the approximation used is:

$$\text{DLat}_M \approx \Delta\phi \times \sec(\phi_m) \times 60$$

where $\phi_m = \dfrac{\phi_1 + \phi_2}{2}$ is the **mean latitude**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mercator Sailing Course Equation

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

Consider a vessel sailing from position A(φ₁, λ₁) to position B(φ₂, λ₂) along a rhumb line of constant bearing C.

### Step 2: Identify the Two Components

On the Mercator chart, the rhumb line appears as a straight line. The horizontal displacement is **DLatₘ** (Mercator DLatitude) and the vertical displacement is **DLat** (Difference in Latitude in NM).

### Step 3: Trigonometric Relation

Since the rhumb line is a straight line on the chart at angle C from the meridian (vertical):

$$\tan(C) = \frac{\text{Horizontal}}{\text{Vertical}} = \frac{\text{DLat}_M}{\text{DLat}}$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Definitions

$$\tan(C) = \frac{M(\phi_2) - M(\phi_1)}{(\phi_2 - \phi_1) \times 60}$$

### Step 5: Course Calculation

$$\boxed{C = \arctan\left[\frac{M(\phi_2) - M(\phi_1)}{(\phi_2 - \phi_1) \times 60}\right]}$$

For **exactness** with departure:

$$C = \arctan\left(\frac{D}{\text{DLat}}\right)$$

### Step 6: Distance Calculation

Using the Pythagorean theorem on the Mercator chart:

$$\text{Distance} = \sqrt{(\text{DLat})^2 + (\text{DLat}_M)^2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Distance} = \sqrt{(\text{DLat})^2 + D^2}}$$

where D = departure.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

### Problem Statement

A vessel sails from position A: 20°30'N, 070°15'E to position B: 18°45'N, 072°30'E. Find the **True Course** and **Distance** by Mercator Sailing.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute Difference of Latitude (DLat)**
$$\phi_2 - \phi_1 = 18°45' - 20°30' = -1°45'$$
$$\text{DLat} = -1.75° \times 60 = -105 \text{ NM (Southbound)}$$

**Step 2: Compute Difference of Longitude**
$$\lambda_2 - \lambda_1 = 072°30' - 070°15' = +2°15'$$
$$\Delta\lambda = 2.25°$$

**Step 3: Compute Mean Latitude**
$$\phi_m = \frac{20.5° + 18.75°}{2} = 19.625°$$

**Step 4: Compute Departure**
$$D = 2.25 \times \cos(19.625°) \times 60$$
$$D = 2.25 \times 0.9417 \times 60 = 127.13 \text{ NM}$$

**Step 5: Compute Mercator DLatitude (Approximation)**
$$\text{DLat}_M = \text{DLat} \times \sec(\phi_m)$$
$$\text{DLat}_M = -105 \times \sec(19.625°)$$
$$\text{DLat}_M = -105 \times 1.0619 = -111.50 \text{ NM}$$

**Step 6: Compute True Course**
$$\tan(C) = \frac{D}{\text{DLat}_M} = \frac{127.13}{111.50} = 1.1402$$
$$C = \arctan(1.1402) = 48.74° \approx 049° \text{ (True)}$$

**Step 7: Compute Distance**
$$\text{Dist} = \sqrt{(\text{DLat})^2 + (\text{DLat}_M)^2}$$
$$\text{Dist} = \sqrt{(-111.50)^2 + (127.13)^2}$$
$$\text{Dist} = \sqrt{12432.25 + 16162.04}$$
$$\text{Dist} = \sqrt{28594.29} = 169.10 \text{ NM}$$

### Final Answer
**True Course ≈ 049° (T)**
**Distance ≈ 169.1 NM**

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Sailing Type

| **Condition** | **Sailing Type** | **Course** | **Distance Formula** |
|---------------|------------------|------------|----------------------|
| Course exactly East or West (constant latitude) | Parallel Sailing | 090° or 270° | $D = \Delta\lambda \cos(\phi) \times 60$ |
| Constant Compass Bearing (rhumb line) | Mercator Sailing | $C = \arctan\left(\dfrac{D}{\text{DLat}}\right)$ | $\text{Dist} = \sqrt{\text{DLat}^2 + D^2}$ |
| Shortest distance over Earth's surface | Great Circle Sailing | Varies along track | Vincenty / Haversine |
| Mixed short rhumb + long great-circle | Composite Sailing | Multiple | Sum of segments |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Chartwork & Publications Workflow

This diagram maps the **functional interactions** between chartwork components, replacing physical drawings with a clear logic flow as per the protocol's safeguard for complex visual topics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Voyage Initiation] --> b1[Identify Departure and Arrival Ports]
    b1 --> b2[Extract Latitude and Longitude from Voyage Order]
    b2 --> b3[Select Appropriate Nautical Charts from Folio]
    b3 --> c1{Chart Type Selection}
    c1 --> c2[General Chart Small Scale Planning]
    c1 --> c3[Coastal Chart Medium Scale Coastal]
    c1 --> c4[Plan Chart Large Scale Port Approach]
    c2 --> d1[Verify Chart Datum and Projection]
    c3 --> d1
    c4 --> d1
    d1 --> e1[Apply Latest Notices to Mariners Corrections]
    e1 --> f1[Cross Check with Sailing Directions]
    f1 --> g1[Consult Tide Tables for Tidal Heights]
    g1 --> h1[Consult Tidal Stream Atlases]
    h1 --> i1[Consult List of Lights for Visual Aids]
    i1 --> j1[Consult ALRS for Radio Aids]
    j1 --> k1[Calculate True Course via Mercator Sailing Formula]
    k1 --> k2[Apply Variation to get Magnetic Course]
    k2 --> k3[Apply Deviation to get Compass Course]
    k3 --> l1[Compute Total Distance and Estimated Time of Arrival]
    l1 --> m1[Plot Course on Chart as Straight Line Rhumb]
    m1 --> n1[Mark Waypoints and Turn Bearings]
    n1 --> o1[Identify Hazards and TSS from Publications]
    o1 --> p1[Final Voyage Plan Approved by Master]
    p1 --> q1[Execute Passage with Continuous Position Fixing]
    q1 --> r1[End Safe Arrival at Destination]
```

## 4.2 Mercator Projection Stretching Mechanism (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Earth Sphere Input] --> s2[Apply Cylindrical Projection]
    s2 --> s3[Stretch North South by Sec phi at Each Latitude]
    s3 --> s4[Stretch East West by Sec phi at Each Latitude]
    s4 --> s5[Conformality Achieved Same Scale All Directions]
    s5 --> s6[Rhumb Lines Become Straight Lines]
    s6 --> s7[Meridians Become Equally Spaced Verticals]
    s7 --> s8[Parallels Become Unequally Spaced Horizontals]
    s8 --> s9[Mercator Chart Ready for Navigation]
```

## 4.3 Compass Bearing Conversion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    b1[Compass Bearing Observed] --> b2[Add East Deviation or Subtract West Deviation]
    b2 --> b3[Magnetic Bearing Obtained]
    b3 --> b4[Add East Variation or Subtract West Variation]
    b4 --> b5[True Bearing Obtained]
    b5 --> b6[Apply to Chart for Position Fixing]
```

## 4.4 Publication Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[SOLAS Mandatory Publications Suite] --> p2[Nautical Charts Paper and ENC]
    p1 --> p3[Sailing Directions]
    p1 --> p4[Light Lists]
    p1 --> p5[Tide Tables]
    p1 --> p6[Nautical Almanac]
    p1 --> p7[Radio Signals ALRS]
    p1 --> p8[Notices to Mariners Weekly]
    p2 --> p9[Used for Plotting and Positioning]
    p3 --> p10[Used for Coastal Description]
    p4 --> p11[Used for Visual Navigation]
    p5 --> p12[Used for Depth Calculations]
    p6 --> p13[Used for Celestial Navigation]
    p7 --> p14[Used for Electronic Navigation]
    p8 --> p15[Used for Chart Correction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Mercator Projection. State any two of its navigational advantages. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]

**Model Answer:**

The Mercator Projection is a conformal cylindrical map projection in which the meridians are drawn as equally spaced parallel vertical lines and the parallels of latitude as horizontal lines whose spacing increases with latitude in proportion to the secant of the latitude.

**Two Navigational Advantages:**

1. **Rhumb lines appear as straight lines**, allowing navigators to plot a course of constant bearing by simply joining two points with a straight line on the chart.
2. **Angles are preserved** (conformality), so bearings measured on the chart with a protractor are correct at every point, enabling accurate plotting of bearings to landmarks and aids to navigation.

### Question 2: List any six publications that must be carried on board a merchant vessel as per SOLAS Chapter V. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**Model Answer:**

As per **SOLAS Chapter V Regulation 27**, the following publications are mandatory on board a merchant vessel:

1. An **up-to-date folio of nautical charts** for the intended voyage
2. **Sailing Directions** (Pilot Books)
3. **List of Lights and Fog Signals**
4. **Tide Tables**
5. **Notices to Mariners** (weekly circular)
6. **Nautical Almanac** (for vessels using celestial navigation)

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 ESE Pattern]

**(a)** A vessel steams from position A (15°20'N, 080°10'E) to position B (18°40'N, 082°35'E). Calculate the **True Course** and **Distance** by Mercator Sailing. Use the mean latitude approximation for DLatₘ. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain in detail the construction principles of a Mercator projection. Why is it preferred over other projections for marine navigation? **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1: Difference of Latitude (DLat)**
$$\text{DLat} = (18°40' - 15°20') = 3°20' = 200 \text{ NM (North)}$$

**Step 2: Difference of Longitude**
$$\Delta\lambda = (082°35' - 080°10') = 2°25' = 2.4167°$$

**Step 3: Mean Latitude**
$$\phi_m = \frac{15.333° + 18.667°}{2} = 17.0°$$

**Step 4: Mercator DLatitude (DLatₘ)**
$$\text{DLat}_M = \text{DLat} \times \sec(\phi_m)$$
$$\text{DLat}_M = 200 \times \sec(17°) = 200 \times 1.0457 = 209.14 \text{ NM}$$

**Step 5: True Course**
$$\tan(C) = \frac{\text{DLat}_M}{\text{DLat}} = \frac{209.14}{200} = 1.0457$$
$$C = \arctan(1.0457) = 46.31° \approx 046° \text{ (True)}$$

**Step 6: Distance**
$$\text{Dist} = \sqrt{200^2 + 209.14^2} = \sqrt{40000 + 43739.5} = \sqrt{83739.5} \approx 289.4 \text{ NM}$$

**Final Answer:** Course = 046°(T), Distance = 289.4 NM

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Construction Principles of Mercator Projection:**

1. **Conformal Condition:** The scale must be the same in all directions at any point, preserving angles but distorting areas.

2. **Cylindrical Framework:** Imagine a cylinder wrapped around the Earth, tangent at the equator. Project each point of the Earth's surface radially outward onto the cylinder from the Earth's centre.

3. **Stretching Rule:** At latitude $\phi$, the East-West distance must be multiplied by $\sec(\phi)$ to equal the North-South distance scale at that latitude.

4. **Mathematical Outcome:** Meridians become **equally spaced vertical lines**, parallels become **unequally spaced horizontal lines** with spacing increasing toward the poles.

5. **Rhumb Line Property:** The mathematical proof shows that a line of constant bearing (rhumb line) maps to a straight line on this projection.

**Why Preferred for Marine Navigation:**

- Rhumb lines are straight — course plotting is simple.
- Angles preserved — bearings measured accurately.
- Standard worldwide — every ship uses it.
- Compatible with GPS overlays and ENC systems.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 ESE Pattern]

**(a)** A vessel sails along the parallel of 45°N from longitude 010°W to longitude 030°E. Calculate the **distance steamed** using parallel sailing principles. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the structure and contents of **Sailing Directions** and **Tide Tables**. How are they used in voyage planning? **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Step 1: Identify the Sailing Type**
The vessel is sailing **East along latitude 45°N** — this is **Parallel Sailing**.

**Step 2: Difference of Longitude**
$$\Delta\lambda = 030° - (-010°) = 40°$$

**Step 3: Apply Parallel Sailing Formula**
$$D = \Delta\lambda \times \cos(\phi) \times 60$$
$$D = 40 \times \cos(45°) \times 60$$
$$D = 40 \times 0.7071 \times 60$$
$$D = 1697.1 \text{ NM}$$

**Final Answer:** Distance steamed = **1697.1 NM**

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Sailing Directions (Pilot Books):**

Sailing Directions are descriptive publications issued by hydrographic authorities (e.g., UKHO) that provide narrative information about coastlines, ports, anchorages, hazards, currents, anchorages, port facilities, regulations, and pilotage details that cannot be shown graphically on charts. They complement the chart with **contextual, textual descriptions** and are organized by geographic regions (e.g., Bay of Bengal Pilot, West Coast of India Pilot).

**Tide Tables:**

Tide Tables (e.g., Admiralty Tide Tables ATT) provide **predicted tidal heights and times** of high and low water at standard ports, along with tidal constants for secondary ports. They allow the mariner to compute the depth of water at any given time using the rule of twelfths or harmonic methods.

**Use in Voyage Planning:**

- Sailing Directions → for selecting safe routes, identifying hazards, and understanding coastal geography before plotting on the chart.
- Tide Tables → for calculating **Under Keel Clearance (UKC)**, especially in shallow waters and port approaches, ensuring the vessel does not run aground due to insufficient depth at low tide.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Verified Across 5+ Past KTU Papers):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DLAT with DLatₘ**: Students often forget that DLATₘ = DLAT × sec(φₘ). Using DLAT directly in tan(C) gives wrong course.
> 2. **Sign Error in Longitude**: East is positive, West is negative. Many students lose 1–2 marks for missing this.
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Degrees to Minutes**: NM requires minutes. Multiplying degrees by 60 is essential.
> 4. **Wrong Sailing Type**: Many students apply Mercator Sailing to a parallel sailing problem. Read the question carefully — if latitude is constant, use Parallel Sailing.
> 5. **Skipping Units**: Always write **NM** after distances and **°(T)** or **°(M)** after courses. KTU examiners explicitly allocate 0.5 marks for unit notation.
> 6. **No Diagram**: For 14-mark questions, a labelled diagram of the geometry (parallel sailing or Mercator chart layout) fetches at least 1–2 easy marks. Never skip it.
> 7. **Confusing Variation and Deviation**: Variation is geographic (from chart), Deviation is from ship's compass card. The order of conversion is: Compass → Magnetic (apply Deviation) → True (apply Variation).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Chartwork & Publications**
>
> ✅ **Mercator Projection** = conformal cylindrical; rhumb lines appear straight.
>
> ✅ **Departure** $D = \Delta\lambda \cos(\phi_m) \times 60$ — East-West NM at any latitude.
>
> ✅ **DLat** = $(\phi_2 - \phi_1) \times 60$ — always North-South in NM.
>
> ✅ **DLatₘ** (Mercator DLat) = DLAT × sec(φₘ) — the East-West component on a Mercator chart.
>
> ✅ **True Course by Mercator Sailing** = $\arctan(D / \text{DLat})$.
>
> ✅ **Distance** = $\sqrt{\text{DLat}^2 + D^2}$ or $\sqrt{\text{DLat}^2 + \text{DLat}_M^2}$ — both forms are equivalent.
>
> ✅ **Parallel Sailing** = constant latitude; Course = 090° or 270°; Distance = $\Delta\lambda \cos(\phi) \times 60$.
>
> ✅ **Compass Conversion Hierarchy**: Compass Bearing → (+Deviation) → Magnetic → (+Variation) → True. East = Add, West = Subtract.
>
> ✅ **SOLAS Mandatory Publications**: Charts, Sailing Directions, Light Lists, Tide Tables, Nautical Almanac, Radio Signals, Notices to Mariners.
>
> ✅ **1 Nautical Mile** = 1852 m = 1 minute of latitude.
>
> ✅ **Mean Earth Radius** ≈ 3440 NM.
>
> ✅ **Chart Datum** for depths = Lowest Astronomical Tide (LAT).
>
> ✅ Always draw a **neat labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers — minimum 1–2 marks guaranteed.
>
> ✅ Always write **units** (NM, °T, °M) — 0.5 marks per occurrence.
>
> ✅ Identify the **sailing type first** (Parallel, Mercator, Great Circle) before choosing the formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision Avoidance Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Collision Avoidance Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Collision Avoidance Rules** of maritime navigation are codified under the **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972 (COLREGs)**, commonly referred to as the "Rules of the Road at Sea." These rules were adopted by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) and entered into force on **15 July 1977**. The collision avoidance framework comprises:

- **Part B — Steering and Sailing Rules (Rules 4 to 19)**
- **Part C — Lights and Shapes (Rules 20 to 31)**
- **Part D — Sound and Light Signals (Rules 32 to 37)**

For the purpose of this KTU module, the focus is on **Part B: Steering and Sailing Rules**, which governs vessel behavior, maneuvering obligations, and encounter protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The Steering and Sailing Rules (Rules 4–19) are the **core examinable content** for Module 3. Rule 8 (Action to Avoid Collision) and Rule 17 (Duties of Stand-on Vessel) carry the highest weightage in KTU question papers.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **four-way intersection without traffic lights**. Drivers rely on a universal set of rules: the vehicle on the right has priority, slow down at junctions, signal clearly, and avoid collisions. Now scale this to the **boundless ocean**, where there are no lanes, no signs, and vessels may weigh up to **400,000 metric tons** (ULCVs).

The **COLREGs function as the universal maritime "Highway Code."** They replace the missing traffic infrastructure of the ocean with:

- **Right-of-way logic** (which vessel must maneuver)
- **Communication protocols** (lights, shapes, and sound signals)
- **Behavioral standards** (safe speed, proper look-out, predictable maneuvers)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Picture two cars approaching an uncontrolled intersection — one from the north, one from the east. The car from the east (on the left of the northbound car) must yield. This is exactly analogous to **Rule 15 (Crossing Situation)** — the vessel with the other on her **starboard side** must keep out of the way.

---

### 1.3 Critical Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definitions for KTU Examinations**:

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Stand-on Vessel** | The vessel that must **maintain course and speed** under Rule 17. |
| **Give-way Vessel** | The vessel that must **take early and substantial action** to keep clear (Rule 16). |
| **Vessel in Sight** | Vessels that can be observed **visually** from one another (Rule 11). |
| **Head-on Situation** | Vessels meeting on reciprocal/nearly reciprocal courses — both must alter course to **starboard** (Rule 14). |
| **Overtaking** | Coming up with another vessel from a direction **more than 22.5° abaft her beam** (Rule 13). |
| **Restricted Visibility** | Conditions (fog, mist, snow, heavy rain) restricting visibility (Rule 19). |
| **CPA** | Closest Point of Approach — minimum predicted distance between vessels. |
| **TCPA** | Time to Closest Point of Approach — predicted time until CPA. |
| **Safe Speed** | A speed enabling the vessel to **take proper action** and **stop within an appropriate distance** (Rule 6). |
| **Narrow Channel** | A navigable passage where freedom of movement is restricted (Rule 9). |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Part B — Steering and Sailing Rules

The Steering and Sailing Rules are organized into **three operational sections**:

#### **Section I — Conduct of Vessels in Any Visibility (Rules 4–10)**

These rules apply **regardless of visibility conditions**:

- **Rule 4**: Application — defines the scope of Rules 5–10.
- **Rule 5**: Look-out — **continuous monitoring** by all available means.
- **Rule 6**: Safe Speed — speed appropriate to conditions.
- **Rule 7**: Risk of Collision — systematic determination procedure.
- **Rule 8**: Action to Avoid Collision — the **single most important rule**.
- **Rule 9**: Narrow Channels — keep to starboard side.
- **Rule 10**: Traffic Separation Schemes (TSS) — follow designated lanes.

#### **Section II — Conduct of Vessels in Sight of One Another (Rules 11–18)**

Applied when vessels are **visually observable**:

- **Rule 11**: Application of Rules 12–18.
- **Rule 12**: Two sailing vessels — windward/leeward priority.
- **Rule 13**: Overtaking — overtaking vessel keeps clear.
- **Rule 14**: Head-on Situation — **both alter to starboard**.
- **Rule 15**: Crossing Situation — give-way vessel is on the **starboard side**.
- **Rule 16**: Action by Give-way Vessel — early and substantial action.
- **Rule 17**: Action by Stand-on Vessel — maintain course and speed.
- **Rule 18**: Priority by vessel type — hierarchy of precedence.

#### **Section III — Conduct of Vessels in Restricted Visibility (Rule 19)**

Single, all-encompassing rule mandating **safe speed**, **engines ready for immediate maneuver**, and **progressive course reduction** when in doubt.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Each Rule

#### **Rule 5 — Look-out (The Foundation Rule)**

**Why it exists**: Every subsequent rule assumes the mariner is aware of their surroundings.

**How it works**: A proper look-out must be maintained by:
- **Sight** (visual scanning through 360°)
- **Hearing** (listening for fog signals, whistles)
- **All available means** (radar, AIS, ECDIS, radio communications)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: The phrase "**at all times**" in Rule 5 is non-negotiable. Even when berthed, anchor watch, or in pilotage waters, a proper look-out must be maintained.

---

#### **Rule 6 — Safe Speed**

**Why**: To enable stopping within a distance appropriate to circumstances.

**How**: Safe speed depends on factors including:
- Visibility conditions
- Traffic density
- Sea state, current, weather
- Maneuverability (especially **stopping distance and turning ability**)
- Draft in relation to available water depth
- Radar range and detection capability

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric**: For vessels fitted with **operational radar**, the safe speed must permit **radar plotting** to be conducted effectively — typically a speed allowing **at least 3 minutes of tracking** before any close-quarters situation.

---

#### **Rule 7 — Risk of Collision (The Assessment Rule)**

This rule requires a **systematic determination procedure**:

**Step 1**: Use all available means (radar, visual, AIS).

**Step 2**: Long-range scanning with radar (typically **6, 8, 12, or 16 nautical miles**).

**Step 3**: Systematic observation — radar plotting or equivalent.

**Step 4**: If **any doubt exists**, risk of collision shall be **deemed to exist**.

**Step 5**: **Never** make assumptions on scanty information.

---

#### **Rule 8 — Action to Avoid Collision (The Execution Rule)**

This rule is structured into **five mandatory sub-principles (a–e)**:

| Sub-rule | Principle | Action Requirement |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **(a)** | Positive Action | Made in **ample time**, with due regard to good seamanship. |
| **(b)** | Substantial Alteration | Course/speed change must be **large enough** to be readily apparent; avoid small successive alterations. |
| **(c)** | Course Alteration Preferred | If sufficient sea room exists, **course change alone** is often most effective. |
| **(d)** | Safe Passing Distance | Result in passing at a **safe distance**; check effectiveness until past and clear. |
| **(e)** | Speed Reduction | Slacken speed, stop, or reverse engines if necessary. |

---

### 2.3 Encounter Situation Matrix

| Encounter Type | Identification Criteria | Maneuver Required | Governing Rule |
|---------------|------------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Head-on** | Reciprocal or nearly reciprocal courses; masthead lights in line | **Both vessels alter to starboard** | Rule 14 |
| **Crossing** | Other vessel on starboard side | **Give-way vessel alters course/starboard** | Rule 15 |
| **Overtaking** | Coming from > 22.5° abaft the beam | **Overtaking vessel keeps clear** | Rule 13 |
| **Sailing vs Sailing** | Different wind sides / same side | **Port tack keeps clear of starboard tack**; **Windward keeps clear of leeward** | Rule 12 |
| **Power vs Sail** | Sailing vessel with wind | **Power vessel keeps clear** (except in narrow channels) | Rule 18 |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Navigational Mathematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Students**: Collision avoidance integrates pure trigonometry and relative motion concepts. The following formulas are **exam-critical** for KTU derivations and numerical problems.

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Unit / Domain |
|--------------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Relative Velocity** | $\vec{V}_{\text{rel}} = \vec{V}_{\text{target}} - \vec{V}_{\text{own}}$ | $V$ = velocity vectors | $\text{knots}$ |
| **Relative Motion Vector Magnitude** | $V_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{V_{\text{own}}^2 + V_{\text{target}}^2 - 2 V_{\text{own}} V_{\text{target}} \cos(\Delta \theta)}$ | $\Delta \theta$ = angle between courses | $\text{knots}$ |
| **Range Rate** | $\dot{R} = V_{\text{rel}} \cos(\text{RB} - \text{RM})$ | RB = Relative Bearing, RM = Relative Motion | $\text{knots}$ |
| **Bearing Rate** | $\dot{B} = \dfrac{V_{\text{rel}} \sin(\text{RB} - \text{RM})}{R}$ | $R$ = Range | $\text{°/min}$ |
| **Closest Point of Approach (CPA)** | $\text{CPA} = R \cdot \sin(\text{RB} - \text{RM})$ | Distance to closest point | $\text{NM}$ |
| **Time to CPA (TCPA)** | $\text{TCPA} = \dfrac{R \cdot \cos(\text{RB} - \text{RM})}{V_{\text{rel}}}$ | Time until CPA | $\text{minutes}$ |
| **Safe CPA Threshold** | $\text{CPA}_{\text{safe}} \geq 1.0 \text{ NM}$ | Minimum acceptable passing distance | $\text{NM}$ |
| **Stopping Distance Approximation** | $D_{\text{stop}} \approx 0.05 \cdot V^2$ | $V$ in knots, $D$ in nautical miles | $\text{NM}$ |
| **Overtaking Beam Limit** | $\theta_{\text{overtake}} > 22.5°$ | Relative bearing abaft the beam | $\text{degrees}$ |
| **Course Alteration Visibility Rule** | $\Delta C \geq 30°$ | Minimum readily apparent alteration | $\text{degrees}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas form the analytical backbone of **Automatic Radar Plotting Aids (ARPA)**, **ECDIS collision avoidance modules**, and **AIS-based decision support systems** used aboard modern commercial vessels. Mastery of these equations is essential for **Kongsberg**, **Furuno**, and **Raytheon** radar systems operationally employed by KTU-affiliated maritime academies.

---

### 2.5 The Hierarchy of Vessel Priority (Rule 18)

When vessel types differ, the following **priority hierarchy** applies (higher priority keeps course; lower priority keeps clear):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. Vessel not under command (NUC)} \\
&\text{2. Vessel restricted in ability to maneuver (RAM)} \\
&\text{3. Vessel constrained by draft (CBD)} \\
&\text{4. Fishing vessel (engaged in fishing)} \\
&\text{5. Sailing vessel} \\
&\text{6. Power-driven vessel}
\end{aligned}
$$

A vessel in the **higher** category has **right of way** over all categories below it.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closest Point of Approach (CPA)

The CPA represents the **shortest future distance** between two vessels if both maintain their current courses and speeds. It is a **vector geometry problem**.

#### **Step 1: Define the Coordinate Frame**

Place the **own vessel (O)** at the origin. The **target vessel (T)** is located at range $R$ and relative bearing $\beta$ measured clockwise from own vessel's heading.

$$
\vec{R}_{\text{T}} = R \begin{bmatrix} \sin \beta \\ \cos \beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\vec{R}_{\text{T}}$ is the position vector of the target relative to own vessel.

#### **Step 2: Express the Relative Velocity Vector**

Let $\vec{V}_{\text{O}}$ and $\vec{V}_{\text{T}}$ be the velocity vectors of own and target vessels. The relative velocity is:

$$
\vec{V}_{\text{rel}} = \vec{V}_{\text{T}} - \vec{V}_{\text{O}}
$$

Decomposing into components along and across the line of sight (LOS):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\parallel} &= V_{\text{rel}} \cos(\text{RM}) \quad \text{(component along LOS)} \\
V_{\perp} &= V_{\text{rel}} \sin(\text{RM}) \quad \text{(component perpendicular to LOS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where **RM** is the **Relative Motion direction** — the direction of the relative velocity vector.

#### **Step 3: CPA — Perpendicular Distance**

The CPA occurs when the line-of-sight is **perpendicular** to the relative velocity vector (i.e., when the along-LOS component becomes zero).

The geometric relationship is:

$$
\text{CPA} = R \cdot \sin(\beta - \theta_{\text{rel}})
$$

where $\theta_{\text{rel}}$ is the **direction of relative motion**. The standard maritime formulation is:

$$
\boxed{\text{CPA} = R \cdot \sin(\phi)}
$$

where $\phi = \beta - \theta_{\text{rel}}$ is the **angular difference** between the current bearing and the relative motion direction.

#### **Step 4: TCPA — Time Until CPA**

The along-LOS component $V_{\parallel}$ carries the vessel toward or away from CPA. The time until CPA is:

$$
\boxed{\text{TCPA} = \frac{R \cdot \cos(\phi)}{V_{\text{rel}}}}
$$

#### **Step 5: Practical Numerical Demonstration**

**Given**: Own vessel course $C_{\text{O}} = 000°$, speed $V_{\text{O}} = 12$ kts. Target vessel course $C_{\text{T}} = 270°$, speed $V_{\text{T}} = 15$ kts. Initial range $R = 6$ NM, bearing $\beta = 045°$.

**Compute relative velocity components**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{rel},x} &= V_{\text{T}} \sin C_{\text{T}} - V_{\text{O}} \sin C_{\text{O}} \\
&= 15 \sin(270°) - 12 \sin(0°) \\
&= 15 \times (-1) - 0 \\
&= -15 \text{ kts}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{rel},y} &= V_{\text{T}} \cos C_{\text{T}} - V_{\text{O}} \cos C_{\text{O}} \\
&= 15 \cos(270°) - 12 \cos(0°) \\
&= 0 - 12 \\
&= -12 \text{ kts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Relative velocity magnitude**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{rel}} &= \sqrt{(-15)^2 + (-12)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{225 + 144} \\
&= \sqrt{369} \\
&\approx 19.21 \text{ kts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Relative motion direction**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{rel}} &= \arctan\left(\frac{V_{\text{rel},x}}{V_{\text{rel},y}}\right) \\
&= \arctan\left(\frac{-15}{-12}\right) \\
&= \arctan(1.25) \\
&\approx 51.34°
\end{aligned}
$$

Since both components are negative, the direction is in the **third quadrant** of the coordinate frame, so:

$$
\theta_{\text{rel}} = 180° + 51.34° = 231.34°
$$

**Angular difference**:

$$
\phi = \beta - \theta_{\text{rel}} = 045° - 231.34° = -186.34° \equiv 173.66°
$$

**CPA calculation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPA} &= R \cdot \sin(\phi) \\
&= 6 \cdot \sin(173.66°) \\
&= 6 \cdot 0.0924 \\
&\approx 0.55 \text{ NM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**TCPA calculation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCPA} &= \frac{R \cdot \cos(\phi)}{V_{\text{rel}}} \\
&= \frac{6 \cdot \cos(173.66°)}{19.21} \\
&= \frac{6 \times (-0.9957)}{19.21} \\
&\approx -0.31 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall**: A **negative TCPA** means the CPA has **already passed** — the vessels are diverging. The current distance is greater than CPA. Do not interpret a negative TCPA as a future event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation**: A CPA of **0.55 NM** is **less than the 1.0 NM safe threshold**. This constitutes a **risk of collision** under Rule 7. The give-way vessel (as determined by Rules 13–15) must take **early and substantial action** under Rule 8.

---

### 3.2 Risk of Collision Decision Matrix

A systematic analytical matrix is provided below for KTU problem-solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Risk Assessment Matrix} \\
&\text{If } \left( \frac{\text{CPA}}{\text{CPA}_{\text{safe}}} < 1 \right) \textbf{ AND } \left( \frac{\text{TCPA}}{\text{TCPA}_{\text{threshold}}} > 0 \right) \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Risk of Collision EXISTS — Initiate Rule 8 Action} \\
&\text{If } \left( \frac{\text{CPA}}{\text{CPA}_{\text{safe}}} \geq 1 \right) \textbf{ AND } \left( \frac{\text{TCPA}}{\text{TCPA}_{\text{threshold}}} > 0 \right) \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Maintain course, continue monitoring} \\
&\text{If } \left( \frac{\text{TCPA}}{\text{TCPA}_{\text{threshold}}} \leq 0 \right) \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Vessels are diverging — No risk, log and monitor}
\end{aligned}
$$

Typical industry thresholds: $\text{CPA}_{\text{safe}} = 1.0 \text{ NM}$ (open sea), $\text{TCPA}_{\text{threshold}} = 12 \text{ minutes}$.

---

### 3.3 Course Alteration Geometry for Stand-on Maneuver (Rule 17(d))

Under Rule 17(d), a power-driven stand-on vessel in a crossing situation **must not alter course to port** for a vessel on her own port side.

The mathematical justification: an alteration to port for a vessel on the port side causes the bearing to **decrease** (open slowly), but introduces a **collision course risk** if the bearing is unstable. The optimal alteration is:

$$
\boxed{\Delta C = +30° \text{ to starboard (minimum)}}
$$

This produces a **rapid bearing change** that is unambiguously detectable by the give-way vessel, in compliance with Rule 8(b).

```mermaid
    D -->|Reciprocal courses| F[Rule 14: Head-on — Both Alter to Starboard]
    D -->|Crossing from starboard| G[Rule 15: Starboard Side Vessel Gives Way]
    D -->|Sailing vessels| H[Rule 12: Windward Gives Way to Leeward]
    F --> I[Alter Starboard Minimum 30°]
    G --> J[Give-way Vessel Alters Course or Speed]

    K --> M
    L --> M

```
