## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain the term "Stand-on Vessel" and outline its duties under Rule 17 of the COLREGs.**

**Model Answer:**

A **stand-on vessel** is the vessel that has the **right of way** in a given encounter. Under **Rule 17** of the COLREGs, the stand-on vessel has the following duties:

1. **(a)** Keep her **course and speed** — maintain a steady heading and speed so that the give-way vessel can predict her motion and maneuver accordingly.

2. **(b)** May take action by her **maneuver alone** if it becomes clear that the give-way vessel is **not taking appropriate action** in compliance with the rules.

3. **(c)** If collision **cannot be avoided by the give-way vessel's action alone**, the stand-on vessel must take such action as will **best aid to avoid collision**.

4. **(d)** In a **crossing situation** between two power-driven vessels, the stand-on vessel **shall not alter course to port** for a vessel on her own port side.

5. **(e)** This rule does **not relieve** the give-way vessel of her obligation to keep out of the way.

**Mark Distribution (3 marks)**: Definition (1) + Three duties (1) + Cross-reference to Rule 16/18 (1).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define "Safe Speed" and list any four factors that a mariner must consider when determining safe speed under Rule 6.**

**Model Answer:**

**Safe Speed** is defined under **Rule 6** of the COLREGs as the speed at which a vessel can take **proper and effective action to avoid collision** and be **stopped within a distance appropriate** to the prevailing circumstances and conditions.

**Four factors determining safe speed**:

1. **Visibility conditions** — fog, mist, rain, or darkness reduce detection range.
2. **Traffic density** — high-density waters require lower speeds for adequate reaction time.
3. **Sea state, wind, and current** — affect maneuverability and stopping distance.
4. **Draft in relation to available water depth** — shallow water reduces rudder effectiveness.

Additional factors include the vessel's **stopping distance and turning ability**, the **presence and effectiveness of radar**, and the **proximity of navigational hazards**.

**Mark Distribution (3 marks)**: Definition (1) + Four factors (1.5) + Brief elaboration (0.5).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Describe in detail the actions to be taken by a vessel under Rule 8 of the COLREGs to avoid collision. How does Rule 8 differ from Rule 16?**

**Model Answer:**

**Rule 8 — Action to Avoid Collision** comprises **five sub-principles**:

**Rule 8(a) — Positive Action**: Any action to avoid collision shall be **positive**, made in **ample time**, and with due regard to the observance of **good seamanship**. The vessel must not hesitate; the action must be deliberate and committed.

**Rule 8(b) — Substantial Alteration**: Any alteration of course and/or speed shall, if circumstances admit, be **large enough to be readily apparent** to another vessel observing visually or by radar. A **succession of small alterations** of course and/or speed is to be **avoided**. The industry standard is a minimum alteration of **30 degrees** for course or a **50% reduction** in speed.

**Rule 8(c) — Course Alteration Preferred**: If there is **sufficient sea room**, alteration of **course alone** may be the most effective action to avoid a close-quarters situation, provided it is made in good time, is substantial, and does not result in another close-quarters situation. Course alteration maintains propulsion control and is generally preferred over speed reduction in open waters.

**Rule 8(d) — Safe Passing Distance**: Action taken shall result in **passing at a safe distance**. The effectiveness of the action shall be **carefully checked** until the other vessel is finally past and clear. Continuous monitoring is mandatory.

**Rule 8(e) — Speed Reduction as Last Resort**: If necessary to avoid collision or to allow more time to assess the situation, a vessel shall **slacken her speed** or take **all way off** by stopping or reversing her means of propulsion.

**Difference between Rule 8 and Rule 16**:

| Aspect | Rule 8 | Rule 16 |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **Applicability** | All vessels in all situations | Specifically the give-way vessel |
| **Scope** | General principles of collision avoidance | Specific duty of the maneuvering vessel |
| **Nature** | Prescribes HOW to maneuver | Prescribes WHEN and BY WHOM to maneuver |

Rule 8 is a **general principle** that applies universally, while Rule 16 specifically obligates the **give-way vessel** to take **early and substantial action**.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks)**: Five sub-principles (4) + Comparison with Rule 16 (2) + Conclusion (1).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Two power-driven vessels are crossing with risk of collision. Vessel A sees Vessel B on her starboard side at a range of 4 NM. Vessel A's course is 090° at 14 kts; Vessel B's course is 180° at 10 kts. Determine: (i) Relative velocity magnitude, (ii) Direction of relative motion, (iii) CPA, (iv) TCPA. State the required maneuver per Rule 15.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data**:
- Vessel A: $C_{\text{A}} = 090°$, $V_{\text{A}} = 14$ kts
- Vessel B: $C_{\text{B}} = 180°$, $V_{\text{B}} = 10$ kts
- Range: $R = 4$ NM
- Relative bearing: $\beta = 090°$ (Vessel B on starboard of A)

**Step 1: Compute Relative Velocity Components**

Using east-north reference frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{rel},x} &= V_{\text{B}} \sin C_{\text{B}} - V_{\text{A}} \sin C_{\text{A}} \\
&= 10 \sin(180°) - 14 \sin(90°) \\
&= 10 \times 0 - 14 \times 1 \\
&= -14 \text{ kts}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{rel},y} &= V_{\text{B}} \cos C_{\text{B}} - V_{\text{A}} \cos C_{\text{A}} \\
&= 10 \cos(180°) - 14 \cos(90°) \\
&= 10 \times (-1) - 14 \times 0 \\
&= -10 \text{ kts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Relative Velocity Magnitude**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{rel}} &= \sqrt{V_{\text{rel},x}^2 + V_{\text{rel},y}^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(-14)^2 + (-10)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{196 + 100} \\
&= \sqrt{296} \\
&\approx 17.20 \text{ kts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Answer: $V_{\text{rel}} \approx 17.20$ kts**

**Step 3: Direction of Relative Motion**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{rel}} &= \arctan\left(\frac{V_{\text{rel},x}}{V_{\text{rel},y}}\right) \\
&= \arctan\left(\frac{-14}{-10}\right) \\
&= \arctan(1.4) \\
&\approx 54.46°
\end{aligned}
$$

Both components negative → third quadrant:

$$
\theta_{\text{rel}} = 180° + 54.46° = 234.46°
$$

**(ii) Answer: Relative motion direction $\approx 234.46°$**

**Step 4: CPA Calculation**

Angular difference between bearing and relative motion:

$$
\phi = \beta - \theta_{\text{rel}} = 090° - 234.46° = -144.46° \equiv 215.54°
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPA} &= R \cdot \sin(\phi) \\
&= 4 \cdot \sin(215.54°) \\
&= 4 \times (-0.5774) \\
&\approx -2.31 \text{ NM}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates the target is moving to the **port side** of own vessel's heading reference. The **CPA magnitude is 2.31 NM** (magnitude is what matters for collision assessment).

**Step 3 (Corrected): CPA Calculation with Proper Sign Convention**

Reorienting with bearing 090° directly:

$$
\text{CPA} = \lvert R \cdot \sin(090° - 234.46°) \rvert = \lvert 4 \cdot \sin(-144.46°) \rvert \approx 2.31 \text{ NM}
$$

**(iii) Answer: $\text{CPA} \approx 2.31$ NM**

**Step 4 (Recalculated): TCPA**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCPA} &= \frac{R \cdot \cos(\phi)}{V_{\text{rel}}} \\
&= \frac{4 \cdot \cos(215.54°)}{17.20} \\
&= \frac{4 \times (-0.8165)}{17.20} \\
&\approx -0.19 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **negative TCPA** indicates the CPA has **already passed** — vessels are now diverging.

**(iv) Answer: $\text{TCPA} \approx -0.19$ minutes (CPA already passed, vessels diverging)**

**Required Maneuver per Rule 15**:

Since Vessel B is on the **starboard side** of Vessel A, **Vessel A is the give-way vessel** and must:
- **Alter course to starboard** (preferred per Rule 8c) — minimum **30° alteration** to pass astern of Vessel B
- OR **reduce speed** to increase CPA further

If the CPA had been less than the safe threshold (1 NM) and TCPA positive, **immediate action** would be required. Given the current calculation, the situation is **already resolving**, but the maneuver should still be executed to ensure **safe passing distance** as required by Rule 8(d).

**Mark Distribution (7 marks)**: Velocity components (2) + Magnitude and direction (1.5) + CPA and TCPA (2) + Maneuver decision (1.5).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Explain the hierarchy of vessel priority under Rule 18 of the COLREGs. Discuss the special cases of vessels not under command (NUC) and vessels constrained by draft.**

**Model Answer:**

**Rule 18 — Responsibilities Between Vessels** establishes a clear **priority hierarchy** based on vessel type and operational status. The rule addresses four special categories of vessels:

**1. Vessel Not Under Command (NUC)** — A vessel which through some exceptional circumstance is unable to maneuver as required by these Rules and is therefore unable to keep out of the way of another vessel. NUC vessels display:
- **Two all-round red lights** vertically (night)
- **Two black balls** vertically (day)
- **Sound signal**: Five short blasts at intervals of not more than 1 minute

All other powered and sailing vessels (except those also unable to maneuver) must keep clear of NUC.

**2. Vessel Restricted in Ability to Maneuver (RAM)** — A vessel which from the nature of her work is restricted in her ability to maneuver as required by these Rules and is therefore unable to keep out of the way of another vessel. RAM vessels are engaged in activities such as:
- Laying or servicing navigational marks, submarine cables, or pipelines
- Dredging, surveying, or underwater operations
- Replenishment or transfer operations at sea
- Launching or recovery of aircraft
- Mine clearance operations

RAM vessels display **ball-diamond-ball** shapes and **red-white-red lights**.

**3. Vessel Constrained by Draft (CBD)** — A power-driven vessel which, because of her draft in relation to the available depth and width of navigable water, is severely restricted in her ability to deviate from the course she is following. CBD vessels display **cylinder** shapes (day) and **three all-round red lights** (night).

**4. Sailing Vessel** vs **Fishing Vessel** vs **Power-driven Vessel** — Among these, the priority order (highest to lowest) is: Fishing > Sailing > Power-driven.

**Complete Priority Hierarchy**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Highest Priority (Must Be Avoided By All Others)} \\
&\text{NUC} \rightarrow \text{RAM} \rightarrow \text{CBD} \\
&\textbf{Mid Priority} \\
&\text{Fishing (engaged)} \rightarrow \text{Sailing Vessel} \\
&\textbf{Lowest Priority (Must Keep Clear)} \\
&\text{Power-driven Vessel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-references**: A power-driven vessel underway must keep out of the way of sailing vessels and fishing vessels (Rule 18). However, a sailing vessel underway must keep out of the way of NUC, RAM, and CBD vessels.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks)**: Priority list (3) + NUC and CBD explanation (3) + Visual/sound signals (1).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Discuss the rules applicable to vessels navigating in restricted visibility under Rule 19. Why is Rule 19 considered a "catch-all" rule?**

**Model Answer:**

**Rule 19 — Conduct of Vessels in Restricted Visibility** is a single, comprehensive rule applicable to **all vessel types** when proceeding in or near an area of restricted visibility (fog, mist, snowfall, heavy rainstorms, sandstorms, or any other similar phenomenon).

**Core Provisions of Rule 19**:

**1. Safe Speed (Rule 19 requirement)**: Every vessel shall proceed at a **safe speed adapted to the prevailing circumstances and conditions of restricted visibility**. The speed must allow:
- Proper use of radar (if fitted) for early detection
- Ability to take effective avoiding action
- Stopping within the available visibility distance

**2. Engines Ready for Immediate Maneuver**: Every vessel shall have her **engines ready for immediate maneuver**. This means:
- Main engines on standby with telegraph tested
- Personnel alert at the maneuvering position
- No scheduled maintenance or operation preventing immediate response

**3. Radar Watch**: Vessels with operational radar shall use it **properly**, including **long-range scanning** for early detection. Continuous radar watch is essential.

**4. Avoidance of Head-on Crossing**: As a general rule in restricted visibility, vessels should:
- **Avoid crossing ahead** of another vessel at close range
- **Reduce speed** or take all way off when an approaching vessel is detected by radar alone and risk of collision is suspected
- If in doubt, **assume risk of collision exists** (Rule 7(c) principle)

**Why Rule 19 is Considered a "Catch-All" Rule**:

**1. Universal Applicability**: Unlike Rules 12–18 (which apply only when vessels are in sight of one another), Rule 19 applies to **all vessel types simultaneously** — NUC, RAM, CBD, fishing, sailing, and power-driven.

**2. No Give-way/Stand-on Distinction**: Rule 19 **eliminates** the typical give-way/stand-on classification. All vessels have **equal responsibility** to take avoiding action when in doubt.

**3. Procedural Not Encounter-Based**: Rule 19 governs **what to do** (safe speed, engines ready) rather than **who must maneuver** (which vessel is give-way).

**4. Sound Signal Compliance**: In restricted visibility, vessels must sound appropriate **fog signals** per Rule 35:
- Power-driven vessel making way: One prolonged blast every 2 minutes
- Power-driven vessel stopped: Two prolonged blasts every 2 minutes
- Vessel NUC, RAM, CBD: One prolonged + two short blasts every 2 minutes

**Engineering Insight**: Modern vessels supplement Rule 19 with **AIS-based decision support**, **integrated bridge systems (IBS)**, and **forward-looking infrared (FLIR)** to enhance situational awareness in low-visibility conditions.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks)**: Core provisions (3) + Reasons for catch-all status (3) + Sound signals (1).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
>
> 1. **Confusing Rules 13 and 14**: Students frequently confuse overtaking (Rule 13) with head-on situations (Rule 14). Remember: **Overtaking is from abaft the beam** (>22.5°); **head-on is ahead or nearly ahead**.
>
> 2. **Ignoring Rule 8(b)**: Students often describe "small alterations" in their answers. Rule 8(b) **explicitly forbids** successive small alterations. Minimum **30° course change** is required.
>
> 3. **Misapplying Rule 17(d)**: In a crossing situation, the stand-on vessel **must NOT alter to port** for a vessel on her own port side. Many candidates fail to recall this nuance.
>
> 4. **Forgetting CPA/TCPA Sign Convention**: A **negative TCPA** means CPA is in the **past** (vessels diverging). A **positive TCPA** with **CPA < 1 NM** means active collision risk.
>
> 5. **Skipping Rule 18 hierarchy**: When asked about vessel priority, students often list vessel types but **fail to order them** hierarchically. Always present in **descending priority order**.
>
> 6. **Neglecting Rule 9 narrow channels**: In narrow channels, vessels must keep to the **starboard side** as near as safe and practicable — a frequent miss in KTU answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Examinations**:

- [ ] **Rule 5**: Look-out is **continuous** by all available means.
- [ ] **Rule 6**: Safe speed depends on **visibility, traffic, maneuverability, and draft**.
- [ ] **Rule 7**: If **any doubt exists**, risk of collision **shall be deemed to exist**.
- [ ] **Rule 8(a–e)**: Positive action, substantial alteration, prefer course change, safe passing distance, speed reduction last.
- [ ] **Rule 8(b)**: Minimum **30° course alteration** — no small successive changes.
- [ ] **Rule 13**: Overtaking limit is **22.5° abaft the beam**.
- [ ] **Rule 14**: Head-on — **both alter to starboard** and pass port-to-port.
- [ ] **Rule 15**: Crossing — vessel with other on **starboard side gives way**.
- [ ] **Rule 16**: Give-way vessel takes **early and substantial** action.
- [ ] **Rule 17(a)**: Stand-on vessel maintains course and speed.
- [ ] **Rule 17(d)**: Stand-on vessel **cannot alter to port** for vessel on port side.
- [ ] **Rule 18 hierarchy**: NUC > RAM > CBD > Fishing > Sailing > Power.
- [ ] **Rule 19**: Catch-all rule for **restricted visibility** — safe speed and engines ready.
- [ ] **CPA safe threshold**: **1.0 NM** in open sea.
- [ ] **CPA formula**: $\text{CPA} = R \sin(\phi)$.
- [ ] **TCPA formula**: $\text{TCPA} = \dfrac{R \cos(\phi)}{V_{\text{rel}}}$.
- [ ] **Relative velocity**: $\vec{V}_{\text{rel}} = \vec{V}_{\text{target}} - \vec{V}_{\text{own}}$.
- [ ] **Standard maneuver**: Always pass astern of give-way vessel.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COLREGs — Formal Definition**
> The **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs)**, commonly called the "**Rules of the Road at Sea**," are a set of international rules established by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** that govern the conduct of vessels at sea to prevent collisions. The current version was adopted at the **1972 Conference** and entered into force on **15 July 1977**. They apply to all vessels upon the **high seas** and all waters connected therewith navigable by seagoing vessels.

The COLREGs consist of **38 Rules** organized into **six parts (Parts A through F)** plus **four annexes** containing technical specifications for lights, shapes, and sound signal appliances.

### Six-Part Architecture of COLREGs

| Part | Title | Rule Numbers | Primary Focus |
|------|-------|--------------|---------------|
| **A** | General | Rules 1–3 | Application, responsibility, definitions |
| **B** | Steering and Sailing | Rules 4–19 | Conduct of vessels in any visibility |
| **C** | Lights and Shapes | Rules 20–31 | Light/shape configuration |
| **D** | Sound and Light Signals | Rules 32–37 | Manoeuvring & warning signals |
| **E** | Exemptions | Rule 38 | Special allowances |
| **Annexes** | I–IV | Technical specs | Lights, shapes, sound appliances |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Traffic Law" of the Oceans

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Think of the Sea as a Roundabout"**
> Imagine driving on a highway where there are no painted lanes, no traffic lights, no stop signs, and every vehicle is potentially the size of a building moving at highway speeds in **fog, rain, or pitch darkness**. Now imagine you cannot easily see other vehicles, and a collision means catastrophic loss of life and environmental disaster. **COLREGs are the universal "driving test rules" that every mariner must learn before being allowed to "drive" a ship.** Just as road traffic laws establish who has the right of way, how to overtake, and what signals to use, COLREGs do exactly the same — but for the world's oceans.

The analogy maps cleanly:

| Road Traffic | Maritime Equivalent (COLREGs) |
|--------------|-------------------------------|
| Headlights & brake lights | Navigation lights (masthead, stern, sidelights) |
| Turn signals (indicators) | Whistle signals (1 short = altering to starboard) |
| Yield / Stop signs | Stand-on vs. Give-way vessel relationship |
| Right-of-way at intersections | "Vessel with right of way" priority rules |
| Lane markings | Stand-on / Give-way / Cross-bearing sectors |

---

## 1.3 Why COLREGs Exist — The Historical Catalyst

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin Trigger: The SS *Titanic* Disaster (1912)**
> Although COLREGs were codified in 1972, their roots trace back to the **1912 Titanic disaster**, which exposed the fatal absence of standardized maritime rules. The first international rules were adopted in **1889** (following the sinking of the British frigate HMS *Victoria* in 1893, often called the "Mediterranean Titanic"), and have been revised repeatedly — culminating in the **1972 COLREGs** which remain in force globally today.

---

## 1.4 The Three Foundational Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of COLREG Philosophy (Rules 2 & 5)**
> 1. **Rule 2 — Responsibility**: Nothing in the Rules exonerates any vessel, owner, master, or crew from the consequences of neglecting to comply or taking necessary precautions.
> 2. **Rule 5 — Lookout**: Every vessel must maintain a proper lookout by **sight, hearing, and all available means** at all times to make a full appraisal of the situation.
> 3. **Rule 8 — Action to Avoid Collision**: Action taken to avoid collision must be **positive, made in ample time, and follow good seamanship.**

These three rules act as an "**overarching safety net**" — even if no specific rule applies, mariners must still act prudently.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 38 Rules — Logical Decomposition

### **Part A: General Rules (Rules 1–3)**

| Rule | Title | Core Mandate |
|------|-------|--------------|
| **Rule 1** | Application | Applies to all vessels on the high seas and connected navigable waters. |
| **Rule 2** | Responsibility | No relief from consequences of neglect, precaution, or customary practice. |
| **Rule 3** | General Definitions | Defines key terms like "vessel," "power-driven vessel," "underway," etc. |

### **Part B: Steering and Sailing Rules (Rules 4–19)**

#### **Rules 4–10 — Conduct in Any Visibility**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 5 — Lookout (The "Eyes of the Ship")**
> Every vessel must at all times maintain a proper lookout by **sight AND hearing** as well as by all available means appropriate in the prevailing circumstances. This is the **most-cited rule in collision litigation.**

**Rule 6 — Safe Speed**
A vessel must proceed at a **safe speed** so that she can take proper and effective action to avoid collision. The factors determining safe speed include:
- State of visibility
- Traffic density (including fishing vessels)
- Maneuverability of the vessel (with particular reference to stopping distance and turning ability)
- At night — presence of background lights
- Wind, sea, current, and proximity of navigational hazards
- Draft in relation to available water

**Rule 7 — Risk of Collision (The CPA/TCPA Test)**
Risk of collision shall be deemed to exist if the **compass bearing of an approaching vessel does not appreciably change**. Mariners use the modern equivalent: **CPA (Closest Point of Approach) ≤ safe distance AND TCPA (Time to CPA) ≤ alert threshold**.

**Rule 8 — Action to Avoid Collision**
- Action must be **positive, timely, and follow good seamanship**
- Alterations of course and/or speed must be **large enough to be readily apparent** to other vessels (a small alteration is the *worst* maneuver)
- If necessary, **reduce speed, stop, or reverse**

**Rule 9 — Narrow Channels**
- A vessel proceeding along a narrow channel must keep **as near to the outer limit of the channel on her starboard side as is safe and practicable**
- A vessel of less than **20 meters** or a sailing vessel must not impede the passage of a larger power-driven vessel confined to the channel

**Rule 10 — Traffic Separation Schemes (TSS)**
- Vessels using a TSS must follow the general flow of traffic in the correct direction
- Cross only when necessary; entry/exit at terminals
- Avoid crossing traffic lanes; if joining/leaving, do so at small angles

#### **Rules 11–18 — Conduct in Sight of One Another**

| Situation | Give-way (Burdened) | Stand-on (Privileged) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Power vs. Power (Crossing) | The vessel which has the other on her **starboard** | The vessel which has the other on her **port** |
| Power vs. Power (Head-on) | Both alter course **to starboard** | Both alter course **to starboard** |
| Power vs. Sail | Power-driven vessel | Sailing vessel |
| Sail vs. Sail | Wind on **port** | Wind on **starboard** |
| Overtaking | Overtaking vessel (Rule 13) | The vessel being overtaken |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 13 — Overtaking (The "Definitional Rule")**
> A vessel is deemed to be overtaking when she approaches another from **more than 22.5° abaft her beam** (i.e., from the stern sector). She remains an overtaking vessel until **she is finally past and clear**. The overtaking vessel keeps out of the way of the vessel being overtaken — **regardless of the latter's category.**

**Rule 14 — Head-on Situation**
When two power-driven vessels are meeting on **reciprocal or nearly reciprocal courses** with the other vessel **forward of the beam**, both must alter course **to starboard** so that each passes on the **port side of the other**.

**Rule 15 — Crossing Situation**
When two power-driven vessels are crossing and the risk of collision exists, the vessel which has the other on her **starboard side** must keep out of the way (give way). The other (stand-on) vessel must **maintain course and speed**.

**Rule 18 — Responsibilities Between Vessels**
Hierarchy of priority (from least to most privileged):
1. Power-driven vessel (least privileged against others below)
2. Sailing vessel
3. Fishing vessel (engaged in trawling)
4. Vessel restricted in her ability to maneuver (RAM)
5. Vessel constrained by her draft (CBD)
6. Vessel not under command (NUC) — **most privileged**

#### **Rule 19 — Conduct in Restricted Visibility (Fog)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 19 — The "Fog Rule"**
> In restricted visibility, every vessel must proceed at a **safe speed adapted to circumstances**. Power-driven vessels must have their **engines ready for immediate maneuver**. The most important aspect: **avoid crossing ahead** of another vessel in fog.

---

### **Part C: Lights and Shapes (Rules 20–31)**

**Rule 20 — Application**
- Lights from **sunset to sunrise** (and in restricted visibility)
- Shapes must be displayed **during daylight** in restricted visibility

**Rule 21 — Definitions of Lights**

| Light | Color | Arc of Visibility | Min. Range (≥50m vessel) |
|-------|-------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Masthead | White | 225° (front) | 6 nautical miles |
| Sidelight (Port) | Red | 112.5° | 3 nautical miles |
| Sidelight (Starboard) | Green | 112.5° | 3 nautical miles |
| Stern | White | 135° | 3 nautical miles |
| Towing | Yellow (over stern) | 135° | 3 nautical miles |
| All-round | White/Red/Green/Yellow | 360° | 3 nautical miles |

**Rule 23 — Power-Driven Vessels Underway**
- Masthead light forward
- Sidelights (port red, starboard green)
- Stern light

**Rule 24 — Towing and Pushing**
- A towing vessel displays **two masthead lights in a vertical line** (the second is also white, positioned forward or aft of the first)
- A vessel being towed displays sidelights and a stern light
- If tow length exceeds **200 m**, an additional masthead light on the aftermast

**Rule 27 — Vessel Not Under Command (NUC)**
- **Two all-round red lights in a vertical line**
- Day shape: **Two black balls in a vertical line**

**Rule 28 — Vessel Restricted in Ability to Maneuver (RAM)**
- **Red-White-Red all-round lights vertically** + sidelights + stern light
- Day shape: **Ball-Diamond-Ball** vertically

**Rule 30 — Anchored Vessels**
- All-round **white** light (or ball) at the **foremast** + another at the **stern** (vessels >50 m)
- Day shape: **One black ball** in the forward part

---

### **Part D: Sound and Light Signals (Rules 32–37)**

**Rule 34 — Maneuvering and Warning Signals (Whistle Signals)**

| Signal | Meaning | Applies To |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 1 short blast | I am altering my course to **starboard** | Power underway, making way |
| 2 short blasts | I am altering my course to **port** | Power underway, making way |
| 3 short blasts | I am operating **astern** (astern propulsion) | Power underway |
| 5 short blasts | **Danger / doubt** of your intentions | Any vessel near |
| 4 short blasts | (Not defined in COLREGs — used for special cases like fishing) | — |

**Rule 35 — Sound Signals in Restricted Visibility**

| Vessel Type | Signal | Meaning |
|-------------|--------|---------|
| Power underway, making way | 1 prolonged blast every 2 min | "Here I am" |
| Power underway, stopped | 2 prolonged blasts every 2 min | "Here I am, stopped" |
| Vessel at anchor | Rapid ringing of bell for 5 sec every 1 min | "I'm anchored" |
| Vessel aground | Bell + gong sequence + 3 separate bell strokes | "I'm aground" |
| Pilot vessel on duty | 4 short blasts (identity signal) | Pilot vessel |

**Rule 36 — Signals to Attract Attention**
Any vessel may use light or sound signals (e.g., flashing light, searchlight) to attract attention, **provided they cannot be mistaken** for any other authorized signal.

**Rule 37 — Distress Signals**
At least **12 different distress signals** are listed in Annex IV, including:
- Continuous sounding of foghorn
- Flames on the vessel
- Rocket parachute flares
- Square flag with ball above/below
- Mayday by radio (voice or DSC)
- Slowly and repeatedly raising and lowering outstretched arms

---

### **Part E: Exemptions (Rule 38)**
- Vessels built before the COLREGs took effect (15 July 1977) were given grace periods for compliance with certain light/shape specifications.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Reference Summary Table — Decision-Making Logic**

| Situation | Visible Bearing | Action Required | Whistle Signal |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Head-on (Power vs. Power) | Forward of beam, reciprocal | Both alter to **starboard** | 1 short blast each |
| Crossing (Power vs. Power) | Other on **starboard** | You are **give-way** | 1 short (alter stbd) or 2 short (alter port) |
| Overtaking | More than 22.5° abaft beam | You are **give-way** | 1 or 2 short blasts |
| Power vs. Sail | Sail anywhere | Power is **give-way** | 1 or 2 short blasts |
| Restricted Visibility | Fog/Rain/Heavy spray | Safe speed, engines ready | 1 long blast / 2 min |
| Anchored | At anchor | 360° white light + ball | Bell 5 sec / 1 min |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mariners and Naval Architects Study COLREGs**
> 1. **Naval Architecture**: Bridge visibility requirements depend on Rule 5 (lookout) and Rule 22 (position of lights) — affects superstructure design.
> 2. **Marine Electronics**: ARPA (Automatic Radar Plotting Aid) and ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display and Information System) encode COLREG-compliant target symbols.
> 3. **Marine Insurance**: Collision liability cases hinge on Rule 2, Rule 5, Rule 7, and Rule 8 violations.
> 4. **Port State Control**: Inspectors check light configurations and signal equipment against Annex I specifications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The "Colregs Decision Tree" Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a "Derivation" for a Regulatory Topic?**
> Although COLREGs are legal text, the **application logic** is fully algorithmic. Below, we derive the official IMO decision-flow as a rigorous, step-by-step procedure — exactly how a mariner, OOW (Officer of the Watch), or autopilot algorithm would apply them.

---

### **Step 1: Initial Visibility Assessment**

A mariner must first determine the visibility condition:

$$
\text{Visibility} = \begin{cases} \text{Restricted} & \text{if visibility} < 2 \text{ nautical miles} \\ \text{Clear} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

- If **Restricted Visibility** → Apply **Rule 19** (proceed at safe speed, engines ready, fog signals every 2 minutes).
- If **Clear Visibility** AND vessels are **in sight of one another** → Proceed to Step 2.

---

### **Step 2: Vessel Type Classification**

Each vessel must be classified by category (from lowest to highest priority):

$$
\text{Category} = \begin{cases} \text{NUC} & \text{Rule 3(f), Rule 27} \\ \text{CBD} & \text{Rule 3(h), Rule 28} \\ \text{RAM} & \text{Rule 3(g), Rule 27} \\ \text{Fishing (trawling)} & \text{Rule 3(c), Rule 26} \\ \text{Fishing (other)} & \text{Rule 26(c)} \\ \text{Sailing} & \text{Rule 25} \\ \text{Power-driven} & \text{Rule 23} \end{cases}
$$

---

### **Step 3: Determine the Geometric Relationship**

The bearing of the other vessel from your vessel determines the encounter type:

$$
\theta = \text{Compass bearing of other vessel (relative to own ship's heading)}
$$

$$
\text{Encounter} = \begin{cases} \text{Overtaking} & \text{if } \theta \in (112.5°, 247.5°) \text{ i.e., } > 22.5° \text{ abaft beam} \\ \text{Head-on} & \text{if } \theta \in (355°, 5°) \text{ reciprocal courses} \\ \text{Crossing (other on stbd)} & \text{if } \theta \in (5°, 112.5°) \\ \text{Crossing (other on port)} & \text{if } \theta \in (247.5°, 355°) \end{cases}
$$

---

### **Step 4: Assign Roles (Give-way / Stand-on / Cross-equal)**

$$
\text{Role} = \begin{cases} \text{Give-way} & \text{if your category is lower priority AND you are the burdened vessel} \\ \text{Stand-on} & \text{if you have priority} \\ \text{Both alter to stbd} & \text{in head-on, both alter} \end{cases}
$$

**Rule 13 Override:** If overtaking, the overtaking vessel is **always** give-way, regardless of category.

---

### **Step 5: Apply Maneuver**

For the give-way vessel, alteration must be:

$$
\Delta \text{Course} \geq 30° \text{ (Rule 8 — "large enough to be readily apparent")}
$$

The sign of $\Delta$Course is determined by which side the other vessel is on (right-of-way traffic pattern).

**For the stand-on vessel (Rules 17):**
- Initially: **Maintain course and speed**
- If give-way vessel is **not taking appropriate action** → stand-on may **sound 5 short blasts** and may take **avoiding action**

---

### **Step 6: Sound Signal Confirmation (Rule 34)**

$$
\text{Whistle} = \begin{cases} 1 \text{ short} & \Delta \text{Course} = \text{Starboard} \\ 2 \text{ short} & \Delta \text{Course} = \text{Port} \\ 3 \text{ short} & \text{Astern propulsion} \\ 5 \text{ short} & \text{Doubt / Danger signal} \end{cases}
$$

Each "short blast" = approximately **1 second**. A "prolonged blast" = **4 to 6 seconds**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Light Recognition Decision Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading another vessel's lights — the mariner's "diagnostic" tool**
> When a mariner sights lights at night, the configuration reveals the vessel's category and aspect. Below is the analytical matrix a student must internalize.

### **Sighted at Night — Power-Driven Vessel (Rule 23)**

| Lights Visible | Aspect | Interpretation |
|----------------|--------|----------------|
| Red only (sidelight) | Other vessel approaching from your port | Crossing from port — **you are stand-on** (Rule 15) |
| Green only (sidelight) | Other vessel approaching from your starboard | Crossing from stbd — **you are give-way** (Rule 15) |
| Red + Green together (sidelights) | Other vessel heading directly at you | **Head-on** — both alter stbd (Rule 14) |
| White only (stern light) | Other vessel moving away | **Overtaken** — safe (you may be overtaking) |
| Three white lights (masthead + stern + range) | Vessel ahead, in same direction, very close | **Overtaking target** — Rule 13 |

### **Sighted at Night — Special Vessels (Rule 27, 28, 30)**

| Light Configuration | Day Shape | Meaning |
|---------------------|-----------|---------|
| 2 red lights vertical | 2 black balls vertical | **NUC** (Not Under Command) |
| Red-White-Red vertical | Ball-Diamond-Ball vertical | **RAM** (Restricted in Ability to Maneuver) |
| 3 red lights vertical | 3 black balls vertical | Vessel aground + NUC |
| Red over White (2 all-round) | 2 cones apex together | Fishing vessel (trawling) |
| Single white all-round (anchored, <50m) | 1 black ball | At anchor |
| Two white lights (anchored, ≥50m) | 1 black ball (forward) | At anchor (large vessel) |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Crossing Situation — Full Logical Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scenario**: Vessel **A** (power-driven, 200 m cargo ship) is on course **090°** at 15 knots. Vessel **B** (power-driven, 150 m tanker) is on course **000°** at 12 knots. Visibility is clear, and the CPA is **0.3 nm** with TCPA = **8 minutes**. Both vessels are in sight of one another.

### **Step-by-step Application:**

**Step 1 — Visibility:** Clear, both in sight → Apply Rules 4–18 (not Rule 19).

**Step 2 — Categories:** Both are power-driven vessels → Rule 18(a) applies.

**Step 3 — Geometric Relationship:**
- B's bearing from A = ~000° (forward of A's beam, on the port side)
- This is a **crossing situation** with B on A's **port** side.
- By Rule 15: A is the **stand-on vessel**; B is the **give-way vessel**.

**Step 4 — Roles:**
$$
\text{Role}_A = \text{Stand-on (maintain course and speed)} \quad \text{(Rule 15)}
$$
$$
\text{Role}_B = \text{Give-way (alter course to avoid)} \quad \text{(Rule 15)}
$$

**Step 5 — B's Required Action (Rule 8):**
- B must alter to **starboard** (pass astern of A) — this is a "right-handed" road convention.
- Alteration must be **≥ 30°** to be readily apparent.
- Speed reduction is **not** a substitute for course change but may be combined.

**Step 6 — B's Sound Signal (Rule 34):**
- B sounds **1 short blast** ("I am altering to starboard").

**Step 7 — A's Response (Rule 17):**
- A maintains course and speed initially.
- If A observes that B is **not taking appropriate action** (e.g., B is not altering, or CPA remains dangerous), A may:
  - Sound **5 short blasts** (danger signal)
  - Take **independent avoiding action** — typically, A may alter to starboard to "draw aft" the contact (never cross ahead of B).

**Step 8 — Final Verification:**
- After maneuver, monitor new bearing — confirm the bearing **changes appreciably** (Rule 7) → risk of collision is reducing.
- Continue to monitor CPA/TCPA values.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error**
> Forgetting that the **stand-on vessel is not absolved from responsibility** under Rule 17. The stand-on vessel must act if the give-way vessel fails to do so. A common exam trap: "A is the stand-on, so A can do nothing" — **WRONG**. A must still take action if B is not maneuvering properly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: COLREG Architecture Overview

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[COLREGs 1972]
    root --> partA[Part A General Rules 1 to 3]
    root --> partB[Part B Steering and Sailing Rules 4 to 19]
    root --> partC[Part C Lights and Shapes Rules 20 to 31]
    root --> partD[Part D Sound and Light Signals Rules 32 to 37]
    root --> partE[Part E Exemptions Rule 38]
    root --> annex[Annexes I to IV]

    partA --> r1[Rule 1 Application]
    partA --> r2[Rule 2 Responsibility]
    partA --> r3[Rule 3 Definitions]

    partB --> vis[Visibility Check]
    vis --> clear[In Sight]
    vis --> restricted[Restricted Visibility Rule 19]
    clear --> conduct[Rules 4 to 18]
    conduct --> power[Power vs Power]
    conduct --> sail[Power vs Sail]
    conduct --> fish[Fishing Vessels]
    conduct --> special[Special Vessels]

    partC --> lights[Light Configurations]
    partC --> shapes[Day Shapes]

    partD --> maneuv[Maneuvering Signals Rule 34]
    partD --> fog[Fog Signals Rule 35]
    partD --> attention[Attention Signals Rule 36]
    partD --> distress[Distress Signals Rule 37]

    annex --> an1[Annex I Lights and Shapes Specs]
    annex --> an2[Annex II Sound Signal Appliances]
    annex --> an3[Annex III Additional Signals for Fishing]
    annex --> an4[Annex IV Distress Signals]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Light Configuration by Vessel Category

```mermaid
graph TD
    cat[Vessel Type]
    cat --> power[Power Driven]
    cat --> sail[Sailing Vessel]
    cat --> nuc[Not Under Command]
    cat --> ram[Restricted in Maneuver]
    cat --> anchor[At Anchor]

    power --> pLight[Masthead White 225 deg plus Port Red 112.5 plus Starboard Green 112.5 plus Stern White 135 deg]
    sail --> sLight[Sidelights Red Green plus Stern White plus Optional Tricolor at Masthead]
    nuc --> nLight[Two Red All Round Vertical]
    ram --> rLight[Red White Red All Round Vertical plus Sidelights plus Stern]
    anchor --> aLight[White All Round at Foremast plus Second White at Stern if 50m plus]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Encounter Geometry & Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    encounter[Encounter Type]
    encounter --> over[Overtaking more than 22.5 deg abaft beam]
    encounter --> head[Head On Reciprocal Bearing Forward of Beam]
    encounter --> cross[Crossing Other on Port or Starboard Bow]

    over --> overAct[You are Give Way Vessel Rule 13]
    head --> headAct[Both Alter to Starboard Rule 14]
    cross --> crossPort[Other on Starboard You are Give Way]
    cross --> crossStbd[Other on Port You are Stand On]
    crossPort --> crossAct[Alter to Starboard and Pass Astern]
    crossStbd --> crossStand[Maintain Course and Speed Rule 17]
    crossStand --> crossFail[If Give Way Fails Sound 5 Short Blasts and Maneuver]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Whistle Signal Logic (Rule 34)

```mermaid
graph TD
    sig[Required Signal]
    sig --> s1[1 Short Blast Altering Starboard]
    sig --> s2[2 Short Blasts Altering Port]
    sig --> s3[3 Short Blasts Operating Astern]
    sig --> s5[5 Short Blasts Danger or Doubt Signal]
    sig --> s4Fog[1 Prolonged Blast every 2 min Fog Making Way]
    sig --> s5Fog[2 Prolonged Blasts every 2 min Fog Stopped]
    sig --> bell[Bell 5 sec every 1 min Anchored]
    sig --> gong[Gong plus Bell Aground]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Vessel Priority Hierarchy (Rule 18)

```mermaid
graph TD
    prio[Priority Order Lowest to Highest]
    prio --> p1[Power Driven Vessel]
    prio --> p2[Sailing Vessel]
    prio --> p3[Fishing Trawling]
    prio --> p4[Fishing Other Gear]
    prio --> p5[Restricted in Ability to Maneuver]
    prio --> p6[Constrained by Draft]
    prio --> p7[Not Under Command]
    p7 --> top[Highest Priority Must Be Avoided by All]
```

## 4.6 ASCII Schematic — Light Arc Coverage (Rule 21)

```
                000° (Forward / Bow)
                  |
                  |  MASTHEAD LIGHT (White, 225°, from -11.25° to +011.25°
                  |     crossing forward)
                  |
       STARBOARD  |  PORT
       Sidelight  |  Sidelight
       (Green)    |  (Red)
       112.5°     |  112.5°
       arc        |  arc
    __________|  |__________
   /           |           \
  /            |            \
 /             |             \
Stern ---  STERN LIGHT ---  Stern
              (White)
              135° arc
              (from 112.5° aft stbd to 112.5° aft port)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic**: This is a top-down view of a power-driven vessel under sail. The masthead light (white) covers the forward 225° (from 11.25° on the port side, around the bow, to 11.25° on the starboard side). The two sidelights each cover 112.5° starting from the bow. The stern light covers the rear 135°. Together they give a complete 360° halo of visible light at night.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: Define "proper lookout" as per COLREGs. State the Rule number and explain the responsibilities of a mariner in maintaining it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Rule 5** of the COLREGs deals with the "Lookout." It states that every vessel must at all times maintain a proper lookout by **sight and hearing**, as well as by **all available means appropriate in the prevailing circumstances**, so as to make a full appraisal of the situation and of the risk of collision.

**Responsibilities include:**
1. Continuous visual scanning of the horizon and surrounding waters for vessels, navigation marks, hazards, and obstacles (1 mark).
2. Use of hearing to detect fog signals, whistles, and other audio cues — particularly important in restricted visibility (1 mark).
3. Use of all available means such as **radar, ARPA, AIS (Automatic Identification System), and ECDIS** to detect contacts beyond visual range or in poor visibility (1 mark).

The lookout must be a **dedicated person** (not the same individual as the helmsman or the navigating officer under normal circumstances) and must be maintained **continuously**, not just at fixed intervals.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between a "vessel not under command" (NUC) and a "vessel restricted in her ability to maneuver" (RAM). State the lights and shapes each displays.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | NUC (Rule 3(f), Rule 27) | RAM (Rule 3(g), Rule 28) |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Definition** | A vessel which through some exceptional circumstance is unable to maneuver as required by the Rules (e.g., steering/rudder failure) | A vessel which from the nature of her work is restricted in her ability to maneuver (e.g., diving, cable-laying, dredging, replenishment at sea) |
| **Night Lights** | Two all-round **red** lights in a vertical line (1 mark) | **Red-White-Red** all-round lights in vertical line, plus sidelights and stern light (1 mark) |
| **Day Shape** | Two **black balls** in a vertical line (0.5 mark) | **Ball-Diamond-Ball** in vertical line (0.5 mark) |

> **Distinction Summary**: An NUC vessel is **incapable** of maneuvering (forced by emergency), while a RAM vessel is **deliberately limited** by her operational task. Both enjoy high priority under Rule 18, but NUC ranks higher.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**Q: (a) Explain the steering and sailing rules applicable to power-driven vessels meeting head-on, crossing, and overtaking situations. (7 marks)**

**(b) With the aid of a diagram, describe the configuration and arc of visibility of navigation lights for a power-driven vessel underway. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part A(a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Head-on Situation — Rule 14 (2 marks):**
When two power-driven vessels are meeting on **reciprocal or nearly reciprocal courses** with the other vessel **forward of the beam**, each vessel must alter course to **starboard** so that each passes on the **port side** of the other. Both vessels must sound **1 short blast** on the whistle to indicate the alteration. This rule ensures standardized, predictable passing (port-to-port) and eliminates ambiguity.

**2. Crossing Situation — Rule 15 (3 marks):**
When two power-driven vessels are crossing and risk of collision exists, the vessel which has the other on her **starboard side** is the **give-way (burdened) vessel** and must keep out of the way. The vessel with the other on her **port** is the **stand-on (privileged) vessel** and must **maintain course and speed**. This is the "right-hand rule" — the same convention used in road traffic. The give-way vessel alters course, typically to starboard, to pass astern of the stand-on vessel.

**3. Overtaking Situation — Rule 13 (2 marks):**
A vessel approaching another from **more than 22.5° abaft the beam** is deemed to be overtaking. The overtaking vessel keeps out of the way of the vessel being overtaken, **regardless of the latter's category** (power, sail, NUC, etc.). She remains an overtaking vessel until she is **finally past and clear**. The overtaking vessel sounds **1 or 2 short blasts** to indicate her alteration.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Power-driven vessel underway (length ≥ 50 m) — Rule 23:**

| Light | Color | Arc | Purpose |
|-------|-------|-----|---------|
| Masthead | White | 225° (from 11.25° port to 11.25° stbd via the bow) | Indicates vessel's forward arc |
| Port sidelight | Red | 112.5° (from forward to 22.5° abaft beam) | Indicates port side |
| Starboard sidelight | Green | 112.5° | Indicates starboard side |
| Stern | White | 135° (from 22.5° abaft beam each side via stern) | Indicates vessel's stern |

**Diagram:**

```
                  Bow (000°)
                    |
            Masthead (White, 225°)
            /                 \
           /                   \
   --- 11.25° |              11.25° ---
              |  RED (port) | GREEN (stbd)
              |   112.5°    |  112.5°
              |              |
              |   ___________|
              |  /           
       Stern  | Stern Light (White, 135°)
              |  
```

**Visibility Ranges (≥ 50 m vessel):** Masthead = 6 nm, Sidelights = 3 nm, Stern = 3 nm (2 marks for ranges and arcs).

**Practical significance (1 mark):** The configuration allows a mariner to determine the **aspect** (bearing) of another vessel at night:
- See **red only** → other vessel on your port bow (crossing from port)
- See **green only** → other vessel on your starboard bow (crossing from starboard — you give way)
- See **red + green together** → head-on situation
- See **white stern light** → vessel moving away

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: (a) State and explain the whistle signals prescribed in Rule 34 of the COLREGs for vessels in sight of one another. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the conduct of vessels in restricted visibility as per Rule 19. What are the fog signals for a power-driven vessel underway making way and for a vessel at anchor? (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Rule 34 — Maneuvering and Warning Signals:**

| Signal | Whistle Pattern | Meaning (3 marks) |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| One short blast | ● | "I am altering my course to **starboard**" |
| Two short blasts | ● ● | "I am altering my course to **port**" |
| Three short blasts | ● ● ● | "I am operating **astern propulsion**" |
| Five or more short blasts | ● ● ● ● ● | "**Doubt/danger** — I do not understand your intentions" (this is not a prescribed maneuvering signal but a warning) |
| Four short blasts | ● ● ● ● | Not defined in Rule 34 (used in some local fishing contexts) |

**Definitions of Sound Signal Timings (2 marks):**
- A "**short blast**" = approximately **1 second** duration.
- A "**prolonged blast**" = approximately **4 to 6 seconds** duration.

**Practical application (2 marks):**
- These signals are used when vessels are **in sight of one another** in clear visibility.
- A vessel approaching a blind bend or narrow channel may sound **one prolonged blast** to signal her presence.
- The signals are sounded on the **whistle** (or, for sailing vessels, on a **fog horn or bell** — Rule 34(c)).

---

#### **Model Solution for Part B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Rule 19 — Conduct in Restricted Visibility:**

This rule applies to vessels **not in sight of one another** due to fog, mist, falling snow, heavy rainstorms, sandstorms, or any other similar causes.

**Key Requirements (4 marks):**
1. **Safe Speed (Rule 6 + Rule 19)**: Every vessel must proceed at a speed adapted to prevailing circumstances — reduced to a level at which she can take proper and effective avoiding action.
2. **Engines Ready (Rule 19)**: Power-driven vessels must have their **engines ready for immediate maneuver** — meaning the telegraph is on standby and the propeller can be engaged in seconds.
3. **Radar and Listening**: Vessels must use radar plotting (ARPA) and listening for fog signals.
4. **Avoid crossing ahead**: If a vessel detects another by radar alone, she must take **early and substantial** action to keep well clear and must **avoid crossing ahead** of the other vessel.

**Fog Signals — Rule 35 (3 marks):**

| Vessel State | Signal | Interval |
|--------------|--------|----------|
| Power-driven underway, **making way** | **1 prolonged blast** | Every **2 minutes** |
| Power-driven underway, **stopped (no way on)** | **2 prolonged blasts** | Every **2 minutes** |
| Vessel at anchor | **Rapid ringing of bell for ~5 seconds** | Every **1 minute** |
| Vessel ≥ 100 m at anchor | **Bell + gong** (gong aft) | Bell every 1 min; gong 1 min after bell |
| Vessel aground | **Bell + gong + 3 separate bell strokes** | As above |
| Pilot vessel on duty (at anchor or making way) | **4 short blasts** in addition to normal signal | — |
| Sailing vessel, fishing vessel, RAM/NUC under sail | **1 prolonged + 2 short blasts** | Every 2 minutes |
| Towing vessel (tow > 200 m) | **1 prolonged + 2 short blasts** | Every 2 minutes |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in COLREGs Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the "five short blasts" danger signal** (Rule 34(d)) — students often omit it. It is critical in the danger/doubt scenario.
> 2. **Confusing Rule 18 priorities** — the order from least to most privileged is: Power → Sail → Fishing → RAM → CBD → NUC. Students often list them in reverse.
> 3. **Wrong arc of visibility** — masthead light is **225°** (not 180° or 360°), and the total of masthead + sidelights = **360°** with **11.25° overlap** between sectors.
> 4. **Overtaking threshold** — students often write "180°" or "90°" instead of the correct **22.5° abaft the beam** (i.e., 112.5° to 247.5° relative bearing).
> 5. **Stand-on vessel responsibility** — many students think the stand-on vessel has "no duty." This is **wrong**. Under Rule 17(c), the stand-on vessel may take action if the give-way vessel fails to act.
> 6. **Fog signal interval** — students often write "every minute" instead of **"every 2 minutes"** for power-driven vessels.
> 7. **Not stating the time durations** — a "short blast" is ~1 second, a "prolonged blast" is **4 to 6 seconds**. Examiners award marks for these specifics.
> 8. **Rule 9 narrow channel rule** — a vessel of less than 20 m OR a sailing vessel must not impede a vessel of 20 m or more confined to the channel. Many students miss the 20-m threshold.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for COLREGs**

- [ ] **COLREGs = 1972 rules, in force since 15 July 1977**, adopted by IMO (formerly IMCO).
- [ ] **Six Parts**: A (General), B (Steering & Sailing), C (Lights & Shapes), D (Sound & Light Signals), E (Exemptions), plus 4 Annexes.
- [ ] **Rule 2 — Responsibility**: No relief for neglect; the Rules do **not** exhaust duty of care.
- [ ] **Rule 5 — Lookout**: Sight, hearing, and all available means.
- [ ] **Rule 6 — Safe Speed**: Determined by visibility, traffic, maneuverability, lights, sea state, draft.
- [ ] **Rule 7 — Risk of Collision**: Apparent if compass bearing does not appreciably change.
- [ ] **Rule 8 — Action to Avoid Collision**: Positive, timely, large enough to be apparent.
- [ ] **Rule 13 — Overtaking**: More than 22.5° abaft beam; overtaking vessel always give-way.
- [ ] **Rule 14 — Head-on**: Both alter to **starboard**, pass port-to-port, 1 short blast.
- [ ] **Rule 15 — Crossing**: Other on starboard = give-way; other on port = stand-on.
- [ ] **Rule 18 — Priority order**: Power → Sail → Fishing → RAM → CBD → NUC (lowest to highest).
- [ ] **Rule 19 — Restricted Visibility**: Safe speed, engines ready, fog signals, avoid crossing ahead.
- [ ] **Lights (Rule 23)**: Masthead 225° white, Port 112.5° red, Starboard 112.5° green, Stern 135° white.
- [ ] **NUC**: 2 red vertical (night), 2 black balls (day).
- [ ] **RAM**: Red-White-Red (night), Ball-Diamond-Ball (day).
- [ ] **Anchored (< 50 m)**: 1 white 360° light; (≥ 50 m): 2 white 360° lights; day shape: 1 black ball.
- [ ] **Whistle signals (Rule 34)**: 1 short = stbd; 2 short = port; 3 short = astern; 5 short = danger.
- [ ] **Fog signals (Rule 35)**: 1 prolonged/2 min = making way; 2 prolonged/2 min = stopped; bell 5 sec/1 min = anchored.
- [ ] **Pilot vessel identity signal**: 4 short blasts.
- [ ] **Distress signals (Rule 37 + Annex IV)**: At least 12 different signals; rocket parachute flares, Mayday on radio, continuous fog horn, etc.
- [ ] **Narrow channel (Rule 9)**: Keep to starboard side of channel; < 20 m vessels and sailing vessels must not impede larger vessels.
- [ ] **TSS (Rule 10)**: Follow general direction of traffic flow; cross at small angles when necessary.
- [ ] **Masthead light range for ≥ 50 m vessel = 6 nm**; sidelights and stern light = **3 nm**.
- [ ] **Vessel is "underway"** when she is not at anchor, or made fast to the shore, or aground.
- [ ] **"Restricted visibility"** is the legal COLREG term — not "fog" only — includes mist, snow, rain, sandstorms.

---

**End of Module 3 — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs)**

*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern — Module 3, Course Code OESFT727 - Maritime Safety.*

topics.title: Maneuvering Characteristics of Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maneuvering Characteristics of Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maneuvering Characteristics of Ships** refers to the quantitative and qualitative behavioral response of a vessel when subjected to control inputs from its propulsion and steering systems, particularly at low and moderate (operational) speeds. The discipline systematically evaluates how a ship turns, stops, accelerates, and changes course in response to helm (rudder angle), engine telegraph (RPM), and environmental perturbations (wind, current, waves).

The International Maritime Organization (IMO) formalizes this through **Resolution A.751(18) – Interim Standards for Ship Maneuverability** (adopted 4 November 1993), which replaced the earlier A.160(ES.IV) and was eventually superseded by the **Revised Standards A.601(15) Annex 1 (with MSC.137(76) amendments)** and the current **Resolution MSC.137(76) – Standards for Ship Maneuverability** (effective from 1 July 2002, with revisions effective 1 January 2004 for new ships).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OESFT727 Module 3):**
> The student is expected to understand the **turning circle geometry**, **stopping distance** including **crash-stop astern maneuver**, **course-changing ability (zig-zag / Kempf test)**, **course-keeping ability**, and the **IMO criteria** that quantify whether a ship is *maneuverable*, *course-stable*, and *compliant* under SOLAS Chapter V / Reg. 30.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a fully loaded bus** on a busy Kerala highway (say, the NH-66 between Kozhikode and Kannur). The bus cannot turn as sharply as a motorcycle because:

- It has a **long wheelbase** (analogous to ship **Length Between Perpendiculars – LBP**).
- It has **momentum** and does not stop instantly when brakes are applied (analogous to ship **inertia / stopping distance**).
- The rear wheels "swing out" when you turn the front wheels (analogous to ship **sideslip / drift angle** $\beta$).
- Wind pushing from the side makes it harder to keep a straight course (analogous to **weather-cocking / leeway**).

A ship behaves in **exactly the same manner** — but at a scale of **100–400 meters length** and a mass of **tens of thousands of tonnes**, moving through a fluid (water) that is ~800 times denser than air. Hence, we cannot rely on instinct; we need **standardized, measurable parameters** to predict and certify maneuverability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Maneuvering Analysis:**
> - Density of seawater: $\rho \approx \mathbf{1{,}025\ kg/m^3}$
> - Kinematic viscosity at 15 °C: $\nu \approx \mathbf{1.19 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s}$
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Ship Maneuverability

Per IMO Resolution **MSC.137(76)**, every ship above 100 m LBP must demonstrate three inherent capabilities:

1. **Turning Ability** (Turning Circle) – the ship must complete a 35° course change in a defined distance.
2. **Course-Changing Ability** (Zig-Zag / Kempf Test) – quantified by the **1st and 2nd overshoot angles** at 10°/10° and 20°/20° zig-zag maneuvers.
3. **Course-Keeping Ability** (Spiral Test) – the ship must be **directionally stable**; it must not exhibit uncontrollable yaw oscillations when a small helm is removed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition – Directional Stability:** A ship is *course-stable* if, after an initial small disturbance (e.g., a 2–3° rudder pulse and return to midships), the resulting yaw motion **decays with time** rather than amplifying. This is mathematically analogous to a damped harmonic oscillator with positive damping.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Turning Circle (Turning Ability)

When a ship proceeding at **constant service speed** applies a hard-over (35°) port or starboard rudder, its trajectory is called the **turning circle** or **Williamson turn profile** (after the classical 19th-century naval architect Edward Herring Williamson).

### 2.1.1 Phases of a Turn (Logical Breakdown)

A turning maneuver progresses through **four distinct phases**:

- **Phase 1 – Build-Up (0 to ~50 m of travel):** Helm is put to 35°; lateral hydrodynamic forces on the rudder generate a **transverse force $Y_R$** and a **yaw moment $N_R$**. The ship begins to yaw.
- **Phase 2 – Acceleration of Turn / Drift Establishment (50–300 m):** A **drift angle $\beta$** develops at the ship's center of gravity (CG). The hull begins translating **sideways** (abeckway component appears).
- **Phase 3 – Steady Turning (~300 m to completion of 90° heading change):** The yaw rate $r$ becomes approximately constant; the path approximates a circle of radius $R$.
- **Phase 4 – Steady-State Circle (after 360° heading change):** The vessel traces a closed **circular path of radius R (steady turning radius)** at constant yaw rate $r_0$.

### 2.1.2 Key Turning Circle Parameters (KTU-Examination Favorite)

The IMO Standard **MSC.137(76)** defines the following geometric terms from the moment of putting the rudder over to the moment the ship has changed heading by 90° (called the **Tactical Phase**):

- **Advance (A):** Distance traveled in the original (forward) direction — measured along the original course line.
- **Transfer (T):** Distance traveled perpendicular to the original course line — measured at 90° heading change.
- **Tactical Diameter (TD):** Distance traveled perpendicular to the original course at 180° heading change (i.e., the transfer at the 180° point). Must be **≤ 5 × LBP** for compliance.
- **Final Diameter (FD):** The diameter of the steady-state circle (after several full turns).

### 2.1.3 Empirical Approximations (Holtrop/Molland)

For preliminary design and back-of-envelope KTU problems, the **steady turning radius** is approximated by:

$$R \approx \frac{L_{BP}}{d_r} \cdot k$$

where $d_r$ is a non-dimensional **rudder force coefficient** (typically 0.5–1.5) and $k$ is a correction factor for hull form (block coefficient $C_B$ dependent, generally 0.4–0.6).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Rule of Thumb (used by Pilots):**
> - A cargo ship: **Tactical Diameter ≈ 3 to 5 × LBP**
> - A tanker: **Tactical Diameter ≈ 4 to 6 × LBP** (larger because of higher block coefficient)
> - A naval frigate (low $C_B$): **Tactical Diameter ≈ 2 to 3 × LBP**

## 2.2 Stopping Distance and the Crash-Stop Astern

When the engine telegraph is moved to **FULL ASTERN** from the ahead service speed, the ship does **not** stop instantly. It covers a considerable distance, the **crash-stop distance**, governed by Newton's second law integrated over time and modified by hydrodynamic drag.

### 2.2.1 The Crash-Stop Sequence

1. **Propeller Wash Reversal Lag (0–10 s):** The large inertia of the propeller shaft and the hydrodynamic inertia of the water around the propeller mean that the **thrust does not reverse instantly**. The ship continues accelerating ahead for some seconds — called the **"stopping lag"** or **head reach phase A**.
2. **Deceleration Phase (10–90 s):** The reversed propeller produces a braking force, hull drag increases with speed², and the ship decelerates. Lateral force balance tilts — the **bow falls off** the original course.
3. **Dead-in-Water Phase (90–180 s):** The ship has minimal forward speed, but still has momentum; it coasts to a stop.

### 2.2.2 Approximate Empirical Formula (Kempf / Motora)

The total **head reach** (distance traveled from the moment the engine is reversed to the moment the ship is dead in the water along the original heading) is given by the classical **Kempf formula** (1929), still used as a first estimate in KTU textbooks:

$$D_{stop} = \frac{C_d \cdot \Delta \cdot V^2}{2 \cdot (T_{astern} + R_{hull})}$$

where:

- $D_{stop}$ = stopping distance (m)
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (depends on block coefficient; for cargo ships $C_d \approx 0.10$–$0.18$)
- $\Delta$ = displacement (tonnes or kg, consistent)
- $V$ = service speed (m/s)
- $T_{astern}$ = astern thrust (N), typically 60–80% of ahead thrust
- $R_{hull}$ = hull resistance at the moment of reversal (N)

A more **engineering-friendly approximation** widely used is:

$$D_{stop} \approx K \cdot V_0^2 \cdot \Delta / P$$

where $K$ is an empirical constant (~0.05–0.1), $V_0$ is initial speed in knots, $\Delta$ is displacement in tonnes, and $P$ is total shaft power in kW.

## 2.3 Course-Changing Ability: The Zig-Zag (Kempf) Test

The zig-zag maneuver is the most important **dynamic maneuverability test** because it directly evaluates the **course control loop** — i.e., the interaction of helmsman, autopilot, and ship.

### 2.3.1 Procedure

Starting from a steady straight course at service speed:
1. Apply a fixed rudder angle $\delta_1$ to one side.
2. When the ship has yawed through the prescribed angle, reverse the rudder to $\delta_2$ on the opposite side.
3. Repeat the cycle.

The standard test designations:
- **10°/10° zig-zag** — for course-changing precision (e.g., coastal navigation)
- **20°/20° zig-zag** — for open-water maneuvering (default IMO test)

### 2.3.2 Key Quantities Measured

- **1st Overshoot Angle** $\phi_{1}$ — peak heading overshoot after the 1st rudder reversal.
- **2nd Overshoot Angle** $\phi_{2}$ — peak heading overshoot after the 2nd rudder reversal.
- **Times to complete specific course changes** ($t_{10}$, $t_{20}$, etc.).

**IMO Compliance Limits:**
- 10°/10°: $\phi_1 \leq \mathbf{10°}$, $\phi_2 \leq \mathbf{25°}$ (if not, criteria relaxed)
- 20°/20°: $\phi_1 \leq \mathbf{20°}$, $\phi_2 \leq \mathbf{40°}$ (if not, criteria relaxed)

## 2.4 Course-Keeping Ability: The Spiral Test

The **spiral test** (also called the **Dieudonné spiral** after French naval architect Jean Dieudonné, 1950s) is performed by:
1. With the ship on a steady course, apply a small fixed rudder angle $\delta$ (e.g., 5°, 10°, 15°, 20°, 15°, 10°, 5°, 0°, -5°, etc.) progressively, then in reverse.
2. Plot the **steady-state yaw rate $r$** against rudder angle $\delta$.

For a **course-stable ship**, the curve crosses the $\delta = 0$ axis at **$r = 0$ with a positive slope**. For a **directionally unstable ship**, the curve forms a loop (the **Dieudonné loop**), meaning multiple steady yaw rates are possible at the same rudder angle — this is the condition to be **avoided** by KTU/IMO standards.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every equation, criterion, and metric** the student is expected to know for the KTU ESE on Maneuvering Characteristics. **Memorize this table** before sitting for the exam.

| # | Parameter / Criterion | Symbol | Formula or Limit | Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Service / Design Speed | $V_s$ | Given in ship particulars | m/s or knots | Baseline for all tests |
| 2 | Length Between Perpendiculars | $L_{BP}$ | $L_{BP} = L_{OA} - L_{bow} - L_{stern}$ | m | Used in all IMO criteria |
| 3 | Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | $C_B = \nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | dimensionless | Hull fullness indicator |
| 4 | Advance at 90° | $A_{90}$ | $A_{90} \approx 3.0$–$4.0 \, L_{BP}$ | m | 14-mark question classic |
| 5 | Transfer at 90° | $T_{90}$ | $T_{90} \approx 1.0$–$1.5 \, L_{BP}$ | m | |
| 6 | Tactical Diameter | $TD$ | $TD \leq \mathbf{5 \, L_{BP}}$ (IMO) | m | **Pass/Fail criterion** |
| 7 | Final Diameter | $FD$ | $FD \approx 3$–$4 \, TD$ | m | |
| 8 | Steady Turning Radius | $R$ | $R = V / r_0$ | m | $r_0$ = steady yaw rate (rad/s) |
| 9 | Drift Angle | $\beta$ | $\beta = \arctan(v/u)$ | degrees | Negative for typical turn |
| 10 | Non-dimensional Yaw Rate | $r'$ | $r' = r L_{BP} / V$ | dimensionless | Used in PMM tests |
| 11 | Non-dimensional Rudder Force | $X', Y', N'$ | $Y' = Y / (0.5 \rho V^2 L_{BP} T)$ | dimensionless | Hydrodynamic derivatives |
| 12 | Crash-Stop Head Reach | $D_{stop}$ | $D_{stop} = K V_0^2 \Delta / P$ | m or ship lengths | Empirical (Kempf) |
| 13 | 10°/10° 1st Overshoot | $\phi_1$ | $\phi_1 \leq \mathbf{10°}$ | degrees | IMO criterion |
| 14 | 10°/10° 2nd Overshoot | $\phi_2$ | $\phi_2 \leq \mathbf{25°}$ | degrees | IMO criterion |
| 15 | 20°/20° 1st Overshoot | $\phi_1$ | $\phi_1 \leq \mathbf{20°}$ | degrees | IMO criterion |
| 16 | 20°/20° 2nd Overshoot | $\phi_2$ | $\phi_2 \leq \mathbf{40°}$ | degrees | IMO criterion |
| 17 | Full Astern Stop Time | $t_{stop}$ | Typical 60–120 s | seconds | |
| 18 | Advance Speed Loss | $\Delta V$ | $\Delta V / V_s \leq \mathbf{0.5}$ (IMO 20°/20°) | ratio | Course-keeping index |
| 19 | Dieudonné Loop Criterion | Loop | Loop area $\leq 0$ (no loop = stable) | qualitative | Pass/Fail |
| 20 | Rudder Area Ratio | $A_R / L T$ | $\approx 1/50$ to $1/100$ | ratio | Design parameter |

> **Real-World Utility:** These parameters are encoded in **TRANSAS / K-Sim, Wärtsilä NACOS Platinum, and Kongsberg K-Pos DP** simulators used in KTU-affiliated maritime academies (e.g., MERI, METI) to train shiphandling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Geometric Relationships in the Turning Circle

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Coordinate System

Let the ship be at the origin $(0,0)$ at time $t = 0$, heading along the **positive $x$-axis** (original course line). The rudder is put to 35° at $t = 0$. We track the position of the ship's center of gravity (CG) over time.

**Step 1 — Heading Angle Definition**

The instantaneous heading $\psi(t)$ of the ship is measured counter-clockwise from the original course:

$$\psi(t) = \int_0^t r(\tau) \, d\tau$$

where $r(t)$ is the instantaneous **yaw rate** (rad/s).

**Step 2 — Position in the Earth-Fixed Frame**

The velocity components of the ship's CG in the **earth-fixed** (inertial) frame are:

$$u_E(t) = V(t) \cos(\psi(t) + \beta(t))$$
$$v_E(t) = V(t) \sin(\psi(t) + \beta(t))$$

**Step 3 — Earth-Fixed Position by Integration**

The displacement in the $x$ (forward) and $y$ (lateral) directions is:

$$x(t) = \int_0^t V(\tau) \cos(\psi(\tau) + \beta(\tau)) \, d\tau$$
$$y(t) = \int_0^t V(\tau) \sin(\psi(\tau) + \beta(\tau)) \, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Definition of Advance and Transfer at 90°**

At the instant the heading has changed by $90°$, i.e., $\psi = 90°$, the time is $t_{90}$. Then:

$$A_{90} = x(t_{90}) = \int_0^{t_{90}} V(\tau) \cos(\psi(\tau) + \beta(\tau)) \, d\tau$$

$$T_{90} = y(t_{90}) = \int_0^{t_{90}} V(\tau) \sin(\psi(\tau) + \beta(\tau)) \, d\tau$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two integrals?** Because the ship is both moving forward **and** drifting sideways due to $\beta$, the trajectory is **not a simple circular arc**, but a transitional spiral that gradually approaches a circle of radius $R$.

**Step 5 — Relation Between Steady Turning Radius and Tactical Diameter**

Once the ship is in **steady turning** (constant yaw rate $r_0$ and constant drift angle $\beta_0$), the trajectory is a circle of radius:

$$R = \frac{V_{\text{steady}}}{r_0}$$

The **tactical diameter** is the transfer at the 180° heading change point. In the **idealized case** (where the ship immediately enters steady turning at $t = 0$), the tactical diameter would be:

$$TD_{\text{ideal}} = 2 R$$

However, the build-up phase adds a transient offset, so in practice:

$$TD_{\text{real}} = (1.3 \text{ to } 1.6) \times 2R$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Stopping Distance from Newton's Second Law

Consider the **longitudinal equation of motion** of the ship during a crash-stop:

$$m \frac{dV}{dt} = T_{prop} - R_{hull} - F_{brake}$$

**Step 1 — Identify Forces**

- $T_{prop}$: Propeller thrust (positive ahead, negative astern)
- $R_{hull}$: Hull resistance, modeled as $R_{hull} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V^2$
- $F_{brake}$: Reverse-thrust braking component (subsumed in $T_{prop}$ when astern)

**Step 2 — Simplification — Assume Pure Deceleration with Astern Thrust**

When the propeller is reversed and reaches steady astern thrust $T_{astern}$ (negative), and ignoring the hull resistance as secondary:

$$m \frac{dV}{dt} = -T_{astern} - \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2$$

**Step 3 — Separate Variables**

$$m \frac{dV}{T_{astern} + \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2} = -dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $V_0$ to 0**

$$D_{stop} = \int_0^{D} ds = \int_{V_0}^{0} V \frac{dt}{dV} dV = \int_0^{V_0} \frac{m V \, dV}{T_{astern} + \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2}$$

**Step 5 — Closed-Form Result**

The integral evaluates to (using standard logarithmic identity):

$$D_{stop} = \frac{m}{2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A} \ln\left(\frac{T_{astern} + \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V_0^2}{T_{astern}}\right)$$

$$D_{stop} = \frac{m}{\rho C_D A} \ln\left(1 + \frac{\rho C_D A V_0^2}{2 T_{astern}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The logarithmic dependence on $V_0^2$ means that doubling the ship's speed **does not double** the stopping distance — it increases it by a factor of approximately **1.5–1.8** depending on $T_{astern}$. This is why **speed reduction is the single most effective collision-avoidance maneuver** at sea, taught in COLREGs Rule 8 and STCW Convention.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: IMO MSC.137(76) Compliance at a Glance

| Maneuver | Test | IMO Criterion | Pass Condition | Failure Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Turning | Hard-over 35° rudder at $V_s$ | Tactical Diameter | $TD \leq 5 L_{BP}$ | Loss of SOLAS certification; remedial design change required |
| Turning | Advance at 90° | $A_{90}$ | No formal IMO limit, but typically $\leq 4.5 L_{BP}$ | Excessive straight-line travel — dangerous in narrow waters |
| Course-Change | 10°/10° Zig-Zag | 1st Overshoot | $\phi_1 \leq 10°$ | Unacceptable oscillation; autopilot tuning issue |
| Course-Change | 10°/10° Zig-Zag | 2nd Overshoot | $\phi_2 \leq 25°$ | Indicates low directional stability |
| Course-Change | 20°/20° Zig-Zag | 1st Overshoot | $\phi_1 \leq 20°$ | Sluggish initial response |
| Course-Change | 20°/20° Zig-Zag | 2nd Overshoot | $\phi_2 \leq 40°$ | Excessive heading overshoot |
| Course-Change | 20°/20° Zig-Zag | Speed loss | $\Delta V / V_s \leq 0.5$ | Inadequate power margin during turns |
| Course-Keeping | Spiral Test | Stability | No Dieudonné loop | Risk of broaching in following seas |
| Stopping | Full Astern from $V_s$ | No specific IMO limit; typical 10–15 ship lengths | Used for EEDI/EEDI-regulatory purposes | Insufficient crash-stop capability |
