# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Turning Circle Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ship at Origin, Heading 0 deg, Rudder at Midships] --> B[Helm Hard-Over 35 deg to Starboard]
    B --> C[Build-Up Phase: Yaw Rate r Increases]
    C --> D[Drift Angle Beta Develops, Hull Slides Sideways]
    D --> E[Phase 3: 90 deg Heading Change]
    E --> F[Tactical Phase: Advance A90 and Transfer T90 Measured]
    F --> G[Phase 4: 180 deg Heading Change, Tactical Diameter TD Measured]
    G --> H[Phase 5: 360 deg Heading Change, Steady Circle of Radius R]
    H --> I[Final Diameter FD = 2R Established]
    I --> J{IMO Check: TD <= 5 LBP ?}
    J -->|Yes| K[PASS: Maneuverable]
    J -->|No| L[FAIL: Remedial Design Required]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Crash-Stop Astern Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Service Speed Vs, Telegraph FULL AHEAD] --> P1[Helmsman: Telegraph FULL ASTERN]
    P1 --> P2[Phase A: Stopping Lag, Ship Still Accelerating, Shaft Windmilling]
    P2 --> P3[Phase B: Astern Thrust Builds, Hull Drag Dominates, Deceleration Begins]
    P3 --> P4[Phase C: Bow Falls Off Original Heading, Side Slip Develops]
    P4 --> P5[Phase D: Speed Approaches Zero, Drift Continues]
    P5 --> P6[Phase E: Dead in Water, Distance = Head Reach]
    P6 --> P7[Engine to STOP, Ship Drifts with Wind and Current]
    P7 --> P8[Anchor Dropped if Required]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Zig-Zag (Kempf) Test Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Steady Course at Service Speed Vs] --> S1[Apply 20 deg Rudder to Starboard]
    S1 --> S2{Heading Changed by 20 deg?}
    S2 -->|No| S1
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Reverse Rudder to 20 deg Port]
    S3 --> S4[Ship Continues Yawing Past Original Course]
    S4 --> S5[Peak Heading 1st Overshoot phi1 Recorded]
    S5 --> S6{Heading Changed by 20 deg to Port of Original?}
    S6 -->|No| S4
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Reverse Rudder to 20 deg Starboard]
    S7 --> S8[Peak Heading 2nd Overshoot phi2 Recorded]
    S8 --> S9{phi1 <= 20 deg AND phi2 <= 40 deg?}
    S9 -->|Yes| ZP[PASS: IMO 20/20 Compliant]
    S9 -->|No| ZF[FAIL: Course Changing Deficient]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Maneuvering Analysis System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUTS]
        I1[Ship Particulars: LBP, B, T, Delta, CB]
        I2[Propulsion: MCR, RPM, Thrust Curves]
        I3[Rudder: Area, Aspect Ratio, Max Angle]
        I4[Environment: Wind, Wave, Current]
        I5[Speed: Service Speed Vs]
    end

    subgraph MODELS[HYDRODYNAMIC MODELS]
        M1[Hull Force Derivatives: Yv, Yr, Nv, Nr]
        M2[Rudder Force Model: YR, NR]
        M3[Propulsion Model: Thrust T vs V, RPM]
        M4[Added Mass and Damping Matrix]
    end

    subgraph SOLVER[NUMERICAL SOLVER]
        S1[3 DOF Equations of Motion: Surge Sway Yaw]
        S2[Time Integration: RK4 or Euler]
        S3[Output: Trajectory, Heading, Speed]
    end

    subgraph POST[POST PROCESSING]
        P1[Turning Circle: A90, T90, TD, FD]
        P2[Crash Stop: Head Reach, Track Off]
        P3[Zig Zag: phi1, phi2, Speed Loss]
        P4[Spiral: Dieudonne Loop Check]
    end

    subgraph COMPLIANCE[IMO COMPLIANCE]
        C1{MSC.137 76 Check}
        C2[PASS: Maneuverable]
        C3[FAIL: Design Iteration]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M3
    I3 --> M2
    I4 --> M1
    I5 --> S1
    M1 --> S1
    M2 --> S1
    M3 --> S1
    M4 --> S1
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    S3 --> P1 & P2 & P3 & P4
    P1 & P2 & P3 & P4 --> C1
    C1 -->|All Pass| C2
    C1 -->|Any Fail| C3
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Effect of Environmental Factors on Maneuverability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Maneuvering Performance] --> B[Wind Effect]
    A --> C[Current Effect]
    A --> D[Wave Effect]
    A --> E[Shallow Water Effect]
    A --> F[Ship Loading Effect]

    B --> B1[Weather Helm Bias, Leeway Drift]
    C --> C1[Track Offset, Effective Speed Change]
    D --> D1[Added Resistance, Yaw Oscillation, Broaching Risk]
    E --> E1[Squat, Increased Yaw Damping, Bank Effect]
    F --> F1[Draft and Trim: Under Keel Clearance, Block Coefficient Change]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define "Tactical Diameter" of a ship and state the IMO compliance limit for it as per MSC.137(76).**

**Model Answer:**

The **Tactical Diameter (TD)** of a ship is defined as the **distance traveled by the ship's center of gravity in the direction perpendicular to the original (straight) course line** at the moment the ship has completed a **180° heading change** during a turning circle maneuver executed with hard-over (35°) rudder at the **service speed**.

It is a primary indicator of the ship's **turning ability** and directly determines the **space required** for the ship to turn around in restricted waters.

As per **IMO Resolution MSC.137(76)**, the tactical diameter must satisfy:

$$TD \leq 5 \times L_{BP}$$

where $L_{BP}$ is the **Length Between Perpendiculars** of the vessel. For a ship of $L_{BP} = 200$ m, this means $TD \leq 1000$ m.

**[End of Answer — 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for procedure, 1 for IMO criterion]**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Differentiate between the "Advance" and the "Transfer" of a ship during a turning maneuver.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Advance (A) | Transfer (T) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Distance traveled in the **original (forward) direction** of the ship's course | Distance traveled **perpendicular to the original course** |
| **Direction** | Along the original heading axis | At right angles to the original heading |
| **Measured at** | Usually reported as $A_{90}$ (at 90° heading change) | Usually reported as $T_{90}$ (at 90° heading change) |
| **Significance** | Indicates how much **straight-line distance** the ship consumes during the turn | Indicates how much **lateral space** the ship uses |
| **Typical Ratio (cargo ship)** | $A_{90} \approx 3.0$–$4.0 \, L_{BP}$ | $T_{90} \approx 1.0$–$1.5 \, L_{BP}$ |
| **Pilot concern** | "How far ahead will she go before turning?" | "How far sideways will she swing?" |

**[End of Answer — 3 marks: 1 each for definition + 1 for distinguishing]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**A bulk carrier has the following particulars:**
- Length Between Perpendiculars: $L_{BP} = \mathbf{180\ m}$
- Beam: $B = \mathbf{28\ m}$
- Draft (design): $T = \mathbf{10.5\ m}$
- Displacement: $\Delta = \mathbf{32{,}000\ tonnes}$
- Block coefficient: $C_B = \mathbf{0.78}$
- Service speed: $V_s = \mathbf{14\ knots}$
- Total shaft power: $P = \mathbf{7{,}200\ kW}$
- Rudder area: $A_R = \mathbf{24\ m^2}$

**Answer the following sub-parts:**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Calculate the **tactical diameter** that this ship would have if the empirical relationship $TD \approx 4.0 \times L_{BP}$ holds. Comment on **IMO MSC.137(76) compliance**.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Estimate the **head reach distance** during a full-astern crash-stop maneuver using the empirical formula $D_{stop} \approx K \cdot V_0^2 \cdot \Delta / P$, where $K = \mathbf{0.08}$ (a typical empirical constant for bulk carriers). State the result in **meters** and in **ship lengths**.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Apply the Empirical Formula**

$$TD = 4.0 \times L_{BP} = 4.0 \times 180\ m = \mathbf{720\ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with IMO Criterion**

The IMO MSC.137(76) limit is:

$$TD_{limit} = 5 \times L_{BP} = 5 \times 180\ m = 900\ m$$

**Step 3 — Compliance Decision**

$$TD_{\text{calculated}} = 720\ m \ \leq \ 900\ m = TD_{limit}$$

Therefore, the ship **COMPLIES** with IMO MSC.137(76).

**Step 4 — Safety Margin**

$$\text{Safety Margin} = \frac{900 - 720}{900} \times 100\% = 20\%$$

> **[Mark Allocation: 2 marks formula application, 2 marks IMO criterion, 2 marks comparison, 1 mark comment]**

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Convert Speed to Consistent Units**

$$V_0 = 14\ \text{knots} = 14 \times 0.5144\ m/s = 7.20\ m/s$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Empirical Formula**

$$D_{stop} = K \cdot V_0^2 \cdot \Delta / P$$

$$D_{stop} = 0.08 \times (7.20)^2 \times 32{,}000 / 7{,}200$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Numerically**

$$V_0^2 = (7.20)^2 = 51.84\ m^2/s^2$$

$$D_{stop} = 0.08 \times 51.84 \times 32{,}000 / 7{,}200$$

$$D_{stop} = 0.08 \times 51.84 \times 4.444$$

$$D_{stop} = 0.08 \times 230.4$$

$$D_{stop} = \mathbf{18.43\ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL:** The candidate has likely forgotten to convert **knots → m/s** (or **tonnes → kg** consistently). When $V_0$ is in knots, the numerical answer comes out in the wrong units. **Always re-check units** before writing the final answer.

**Step 4 — Convert to Ship Lengths**

$$\frac{D_{stop}}{L_{BP}} = \frac{18.43\ m}{180\ m} = 0.10\ \text{ship lengths}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation**

A head reach of only **0.10 ship lengths (≈ 18 m)** is unrealistically small for a bulk carrier — typical head reach is **10–15 ship lengths**. This indicates that the empirical constant **$K = 0.08$ is too small for a vessel of this size** at this speed, OR the formula is being used outside its calibration range. For a more realistic estimate using the **Kempf formula** (with proper drag coefficient and astern thrust data), the head reach for this ship would be approximately:

$$D_{stop}^{\text{Kempf}} \approx 12\ \text{to}\ 15 \times L_{BP} = 2{,}160\ \text{to}\ 2{,}700\ m$$

> **[Mark Allocation: 2 marks formula, 2 marks numerical evaluation, 2 marks unit conversion, 1 mark engineering interpretation]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**A container vessel undergoes a 20°/20° zig-zag maneuver at its service speed of $V_s = \mathbf{22\ knots}$ ($L_{BP} = \mathbf{250\ m}$). The trial results are:**
- 1st overshoot angle: $\phi_1 = \mathbf{14°}$
- 2nd overshoot angle: $\phi_2 = \mathbf{32°}$
- Speed loss during 2nd overshoot: $\Delta V = \mathbf{9\ knots}$
- Rudder angle used: $\delta = \mathbf{20°}$ on either side

#### (a) [7 Marks] **Evaluate IMO MSC.137(76) compliance** for the 20°/20° zig-zag test. What remedial action would you suggest if the ship fails?

#### (b) [7 Marks] **Calculate the non-dimensional yaw rate** $r' = r L_{BP} / V$ if the steady turning rate observed in a later turning circle test is $r_0 = \mathbf{1.8°/min}$. Comment on whether the ship is in the **"advanced" turning regime** ($r' < 0.3$) or **"normal" regime** ($r' \approx 0.3$–$0.5$).

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Recall the IMO 20°/20° Criteria**

Per **IMO MSC.137(76)**:

$$\phi_1^{limit} = 20°, \quad \phi_2^{limit} = 40°$$

Additionally, the speed loss criterion:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_s} \leq 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Compare 1st Overshoot**

$$\phi_1^{measured} = 14° \ \leq \ 20° = \phi_1^{limit} \quad \checkmark \ \text{PASS}$$

**Step 3 — Compare 2nd Overshoot**

$$\phi_2^{measured} = 32° \ \leq \ 40° = \phi_2^{limit} \quad \checkmark \ \text{PASS}$$

**Step 4 — Compare Speed Loss**

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_s} = \frac{9}{22} = 0.41 \ \leq \ 0.5 \quad \checkmark \ \text{PASS}$$

**Step 5 — Compliance Decision**

**The ship COMPLIES with all three IMO 20°/20° criteria.**

> However, since $\phi_1$ is **70% of the limit** and $\phi_2$ is **80% of the limit**, the ship is "borderline." A more conservative design margin would be desirable.

**Step 6 — Remedial Actions (Hypothetical Failure Scenario)**

If any criterion were **failed**, the following remedial actions would be considered:

1. **Increase rudder area** $A_R$ by 10–20% (improves course-changing responsiveness).
2. **Modify hull form** at the stern (e.g., add a **trim wedge** or **propeller duct** to increase effective yaw moment).
3. **Tune the autopilot** PID gains (operator-side fix, not a design fix).
4. **Re-evaluate propeller-rudder interaction** (e.g., a **Hiromipu** or **Schilling rudder** can produce 30–40% more side force at low speeds).
5. **Re-distribute loading** to maintain a balanced trim.

> **[Mark Allocation: 2 marks criteria, 3 marks comparison, 1 mark compliance decision, 1 mark remedial action]**

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Convert Yaw Rate to SI Units (rad/s)**

$$r_0 = 1.8°/\text{min} = \frac{1.8 \times \pi}{180} \text{rad/min} = 0.0314\ \text{rad/min}$$

Convert to rad/s:

$$r_0 = \frac{0.0314}{60}\ \text{rad/s} = 5.236 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Service Speed to SI Units**

$$V_s = 22\ \text{knots} = 22 \times 0.5144\ m/s = 11.32\ m/s$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Non-Dimensional Yaw Rate Definition**

$$r' = \frac{r_0 \cdot L_{BP}}{V_s}$$

$$r' = \frac{(5.236 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad/s}) \times 250\ m}{11.32\ m/s}$$

$$r' = \frac{0.1309}{11.32} = \mathbf{0.0116}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result**

| Regime | $r'$ Range | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Slow / Advanced** | $r' < 0.3$ | Small turning rate, large radius, ship barely turns |
| **Normal Turning** | $0.3 \leq r' \leq 0.5$ | Typical cargo ship turning |
| **Hard Turn / Tactical** | $r' > 0.5$ | Aggressive maneuver, bank-effect possible |

> **With $r' = 0.0116 \ll 0.3$, the ship is operating in the EXTREMELY SLOW turning regime.** This is far below the typical value for a maneuvering ship ($r' \approx 0.3$–$0.5$). It indicates either:
> - The reported $r_0 = 1.8°/\text{min}$ is the **steady-state rate of turn** at a very small rudder angle (likely the **spiral test** at $\delta \approx 5°$), **not** the hard-over turning circle rate.
> - The ship was turning at **a small drift angle** with partial helm.

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL:** KTU examiners have noted that students **forget to convert degrees to radians**, leading to an answer that is **off by a factor of 57.3**. Always remember: **radians = degrees × $\pi/180$**.

> **[Mark Allocation: 2 marks unit conversion, 2 marks formula, 2 marks numerical evaluation, 1 mark interpretation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Units, units, units:** Most mark loss is due to knots vs. m/s confusion. Always show your unit conversion step.
> 2. **Formula scope:** The Kempf formula $D_{stop} = K V_0^2 \Delta / P$ is empirical and valid only for first-order estimates. Do not present it as a "fundamental law."
> 3. **Sign conventions:** Drift angle $\beta$ is typically **negative** during a starboard turn (the stern moves to port of the heading vector). Getting this wrong will cost you 1–2 marks in interpretation questions.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the IMO Resolution number:** Always say **"IMO MSC.137(76)"** and **"Resolution A.751(18)"** in your answers, not just "IMO standards." Examiners reward exact terminology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Maneuvering Characteristics of Ships**

- **Three Pillars of Maneuverability:** Turning, Course-Changing, Course-Keeping.
- **Turning Circle Parameters:** Advance $A_{90}$ (≈ 3–4 $L_{BP}$), Transfer $T_{90}$ (≈ 1–1.5 $L_{BP}$), Tactical Diameter $TD$ (≤ 5 $L_{BP}$), Final Diameter $FD$ (≈ 3–4 × $TD$).
- **IMO MSC.137(76) Criteria (memorize these limits):**
  - $TD \leq 5 \, L_{BP}$
  - 10°/10° zig-zag: $\phi_1 \leq 10°$, $\phi_2 \leq 25°$
  - 20°/20° zig-zag: $\phi_1 \leq 20°$, $\phi_2 \leq 40°$, $\Delta V/V_s \leq 0.5$
  - Spiral test must show **no Dieudonné loop** (directionally stable).
- **Crash-Stop Astern** has three distinct phases: stopping lag → deceleration → coasting. Head reach is on the order of **10–15 ship lengths** for a fully-laden cargo vessel.
- **Speed-squared law:** Stopping distance scales with $V_0^2$ approximately; doubling speed increases stopping distance by ~1.5–1.8×, not 2×.
- **Kempf's formula** is empirical: $D_{stop} = K V_0^2 \Delta / P$ with $K \approx 0.05$–$0.1$.
- **Drift angle $\beta$** is the angle between the ship's heading and the velocity vector of the CG.
- **Yaw rate $r$** has units of **rad/s** in SI and **deg/min** in nautical usage. **1°/min = 2.909 × 10⁻⁴ rad/s**.
- **Course-stable ship:** The spiral test curve crosses the origin (r=0, δ=0) with **positive slope** — no loop.
- **Directionally unstable ship:** Spiral test produces a **Dieudonné loop** — multiple steady-state yaw rates at the same rudder angle.
- **Course-changing tests are dynamic; course-keeping tests are quasi-static.** The IMO separates them for a reason.
- **A 14-mark KTU ESE question typically involves:** a numerical problem (5–7 marks) + a theory/interpretation component (5–7 marks) + a 1–2 mark "comment on IMO compliance" closure.
- **Units checklist before submitting any numerical answer:** knots vs. m/s, tonnes vs. kg, degrees vs. radians, ship lengths vs. meters.
- **Pilots' Rule of Thumb (also accepted in KTU viva):** "Tactical Diameter in ship lengths is approximately 3 to 5 times LBP for a cargo ship."
- **For a 3-mark definition question**, always give: (i) the formal definition, (ii) the procedure or units, (iii) the IMO criterion or one numerical example.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Collision Avoidance Techniques # Module 3 — Collision Avoidance Techniques ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **COLLISION AVOIDANCE TECHNIQUE** — A structured, rule-based, and technologically assisted sequence of watchkeeping, maneuvering, signaling, and navigational actions taken by a vessel's officer of the watch (OOW) to ensure that two or more ships pass each other at a **safe distance (CPSP – Closest Point of Separation)** without physical contact, structural damage, or risk to life, cargo, or environment. ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In strict compliance with the **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs), 1972** — adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and made mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter V**, Regulation 19** — collision avoidance is the proactive and reactive process by which a mariner detects, evaluates, identifies, and resolves a risk of collision between own vessel and one or more targets using: 1. **Visual lookout** (Rule 5) 2. **Safe speed** (Rule 6) 3. **Risk assessment** (Rule 7) 4. **Action to avoid collision** (Rule 8) 5. **Vessel-specific conduct rules** (Rules 9–18) 6. **Navigation lights and shapes** (Rules 20–31) 7. **Sound and light signals** (Rules 32–37) 8. **Conduct in restricted visibility** (Rules 19, 36) The **key metric** governing every collision avoidance maneuver is **CPA (Closest Point of Approach)** and **TCPA (Time to Closest Point of Approach)**. > [!NOTE] > **CPA** = the minimum distance predicted between own ship and target at their point of closest approach. > **TCPA** = the time remaining until that closest point is reached. > **Standard "Safe CPA"** = **2.0 nautical miles** (open sea) and **0.5 nautical miles** (congested/coastal waters). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Driving Parallel Imagine driving a car on a busy two-lane highway at night with oncoming traffic, intersections, and pedestrians. The maritime equivalent of this is a ship navigating in open or congested waters. | Highway Driving | Maritime Collision Avoidance | |---|---| | Headlights of approaching car | **Navigation lights** (masthead, sidelights, stern light) | | Car horn | **Ship's whistle** (sound signals) | | Brake lights / hazard lights | **Light signals** (Morse signals, flashlights) | | Traffic signs / lane markings | **Traffic Separation Schemes (TSS)** | | Rear-view mirror + blind spot check | **Radar / ARPA plotting** | | Speed limit sign | **Rule 6 – Safe Speed** | | Highway patrol | **Vessel Traffic Services (VTS)** | | Defensive driving course | **COLREGs training (STCW)** | Just as a defensive driver anticipates and reacts **before** a crash becomes imminent, the Officer of the Watch (OOW) uses **anticipatory (proactive) collision avoidance** rather than reactive (panic) maneuvers. A ship's stopping distance can be **1 to 3 nautical miles** — often longer than the entire visible horizon of an approaching vessel — making **early, large, and obvious maneuvers** the gold standard. --- ### 1.3 The Five Pillars of Collision Avoidance ```mermaid flowchart LR A[1. DETECT
Visual / Radar / AIS] B[2. ASSESS
CPA / TCPA / Bearing Rate] C[3. IDENTIFY
Stand-on / Give-way / Cross] D[4. DECIDE
Alteration / Speed / Signal] E[5. EXECUTE
Maneuver + Monitor] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> A ``` > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Trick:** Examiners often award full 14-mark marks only when the student clearly links **ALL FIVE pillars** in their answer. Missing even one pillar drops you to a 10–12 grade band. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Collision Avoidance Techniques Collision avoidance techniques operate on **three concentric rings of safety**, each functioning both as a **standalone** technique and as a **complementary layer** within an integrated bridge system. ### Ring 1 — Regulatory & Procedural Layer (Human + Rule-Based) - **COLREGs** (The "Rule Book") - **Bridge Procedures Guide (BPG)** — IMO Resolution A.601(15) - **Watchkeeping arrangements** under STCW Manila Amendments ### Ring 2 — Manoeuvring & Signaling Layer (Human + Mechanical) - Course alteration (Rule 8 – Action to Avoid Collision) - Speed reduction (Rule 8(e) – Slacken speed / Stop / Reverse) - Sound signals (Rule 34 – Maneuvering / Warning signals) - Light signals (Rule 37 – Distress / Warning) ### Ring 3 — Electronic & Informational Layer (Hardware + Software) - **Radar** (X-band 9 GHz and S-band 3 GHz) - **ARPA** (Automatic Radar Plotting Aid) – IMO Resolution A.823(19) - **AIS** (Automatic Identification System) – SOLAS Reg. V/19.2.4 - **ECDIS** (Electronic Chart Display and Information System) - **VTS** (Vessel Traffic Services) – IMO Resolution A.827(19) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchy Rule:** When in doubt, **the Rules (COLREGs) always override any electronic or ARPA-suggested maneuver**. ARPA is an *aid* to interpretation, never a substitute for Rule 7 judgement. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **every** numerically testable formula that has appeared in KTU Question Papers (2014–2023 ESE pattern) under this topic. Master these — they account for nearly 60% of the numerical/derivation marks in Module 3. | # | Formula / Rule | Physical Meaning | Units / Standard Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{CPA} = D \cdot \sin(\beta - \alpha)$ | Closest Point of Approach from triangle geometry | Nautical Miles (NM) | | 2 | $\text{TCPA} = \dfrac{D \cdot \cos(\beta - \alpha)}{V_{rel}}$ | Time to Closest Point of Approach | Minutes (min) | | 3 | $V_{rel} = \sqrt{V_O^2 + V_T^2 - 2 V_O V_T \cos(\alpha)}$ | Relative Velocity of Target | Knots (kn) | | 4 | $B_R = \dfrac{V_{rel} \cdot \sin(\beta - \alpha)}{D}$ | True Bearing Rate (range-independent) | degrees/min | | 5 | $D_{\text{stop}} = k \cdot V^2$ | Stopping Distance (k ≈ 0.025–0.04 for laden tanker) | Nautical Miles | | 6 | $T_{\text{turn}} = \dfrac{SOG}{R_{turn}}$ | Time to alter course through 90° | Minutes | | 7 | $\text{SD} = \text{Range} - \text{CPA}$ | Safe Distance Margin | Nautical Miles | | 8 | $P_D = 1 - \exp(-0.0062 \cdot N)$ | Probability of Detection (N = number of scans) | Dimensionless (0–1) | | 9 | $\text{Safe Speed} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\text{visibility}}}$ | Speed inversely proportional to √visibility | Knots | | 10 | $\Delta \text{CPA}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{V_{rel} \cdot D \cdot \sin(\theta)}{V_O}$ | Course alteration (θ) effect on CPA | Nautical Miles | | 11 | $R_{\text{turn}} = \dfrac{0.0079 \cdot V^2}{T}$ | Radius of turn (V in knots, T = rudder angle factor) | Nautical Miles | | 12 | $\text{CCT} = \dfrac{V_O \cdot \cos(\theta_{new}) - V_T \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{V_{rel}}$ | New Course to Steer (parallel index method) | Degrees | **Where in the formula sheet:** - $D$ = Range to target (NM) - $V_O$ = Own ship speed (knots) - $V_T$ = Target ship speed (knots) - $\alpha$ = Relative bearing of target (degrees) - $\beta$ = Relative motion vector direction (degrees) - $\theta$ = Course alteration applied (degrees) - $R_{turn}$ = Radius of turn - $T$ = Rudder effectiveness factor --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | Commercial Shipping | Bulk carriers, tankers, container vessels — COLREGs compliance in TSS | | Naval Warfare | Fleet maneuver doctrine, anti-collision automated systems | | Autonomous Shipping | MASS (Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships) — IMO regulatory scoping | | Offshore Platforms | Supply vessel DPS (Dynamic Positioning) collision avoidance | | Bridge Design | Integrated Bridge Systems (IBS) — INS / NAVEX / RADAR fusion | | Insurance (P&I Clubs) | Determines liability in collision claims under "fault apportionment" | --- ## 2.4 The CPA/TCPA Decision Logic (Rule 7 Application) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule 7 — Risk of Collision** mandates that the OOW assumes risk exists whenever: > - The compass bearing of an approaching vessel **does not appreciably change** > - TCPA ≤ **6 minutes** in open sea > - CPA ≤ **2 NM** in open sea or **0.5 NM** in restricted waters > - The target is **closing** (negative TCPA convention) or **opening** (positive TCPA) $$\boxed{\text{If CPA} \leq \text{Safe CPA AND TCPA} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Action required}}$$ If **TCPA < 0**, the target has already passed its closest point — meaning the bearing change happened *too late*. This is called a **"developing situation"** and demands an **immediate alteration of not less than 30°** (Rule 8(c)) plus sound signal. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closest Point of Approach (CPA) from Relative Motion Triangle This derivation appears in nearly every KTU board paper. It is built on **classical relative-motion geometry** in the horizontal plane. ### Given: - Own ship at origin: Position $O$ with velocity $\vec{V_O}$ - Target at position $T$: Range $D$, bearing $\alpha$ - Target velocity vector $\vec{V_T}$ making angle $\beta$ with line OT ### To Derive: $CPA$ and $TCPA$ **Step 1 — Set up the relative motion frame** In the relative motion frame, we make own ship stationary by subtracting $\vec{V_O}$ from all velocities. The relative velocity of the target becomes: $$ \vec{V}_{rel} = \vec{V_T} - \vec{V_O} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the magnitude** Using the law of cosines on the velocity triangle: $$ V_{rel} = \sqrt{V_T^2 + V_O^2 - 2 \cdot V_T \cdot V_O \cdot \cos(\gamma)} $$ where $\gamma$ is the angle between $\vec{V_T}$ and $\vec{V_O}$. In a coastal setup, the angle between own course and the bearing of the target is $(\alpha + 180° - \phi)$ — call this $\alpha'$. So: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{rel} &= \sqrt{V_T^2 + V_O^2 - 2 \cdot V_T \cdot V_O \cdot \cos(\alpha')} \\ &= \sqrt{V_T^2 + V_O^2 + 2 \cdot V_T \cdot V_O \cdot \cos(\alpha)} \quad (\text{since } \alpha' = 180° - \alpha) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Project onto the line of sight** The relative velocity vector $\vec{V}_{rel}$ can be split into two components: - Along the line of sight (radial — closing or opening) - Perpendicular to the line of sight (tangential — no range effect) The component **along OT** is: $$ V_{along} = V_{rel} \cdot \cos(\beta - \alpha) $$ **Step 4 — Compute CPA** The shortest distance between two paths (own + target) occurs when the **perpendicular distance** between $\vec{V_O}$ and $\vec{V_T}$ is at its minimum. By simple trigonometric projection: $$ \begin{aligned} CPA &= D \cdot \sin(\beta - \alpha) \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** If $(\beta - \alpha) = 0$, the target is on a **collision course** → $CPA = 0$. > If $(\beta - \alpha) = 90°$, the target will pass at its **current range** → $CPA = D$. **Step 5 — Compute TCPA** The time required for the target to traverse the **along-sight component** to reach the perpendicular foot: $$ \begin{aligned} TCPA &= \frac{D \cdot \cos(\beta - \alpha)}{V_{rel}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Sign convention:** If $TCPA > 0$ → target closing (action required). > If $TCPA < 0$ → target already past CPA (situation resolved or about to be). **Step 6 — Numerical Example** Given: $D = 6$ NM, $V_O = 12$ kn, $V_T = 14$ kn, $\alpha = 45°$, target course offset by $\gamma = 60°$. $$ \begin{aligned} V_{rel} &= \sqrt{12^2 + 14^2 - 2(12)(14)\cos(60°)} \\ &= \sqrt{144 + 196 - 168} \\ &= \sqrt{172} \\ &= 13.11 \text{ kn} \end{aligned} $$ Assume relative bearing motion $(\beta - \alpha) = 30°$: $$ \begin{aligned} CPA &= 6 \cdot \sin(30°) = 6 \times 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ NM} \\ TCPA &= \frac{6 \cdot \cos(30°)}{13.11} = \frac{5.196}{13.11} = 0.396 \text{ h} = 23.78 \text{ min} \end{aligned} $$ Since $CPA = 3.0 \text{ NM} > 2.0 \text{ NM}$ (safe in open sea), **no action required**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Course Alteration on CPA When own ship alters course by $\theta$ degrees, the relative velocity vector rotates. The new CPA becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} CPA_{new} &= D \cdot \sin(\beta' - \alpha) \end{aligned} $$ where $\beta'$ is the new relative motion direction. The **course alteration required** to bring CPA to a chosen safe value (say $CPA_{safe}$) is given by: $$ \begin{aligned} \sin(\theta_{req}) = \frac{V_T \cdot \sin(\alpha)}{V_O} \cdot \frac{(CPA_{safe} - CPA_{old})}{D \cdot V_{rel}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step-by-Step Worked Example **Given:** $V_O = 15$ kn, $V_T = 12$ kn, $D = 4$ NM, current $CPA = 1.0$ NM, target bearing $\alpha = 30°$, $V_{rel} = 10$ kn, desired $CPA_{safe} = 3.0$ NM. **Step 1 — Compute course change needed** $$ \begin{aligned} \sin(\theta_{req}) &= \frac{12 \cdot \sin(30°)}{15} \cdot \frac{(3.0 - 1.0)}{4 \cdot 10} \\ &= \frac{12 \times 0.5}{15} \cdot \frac{2.0}{40} \\ &= \frac{6}{15} \cdot \frac{1}{20} \\ &= 0.4 \times 0.05 \\ &= 0.02 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \theta_{req} = \arcsin(0.02) \approx 1.15° $$ **Step 2 — Apply Rule 8(c) — "Large, Clear, and Obvious"** COLREGs Rule 8(c) requires alteration to be **of a magnitude to be readily apparent to another vessel**. A **1.15°** alteration does **NOT** meet this criterion. Therefore, the mariner must apply a **minimum of 30° alteration**, far exceeding the calculated minimum. $$ \theta_{applied} = \max(\theta_{req}, 30°) = 30° $$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Insight:** A common student mistake is to **only compute $\theta_{req}$** and submit the small value, losing 4–6 marks for not citing Rule 8(c) minimum alteration. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Action vs. Range Bands This decision matrix is **directly testable** in KTU board exams as a "Construct a Decision Table" question worth 7 marks. | Range Band | TCPA | Action Type | Sound Signal | Light Signal | |---|---|---|---|---| | **D > 12 NM** | > 60 min | Continued monitoring, plot every 3 min | None | None | | **6 NM < D ≤ 12 NM** | 30–60 min | Verify ARPA acquisition, ping rate increased to 10 s | None | None | | **3 NM < D ≤ 6 NM** | 15–30 min | Inform Master, call Master to bridge, prepare engine | One short blast if altering to **starboard** | None | | **2 NM < D ≤ 3 NM** | 6–15 min | Alter course **≥ 30°**, reduce speed if needed | 1 short (stbd) or 2 short (port) | None | | **D ≤ 2 NM** | ≤ 6 min | **EMERGENCY MANEUVER**: full alteration + standby engine | 5 short blasts (Rule 34(d)) — danger signal | Flash Morse "U" (−⋅−) | | **D ≤ 0.5 NM** | ≤ 2 min | **Last resort**: hard rudder, full astern, anchors ready | Continuous sound signal | May show distress light | > [!WARNING] > **Mark-Loss Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Rule 34(c)** (intention signals) with **Rule 34(d)** (danger signal). The 5-short-blast signal is **reserved for danger**, not routine manoeuvring. Examiners deduct **2 marks** per such error. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Stopping Distance (Vessel Dynamics) The stopping distance of a vessel depends on: 1. Approach speed $V_0$ 2. Vessel displacement (mass) $m$ 3. Propeller reverse thrust $T$ 4. Hydrodynamic drag coefficient $C_D$ The equation of motion (simplified) is: $$ \begin{aligned} m \frac{dV}{dt} &= -T(V) - C_D \cdot V^2 \end{aligned} $$ For a fully laden supertanker, $T(V)$ (reversing thrust) builds up gradually over **20–30 seconds** (the "dead period"), so the deceleration is initially small. The total stopping distance $D_{stop}$ for a fully laden VLCC from full ahead to dead-stop is: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{stop} &= \int_0^{t_{stop}} V(t) \, dt \\ &\approx k \cdot V_0^2 \quad \text{where} \ k \approx 0.025 \text{ NM/kn}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical example:** Laden VLCC at 15 knots $$ D_{stop} = 0.025 \times 15^2 = 0.025 \times 225 = 5.625 \text{ NM} \approx 10.4 \text{ km} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** A VLCC at 15 knots needs over **10 km** to stop. This is why **course alteration**, not braking, is the primary collision avoidance technique. **Stopping is a fallback**, not a default. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Integrated Collision Avoidance System (ICAS) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Inputs A1[Visual Lookout
Masthead Sight] A2[Radar Scanner
X-band 9 GHz] A3[AIS Receiver
VHF Channel 87 88] A4[GPS Compass
Heading Sensor] A5[Speed Log
Doppler / EM] end subgraph ProcessingCore[Processing Core - Bridge Console] B1[ARPA Tracker
IMO A.823 19] B2[ECDIS Navigator
SOLAS V 19] B3[CPA TCPA
Calculator] B4[COLREGs Rule
Engine] B5[Decision
Support Module] end subgraph Outputs C1[Manoeuvre
Display] C2[Audible Alarm
CPA TCPA Limit] C3[Rudder Pump
Auto Helm] C4[Engine Telegraph
Auto Slow Down] C5[VHF Radio
AIS DSC Alert] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B2 A4 --> B3 A5 --> B3 B1 --> B3 B2 --> B4 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 B5 --> C1 B5 --> C2 B5 --> C3 B5 --> C4 B5 --> C5 classDef inputClass fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1F618D,color:#000000 classDef processClass fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B,color:#000000 classDef outputClass fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#196F3D,color:#000000 class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 inputClass class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 processClass class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 outputClass ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Five **input sensors** feed the bridge processing core. - The **ARPA tracker** fuses radar + heading + speed into a real-time target plot. - **ECDIS** overlays target positions on electronic charts with NAVTEX/MSA (Maritime Safety Information). - The **COLREGs Rule Engine** interprets each tracked contact against Rule 5, 7, 8, 13, 14, 15, 18, 19 and outputs a recommended manoeuvre. - Five **output actuators** carry out the avoidance — display, alarm, helm, engine, radio. --- ## 4.2 Flow Diagram — COLREGs Encounter Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Two Vessels in Sight] --> Q1{Is risk of collision
established by Rule 7?} Q1 -- No --> Continue[Continue at safe speed
Maintain lookout] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Relative bearing
change appreciable?} Q2 -- Yes --> Cross[Crossing Situation
Rule 15] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Same or opposite
course?} Q3 -- Opposite --> HeadOn[Head-on Situation
Rule 14] Q3 -- Same --> Q4{Target astern
of own ship?} Q4 -- Yes --> Overtake[Overtaking Situation
Rule 13] Q4 -- No --> Restricted[Restricted Visibility
Rule 19] Cross --> R15[Give-way vessel alters
starboard cross astern] HeadOn --> R14[Both alter to starboard
pass port to port] Overtake --> R13[Overtaking vessel
keeps clear] Restricted --> R19[Reduce to minimum
manoeuvring speed
Sound fog signal] ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic — Relative Motion Triangle (Radar Plotting) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Triangle [Relative Motion Triangle] O[Own Ship
Position 0 0] T[Target Ship
Range D Bearing alpha] CPA_POINT[CPA Point
Perpendicular foot] VR[V rel vector
Direction beta] end O -->|Range D| T O -->|Vector V rel| VR VR -->|Endpoint| T O -.->|Perpendicular| CPA_POINT CPA_POINT -->|Time TCPA| T O -->|Angle alpha| T VR -->|Angle beta| O ``` **How to interpret:** - Own ship is at the origin. - The **relative motion vector** $V_{rel}$ originates at own ship and ends at the target's predicted position. - The **CPA point** is the foot of perpendicular from own ship's predicted track to the relative motion line. - A **safe maneuver** is one that swings $V_{rel}$ so the perpendicular foot lands at **≥ 2 NM** from own ship's planned track. --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Sound and Light Signal Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vessel intends to manoeuvre] --> B{Action type?} B -- Alter Starboard --> C[ONE Short Blast
100 m visibility min] B -- Alter Port --> D[TWO Short Blasts
100 m visibility min] B -- Astern Propulsion --> E[THREE Short Blasts
100 m visibility min] B -- Danger Unclear --> F[FIVE Short Blasts
Rule 34 d] B -- Cannot Comprehend --> G[Four Short Blasts
Rule 34 e] H[Vessel in restricted visibility] --> I[RULE 35
Power driven making way
One prolonged every 2 min] J[Vessel stopped] --> K[Two prolonged
with two short every 2 min] L[Vessel constrained by draft] --> M[Four short blasts
Rule 27] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > All whistle signals above require visibility of **at least 1 nautical mile**. Below that, only the **fog signals (Rule 35)** are used. Refer to the **Annex III** of COLREGs for the complete signal ladder. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the term "Risk of Collision" as per COLREGs Rule 7. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, ESE, Module 3]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** According to **Rule 7** of the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs), 1972: > "Every vessel shall use all available means appropriate to the prevailing circumstances and conditions to determine if a risk of collision exists. If there is any doubt such risk shall be deemed to exist." **Proper determination involves:** 1. Radar plotting (range, bearing, CPA, TCPA). 2. Systematic observation of compass bearing — **if the bearing does not appreciably change, risk exists**. 3. Long-range visual scanning using azimuth ring on radar PPI. 4. AIS data cross-check for target identity and SOG/COG. 5. Sound and light signal awareness. A vessel operating with **ARPA auto-acquisition** must still maintain a manual plotting log every 6 minutes for verification. --- ### Question 2: List the maneuvering and warning signals specified in Rule 34 of COLREGs. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, ESE, Module 3]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Signal | Meaning | Rule Reference | |---|---|---| | 1 short blast | Altering course to **starboard** | 34(a)(i) | | 2 short blasts | Altering course to **port** | 34(a)(ii) | | 3 short blasts | Operating **astern propulsion** | 34(a)(iii) | | 4 short blasts | **Uncertainty** about intentions | 34(e) | | 5 or more short blasts | **Danger signal** | 34(d) | | At least 5 short + rapid bell | Emergency in restricted visibility | 35(d) | All signals must be **≥ 4 seconds** long, with **1-second intervals** between them, audible to **1 nautical mile minimum**. --- ## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, ESE, Module 3, Optional Choice A]** **Q.** (a) Describe in detail the **principles, hierarchy, and tactical application** of Collision Avoidance Techniques under COLREGs, with reference to Rules 5, 6, 7, and 8. **(7 marks)** (b) A vessel observes a target on radar at **range 8 NM**, **bearing 045°** relative, target **speed 14 knots**, **course 270°**. Own ship is on **course 090°, speed 12 knots**. Compute the **CPA, TCPA**, and recommend whether action is needed. If action is required, propose the **minimum magnitude of course alteration** as per Rule 8(c). **(7 marks)** #### (a) Model Solution (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** Collision avoidance in maritime operations is governed primarily by **COLREGs (1972)** — a set of 38 rules organized into five parts (A–E). The four foundational rules for OOW are 5, 6, 7, and 8. **Rule 5 — Lookout (1.5 marks):** - Every vessel must maintain a **proper lookout by all available means** — visual,听觉 (aural), radar, AIS, in clear weather and restricted visibility. - This is an **unbroken duty** — no relief except handover under formal watchkeeping procedure. - A proper lookout = qualified, alert, in a position from which they can see/ hear fully. **Rule 6 — Safe Speed (1.5 marks):** - Speed must be such that **proper and effective action can be taken** to avoid collision. - Factors: visibility, traffic density, maneuverability, stopping distance, weather, draft vs. depth, radar characteristics. - In restricted visibility, **Rule 19** additionally mandates **safe speed appropriate to the circumstances**. **Rule 7 — Risk of Collision (1.5 marks):** - Assumed if **compass bearing does not appreciably change**. - ARPA may show **passing CPA of zero** — even then, mariner must verify, not assume. - Doubt = risk. **Rule 8 — Action to Avoid Collision (1.5 marks):** - Action must be **positive, made in ample time, and with due regard to good seamanship**. - Must be **large enough to be readily apparent** (Rule 8(c) — minimum 30° alteration, or equivalent speed change). - **Avoid** a succession of small alterations. - If space is restricted, **reduce speed, stop, or reverse** — preferable to course change. - Avoid crossing traffic ahead of another vessel. **Diagram Suggestion for full marks (1 mark):** Draw the **relative motion triangle** showing own ship, target, and relative velocity vector, with CPA point foot of perpendicular. --- #### (b) Model Solution (7 marks) **Step 1 — Set up the geometry (1 mark)** Given: - Own ship: $V_O = 12$ kn, Course = 090° - Target: $V_T = 14$ kn, Course = 270° (so target is moving westward; own ship is moving eastward → opposite courses) - Range $D = 8$ NM, relative bearing $\alpha = 045°$ (target is off own ship's starboard bow) - Angle between own course and target course = 270° - 090° = 180° → **Head-on situation (Rule 14)** **Step 2 — Compute relative speed (2 marks)** For opposite courses, $\cos(180°) = -1$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{rel} &= \sqrt{V_T^2 + V_O^2 - 2 V_T V_O \cos(180°)} \\ &= \sqrt{14^2 + 12^2 - 2(14)(12)(-1)} \\ &= \sqrt{196 + 144 + 336} \\ &= \sqrt{676} \\ &= 26 \text{ knots} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute CPA (2 marks)** In a **head-on situation** with target exactly on bearing 045° and target course directly opposite, the relative motion vector lies **along the line of sight**, meaning: $$ \begin{aligned} CPA &= D \cdot \sin(0°) = 0 \text{ NM} \end{aligned} $$ (0° because $\beta = \alpha$ when courses are reciprocal and bearings are reciprocal) **Step 4 — Compute TCPA (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} TCPA &= \frac{D}{V_{rel}} = \frac{8}{26} \times 60 = 18.46 \text{ min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Decision and Maneuver (1 mark)** - $CPA = 0 \text{ NM}$ → **collision imminent**. - $TCPA = 18.46 \text{ min}$ → ample time. - **Head-on situation, Rule 14** → both vessels must **alter course to starboard**, passing **port-side to port-side**. - Minimum alteration per **Rule 8(c)** = **30° to starboard**. - New own course = 090° + 30° = **120°**. - Sound signal: **one short blast** (Rule 34(a)(i)). **Conclusion:** Immediate starboard alteration of **≥ 30°**, accompanied by **one short blast** sound signal. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, ESE, Module 3, Optional Choice B]` **Q.** (a) Explain the **construction, working, and limitations** of ARPA as an aid to collision avoidance. Discuss the **ARPA IMO Performance Standards (Resolution A.823(19))**. **(7 marks)** (b) Discuss the **special conduct rules** for vessels in **restricted visibility** under Rule 19 and compare the **navigation lights displayed** by a power-driven vessel underway vs. a vessel at anchor vs. a vessel constrained by her draught. **(7 marks)** #### (a) Model Solution (7 marks) **ARPA Definition (1 mark):** **Automatic Radar Plotting Aid (ARPA)** is a computer-assisted radar system that automatically tracks radar targets, calculates their motion parameters (CPA, TCPA, course, speed), and provides graphical and audible collision avoidance warnings. **Construction (2 marks):** 1. **Radar Transceiver** (X-band 3 cm / S-band 10 cm) — emits pulses, receives echoes. 2. **Display Unit (PPI)** — Plan Position Indicator. 3. **Tracking Module (Computer)** — digitizes echo signals, performs **alpha-beta or Kalman filtering** for trajectory smoothing. 4. **Operator Console** — input gain, sea clutter, rain clutter, range scale. 5. **Acquisition Subsystem** — manual (joystick/zone) or automatic (guard zones of 1.5–4 NM radius). 6. **Output Display** — vector (true/relative), predicted area of danger (PAD), trails (true/relative). **Working (2 marks):** - Targets are **acquired** (manually or auto) within guard zones. - Computer assigns a **unique tracking number** to each target. - Smoothing filter extrapolates motion over successive scans (typically every 3 s or 12 s). - Computes **CPA, TCPA, BCR (Bow Crossing Range), BCT (Bow Crossing Time)**. - Generates **alarms** when CPA < user-set limit (typically 2 NM open sea). - Predicted **Area of Danger (PAD)** marks zone of possible future collision. **IMO Resolution A.823(19) Performance Standards (1.5 marks):** - Tracking capacity: minimum **20 targets simultaneously**. - **Accuracy**: course ± 10°, speed ± 1 kn (or 10%). - Detection of course/speed change within **1 minute** of target maneuver. - Tracking reliability: **P_D > 0.9** for targets within radar range. - **CPA/TCPA accuracy** ± 0.1 NM, ± 0.5 min. **Limitations (0.5 marks):** - Cluttered environments (rain, sea) reduce detection. - Cannot detect **non-radar-reflective** targets (wooden boats). - Failure to acquire small or fast maneuvers. - Operator over-reliance ("automation complacency"). --- #### (b) Model Solution (7 marks) **Rule 19 — Conduct in Restricted Visibility (3.5 marks):** Applicable when vessels are **not in sight of one another** due to fog, mist, snow, heavy rain, dust storms. - Every vessel must **proceed at a safe speed** adapted to visibility, traffic density, maneuverability. - Power-driven vessel **making way through water**: 1 prolonged blast every 2 minutes. - Power-driven vessel **stopped and making no way through water**: 2 prolonged blasts every 2 minutes. - Vessel **constrained by draught**, **vessel not under command**, **sailing vessel**, **vessel engaged in fishing**, **vessel engaged in towing** → 1 prolonged + 2 short every 2 minutes. - **Sailing vessel** and **vessel under oars** → 1 prolonged + 2 short every 2 minutes. - **Vessel at anchor** → ring bell rapidly for 5 seconds at intervals of not more than 1 minute. **Navigation Lights Comparison Table (3.5 marks):** | Vessel Status | Masthead Light | Sidelights | Stern Light | Special Lights | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Power-driven vessel underway (≥ 50 m)** | 2 (fore + main) | Port (red) + Stbd (green) | White (visible 135°) | None | | **Power-driven vessel underway (< 50 m)** | 1 (main) | Port + Stbd | White | Optional all-round | | **Vessel at anchor** | None (masthead off) | None | None (or working lights) | **White all-round 360°** at fore + lower deck | | **Vessel aground** | None | None | None | **2 red all-round** vertically | | **Vessel constrained by draught** | Same as power-driven | Same | Same | **3 red all-round** vertically | | **Vessel not under command (NUC)** | Same | Same | Same | **2 red all-round** vertically | | **Vessel engaged in fishing (trawling)** | None | None | None | Green over white all-round | | **Vessel towing (push/tow)** | 3 masthead in line | Port + Stbd | Yellow (towing) | Yellow towing light above stern | **Visibility range requirements:** All lights visible to minimum **2 NM** (sidelights), **3 NM** (masthead, stern), **6 NM** for vessels ≥ 50 m masthead. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Loss-of-Marks Scenarios in Collision Avoidance Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "Risk of Collision" with "Collision Imminent"** — Rule 7 mandates *early* detection. Many students write about imminent situations (within 1 NM), losing 2 marks for missing the early-action context. > 2. **Quoting Rule 8(c) incorrectly** — The minimum 30° alteration is **"readily apparent"** benchmark, not an arbitrary value. Stating "30° to 60° alteration" is fine, but quoting exactly "30°" without justification costs marks. > 3. **Neglecting the "Sound Signal" step** — Every course alteration requires **one short blast (stbd) or two short blasts (port)** under Rule 34. Many students forget this — losing 1–2 marks. > 4. **Using 1 short blast + 2 short blasts simultaneously** — This is invalid; only one type per maneuver. > 5. **Confusing Rule 13 (Overtaking) with Rule 14 (Head-on)** — Overtaking means target is in **sector 22.5° abaft the beam**. Anything within the 22.5° forward sector is either head-on (reciprocal) or crossing (Rule 15). > 6. **Forgetting the speed-reduction option in Rule 8** — Rule 8(e) explicitly allows **slackening speed, stopping, or reversing**. Failing to mention these costs a deduction. > 7. **ARPA accuracy mention missing** — When asked about ARPA, students frequently describe "what it shows" but not "the accuracy standards from A.823(19)". Examiners specifically look for **±10° course, ±1 kn speed**. > 8. **Mishandling Rule 19 in restricted visibility** — All vessels are deemed **"give-way"** in restricted visibility; there is no stand-on vessel. Students who claim a "stand-on vessel" in fog lose 3+ marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **COLREGs** is the **supreme law** of collision avoidance at sea, made binding under **SOLAS Chapter V**. - The **Five Pillars**: Detect → Assess → Identify → Decide → Execute. - **Rule 5** mandates lookout; **Rule 6** mandates safe speed; **Rule 7** mandates risk assessment; **Rule 8** mandates action. - **Rule 13** governs **overtaking** (give-way vessel = overtaker); **Rule 14** governs **head-on** (both alter stbd); **Rule 15** governs **crossing** (give-way alters stbd, crosses astern of stand-on). - **Rule 18** establishes the **priority hierarchy**: NUC > RAM > CBD > Fishing > Sailing > Power. - **Rule 19** is a **stand-alone regime** for restricted visibility — no stand-on vessel exists. - **Rule 8(c)**: Minimum alteration = **30°**, **Rule 8(e)**: Speed reduction is **a valid and often preferred** alternative to course change. - **CPA < 2 NM open sea / 0.5 NM restricted** → Action mandatory. - **Sound signals**: 1 short (stbd), 2 short (port), 3 short (astern), 4 short (uncertainty), 5 short (danger). - **Light signals**: Morse **"U"** (⋅⋅⋅⋅ — − − − − —) is the international **"You are running into danger"** signal (Rule 37). - **ARPA** must track **≥ 20 targets** with **CPA ±0.1 NM** and **TCPA ±0.5 min** accuracy. - **AIS** complements ARPA; **does not replace it**. AIS may lie if GPS feed is jammed or spoofed. - **Stopping distance** of a fully laden VLCC at 15 kn ≈ **5.6 NM** — so course change is **preferred** over stopping. - **VTS** is an *advisory* system; **COLREGs always trump VTS instructions** unless danger is imminent and VTS officer is competent. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Memory Aid — The "8-13-14-15-19" Sequence:** > Rule 8 (Action), 13 (Overtake), 14 (Head-on), 15 (Crossing), 19 (Restricted Visibility). > Remember them in this order — it mirrors **most KTU paper sequence**.

topics.title: Search and Rescue (SAR) Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Search and Rescue (SAR) Operations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Search and Rescue (SAR)** is the internationally coordinated, multi-phase operational activity employed to locate persons in distress, recover them, and deliver them to a place of safety, conducted under the framework of the **International Convention on Maritime Search and Rescue (SAR Convention), 1979**, as amended, and operationalized through the **International Aeronautical and Maritime Search and Rescue Manual (IAMSAR Manual)** published jointly by the **International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO)** and the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Definition Block** > SAR Operations in the KTU framework is treated as a sub-system of the **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)**. The syllabus expects students to master the **five SAR phases**, **search patterns**, **sweep width theory**, and **rescue coordination architecture (RCC/RSC)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you drop your car keys in a vast, dark, foggy football stadium at night. You have only one flashlight, two friends, and 30 minutes before the stadium closes. What do you do? - You don't randomly run around — that wastes time. - You estimate **where** the keys most likely fell (**datum point**). - You plan a **systematic sweep pattern** (e.g., walking the rows in a grid, or spiraling outward). - You assign **roles** — one person calls out, one shines the light, one guides. - When found, you **secure** the keys and **exit safely**. That is essentially SAR at sea: the stadium is the **search area**, the keys are the **distressed vessel/person**, the flashlight is the **search asset (ship/aircraft)**, and the 30-minute deadline is the **survival time** of those in distress. > [!NOTE] > The **single most important principle** in SAR: *Speed saves lives.* Every hour of delay reduces the survival probability of a person in cold water by approximately **40–50%** (IMO cold-water immersion tables). This is why IAMSAR emphasizes **rapid initial action** before a full plan is even complete. ## 1.3 Key Entities and Standard Metrics - **Search Object (SO)**: The distressed unit (vessel, aircraft, person, raft). - **Datum (D)**: The most probable position of the SO at the search commencement time. - **Search Area (A)**: The geographic region in which the SO is believed to be located. - **Total Probable Error (E)**: The statistical uncertainty in the datum position, typically expressed in **Nautical Miles (NM)**. - **Sweep Width (W)**: The width of the search path within which a sensor can detect the SO with a probability of **100%** (if the SO is within the path). - **Coverage Factor (C)**: The ratio of actual track spacing to sweep width, indicating search intensity. - **Probability of Detection (POD)**: The likelihood that a sensor will detect the SO if it passes within sensor range. > [!NOTE] > All distances in maritime SAR are expressed in **Nautical Miles (1 NM = 1.852 km)**, and all times in **Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)**. KTU examinations universally expect NM and UTC as the standard units. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The IAMSAR Five-Phase SAR Model The IAMSAR Manual divides every SAR event into **five sequential phases**. KTU questions frequently test the boundaries and outputs of each phase. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Five Phases (memorize in order):** > 1. **Awareness** — Information receipt. > 2. **Initial Action** — Immediate response and preliminary plan. > 3. **Planning** — Detailed operational plan development. > 4. **Operations** — Execution of the search and rescue. > 5. **Conclusion** — Return of assets, debriefing, and case closure. ### 2.1.1 Phase 1 — Awareness - Triggered by receipt of a distress signal, mayday relay, or sighting report. - Key output: **Acknowledgement of distress** to the originator. - Time-critical: the **first 30 minutes** determine the survivability curve. ### 2.1.2 Phase 2 — Initial Action - The **Rescue Coordination Centre (RCC)** takes charge. - Issues **Mayday Relay** on appropriate frequencies (VHF Channel 16, MF/HF DSC, INMARSAT). - Alerts adjacent RCCs along the **Mutual Aid boundary**. - Designates an **On-Scene Coordinator (OSC)** for surface assets or a **Coordinator Surface Search (CSS)**. ### 2.1.3 Phase 3 — Planning This is the most calculation-intensive phase in KTU theory papers. Planning involves: - **Datum Computation**: Estimating the SO's likely position using: - Last Known Position (LKP) - Time since LKP - Drift (leeway + current + wind) - **Search Area Determination**: Drawing an ellipse or circle around the datum with radius equal to the Total Probable Error. - **Asset Allocation**: Matching available SRUs (Search and Rescue Units) to sub-areas. - **Pattern Selection**: Choosing the optimal search pattern based on: - Environment (open sea, coastal, ice) - SO type (survivor in water, liferaft, vessel) - Weather and visibility ### 2.1.4 Phase 4 — Operations - **Search Execution**: SRUs fly/sail assigned tracks. - **Rescue Execution**: Recovery of survivors using: - **Recovery by boat** (alongside, towing, slip-line) - **Recovery by helicopter** (highline, double-lift, single-lift, winch) - **Recovery in surf** (breaker-line technique, pier-head jumping) - **On-Scene Coordination** maintained by OSC. ### 2.1.5 Phase 5 — Conclusion - All SRUs released; OSC stands down. - RCC coordinates **medical evacuation (MEDEVAC)** if needed. - Incident report filed. - **Critical Incident Stress (CIS)** debriefing conducted for responders. ## 2.2 Search Patterns — The Four IAMSAR Standards | Pattern | Best For | Geometry | |---|---|---| | **Expanding Square Search (SS)** | Datum is well-defined; one SRU | Squares of increasing leg length, 90° turns | | **Sector Search (VS)** | Datum is well-defined; one SRU | Three expanding sectors from datum | | **Parallel Track Search (PS)** | Datum uncertain; multiple SRUs | Parallel legs, equal spacing | | **Track Line Search (TS)** | Vessel with known course & speed | Rectangle along intended track | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Hook:** "**E**xpanding Square, **S**ector, **P**arallel = **ESP**" for single asset at a defined datum; **Parallel Track = PS** = "**P**lural assets, **S**tandard pattern." ## 2.3 Sweep Width, Coverage, and POD Theory This is the **mathematical heart** of SAR planning and a guaranteed 7–14 mark topic in KTU Part B. ### 2.3.1 Sweep Width (W) $$W = 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan(\theta/2)$$ Where: - $d$ = detection range of the sensor - $\theta$ = sensor scan angle (for radar/optical sensors) ### 2.3.2 Coverage Factor (C) $$C = \frac{W}{S}$$ Where: - $S$ = Track Spacing (distance between parallel search legs) - If $C \geq 1.0$ → **Effective Sweep** (overlap ensures no gaps) - If $C < 1.0$ → **Less Effective Sweep** (gaps exist) - $C = 0.5$ → **Standard IAMSAR Search** (50% overlap) ### 2.3.3 Area of Probability of Detection (POD) — Single Pass $$POD_{\text{pass}} = POD_{\max} \cdot \left[1 - e^{-C}\right]$$ For radar/optical sensors where $POD_{\max} = 1.0$ (perfect detection if within sweep). ### 2.3.4 Cumulative POD (Multiple Passes) $$POD_{\text{cum}} = 1 - (1 - POD_{\text{pass}})^n$$ Where $n$ = number of search passes. ### 2.3.5 Probability of Success (POS) $$POS = POD_{\text{cum}} \times POC$$ Where $POC$ = Probability of Containment (likelihood the SO is actually inside the searched area). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D = LKP + (V_d \times t)$ | Datum = Last Known Position + (drift speed × time) | NM | | 2 | $A = \pi \cdot E^2$ (circular) or $A = \pi \cdot a \cdot b$ (elliptical) | Search area from probable error ellipse/circle | NM² | | 3 | $W = 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan(\theta/2)$ | Sweep width | NM | | 4 | $C = W / S$ | Coverage factor | dimensionless | | 5 | $POD = 1 - e^{-C}$ | Probability of detection (single pass, $POD_{\max}=1$) | 0 to 1 | | 6 | $POD_{\text{cum}} = 1 - (1-POD)^n$ | Cumulative POD over $n$ passes | 0 to 1 | | 7 | $POS = POD_{\text{cum}} \times POC$ | Probability of mission success | 0 to 1 | | 8 | $T_{\text{survival}} = f(T_{\text{water}})$ | Survival time vs. water temperature | hours | | 9 | $S_{\text{opt}} = W \cdot \sqrt{\frac{POC}{T}}$ | Optimal track spacing (advanced) | NM | | 10 | $R = \sqrt{A / C}$ | Area covered per unit time | NM²/hr | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** In Coverage Factor problems, students frequently forget that $C = 1$ means **edge-to-edge** sweep, **not** a 100% overlap. To get a 100% overlap, you need $C = 2.0$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Maritime RCCs** (e.g., Indian Coast Guard MRCC Mumbai, MRCC Chilika) use these exact formulas in their **SAR Planning System (SARPS)** workstations. - **Aviation SAR** uses parallel track patterns at **3 NM and 5 NM** spacing for night vs. day searches. - **Oil Rig / Offshore** emergency response plans embed sweep width calculations for helicopter rescue baskets. - **Autonomous USVs (Unmanned Surface Vessels)** like the Saildrone use these patterns for **wide-area persistent SAR** in the Arctic. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Datum Computation with Drift > [!NOTE] > This derivation is a **KTU 2024 favorite** — it appears almost every year in Part B (14 marks) as a full sub-question. ### Problem Setup A vessel made a **Mayday call** at **0900 UTC** from position **20°00'N, 070°00'E**. She was reported **adrift** (engines failed) heading **unknown**, with an estimated **leeway drift of 1.5 knots** toward the **South-Southwest (SSW, 202.5° True)**. Wind and current set is **0.5 knots** toward the **West (270° True)**. The RCC computes a search datum at **1200 UTC**. ### Step 1 — Resolve Drift into North-South and East-West Components Total drift speed vector: - $V_{leeway} = 1.5$ kt toward $202.5°T$ - $V_{current} = 0.5$ kt toward $270°T$ North-South component (using $N = V \cos(\theta)$ where $\theta$ is measured from North, positive northward): $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{leeway}} &= 1.5 \cdot \cos(202.5°) \\ &= 1.5 \cdot (-0.9239) \\ &= -1.3858 \text{ kt (southward)} \\[4pt] N_{\text{current}} &= 0.5 \cdot \cos(270°) \\ &= 0.5 \cdot (0) \\ &= 0.0000 \text{ kt} \\[4pt] N_{\text{total}} &= -1.3858 + 0.0000 = -1.3858 \text{ kt (southward)} \end{aligned} $$ East-West component (using $E = V \sin(\theta)$, positive eastward): $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{leeway}} &= 1.5 \cdot \sin(202.5°) \\ &= 1.5 \cdot (-0.3827) \\ &= -0.5740 \text{ kt (westward)} \\[4pt] E_{\text{current}} &= 0.5 \cdot \sin(270°) \\ &= 0.5 \cdot (-1) \\ &= -0.5000 \text{ kt (westward)} \\[4pt] E_{\text{total}} &= -0.5740 + (-0.5000) = -1.0740 \text{ kt (westward)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Resultant Drift Speed and Direction $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{drift}} &= \sqrt{N_{\text{total}}^2 + E_{\text{total}}^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(-1.3858)^2 + (-1.0740)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{1.9204 + 1.1535} \\ &= \sqrt{3.0739} \\ &= 1.7533 \text{ kt} \\[6pt] \theta_{\text{drift}} &= \arctan\left(\frac{E}{N}\right) \\ &= \arctan\left(\frac{-1.0740}{-1.3858}\right) \\ &= \arctan(0.7749) \\ &= 37.78° \end{aligned} $$ Since both $N$ and $E$ are negative (the third quadrant of the compass), the true bearing is: $$ \theta_{\text{True}} = 180° + 37.78° = 217.78°T $$ This corresponds to a **South-Southwest** direction, which is consistent with the dominant leeway vector — a good **self-check** of the calculation. ### Step 3 — Compute Elapsed Time $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta t &= 1200 \text{ UTC} - 0900 \text{ UTC} = 3 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Drift Distance $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\text{drift}} &= V_{\text{drift}} \times \Delta t \\ &= 1.7533 \text{ kt} \times 3 \text{ hr} \\ &= 5.2599 \text{ NM} \approx 5.26 \text{ NM} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Convert Drift Distance to Latitude and Longitude Offsets Using 1 NM = 1 minute of latitude: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta \text{Lat} &= D \cdot \cos(217.78°) = 5.2599 \cdot (-0.7904) = -4.157 \text{ min} \\ \Delta \text{Lon} &= D \cdot \sin(217.78°) = 5.2599 \cdot (-0.6126) = -3.222 \text{ min} \end{aligned} $$ Applying these offsets to the LKP (20°00.0'N, 070°00.0'E): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{New Latitude} &= 20°00.0'N - 4.157' = 19°55.84'N \\ \text{New Longitude} &= 070°00.0'E - 3.222' = 069°56.78'E \end{aligned} $$ ### **Final Datum: 19°55.8'N, 069°56.8'E** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Significance:** This datum is the **center of the search ellipse**. The RCC will then draw a circle of radius equal to the Total Probable Error (often **5–10 NM** for a drifting vessel over 3 hours) around this point to define the **Search Area**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Cumulative POD After Three Search Passes > [!NOTE] > This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark KTU Part B sub-question** whenever sweep width theory appears. ### Given - Sensor: Maritime patrol aircraft radar - Sweep width: $W = 4.0$ NM - Track spacing: $S = 2.0$ NM - Number of passes: $n = 3$ ### Step 1 — Coverage Factor $$ C = \frac{W}{S} = \frac{4.0}{2.0} = 2.0 $$ This is a **100% overlap** search — every point is swept at least twice on each pass. ### Step 2 — Single-Pass POD $$ POD_{\text{pass}} = 1 - e^{-C} = 1 - e^{-2.0} = 1 - 0.1353 = 0.8647 $$ So each pass has an **86.47%** chance of detecting the SO if it lies within the sweep. ### Step 3 — Cumulative POD After 3 Passes $$ \begin{aligned} POD_{\text{cum}} &= 1 - (1 - POD_{\text{pass}})^n \\ &= 1 - (1 - 0.8647)^3 \\ &= 1 - (0.1353)^3 \\ &= 1 - 0.00248 \\ &= 0.9975 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Probability of Success (Assuming POC = 0.8) $$ POS = POD_{\text{cum}} \times POC = 0.9975 \times 0.8 = 0.798 \approx 79.8\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the third pass adds almost nothing (from 96.5% to 99.75%). This is the **law of diminishing returns** in SAR — the IAMSAR Manual uses this principle to recommend **ceasing search** when the marginal POS gain from an additional pass falls below a threshold (typically 5%). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: IAMSAR Five-Phase Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: AWARENESS
Distress Signal Received] --> B[Phase 2: INITIAL ACTION
Mayday Relay + RCC Alert] B --> C[Phase 3: PLANNING
Datum + Search Area + Pattern] C --> D[Phase 4: OPERATIONS
Search Execution + Rescue] D --> E[Phase 5: CONCLUSION
Release Assets + Debrief] E --> F[Case Closed
Reports Archived] B -.-> G[OSC / SMC Appointed] C -.-> H[SRUs Allocated] D -.-> I[MEDEVAC if Required] D -.-> J[Survivors Delivered to Safety] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style B fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C fill:#a16207,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style D fill:#15803d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style E fill:#6b21a8,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style F fill:#374151,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Search Pattern Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Start: Pattern Selection] --> Q{Datum Accuracy?} Q -->|Well Defined
Less than 5 NM| R{Number of SRUs?} Q -->|Poorly Defined
Greater than 5 NM| S[Use PARALLEL TRACK
Track Spacing equals W] R -->|One SRU| T{Large Area?} R -->|Multiple SRUs| S T -->|Yes Less than 100 sq NM| U[Use EXPANDING SQUARE
Search] T -->|No Larger Area| V[Use SECTOR SEARCH
Three Sectors 120 deg] S --> W[Coordinate SRU Start Points
at Pattern Entry Points] U --> X[Begin at Datum
First Leg 1 NM
Turn 90 deg right] V --> Y[Begin at Datum
Three Legs outward 2 NM
then return] W --> Z[Operations Phase Begins] X --> Z Y --> Z style P fill:#0f766e,color:#fff style S fill:#1d4ed8,color:#fff style U fill:#7c2d12,color:#fff style V fill:#7c2d12,color:#fff style Z fill:#166534,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: RCC Information Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Senders D1[Vessel MAYDAY] D2[EPIRB Signal 406 MHz] D3[SART Activation] D4[Inmarsat-C Distress] D5[Ship Sighting Report] end Senders --> R1[Coast Earth Station
or Local Coast Station] R1 --> R2[Rescue Coordination Centre
RCC] R2 --> R3[Search Mission Coordinator
SMC] R3 --> R4[On Scene Coordinator
OSC] R4 --> R5[SRU 1 Ship] R4 --> R6[SRU 2 Aircraft] R4 --> R7[SRU 3 Helicopter] R2 -.Adjacent RCC.-> R8[Mutual Aid RCC] style R2 fill:#b91c1c,color:#fff style R3 fill:#a16207,color:#fff style R4 fill:#15803d,color:#fff style R8 fill:#6b21a8,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Sweep Width and Coverage Visual ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Search Sensor Footprint] --> B[Sweep Width W] B --> C{Coverage Factor C = W / S} C -->|C greater than 1| D[Effective Sweep
Overlapping Coverage] C -->|C equals 1| E[Edge to Edge
No Gap] C -->|C less than 1| F[Gap Exists
SO may be Missed] D --> G[Single Pass POD high] E --> H[Single Pass POD moderate] F --> I[Single Pass POD low] G --> J[Reduce Track Spacing S] H --> J I --> J style C fill:#1e40af,color:#fff style D fill:#166534,color:#fff style E fill:#ca8a04,color:#fff style F fill:#991b1b,color:#fff style J fill:#374151,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Search and Rescue (SAR)** operations. List the **five phases** of SAR as per the IAMSAR Manual. #### Model Answer **Search and Rescue (SAR) Operations** are internationally coordinated activities carried out to locate persons in distress at sea, recover them, and deliver them to a place of safety, as defined under the **SAR Convention 1979** and operationalized via the **IAMSAR Manual**. **The Five Phases:** 1. **Awareness** — Receipt of distress information. 2. **Initial Action** — Preliminary response, Mayday relay, RCC alert. 3. **Planning** — Datum computation, search area definition, pattern selection. 4. **Operations** — Execution of search patterns and recovery of survivors. 5. **Conclusion** — Release of SRUs, debriefing, and case closure. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is the difference between a **Rescue Coordination Centre (RCC)** and an **On-Scene Coordinator (OSC)**? #### Model Answer | Aspect | RCC | OSC | |---|---|---| | **Location** | Shore-based | At or near the search scene | | **Role** | Overall mission coordination, planning, and resource allocation | Tactical coordination of SRUs in the search area | | **Authority** | Full SAR mission authority | Limited to on-scene tactical decisions | | **Appointed by** | State administration | RCC | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **International Convention on Maritime Search and Rescue (SAR Convention), 1979**, highlighting its **objectives, obligations of contracting states, and the concept of Search and Rescue Regions (SRRs)**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fishing vessel transmits a Mayday at **0600 UTC** from position **15°30'N, 072°45'E**. The vessel is disabled and drifting at **2.0 knots** toward **South (180°T)**. A surface current of **0.8 knots** sets toward **East (090°T)**. The RCC plans search commencement at **1100 UTC**. - (i) Compute the **search datum**. **(4 marks)** - (ii) If the **Total Probable Error (E)** is **6 NM**, calculate the **search area**. **(3 marks)** #### Model Answer **(a) SAR Convention 1979 — Detailed Explanation** The **International Convention on Maritime Search and Rescue (SAR Convention)**, adopted in **Hamburg, Germany, in 1979** and entered into force on **22 June 1985**, is the foundational international treaty governing maritime SAR cooperation. **Objectives:** 1. To develop an **international SAR plan** ensuring cooperation among states. 2. To establish **Search and Rescue Regions (SRRs)** in which states are responsible for providing SAR services. 3. To facilitate **mutual assistance** between neighbouring RCCs. 4. To standardize **operating procedures** through the IAMSAR Manual. **Obligations of Contracting States:** - Each contracting state must **establish national SAR services** and provide **adequate facilities**. - States must **agree on the boundaries of their SRRs** through regional agreements. - States must **accept assistance** from other states in distress situations. - States must **cooperate with neighbouring states** for the rescue of persons in distress regardless of nationality or circumstances. **Search and Rescue Region (SRR):** - A defined geographic area within which a state assumes **primary responsibility** for SAR operations. - SRRs are typically delimited to **avoid gaps or overlaps**, ensuring global coverage. - India, for instance, has **13 SRRs** coordinated by the **Indian Coast Guard** (e.g., MRCC Mumbai, MRCC Port Blair). **Conclusion:** The SAR Convention 1979 transformed SAR from a national, ad-hoc activity into a **coordinated, international, treaty-bound obligation** that saves thousands of lives annually. --- **(b) Datum and Search Area Calculation** **(i) Datum Computation** **Step 1:** Resolve drift vectors into N-S and E-W components. North-South: $$ N_{\text{leeway}} = 2.0 \cdot \cos(180°) = 2.0 \cdot (-1) = -2.000 \text{ kt} $$ $$ N_{\text{current}} = 0.8 \cdot \cos(90°) = 0.8 \cdot 0 = 0.000 \text{ kt} $$ $$ N_{\text{total}} = -2.000 \text{ kt (southward)} $$ East-West: $$ E_{\text{leeway}} = 2.0 \cdot \sin(180°) = 0.000 \text{ kt} $$ $$ E_{\text{current}} = 0.8 \cdot \sin(90°) = 0.8 \cdot 1 = 0.800 \text{ kt (eastward)} $$ $$ E_{\text{total}} = 0.800 \text{ kt (eastward)} $$ **Step 2:** Resultant drift speed and direction. $$ V_{\text{drift}} = \sqrt{(-2.000)^2 + (0.800)^2} = \sqrt{4.00 + 0.64} = \sqrt{4.64} = 2.154 \text{ kt} $$ $$ \theta = \arctan\left(\frac{0.800}{-2.000}\right) = \arctan(-0.400) = -21.8° $$ Since $N$ is negative and $E$ is positive (fourth quadrant of compass): $$ \theta_{\text{True}} = 360° - 21.8° = 338.2°T \text{ (NNW direction — physical sense check passes)} $$ Wait — recalculating: the drift is southward AND eastward, so the true bearing should be in the **SE quadrant** (between 90° and 180°). The angle $\arctan(0.8 / -2.0) = -21.8°$ is referenced to the south axis. The true bearing is: $$ \theta_{\text{True}} = 180° - 21.8° = 158.2°T \text{ (SSE — physically correct)} $$ **Step 3:** Elapsed time. $$ \Delta t = 1100 - 0600 = 5 \text{ hours} $$ **Step 4:** Drift distance. $$ D = 2.154 \text{ kt} \times 5 \text{ hr} = 10.77 \text{ NM} $$ **Step 5:** Convert to lat/lon offsets. $$ \Delta \text{Lat} = 10.77 \cdot \cos(158.2°) = 10.77 \cdot (-0.931) = -10.03 \text{ min} $$ $$ \Delta \text{Lon} = 10.77 \cdot \sin(158.2°) = 10.77 \cdot 0.364 = 3.92 \text{ min} $$ **Final Datum:** $$ \text{Latitude} = 15°30.0'N - 10.03' = 15°19.97'N \approx 15°20.0'N $$ $$ \text{Longitude} = 072°45.0'E + 3.92' = 072°48.92'E \approx 072°48.9'E $$ **Datum Position: 15°20.0'N, 072°48.9'E** **(ii) Search Area** Assuming a circular search area (no preferred axis): $$ A = \pi \cdot E^2 = \pi \cdot (6)^2 = 113.1 \text{ NM}^2 $$ For an elliptical search area (if a preferred direction exists with semi-minor axis $b = 3$ NM and semi-major axis $a = 6$ NM): $$ A = \pi \cdot a \cdot b = \pi \cdot 6 \cdot 3 = 56.55 \text{ NM}^2 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A)** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Using degrees directly in $\cos/\sin$ without considering the **compass quadrant** correction. The bearing $158.2°$ is in the SE quadrant; sign errors here are the **#1 cause of mark loss**. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert **knots × hours to NM** before applying latitude/longitude offsets. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Reporting the datum with **excessive decimal places** (e.g., 15°19.97'N) instead of rounding to **0.1 minute precision** consistent with IAMSAR standards. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** **(a)** Describe the **four standard search patterns** used in maritime SAR. For each pattern, state: - (i) The condition under which it is preferred. **(2 marks)** - (ii) The geometry of the search legs. **(1 mark)** **(b)** A maritime patrol aircraft has a **sweep width of 3.0 NM** and is tasked with searching a region using a **track spacing of 1.0 NM**. The mission comprises **4 search passes** over the area, and the **Probability of Containment (POC)** is **0.7**. - (i) Calculate the **single-pass Probability of Detection (POD)**. **(3 marks)** - (ii) Calculate the **cumulative POD** after all 4 passes. **(3 marks)** - (iii) Calculate the **overall Probability of Success (POS)**. **(1 mark)** #### Model Answer **(a) Four Standard Search Patterns** | Pattern | Preferred When | Geometry | |---|---|---| | **Expanding Square Search (SS)** | Datum is well-defined; **one SRU**; small-to-medium area | First leg along the **major axis** of probable error from datum; each subsequent leg is **longer** by the track spacing, with **90° right turns** | | **Sector Search (VS)** | Datum is well-defined; **one SRU**; small area; open sea with no obstacles | Three **120° sectors** radiating from datum; first leg **2 × track spacing**, return to datum, expand outward | | **Parallel Track Search (PS)** | Datum is **uncertain or diffuse**; **multiple SRUs** available | Two or more **parallel legs** of equal length at a fixed **track spacing**; legs perpendicular to the major axis of error | | **Track Line Search (TS)** | SO is a **vessel with known course and speed** that may have drifted along a track | A **rectangle** straddling the intended track, with legs parallel to the track | > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Note:** The choice between Expanding Square and Sector Search when only one SRU is available depends on whether the **search area is square or circular** in shape. Expanding Square is preferred for **square or rectangular** error distributions; Sector Search is preferred for **circular** distributions. --- **(b) POD and POS Calculations** **(i) Single-Pass POD** **Step 1:** Coverage Factor. $$ C = \frac{W}{S} = \frac{3.0}{1.0} = 3.0 $$ **Step 2:** Single-pass POD. $$ POD_{\text{pass}} = 1 - e^{-C} = 1 - e^{-3.0} = 1 - 0.0498 = 0.9502 $$ **Result: Single-pass POD = 0.9502 (95.02%)** **(ii) Cumulative POD After 4 Passes** $$ \begin{aligned} POD_{\text{cum}} &= 1 - (1 - POD_{\text{pass}})^n \\ &= 1 - (1 - 0.9502)^4 \\ &= 1 - (0.0498)^4 \\ &= 1 - 6.16 \times 10^{-6} \\ &\approx 0.999994 \end{aligned} $$ **Result: Cumulative POD ≈ 1.0000 (99.9994%)** **(iii) Probability of Success** $$ POS = POD_{\text{cum}} \times POC = 0.999994 \times 0.7 = 0.699996 \approx 0.700 $$ **Result: POS = 0.700 (70.0%)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B)** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Computing $POD$ as **W × S** (multiplication) instead of **W / S** (division) for Coverage Factor. Multiplication is the **#1 formula error** in SAR papers. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Using the **wrong base** in the cumulative POD formula. The base must be **$(1 - POD_{\text{pass}})$**, not $POD_{\text{pass}}$ itself. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to **multiply by POC** for the POS. Many students stop at cumulative POD and lose the final mark. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to show the **natural exponential** $e^{-C}$ in the POD derivation; examiners award partial marks for the formula but **full marks require the substitution step**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)** - ✅ **SAR Convention 1979** — adopted in Hamburg, entered into force 22 June 1985. - ✅ **IAMSAR Manual** — joint publication of **ICAO and IMO**. - ✅ **Five Phases**: Awareness → Initial Action → Planning → Operations → Conclusion. - ✅ **Key Personnel**: **RCC** (shore), **SMC** (mission), **OSC** (on-scene). - ✅ **Four Patterns**: Expanding Square, Sector, Parallel Track, Track Line. - ✅ **Datum Drift Formula**: $D_{\text{drift}} = V_{\text{drift}} \times \Delta t$ — apply to LKP. -
