✅ **Coverage Factor**: $C = W / S$ — **not** $W \times S$. - ✅ **Single-Pass POD**: $POD = 1 - e^{-C}$ (when sensor is perfect within sweep). - ✅ **Cumulative POD**: $POD_{\text{cum}} = 1 - (1 - POD)^n$. - ✅ **Probability of Success**: $POS = POD_{\text{cum}} \times POC$. - ✅ **Circular Search Area**: $A = \pi \cdot E^2$. - ✅ **Elliptical Search Area**: $A = \pi \cdot a \cdot b$. - ✅ **Units**: Always **Nautical Miles (NM)** for distance, **knots (kt)** for speed, **UTC** for time. - ✅ **Distress Signals**: MAYDAY (grave/eminent danger), PAN-PAN (urgency), SECURITE (safety). - ✅ **GMDSS Devices**: EPIRB (406 MHz), SART (9 GHz radar), INMARSAT-C, VHF DSC. - ✅ **Standard IAMSAR Search**: $C = 0.5$ (50% overlap). - ✅ **Survival Time Rule**: Cold-water immersion survival drops sharply after **3 hours** in temperate waters. - ✅ **Cease Search Criterion**: Marginal POS gain falls below **5%** per pass. - ✅ **Mutual Aid**: Adjacent RCCs must be informed along shared SRR boundaries. - ✅ **Common Sign Error in Bearing Problems**: Always verify the **quadrant** (N/E/S/W) of the resultant drift before computing the datum. > [!NOTE] > **End of Notes** — Students are advised to practice the **Part B Question A (Datum) and Question B (POD/POS)** numerically with at least **three variations each** before the KTU ESE for guaranteed full marks in the **14-mark** category.

topics.title: SAR Principles and Procedures
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SAR Principles and Procedures

---

# Module 3 — SAR Principles and Procedures

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Search and Rescue (SAR)** is an internationally coordinated humanitarian operation designed to locate persons in distress, recover them, and deliver them to a place of safety. Under the **International Convention on Maritime Search and Rescue (SAR Convention), Hamburg 1979** — adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and developed jointly with the **International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO)** — every coastal State is legally obligated to provide SAR services within its designated **Search and Rescue Region (SRR)**, either individually or through bilateral/multilateral arrangements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The SAR Convention (1979) entered into force on **22 June 1985**. It establishes a global framework of Search and Rescue Regions (SRRs) so that, irrespective of where a mariner or aviator is distressed, a Rescue Coordination Centre (RCC) takes charge.

The operational manual guiding SAR globally is the **IAMSAR Manual** (International Aeronautical and Maritime Search and Rescue Manual), published in three volumes by IMO and ICAO.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital's emergency response system, but scaled to the **entire ocean**:

- **911 Dispatcher** → **Rescue Coordination Centre (RCC)**
- **City Ambulance Zones** → **Search and Rescue Regions (SRRs)**
- **First Responders** → **SAR Units (coast guard vessels, aircraft, lifeboats)**
- **Hospital ER** → **Place of Safety**

When a ship reports distress (analogous to a 911 call), the nearest RCC "dispatches" rescue assets from across the world within pre-defined zones, following standardized IAMSAR procedures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English:** SAR = a global "911 system for the sea and sky," built on treaties, regions, and trained coordinators who scramble ships, helicopters, and lifeboats to pluck distressed people from danger.

---

### 1.3 The Three Phases of a SAR Mission

| Phase | Trigger Condition | Outcome if Successful |
|---|---|---|
| **Uncertainty Phase (1)** | Doubt exists about the safety of a vessel/person, OR lack of information | Position confirmed OR alert cancelled |
| **Alert Phase (2)** | Apparent danger exists, but assistance not yet needed | Vessel/person located, alert cancelled |
| **Distress Phase (3)** | Certain danger exists, immediate assistance required | Survivors rescued, delivered to safety |

> [!NOTE]
> Each phase escalates the level of response: from passive monitoring → active search → full rescue dispatch. This is a **graded response** principle, ensuring resources are not wasted.

---

### 1.4 Key Architectural Entities

- **SRR (Search and Rescue Region):** A geographically defined area (often coincident with EEZ or FIR) within which a State accepts SAR responsibility.
- **RCC (Rescue Coordination Centre):** The nerve centre — 24/7 manned facility coordinating SAR operations.
- **RSC (Rescue Sub-Centre):** A subordinate unit of RCC handling localized SAR in busy zones.
- **SAR Units:** Aircraft, vessels, lifeboats, helicopters deployed to perform the actual search.
- **OSC (On-Scene Coordinator):** Designated person who takes command at the rescue site.
- **SMC (Search and Rescue Mission Coordinator):** The RCC officer directing the entire SAR mission.
- **DAT (Datum):** The most probable location of the distressed craft, around which a **Search Area** is calculated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The SAR Process — Structured Logic Flow

The IAMSAR Manual breaks every SAR mission into **six sequential steps** performed by the SMC:

1. **Awareness:** Recognize that a situation requires SAR response.
2. **Initial Action:** Activate appropriate RCC, broadcast distress alert.
3. **Planning:** Compute datum, search area, deploy appropriate pattern.
4. **Operations:** Execute search, locate survivors, effect rescue.
5. **Conclusion:** Suspend or terminate the search; return to base.
6. **Mission Review / Critique:** Post-mission analysis, lessons learned.

---

### 2.2 Search Planning — The "Why" and "How"

The **core problem** of search planning is: *We don't know exactly where the survivors are, but we have probabilities.*

The SMC must:
- Estimate the **Datum** (best guess position).
- Compute **Drift** (cumulative effect of leeway + current + wind) since the incident.
- Establish **Search Area (A)** based on probable error.
- Decide **Search Pattern** based on environment, target type, and assets.
- Compute **Track Spacing (S)** based on sensor sweep width (W).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the canonical IAMSAR equations, routinely tested in KTU ESE for module-level problems.

| Symbol | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Datum displacement | $D = L \cdot t$ | nautical miles (NM) |
| $L$ | Leeway (drift due to wind on target) | $L = K \cdot V_w$ | knots (kt) |
| $t$ | Elapsed time since incident | – | hours (h) |
| $A$ | Search Area | $A = \pi \cdot r^2$ | NM² |
| $r$ | Search radius | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}}$ | NM |
| $n$ | Number of search tracks required | $n = \dfrac{A}{\dfrac{S^2}{2}}$ (parallel sweep) | dimensionless |
| $S$ | Track spacing | $S = k \cdot W$ | NM |
| $W$ | Sweep width | Sensor- & target-dependent | NM |
| $C$ | Coverage | $C = \dfrac{S \cdot L_t}{A}$ | dimensionless |
| $POC$ | Probability of Containment | $POC = 1 - e^{-C}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> Where:
> - $K$ = leeway coefficient (object-dependent, dimensionless — supplied by IAMSAR tables, typically 0.01 to 0.07).
> - $V_w$ = wind speed in knots.
> - $L_t$ = total track length searched.

---

### 2.4 Drift Mechanics — The Real-World Utility

**Total Drift** of a survival craft or person-in-water = **Leeway** + **Current Drift (Set & Drift)** + **Search Object's own propulsion**.

- **Leeway (L):** Wind-driven movement downwind. Calculated using leeway tables in IAMSAR Vol. III.
- **Current Set & Drift:** Oceanographic component. Set = direction, Drift = speed (kt).
- **Vector Sum:** Combined drift = geometric addition of vectors.

$$\vec{D}_{total} = \vec{D}_{leeway} + \vec{D}_{current}$$

This combined drift, multiplied by elapsed time, positions the **Datum**.

---

### 2.5 Standard Search Patterns (IAMSAR)

| Pattern | Best Use |
|---|---|
| **Expanding Square Search** | Datum is accurate; single target; ship or boat |
| **Sector Search (Creeping Line)** | Datum is approximate; small target area |
| **Parallel Track (Creeping Line Co-ordinated)** | Long, rectangular search area; aircraft |
| **Contour Search** | Strong current; area near shoreline |
| **Track Line Search** | Vessel on known track, e.g., flight route |

---

### 2.6 Detection Theory & Probability

Search effectiveness is governed by **two probabilities**:

- **$POC$ (Probability of Containment):** Probability the target lies within the searched area.
- **$POD$ (Probability of Detection):** Probability that, given a sweep over the target, the sensor finds it.

The **Combined Probability of Success (POS)** is:

$$POS = POC \times POD$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love asking the conceptual question: *"Why does doubling coverage NOT double the probability of detection?"* — Answer: Because $POD$ saturates near 1, and $POS$ is multiplicative, not additive.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Datum Displacement via Drift

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Pattern):**

A small fishing vessel capsized at **14:00 UTC** at position **22°30'N, 068°15'E**. A life-raft with 3 persons was seen launching. The SMC must compute the **Datum at 20:00 UTC** (6 hours later), given:
- Leeway coefficient, $K = 0.04$
- Wind speed, $V_w = 25$ knots from **East (090°)**
- Surface current: Set **045°**, Drift **1.5 kt**

---

**Step 1 — Compute Leeway Speed ($L$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= K \cdot V_w \\
L &= 0.04 \cdot 25 \\
L &= 1.0 \text{ kt}
\end{aligned}$$

The raft drifts **1.0 knot downwind** (i.e., towards **West — 270°**).

---

**Step 2 — Compute Current Drift Distance**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{current} &= V_{current} \cdot t \\
D_{current} &= 1.5 \text{ kt} \cdot 6 \text{ h} \\
D_{current} &= 9.0 \text{ NM in direction 045°}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 3 — Resolve Both Drift Vectors into N-S and E-W Components**

**Leeway (1.0 kt × 6 h = 6.0 NM @ 270°):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta NS_{leeway} &= 6.0 \cdot \cos(270°) = 0.0 \text{ NM} \\
\Delta EW_{leeway} &= 6.0 \cdot \sin(270°) = -6.0 \text{ NM} \quad (\text{Westward})
\end{aligned}$$

**Current (9.0 NM @ 045°):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta NS_{current} &= 9.0 \cdot \cos(045°) = 9.0 \cdot 0.7071 = 6.36 \text{ NM (North)} \\
\Delta EW_{current} &= 9.0 \cdot \sin(045°) = 9.0 \cdot 0.7071 = 6.36 \text{ NM (East)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 4 — Sum the Components**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta NS_{total} &= 0.0 + 6.36 = +6.36 \text{ NM (North)} \\
\Delta EW_{total} &= -6.0 + 6.36 = +0.36 \text{ NM (East)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute Total Drift Distance and Bearing**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= \sqrt{(6.36)^2 + (0.36)^2} \\
D_{total} &= \sqrt{40.45 + 0.13} \\
D_{total} &= \sqrt{40.58} \\
D_{total} &\approx 6.37 \text{ NM}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \arctan\left(\frac{\Delta EW}{\Delta NS}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{0.36}{6.36}\right) \\
\theta &= \arctan(0.0566) \\
\theta &\approx 3.24° \text{ East of North (i.e., bearing 003.2°)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 6 — Convert NM to Latitude / Longitude**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Latitude shift} &= \frac{6.36}{60} \approx 0.106° \text{ North} \\
\text{Longitude shift (at 22°30'N)} &= \frac{0.36}{60 \cdot \cos(22.5°)} \approx \frac{0.36}{55.43} \approx 0.0065°
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 7 — Compute Datum Position at 20:00 UTC**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Datum Latitude} &= 22°30'N + 0.106° = 22°36.4'N \\
\text{Datum Longitude} &= 068°15'E + 0.0065° \approx 068°15.4'E
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Datum: 22°36.4'N, 068°15.4'E** — this is the centre around which the search pattern will be expanded.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Pattern

| Scenario | Search Pattern | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Accurate datum, single vessel, daylight | **Expanding Square** | Concentric coverage from datum outward |
| Datum approximate, small area, single aircraft | **Sector Search** | Concentric sectors expand outward |
| Long coastal area, parallel coast | **Parallel Track** | Efficient coverage of elongated areas |
| Datum in strong current, near shoreline | **Contour Search** | Follows isobaths/currents |
| Vessel on known course, must be intercepted | **Track Line Search** | Covers the expected route |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SAR Organizational Hierarchy (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[IMO and ICAO Global Coordination]
    nodeB[National SAR Authority]
    nodeC[Rescue Coordination Centre RCC]
    nodeD[Rescue Sub Centre RSC]
    nodeE[Alerting Posts Coast Stations Ships Aircraft]
    nodeF[SAR Units Aircraft Helicopters Vessels Lifeboats]
    nodeG[On Scene Coordinator OSC]
    nodeH[Survivors at Place of Safety]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeF
```

---

### 4.2 SAR Phase Flow — Escalation Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    phase1[Phase 1 Uncertainty]
    phase2[Phase 2 Alert]
    phase3[Phase 3 Distress]
    outcomeA[Position Confirmed OR Alert Cancelled]
    outcomeB[Vessel Located OR Rescue Initiated]
    outcomeC[Survivors Rescued and Delivered to Safety]

    phase1 -->|Information Sought| outcomeA
    phase1 -->|Danger Suspected| phase2
    phase2 -->|Position Located| outcomeB
    phase2 -->|Immediate Assistance Needed| phase3
    phase3 --> outcomeC
```

---

### 4.3 Search Planning Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    input1[Last Known Position LKP]
    input2[Drift Inputs Leeway Current Wind]
    input3[Elapsed Time t]
    process1[Compute Datum]
    process2[Compute Search Area A]
    process3[Select Search Pattern]
    process4[Compute Track Spacing S]
    process5[Deploy SAR Units]
    output1[Datum Coordinates]
    output2[Search Area Radius r]
    output3[Pattern Plan]
    output4[Coverage and Track Plan]
    output5[Mission Execution]

    input1 --> process1
    input2 --> process1
    input3 --> process1
    process1 --> output1
    output1 --> process2
    process2 --> output2
    output2 --> process3
    process3 --> output3
    output3 --> process4
    process4 --> output4
    output4 --> process5
    input1 --> process5
```

---

### 4.4 Notification & Alerting Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Distressed Vessel]
    srcB[Mayday or DSC Alert]
    srcC[Coast Station or RCC]
    srcD[RCC Activates Plan]
    srcE[Notify Adjacent RCCs]
    srcF[Dispatch SAR Units]
    srcG[OSC Takes Command On Scene]

    srcA --> srcB
    srcB --> srcC
    srcC --> srcD
    srcC --> srcE
    srcD --> srcF
    srcF --> srcG
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define the "Alert Phase" of a Search and Rescue operation. Under what circumstances does it get escalated to the Distress Phase?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Alert Phase** is the second stage of SAR response, declared when *"appparent danger exists requiring assistance, but rescue is not yet immediately necessary"* (per IAMSAR). Typical triggers include: a vessel being overdue, failure to report position, or receiving a Mayday Relay.

**Escalation to Distress Phase** occurs when:
- Definitive information indicates the vessel/person is in grave and imminent danger.
- Immediate assistance is required (e.g., confirmed sinking, persons in water).
- Survival conditions are deteriorating rapidly (cold water, no supplies).

> ⚠️ *[Examiner Note: Students often confuse Uncertainty Phase with Alert Phase — remember Uncertainty is "doubt exists"; Alert is "danger suspected, no SOS yet."]*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
**What is the role of the On-Scene Coordinator (OSC) in a SAR operation? How is the OSC designated?**

**Model Answer:**

The **OSC (On-Scene Coordinator)** is the designated person who assumes command of all SAR units at the actual rescue site, coordinating their on-scene movements, communications, and rescue execution.

**Designation:**
- Normally appointed by the **SMC (Search and Rescue Mission Coordinator)** at the RCC.
- Usually the **commander of the first SAR asset to arrive on scene** or the unit best suited (e.g., a coast guard cutter in preference to a small fishing boat).
- Clearly identified in communications (e.g., *"This is Rescue 42 acting as OSC"*).

---

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice between Question A and Question B is mandatory under KTU 2024 ESE pattern.**

---

### ✍️ Question A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**  
**(a)** Explain the **International SAR Convention (1979)** and outline the **obligations of Coastal States** under it. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **six-step SAR process** as defined by the IAMSAR Manual. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **International Convention on Maritime Search and Rescue, 1979** (signed at **Hamburg**, adopted by IMO, jointly developed with ICAO) entered into force on **22 June 1985**.

**Objectives of the Convention:**
1. Establish an **international framework** for SAR cooperation.
2. Encourage regional cooperation between neighbouring coastal States.
3. Ensure that **persons in distress are rescued without regard to nationality or circumstances.**

**Obligations of Coastal States:**

- **Establish SRRs (Search and Rescue Regions):** Each Party must define and publish its SRRs, ensuring no gap exists globally.
- **Provide RCC(s):** Each State must establish 24/7 manned Rescue Coordination Centres with trained personnel.
- **Coordinate internationally:** A State whose SRR abuts another's must establish **bilateral arrangements** for seamless handover of distress cases near borders.
- **Accept assistance from other States:** Parties must allow vessels, aircraft, or personnel from other States to render assistance, including entry into territorial waters during emergencies.
- **Facilitate communications:** Provide adequate distress communications infrastructure (Coast Stations, DSC, INMARSAT, COSPAS-SARSAT ground stations).
- **Provide medical and post-rescue care:** Survivors rescued must be delivered to a "place of safety," with appropriate medical attention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Place of Safety** — A location where rescue operations are considered to terminate; where the survivors' safety, basic human needs (food, shelter, medical) can be ensured.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The **IAMSAR Manual** specifies a six-step process for every SAR mission:

1. **Awareness:** The initial step where a person, vessel, or system recognizes a distress situation. Triggers include Mayday calls, DSC alerts, EPIRB signals, or overdue vessels.

2. **Initial Action:** The RCC activates, evaluates the alert, designates a SMC, and issues preliminary broadcasts to vessels in the vicinity.

3. **Planning:** The most technically demanding step. The SMC:
   - Computes **Datum** (probable position using drift analysis).
   - Determines **Search Area** based on datum uncertainty.
   - Selects the appropriate **Search Pattern** (Expanding Square / Sector / Parallel Track / Contour).
   - Decides **Search Unit Allocation** and **Track Spacing**.

4. **Operations:** SAR units are dispatched and conduct the search. The OSC coordinates on-scene activities.

5. **Conclusion:** Search is suspended or terminated when:
   - Survivors are recovered, OR
   - No reasonable hope of success remains (e.g., survival time exceeded).

6. **Mission Review/Critique:** A post-mission debrief to identify lessons learned and improve future operations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students list only 4 steps (Awareness, Planning, Operations, Conclusion). The full IAMSAR sequence has **6 steps** — losing marks for missing Initial Action and Mission Review is common.

---

---

### ✍️ Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**(a)** Define **Datum** and **Search Area** in SAR operations. Derive the relationship between search radius and search area for a circular search zone. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A vessel is reported disabled at **10:00 UTC** with a last known position. The SMC at 16:00 UTC needs to compute the **Datum**. Given: Leeway coefficient K = 0.03, wind speed = 20 kt from North, current set 180° at 1.0 kt. Find the new Datum position using vector addition. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Datum:** The datum is the **most probable location** of the distressed craft or survivors, computed by applying the cumulative drift (due to leeway, current, and wind) to the **Last Known Position (LKP)** over the elapsed time.

**Search Area:** The search area is the **geographical region** around the datum within which the SMC believes the target is located, bounded by probable position error. It defines where SAR units will search.

**Derivation:**

For a circular search area of radius $r$, the area is:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi r^2
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for $r$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi r^2 &= A \\
r^2 &= \frac{A}{\pi} \\
r &= \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}
\end{aligned}$$

This gives the search radius from the datum.

**Refinement (non-circular zones):**
For non-circular (rectangular) zones, e.g., parallel-track search of length $l$ and width $w$:

$$A = l \cdot w$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $K = 0.03$, $V_w = 20$ kt from **North (000°)**
- Current set **180° (South)** at 1.0 kt
- Elapsed time $t = 6$ hours (10:00 → 16:00 UTC)

**Step 1 — Compute Leeway Speed and Distance**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= K \cdot V_w = 0.03 \cdot 20 = 0.6 \text{ kt}
\end{aligned}$$

Direction of leeway: **Southward (180°)** — wind from North pushes raft South.

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{leeway} &= 0.6 \cdot 6 = 3.6 \text{ NM South}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Current Drift**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{current} &= 1.0 \text{ kt} \cdot 6 \text{ h} = 6.0 \text{ NM South}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Vector Addition**

Both vectors point **South**, so they sum directly:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= 3.6 + 6.0 = 9.6 \text{ NM South}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Degrees Latitude**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta \text{Latitude} &= \frac{9.6}{60} = 0.16° \text{ South}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Datum Position**

If LKP was $\phi$°N, then:

$$\text{Datum} = (\phi - 0.16)°\text{N, same longitude}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to assume leeway direction = wind direction. **Leeway is DOWNWIND**, so wind from North pushes the object **South**, not North. Always visualize: leeway = drift in the direction wind is blowing TO.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SAR Convention (1979)** — Hamburg — entered into force **22 June 1985**.
- ✅ **Three Phases:** Uncertainty → Alert → Distress (escalate as danger becomes more certain).
- ✅ **Six-step IAMSAR process:** Awareness → Initial Action → Planning → Operations → Conclusion → Mission Review.
- ✅ **Key Entities:** SRR, RCC, RSC, SMC, OSC, SAR Units.
- ✅ **Datum** = LKP + Drift Vector Sum.
- ✅ **Leeway** $L = K \cdot V_w$, with $K$ from IAMSAR tables.
- ✅ **Search Area** $A = \pi r^2$ for circular; $A = l \cdot w$ for rectangular.
- ✅ **$POC \times POD = POS$** — multiplicative, NOT additive.
- ✅ **Place of Safety** = end-state of a successful SAR mission.
- ✅ **Patterns:** Expanding Square (accurate datum), Sector (datum approximate), Parallel Track (long zones), Contour (strong current), Track Line (intercept).
- ✅ **Leeway direction = DOWNWIND**, not upwind — most commonly missed in KTU answers.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** Always state the **Convention**, the **Phase definitions**, and **explicitly compute Datum first** in any numerical problem. Examiners reward structured answers that mirror the IAMSAR logic flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maritime Search Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maritime Search Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maritime Search Patterns (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A **Maritime Search Pattern** is a standardized, geometrically defined flight or vessel trajectory executed by Search and Rescue (SAR) units to systematically cover a designated search area for a target (survivors, wreckage, liferafts, or distressed vessels). These patterns are governed by the **IAMSAR Manual (International Aeronautical and Maritime Search and Rescue Manual)** published jointly by the **ICAO** and the **IMO**, and form the operational backbone of any coordinated maritime search mission.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OESFT727, students must understand that a search pattern is not merely a random sweep — it is a mathematically optimized trajectory designed to maximize the **Probability of Success (POS)** given finite resources, time, weather, and target uncertainty.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Lost Key on the Beach" Problem**
> Imagine you drop your house key on a long sandy beach at night. You remember dropping it "somewhere around here," but not exactly where. How do you search?
>
> - **If you walk straight lines back and forth (parallel tracks)**: You cover the area methodically — this is a **Parallel Track / Creeping Line Search**.
> - **If you walk outward in a spiral from where you last saw your hand**: You start tight and expand — this is an **Expanding Square Search**.
> - **If you walk three pie-slice shaped paths from one point**: This is a **Sector Search**, used when you have high confidence in a single datum point.
>
> Maritime SAR planners face the exact same problem, but with variables like currents, wind drift, vessel drift, and millions of square nautical miles.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standardized under the **IAMSAR Manual (Vol. II)** and are emphasized in KTU examination questions:

- **1 Nautical Mile (NM)** = **1852 meters** (the standard distance unit in maritime search)
- **Search Altitude (helicopter/fixed-wing)** = typically **300–1500 feet** (≈ 91–457 m)
- **Search Vessel Speed** = typically **10–20 knots** for surface units
- **Crews' visual horizon** for a searcher at height $h$ (in feet) ≈ **$1.17\sqrt{h}$ NM**
- **Standard Search Endurance** = typically 4–6 hours per sortie
- **Coverage Factor (C)** = **dimensionless ratio** of Sweep Width to Track Spacing
- **POD (Probability of Detection)** is typically assumed between **0.4 and 0.9** depending on conditions.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often test the **terminology**: sweep width, track spacing, POD, POS, datum, and search area — make sure you can define each in a single sentence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Search Planning

The modern maritime search theory rests on **five pillars**, each of which is testable in KTU examinations:

**1. The Datum (Most Probable Position)**
- The datum is the search planner's **best estimate of where the target currently is**, computed from the last known position (LKP), vessel drift (leeway), and current/wind vectors.
- Without a datum, no search pattern has meaning — it is the **origin point** of the search.

**2. Search Area (A)**
- The total geographic region over which the search effort is distributed. Calculated from the uncertainty in the datum radius ($r$) as $A = \pi r^{2}$, or from a rectangular grid.

**3. Sweep Width (W)**
- The lateral distance on either side of the search unit's track within which a target has a **high probability of being detected**.
- **Units: Nautical Miles (NM)**. Depends on target size, visibility, search altitude, and sensor type.

**4. Track Spacing (S)**
- The perpendicular distance between two adjacent parallel search legs.
- **Units: Nautical Miles (NM)**.

**5. Coverage Factor (C)**

$$C = \frac{W}{S}$$

This is the **central dimensionless ratio** of maritime search theory:

- If $C > 1$ → tracks overlap (over-search, common when target is small/hard to detect).
- If $C < 1$ → gaps between tracks (under-search, common for large obvious targets).
- **Optimal** value of $C$ for visual search is typically **0.5 to 1.0**.

### 2.2 The Six Standard IAMSAR Maritime Search Patterns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus High-Yield Table — Master These Six Patterns**

| # | Pattern Name | Geometry | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Parallel Track / Creeping Line** | Parallel legs, uniform spacing | Datum reasonably accurate; large area to cover |
| 2 | **Expanding Square (Square Search)** | Outward-growing square spirals | Datum accuracy good; target likely near center |
| 3 | **Sector Search** | Three pie-sectors from datum | High datum confidence; short endurance |
| 4 | **Track Line Search** | Two parallel legs offset from LKP route | Vessel missing along a known track |
| 5 | **Contour Search** | Along depth contours or shoreline | Near coast/islands; drifted toward shore |
| 6 | **Combined Pattern** | Hybrid of two patterns | Long endurance + shifting datum |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU OESFT727 ESE and internal assessments. Memorize units carefully.**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Search Area (Circle) | $A = \pi \cdot r^{2}$ | $\text{NM}^{2}$ |
| Search Area (Rectangle) | $A = L \times W$ | $\text{NM}^{2}$ |
| Coverage Factor | $C = W \div S$ | dimensionless |
| Area Effectively Swept | $A_{swept} = W \times V \times T$ | $\text{NM}^{2}$ |
| Probability of Success (POS) | $POS = 1 - e^{-C \cdot POD \cdot (A_{eff}/A)}$ | dimensionless |
| Uncovered Area | $A_{u} = A - A_{eff}$ | $\text{NM}^{2}$ |
| Visual Horizon | $D = 1.17\sqrt{h}$ | NM (h in feet) |
| Drift Distance | $D_{drift} = V_{drift} \times t$ | NM |
| Number of Legs | $N_{legs} = \text{roundup}(r \div S) \times 2$ | count |
| Total Track Length | $L_{total} = N_{legs} \times \text{LegLength}$ | NM |

> [!WARNING]
> In the formula table above, the symbol `÷` is used for division. Do NOT use the vertical pipe `\|` character in markdown tables, as it breaks table rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX when needed (e.g., $C = W \mid S$ would be incorrect inside a table; use $C = W/S$ or $C = W \div S$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Maritime search pattern theory is foundational to:

- **Coast Guard operations** (Indian Coast Guard, USCG, etc.)
- **Offshore oil & gas platform emergency response**
- **Drone/UAV maritime surveillance** (modern SAR UAVs use modified parallel track patterns)
- **AIS-based autonomous search algorithms** using Reinforcement Learning
- **Submarine rescue** (specialized contour search patterns)
- **Marine pollution response** (tracking oil spills with sector/parallel track sweeps)

> [!TIP]
> Modern autonomous surface vessels (USVs) are programmed to execute **adaptive parallel track patterns** where track spacing $S$ is dynamically adjusted based on real-time detection probabilities from onboard sensors (RADAR, EO/IR cameras, AIS receivers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Search Radius for a Circular Datum Area

The search area is often modeled as a circle centered on the **datum point** with radius $r$. Given the total uncertainty radius $r$ (in NM), derive the search area.

$$
A = \pi \cdot r^{2}
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1:** A circular search region is chosen when the search planner has equal uncertainty in all directions around the datum.
- **Step 2:** The geometric area of a circle with radius $r$ is defined by the integral of circumference from $0$ to $r$, which yields $\pi r^{2}$.
- **Step 3:** Substitute the radius in nautical miles (NM) to obtain the area in square nautical miles ($\text{NM}^{2}$).

**Numerical Example:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 1 (3-mark KTU type):**
> A datum has an uncertainty radius of 15 NM. Compute the search area.

Given: $r = 15$ NM.

$$
A = \pi \cdot (15)^{2}
$$

$$
A = \pi \cdot 225
$$

$$
A \approx 706.86 \; \text{NM}^{2}
$$

Therefore, the search area is approximately **706.86 NM²**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Search Legs in a Parallel Track Pattern

For a **parallel track search** over a rectangular search area of length $L$ (along track direction) and width $W_{area}$ (across track direction), with track spacing $S$:

$$
N_{legs} = \left\lceil \frac{W_{area}}{S} \right\rceil + 1
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling function** (round up to the next integer).

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1:** The first search leg runs along one edge of the search area.
- **Step 2:** Each subsequent leg is offset by track spacing $S$ perpendicular to the previous track.
- **Step 3:** The number of full intervals of width $S$ that fit across $W_{area}$ is $W_{area}/S$.
- **Step 4:** Add 1 because the count of lines is one more than the count of spaces.

**Numerical Example:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 2 (7-mark KTU type):**
> A search area is 40 NM wide. Track spacing is 8 NM. Compute the number of parallel legs.

Given: $W_{area} = 40$ NM, $S = 8$ NM.

$$
N_{legs} = \left\lceil \frac{40}{8} \right\rceil + 1 = \lceil 5 \rceil + 1
$$

$$
N_{legs} = 5 + 1 = 6 \; \text{legs}
$$

Therefore, the search requires **6 parallel legs**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Coverage Factor & Probability of Success (POS)

The Probability of Success is the cornerstone of modern search theory. Derive it step by step.

**Assumption:** Targets are randomly and uniformly distributed within the search area.

**Step 1:** Define the **effective search area** swept by one pass.

$$
A_{eff} = W \cdot V \cdot T
$$

where $V$ is search speed (NM/hr) and $T$ is search endurance (hr). For multi-leg searches, total $T$ replaces the single-pass time.

**Step 2:** Define the **fraction of area covered**.

$$
f = \frac{A_{eff}}{A}
$$

**Step 3:** The probability of **missing** a target in a single sweep, assuming uniform distribution and a detection probability $POD$, is:

$$
P_{miss} = 1 - POD \cdot \frac{A_{eff}}{A} = 1 - POD \cdot C \cdot \frac{N_{legs} \cdot L_{leg}}{A/S}
$$

For the simplified exponential detection model (most commonly used in KTU problems):

$$
POS = 1 - e^{-C \cdot POD \cdot f}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $f = A_{eff}/A$ and $C = W/S$:

$$
POS = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{W}{S} \cdot POD \cdot \frac{A_{eff}}{A}\right)
$$

**Numerical Example:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 3 (14-mark KTU type):**
> Given: $W = 2$ NM, $S = 4$ NM, $A_{eff} = 200$ NM², $A = 800$ NM², $POD = 0.7$. Compute POS.

Given: $W = 2$, $S = 4$, $A_{eff} = 200$, $A = 800$, $POD = 0.7$.

$$
C = \frac{W}{S} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5
$$

$$
\frac{A_{eff}}{A} = \frac{200}{800} = 0.25
$$

$$
C \cdot POD \cdot \frac{A_{eff}}{A} = 0.5 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.25 = 0.0875
$$

$$
POS = 1 - e^{-0.0875}
$$

$$
POS = 1 - 0.9162 \approx 0.0838
$$

$$
POS \approx 8.38\%
$$

Therefore, the probability of success is approximately **8.4 %**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Expanding Square Pattern — Leg Length Calculation

For an **Expanding Square Search** with initial leg length $L_{1}$ and increment $\Delta L$:

- Leg 1: $L_{1}$
- Leg 2: $L_{1} + \Delta L$
- Leg 3: $L_{1} + 2\Delta L$
- Leg $n$: $L_{n} = L_{1} + (n-1) \cdot \Delta L$

**Total track length** after $n$ legs:

$$
L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{i} = n \cdot L_{1} + \Delta L \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1:** Identify the arithmetic progression of leg lengths.
- **Step 2:** Apply the sum formula for an arithmetic series: $S = n/2 \cdot (2a + (n-1)d)$.
- **Step 3:** Substitute $a = L_{1}$ and $d = \Delta L$:

$$
L_{total} = \frac{n}{2} \cdot \bigl[2 L_{1} + (n-1) \cdot \Delta L\bigr]
$$

**Numerical Example:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 4:**
> An expanding square search starts with $L_{1} = 2$ NM and increment $\Delta L = 1$ NM. After 5 legs, find total track length.

Given: $L_{1} = 2$, $\Delta L = 1$, $n = 5$.

$$
L_{total} = \frac{5}{2} \cdot \bigl[2(2) + (5-1)(1)\bigr]
$$

$$
L_{total} = 2.5 \cdot \bigl[4 + 4\bigr] = 2.5 \cdot 8
$$

$$
L_{total} = 20 \; \text{NM}
$$

Therefore, the SAR unit must fly/sail a total track length of **20 NM**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Pattern to Use?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Decision Matrix for Choosing a Search Pattern**

| Datum Accuracy | Area Size | Endurance | Pattern Choice | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| High (≤ 5 NM) | Small | Short | **Sector Search** | Maximizes coverage near datum |
| High (≤ 5 NM) | Small | Long | **Expanding Square** | Outward spiral from datum |
| Medium (5–20 NM) | Medium | Medium | **Parallel Track** | Uniform coverage of strip |
| Low (> 20 NM) | Large | Long | **Parallel Track (multi-sector)** | Distributes effort over wider area |
| Vessel on route | Linear corridor | Any | **Track Line Search** | Two legs parallel to intended track |
| Near coastline | Variable | Variable | **Contour Search** | Follows shoreline/depth contour |

---

### 3.6 Derivation 5: Search Altitude vs. Sweep Width Relation

For visual search, the sweep width depends on the search altitude $h$ (ft), the target size, and visibility. A simplified empirical relation (Koopman-style):

$$
W = k \cdot \sqrt{h}
$$

where $k$ is an empirical coefficient depending on target type, visibility, and observer. **Units: W in NM, h in feet.**

**Numerical Example:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 5:**
> A helicopter searches at $h = 900$ ft, $k = 0.05$. Compute the sweep width.

$$
W = 0.05 \cdot \sqrt{900}
$$

$$
W = 0.05 \cdot 30 = 1.5 \; \text{NM}
$$

Therefore, the sweep width is **1.5 NM**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Maritime Search Planning Process

The following block diagram illustrates the high-level sequence of **search planning** as per the IAMSAR Manual workflow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Reported] --> B[Initial Assessment]
    B --> C[Compute Datum with Drift]
    C --> D[Estimate Uncertainty Radius]
    D --> E[Compute Search Area]
    E --> F[Select Search Pattern Type]
    F --> G[Compute Track Spacing and Legs]
    G --> H[Dispatch SAR Units]
    H --> I[Execute Pattern]
    I --> J{Monitor Detection}
    J -->|No Contact| K[Re-evaluate and Re-task]
    J -->|Contact Made| L[Switch to Recovery Operation]
    K --> C
    L --> M[Mission Complete]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Parallel Track Search Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start Point] --> Q1[Leg 1 North to South]
    Q1 --> R1[Turn Offset S]
    R1 --> S1[Leg 2 South to North]
    S1 --> T1[Turn Offset S]
    T1 --> U1[Leg 3 North to South]
    U1 --> V1[Turn Offset S]
    V1 --> W1[Leg 4 South to North]
    W1 --> X1[End Point]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows represent sequential legs. Each turn is a **track offset equal to the spacing S**, allowing the SAR unit to "comb" the rectangular search area in straight parallel passes. The **first leg** is usually oriented **into the wind** to minimize drift error.

### 4.3 Schematic: Expanding Square Search Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start at Datum Point] --> B1[Leg 1 along heading 000 degrees]
    B1 --> C1[Turn 90 degrees right]
    C1 --> D1[Leg 2 longer by increment dL]
    D1 --> E1[Turn 90 degrees right]
    E1 --> F1[Leg 3 longer by dL]
    F1 --> G1[Turn 90 degrees right]
    G1 --> H1[Leg 4 longer by dL]
    H1 --> I1[Return near datum]
    I1 --> J1{Continue expanding?}
    J1 -->|Yes| B1
    J1 -->|No| K1[Pattern Complete]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Sector Search Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Start at Datum] --> B2[Leg 1 along bearing 000 deg]
    B2 --> C2[Return to Datum]
    C2 --> D2[Leg 2 along bearing 120 deg]
    D2 --> E2[Return to Datum]
    E2 --> F2[Leg 3 along bearing 240 deg]
    F2 --> G2[Return to Datum]
    G2 --> H2[Sector Search Complete]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Adaptive Search System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src[SAR Coordinator] --> Plan[Search Planning Module]
    Plan --> Pat[Pattern Generator]
    Pat --> Nav[Navigation Output]
    Nav --> Veh[SAR Vehicle UAV or Vessel]
    Veh --> Sens[Sensor Payload]
    Sens --> Det[Detector Module]
    Det --> Pod[POD Estimator]
    Pod --> FB[Feedback Loop to Pattern Generator]
    FB --> Pat
```

> [!TIP]
> This block diagram represents the **modern adaptive search loop** — the POD estimator continuously updates the pattern generator's track spacing in real time. If detection probability drops below a threshold, the system **reduces track spacing automatically**, increasing the coverage factor $C$.

### 4.6 Comparison Diagram: Pattern Coverage Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PatDecision{Choose Pattern Based on Conditions}
    PatDecision -->|Datum Accurate| SecPat[Sector Search]
    PatDecision -->|Datum Moderate| ExpPat[Expanding Square]
    PatDecision -->|Datum Uncertain| ParPat[Parallel Track]
    PatDecision -->|Vessel on Route| TrkPat[Track Line Search]
    PatDecision -->|Coastal| ConPat[Contour Search]
    SecPat --> Res1[High POS near center]
    ExpPat --> Res2[Balanced outward growth]
    ParPat --> Res3[Uniform area coverage]
    TrkPat --> Res4[Coverage along route corridor]
    ConPat --> Res5[Shoreline-aligned coverage]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define "Sweep Width" and "Track Spacing" in maritime search theory. How is the Coverage Factor derived from them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Sweep Width (W):** The lateral distance on either side of a search unit's track within which the target has a high probability of being detected. **Unit: Nautical Miles.**
- **Track Spacing (S):** The perpendicular distance between two adjacent parallel search legs. **Unit: Nautical Miles.**
- **Coverage Factor (C):** Defined as the dimensionless ratio:

$$
C = \frac{W}{S}
$$

When $C > 1$, search tracks overlap; when $C < 1$, gaps exist between tracks.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. What is a "Datum" in maritime search planning? List any four factors that affect datum accuracy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Datum** is the most probable position of the search object at the time search planning begins, calculated from the Last Known Position (LKP) and drift vectors.

**Four factors affecting datum accuracy:**

1. Accuracy of the **Last Known Position** (LKP).
2. **Leeway** of the craft/survival craft due to wind.
3. **Surface current** speed and direction.
4. **Elapsed time** since LKP and any navigational errors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Parallel Track Search Pattern** in detail. With a neat schematic, describe its execution and the conditions when it is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Parallel Track Search Pattern** (also called the Creeping Line Pattern) consists of a series of parallel, equally-spaced search legs traversed back and forth across the search area.

**Step-by-step execution:**

1. The search planner computes the search area rectangle: length $L$ (along the direction of legs) and width $W_{area}$ (perpendicular).
2. Track spacing $S$ is chosen based on sweep width $W$ and target coverage factor $C$.
3. The first leg is flown/sailed along one edge of the search area, **oriented into the wind** to minimize lateral drift error.
4. At the end of Leg 1, the unit turns and offsets by track spacing $S$, then executes Leg 2 in the opposite direction.
5. The process continues until the entire search area is covered.

**Number of legs required:**

$$
N_{legs} = \left\lceil \frac{W_{area}}{S} \right\rceil + 1
$$

**Conditions when preferred:**

- Datum accuracy is **moderate to low** (large search area).
- Multiple SAR units are available for **parallel sector subdivision**.
- The target may be anywhere within a broad rectangular zone.

**(b)** A maritime search area is **60 NM long and 40 NM wide**. Track spacing is **5 NM**. The search unit has a sweep width of **3 NM** and a detection probability of **0.8**. Compute the number of legs, the coverage factor, and the probability of success (assume $A_{eff}/A = 0.3$). **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Number of Legs**

$$
N_{legs} = \left\lceil \frac{40}{5} \right\rceil + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9 \; \text{legs}
$$

**Step 2: Coverage Factor**

$$
C = \frac{W}{S} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6
$$

**Step 3: Probability of Success**

$$
POS = 1 - e^{-C \cdot POD \cdot (A_{eff}/A)}
$$

$$
POS = 1 - e^{-0.6 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.3}
$$

$$
POS = 1 - e^{-0.144}
$$

$$
POS = 1 - 0.8659 \approx 0.134
$$

$$
POS \approx 13.4\%
$$

**Final Answer:** 9 legs, $C = 0.6$, and POS ≈ **13.4 %**.

---

#### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the **Expanding Square Search Pattern** and **Sector Search Pattern** with diagrams. Compare their applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Expanding Square Search:**

- The SAR unit starts at the datum and moves outward in a square spiral.
- Initial leg length $L_1$ is small (e.g., 1–2 NM); each subsequent leg is increased by a fixed increment $\Delta L$.
- Total track length after $n$ legs:

$$
L_{total} = \frac{n}{2}\bigl[2L_1 + (n-1)\Delta L\bigr]
$$

- **Used when:** datum accuracy is good and the target is likely near the datum.

**Sector Search:**

- Three legs, each 120° apart, emanate from the datum point.
- Total angular coverage = 360°, divided into three equal pie-slices.
- **Used when:** high confidence in datum, short endurance, and target is immobile.

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Expanding Square | Sector |
|---|---|---|
| Datum confidence | Moderate–High | Very High |
| Endurance required | Medium | Short |
| Area coverage | Outward spiral | Three pie slices |
| Best for | Moving targets drifting outward | Stationary targets near datum |

**(b)** A datum point has an uncertainty radius of **10 NM**. An expanding square search is initiated with $L_1 = 2$ NM and $\Delta L = 2$ NM. Compute the total track length after **6 legs**. Also, find the search area if it is circular. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Search Area (Circular)**

$$
A = \pi \cdot r^{2} = \pi \cdot (10)^{2} = 100\pi
$$

$$
A \approx 314.16 \; \text{NM}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Total Track Length after 6 Legs**

Given: $L_1 = 2$ NM, $\Delta L = 2$ NM, $n = 6$.

$$
L_{total} = \frac{n}{2}\bigl[2L_1 + (n-1)\Delta L\bigr]
$$

$$
L_{total} = \frac{6}{2}\bigl[2(2) + (5)(2)\bigr]
$$

$$
L_{total} = 3 \cdot \bigl[4 + 10\bigr] = 3 \cdot 14 = 42 \; \text{NM}
$$

**Final Answer:** Total track length = **42 NM**, Search area ≈ **314.16 NM²**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in OESFT727 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing POD and POS** — POD is the probability of detecting a target on a single sweep; POS is the cumulative probability across the entire search. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in $N_{legs}$** — Many students compute $\lceil W_{area}/S \rceil$ only, forgetting the initial leg. Always add **1**.
> 3. **Unit confusion** — Sweep Width and Track Spacing are in **NM**, search area is in **NM²**, while search altitude $h$ is in **feet** when using the empirical horizon formula.
> 4. **Not using ceiling function correctly** — Round UP, not down, for number of legs.
> 5. **Direction of first leg** — Examiners often award 1 mark for stating "first leg oriented into the wind" — easy free mark, frequently missed.
> 6. **No diagram in Part B** — A neatly labeled parallel-track diagram earns **at least 1.5 marks** even if the calculations are slightly off.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Maritime Search Patterns**

- **Datum** = Most Probable Position (MPP) — origin of all search patterns.
- **Search Area (Circle)** = $\pi r^{2}$; **(Rectangle)** = $L \times W$.
- **Sweep Width (W)** — lateral detection distance on each side of track.
- **Track Spacing (S)** — perpendicular distance between parallel legs.
- **Coverage Factor** = $W / S$ (dimensionless; ideal range 0.5–1.0).
- **POS** = $1 - e^{-C \cdot POD \cdot (A_{eff}/A)}$ — exponential detection model.
- **Number of Legs** = $\lceil W_{area}/S \rceil + 1$ — always round up.
- **Parallel Track** — for moderate-to-low datum accuracy and large rectangular areas.
- **Expanding Square** — for good datum and moderate endurance; arithmetic progression of leg lengths.
- **Sector Search** — for very high datum confidence and short endurance; three legs at 120° intervals.
- **Track Line Search** — used when vessel missing along a known intended route.
- **Contour Search** — used near coastlines and along depth contours.
- **Standard distance unit**: 1 Nautical Mile = 1852 meters.
- **Visual horizon**: $D = 1.17 \sqrt{h}$ (h in feet, D in NM).
- **First leg of any pattern** should be **oriented into the wind** to reduce drift error.
- **IAMSAR Manual** is the authoritative reference for all maritime search patterns globally.
- **KTU loves POS calculation problems** — practice at least three numerical POS problems before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SAR Equipment and Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** SAR Equipment and Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of SAR Equipment and Technology

**Search and Rescue (SAR) Equipment and Technology** refers to the integrated suite of electronic, mechanical, pyrotechnic, and communication devices mandated under the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** framework—specifically the **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)**—designed to detect, locate, communicate with, and recover vessels and persons in distress at sea.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (GMDSS Anchor):**
> "SAR equipment and technology encompasses all distress alerting, locating, and coordinating apparatus carried on board SOLAS-compliant vessels, operating through terrestrial and satellite communication infrastructures, including COSPAS-SARSAT, INMARSAT, and VHF/MF/HF radio systems."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a vast, dark ocean where a ship has capsized. The survivors are floating in a tiny life raft, hundreds of nautical miles from shore. How will rescuers ever find them?

**Think of SAR equipment as a "digital mayday flashlight."** Just as a lighthouse beams its light across the sea to warn ships of danger, SAR equipment "beams" distress signals—through radio waves and satellites—into the sky, essentially saying, *"I am here, come quickly."* The system is layered like a safety net:

- **Layer 1 (Local):** Handheld VHF radios talk to nearby ships within ~**20–30 nautical miles**.
- **Layer 2 (Regional):** EPIRBs and SARTs reach satellites overhead within seconds, and the satellites "ping" the location to rescue coordination centers (RCCs) worldwide.
- **Layer 3 (Global):** Long-range systems like INMARSAT and HF DSC connect a small fishing boat in the Indian Ocean to a rescue team in another continent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle — The Distress Communication Chain:**
> `Distress Signal → Satellite/Ground Station → Mission Control Center (MCC) → Rescue Coordination Center (RCC) → SAR Units → Survivor Recovery`

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| COSPAS-SARSAT Frequency (EPIRB) | **406 MHz** | International satellite distress frequency |
| SART Response Frequency | **9 GHz (X-band, 9200–9500 MHz)** | Detected by marine radar |
| AIS-SART Frequencies | **161.975 MHz & 162.025 MHz** | AIS channel 1 & 2 |
| VHF Channel 16 | **156.800 MHz** | International distress, safety, and calling |
| DSC Channel 70 | **156.525 MHz** | Digital Selective Calling (VHF) |
| EPIRB Battery Life | **Minimum 48 hours** | Continuous operation at -20°C |
| SART Battery Life | **96 hours in standby, 8 hours active** | |
| GMDSS Sea Areas | A1, A2, A3, A4 | Coverage zones for equipment carriage |

> [!NOTE]
> **GMDSS Sea Areas (Critical for KTU):**
> - **A1:** Within range of VHF coast station with DSC (~20–30 nm)
> - **A2:** Beyond A1, within MF coast station DSC range (~100 nm)
> - **A3:** Beyond A2, covered by INMARSAT geostationary satellites (76°N–76°S)
> - **A4:** Polar regions, outside INMARSAT coverage (requires HF DSC + COSPAS-SARSAT)

## 1.4 Classification of SAR Equipment

SAR equipment is broadly classified into **four functional categories**:

1. **Distress Alerting Equipment** — Initiates the SOS (EPIRB, DSC, INMARSAT)
2. **Locating Equipment** — Pinpoints survivor position (SART, AIS-SART, RDF)
3. **Communication Equipment** — Two-way voice/data (VHF, HF, Satellite phones)
4. **On-scene Support Equipment** — Survival aids (life rafts, immersion suits, flares, PLBs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GMDSS Framework — Theoretical Foundation

The **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)**, adopted by IMO under the **SOLAS Convention Chapter IV**, came into full force on **1 February 1999**. It revolutionized maritime safety by shifting from the traditional "ship-to-ship" Morse-based distress system to a **ship-to-shore** satellite-based architecture.

### 2.1.1 The Three Functional Groups of GMDSS

The IMO Resolution A.801(19) defines three functional groups:

| Functional Group | Purpose | Equipment Examples |
|------------------|---------|--------------------|
| **FG-1: Distress Alerting** | Send initial SOS | EPIRB, DSC, INMARSAT SES, HF/MF/VHF |
| **FG-2: Search and Rescue Coordination** | Locate & coordinate | SART, AIS-SART, RDF, On-scene communications |
| **FG-3: Maritime Safety Information (MSI)** | Navigational/meteorological warnings | NAVTEX, SafetyNET, EGC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why GMDSS Matters:** Before GMDSS, a ship's survival depended on nearby vessels hearing the Mayday call. With GMDSS, a single button press on an EPIRB alerts satellites that cover the **entire planet within 90 seconds**.

## 2.2 Deep Dive: COSPAS-SARSAT System

**COSPAS** (Cosmicheskaya Sistyema Poiska Avariynych Sudov — Russian for "Space System for Search of Distress Vessels") and **SARSAT** (Search and Rescue Satellite-Aided Tracking) form a joint international humanitarian satellite system.

### 2.2.1 Operational Architecture

The system operates in three segments:

1. **Beacons (406 MHz)** — EPIRBs, PLBs, ELTs
2. **Space Segment** — LEO satellites (LEOSAR at ~850 km) + GEO satellites (GEOSAR at ~36,000 km) + MEO satellites (MEOSAR at ~19,000–24,000 km)
3. **Ground Segment** — LUTs (Local User Terminals) + MCCs (Mission Control Centers) + RCCs (Rescue Coordination Centers)

### 2.2.2 Why 406 MHz? The Engineering Rationale

- **Penetrates rain, fog, and vegetation** better than lower frequencies
- **Allocated internationally** for distress only (no commercial use)
- **Allows Doppler shift measurement** to calculate beacon position
- **Supports digital encoding** of vessel ID, GPS coordinates

## 2.3 Equipment-by-Equipment Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon)

- **Activation:** Manual switch OR automatic via hydrostatic release at **1.5–4 m water depth**
- **Transmission:** 406 MHz burst every **50 seconds** for ~520 ms duration
- **Homing Signal:** 121.5 MHz continuous tone for final-stage RDF by aircraft/ships
- **GNSS-EPIRB:** Embeds GPS/GLONASS receiver for position accuracy of **<50 meters**
- **Strobe Light:** High-intensity LED, flash rate ~20/min, visible up to 5 nm

**Theoretical Operation Logic:**
$$\text{EPIRB Activation} \rightarrow \text{406 MHz Burst} \rightarrow \text{Satellite Detection} \rightarrow \text{Doppler Position Fix} \rightarrow \text{MCC Routing} \rightarrow \text{RCC Alert} \rightarrow \text{SAR Dispatch}$$

### 2.3.2 SART (Search and Rescue Transponder)

- **Operating Principle:** Receives **9 GHz X-band** pulses from any nearby marine radar (range ~5 nm radar detection, ~30 nm aircraft radar)
- **Response:** Replies with **12 swept frequency blips** extending across the radar's bandwidth
- **Visual/Audio Cue:** The blips appear as **a line of dots on the survivor's radar**, pointing from the search vessel directly TO the SART
- **Distance Indication:** Closer approach = wider blip spread; first dot = 8 nm, last dot = <1 nm

### 2.3.3 AIS-SART (Automatic Identification System SART)

- **Advantage over SART:** Works on **any vessel with an AIS receiver**, not just radar
- **GPS Position:** Integrated GNSS sends precise position every minute
- **Message:** Standard AIS Message 1 (Position Report) with SART-specific MMSI prefix **970xxxyy**
- **Display:** Shows as a circle on the radar/ECDIS with text "SART ACTIVE"

### 2.3.4 VHF DSC (Digital Selective Calling)

- **Channel:** 70 (156.525 MHz) for digital signalling
- **Categories of Calls:**
  - **Distress (DISTRESS button)** — Priority highest
  - **Urgency** — "PAN PAN"
  - **Safety** — "SECURITE"
- **Alert Format:** MMSI (9-digit) + Nature of Distress (e.g., Fire, Flooding, MOB) + Position + Time + Communication mode

### 2.3.5 INMARSAT-C (Satellite Communication)

- **Coverage:** Global, except polar regions (A3 area)
- **Function:** Two-way store-and-forward text/email + distress alerting
- **Distress Button:** Single-key activation sends "Distress Alert" with ship's ID and position to a land Earth Station (LES)

### 2.3.6 NAVTEX (Navigational Telex)

- **Frequency:** 518 kHz (international), 490 kHz (national)
- **Range:** ~400 nm
- **Content:** Weather, navigational warnings, SAR information
- **SART-related messages:** Category "D" (Search and Rescue)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| $f_d = \frac{2 v f_0}{c}$ | Doppler shift for satellite-beacon geometry (LEOSAR) | Hz |
| $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Free-space path loss (Friis transmission) | Watts |
| $\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength (c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s) | meters |
| $R_{radar} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 kT_s B_n \cdot SNR_{min}}}$ | Radar detection range equation | meters |
| $T_{positioning} \approx 90\text{ s}$ | Average COSPAS-SARSAT alert latency | seconds |
| $R_{VHF} \approx 2.5 \cdot (\sqrt{H_t} + \sqrt{H_r})$ | VHF horizon range formula (H in meters, R in nm) | nautical miles |
| $\text{MMSI Format}$ | MID XXX XXXX (MID = Maritime Identification Digits) | 9 digits |
| $\text{GMDSS A1 Range}$ | ~20–30 nm from VHF-DSC coast station | nautical miles |
| $\text{EPIRB Accuracy (no GNSS)}$ | ±5 km (Doppler method) | kilometers |
| $\text{EPIRB Accuracy (with GNSS)}$ | ±0.05 km (50 m) | kilometers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion Reminder for KTU:**
> - **1 nautical mile (nm) = 1.852 km = 1852 m**
> - **1 MHz = 10⁶ Hz**, **1 GHz = 10⁹ Hz**
> - **Speed of light c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Commercial Shipping** | Mandatory carriage under SOLAS Chapter IV |
| **Offshore Oil & Gas** | Crew transfer vessels require PLBs and VHF-DSC |
| **Recreational Boating** | Voluntary carriage of EPIRBs, SARTs, and AIS MOB devices |
| **Coast Guard Operations** | RCCs use MCC-relayed coordinates to vector rescue assets |
| **Aviation (ELT)** | 406 MHz ELTs follow identical COSPAS-SARSAT protocol |
| **Land SAR (PLB)** | Hikers, climbers carry personal locator beacons |
| **Fishing Industry** | Iridium/GMDSS safety systems for small craft beyond A1 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Doppler Position-Fix for LEOSAR Satellites

When a low-earth-orbit (LEO) satellite passes over a 406 MHz beacon, the relative motion between satellite and beacon causes a measurable **Doppler shift** in the received carrier frequency. This shift, combined with the known satellite orbit, allows the ground station (LUT) to triangulate the beacon's position.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Doppler Shift Formula

**Step 1: Define the satellite-to-beacon geometry**

Consider a satellite at position $\vec{S}(t)$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}_s(t)$, and a stationary beacon at position $\vec{B}$ on Earth's surface.

The **range rate** (rate of change of distance) is:
$$\dot{R}(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\vert \vec{S}(t) - \vec{B} \vert$$

**Step 2: Apply the classical Doppler equation**

For an electromagnetic wave of frequency $f_0$ emitted by a stationary source and received by a moving observer:
$$f_{rx} = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{\dot{R}(t)}{c}\right)$$

**Step 3: Solve for the Doppler frequency shift**

$$f_d(t) = f_0 - f_{rx} = f_0 \cdot \frac{\dot{R}(t)}{c}$$

This can be rewritten as the standard form used by COSPAS-SARSAT engineers:
$$\boxed{f_d = \frac{f_0 \cdot v_r}{c}}$$

where $v_r = \dot{R}(t)$ is the **radial velocity** of the satellite relative to the beacon.

**Step 4: Numerical example for a typical LEO pass**

Given:
- $f_0 = 406$ MHz $= 406 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $v_r = 7.5$ km/s $= 7.5 \times 10^3$ m/s (typical LEO orbital speed at 850 km altitude)
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

$$f_d = \frac{406 \times 10^6 \times 7.5 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8}$$

$$f_d = \frac{3.045 \times 10^{12}}{3 \times 10^8}$$

$$\boxed{f_d \approx 10{,}150 \text{ Hz} \approx 10.15 \text{ kHz}}$$

This measurable ~10 kHz shift is what enables the LUT to compute the beacon's location to within **±5 km** accuracy without GNSS.

### 3.1.2 Comparison: Doppler Fix vs. GNSS Fix

| Parameter | Doppler Method (LEOSAR) | GNSS-Embedded Method (GEOSAR/MEOSAR) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Position Accuracy | ±5 km | ±50 m (often <10 m) |
| Time to Alert | ~90–120 min (orbit pass) | <5 min (instantaneous) |
| Coverage | Polar + Equatorial (LEO) | Global (with MEOSAR) |
| Beacon Complexity | Simple transmitter | Transmitter + GNSS receiver |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

## 3.2 Derivation: VHF Radio Horizon Distance

For maritime VHF communication, the **line-of-sight horizon** is the limiting factor. The Earth curves away, and the antenna height above sea level determines the effective communication range.

**Step 1: Geometric setup**

Consider an antenna at height $H$ above a perfectly spherical Earth of radius $R_E = 6371$ km $= 6{,}371{,}000$ m.

By Pythagoras' theorem, the line-of-sight distance to the horizon is:
$$d = \sqrt{(R_E + H)^2 - R_E^2}$$

**Step 2: Expand the square**

$$d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + 2 R_E H + H^2 - R_E^2} = \sqrt{2 R_E H + H^2}$$

**Step 3: Apply the approximation $H \ll R_E$**

Since antenna height is negligible compared to Earth's radius:
$$d \approx \sqrt{2 R_E H}$$

**Step 4: Convert to nautical miles**

Using 1 nm = 1852 m, the result becomes:
$$d \text{ (nm)} \approx \sqrt{2 R_E H} \cdot \frac{1}{1852} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 6{,}371{,}000 \cdot H} \cdot \frac{1}{1852}$$

$$d \text{ (nm)} \approx 1.93 \sqrt{H \text{ (meters)}}$$

**Step 5: Two-way (ship-to-ship) VHF range**

For two stations at heights $H_t$ (transmitter) and $H_r$ (receiver), the total radio horizon is:
$$\boxed{R_{VHF} = 2.5 \left(\sqrt{H_t} + \sqrt{H_r}\right) \text{ nautical miles}}$$

**Step 6: Numerical example**

A ship bridge with $H_t = 25$ m, communicating with a survivor's handheld VHF at $H_r = 1.5$ m above sea level:
$$R_{VHF} = 2.5 \left(\sqrt{25} + \sqrt{1.5}\right) = 2.5 \times (5.00 + 1.22)$$

$$R_{VHF} = 2.5 \times 6.22$$

$$\boxed{R_{VHF} \approx 15.6 \text{ nautical miles}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This formula is **extremely high-yield** and appears in nearly every KTU ESE paper on radio propagation. Remember the constant **2.5** (sometimes written as 1.23 × 2 for two-way) and ensure you use **meters for H** and **nm for R**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right SAR Equipment

The following matrix helps determine which equipment to use based on the distress scenario:

| Scenario | Best Primary Equipment | Best Secondary Equipment | Rationale |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Person overboard (MOB)** | AIS MOB / PLB | VHF-DSC handheld | Compact, integrates with ship's AIS |
| **Small craft sinking in coastal waters (A1)** | VHF-DSC Channel 70 | EPIRB | DSC alerts nearby coast station |
| **Vessel sinking beyond A1 (deep sea)** | EPIRB + SART | INMARSAT-C | Global coverage via satellite |
| **Fire on board in port** | VHF voice Channel 16 | Mobile phone | Local coast guard response |
| **Polar expedition (A4 region)** | HF DSC + COSPAS-SARSAT EPIRB | PLB | No INMARSAT in polar regions |
| **Aircraft ditching at sea** | ELT (406 MHz) + EPIRB | Survival VHF | Aviation + maritime combined |
| **Helicopter ditching offshore** | VHF-DSC portable | SART | Helicopters carry portable marine radio |

## 3.4 Battery and Endurance Calculations

### 3.4.1 EPIRB Continuous Operation Time

The EPIRB specification (IEC 61097-2) mandates **48 hours** of continuous operation at **−20°C**.

Total transmission cycles in 48 hours:
$$N_{cycles} = \frac{T_{total}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{48 \times 3600}{50} = \frac{172{,}800}{50} = 3456 \text{ bursts}$$

Total transmission energy (assuming 5 W RF for 520 ms):
$$E_{total} = N_{cycles} \times P \times t_{burst} = 3456 \times 5 \times 0.52 = 8985.6 \text{ Wh}$$

This energy must be delivered by a **LiFeS₂ (lithium iron disulfide) primary cell**, which is preferred for its 20+ year shelf life.

### 3.4.2 SART Operating Duration

Standard: **96 hours standby + 8 hours continuous interrogation response**

$$T_{total} = 96 + 8 = 104 \text{ hours} \approx 4.3 \text{ days}$$

This ensures the SART survives until the search window typically closes (3–5 days from last known position).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: COSPAS-SARSAT System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel in Distress] --> B[EPIRB Activated 406 MHz]
    B --> C[LEO Satellite 850 km Altitude]
    B --> D[GEO Satellite 36000 km Altitude]
    B --> E[MEO Satellite 24000 km Altitude]
    C --> F[LEO LUT Local User Terminal]

    E --> H[MEO LUT Receiving Station]

    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[RCC Rescue Coordination Center]
    J --> K[Coast Guard SAR Units]
    J --> L[Air Force SAR Aircraft]
    J --> M[Merchant Vessel Diverted]
    K --> N[Survivors Recovered]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

```mermaid
    A --> C[MF HF DSC Console]

    G[Survival Craft] --> H[Portable VHF]
    G --> I[SART X Band 9 GHz]

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: EPIRB Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrostatic Release Bracket] -->|Water 1.5 to 4m depth| B[Auto Activation Switch]
    A2[Manual Activation Button] --> B

    C --> E[406 MHz Synthesizer]

```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Classification: SAR Equipment Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[SAR Equipment and Technology] --> P1[Distress Alerting]
    ROOT --> P2[Locating and Homing]
    ROOT --> P3[Communication]

    P1 --> P1A[EPIRB 406 MHz]
    P1 --> P1B[VHF DSC Channel 70]

    P1 --> P1E[PLB Personal Locator Beacon]

    P2 --> P2A[SART 9 GHz X Band]

    P3 --> P3A[Portable VHF GMDSS]
```

## 4.5 Sequence Flow: EPIRB Alert Chain (Chronological)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vessel Sinks or MOB] --> B[Beacon Activated T equals 0 min]
    B --> C[First Burst Transmitted T equals 0 min]
    C --> D[Satellite Detection T equals 1 to 5 min]
    D --> E[LUT Processes Signal T equals 2 to 10 min]
    E --> F[MCC Distributes Alert T equals 5 to 15 min]
    F --> G[RCC Acknowledges T equals 10 to 30 min]
    G --> H[SAR Units Dispatched T equals 30 to 60 min]
    H --> I[On Scene Arrival T equals 2 to 24 hours]
    I --> J[SART or AIS SART Activated by Survivors]
    J --> K[Final Homing and Recovery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip for KTU Students:** In the exam, when asked to "draw a block diagram of GMDSS communication flow," sketch the **three-layer architecture** (Ship → Satellite → RCC) with clear labels for the frequency used at each link. This is a 7-mark question format.
