# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`
**Define the terms "Center of Buoyancy" and "Metacenter" as applied to a floating ship. Why is the position of the Metacenter considered to be constant at small angles of heel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Center of Buoyancy ($B$):** The point through which the resultant buoyant force on a floating body is considered to act. It is the centroid of the displaced volume of fluid. (1 mark)

- **Metacenter ($M$):** The point of intersection of the vertical line through the *new* center of buoyancy (when the ship is heeled through a small angle) with the original vertical centerline of the ship. (1 mark)

- **Constant position at small angles:** At small angles of heel (typically $< 10°$), the metacenter $M$ remains effectively at a fixed position above the keel because the locus of the center of buoyancy approximates a circular arc centered on $M$. This constancy makes $GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin\phi$ a valid approximation. (1 mark)

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`
**What is "Free Surface Effect" and how does it affect the metacentric height of a ship?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** Free Surface Effect (FSE) is the phenomenon in which a liquid in a partially filled tank of a ship shifts its position as the ship heels, creating a moment that effectively **reduces the righting moment** of the ship. (1.5 marks)

- **Effect on $GM$:** FSE causes a *virtual rise* in the center of gravity $G$ (or equivalently, a *virtual fall* in the metacenter $M$). The effective metacentric height becomes:

$$GM_{eff} = GM - \dfrac{i_{FS}}{V} = GM - \sum \left( \dfrac{L_T \cdot b^3}{12 V} \right)$$

(1.5 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question B.A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Q — Choice A]`

**A ship of length 100 m and beam 16 m floats at a draft of 4.5 m in seawater ($\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$). The displacement is 6500 tonnes. The center of buoyancy is 2.4 m above the keel and the center of gravity is 5.8 m above the keel.**

**(a)** Calculate the metacentric height ($GM$) of the ship, treating the waterplane as rectangular. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine the righting moment when the ship heels through an angle of $8°$. Comment on the stability of the ship. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for B.A(a)

**Given:**
- $L = 100$ m, $B = 16$ m, $T = 4.5$ m
- $\Delta = 6500$ t, $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$
- $KB = 2.4$ m, $KG = 5.8$ m

**Step 1 — Volume of displacement**

$$\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho} = \dfrac{6500}{1.025} = 6341.46 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Transverse second moment of waterplane area (rectangular)**

$$I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 16^3}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 4096}{12} = 34133.33 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Metacentric radius**

$$BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} = \dfrac{34133.33}{6341.46} = 5.382 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Distance from keel to metacenter**

$$KM = KB + BM = 2.4 + 5.382 = 7.782 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Metacentric height**

$$GM = KM - KG = 7.782 - 5.8 = 1.982 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{GM = 1.98 \text{ m (positive)}}$

---

#### Model Solution for B.A(b)

**Step 1 — Righting lever at $8°$**

$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin(8°) = 1.982 \times 0.13917 = 0.2758 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Weight of the ship**

$$W = \Delta \cdot g = 6500 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 6.3765 \times 10^7 \text{ N} = 63765 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Righting moment**

$$\text{RM} = W \times GZ = 63765 \times 0.2758 = 17586.6 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4 — Stability comment**

Since $GM = +1.98$ m is **positive**, the metacenter $M$ lies above the center of gravity $G$, and the ship is **stable at small angles of heel**. A $GM$ of approximately 2 m is on the **high side** for a typical merchant ship, indicating a stiff ship with a short roll period — it will be uncomfortable in heavy seas but is safe from capsize at small angles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** between tonnes and kg when computing $W$ in Newtons. KTU valuators specifically check this — wrong units = 1 to 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Mixing up $BM$ and $GM$** — $BM$ is from the buoyancy center, $GM$ is from the gravity center. They are *not* the same.
> 3. **Using degrees directly** in $\sin$ without setting the calculator to degree mode. This is a common and silent error.

---

### Question B.B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q — Choice B (Internal Choice)]`

**A box-shaped vessel has the following dimensions: $L = 80$ m, $B = 12$ m, $T = 4$ m (draft). It floats in seawater at a draft of 3.5 m. The center of gravity is 3.0 m above the keel. A weight of 100 tonnes is then loaded onto the deck at a height of 6 m above the keel, at the center of the ship.**

**(a)** Calculate the new draft and the new $KG$ after loading. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine whether the ship is stable after loading. If stable, find the righting moment at $6°$ heel. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for B.B(a)

**Step 1 — Initial displacement**

$$\nabla_1 = L \cdot B \cdot T_1 = 80 \times 12 \times 3.5 = 3360 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\Delta_1 = \rho \cdot \nabla_1 = 1.025 \times 3360 = 3444 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2 — Weight of ship (lightweight) using original $KG_1 = 3.0$ m**

We must back-calculate $W_1$ from the buoyancy condition. $W_1 = \Delta_1 \cdot g = 3444 \text{ t}$.

**Step 3 — Apply loading: weight $w = 100$ t at $z_w = 6$ m above keel**

$$W_2 = W_1 + w = 3444 + 100 = 3544 \text{ t}$$

**Step 4 — New $KG$ by moment balance about the keel**

$$KG_2 = \dfrac{W_1 \cdot KG_1 + w \cdot z_w}{W_2}$$

$$KG_2 = \dfrac{3444 \times 3.0 + 100 \times 6.0}{3544} = \dfrac{10332 + 600}{3544} = \dfrac{10932}{3544} = 3.084 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — New draft (assuming constant waterplane area and rectangular block)**

$$A_W = L \cdot B = 80 \times 12 = 960 \text{ m}^2$$

$$T_2 = \dfrac{\Delta_2}{A_W \cdot \rho} = \dfrac{3544}{960 \times 1.025} = \dfrac{3544}{984} = 3.602 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{T_2 = 3.60 \text{ m},\ KG_2 = 3.08 \text{ m}}$

---

#### Model Solution for B.B(b)

**Step 1 — New volume of displacement**

$$\nabla_2 = L \cdot B \cdot T_2 = 80 \times 12 \times 3.602 = 3457.9 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Transverse $I_T$ (rectangular waterplane)**

$$I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{80 \times 12^3}{12} = \dfrac{80 \times 1728}{12} = 11520 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — New $BM$**

$$BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla_2} = \dfrac{11520}{3457.9} = 3.331 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — $KB$ for a box-shaped ship is at $T/2$**

$$KB_2 = \dfrac{T_2}{2} = \dfrac{3.602}{2} = 1.801 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — $KM$**

$$KM = KB + BM = 1.801 + 3.331 = 5.132 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — $GM$**

$$GM = KM - KG_2 = 5.132 - 3.084 = 2.048 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Stability check**

Since $GM = +2.048$ m $> 0$, the ship is **stable**.

**Step 8 — Righting lever at $6°$**

$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin(6°) = 2.048 \times 0.10453 = 0.2141 \text{ m}$$

**Step 9 — Righting moment**

$$W_2 = 3544 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 34.766 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

$$\text{RM} = W_2 \times GZ = 34.766 \times 10^6 \times 0.2141 = 7.443 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m} = 7443 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{GM = 2.05 \text{ m},\ \text{RM} = 7.44 \text{ MN·m (stable)}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Errors in B.B:**
> 1. **Using the original $KG$** instead of the recomputed $KG$ after weight addition — this is a 2-mark deduction. The $KG$ always changes when weights are added, removed, or shifted.
> 2. **Forgetting to add the added weight** to $W$ when computing the new displacement. This is the single most common error in loading problems.
> 3. **Not computing the new draft** — students often use the original draft and get a wrong $BM$ and a wrong $GM$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Ship Hydrostatics and Stability**

- **Buoyancy = Weight** is the equilibrium condition: $W = \rho g \nabla$.
- **Three stability points:** $G$ (gravity), $B$ (buoyancy), $M$ (metacenter). Memorize their relative positions and definitions.
- **Master formula:** $GM = KB + BM - KG$ — derive this from memory in 30 seconds.
- **Metacentric radius:** $BM = I_T / V$. This is *not* a definition — it is a derivation result. KTU may ask you to derive it.
- **Righting lever at small angle:** $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$. Valid only for $\phi < 10°$ to $15°$.
- **Stability criterion:** $GM > 0 \Rightarrow$ stable; $GM = 0 \Rightarrow$ neutral; $GM < 0 \Rightarrow$ unstable.
- **Transverse vs Longitudinal:** $GM_T$ is small (decimetres to metres), $GM_L$ is large (tens to hundreds of metres). Transverse stability is the *critical* one.
- **Free Surface Effect** reduces $GM$ — always correct for slack tanks.
- **Reserve of Buoyancy** = volume above waterline up to the freeboard deck. Critical for survival after hull damage.
- **Static stability** = $GZ$ at a given angle. **Dynamic stability** = area under the $GZ$ curve.
- **Displacement units:** 1 tonne of seawater = 0.9755 $\text{m}^3$ (since $\rho = 1.025$ t/$\text{m}^3$).
- **Weight-to-Force conversion:** Multiply tonnes by 1000 to get kg, then by $g = 9.81$ m/s² to get Newtons.
- **IMO Code of Intact Stability** requires $GM \geq 0.15$ m for passenger ships and $GM \geq 0.05$ to $0.10$ m for cargo ships under all loading conditions.
- **Inclining Experiment** is the standard method to determine lightship $KG$ — a known weight is moved transversely, and the heel angle is measured; $GM = (w \cdot d) / (\Delta \cdot \tan\phi)$, where $d$ is the transverse shift of the weight.
- **Stiff ship** = high $GM$, short roll period (uncomfortable in waves). **Tender ship** = low $GM$, long roll period (sluggish, may have large amplitudes).
- **Angle of vanishing stability** is the angle beyond which the ship will not right itself — read from the $GZ$ curve.
- **Angle of maximum $GZ$** is the angle of greatest righting moment — usually between $25°$ and $40°$ for merchant ships.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a neat diagram showing $G$, $B$, $M$, and the heeled position $B_1$ with the $GZ$ lever. Examiners award 1 to 2 marks specifically for a clean, labelled diagram — even if your numerical answer has a small error, the diagram can earn you partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buoyancy, Stability, and Trim
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Buoyancy, Stability, and Trim

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 | Topic: Buoyancy, Stability, and Trim

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Buoyancy — Formal Definition

**Buoyancy** is the upward vertical force exerted by a fluid (in a marine context, **seawater**) on a body that is partially or fully immersed in it. This force is the direct physical manifestation of **Archimedes' Principle (c. 250 BC)**, which states that *the buoyant force acting on a body is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body*.

In Naval Architecture, this upward force acts at the **Center of Buoyancy (B)**, which is the geometric centroid of the underwater volume (the displaced volume $V$). When the ship is in static equilibrium floating freely, the buoyant force must exactly counterbalance the **Weight (W)** of the vessel, which acts downward through the **Center of Gravity (G)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> $$F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$
> Where:
> - $F_B$ = Total Buoyant Force (in Newtons or Tonnes-force)
> - $\rho$ = Density of seawater (typically **1.025 t/m³** for standard ocean conditions)
> - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
> - $\nabla$ = Volume of displacement (m³)

### 1.2 Stability — Formal Definition

**Stability** is the inherent tendency of a floating vessel to return to its original upright equilibrium position when disturbed by an external heeling moment (e.g., wind, turning, wave impact). It is fundamentally a measure of the vessel's resistance to capsizing.

The mathematical quantification of stability revolves around the **Metacenter (M)**, an imaginary point about which the vessel rotates when slightly inclined. The critical metric is the **Metacentric Height (GM)**, which is the vertical distance between the ship's Center of Gravity (G) and the Metacenter (M).

### 1.3 Trim — Formal Definition

**Trim** is the longitudinal inclination of a vessel. It is defined as the difference between the **Forward Draft (Draft Forward, $T_F$)** and the **Aft Draft (Draft Aft, $T_A$)**, measured at the designed draft marks on the bow and stern perpendiculars.

$$\text{Trim} = T_F - T_A$$

> - **Trim by the Bow** (positive): $T_F > T_A$ (bow deeper)
> - **Trim by the Stern** (negative): $T_A > T_F$ (stern deeper)
> - **Even Keel**: $T_F = T_A$

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Floating Cork:**
> Imagine pushing a cylindrical cork completely underwater in a swimming pool and releasing it. It shoots upward violently — this is the powerful buoyant force acting on a fully submerged object. Now imagine a small toy boat floating in a bathtub. The boat sinks slightly until the water displaced weighs exactly as much as the boat itself. This equilibrium point is the **Waterline (W)**. If you tilt the boat (heel it) and let go, the water's upward push shifts to one side, generating a restoring couple that rights the boat. This *self-righting moment* is **Stability**. If you tape a heavy rock to the top of the boat, making it top-heavy, it will capsize — a state of **negative stability**.
>
> For **Trim**, picture a cargo ship loading heavy containers in the forward hold first, then in the aft hold. The bow sinks more than the stern, creating a "Trim by the Bow" condition. The officer must monitor these drafts to keep the propeller submerged and ensure the bow has sufficient freeboard.

### 1.5 Standard Reference Constants in KTU Examinations

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Density of seawater | **1.025 t/m³** | $\rho_{sw}$ |
| Density of freshwater | **1.000 t/m³** | $\rho_{fw}$ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | $g$ |
| Standard acceleration (g) | **1 tonne-force = 9.81 kN** | $g$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Buoyancy

### 2.1.1 The Displacement Principle

When a ship floats at rest, the **Total Displacement ($\Delta$)** is defined as the *mass of water displaced by the underwater hull*. Since the ship is in static equilibrium:

$$W = \Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

In maritime engineering, displacement is conventionally expressed in **metric tonnes (t)**. By absorbing the gravitational constant $g$ into the density, the relationship simplifies to:

$$\text{Displacement (tonnes)} = \rho \text{ (t/m}^3\text{)} \times \nabla \text{ (m}^3\text{)}$$

### 2.1.2 Reserve Buoyancy

**Reserve Buoyancy** is the additional volume of water the ship could displace *above the actual waterline* before becoming completely submerged. It represents the *safety margin* against sinking due to waves, flooding, or overloading.

$$\nabla_{\text{Reserve}} = \nabla_{\text{Block (Full)}} - \nabla_{\text{Load Waterline}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Most commercial vessels are designed with a **Reserve Buoyancy of 20% to 50%** of the total volume to ensure survivability in heavy seas.

### 2.1.3 TPC (Tonnes Per Centimetre Immersion)

**TPC** is the mass required to sink a ship by **1 cm** deeper in the water. It is a critical operational parameter for cargo loading.

$$\text{TPC} = \frac{A_{WP} \cdot \rho}{100}$$

Where $A_{WP}$ is the ship's **Waterplane Area** in m². Note that TPC increases with draft because the waterplane area generally widens before tapering.

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework of Stability

### 2.2.1 The Metacenter Concept

When a ship heels by a small angle $\theta$, the Center of Buoyancy $B$ shifts to a new position $B'$. The line of action of the new buoyant force (vertical through $B'$) intersects the original centerline at a point called the **Metacenter (M)**.

For a ship to possess **initial positive stability**, the metacenter $M$ must lie above the center of gravity $G$, i.e., $GM > 0$.

### 2.2.2 The Transverse Metacentric Radius (BM)

By the **"Shift of B" Theorem**, the transverse BM is given by:

$$BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}$$

Where $I_T$ is the **Second Moment of Area** of the waterplane about the longitudinal centerline axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Transverse BM ($BM_T$)** governs side-to-side rolling stability. It is typically small (1–2 m for cargo ships).
> - **Longitudinal BM ($BM_L$)** governs fore-aft pitching stability. It is typically very large (50–200 m), making longitudinal stability almost always positive.

### 2.2.3 Transverse Stability at Large Angles — The GZ Curve

For inclinations beyond ~10°–15°, the small-angle assumption $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ breaks down. The exact expression for the righting lever is:

$$GZ = KB \cdot \sin\theta + BM \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta - KG \cdot \sin\theta$$

Or equivalently using the *cross-curve of stability* method:

$$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin\theta$$

The **GZ curve** is plotted against the heel angle $\theta$ and the area under this curve represents the **Dynamic Stability** (the energy required to roll the ship to a given angle).

## 2.3 Theoretical Framework of Trim

### 2.3.1 The First and Second Moments of the Waterplane Area

The change in draft at any longitudinal position $x$ from the midships point due to a shift in the center of flotation is governed by the **Moment to Change Trim (MCT)**.

$$\text{MCT 1 cm} = \frac{W \cdot GM_L}{100 \cdot L_{BP}}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Displacement in tonnes
- $GM_L$ = Longitudinal metacentric height in metres
- $L_{BP}$ = Length Between Perpendiculars in metres
- The factor **100** converts the trim from cm to metres (since 1 cm = 0.01 m)

### 2.3.2 Trim Calculations — Parallel Sinkage vs. Trim Correction

A change in loading causes two simultaneous effects:
1. **Parallel Sinkage** ($d_{par}$): The ship sinks uniformly due to the added weight.
2. **Trim** ($t$): The ship rotates about its longitudinal center of flotation.

The total change in draft at any point $x$ from midships is:

$$\Delta d_x = d_{par} \pm \frac{t \cdot x}{L_{BP}}$$

> - Use **(+)** for points aft of midships when trimming by the stern.
> - Use **(−)** for points forward of midships when trimming by the stern.

### 2.3.3 Draft Survey (KTU Favourite Problem)

A **Draft Survey** is a method to determine the cargo loaded/unloaded by reading the vessel's drafts before and after. The governing equation is:

$$Q = W_2 - W_1 + \text{Hydrostatic Corrections (Density, List, Trim)}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — these are the most-tested equations in KTU Maritime Safety papers.**

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Archimedes' Buoyancy | $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | $\rho$: t/m³, $g$: m/s², $\nabla$: m³ | All floating bodies |
| 2 | Displacement (mass) | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes | Floating equilibrium |
| 3 | TPC | $\text{TPC} = \frac{A_{WP} \cdot \rho}{100}$ | t/cm | Small immersion changes |
| 4 | Transverse BM | $BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | metres | Small heel angles |
| 5 | Longitudinal BM | $BM_L = \frac{I_L}{\nabla}$ | metres | Small trim angles |
| 6 | KM | $KM = KB + BM$ | metres | All conditions |
| 7 | KG | $KG = KB + BG$ | metres | Where $BG = KG - KB$ |
| 8 | GM (Transverse) | $GM = KM - KG$ | metres | Must be **> 0** for stability |
| 9 | Righting Lever (small $\theta$) | $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | metres | $\theta < 10°$ to $15°$ |
| 10 | Righting Lever (large $\theta$) | $GZ = KB\sin\theta + BM\sin\theta\cos\theta - KG\sin\theta$ | metres | All angles |
| 11 | Moment of Statical Stability | $M = W \cdot GZ$ | tonne-metres | Heeling/Righting |
| 12 | MCT 1 cm | $\text{MCT} = \frac{W \cdot GM_L}{100 \cdot L_{BP}}$ | t·cm | 1 cm trim change |
| 13 | Trim Formula | $\text{Trim} = \frac{\text{Trimming Moment}}{MCT}$ | cm or m | Longitudinal only |
| 14 | Draft at any point | $d_x = d_m \pm \frac{\text{Trim} \cdot x}{L_{BP}}$ | metres | Perpendicular to WL |
| 15 | Free Surface Effect | $GG' = \frac{i \cdot \rho}{\Delta}$ | metres (virtual rise of G) | Slack tanks only |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Loading Masters** use TPC and MCT values to plan cargo distribution so that the ship is not overloaded and remains within allowable trim limits (typically $\pm$ 0.5% to 1% of LBP).
- **Naval Architects** design the hull form to ensure a sufficiently large $GM$ (typically 0.15 m to 1.5 m for cargo ships) for a comfortable, safe rolling period (typically > 12 seconds).
- **Draft Surveyors** apply trim corrections to certify the exact cargo weight loaded or discharged, a critical commercial procedure worth millions of dollars in shipping contracts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Archimedes' Principle and the Displacement Equation

**Statement to Prove:** The buoyant force on a floating body equals the weight of fluid displaced.

### Mathematical Derivation

Consider a small elemental horizontal slice of the immersed hull at depth $z$ below the waterline, with thickness $dz$ and cross-sectional area $A(z)$.

The pressure at depth $z$ in a static fluid is given by **Pascal's Law**:
$$P(z) = P_0 + \rho \cdot g \cdot z$$

The vertical force on the elemental slice is:
$$dF = P(z) \cdot A(z) \cdot dz$$

Integrating from the keel (depth $T$) to the waterline (depth $0$):
$$F_B = \int_{0}^{T} (P_0 + \rho \cdot g \cdot z) \cdot A(z) \, dz$$

$$F_B = P_0 \int_{0}^{T} A(z) \, dz + \rho \cdot g \int_{0}^{T} z \cdot A(z) \, dz$$

The first integral equals $P_0 \cdot \nabla$ (force on a *horizontal projection*). The second integral is the **vertical hydrostatic force** on the curved hull. For a **closed immersed volume**, the horizontal forces cancel and the net vertical force is:

$$F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

> **Engineering Conversion to Tonnes:**
> In maritime practice, $g$ is absorbed into $\rho$, giving:
> $$\boxed{W = \Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla \text{ (tonnes)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Metacentric Radius (BM) by the Shift of B Method

**Objective:** Prove that $BM = I / \nabla$ for a ship heeled by a small angle $d\theta$.

### Setup

When the ship heels through a small angle $d\theta$, an elemental wedge of waterplane area $dA$ on the high side is *added* to the displaced volume, while a symmetric wedge on the low side is *removed* (or vice versa). The center of buoyancy shifts laterally.

Let $b$ be the transverse distance of the elemental area $dA$ from the longitudinal centerline. The wedge volume formed is $b \cdot d\theta \cdot dA$.

The transverse shift of the Center of Buoyancy, $BB'$, equals the moment of these wedge volumes about the centerline, divided by the total volume $\nabla$:

$$BB' = \frac{1}{\nabla} \int b \cdot (b \cdot d\theta) \, dA = \frac{d\theta}{\nabla} \int b^2 \, dA$$

By definition, the **Second Moment of Area** of the waterplane about the centerline is:

$$I_T = \int b^2 \, dA$$

Substituting:
$$BB' = \frac{I_T \cdot d\theta}{\nabla}$$

In the limit as $d\theta \to 0$, the metacentric radius is defined as:

$$\boxed{BM = \lim_{d\theta \to 0} \frac{BB'}{d\theta} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Righting Lever Equation (GZ)

**Objective:** Derive the exact equation for the righting lever at any angle of heel.

### Geometry

When the ship heels by angle $\theta$:
- $B$ moves to $B'$ along an arc of radius $BM$ about $M$.
- $G$ remains fixed in the body (assuming the weight distribution is constant).
- The buoyant force $F_B$ acts vertically through $B'$.
- The weight $W$ acts vertically downward through $G$.
- The horizontal separation between the two vertical lines of action is the **Righting Lever**, $GZ$.

Using the **Law of Sines** in the triangle $GMZ'$ (where $Z'$ is the foot of the perpendicular from $B'$ to the original centerline):

By inspecting the geometry of triangle $GBM'$ (where $M'$ is the new metacentre on the heeling centerline):

$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta + (B'B'' \text{ corrections})$$

The exact, full expression is:

$$\boxed{GZ = KB \cdot \sin\theta + BM \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta - KG \cdot \sin\theta}$$

**Limiting Case (Small Angles, $\theta \to 0$):**
For $\theta < 10°$, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians) and $\cos\theta \approx 1$, simplifying to:

$$GZ \approx \theta(KB + BM - KG) = GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

This is the basis of **Initial Stability** calculations.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Moment to Change Trim by 1 Centimetre (MCT 1 cm)

**Objective:** Derive the formula for the trimming moment required to alter the trim by exactly 1 cm.

### Setup

When a ship is trimmed by a small angle $\alpha$ (in radians), the after draft changes by approximately:
$$\Delta T_A = \frac{L_{BP}}{2} \cdot \alpha$$

The change in trim $\Delta t = L_{BP} \cdot \alpha$ (the difference between forward and aft draft changes).

This rotation is caused by a **longitudinal righting moment** equal to:

$$M_{\text{trim}} = W \cdot GZ_L \approx W \cdot GM_L \cdot \alpha$$

Combining the two relationships to eliminate $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{\Delta t}{L_{BP}}$$

$$M_{\text{trim}} = W \cdot GM_L \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{L_{BP}}$$

For $\Delta t = 1 \text{ cm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$, and the trimming moment conventionally expressed in **tonne-centimetres (t·cm)**:

$$M_{\text{trim}} = W \cdot GM_L \cdot \frac{1 \text{ cm}}{L_{BP} \cdot 100 \text{ cm/m}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MCT 1 cm} = \frac{W \cdot GM_L}{100 \cdot L_{BP}} \text{ (tonne·cm)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Insight:** For a 50,000 t ship with $GM_L = 150$ m and $L_{BP} = 200$ m:
> $$\text{MCT 1 cm} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 150}{100 \times 200} = 375 \text{ t·cm}$$
> This means a 375-tonne weight shifted longitudinally by 100 m would trim the vessel by 1 cm.

## 3.5 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix (KTU Standard Methodology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this decision matrix when tackling numerical problems in the KTU exam:**

| Problem Type | Step 1 | Step 2 | Step 3 | Final Answer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Buoyancy / Displacement** | Identify $W$, $\nabla$, $\rho$ | Apply $W = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Solve for unknown | Tonnes or m³ |
| **Initial Stability** | Find $KB$, $BM$, $KG$ | $KM = KB + BM$ | $GM = KM - KG$ | State positive/negative |
| **Righting Moment** | Compute $GZ = GM\sin\theta$ | $M = W \cdot GZ$ | Sum moments for equilibrium | t·m |
| **Trim Calculation** | Compute MCT 1 cm | Calculate trimming moment | $\text{Trim} = \text{Mom} / \text{MCT}$ | cm or m |
| **Draft at Point** | Find mean draft | Get trim from drafts | $d_x = d_m \pm \text{trim} \cdot x / L_{BP}$ | metres |
| **Free Surface Effect** | Find $i$ (transverse $I$ of free surface) | $\text{loss in GM} = i \cdot \rho / \Delta$ | Subtract from $GM$ | Reduced $GM$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Buoyancy — Force Balance Diagram

This block diagram illustrates the fundamental force interaction governing a floating vessel in static equilibrium, including the critical coordinates along the ship's vertical centerline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["GRAVITATIONAL FORCE W (Weight of Ship)"] --> nodeB["Acts Vertically Downward through Center of Gravity G"]
    nodeC["HYDROSTATIC PRESSURE P = rho times g times z"] --> nodeD["Integrates over entire Underwater Hull Surface"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Resultant Vertical Force F_B (Buoyancy)"]
    nodeB --> nodeF{"EQUILIBRIUM CONDITION: W equals F_B"}
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["G and B lie on the same VERTICAL LINE"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Ship Floats in Static Equilibrium"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Displacement DELTA equals rho times NABLA"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Reserve Buoyancy equals Full Volume minus Displaced Volume"]
```

## 4.2 Stability — Metacenter Geometry and Righting Lever

The following architecture flow maps the geometric construction of the metacenter M, the righting lever GZ, and the influence of the GZ curve on a vessel's behaviour at increasing heel angles.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Initial Upright Ship at theta equals 0"] --> nodeB["Apply Heeling Moment M_h"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Ship Heels to angle theta"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Center of Buoyancy shifts from B to B prime"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Vertical line through B prime intersects Original Centerline at METACENTRE M"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute METACENTRIC HEIGHT GM equals KM minus KG"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"GM greater than 0"}
    nodeG --> nodeH["POSITIVE INITIAL STABILITY: GZ = GM times sin theta"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["NEGATIVE INSTABILITY: Vessel Capsizes"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["GZ CURVE Plot GZ vs theta from 0 to 90 degrees"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Max GZ typically at 25 to 40 degrees"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["Angle of Vanishing Stability at 60 to 75 degrees"]
    nodeJ --> nodeM["Area under GZ Curve = DYNAMIC STABILITY in tonne-metres"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Energy Required to Roll Ship to Given Angle"]
```

## 4.3 Trim — Longitudinal Force and Moment Architecture

The trim architecture visualizes how longitudinal weight shifts cause the vessel to rotate about its center of flotation (LCF), producing forward and aft draft changes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Initial Loading Condition"] --> nodeB["Add or Shift Weight W_s at distance x from Midships"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Longitudinal Trimming Moment = W_s times x in tonne-metres"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Ship Rotates about Longitudinal Center of Flotation LCF"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Parallel Sinkage d_par in metres"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Trim Angle alpha in radians"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["MCT 1 cm = W times GM_L divided by 100 times L_BP"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Compute Trim = Trimming Moment divided by MCT 1 cm in cm"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Draft at distance x from midships"]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["d_x = d_mean plus minus trim times x divided by L_BP"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Resulting Draft Forward T_F and Draft Aft T_A"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Trim equals T_F minus T_A"]
    nodeL --> nodeM{"Trim within Allowable Limits"}
    nodeM --> nodeN["PROCEED with Voyage"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["ADJUST Ballast or Cargo to Correct"]
```

## 4.4 Stability Curve — GZ Diagram Block Architecture

The complete statical stability curve is the most-tested graphical element in KTU exams. The following block architecture maps the key zones of the GZ curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Zone 1: 0 to 10 degrees - INITIAL STABILITY Linear GM sin theta"] --> nodeB["Zone 2: 10 to 30 degrees - STABILITY RISES Curvature dominated by BM sin theta cos theta"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Zone 3: 30 to 40 degrees - MAXIMUM GZ Peak of stability"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Zone 4: 40 to 60 degrees - GZ DECREASES Deck edge immersion begins"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Zone 5: 60 to 75 degrees - ANGLE OF VANISHING STABILITY GZ = 0"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Zone 6: 75 to 90 degrees - NEGATIVE STABILITY Ship will capsize"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["AREA UNDER CURVE = Dynamic Stability Energy J"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["IMO Criterion: Area 0 to 30 degrees must be greater than or equal to 0.055 metre-radians"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["IMO Criterion: Area 0 to 40 degrees must be greater than or equal to 0.090 metre-radians"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Area 30 to 40 degrees must be greater than or equal to 0.030 metre-radians"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Center of Buoyancy." Explain its significance in determining the stability of a floating body.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The Center of Buoyancy (B) is defined as the *centroid of the displaced volume of water*, i.e., the geometric center of the underwater portion of the hull at any given draft.
- **Significance in Stability (2 Marks):** As the ship heels, B shifts laterally to $B'$ because the shape of the underwater volume changes. The line of action of the buoyant force through $B'$ intersects the original vertical centerline at the **Metacenter (M)**. The transverse distance between the shifted buoyant force and the weight acting at G gives the **Righting Lever (GZ)**, which is the fundamental measure of stability. Without locating B, it is mathematically impossible to compute GZ, and hence to assess whether the vessel has positive or negative stability.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "TPC" of a ship? A ship floating in seawater of density 1.025 t/m³ has a waterplane area of 4500 m². Calculate the TPC.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** TPC (Tonnes Per Centimetre Immersion) is defined as the *mass in tonnes required to sink a ship by exactly 1 cm deeper* in water of a given density.
- **Formula (1 Mark):** $\text{TPC} = \dfrac{A_{WP} \cdot \rho}{100}$
- **Calculation (1 Mark):**
  $$\text{TPC} = \frac{4500 \times 1.025}{100} = 46.125 \text{ t/cm}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A box-shaped vessel of length 100 m, breadth 20 m, and depth 10 m floats at a draft of 6 m in seawater of density 1.025 t/m³.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the displacement, the position of the Center of Buoyancy (KB), and the Transverse Metacentric Radius (BM).

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the vessel is loaded such that the Center of Gravity (KG) is 5.5 m above the keel, determine the initial metacentric height (GM) and state whether the ship is in stable, neutral, or unstable equilibrium. Also, find the righting moment when the ship is heeled by 8°.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Volume of Displacement (1 Mark)**
$$\nabla = L \times B \times T = 100 \times 20 \times 6 = 12{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Displacement in Tonnes (1 Mark)**
$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 12{,}000 = 12{,}300 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3: Center of Buoyancy (KB) (2 Marks)**
For a box-shaped vessel, B lies at half the draft from the keel:
$$KB = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Transverse Second Moment of Waterplane Area (1 Mark)**
$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 20^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 8000}{12} = 66{,}666.67 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 5: Transverse BM (1 Mark)**
$$BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{66{,}666.67}{12{,}000} = 5.556 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: KM (1 Mark)**
$$KM = KB + BM = 3.0 + 5.556 = 8.556 \text{ m}$$

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Given KG (1 Mark)**
$$KG = 5.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Initial Metacentric Height GM (1 Mark)**
$$GM = KM - KG = 8.556 - 5.5 = 3.056 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Stability Status (1 Mark)**
Since $GM = 3.056 \text{ m} > 0$, the ship has **POSITIVE INITIAL STABILITY** and is in **Stable Equilibrium**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Many students incorrectly conclude "unstable" when $GM$ is small. *Any positive GM* (no matter how small, e.g., 0.1 m) means the ship is stable. Only $GM < 0$ implies instability. Marks are awarded for the **reasoning**, not the value of GM.

**Step 4: Righting Lever at 8° (1 Mark)**
Using the small-angle formula (valid because 8° < 10°):
$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin(8°) = 3.056 \times 0.1392 = 0.425 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Righting Moment (1 Mark)**
$$M_R = W \cdot GZ = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GZ$$
$$M_R = 12{,}300 \times 9.81 \times 0.425 = 51{,}283 \text{ kN·m}$$

Or, expressed in tonne-metres:
$$M_R = 12{,}300 \times 0.425 = 5{,}227.5 \text{ t·m}$$

**Step 6: Interpretation (1 Mark)**
This righting moment of approximately **5,228 t·m** acts to restore the vessel to upright when heeled to 8°.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A bulk carrier of displacement 30,000 tonnes, length between perpendiculars 180 m, and longitudinal metacentric height ($GM_L$) of 150 m has forward and aft drafts of 8.5 m and 9.5 m respectively.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the trim, the present MCT 1 cm, and the trimming moment being applied by the existing loading condition.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If 2000 tonnes of cargo is to be discharged from a forward hold whose center of gravity is 70 m forward of midships, calculate the new drafts forward and aft, and comment on the change in trim.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Present Trim (1 Mark)**
$$\text{Trim} = T_F - T_A = 8.5 - 9.5 = -1.0 \text{ m} \text{ (trim by the stern)}$$

**Step 2: MCT 1 cm Formula and Calculation (2 Marks)**
$$\text{MCT 1 cm} = \frac{W \cdot GM_L}{100 \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 150}{100 \times 180}$$

$$\text{MCT 1 cm} = \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{18{,}000} = 250 \text{ t·cm}$$

**Step 3: Total Trim in cm (1 Mark)**
$$\text{Trim} = 1.0 \text{ m} = 100 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Trimming Moment Present (1 Mark)**
$$M_{\text{trim}} = \text{Trim (cm)} \times \text{MCT 1 cm} = 100 \times 250 = 25{,}000 \text{ t·cm}$$

**Step 5: Convert to Tonne-Metres (1 Mark)**
$$M_{\text{trim}} = 25{,}000 \text{ t·cm} = 250 \text{ t·m}$$

**Step 6: Direction Statement (1 Mark)**
The existing trim of 1.0 m by the stern indicates a net longitudinal centre of gravity located **aft of the longitudinal centre of flotation (LCF)**.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Effect of Discharging Cargo (1 Mark)**
Discharging 2000 t from forward (70 m forward of midships) creates a **lightening moment** that is *equivalent* to a virtual **adding** of 2000 t at the same longitudinal distance, but on the *opposite* side (i.e., shifting the weight effectively toward the stern).

Wait — let us re-evaluate: Removing 2000 t from forward causes the bow to rise, equivalent to a virtual addition at the aft side. This produces a trimming moment:

**Step 1: Trimming Moment due to Discharge (1 Mark)**
$$M = 2000 \text{ t} \times 70 \text{ m} = 140{,}000 \text{ t·m} = 14{,}000{,}000 \text{ t·cm}$$

**Step 2: Change in Trim (1 Mark)**
$$\Delta t = \frac{M_{\text{trim}}}{\text{MCT 1 cm}} = \frac{14{,}000{,}000}{250} = 56{,}000 \text{ cm}$$

This is the *virtual* equivalent; more correctly, when cargo is *removed* from forward, the ship trims **less by the stern** (or more by the bow). The bow will rise.

**Step 3: Correct Approach — Sign Convention (2 Marks)**
Removing 2000 t at 70 m forward of midships reduces the forward draft and increases the aft draft — the ship becomes **less trimmed by the stern**, or in this case, it trims by the bow.

$$\text{Change in trim (magnitude)} = \frac{W_s \cdot x}{MCT \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{2000 \times 70}{250 \times 1.8} \text{ m (approx)}$$

Using a clearer relationship:
$$\Delta t = \frac{W_s \cdot x}{\text{MCT 1 cm (t/m)}} = \frac{2000 \times 70}{2.5 \text{ t/m}} = 56{,}000 \text{ m}$$
(Using MCT in t/m: $250 \text{ t·cm} = 2.5 \text{ t·m}$)

**Simpler Formulation (Step 3 Revised):**
$$\Delta t = \frac{W_s \cdot x}{W \cdot GM_L / L_{BP}} = \frac{2000 \times 70 \times 180}{30{,}000 \times 150} = 0.56 \text{ m}$$

So the trim changes by **0.56 m** — but the direction depends on sign convention.

**Step 4: New Forward Draft (1 Mark)**
With the bow rising (cargo removed forward), $T_F$ decreases. Half of the trim change is applied to each end:

$$\Delta T_F = -\frac{\Delta t}{2} \times \text{(forward contribution)}$$

Using the standard LCF method with LCF at midships (assuming LCF coincides with midships for simplicity):
$$T_{F,\text{new}} = T_{F,\text{old}} + \frac{\Delta t}{2} = 8.5 + 0.28 = 8.78 \text{ m}$$
$$T_{A,\text{new}} = T_{A,\text{old}} - \frac{\Delta t}{2} = 9.5 - 0.28 = 9.22 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: New Trim (1 Mark)**
$$\text{New Trim} = 8.78 - 9.22 = -0.44 \text{ m (still by the stern, but reduced from 1.0 m)}$$

**Step 6: Commentary (1 Mark)**
The vessel's trim by the stern has been **reduced from 1.0 m to 0.44 m**, which is a **safer and more efficient operating condition**, as excessive trim by the stern can cause propeller emergence and loss of steering control.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes in Trim Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units**: MCT 1 cm formula requires $W$ in tonnes and $L_{BP}$ in metres. Forgetting the factor 100 (cm-to-m conversion) is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Sign convention errors**: Students confuse whether cargo addition *increases* or *decreases* the draft. Always draw a free-body diagram.
> 3. **MCT with new displacement**: After cargo change, the *new* $W$ should ideally be used to compute the new MCT. Many KTU problems accept using the original MCT for simplicity, but in rigorous 14-mark problems, **always check** whether MCT must be updated.
> 4. **Confusing GM_T and GM_L**: Transverse and longitudinal metacentric heights are vastly different. Be sure the question specifies which one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY QUICK-REFERENCE — Read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Archimedes' Principle** is the foundation: $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$. The buoyant force acts at the **Center of Buoyancy (B)**, which is the centroid of the *displaced* volume.
- [ ] A ship floats in **static equilibrium** only when $W = F_B$ and $G$ and $B$ lie on the same vertical line.
- [ ] **Reserve Buoyancy** is the safety volume above the waterline; typically 20–50% of total hull volume.
- [ ] **TPC** = Tonnes Per Centimetre immersion. Formula: $\text{TPC} = A_{WP} \cdot \rho / 100$.
- [ ] The **Metacenter (M)** is the intersection of the *new* vertical buoyancy line with the *original* centerline after a small heel.
- [ ] **Transverse BM** is always *small* (1–2 m for cargo ships) and governs **roll**. **Longitudinal BM** is *very large* (50–250 m) and governs **pitch**.
- [ ] **Initial Stability** requires $GM > 0$. The bigger the $GM$, the *stiffer* (quicker rolling) the ship.
- [ ] Small-angle righting lever: $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$ (valid only for $\theta \lesssim 10°$).
- [ ] Exact righting lever: $GZ = KB \sin\theta + BM \sin\theta \cos\theta - KG \sin\theta$.
- [ ] The **GZ Curve** peaks at 25°–40°; the area under the curve is the **Dynamic Stability**.
- [ ] IMO Statical Stability Criteria: $A_{0-30} \geq 0.055$ m·rad, $A_{0-40} \geq 0.090$ m·rad, $A_{30-40} \geq 0.030$ m·rad.
- [ ] **Trim** = $T_F - T_A$. Positive = by the bow; negative = by the stern.
- [ ] **MCT 1 cm** = $W \cdot GM_L / (100 \cdot L_{BP})$, expressed in **t·cm**.
- [ ] Draft at any point $x$ from midships: $d_x = d_m \pm (\text{Trim} \cdot x / L_{BP})$.
- [ ] **Free Surface Effect** (slack tanks) causes an *effective rise* in G: $GG' = i \cdot \rho / \Delta$, reducing $GM$.
- [ ] **Density corrections** between freshwater and seawater use $\Delta_{sw} = \Delta_{fw} \times (\rho_{sw} / \rho_{fw})$.
- [ ] **Standard seawater density** for KTU problems = **1.025 t/m³** unless stated otherwise.
- [ ] **Draft Survey** is a commercial procedure that uses trim/density corrections to compute the exact cargo loaded or discharged.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inclining Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inclining Experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Inclining Experiment** is a practical, on-board procedure conducted on a newly built ship (or one that has undergone major modifications) to determine the ship's **lightship weight (Δ_LS)** and the **longitudinal and transverse positions of the center of gravity (LCG and VCG/KG)**. From these primary measurements, the critical stability parameter — the **transverse Metacentric Height (GM)** — is calculated.

The experiment is governed internationally by **IMO Resolution A.749(18)** — *"Code on Intact Stability for all Types of Ships covered by IMO Instruments"*, and the standards for execution are codified under **ISO 16855:2015** and classification society rules (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The inclining experiment is NOT an emergency drill. It is a controlled engineering test typically performed *once in the life of a ship* — at the time of delivery, after which the results become the **permanent stability baseline** for the ship's entire operational life.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Tilted Weightlifter"**
> Imagine a person standing on a perfectly level bathroom scale. The scale reads their body weight. Now imagine you secretly move a heavy dumbbell from their left pocket to their right pocket. The person tilts to the right, and a plumb line hanging from their shoulder shifts by a measurable amount.
>
> - The **person + dumbbell** = the **ship (total weight Δ)**
> - The **dumbbell movement (w × d)** = the **known transverse shift of test weights**
> - The **plumb-line deflection** = the **angle of heel (θ)**
> - The **resistance to tilt** = the ship's **metacentric height (GM)**
>
> Just as a tall, narrow person would tilt more than a short, wide person for the same weight shift, a ship with a small GM will heel more than one with a large GM. The experiment essentially *measures how much the ship resists being tilted* — and from that, we calculate GM.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Foundation Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Displacement | Δ | Total weight of ship (W) in tonnes |
| Center of Gravity | G (or KG) | Point where ship's total weight acts vertically |
| Center of Buoyancy | B (or KB) | Point where buoyant force acts vertically |
| Metacenter | M | Point where the new vertical line through B (when heeled) intersects the original vertical centerline |
| Metacentric Radius | BM | $\text{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V} = \dfrac{I}{\nabla}$ (I = second moment of waterplane area, V = displaced volume) |
| Metacentric Height | **GM** | $\text{GM} = \text{BM} - \text{KG}$ — the **single most critical stability indicator** |
| Lightship Weight | Δ_LS | Weight of the ship with no cargo, fuel, water, stores, crew, or ballast (only permanent structure + machinery) |
| Test Weights | w (or W_t) | Known solid weights (usually 1–25 tonnes each) shifted transversely |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Inclining" and not "Stability Test"?** The experiment does not measure stability directly; it *inclines the ship* deliberately by moving weights, and from the resulting heel angle, stability is *back-calculated*. The name reflects the physical act, not the outcome.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Formula Works

The inclining experiment is grounded in the **principle of moments** applied to a floating body in static equilibrium.

**The Setup:**
1. A ship of displacement **Δ** floats upright. The forces of weight (acting at G) and buoyancy (acting at B) are collinear and equal in magnitude but opposite in direction.
2. Known test weights **w** (total) are moved transversely across the deck by a known distance **d**.
3. The ship heels to a new equilibrium angle **θ**, where weight and buoyancy forces again form a couple, but now separated by a horizontal distance.
4. This couple is the **righting moment (RM)** of the ship at angle θ.

### 2.2 Logic Steps — The Equilibrium Balance

- **Heeling Moment (HM):** Created by the transverse movement of the test weight.
$$\text{HM} = w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$$

- **Righting Moment (RM):** Provided by the ship's own stability (the GM effect).
$$\text{RM} = \Delta \cdot \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

- **At static equilibrium (heeling complete, ship steady):**
$$\text{HM} = \text{RM}$$

- **Solving for GM (the classical derivation):**
$$w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta = \Delta \cdot \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

$$\text{GM} = \frac{w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta}{\Delta \cdot \sin\theta} = \frac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Small-Angle Simplification:** For the typical inclining heel of **1° to 4°**, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians). This allows the compact formula $\text{GM} = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$ to be used directly.

### 2.3 The Generalized Formula (Multiple Weight Movements)

In practice, weights are often moved in **multiple stages** (port → starboard, and back) to verify consistency. The cumulative form is:

$$\text{GM} = \frac{\sum (w_i \cdot d_i \cdot \cos\theta_i)}{\Delta \cdot \sin\theta}$$

For very small angles, this simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\text{GM} = \frac{\sum (w_i \cdot d_i)}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}}$$

### 2.4 Displacement Correction (Draft Survey)

Because the ship heels during the experiment, the draft changes slightly. The displacement **Δ** used in the formula is the **mean displacement** calculated from the **mean of the port and starboard drafts**, corrected for water density (ρ):

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot A_{WP} \cdot T_{mean} \quad \text{(in tonnes)}$$

Where $A_{WP}$ is the waterplane area and $T_{mean}$ is the mean draft during the experiment.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE — All Formulas Required for Inclining Experiment Problems**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{GM} = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$ | **Core GM formula (single shift)** | **metres (m)** |
| 2 | $\text{GM} = \dfrac{\sum w_i d_i}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$ | **Cumulative GM (multiple shifts)** | **m** |
| 3 | $\text{GM} = \text{BM} - \text{KG}$ | Metacentric height definition | m |
| 4 | $\text{BM} = \dfrac{I}{\nabla}$ | Metacentric radius | m |
| 5 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$ | Displacement (block coefficient form) | tonnes |
| 6 | $GZ = \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$ | Righting lever at small angles | m |
| 7 | $\text{RM} = \Delta \cdot GZ = \Delta \cdot \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$ | Righting moment | tonne·m |
| 8 | $\text{HM} = w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$ | Heeling moment | tonne·m |
| 9 | $\text{KG}_{\text{corrected}} = \text{KG}_{\text{exp}} - \delta_{\text{FSE}}$ | Free Surface Effect correction (added to KG) | m |
| 10 | $\text{KG}_{\text{final}} = \text{KG}_{\text{lightship}} + \dfrac{\text{VCG of variable loads} \cdot \text{Weight}}{\Delta}$ | Variable load correction | m |

> **CRITICAL SYMBOL CONVENTIONS USED BY KTU EXAMINERS:**
> - Use `tan` for trigonometric function, NEVER $\tan$ in tables.
> - Density of seawater: **ρ = 1.025 t/m³**; freshwater: **ρ = 1.000 t/m³**.
> - Distances **d** are measured along the **deck (athwartships)**, not along the inclined deck.
> - Angle **θ** must be in **degrees** when using calculator `tan` button (or convert to radians).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Ship Design** | Validates theoretical GM calculated during design phase; flag discrepancies early. |
| **Loading Computer Calibration** | Establishes the `KM` and `Lightship Weight` constants in the onboard stability computer. |
| **Damage Stability** | Baseline for flooding calculations under SOLAS Chapter II-1. |
| **Insurance & Class** | Mandatory for issuance of Class certificate; failure = ship cannot trade. |
| **Port State Control** | Documents must be available during PSC inspections (Form ISS-1). |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the GM Formula

> **Starting Point:** A ship floats upright in still water. Its weight **Δ** acts downward through **G**, and buoyancy **Δ** acts upward through **B**. Both forces are collinear along the centerline.

**Step 1: Apply a known transverse weight shift**

A known test weight **w** is moved transversely across the deck by a horizontal distance **d** (measured athwartships at deck level).

This creates a **heeling moment (HM)** about the original centerline. The moment arm is the horizontal projection of the displacement vector:

$$\text{HM} = w \times d \times \cos\theta$$

> **Logic:** The weight moves along the deck (horizontal), so the lever arm is $d \cos\theta$, not $d$.

**Step 2: The ship heels to a new equilibrium angle θ**

As the ship heels, the center of buoyancy **B** shifts outward. The new buoyancy force passes through **B'**, and its vertical line intersects the original centerline at the **metacenter M**.

The horizontal separation between the weight vector (through G) and the buoyancy vector (through B') is the **righting lever (GZ)**.

**Step 3: Express the righting moment**

By definition of metacentric height (small angle):

$$GZ = \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

Therefore, the **righting moment** provided by the ship's stability is:

$$\text{RM} = \Delta \cdot GZ = \Delta \cdot \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4: Apply static equilibrium**

The ship comes to rest when heeling and righting moments balance:

$$\text{HM} = \text{RM}$$

$$w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta = \Delta \cdot \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to isolate GM**

$$\text{GM} = \frac{w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta}{\Delta \cdot \sin\theta}$$

**Step 6: Simplify using trigonometric identity**

$$\frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \cot\theta = \frac{1}{\tan\theta}$$

$$\therefore \text{GM} = \frac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Validity:** This derivation is valid for $\theta < 7°$ (the linear range of the GZ curve). Inclining experiments are deliberately kept in the 1°–4° range for this reason.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step Solution)

> **PROBLEM:** A container ship of displacement **15,000 tonnes** is to undergo an inclining experiment. Four test weights, each of **20 tonnes**, are placed on the weather deck and shifted transversely by a distance of **12 m** from port to starboard. The resulting mean angle of heel is **2.5°**. Water density is **1.025 t/m³**. Calculate the ship's metacentric height (GM).

**Given Data:**
- Displacement, Δ = 15,000 t
- Number of weights = 4
- Weight per unit, w = 20 t
- Total test weight moved, W = 4 × 20 = 80 t
- Transverse distance moved, d = 12 m
- Angle of heel, θ = 2.5°
- Water density, ρ = 1.025 t/m³

**Step 1: Compute the Heeling Moment (HM)**

$$\text{HM} = w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$$

$$= 80 \times 12 \times \cos(2.5°)$$

$$= 80 \times 12 \times 0.99905$$

$$= 959.09 \text{ tonne·m}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Righting Moment (RM)**

$$\text{RM} = \Delta \cdot \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

This contains the unknown GM. We must solve the next step.

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium (HM = RM)**

$$80 \times 12 \times \cos(2.5°) = 15{,}000 \times \text{GM} \times \sin(2.5°)$$

$$959.09 = 15{,}000 \times \text{GM} \times 0.04362$$

**Step 4: Solve for GM**

$$\text{GM} = \frac{959.09}{15{,}000 \times 0.04362}$$

$$\text{GM} = \frac{959.09}{654.30}$$

$$\boxed{\text{GM} = 1.466 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5: Verification using the compact formula**

$$\text{GM} = \frac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$$

$$= \frac{80 \times 12}{15{,}000 \times \tan(2.5°)}$$

$$= \frac{960}{15{,}000 \times 0.04366}$$

$$= \frac{960}{654.9}$$

$$\boxed{\text{GM} = 1.466 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark}$$

**Step 6: Comment on the result**

A GM of **1.466 m** is **typical for a container ship** (range 0.5–1.5 m). For passenger ships, GM should be ≥ 0.15 m (IMO minimum). The result is physically reasonable.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Calculation Scenarios

| Scenario | Modification to Formula | When Used |
|----------|------------------------|-----------|
| **Weights moved both P→S and S→P** | $\text{GM} = \dfrac{(W_1 d_1 - W_2 d_2)}{\Delta \tan\theta}$ | When checking symmetry / cancelling residual list |
| **Free Surface Effect (FSE)** | Subtract FSE: $\text{GM}_{\text{true}} = \text{GM}_{\text{exp}} - \text{FSE}$ | When slack tanks exist (e.g., partially filled fuel bunker) |
| **Wind Heel Correction** | Add wind-induced heeling moment: $\text{HM}_{\text{total}} = \text{HM}_{\text{test}} + \text{HM}_{\text{wind}}$ | When wind speed > 5 m/s during experiment |
| **Mooring Forces Correction** | Account for asymmetric mooring line tensions | Ship not fully freed from moorings |
| **Density Correction** | Recalculate Δ using local ρ | River water or brackish conditions |

### 3.4 Lightship Weight Determination (Secondary Output)

In addition to GM, the experiment also yields the **lightship weight** by drawing a **Deflection vs. Moment graph**:

| Variable | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| Y-axis | Pendulum deflection (proportional to $\tan\theta$) |
| X-axis | Cumulative heeling moment ($\sum w_i d_i$) |
| Slope of best-fit line | $\dfrac{1}{\Delta \cdot \text{GM}}$ |
| X-intercept of best-fit line | **Residual heeling moment** (used to correct for unmeasured inclinations) |

The lightship weight is then determined by a separate **deadweight survey** (weighing all fuel, water, cargo, stores) and subtracting from the known total displacement:

$$\Delta_{LS} = \Delta - \text{Total Deadweight}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Experimental Setup Diagram (Block Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **physical layout and force interactions** of the inclining experiment. Because a true 3D ship drawing is impractical in a flowchart, this block diagram captures the **functional architecture** of the test arrangement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Floating Upright at Even Keel] --> B[Place Test Weights on Port Side at Known Distance d]
    B --> C[Measure Initial Pendulum Position - Baseline]
    C --> D[Shift All Test Weights Transversely to Starboard Side]
    D --> E[Record New Pendulum Deflection - delta s]
    E --> F[Calculate Heel Angle theta = arctan delta s divided by Pendulum Length L]
    F --> G[Compute Heeling Moment HM = w times d times cos theta]
    G --> H[Read Mean Draft and Compute Displacement delta from Draft Survey]
    H --> I[Apply Equilibrium HM equals RM to Solve GM = HM divided by delta times sin theta]
    I --> J{Apply Corrections}
    J --> J1[Free Surface Effect]
    J --> J2[Wind Heeling Moment]
    J --> J3[Mooring Line Asymmetry]
    J1 --> K[Final Corrected GM and KG]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Compile Stability Booklet and Form ISS-1]
```

### 4.2 Force Interaction Schematic (Transverse Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHIP[Ship Heeling at Angle theta]
        W[Total Weight delta acts downward at G] 
        B[Buoyancy delta acts upward at B shifted]
        M[Metacenter M on original centerline]
    end
    
    subgraph MOMENTS[Equilibrium of Moments]
        HM1[Heeling Moment = w times d times cos theta]
        RM1[Righting Moment = delta times GM times sin theta]
    end
    
    W --- HM1
    B --- RM1
    HM1 --> EQUIL[Static Equilibrium HM equals RM]
    RM1 --> EQUIL
    EQUIL --> RESULT[GM equals w times d divided by delta times tan theta]
```

### 4.3 Procedure Flowchart (Step-by-Step Sequential Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Pre-Experiment Preparation] --> P2[Step 2: Ship in Light Condition - No Cargo or Stores]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Slack Tanks Sounded and FSE Calculated]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Mooring Lines Slacked - Ship Floating Freely]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Wind Speed Recorded - Should be below 5 m per s]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Pendulums Suspended - 2 port side and 2 starboard side]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Test Weights Pre-positioned on Port Side at Marked Distance d]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Baseline Readings of Pendulums and Drafts Recorded]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Shift All Weights to Starboard Side]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Wait for Steady Heel - Record New Pendulum Deflection]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Shift Weights Back to Port Side - Repeat Reading]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Plot Deflection vs Moment Graph - Linear Regression]
    P12 --> P13[Step 13: Calculate Lightweight by Deadweight Survey]
    P13 --> P14[Step 14: Calculate Final GM KG and Produce Stability Booklet]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above diagrams use a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** approach, which is the recommended fallback when physical drawings of ships (with their complex 3D geometry) cannot be rendered in a flowchart. Each block represents a **functional station** in the experiment, and the arrows show the **information/force flow** between them — the same way the actual procedure is sequenced on board.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Define the Inclining Experiment and state its primary objective.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Inclining Experiment** is a practical, on-board procedure performed on a newly built ship (or one that has undergone major structural modifications) to determine the ship's **lightship weight (Δ_LS)** and the **vertical position of the center of gravity (KG)**. From these, the **transverse metacentric height (GM)** is calculated.

**Primary Objective:** To obtain the as-built stability data of the ship for use in the **onboard Stability Booklet** and **Loading Computer**, ensuring the ship can be operated safely throughout its service life in compliance with **IMO Resolution A.749(18)** and **SOLAS Chapter II-1**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for components measured, 1 mark for objective/regulation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**List any six conditions that must be satisfied before commencing an inclining experiment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — list any 6, each 0.5 marks):**

1. The ship must be in or as close to **lightship condition** as possible (no cargo, minimal fuel/water/stores).
2. The ship must be in **still, sheltered water** with **minimal wind** (typically < 5 m/s).
3. All **mooring lines** must be slack so the ship floats freely.
4. **Test weights** of known magnitude and position must be available.
5. **Pendulums** must be installed and verified (usually 4 pendulums — 2 port, 2 starboard).
6. All **partially filled tanks** must be sounded and the **free surface effect (FSE)** calculated.
7. **Drafts** must be read at all six marks (forward, midship, aft on both sides).
8. The ship must be on an **even keel** (≤ 0.5° trim).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each question is 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer ONE full question from the choice of TWO.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the formula $\text{GM} = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$ for the inclining experiment, starting from the principle of moments. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier of displacement **20,000 tonnes** undergoes an inclining experiment. Four test weights of **25 tonnes each** are shifted transversely across the deck by a distance of **10 m**. The resulting angle of heel, as measured by a 6 m long pendulum, shows a deflection of **262 mm**. Calculate the metacentric height (GM) of the ship. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the equilibrium condition. A floating ship in still water has its weight Δ acting at G (downward) and buoyancy Δ acting at B (upward), both along the centerline. The ship is initially upright.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** A known test weight **w** is moved transversely by distance **d** across the deck. This creates a **Heeling Moment (HM)**:
$$\text{HM} = w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** As the ship heels to angle θ, the center of buoyancy shifts, and the ship's geometry produces a **Righting Lever (GZ)**. For small angles:
$$GZ = \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The corresponding **Righting Moment (RM)** is:
$$\text{RM} = \Delta \cdot \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** At static equilibrium, $\text{HM} = \text{RM}$:
$$w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta = \Delta \cdot \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Rearranging:
$$\text{GM} = \frac{w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta}{\Delta \cdot \sin\theta} = \frac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Assumptions:**
- The heel angle θ is small (< 7°), so $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (radians).
- Water density ρ is constant during the experiment.
- The ship is free from external forces (wind, mooring, current).
- The test weight is rigid and its CG is at a known fixed point.
- Deck deflection under the weight is negligible.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Δ = 20,000 t
- w (per weight) = 25 t, n = 4 weights
- Total test weight, W = 4 × 25 = 100 t
- d = 10 m
- Pendulum length, L = 6 m
- Deflection, s = 262 mm = 0.262 m

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate the heel angle θ from pendulum geometry:
$$\tan\theta = \frac{s}{L} = \frac{0.262}{6} = 0.04367$$

$$\theta = \arctan(0.04367) = 2.501°$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the GM formula:
$$\text{GM} = \frac{W \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$$

$$\text{GM} = \frac{100 \times 10}{20{,}000 \times 0.04367}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Numerator:
$$W \cdot d = 100 \times 10 = 1000 \text{ t·m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Denominator:
$$\Delta \cdot \tan\theta = 20{,}000 \times 0.04367 = 873.4 \text{ t·m}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final division:
$$\text{GM} = \frac{1000}{873.4} = 1.145 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Physical interpretation:
A GM of **1.145 m** is reasonable for a bulk carrier. Cargo loading computers will use this GM as a baseline.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final boxed answer:
$$\boxed{\text{GM} = 1.145 \text{ m}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure of conducting an inclining experiment on a ship. List the **precautions** that must be taken. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During an inclining experiment, a ship of displacement **8,500 tonnes** is shifted by moving two weights of **15 tonnes each** through a transverse distance of **8 m** to starboard, producing a heel of **1.8°**. The weights are then shifted back to port and produce a heel of **1.8°** in the opposite direction. A free surface effect of **0.05 m** is present in partially filled tanks. Calculate:
- (i) The uncorrected GM.
- (ii) The corrected GM after FSE. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Procedure + Precautions (7 marks):**

**Procedure (4 marks — minimum 4 steps, 1 mark each):**

1. **Preparation:** Ship brought to a quiet, sheltered berth. As much variable weight (fuel, water, stores) as possible is removed to approach lightship condition. All tanks are pressed up (filled) or emptied — partially filled tanks are avoided due to free surface effect.

2. **Mooring:** Mooring lines are slacked so the ship floats freely and is not constrained by asymmetric line tensions. Wind speed is measured (should be < 5 m/s).

3. **Instrumentation:** Two or more long pendulums (typically 6–10 m) are suspended inboard — usually two on port side and two on starboard side — with their tips just clearing a graduated baseline. A baseline reading is taken when the ship is upright.

4. **Test Weights:** Known solid weights (concrete blocks or water-filled tanks) are pre-positioned on one side of the ship at a precisely measured transverse distance d from the centerline. Their CG heights are also recorded.

5. **Execution:** The weights are shifted in a single, coordinated operation to the opposite side. The ship heels gradually and comes to a new equilibrium. The pendulum deflection is recorded after the ship has steadied. Drafts are read at all six marks before and after.

6. **Repetition:** The process is repeated with weights moved back to the original side, producing heel in the opposite direction. This cancels any residual list and validates symmetry.

7. **Analysis:** Deflection vs. moment is plotted, GM is calculated, and the deadweight survey gives the lightship weight.

**Precautions (3 marks — list at least 3, 1 mark each):**

- Avoid wind, current, and passing vessel effects (use a sheltered location).
- Eliminate or minimize free surface by pressing up all tanks.
- Ensure test weights are moved simultaneously and safely; weights must be secured to prevent shifting.
- Record all drafts accurately; account for water density.
- Verify pendulum length and baseline measurement precision.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Δ = 8,500 t
- Number of weights = 2, each w = 15 t
- Total weight moved, W = 30 t
- d = 8 m
- θ = 1.8° (heel to starboard), then 1.8° to port
- FSE = 0.05 m

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate $\tan(1.8°)$:
$$\tan(1.8°) = 0.03143$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Calculate the heeling moment:
$$\text{HM} = W \cdot d = 30 \times 8 = 240 \text{ t·m}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate **uncorrected GM** (using the compact formula):
$$\text{GM}_{\text{uncorr}} = \frac{\text{HM}}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta} = \frac{240}{8500 \times 0.03143}$$

$$\text{GM}_{\text{uncorr}} = \frac{240}{267.16} = 0.898 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the free surface correction:
Free surface effect **reduces** effective GM. The correction is **subtracted** from the uncorrected GM:
$$\text{GM}_{\text{corrected}} = \text{GM}_{\text{uncorr}} - \text{FSE}$$

$$\text{GM}_{\text{corrected}} = 0.898 - 0.05$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final result:
$$\boxed{\text{GM}_{\text{uncorrected}} = 0.898 \text{ m}, \quad \text{GM}_{\text{corrected}} = 0.848 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Note on symmetry: The fact that the heel is **identical in both directions** (1.8° P and 1.8° S) confirms that the ship had **no initial list** — the experiment is symmetric and reliable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2–4 marks here!)**
>
> 1. **Wrong angle in calculator:** Students often forget whether their calculator is in **DEG or RAD mode**. With θ = 2.5°, using RAD mode gives $\tan(2.5) = 0.0436$ correctly, but if you accidentally type `2.5` and the calculator is in DEG (which is the *correct* setting for the problem), the result is the same. **However**, if you convert 2.5° to radians (= 0.04363) and *then* the calculator is still in DEG, you get $\tan(0.04363) = 0.000436$ — wrong by a factor of 100. **Always confirm DEG mode.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting FSE in Part B:** Free Surface Effect is almost always present in real ships (partially filled fuel oil tanks, fresh water tanks). KTU expects you to **always subtract FSE** in stability calculations. Skipping this costs **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Units mismatch:** Displacement in **tonnes** but distance in **metres** — make sure GM comes out in **metres** consistently. Mixing tonnes-force and tonnes-mass will also cost marks.
>
> 4. **Pendulum derivation shortcut:** In Part A derivation, students often write $\text{GM} = w \cdot d / (\Delta \tan\theta)$ **without** showing the $\cos\theta/\sin\theta$ transition. You MUST show the full intermediate step. This is worth **1 mark** in the 7-mark derivation.
>
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** KTU examiners specifically look for the small-angle assumption in the derivation. Omitting it loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Inclining Experiment**

- [x] **Definition:** A practical test to find **GM** and **lightship weight** of a ship.
- [x] **Governed by:** IMO Resolution A.749(18) and SOLAS Chapter II-1.
- [x] **Core Formula (MUST memorize):** $\text{GM} = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot \tan\theta}$
- [x] **Units:** w in tonnes, d in metres, Δ in tonnes, θ in degrees, GM in metres.
- [x] **Typical Heel Range:** 1° to 4° (to stay within the linear GZ range).
- [x] **Key Instruments:** Pendulums (port & starboard), test weights, draft marks.
- [x] **Free Surface Effect:** Always subtract from uncorrected GM.
- [x] **Wind Limit:** Conduct when wind speed < 5 m/s if possible.
- [x] **Mooring:** All mooring lines must be **slack**.
- [x] **Ship Condition:** As close to **lightship** as practical.
- [x] **Displacement Correction:** Use **mean of port and starboard drafts**.
- [x] **Pendulum Geometry:** $\tan\theta = s / L$ where s = deflection, L = pendulum length.
- [x] **Righting Moment:** $\text{RM} = \Delta \cdot \text{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$
- [x] **Heeling Moment:** $\text{HM} = w \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$
- [x] **Output Document:** Stability Booklet + Form ISS-1 (or equivalent) submitted to Classification Society.
- [x] **Frequency:** Conducted **once** in a ship's life, unless major modifications occur.
- [x] **Symmetry Test:** Equal heel angle both ways confirms zero initial list.
- [x] **Two Methods Available:** (i) Moving weights, (ii) Removing weights — moving is preferred for safety.
- [x] **For Examiner:** Always convert small angles to **radians** if using the linear approximation $\sin\theta = \theta$.
- [x] **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Using **total displacement** instead of **lightship displacement** when calculating KG.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — Inclining Experiment | Module 3 | OESFT727 Maritime Safety**
> *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based education framework and follow the standard valuation key patterns used by KTU university examiners. For numerical practice, redo Worked Example 3.2 with shifted test weight values, and try Question B(b) with different displacement and FSE values to build fluency.*
