topics.title: Damage Stability and Survivability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Damage Stability and Survivability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Damage Stability** is the inherent capability of a vessel to remain afloat, stable, and operational when one or more of its watertight compartments have been breached and flooded following a casualty such as grounding, collision, or shell plating failure. It is a probabilistic, regulatory-driven engineering discipline governed by the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) Chapter II-1, Part B-1**, and is verified through a subdivision-and-load-line assessment of the intact ship.

**Survivability** is the broader operational outcome measured by the *Probability of Survival (Ps)* of the ship and its occupants after a damage event, integrating damage stability, subdivision arrangement, fire integrity, and life-saving appliance readiness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (Board-Exam Verbatim):**
> *"Damage stability is the ability of a damaged ship to provide a safe, stable, and habitable platform from which the crew may evacuate or, where necessary, survive without abandoning the vessel, evaluated against statutory criteria of residual buoyancy, residual metacentric height, heel angle, range of positive righting levers, and immersion of the margin line."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Watertight Honeycomb

Imagine a modern skyscraper built as a **honeycomb of sealed, pressurized rooms**. If a fire breaks out and destroys one room, the rest of the building remains upright and livable. A ship is engineered the same way. Its hull is divided by **transverse watertight bulkheads** into a chain of independent buoyancy chambers. The art of damage stability is choosing where to place those internal "walls" so that flooding any *reasonable* number of compartments does NOT capsize the vessel.

- The **ship's main body** = the building.
- Each **watertight compartment** = a sealed office.
- **Water ingress** = fire/destruction of one office.
- The **ship must not capsize** = the building must not topple.

The engineer therefore asks two questions:

1. *Where* to put the bulkheads? → **Subdivision Layout.**
2. *How stable will she be* after any single assumed casualty? → **Residual Stability Check.**

## 1.3 Fundamental Quantities and Standard Metrics

The following constants and dimensions are mandated by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and must be memorized verbatim for KTU valuation:

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|----------|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | Margin Line immersion limit | — | **Must remain above final waterline** |
| 2 | Permissible heel angle (passenger) | $\phi_{max}$ | **≤ 7°** in intermediate stage, **≤ 15°** final |
| 3 | Permissible heel angle (cargo) | $\phi_{max}$ | **≤ 15°** final, no immersion of margin line |
| 4 | Range of positive residual stability | $\theta_{range}$ | **≥ 20°** for cargo ships |
| 5 | Minimum residual GM | $GM_{min}$ | **≥ 0.05 m (50 mm)** |
| 6 | Required area under GZ curve | $\int GZ$ | **≥ 0.055 m·rad** up to 30° |
| 7 | Permeability (accommodation) | $\mu$ | **0.95** |
| 8 | Permeability (machinery) | $\mu$ | **0.85** |
| 9 | Permeability (dry cargo) | $\mu$ | **0.60** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Permeability ($\mu$)** is the fraction of a compartment's geometric volume that can actually be occupied by floodwater. A passenger cabin with soft furnishings absorbs almost no air, so $\mu \to 1.0$. An empty steel dry-cargo hold is mostly air, so $\mu \approx 0.60$. This single number drives nearly every damage-stability calculation.

## 1.4 Why This Module Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic contributes directly to:

- **CO3:** *Apply damage-control and survivability principles to ship design and emergency response.*
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply / Analyse (L3–L4).
- **Typical KTU mark weight:** **14 marks** in End Semester Examination, often appearing as a Part-B compulsory or internal-choice question.
- It is also a direct feeder topic for **Module 4 (Emergency Response and Abandon Ship)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Damage Stability

The IMO regulatory framework rests on **three sequential engineering checks** that every new build and every major conversion must satisfy:

### Pillar 1 — Probability of Survival After Flooding

The attained subdivision index $A$ must satisfy:

$$
A \geq R
$$

where $R$ is the required subdivision index. The ship is modelled as a chain of **flooding groups** $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$, and:

$$
A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i
$$

- $p_i$ = probability that flooding group $i$ is breached (depends on group length).
- $s_i$ = survival factor of group $i$ (0 to 1, based on residual GZ, heel, and range).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** The summation symbol $\sum p_i s_i$ is the *single most important equation* in Module 3. If you forget everything else, remember $A \geq R$.

### Pillar 2 — Required Subdivision Index $R$

For ships of length $L_s \leq 350$ m, IMO uses the **mathematical ship**:

$$
R = 1 - \frac{1}{3 \left( \dfrac{F}{B} + 2 \right)}
$$

For ships of length $L_s > 350$ m (or as per SOLAS II-1 Reg 6), the value is interpolated using the **Goff formula**:

$$
R = 1 - \frac{22}{19 + \dfrac{F}{B}}
$$

where:

- $F$ = final mean draft of the full-load condition.
- $B$ = moulded breadth.

### Pillar 3 — Survival Factor of Each Group $s_i$

For a given flooding group, the survival factor is computed from the **residual GZ curve** drawn after the damage is applied:

$$
s_i = K \cdot \left[ \dfrac{\text{Area under GZ from } 0° \text{ to } 30°}{0.055} + \dfrac{\text{Area from } 30° \text{ to } 40°}{0.090} + \dfrac{\text{Area from } 30° \text{ to } \theta_f}{0.030} \right]
$$

- $K$ = factor depending on ship length (typically 0.6 to 1.0).
- $\theta_f$ = angle of vanishing stability of the residual curve.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often mark a hard **zero** when students confuse the angle of vanishing stability $\theta_f$ with the angle of downflooding. They are *not* the same: $\theta_f$ is where the GZ curve crosses zero on the right; downflooding is the angle at which a vent or weathertight door is immersed.

## 2.2 Engineering Logic — Step-by-Step Rationale

Why three pillars? Because naval architecture recognises that safety is a **probabilistic**, not deterministic, property:

1. A bulkhead *can* be located perfectly for one casualty and fail completely for another. So we **integrate** over many possible breaches using $p_i$.
2. The *probability* of any single breach decreases as the compartment length decreases — hence the **$1 -$** term in $R$.
3. Each assumed breach must still leave the ship with **positive, usable righting energy** — hence $s_i$ is derived from the **area under the residual GZ curve**, not from a single GM value.

> [!NOTE]
> The genius of the probabilistic approach is that it allows the designer to **shift bulkhead positions** to optimise safety, even though no single damage case may be "perfect".

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $\mu = V_{water} / V_{compartment}$ | Permeability of flooded space | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\nabla_{flooded} = \mu \cdot \nabla_{compartment}$ | Lost buoyancy (volume of water entering) | m³ |
| 3 | $W_{flooded} = \rho g \mu V_{comp}$ | Added weight of floodwater (tonnes or N) | t / kN |
| 4 | $GM_{damaged} = KM_{damaged} - KG_{damaged}$ | Residual metacentric height | m |
| 5 | $KM_{damaged} = \dfrac{I_{T,damaged}}{\nabla_{damaged}}$ | Metacentric radius after damage (lost-buoyancy method) | m |
| 6 | $KM_{damaged} = \dfrac{I_{T,intact} - i \cdot v}{\nabla_{intact}}$ | Added-weight correction form | m |
| 7 | $i = b^{3} L / 12 V$ | Transverse second moment of flooded prism about its centre | m⁴ |
| 8 | $v = $ lost buoyancy volume | Volume of flooded space | m³ |
| 9 | $GG' = \dfrac{\rho g v \cdot \bar{KG}_{lost}}{W_{displacement}}$ | Virtual rise of G (free-surface effect negligible assumption) | m |
| 10 | $A = \sum p_i s_i$ | Attained subdivision index | dimensionless |
| 11 | $R = 1 - \dfrac{1}{3(F/B + 2)}$ | Required subdivision index ($L_s \leq 350$ m) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $R = 1 - \dfrac{22}{19 + F/B}$ | Goff formula ($L_s > 350$ m) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $s_i = f(\int GZ d\theta)$ | Survival factor (function of GZ area) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\phi_{heel} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{y}{GM_{damaged}}\right)$ | Heel angle from off-centre flooding | degrees |
| 15 | $h_{margin} = z_{margin} - z_{waterline,final}$ | Margin line freeboard (must be $\geq 0$) | m |

> [!CAUTION]
> In row 5 and 6, the moment of inertia $I_T$ refers to the **waterplane area of the intact ship**, not the damaged one. For an exact calculation, we approximate the damaged waterplane by removing the flooded rectangle. This is the most common source of valuation marks lost.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Damage-stability calculations directly feed:

- **Bulkhead schedule drafting** — the first drawing in any General Arrangement plan.
- **Loading-manual software** (e.g., LoadMaster, S&P Stability) — every loading condition is re-checked for damage stability if the vessel is a passenger ship.
- **Tonnage and subdivision load lines** — segregating the engine room from the collision bulkhead by at least 5% of $L_s$ or 10 m (whichever is less) per SOLAS II-1 Reg 12.
- **Damage-control posters** placed on every merchant vessel — the "floodable length diagram" is a direct product of this analysis.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Residual GM Using the Lost-Buoyancy Method

**Problem Setup.** A box-shaped vessel of length $L = 120$ m, breadth $B = 18$ m, draft $T = 6$ m is floating at even keel. The centre compartment of length $l = 12$ m and full breadth $B$ is breached and flooded with permeability $\mu = 0.60$. The vertical centre of buoyancy of the intact ship $KB = 3.0$ m. The transverse moment of inertia of the intact waterplane $I_T = 120{,}000$ m⁴. The vertical centre of gravity $KG = 5.0$ m. Calculate the residual metacentric height $GM_{damaged}$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Intact Displacement Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{intact} &= L \times B \times T \\
&= 120 \times 18 \times 6 \\
&= 12{,}960 \ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the volume of water displaced by the ship in her intact condition.

### Step 2 — Compute the Lost Buoyancy (Flooded Volume)

The floodwater occupies a rectangular prism of length $l$, breadth $B$, and height $T$ inside the centre compartment, but only the **permeability fraction** $\mu$ is actually filled:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \mu \cdot l \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 0.60 \times 12 \times 18 \times 6 \\
&= 777.6 \ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

This volume is "lost" as buoyancy because it can no longer support the ship's weight — it is replaced by floodwater of equal weight.

### Step 3 — Compute the Damaged Displacement Volume

For the lost-buoyancy (also called "constant-weight") method, we assume the ship weighs the same, so she must sink deeper to displace an *additional* volume equal to $v$. The new total displaced volume is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{damaged} &= \nabla_{intact} + v \\
&= 12{,}960 + 777.6 \\
&= 13{,}737.6 \ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Approximate the Damaged Waterplane and Its $I_T$

For a first-pass KTU calculation, we treat the **intact waterplane** as a rectangular plate of $L \times B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{T,intact} &= \dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} \\
&= \dfrac{120 \times 18^{3}}{12} \\
&= \dfrac{120 \times 5832}{12} \\
&= \dfrac{699{,}840}{12} \\
&= 58{,}320 \ \text{m}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

To be conservative, we use the intact $I_T$ in the damaged calculation (a slight over-estimate of stability), as the lost-buoyancy method assumes the ship re-equilibrates with a new waterplane only marginally different from the original.

### Step 5 — Compute the New Metacentric Radius $KM_{damaged}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
KM_{damaged} &= \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla_{damaged}} \\
&= \dfrac{58{,}320}{13{,}737.6} \\
&= 4.245 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Approximate the New Vertical Centre of Buoyancy $KB_{damaged}$

Because the added displaced volume sits roughly at mid-draft, the centre of buoyancy shifts slightly downward. A simple shift estimate is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
KB_{damaged} &\approx KB_{intact} \cdot \dfrac{\nabla_{intact}}{\nabla_{damaged}} \\
&= 3.0 \times \dfrac{12{,}960}{13{,}737.6} \\
&= 3.0 \times 0.9434 \\
&= 2.830 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Strictly, $KB_{damaged}$ is found by re-integrating the submerged volume about the new waterplane. For KTU board-level numericals, the proportional-shrinkage shortcut above is accepted.

### Step 7 — Compute the New Centre of Gravity

In the lost-buoyancy method, the ship's *weight* is unchanged, but we conceptually **add a virtual weight** of floodwater at the centroid of the flooded compartment. The new $KG$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
KG_{damaged} &= KG_{intact} + \dfrac{v \cdot \bar{z}_{flooded}}{\nabla_{damaged}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The centroid of the flooded box is at mid-draft = $T/2 = 3.0$ m above keel. The new $KG$ becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
KG_{damaged} &= 5.0 + \dfrac{777.6 \times 3.0}{13{,}737.6} \\
&= 5.0 + \dfrac{2332.8}{13{,}737.6} \\
&= 5.0 + 0.1698 \\
&= 5.170 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute the Residual Metacentric Height

$$
\begin{aligned}
GM_{damaged} &= KM_{damaged} - KG_{damaged} \\
&= 4.245 - 5.170 \\
&= -0.925 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Engineering Interpretation

A **negative** $GM_{damaged}$ means the ship **will capsize** under this single-compartment damage scenario. To pass the SOLAS residual-stability criteria, the designer must:

1. Reduce the length of the centre compartment (move bulkheads closer), or
2. Reduce the permeability (e.g., fit partial fillings), or
3. Lower $KG_{intact}$ by relocating heavy stores to double-bottom tanks, or
4. Increase $B$ (beam) of the ship.

> [!WARNING]
> A common board-mark error: students *forget* to add $v$ in the denominator of $KM_{damaged}$ and use $\nabla_{intact}$. Always re-read Step 3 and Step 5 before writing the final number.

## 3.2 Derivation — Probability of a Single-Compartment Flooding

The probability that a flooding group of length $L_{group}$ is breached is given by the **statistical ship-subdivision formula** derived from the experimental work of the late 1960s:

$$
p_{group} = 1 - \exp\left( -\dfrac{L_{group}}{L_0} \right)
$$

where $L_0$ is a characteristic length, approximately **85 m** for cargo ships and **45 m** for passenger ships. The **smaller** the group, the smaller the probability.

For two adjacent groups of lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$:

$$
p_{combined} = 1 - \exp\left( -\dfrac{L_1 + L_2}{L_0} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus requires you to **state** the form of $p_i$ and explain its inverse relationship with subdivision length. The numerical evaluation of $L_0$ is a 3-mark direct question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Damage Stability Check Flow

The table below summarises the analytical workflow a naval architect must execute for every flooding case:

| Step | Action | Input Data | Output / Pass Criterion |
|------|--------|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Define flooding case (e.g., side shell from frame 30 to 50) | GA plan, bulkhead schedule | Compartment identity |
| 2 | Compute lost buoyancy $v = \mu V_{comp}$ | Compartment volume, $\mu$ table | Volume in m³ |
| 3 | Add virtual weight = floodwater weight | $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Mass in tonnes |
| 4 | Re-trim ship (sinkage + trim) | Hydrostatic data | New draft $T_f$, trim |
| 5 | Re-compute $KB$ and $KG$ | Form coefficients, weight list | $KB_f$, $KG_f$ |
| 6 | Re-compute $KM$ using $I_{T,damaged}$ | Waterplane (with flooded region removed) | $KM_f$ |
| 7 | Check $GM_{damaged} \geq 0.05$ m | Above | Pass / Fail |
| 8 | Plot residual $GZ$ curve | Bonjean or $KN$ cross-curve | Righting lever vs. $\phi$ |
| 9 | Check heel angle at equilibrium | Static $GZ$ = 0 | $\phi_{eq} \leq 7°$ / $15°$ |
| 10 | Check area under $GZ$ from 0° to 30° | Numerical integration | $\geq 0.055$ m·rad |
| 11 | Check margin-line immersion | Final waterline vs. margin line | Margin $\geq 0$ |
| 12 | Compute $s_i$ from $GZ$ area | SOLAS Reg 7 | $s_i \in [0, 1]$ |
| 13 | Compute $p_i$ from group length | $L_0 = 85$ m or 45 m | $p_i$ |
| 14 | Sum $A = \sum p_i s_i$ | All groups | $A$ value |
| 15 | Compare $A \geq R$ | $R$ from Goff formula | Final Pass / Fail |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Damage Stability Assessment Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete procedural flow of a SOLAS damage-stability assessment as described in the analytical matrix of Section 3.3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Define flooding case] --> A2[Compute lost buoyancy v]
    A2 --> A3[Add virtual floodwater weight]
    A3 --> A4[Re-trim ship to new draft]
    A4 --> A5[Compute KB and KG damaged]
    A5 --> A6[Compute KM damaged]
    A6 --> A7{Check GM residual}
    A7 -- Pass --> A8[Plot GZ curve]
    A7 -- Fail --> A9[Move bulkheads or lower KG]
    A8 --> A10{Check heel angle}
    A10 -- Pass --> A11{Check margin line}
    A10 -- Fail --> A12[Re-design subdivision]
    A11 -- Pass --> A13[Compute survival factor s]
    A11 -- Fail --> A14[Increase freeboard or change load]
    A13 --> A15[Compute probability p from group length]
    A15 --> A16[Sum A equals sigma p sub i s sub i]
    A16 --> A17{Compare A versus R}
    A17 -- Pass --> A18[Comply SOLAS]
    A17 -- Fail --> A19[Re-optimise bulkhead layout]
    A9 --> A1
    A12 --> A1
    A14 --> A1
    A19 --> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows returning to $A_1$ at the bottom represent the **iterative nature** of damage-stability design. A ship is rarely compliant on the first pass; the bulkhead schedule is refined through 3–5 loops of this flowchart before approval by class.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of a Damage-Stability Calculation

This second diagram decomposes the *internal* components of the residual GZ calculation, which is the heart of Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Intact ship hydrostatics] --> B2[KM intact]
    B1 --> B3[KG intact]
    B4[Lost buoyancy volume v] --> B5[Virtual weight shift]
    B5 --> B6[New KG damaged]
    B2 --> B7[Damaged waterplane I sub T]
    B7 --> B8[KM damaged]
    B6 --> B9[GM damaged]
    B8 --> B9
    B9 --> B10[Build GZ curve vs heel]
    B10 --> B11[Area under GZ 0 to 30 deg]
    B10 --> B12[Angle of vanishing stability]
    B10 --> B13[Heel at static equilibrium]
    B11 --> B14[Survival factor s]
    B12 --> B14
    B13 --> B14
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Permeability Allocation on a Real Ship GA

The diagram below maps typical permeability values onto a generic General Arrangement, helping students visualise why a machinery space and a passenger cabin have different $\mu$ values.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Forepeak store: mu equals 0.60] --> P2[Number 1 cargo hold: mu equals 0.60]
    P2 --> P3[Crew accommodation: mu equals 0.95]
    P3 --> P4[Engine room: mu equals 0.85]
    P4 --> P5[Boiler / funnel space: mu equals 0.85]
    P5 --> P6[After store: mu equals 0.60]
    P6 --> P7[Aft peak tank: mu equals 0 or 0.95 depending on filling]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **aft peak tank** is given a *double* permeability value because it can be either kept empty ($\mu = 0$, no flooding) or completely full of seawater ($\mu = 0.95$, full flooding). Designers usually use the **worst-case** scenario for SOLAS verification.

## 4.4 Subdivision Plan Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Collision bulkhead] --> S2[Forepeak]
    S2 --> S3[Hold 1]
    S3 --> S4[Hold 2]
    S4 --> S5[Engine room]
    S5 --> S6[Hold 3]
    S6 --> S7[Aft peak]
    S7 --> S8[Stern frame]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The collision bulkhead is positioned at a distance $\geq 0.05 L_s$ or $10$ m, whichever is less, from the forward perpendicular per SOLAS II-1 Regulation 12. This is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU board exams.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Definition of Damage Stability

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q:** Define *damage stability* of a ship. State the four principal criteria a damaged ship must satisfy as per SOLAS Chapter II-1.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words):**

> **Damage stability** is the ability of a ship to remain afloat and stable in a predefined damaged condition, achieved by subdivision into watertight compartments.
>
> A damaged ship must satisfy:
> 1. Final waterline remains **below the margin line** (no progressive flooding).
> 2. Angle of heel $\phi \leq 7°$ (passenger) or $15°$ (cargo) at equilibrium.
> 3. Range of positive residual stability $\geq 20°$.
> 4. Minimum residual metacentric height $GM_{damaged} \geq 0.05$ m.

### Question 2 — Permeability

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** What is *permeability*? Give typical values for (i) passenger accommodation, (ii) machinery spaces, and (iii) dry cargo holds.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Permeability** $\mu$ is the ratio of the volume of floodwater that can enter a compartment to its total geometric volume.
>
> | Compartment | $\mu$ |
> |---|---|
> | Passenger accommodation | **0.95** |
> | Machinery spaces | **0.85** |
> | Dry cargo holds | **0.60** |
>
> The values reflect how much *void* exists in the compartment. A machinery space has lots of structure and air; a passenger cabin is densely packed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Internal Choice Option A]**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A bulk carrier has the following particulars in full-load condition:

- Length $L_{pp} = 150$ m, Moulded breadth $B = 22$ m, Draft $T = 8.5$ m.
- Mean draft at subdivision $F = 8.0$ m.
- The vessel is divided into 4 holds of lengths $L_1 = 35$ m, $L_2 = 30$ m, $L_3 = 30$ m, $L_4 = 35$ m.

Compute the **required subdivision index $R$** using the Goff formula, and the **attained subdivision index $A$** assuming the survival factor for each hold is $s_i = 0.85$ and the characteristic length $L_0 = 85$ m for cargo ships. State whether the vessel complies with SOLAS.

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Required Subdivision Index $R$**

For $L_s > 350$ m the Goff formula would apply, but for $L_{pp} = 150$ m, we use the standard formula for ships $\leq 350$ m:

$$
R = 1 - \dfrac{1}{3 \left( \dfrac{F}{B} + 2 \right)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{F}{B} &= \dfrac{8.0}{22} = 0.3636 \\[4pt]
R &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{3(0.3636 + 2)} \\[4pt]
  &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{3(2.3636)} \\[4pt]
  &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{7.0909} \\[4pt]
  &= 1 - 0.1410 \\[4pt]
  &= 0.859
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Probability of Each Hold**

Using $p = 1 - \exp(-L_{group}/L_0)$ with $L_0 = 85$ m:

- Hold 1: $p_1 = 1 - \exp(-35/85) = 1 - \exp(-0.4118) = 1 - 0.6625 = 0.3375$
- Hold 2: $p_2 = 1 - \exp(-30/85) = 1 - \exp(-0.3529) = 1 - 0.7025 = 0.2975$
- Hold 3: $p_3 = p_2 = 0.2975$ (by symmetry of length)
- Hold 4: $p_4 = p_1 = 0.3375$

**Step 3 — Compute the Attained Subdivision Index $A$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i \cdot s_i \\
  &= 0.3375 \cdot 0.85 + 0.2975 \cdot 0.85 + 0.2975 \cdot 0.85 + 0.3375 \cdot 0.85 \\
  &= 0.85 \cdot (0.3375 + 0.2975 + 0.2975 + 0.3375) \\
  &= 0.85 \cdot 1.2700 \\
  &= 1.0795
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compliance Check**

We require $A \geq R$:

$$
A = 1.0795 \quad \text{vs.} \quad R = 0.859
$$

$$
A > R \implies \textbf{Complies with SOLAS}
$$

The vessel is **safe from the subdivision standpoint**. However, the survival factor of 0.85 must still be verified from the residual GZ curve.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Explain with a neat sketch the **lost buoyancy method** of computing the residual metacentric height $GM_{damaged}$ of a ship after a side-compartment is breached. State the formula used and identify the principal source of error in the simple rectangular-prism approximation.

#### Model Solution (7 marks)

**Method Overview (2 marks):**

The lost-buoyancy method treats the ingress of water as an *equivalent loss* of supporting volume. The ship is conceptually re-weighed so that she must sink deeper to displace the original weight plus the floodwater weight. The damaged waterplane area is computed by **removing the flooded region** from the original waterplane.

**Sketch (3 marks — described in text since Mermaid is structural only):**

> A rectangular intact waterplane of area $L \times B$ is shown. A central rectangle of length $l$ and full breadth $B$ is shaded and labelled *Flooded Region*. The remaining two trapezoidal regions constitute the damaged waterplane. The principal axis of the waterplane shifts downward to a new centre $B'$.

**Governing Formula (1 mark):**

$$
GM_{damaged} = \dfrac{I_{T,damaged}}{\nabla_{damaged}} - KG_{damaged}
$$

where $\nabla_{damaged} = \nabla_{intact} + v$ and $I_{T,damaged} = I_{T,intact} - \delta I_T$, with $\delta I_T$ being the moment-of-inertia contribution of the removed flooded rectangle.

**Principal Source of Error (1 mark):**

> The simple rectangular-prism assumption ignores the **rise of the waterline above the original** (freeboard) and the **trim** caused by asymmetric flooding. The error grows rapidly when the damaged compartment occupies more than 25% of the ship's length, or when permeability exceeds 0.85. For KTU problems this error is acceptable only for short compartments with $\mu \leq 0.70$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Internal Choice Option B]**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** With the aid of a labelled diagram, describe the **three-compartment standard of subdivision** as applied to a passenger ship. Explain why this standard was historically chosen and state the conditions under which a **two-compartment standard** may be permitted by Class.

#### Model Solution

**Definition (2 marks):**

> The *three-compartment standard* requires that a passenger ship remain stable and afloat with any **three adjacent main watertight compartments** simultaneously flooded. This is the historical default applied to vessels of length $L_s \geq 91.5$ m carrying more than 400 passengers.

**Diagram Description (2 marks):**

> A ship shown in profile with bulkheads at frames A, B, C, D, E. The spaces [A–B], [B–C], [C–D], and [D–E] are marked as holds. The flooding case *A–B–C* (three consecutive holds) is shaded blue, with the new waterline drawn marginally below the margin line. The GZ curve residual to this case is annotated with $\phi_{eq} < 7°$ and a positive range of 22°.

**Historical Justification (2 marks):**

> Statistical analyses of major 20th-century maritime casualties (Titanic, 1912; Empress of Ireland, 1914; Andrea Doria, 1956) showed that the *most probable* catastrophic flooding event was the breach of **two or three** adjacent compartments due to a side-on collision. A three-compartment standard gives a survival probability of $> 0.97$ for ocean-going passenger ships.

**Conditions for Two-Compartment Standard (1 mark):**

> Class may permit a **two-compartment standard** for ships of shorter length (typically $L_s < 91.5$ m), with a reduced passenger count, and equipped with a **double bottom** of full depth extending from bilge to bilge. The vessel must also carry an approved *damage control plan* and *Egress Route Lighting*.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A ship of displacement 18,000 tonnes floats at a draft of 7.2 m. The transverse metacentric height is $GM = 0.80$ m. A midship compartment of length 20 m, breadth 18 m and full height equal to the draft is flooded with permeability 0.65.

Compute:

(i) The lost buoyancy $v$.
(ii) The damaged displacement volume $\nabla_{damaged}$.
(iii) The new metacentric radius $KM_{damaged}$ if $I_{T,intact} = 95{,}000$ m⁴.

#### Model Solution

**(i) Lost Buoyancy (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \mu \cdot l \cdot B \cdot T \\
  &= 0.65 \times 20 \times 18 \times 7.2 \\
  &= 0.65 \times 2592 \\
  &= 1684.8 \ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Damaged Displacement Volume (2 marks):**

The intact displacement volume is $W / (\rho g) = 18{,}000 / 1.025 = 17{,}561$ m³.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{damaged} &= \nabla_{intact} + v \\
                 &= 17{,}561 + 1684.8 \\
                 &= 19{,}245.8 \ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Metacentric Radius Damaged (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KM_{damaged} &= \dfrac{I_{T,intact}}{\nabla_{damaged}} \\
             &= \dfrac{95{,}000}{19{,}245.8} \\
             &= 4.936 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation (carried over for residual $GM$ calculation):**

If the intact $KG = 6.40$ m and the flooded centroid is at $T/2 = 3.6$ m:

$$
KG_{damaged} = 6.40 + \dfrac{1684.8 \times 3.6}{19{,}245.8} = 6.40 + 0.315 = 6.715 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
GM_{damaged} = 4.936 - 6.715 = -1.779 \ \text{m}
$$

> The negative residual $GM$ indicates capsize risk. The bulkhead spacing of 20 m is **too large** for this vessel's stability envelope, and the designer must insert an additional bulkhead to break the centre compartment into two.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Frequently Lose Marks in Module 3 — Damage Stability**
>
> 1. **Confusing $A$ and $R$ (3 marks lost):** $A$ is the *attained* index (sum of $p_i s_i$); $R$ is the *required* index (Goff formula). Many students write $A = R$ as a formula — it is an *inequality* $A \geq R$.
> 2. **Forgetting permeability (4 marks lost):** When the question states "compartment flooded with permeability 0.85", you *must* multiply the geometric volume by 0.85. A 0.85-fold error propagates linearly into $GM$ and $A$.
> 3. **Using intact $I_T$ without comment (2 marks lost):** Always state "approximating the damaged waterplane by the intact one" if you do so. Examiners reward method awareness.
> 4. **Mixing degrees and radians in GZ areas (2 marks lost):** The GZ area criteria (0.055 m·rad, 0.090 m·rad) are in **radians**. Convert heel angles accordingly: $1° = 0.01745$ rad.
> 5. **Omitting the static equilibrium heel check (3 marks lost):** Even if $GM > 0.05$ m, the ship may heel $> 15°$ if the flooding is off-centre. Always state this check.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Damage Stability and Survivability: Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Definition (1-liner):** Damage stability = ability of a breached ship to remain afloat and stable; Survivability = broader probability of ship and crew surviving a casualty.
> - **Permeability values (memorise):** Accommodation 0.95, Machinery 0.85, Dry Cargo 0.60.
> - **Key inequality:** $A = \sum p_i s_i \geq R$, where $R = 1 - \dfrac{1}{3(F/B + 2)}$ for $L_s \leq 350$ m.
> - **SOLAS survival criteria (4 items):** Margin line not immersed; $\phi_{eq} \leq 15°$ (7° for passengers); range of positive stability $\geq 20°$; $GM_{damaged} \geq 0.05$ m.
> - **GZ area criteria:** $\int_0^{30°} GZ \, d\theta \geq 0.055$ m·rad and $\int_0^{40°} GZ \, d\theta \geq 0.090$ m·rad.
> - **Probability formula:** $p_i = 1 - \exp(-L_{group}/L_0)$ with $L_0 = 85$ m (cargo) or 45 m (passenger).
> - **Lost-buoyancy equation:** $\nabla_{damaged} = \nabla_{intact} + v$, with $v = \mu \cdot V_{compartment}$.
> - **Residual $GM$ formula:** $GM_{damaged} = I_{T,damaged} / \nabla_{damaged} - KG_{damaged}$.
> - **Three-compartment standard:** Default for ocean-going passenger ships with $L_s \geq 91.5$ m and $> 400$ pax.
> - **Two-compartment standard:** Permitted for short or small pax vessels with double bottom and approved damage-control plan.
> - **Collision bulkhead position:** $\geq 0.05 L_s$ or $10$ m (whichever is less) abaft the forward perpendicular.
> - **Iteration mindset:** Damage-stability design is a 3–5 iteration loop, not a one-shot calculation.
> - **Units vigilance:** Areas under $GZ$ in **m·rad**, not m·deg. Heel angles often in degrees but convert before integration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Materials and Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Marine Materials and Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Marine Materials — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Materials** are engineering substances specifically selected, alloyed, coated, or composited to withstand the aggressive *in-service* conditions of a marine environment — namely **saltwater (NaCl ~3.5%)**, **high humidity (>80% RH)**, **UV radiation**, **biofouling**, **cyclic wet–dry tidal action**, **temperature gradients**, and **mechanical loading (wave slamming, vibration)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the study of marine materials is inseparable from **corrosion science**, because the very purpose of material selection, alloying, and surface engineering in a ship/vessel/offshore platform is to **mitigate the electrochemical, chemical, and biological degradation** of the parent material.

The four broad families of marine materials studied in this module are:

1. **Ferrous alloys** — mild steel, high-tensile steel, low-alloy shipbuilding steels (e.g., AH36, DH36 grades per **ASTM A131**).
2. **Non-ferrous metals & alloys** — aluminum alloys (5083, 5086, 5456), copper–nickel (90/10, 70/30), brass, bronze, titanium.
3. **Stainless steels** — austenitic (e.g., 316L), duplex, super-duplex.
4. **Non-metallic composites** — Fibre-Reinforced Plastics (FRP/GRP), Glass-Reinforced Epoxy (GRE), marine plywood, rubber/elastomers, ceramics.

## 1.2 Corrosion — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Corrosion** is the *physico-chemical interaction* (typically electrochemical in nature for metals in a marine electrolyte) between a metal and its environment, resulting in the *progressive deterioration* of the metal's mechanical, physical, and chemical properties. The governing standard reference is **ISO 8049 / NACE / ASTM G46** for marine corrosion.

The governing chemical reaction for iron in aerated seawater is:

$$4Fe + 3O_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 4Fe(OH)_3 \quad (\text{rust})$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Rusty Iron Bridge"**:
> Imagine you leave a brand-new iron nail on the beach. Within days, it sprouts orange-brown rust, becomes flaky, and eventually crumbles. Why? Because the ocean is essentially a giant **saltwater battery**. Every metal in contact with this electrolyte behaves like the two terminals of a battery — one area becomes the *anode* (sacrificial, dissolves) and another becomes the *cathode* (protected). The salt (NaCl) acts as a *highway* for charged ions to flow, dramatically **accelerating** the rusting process compared to freshwater. Marine materials science is simply the engineering art of **choosing the right metal (or coating it) so this battery effect is either slowed down, redirected, or completely neutralized**.

## 1.4 The Marine Environment — Severity Factors

| Environmental Factor | Typical Value | Effect on Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| **Chloride concentration** | ~19,000 ppm (3.5% NaCl) | Destroys passive oxide films; promotes pitting |
| **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** | 6–8 mg/L | **Cathodic reactant**; rate-limiting in many cases |
| **Temperature** | 0–30 °C (surface seawater) | Doubles corrosion rate per ~10 °C rise (Arrhenius) |
| **pH** | 7.8–8.3 (slightly alkaline) | Mildly protective for steel |
| **Flow velocity** | 0–5 m/s (varies) | Erosion–corrosion above 3 m/s for Cu-alloys |
| **Biofouling** | Microbial slime, barnacles | Creates **differential aeration cells** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Takeaway**: Seawater is roughly **50–100× more corrosive** than freshwater because chloride ions (Cl⁻) penetrate the passive oxide film on stainless steel and aluminum, **initiating pitting corrosion** even on materials that are "rust-proof" in air.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Mechanism of Marine Corrosion

Marine corrosion of a metal **M** in aerated seawater is a **galvanic (electrochemical) process** involving **two simultaneous half-reactions** that must occur at the **same rate** to preserve charge neutrality:

### Step 1 — Anodic (Oxidation) Reaction
The metal dissolves, releasing electrons:
$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$$

*For iron:* $\quad Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$

### Step 2 — Cathodic (Reduction) Reaction
In **aerated** seawater, the dominant cathodic reaction is oxygen reduction:
$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

In **de-aerated** (deep ocean / sediment) conditions:
$$2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$$

### Step 3 — Product Formation
The metal ions and hydroxide ions migrate and form insoluble hydroxide/oxide:
$$Fe^{2+} + 2OH^- \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2 \quad (\text{greenish})$$
$$4Fe(OH)_2 + O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 4Fe(OH)_3 \quad (\text{orange rust})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why'**: Corrosion cannot occur without **all four** components — (1) an anode, (2) a cathode, (3) an electrolyte (seawater), and (4) an electrical connection between anode and cathode. Remove any one — and corrosion stops. This is the foundational principle behind **cathodic protection, coatings, and material selection**.

## 2.2 Eight Primary Forms of Marine Corrosion (KTU High-Yield Classification)

1. **Uniform (General) Corrosion** — even metal loss over the entire exposed surface; predictable; mitigated by coatings and **corrosion allowance** in scantlings.
2. **Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion** — dissimilar metals in electrical contact in an electrolyte (e.g., copper pipe + steel hull via a brass valve).
3. **Pitting Corrosion** — localized, deep, narrow cavities; extremely destructive; governed by **ASTM G46**.
4. **Crevice Corrosion** — under gaskets, washers, barnacle bases, lap joints; differential aeration mechanism.
5. **Intergranular Corrosion** — attack along grain boundaries (e.g., sensitized 304 stainless steel); mitigated by **low-carbon (L) grades** like 316L.
6. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — tensile stress + corrosive environment; e.g., **caustic embrittlement** in boilers, **chloride SCC** in austenitic SS.
7. **Erosion–Corrosion** — mechanical wear + corrosion; high-velocity seawater on condenser tubes.
8. **Cavitation & Fretting Corrosion** — bubble-collapse damage and micro-motion at bolted joints.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following equations are **must-know** for KTU numerical and derivations. Memorize units — unit mistakes are the #1 mark-loser in board valuation.

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's Law of Electrolysis** (mass lost, *m*) | kg |
| 2 | $\text{CR} = \frac{K \cdot I_{\text{corr}} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate from polarization | mm/yr |
| 3 | $\text{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Mils per year (US unit) | mils/yr |
| 4 | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log_{10}Q$ | **Nernst Equation** (electrode potential) | Volts |
| 5 | $\Delta V = E_c - E_a$ | Galvanic corrosion driving potential | Volts |
| 6 | $R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ | **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)** | $\Omega \cdot cm^2$ |
| 7 | $\text{Alloy Efficiency} = \frac{W_{\text{sacrificial}}}{W_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Sacrificial anode utilization | % |
| 8 | $\text{Protection} = \frac{\text{Area coated}}{\text{Total area}} \times 100$ | Coating coverage | % |

**Where:**
- $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic weight (kg/mol), $n$ = valence, $F$ = **96,485 C/mol (Faraday's constant)**
- $EW$ = equivalent weight, $\rho$ = density (g/cm³)
- $W$ = mass loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (in²), $T$ = time (hr)
- $E^{\circ}$ = standard electrode potential, $Q$ = reaction quotient

## 2.4 Cathodic Protection — The Engineering Utility

Since corrosion requires an anode, **cathodic protection (CP)** forces the **entire structure to become the cathode** of an electrochemical cell, eliminating anodic dissolution.

**Two Methods:**
- **Sacrificial Anode CP (Galvanic)** — attach a more active metal (Zn, Al, Mg) which preferentially corrodes; used for small hulls, ballast tanks.
- **Impressed Current CP (ICCP)** — external DC power source with inert anodes (Pt-coated Ti, mixed-metal-oxide); used for **large vessels and offshore platforms** because output is adjustable.

## 2.5 Galvanic Series in Seawater (Engineering Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: This is the **practical** galvanic series (in flowing seawater), **not** the standard EMF series. The practical series accounts for oxide films, kinetics, and real seawater conditions — and is what KTU examiners expect.

| Position | Material | Practical Potential (V vs Ag/AgCl) |
|---|---|---|
| Most Active (Anode) | Magnesium (Mg) | $-1.60$ |
| ↓ | Zinc (Zn) | $-1.10$ |
| ↓ | Aluminum alloy (5052) | $-0.95$ |
| ↓ | Mild Steel | $-0.75$ |
| ↓ | Cast Iron | $-0.70$ |
| ↓ | Lead (Pb) | $-0.55$ |
| ↓ | Copper–Nickel (70/30) | $-0.30$ |
| ↓ | Copper (Cu) | $-0.28$ |
| ↓ | Nickel Aluminum Bronze | $-0.22$ |
| ↓ | Monel (Ni–Cu) | $-0.20$ |
| ↓ | 316 Stainless Steel (passive) | $-0.10$ |
| ↓ | Titanium | $-0.05$ |
| Most Noble (Cathode) | Graphite / Pt | $+0.10$ to $+0.30$ |

> **Rule**: *The further apart two metals sit in the series, the greater the galvanic corrosion risk when they are coupled in seawater.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law Applied to Corrosion Mass Loss

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why'**: This derivation is the cornerstone of all KTU numerical problems on corrosion. It connects measurable electrical quantities (current, time) to the actual mass of metal lost — and hence the design **corrosion allowance** in ship scantlings.

### Starting Point — The Definition of Current and the Mole

Electric current $I$ is the rate of charge flow. In time $t$, total charge $Q$ passed is:

$$Q = I \cdot t \quad [\text{Coulombs, C}]$$

The Faraday constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol represents the charge carried by **one mole of electrons** ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ electrons × $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C).

**Step 1 — Moles of electrons transferred.**
The number of moles of electrons involved in the corrosion process is therefore:

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F} \quad [\text{mol } e^-]$$

**Step 2 — Convert moles of electrons to moles of metal atoms.**

Each metal atom of valence $n$ releases $n$ electrons. Hence moles of metal dissolved:

$$n_{M} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \quad [\text{mol of } M]$$

**Step 3 — Convert moles of metal to mass.**

Multiplying by atomic mass $M_a$ (g/mol):

$$m = n_M \cdot M_a = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_a}{n \cdot F} \quad [\text{grams}]$$

**Step 4 — Final Faraday's Law for Corrosion:**

$$\boxed{\,m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_a}{n \cdot F}\,}$$

### Worked Numerical (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]: A steel plate of area 0.5 m² corrodes in seawater. A current density of $0.15$ mA/cm² is measured. Find the **mass loss in grams over 30 days**. Given: $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**Step 1 — Convert area to cm²:**
$$A = 0.5 \, m^2 \times 10{,}000 = 5{,}000 \, cm^2$$

**Step 2 — Total current:**
$$I = i \cdot A = 0.15 \times 10^{-3} \, A/cm^2 \times 5{,}000 \, cm^2 = 0.75 \, A$$

**Step 3 — Total time in seconds:**
$$t = 30 \times 24 \times 3{,}600 = 2{,}592{,}000 \, s$$

**Step 4 — Apply Faraday's Law:**
$$m = \frac{0.75 \times 2{,}592{,}000 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$m = \frac{1.0854 \times 10^8}{1.9297 \times 10^5} = 562.6 \, g$$

**Result:** $\boxed{m \approx 562.6 \text{ grams of iron lost in 30 days}}$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Corrosion Rate (mm/year) from Mass Loss

The engineering community expresses corrosion rate in **mm/yr** (millimeters per year) so designers can directly calculate the **corrosion allowance** added to a structural plate's thickness.

**Step 1 — Convert mass loss to volume loss** (using density $\rho$):
$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} \quad [cm^3]$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume loss to linear thickness loss** (using exposed area $A$):
$$\text{Thickness loss} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A} \quad [cm]$$

**Step 3 — Convert cm to mm and seconds to year:**
$$1 \, cm = 10 \, mm, \quad 1 \, year = 365.25 \times 24 \times 3{,}600 = 3.156 \times 10^7 \, s$$

$$\boxed{\,CR \left(\frac{mm}{yr}\right) = \frac{m \times 10 \times 3.156 \times 10^7}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t} = \frac{3.156 \times 10^8 \cdot m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nernst Equation (Electrode Potential in Real Conditions)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why'**: Engineers rarely deal with *standard* conditions. Real seawater has variable ion concentrations, pH, and temperature. The Nernst equation converts standard potentials $E^{\circ}$ to actual in-service potential $E$, allowing accurate prediction of galvanic couples.

For a generic redox reaction:
$$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD + ne^-$$

The Nernst equation is derived from the **Gibbs free energy–potential relationship** $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$:

**Step 1 — Equate the two expressions of $\Delta G$:**
$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2 — Divide by $-nF$:**
$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 3 — Convert natural log to base-10 (using $2.303$):**
$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, and $T = 298$ K (standard):**
$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96{,}485} = 0.05916 \approx 0.0592$$

$$\boxed{\,E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q\,}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for Marine Applications

| Ship/Vessel Component | Recommended Material | Justification | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hull plates (large ships) | Mild steel with epoxy anti-fouling | Cost-effective, weldable, predictable uniform corrosion | High |
| Superstructure | AH36/DH36 high-tensile steel | Higher strength-to-weight, IACS UR S6 compliant | High |
| Propellers | Manganese bronze / Ni-Al bronze | Cavitation resistance, biofouling tolerance | High |
| Sea-water piping (main) | 90/10 Cu–Ni (ASTM B466) | Excellent seawater resistance, erosion resistant up to 3 m/s | Very High |
| Heat exchangers / condensers | 70/30 Cu–Ni or Titanium | Handles higher velocity, biofilm resistance | High |
| Small craft hulls | Aluminum 5083/5086 | Lightweight, marine-grade, weldable | Medium |
| Offshore platform splash zone | Duplex / Super-duplex SS | High Cl⁻ SCC resistance, strength | High |
| Internal ballast tank | Mild steel + thick epoxy + CP | Cost-effective, easy to coat | High |
| Outboard motor casings | Al 356 + hard anodizing | Lightweight, cosmetic, salt-resistant | Low |
| Radar mast / electronic enclosures | 316L Stainless | Atmospheric corrosion resistance, low magnetic | Medium |
| Submarine pressure hull | HY-80 / HY-100 steel | Yield strength, fracture toughness | Medium |
| Rudder / shaft | Monel K-500 (Ni–Cu–Al) | Strength + seawater immunity | Low |
| Decks (wood-look) | Marine plywood + phenolic resin | Anti-slip, dimensional stability | Low |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Fouling vs. Anti-Corrosion Coatings — KTU Pitfall**: Students frequently confuse these. **Anti-corrosion coatings** (e.g., epoxy primers, zinc-rich primers) block electrolyte access. **Anti-fouling coatings** (e.g., cuprous oxide + biocide, Tributyltin-free) prevent biofouling organisms from attaching. A hull uses **BOTH systems in layers**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Electrochemical Corrosion Cell in a Steel Hull

The following Mermaid block renders the **charge flow** and **mass flow** of a galvanic corrosion cell formed when a copper alloy valve is fitted to a steel hull — a classic KTU conceptual question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["STEEL HULL PLATE\nANODE REGION\nFe Fe Fe"]:::anodeNode
    A2["Fe atoms\noxidize to Fe2+"]:::reactNode
    A3["Fe2+ enters\nseawater electrolyte"]:::reactNode
    A4["ELECTRONS\nflow through metal\ntoward cathode"]:::electronNode
    B1["COPPER ALLOY VALVE\nCATHODE REGION"]:::cathodeNode
    B2["O2 + 2H2O\naccepts electrons"]:::reactNode
    B3["OH- ions form\nin electrolyte"]:::reactNode
    C1["IONIC PATH\nNa+ Cl- in seawater"]:::elecNode
    C2["Fe OH 2\naccumulates as rust"]:::prodNode

    A1 -- "MASS FLOW" --> A2 --> A3
    A3 --> C2
    A1 -- "e- flow" --> A4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    B3 -- "ion path" --> C1 --> A3

    classDef anodeNode fill:#FFD2D2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cathodeNode fill:#D2E5FF,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reactNode fill:#FFF5D2,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef electronNode fill:#E1D2FF,stroke:#4527A0,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFF
    classDef elecNode fill:#D2FFE2,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef prodNode fill:#FFDEE2,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Functional Architecture of Marine Corrosion Protection Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEEL HULL OR\nOFFSHORE STRUCTURE"]:::structNode
    S2["LAYER 1\nZinc rich primer\nsacrificial protection"]:::coatNode
    S3["LAYER 2\nEpoxy intermediate\nbarrier coating"]:::coatNode
    S4["LAYER 3\nAnti fouling topcoat\nbiocide release"]:::coatNode
    S5["CATHODIC PROTECTION\nImpressed current DC\nor zinc anode blocks"]:::cpNode
    S6["CORROSION ALLOWANCE\nExtra plate thickness\nper class rules"]:::allowNode
    S7["PROTECTED HULL\nservice life 25 plus years"]:::outNode

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    S1 --> S5
    S1 --> S6
    S2 --> S7
    S3 --> S7
    S4 --> S7
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7

    classDef structNode fill:#D2D2FF,stroke:#1A237E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef coatNode fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef cpNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef allowNode fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Decision Tree for Material Selection in a Marine Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Component to be\ndesigned"]:::qNode
    Q2{"Is weight\ncritical?"}:::qNode
    Q3["Use ALUMINUM\n5083 or COMPOSITE"]:::matNode
    Q4{"Is it immersed\nin seawater?"}:::qNode
    Q5["Use COPPER NICKEL\n90 10 or TITANIUM"]:::matNode
    Q6["Use MILD STEEL\nwith COATING and CP"]:::matNode
    Q7{"Will it face\nCl- SCC risk?"}:::qNode
    Q8["Use DUPLEX\nor 316L stainless"]:::matNode
    Q9["Use MILD STEEL\nwith epoxy paint"]:::matNode

    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3
    Q2 -- NO --> Q4
    Q4 -- YES --> Q5
    Q4 -- NO --> Q7
    Q7 -- YES --> Q8
    Q7 -- NO --> Q9

    classDef qNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef matNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Board Exams**: When a KTU question shows a galvanic cell diagram, always label — (1) Anode (oxidation, mass loss), (2) Cathode (reduction, protected), (3) Electron flow direction (in metal), and (4) Ion flow direction (in electrolyte). Examiners award **1 mark per correctly labelled item** in 7-mark sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define pitting corrosion. Why is it considered more dangerous than uniform corrosion in marine service?**

**Model Answer:**
Pitting corrosion is a **localized form of attack** that produces small cavities or "pits" on a metal surface, often with the rest of the surface remaining nearly unaffected. The mechanism involves **autocatalytic** destruction of the passive oxide film by chloride ions (Cl⁻), leading to an anode inside the pit and a large cathode on the surrounding passive area.

It is more dangerous than uniform corrosion because:
1. **Difficult to detect** — pits are small at the surface but penetrate deeply; visual inspection often misses them.
2. **High local penetration rate** — the small anode : large cathode ratio drives intense localized current.
3. **Stress concentrators** — pits act as crack initiation sites leading to fatigue or SCC failure.
4. **Catastrophic failure** — a single pit can perforate a pressure vessel or pipe wall suddenly.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Distinguish between Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) and Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SACP (Galvanic) | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Self-driven (galvanic potential) | External DC rectifier |
| Anode material | Zn, Al, Mg alloys | Pt/Ti, MMO, graphite, scrap steel |
| Current output | Low (limited by alloy) | High and adjustable |
| Typical application | Small craft, short-term, internal tanks | Large hulls, offshore platforms, jetties |
| Installation cost | Low | High |
| Monitoring | Rarely needed | Continuous reference electrode monitoring |
| Design life | 5–10 years (anode consumption) | 20+ years |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the mechanism of galvanic corrosion in a marine environment. With a neat labelled diagram, describe the role of the electrolyte, anode, cathode, and electron flow. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Galvanic corrosion is the accelerated corrosion of a metal when it is electrically coupled to a *more noble* metal in the presence of an electrolyte (seawater).

**Mechanism (4 marks):** When two dissimilar metals are placed in electrical contact in seawater:
- The metal with the **more negative (active) potential** becomes the **anode** and undergoes oxidation: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$
- The **more noble** metal becomes the **cathode** and supports the oxygen reduction reaction: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$
- The seawater acts as the **electrolyte** completing the ionic circuit. **Chloride ions** increase the conductivity, accelerating the corrosion.
- The corrosion rate of the anode is proportional to the **potential difference** ($\Delta E$) between the two metals and inversely proportional to the **anode-to-cathode area ratio**. A small anode coupled to a large cathode is the worst case (e.g., a bronze propeller in a steel hull is fine, but a small steel bolt in a large copper plate is catastrophic).

**Prevention (1 mark):** (i) Use similar metals, (ii) electrically isolate dissimilar metals with insulating gaskets, (iii) use sacrificial anodes, (iv) apply protective coatings, (v) keep anode area larger than cathode area in any unavoidable couple.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the labelled Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 — must show the four essential components (anode, cathode, electrolyte, metallic path) and **arrow directions for electrons and ions**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A steel plate of area 200 cm² is exposed to seawater. A polarization test gives a corrosion current density of 0.20 mA/cm². Calculate (i) the total corrosion current, (ii) the mass of iron corroded in 10 days, and (iii) the corrosion rate in mm/yr. Given: $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total corrosion current (1 mark):**
$$I = i \times A = 0.20 \times 10^{-3} \, A/cm^2 \times 200 \, cm^2 = 0.040 \, A = 40 \, mA$$

**Step 2 — Time in seconds (1 mark):**
$$t = 10 \times 24 \times 3{,}600 = 864{,}000 \, s$$

**Step 3 — Mass of iron corroded (Faraday's Law) (2 marks):**
$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_a}{n \cdot F} = \frac{0.040 \times 864{,}000 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$
$$m = \frac{1{,}930{,}656}{192{,}970} = 10.005 \, g$$

**Result:** $\boxed{m \approx 10.0 \text{ g of iron lost in 10 days}}$

**Step 4 — Corrosion rate in mm/yr (3 marks):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2:
$$CR = \frac{3.156 \times 10^8 \cdot m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$$

$$CR = \frac{3.156 \times 10^8 \times 10.005}{7.87 \times 200 \times 864{,}000}$$

$$CR = \frac{3.158 \times 10^9}{1.3597 \times 10^9} = 2.32 \, mm/yr$$

**Result:** $\boxed{CR \approx 2.32 \text{ mm/year}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — most common mark-loser. Students forget to convert mA to A, days to seconds, or cm² to m². Show every conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting valence $n$** — always state the oxidation state of the corroding metal in your answer. For iron in oxygenated seawater, $n = 2$ (Fe²⁺), not 3.
> 3. **Confusing mm/yr and mpy (mils per year)** — 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr. KTU may ask either; read the unit in the question carefully.
> 4. **Density unit** — must be in **g/cm³**, not kg/m³. Convert before substituting.
> 5. **No diagram marks lost** — Part (a) of Question A carries a **dedicated 1 mark for the labelled diagram**. Drawing a *labelled* galvanic cell with arrows is non-negotiable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the major types of corrosion encountered in marine service. Explain in detail pitting corrosion, crevice corrosion, and stress corrosion cracking. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Pitting Corrosion (2 marks):** Localized attack producing deep, narrow cavities. **Mechanism**: Cl⁻ ions penetrate the passive oxide film at weak points (inclusions, scratches); a micro-anode forms inside the pit, the rest acts as cathode. The pit interior becomes acidic (autocatalytic) due to metal chloride hydrolysis. Common in **stainless steels and aluminum alloys** in seawater.

**2. Crevice Corrosion (2 marks):** Occurs in stagnant micro-environments such as under gaskets, washers, lap joints, and fouling deposits. **Differential aeration cell** forms: oxygen is depleted inside the crevice (anode), and the well-aerated exterior acts as cathode. Cl⁻ ions migrate into the crevice, accelerating the attack. Mitigated by **welded joints, sealed gaskets, and drainage design**.

**3. Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC) (2 marks):** Simultaneous action of **tensile stress** (residual or applied) and a **specific corrosive environment** causes brittle cracking. Examples: **chloride SCC** in austenitic SS above 60 °C; **caustic embrittlement** in boiler riveted seams. **Prevention**: use low-carbon grades (316L), stress-relief heat treatment, avoid chloride-rich stagnant conditions.

**Diagrammatic representation (1 mark):** Cross-section of a pit showing anode/cathode regions; crevice showing oxygen-depleted zone; SCC branching cracks from the surface.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the various corrosion protection methods used for marine structures. Compare cathodic protection methods with surface coatings. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Cathodic Protection (3 marks):**
- **Sacrificial Anode CP (SACP):** Attaches a more active metal (Zn, Al, Mg) to the protected structure. The anode corrodes preferentially. Simple, no power needed, low cost. Limited current and life.
- **Impressed Current CP (ICCP):** Uses an external DC power source with inert anodes. Higher, adjustable current; suitable for **large structures, offshore platforms, and ship hulls** with high current demand. Requires reference electrodes (Ag/AgCl, Zn) for monitoring.

**2. Surface Coatings (2 marks):**
- **Barrier coatings** (epoxy, polyurethane): physically block electrolyte ingress.
- **Zinc-rich primers**: provide *active* sacrificial protection even if scratched.
- **Anti-fouling topcoats**: prevent biofouling (TBT-free cuprous oxide or silicone-based).
- **Coating systems are multi-layer** (primer + intermediate + topcoat) per **ISO 12944** standard.

**3. Comparison (2 marks):**

| Feature | Cathodic Protection | Coatings |
|---|---|---|
| Active vs Passive | Active (sacrificial or driven) | Passive (barrier) |
| Lifespan | 10–25 years (with anode replacement) | 5–15 years (needs recoating) |
| Best for | Submerged, buried, splash zone | Atmospheric, above water |
| Cost | Moderate–High (ICCP) | Lower per m² |
| Failure mode | Anode consumption | Mechanical damage, blistering |
| Combined use | **Yes** — standard practice is to apply CP **under** a coating | |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Marine Materials & Corrosion**

- 🔹 **Marine environment is ~50–100× more corrosive** than freshwater due to **Cl⁻** ions.
- 🔹 **Corrosion requires 4 components**: anode + cathode + electrolyte + metallic connection — remove any one to stop it.
- 🔹 **Anode = site of oxidation** (mass loss); **Cathode = site of reduction** (protected); always check the **galvanic series in seawater**, not the standard EMF table.
- 🔹 **Pitting is the most dangerous** form — small area, deep penetration, hard to detect.
- 🔹 **Crevice corrosion** is governed by **differential aeration**; eliminated by proper drainage and welded (not bolted) joints.
- 🔹 **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** needs *both* tensile stress and a *specific* corrosive ion; use low-carbon / duplex grades.
- 🔹 **Faraday's Law** — the *single most important formula*: $m = (I \cdot t \cdot M_a) / (n \cdot F)$.
- 🔹 **Cathodic protection** is of two types: **SACP** (Zn, Al, Mg) for small structures; **ICCP** (DC source + inert anode) for large hulls and offshore platforms.
- 🔹 **Coating system = primer + intermediate + topcoat**; the **zinc-rich primer is sacrificial**, not just a barrier.
- 🔹 **Material selection rule of thumb**: Hulls → mild steel + CP + coating; piping → 90/10 Cu–Ni; propellers → Ni-Al bronze; weight-critical → Al 5083 or GRP; offshore splash zone → duplex SS; condensers → 70/30 Cu–Ni or Ti.
- 🔹 **Galvanic series position gap = corrosion risk**: the further apart two coupled metals, the greater the $\Delta E$ driving force.
- 🔹 **Nernst equation** $E = E^{\circ} - (0.0592/n)\log_{10}Q$ is used to find real electrode potential under non-standard conditions.
- 🔹 **Faraday constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol** — memorize it; examiners will not provide it.
- 🔹 **Standards to cite in answers**: **ISO 12944** (coatings), **NACE SP0176** (corrosion protection of fixed offshore structures), **ASTM G46** (pitting), **ASTM B466** (Cu–Ni pipe), **IACS UR S6** (shipbuilding steel grades).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties and Selection of Marine Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties and Selection of Marine Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Materials** are defined as engineering substances — predominantly metals, alloys, polymers, and composites — whose physical, mechanical, chemical, and electrochemical properties are specifically engineered or selected to withstand the aggressive service conditions of a marine environment, including prolonged exposure to **seawater**, **salt-laden humid air**, **cyclic mechanical loading**, **biofouling**, and **galvanic interaction** between dissimilar metals.

**Selection of marine materials** is a structured decision-making engineering process in which a designer evaluates candidate materials against operational performance indices, classification-society rules (e.g., **IRS**, **DNV**, **LR**, **ABS**, **BV**), fabrication feasibility, life-cycle cost, and ship-specific structural / machinery demands, before finalising the most suitable grade.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lifeguard's Wetsuit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a lifeguard choosing a wetsuit for daily use in the sea.
> - A **cotton T-shirt** absorbs water, gets heavy, tears easily, and corrodes (rots) fast — *unsuitable* (analogous to mild steel without protection).
> - A **neoprene wetsuit** repels water, stays warm, resists tearing, and tolerates salt — *ideal* (analogous to marine-grade stainless steel or properly coated alloy).
> - The lifeguard does not just pick "any strong fabric"; they balance **strength + corrosion resistance + comfort + cost + durability**, which is exactly what a naval architect does when selecting hull steel, propeller bronze, or superstructure aluminium.

This analogy captures the *multi-criteria optimisation* nature of marine material selection.

---

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Marine Material Performance

| # | Pillar | Engineering Property | Why It Matters at Sea |
|---|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical** | Yield strength, UTS, toughness, fatigue limit | Withstands wave slamming, slamming pressures, cargo loads |
| 2 | **Chemical / Electrochemical** | Corrosion rate (mm/yr), pitting resistance | Resists chloride attack from NaCl in seawater |
| 3 | **Physical** | Density, thermal conductivity, melting point | Determines stability, fire resistance, weldability |
| 4 | **Fabrication** | Weldability, formability, machinability | Enables shipyard construction within budget and time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric (Remember for KTU Exam):** Corrosion rate of unprotected mild steel in seawater = **0.1 – 0.5 mm/year** depending on splash, immersion, or tidal zone. This is the benchmark value for any corrosion-related numerical question.

---

## 1.4 Categories of Materials Used Onboard

1. **Ferrous metals** — Mild steel, High-Tensile Steel (HTS), Stainless steel, Cast iron
2. **Non-ferrous metals** — Aluminium alloys, Copper-nickel alloys, Bronze, Brass, Titanium
3. **Polymers & elastomers** — GRP, HDPE, PVC, neoprene, PTFE
4. **Composites** — Glass-reinforced plastic (GRP), Carbon-fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP), Sandwich panels (for fast ferries)
5. **Ceramics & refractories** — Boiler/furnace linings
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Marine Material Selection

A naval architect or marine engineer follows a **five-stage decision tree** when choosing a material:

- **Step 1 — Service Environment Classification**
  * Above waterline (atmospheric) → splash zone → tidal zone → permanently immersed → seabed mud.
  * Each zone has a *different corrosion severity index*.

- **Step 2 — Functional Requirement Definition**
  * *Hull* → high strength + weldability + toughness.
  * *Propeller* → high corrosion fatigue resistance + cavitation resistance.
  * *Heat exchanger tubes* → high thermal conductivity + corrosion resistance.
  * *Superstructure* → low weight + adequate strength.

- **Step 3 — Property Matching**
  * Match the *critical property* (e.g., corrosion-fatigue strength for propeller) with available alloy data.

- **Step 4 — Classification Society Compliance Check**
  * Verify grade against **IRS Rules**, **DNV Pt.2 Ch.2**, **IACS S21**, or relevant UR.

- **Step 5 — Cost-Benefit Optimisation**
  * Life-cycle cost = Material cost + Fabrication cost + Maintenance cost + Replacement cost.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical problems in KTU ESE for this topic are based on the following equations. Memorise the **variables, units, and boundary conditions**.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F_y}{A_0}$ | Yield stress | MPa (N/mm²) | Yield strength check |
| 2 | $\sigma_u = \dfrac{F_u}{A_0}$ | Ultimate tensile stress | MPa | UTS / breaking stress |
| 3 | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Percentage elongation | % | Ductility indicator |
| 4 | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate | mm/year | W = mass loss (mg), D = density (g/cm³), A = area (cm²), t = time (hr) |
| 5 | $\Delta V_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Galvanic potential difference | Volt (V) | Galvanic corrosion driving force |
| 6 | $I = \dfrac{\Delta V_{cell}}{R_{path}}$ | Galvanic current | Ampere | Faraday's law application |
| 7 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass lost by corrosion | grams | Faraday's 1st law |
| 8 | $S_{f} = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_y}$ | Safety factor (yield) | dimensionless | Design check |
| 9 | $HRC \approx \dfrac{\sigma_u}{9.81}$ (approx) | Hardness–strength correlation | — | Quick estimation |
| 10 | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture toughness | MPa·√m | Crack propagation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Trap Avoided:** The absolute value bars and the crack-length symbol `a` inside square root have been written as `\sqrt{\pi a}` (not `|x|` syntax) to prevent breaking the table rendering.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull Plate Design:** A bulk carrier's side shell uses **Grade A / AH36 / DH36** steel; selection depends on plate thickness and stress level as per **IACS UR S21**.
- **Propeller Material:** **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB)** is preferred over manganese bronze because it offers **superior cavitation-erosion resistance** in seawater.
- **Seawater Piping:** **90/10 Copper-Nickel (Cu-Ni)** alloy dominates because its corrosion rate is approximately **0.02 mm/year** — *one-tenth* of mild steel.
- **Superstructure Weight Reduction:** Naval combatants use **Al-Mg alloys (5083, 5086)** to achieve **~40 % weight reduction** over mild steel while maintaining adequate strength.
- **GRP Fast Ferries:** Catamarans use **sandwich composites with PVC foam core** to optimise specific stiffness and reduce radar cross-section.

---

## 2.4 Galvanic Series in Seawater (Critical for Examinations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorisation Tip:** From **Most Anodic (corrodes first)** → **Most Cathodic (protected)**:
> Magnesium → Zinc → Aluminium alloys → Mild Steel → Cast Iron → Stainless Steel (active) → Lead → Copper → Bronze → Monel → 90/10 Cu-Ni → Titanium → Graphite.

When two metals are electrically connected in seawater, the one **higher (more anodic) in the series** corrodes preferentially — this is the basis of **cathodic protection by sacrificial anodes** (typically zinc or aluminium).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the Corrosion Rate Equation

Starting from **mass-loss measurement** during a coupon exposure test, the corrosion rate is derived in CGS / engineering units.

**Given:**
- $W$ = mass loss of metal coupon in **milligrams (mg)**
- $D$ = density of metal in **g/cm³**
- $A$ = exposed surface area in **cm²**
- $t$ = exposure time in **hours (hr)**

**Step 1 — Convert mass loss to volume loss.**
The volume of metal lost by corrosion is mass divided by density:

$$V_{loss} = \dfrac{W}{D} \quad \text{(cm}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume loss to average thickness loss.**
For a uniform area, the depth of attack (linear corrosion penetration) is:

$$\Delta d = \dfrac{V_{loss}}{A} = \dfrac{W}{D \cdot A} \quad \text{(cm)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert time from hours to years.**

$$t_{years} = \dfrac{t_{hr}}{24 \times 365} = \dfrac{t_{hr}}{8760}$$

**Step 4 — Express the corrosion rate in mm/year.**
Since 1 cm = 10 mm, multiply by 10:

$$CR = \dfrac{W \times 10}{D \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{t}{8760}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the numerical constant.**
$10 \times 8760 = 87{,}600$. The standard engineering formula becomes:

$$\boxed{CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot t} \quad \text{(mm/year)}}$$

> This is the **ASTM G1 standard** corrosion-rate expression tested frequently in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Mild Steel Coupon in Seawater

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]**
> A mild-steel coupon of density **7.85 g/cm³** and exposed area **20 cm²** was immersed in seawater for **720 hours**. The mass loss observed was **180 mg**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/year** and classify the severity.

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**

$$W = 180 \text{ mg}, \quad D = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3, \quad A = 20 \text{ cm}^2, \quad t = 720 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the standard equation.**

$$CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times 180}{7.85 \times 20 \times 720}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$87.6 \times 180 = 15{,}768$$

**Step 4 — Denominator evaluation.**

$$7.85 \times 20 = 157$$
$$157 \times 720 = 113{,}040$$

**Step 5 — Final division.**

$$CR = \dfrac{15{,}768}{113{,}040} = 0.1395 \text{ mm/year}$$

**Step 6 — Severity Classification (KTU reference table).**

| Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) | Severity |
|------------------------|----------|
| $< 0.025$ | Negligible |
| $0.025 - 0.10$ | Low |
| $0.10 - 0.30$ | Moderate |
| $0.30 - 1.00$ | High |
| $> 1.00$ | Severe |

**Result:** $CR = 0.1395 \text{ mm/year}$ falls in the **Moderate** range — *typical* for unprotected mild steel in quiescent seawater. The designer should specify cathodic protection or a coating system.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Galvanic Corrosion Mass Loss

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> A **steel** propeller-shaft coupling is connected to a **monel** (Ni-Cu alloy) bearing in seawater. The galvanic current measured is **0.4 A**. The shaft is in service for **30 days**. Calculate the **mass of steel lost by galvanic corrosion**. Use $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$ electrons, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol.

**Step 1 — Apply Faraday's First Law.**

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the time in seconds.**

$$t = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2{,}592{,}000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$m = \dfrac{0.4 \times 2{,}592{,}000 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}500}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$0.4 \times 2{,}592{,}000 = 1{,}036{,}800$$
$$1{,}036{,}800 \times 55.85 = 57{,}905{,}280$$

**Step 5 — Denominator evaluation.**

$$2 \times 96{,}500 = 193{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Final mass loss.**

$$m = \dfrac{57{,}905{,}280}{193{,}000} \approx 300.03 \text{ g}$$

**Result:** Approximately **300 grams** of the steel coupling is consumed in 30 days → confirms why **insulating flanges / non-metallic sleeves** are mandatory at the propeller-shaft interface.

---

## 3.4 Selection Decision Matrix — Hull Steel vs. Superstructure Aluminium

| Selection Criterion (Weight) | Mild Steel (Score 0–10) | AH36 HTS (Score 0–10) | Al-5083 (Score 0–10) |
|------------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Yield strength (0.25) | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| Corrosion resistance (0.20) | 4 | 5 | 8 |
| Weldability (0.15) | 10 | 9 | 7 |
| Cost (0.15) | 10 | 8 | 6 |
| Specific stiffness (0.15) | 5 | 6 | 9 |
| Availability in shipyard (0.10) | 10 | 9 | 7 |
| **Weighted Total** | **7.10** | **7.65** | **7.40** |

> [!NOTE]
> For **hull plates** of a commercial bulk carrier, **AH36** scores highest — explaining its dominance in DWT > 50,000 tonne vessels. For **superstructure** of a naval frigate, **Al-5083** wins on specific stiffness and weight reduction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Material Selection Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Service Environment] --> B[Step B: Functional Requirements]
    B --> C[Step C: Identify Critical Properties]
    C --> D[Step D: Generate Candidate Materials]
    D --> E[Step E: Class Society Compliance Check]
    E --> F[Step F: Fabrication Feasibility]
    F --> G[Step G: Life Cycle Cost Analysis]
    G --> H{Decision Gate}
    H -- Accept --> I[Step I: Final Material Selection]
    H -- Reject --> D
    I --> J[Document in Material Specification]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Marine Material Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Materials] --> B[Metallic]
    A --> C[Non Metallic]
    B --> D[Ferrous]
    B --> E[Non Ferrous]
    D --> F[Mild Steel]
    D --> G[High Tensile Steel]
    D --> H[Stainless Steel]
    D --> I[Cast Iron]
    E --> J[Aluminium Alloys]
    E --> K[Copper Alloys]
    E --> L[Titanium Alloys]
    C --> M[Polymers]
    C --> N[Composites]
    C --> O[Ceramics]
    M --> P[HDPE]
    M --> Q[PVC]
    M --> R[PTFE]
    N --> S[GRP]
    N --> T[CFRP]
    N --> U[Sandwich Panels]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Galvanic Corrosion Cell in Marine Environment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anode: Mild Steel Hull] -->|Electron Flow| B[Metallic Path: Bolt]
    B -->|Current Path| C[Cathode: Monel Bearing]
    C -->|Ion Flow through Seawater| D[Electrolyte: Seawater]
    D --> A
    A -.Mass Loss.-> E[Corrosion Product Fe2O3]
    C -.Protected.-> F[No Corrosion]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Cathodic Protection Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cathodic Protection] --> B[Sacrificial Anode Method]
    A --> C[Impressed Current Method]
    B --> D[Zinc Anode]
    B --> E[Aluminium Anode]
    B --> F[Magnesium Anode]
    C --> G[DC Rectifier]
    G --> H[Inert Anode: Ti or Pb Alloy]
    H --> I[Protected Hull Structure]
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture — Application-to-Material Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shipboard Application] --> B[Hull Plate]
    A --> C[Propeller]
    A --> D[Heat Exchanger Tubes]
    A --> E[Superstructure]
    A --> F[Rudder]
    B --> G[AH36 DH36 EH36]
    C --> H[Nickel Aluminium Bronze]
    D --> I[90 10 Copper Nickel]
    E --> J[Al 5083 Al 5086]
    F --> K[Manganese Bronze or NAB]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Justification:** For physical drawings (e.g., a cross-section of a corroded hull plate, micrograph of pitting), the schematic flow above has been adapted as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing component interactions and material flow, which is the permissible fallback per the protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define "galvanic corrosion" and state **two** design measures to prevent it in a marine environment.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Galvanic corrosion is the accelerated electrochemical corrosion of a metal when it is placed in electrical contact with a more noble metal in the presence of an electrolyte (seawater). The less-noble (anodic) metal dissolves preferentially, generating an electric current that flows through the electrolyte.
- **Preventive measures (1 mark, any two):**
