# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!TIP]
> All questions below are calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** question paper pattern:
> - **Part A**: 3 marks each, direct, single-concept.
> - **Part B**: 14 marks each, with **internal choice**, sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between a fixed jacket platform and a tension leg platform (TLP) with respect to (a) mode of support, (b) heave response, and (c) typical operational water depth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Jacket Platform | Tension Leg Platform (TLP) |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Mode of support | Rigid steel space-frame fixed to seabed by **driven steel piles** | Semi-submersible hull held down by **vertical, pre-tensioned steel tendons** |
| (b) Heave response | **Negligible** — rigidly fixed, natural period < 3 s | **Suppressed** — tendons are nearly inextensible, heave period ~2–4 s |
| (c) Typical depth | 0 – 412 m (e.g., Bullwinkle) | 300 – 2000 m (e.g., Heidrun) |

*(1 mark per correct point – 3 marks total)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**State any three (3) examples of subsea structures used in modern offshore oil & gas developments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Subsea Christmas Tree (Wellhead Assembly)** – valve assembly controlling flow at the wellhead, including the master valve, wing valve, and SCSSV. *(1 mark)*
2. **Subsea Manifold** – collects flow from multiple wells and routes it via a single export line. *(1 mark)*
3. **Subsea Umbilical** – provides hydraulic power, electrical control, and chemical injection from the host platform to the subsea hardware. *(1 mark)*

*(Alternative correct answers: subsea template, subsea pumping station, flowline, rigid jumper, ROV tooling skid, subsea protection structure.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions in the ESE (End Semester Examination) provide an **internal choice**: students answer either **Question A or Question B** (both 14 marks). Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Both alternatives are given below to maximise revision utility.

---

### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**"With the help of suitable diagrams, classify offshore structures into fixed, floating, and subsea categories. Describe the salient features, mooring/fixing systems, and depth of operation of at least two structures from each category."**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Classify offshore structures with a neat block diagram and explain the fixed-structure category with two examples.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Classification of Offshore Structures (3 marks):**
Offshore structures are broadly classified into:
   - **Fixed (Bottom-founded) Structures** — rigidly attached to seabed by piles or gravity; depths 0 – 500 m.
   - **Floating Structures** — held in position by mooring lines, tendons, or dynamic positioning; depths 500 m – 3000+ m.
   - **Subsea Structures** — located on the seabed; depths 50 – 3000+ m; normally unoccupied.

2. **Fixed-structure category – Example 1: Steel Jacket Platform (2 marks):**
   - A 4/6/8-legged space-frame of steel tubular members.
   - Pile-driven into the seabed through sleeves at the base of each leg.
   - Topside (production deck) sits on the jacket at +EL (+) above sea level.
   - Example: **Bullwinkle platform** in the Gulf of Mexico (~412 m water depth — world's deepest jacket at installation).

3. **Fixed-structure category – Example 2: Concrete Gravity-Based Structure (GBS) (2 marks):**
   - A massive reinforced concrete structure with storage cells in the base.
   - Held in place by **gravity (own weight + ballast)** — no piling required.
   - Towed to site in a controlled submerging operation.
   - Example: **Troll A** in the North Sea, with a 303 m concrete shaft and a 472 m total height.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Explain the floating and subsea categories with two examples each.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Floating-structure category – Example 1: FPSO (2 marks):**
   - A converted or purpose-built **shaped-hull tanker** fitted with a process plant.
   - Station-keeping by a **disconnectable turret mooring** with a weathervaning bow.
   - Stores crude in cargo tanks; offloads via a side-by-side shuttle tanker.
   - Example: **Petrojarl Foinaven FPSO** in the North Sea.

2. **Floating-structure category – Example 2: Tension Leg Platform (2 marks):**
   - Hull is a semi-submersible form; held down by **vertical pre-tensioned tendons**.
   - Tendons suppress heave but allow surge/sway — the platform is *horizontally compliant*.
   - Example: **Heidrun TLP** in the Norwegian Sea (345 m depth).

3. **Subsea-structure category – Example 1: Subsea Christmas Tree (1.5 marks):**
   - Sits on top of the wellhead; contains valves to control and shut in the well.
   - Operated remotely via hydraulic umbilicals from the host.
   - Includes SCSSV (Surface-Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve) and DHSV (Downhole Safety Valve) for emergency shut-in.

4. **Subsea-structure category – Example 2: Subsea Manifold (1.5 marks):**
   - Collects production from multiple wells; houses choke valves for rate control.
   - Connected to the host via a single export flowline.
   - Designed for NUI (Normally Unattended Installation) operation — intervention by ROV.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**"Discuss the safety design philosophy, evacuation strategy, and regulatory framework applicable to (i) Fixed offshore platforms, (ii) Floating offshore platforms, and (iii) Subsea installations. Justify why each class requires a distinct approach."**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Compare the safety design philosophy and evacuation strategy of fixed and floating offshore platforms.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Fixed Platforms — Safety Philosophy (2 marks):**
   - The asset is **immobile**; personnel evacuation can rely on helicopter transfer (the primary means) and stand-by vessel transfer (secondary).
   - **Temporary Refuge (TR)** is a 2-hour fire-rated enclosure designed on the assumption of *stationary* occupants.
   - **Air-gap** between wave crest and deck is engineered through the **jacket elevation design** — there is no wave-induced deck set-down.
   - **Dropped-object risk** is the dominant structural hazard; **blast & fire** dominate process hazards.

2. **Fixed Platforms — Evacuation (1.5 marks):**
   - Primary: Helicopter to onshore or stand-by vessel (per **CAP 437** helideck standard).
   - Secondary: Marine evacuation via **Tender-Assisted Transfer (TAT)** or **Knock-down Knuckle-boom Crane**.
   - Lifeboats: Conventional davit-launched, free-fall in some cases.

3. **Floating Platforms — Safety Philosophy (2 marks):**
   - The asset is **mobile**; the structure can drift, rotate, and oscillate.
   - **TR design must account for motion-induced issues** (sea-sickness, supplies, motion-sensor-activated alarms).
   - **Air-gap accounting** includes both **wave crest** and **platform set-down** under extreme heave response.
   - **Mooring line failure** is a major risk — secondary station-keeping by thrusters and DP-class systems is required.
   - **Green-water loading** on the lowest deck is engineered to be survivable.

4. **Floating Platforms — Evacuation (1.5 marks):**
   - Primary: **Free-fall lifeboats** (per **LSA Code, Chapter IV**), launched from the bow or side.
   - Secondary: Helicopter (with motion-compensated winching if possible).
   - Tertiary: **Survival craft** + **MOB (Man-Overboard) recovery** boats.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Explain the safety philosophy of subsea installations and the regulatory framework common to all three classes.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Subsea Installations — Safety Philosophy (3 marks):**
   - **Normally Unattended Installation (NUI)** principle: no personnel in normal operation.
   - All intervention by **ROV** (Remotely Operated Vehicle) or in special cases divers (shallow water only).
   - **Multi-level Emergency Shutdown (ESD) cascade**: ESD-0 (field) → ESD-1 (network) → ESD-2 (manifold) → ESD-3 (tree) → ESD-4 (DHSV).
   - **Subsea Isolation Valve (SSIV)** and **Surface-Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV)** are the last line of defence against hydrocarbon release.
   - **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** ratings per **IEC 61508 / 61511** govern the subsea control and shutdown system.

2. **Regulatory Framework — Fixed Platforms (1.5 marks):**
   - **API RP 2A / ISO 19902** for fixed steel structures.
   - **SOLAS** for life-safety systems; **MARPOL** for pollution prevention.
   - National: UK HSE Safety Case Regulations, Norway PSA, India OISD.

3. **Regulatory Framework — Floating Platforms (1.5 marks):**
   - **IMO MODU Code (A.414(XI) and later resolutions)** for MODUs.
   - **IMO Resolution A.1023(26)** specifically for FPSOs.
   - **NMA (Norway), HSE (UK), USCG (USA)** for flag-state enforcement.
   - **IMCA** guidance for offshore construction and DP operations.

4. **Regulatory Framework — Subsea (1 mark):**
   - **API 17 series** (17D for subsea trees, 17E for umbilicals, 17J for flowlines, 17N for subsea production systems).
   - **IEC 61508/61511** for safety instrumented systems.
   - **OSPAR** and **IMO London Protocol** for decommissioning.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the depth regimes.** A common error is writing that "TLP works in 0–300 m" — wrong. TLP works in **300–2000 m**. Memorise the table in §2.4.
> 2. **Forgetting that the TLP is a "floating" structure** even though it is "vertically constrained" — students incorrectly classify it as fixed. The KTU key insists on the term "**floating, vertically-moored**".
> 3. **Skipping regulatory references.** A 7-mark question on evacuation without a single standard (CAP 437, MODU, LSA Code) loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing the air-gap concept** for floating platforms. A simple sketch of *wave-crest vs. lowest deck* is worth at least 1 mark and is often missed.
> 5. **Writing "subsea structures have no safety"** — incorrect. The **NUI + ESD cascade + SIL** is the *very essence* of subsea safety.
> 6. **Ignoring the soil/seabed parameter** when justifying a structure choice — the *Bay of Bengal soft clay* vs. *North Sea stiff clay* example in §3.3 illustrates why this matters.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- ✅ **Three classes**: Fixed (0–500 m), Floating (500–3000+ m), Subsea (50–3000+ m).
- ✅ **Fixed sub-types**: Steel Jacket, Concrete GBS, Compliant Tower, Jack-up, Braced Caisson.
- ✅ **Floating sub-types**: Semi-Submersible, FPSO, Spar, TLP, Drillship.
- ✅ **Subsea sub-types**: Manifold, Christmas Tree, Template, Flowlines, Umbilicals, Subsea Pumps.
- ✅ **Jacket mass scales as the SQUARE of water depth** — this is the *primary* reason fixed structures are limited to ~500 m.
- ✅ **TLP is "vertically moored", Spar is "depth-stabilised", FPSO is "weather-vaning", Semi is "moored buoyantly", Drillship is "DP-held"**.
- ✅ **MODU Code (A.414) governs** MODUs including semi-subs and drillships.
- ✅ **IMO A.1023(26)** specifically governs **FPSOs**.
- ✅ **API RP 2A / ISO 19902** for fixed steel; **API 2T** for TLPs; **API 2M** for Spars.
- ✅ **Subsea follows API 17 series + IEC 61508/61511** with a **NUI (Normally Unattended Installation)** design philosophy.
- ✅ **ESD cascade**: ESD-0 → ESD-1 → ESD-2 → ESD-3 → ESD-4 (DHSV) — the last line of defence.
- ✅ **Evacuation primary modes**: Fixed → Helo; Floating → Free-fall lifeboat; Subsea → NUI (no personnel).
- ✅ **CAP 437** governs helideck design; **LSA Code Chapter IV** governs lifeboat systems.
- ✅ **Air-gap** is a *floating-structure* safety metric; **dropped object** is a *fixed-structure* dominant hazard.
- ✅ **Decommissioning**: Per **OSPAR 98/3** and the **IMO London Protocol 1996** — all subsea and fixed structures have specific removal/leave-in-place rules.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark Part B questions, **always draw a labelled diagram** (2–3 marks head-start).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Loads and Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Environmental Loads and Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental loads** are the naturally occurring, time-varying forces and moments imposed on a marine vessel, offshore platform, or coastal structure by the surrounding ocean-atmosphere system. They are statistically described random processes whose magnitudes are functions of the geographical location, water depth, return period, and service life of the structure. According to **ISO 19901-1** and **API RP 2A-WSD**, these loads form the principal category of *metocean* (meteorological + oceanographic) design actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OESFT727 / Module 3):**
> *"Environmental loads refer to all naturally occurring actions on a marine structure arising from wind, waves, currents, tides, ice, temperature, and seismic events, which must be quantified probabilistically and combined with appropriate partial safety factors to ensure structural integrity throughout the design service life."*

The principal categories of environmental loads in maritime engineering are:

1. **Wind Loads** — Aerodynamic drag and lift on superstructure
2. **Wave Loads** — Hydrodynamic pressure from ocean waves (often the *dominant* load)
3. **Current Loads** — Steady drag from tidal/oceanic currents
4. **Ice Loads** — Global pressure from sea-ice (polar regions only)
5. **Seismic Loads** — Inertia forces from earthquake ground motion
6. **Thermal Loads** — Expansion/contraction from temperature gradients
7. **Marine Growth Loads** — Additional drag and weight from biofouling

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Sailing Lemon in a Stormy Tub":**
> Imagine a small lemon floating in a backyard bathtub while you blow at it with a hair dryer, splash water on it with a spoon, and occasionally pour cold juice on it. The lemon is the ship; the hair dryer represents *wind*, the spoon-splashes are *waves*, the steady water flow under the lemon is the *current*, and the cold juice is *temperature*. Each "force" pushes the lemon differently, and the worst possible day combines all of them at once. The engineer's job is to estimate *how hard* each push can be during the lemon's lifetime in the tub, and then design the lemon (or the ship) so it doesn't get squashed.

In real offshore design (e.g., a semi-submersible in the North Sea, or a berthing tanker at Kochi port), environmental loads are **not** deterministic — they are stochastic. A "100-year wave" is **not** the biggest wave seen in 100 years, but a wave height with a 1% probability of being exceeded in any given year.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Constants & Standards**
> - Sea-water density: **ρ_w = 1025 kg/m³**
> - Air density: **ρ_a = 1.225 kg/m³**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Kinematic viscosity of seawater: **ν ≈ 1 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - Design codes: **ISO 19900-series, API RP 2A, DNV-OS-E301, IS 4651**
> - Typical design return period (permanent structures): **100 years**
> - Typical design return period (temporary/mobile units): **1 to 10 years**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Environmental Loads

Marine environmental loads can be categorised along three engineering axes:

| Axis | Category | Typical Magnitude |
|------|----------|-------------------|
| **Time Character** | Static (current, hydrostatic) vs Dynamic (waves, wind gusts) | Static loads dominate ~10–20% of total |
| **Spatial Character** | Distributed (wave pressure) vs Point (anchor tension) | — |
| **Probability of Occurrence** | Operational (50% annual) vs Extreme (10⁻² annual) vs Survival (10⁻⁴ annual) | Drives return-period selection |

## 2.2 Wind Loads

Wind exerts aerodynamic forces on the above-water hull, superstructure, masts, helideck, and cranes.

**Wind pressure at reference height (z):**

$$p(z) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_a\,C_s\,V^2(z)$$

where:
- $\rho_a$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at 15 °C, sea level)
- $C_s$ = shape coefficient (depends on geometry — sharp-edged prisms ~1.5–2.0)
- $V(z)$ = wind speed at height $z$ (1-hour mean at 10 m elevation is the standard reference)

**Wind speed profile (Power Law):**

$$V(z) \;=\; V_{10}\,\left(\dfrac{z}{10}\right)^{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the terrain/shear exponent (open sea: $\alpha \approx 0.10$–$0.12$).

**Resultant wind force on a body:**

$$F_{\text{wind}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_a\,C_D\,A_{\text{proj}}\,V^2$$

where $C_D$ = drag coefficient, $A_{\text{proj}}$ = projected area normal to wind.

## 2.3 Wave Loads — The Dominant Action

### 2.3.1 Regular vs Irregular (Random) Sea States

- **Regular wave** — single sinusoidal component (theoretical; useful for first-cut analysis).
- **Irregular sea state** — superposition of many sinusoids described by a **wave spectrum** $S(\omega)$ (e.g., Pierson-Moskowitz, JONSWAP).

**Significant wave height** $H_s$ — mean of the highest 1/3 of waves in a record:

$$H_s \;\approx\; 4\sqrt{m_0} \;\approx\; 4\,\sigma_\eta$$

where $m_0 = \int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega$ is the zeroth spectral moment and $\sigma_\eta$ is the surface elevation standard deviation.

### 2.3.2 Wave Forces on Slender Members — Morison's Equation

For vertical cylinders where the diameter $D$ is small relative to wavelength $\lambda$ ($D/\lambda < 0.2$), wave forces are computed by **Morison's equation** — sum of **drag** (velocity-squared) and **inertia** (acceleration) contributions:

$$f(z,t) \;=\; f_D(z,t) \;+\; f_I(z,t)$$

$$\boxed{\;f(z,t) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_w\,C_D\,D\,u\,\vert u\vert \;+\; \rho_w\,C_M\,\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}\;}$$

For a linear (Airy) wave at the still-water level, the horizontal water-particle velocity and acceleration are:

$$u(z,t) \;=\; \dfrac{H\,g\,T}{4\,L}\,\dfrac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{\cosh(kd)}\,\cos(kx-\omega t)$$

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} \;=\; \dfrac{H\,g\,\pi}{L}\,\dfrac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{\cosh(kd)}\,\sin(kx-\omega t)$$

where:
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (Keulegan-Carpenter-number dependent, typically **0.6 – 1.2**)
- $C_M$ = inertia coefficient (typically **1.5 – 2.0** for smooth cylinders)
- $D$ = cylinder diameter
- $H$ = wave height
- $T$ = wave period
- $L$ = wavelength
- $k = 2\pi/L$ = wave number
- $d$ = water depth
- $\omega = 2\pi/T$ = angular frequency

### 2.3.3 Wave Forces on Large Bodies — Diffraction Theory

When $D/\lambda > 0.2$, the body scatters the wave field. The **Froude-Krylov** + **diffraction** formulation is used (MacCamy-Fuchs for fixed vertical cylinder). KTU students should remember the threshold; the full integral-equation formulation is reserved for advanced electives.

## 2.4 Current Loads

Tidal and oceanic currents add a steady velocity $U_c$ to the wave-induced velocity $u$. The total horizontal velocity seen by a slender member becomes:

$$u_{\text{total}}(z,t) \;=\; U_c(z) \;+\; u(z,t)$$

and this combined velocity is substituted into the Morison drag term:

$$f_D \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_w\,C_D\,D\,u_{\text{total}}\,\vert u_{\text{total}}\vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering significance:** Current modifies the drag force non-linearly because of the absolute-value operator. Even a small current can *asymmetrically* load a slender structure during each wave cycle, producing mean-drift oscillations.

## 2.5 Ice Loads & Seismic Loads (Briefly)

- **Ice loads** — Global ice pressure on the hull is computed from ISO 19906: $F_{\text{ice}} = p_g \cdot A$, where $p_g$ is the global ice pressure (typically 0.5–2.5 MPa for first-year level ice).
- **Seismic loads** — For offshore platforms in seismically active zones (e.g., Japanese waters), site-specific response spectra replace wave spectra. Inertia forces are computed as $F_s = m \cdot S_a(T)$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application | Variables / Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $V(z) = V_{10}\,(z/10)^{\alpha}$ | Wind speed profile | $V$ in m/s, $\alpha$ dimensionless |
| 2 | $F_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac12 \rho_a C_D A V^2$ | Wind force on structure | $F$ in N, $A$ in m² |
| 3 | $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ | Significant wave height | $H_s$ in m |
| 4 | $u(z,t) = \dfrac{HgT}{4L}\dfrac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\cosh(kd)}\cos(kx-\omega t)$ | Linear wave kinematics | $u$ in m/s |
| 5 | $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$ | Linear dispersion relation | $\omega$ in rad/s, $k$ in rad/m |
| 6 | $f = \tfrac12\rho_w C_D D u\vert u\vert + \rho_w C_M (\pi D^2/4)\,\partial u/\partial t$ | Morison force per unit length | $f$ in N/m |
| 7 | $F_{\text{ice}} = p_g A$ | Global ice pressure force | $F$ in N |
| 8 | $P_e = 1 - (1 - 1/T_R)^{L}$ | Probability of exceedance | $T_R$ = return period (yr), $L$ = design life (yr) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations power every offshore design — from jacket platforms in the Arabian Sea (wave-dominated) to FPSOs off the Brazilian coast (current + wave + wind combined) to LNG terminals in Arctic Russia (ice-dominated). Mis-estimating any of them is the leading cause of structural failure in marine environments.

## 2.7 Design Considerations — Limit States & Return Period

Modern maritime design is **limit-state based**, not working-stress based. The key limit states per **ISO 19900**:

1. **SLS** — Serviceability Limit State (operational, no damage)
2. **ULS** — Ultimate Limit State (extreme, no collapse)
3. **FLS** — Fatigue Limit State (cyclic, no crack propagation)
4. **ALS** — Accidental Limit State (fire, collision, dropped object)

**Return period** $T_R$ is tied to the **annual probability of exceedance** $P_a = 1/T_R$ and the **design service life** $L$:

$$P_e(L) \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{T_R}\right)^{L}$$

A 100-year return-period wave on a 25-year service-life structure has only a **22% probability** of being exceeded at least once.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of Exceedance vs Return Period

**Goal:** Show how a 100-year event behaves over a 25-year service life.

Let the annual probability of exceedance be:

$$p_a \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_R}$$

The annual probability of **non-exceedance** (survival) is:

$$q_a \;=\; 1 - p_a \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{T_R}$$

Assuming independent years, the probability of non-exceedance over $L$ years is:

$$q_L \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{T_R}\right)^{L}$$

Therefore the probability of exceedance over the design life $L$ is:

$$\boxed{\;P_e(L) \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{T_R}\right)^{L}\;}$$

**Numerical evaluation** — Take $T_R = 100$ yr, $L = 25$ yr:

$$P_e \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{100}\right)^{25}$$

$$P_e \;=\; 1 - \left(0.99\right)^{25}$$

Compute $(0.99)^{25}$ step by step:

$$(0.99)^{2} \;=\; 0.9801$$

$$(0.99)^{4} \;=\; (0.9801)^2 \;=\; 0.96059601$$

$$(0.99)^{8} \;=\; (0.96059601)^2 \;=\; 0.92274469$$

$$(0.99)^{16} \;=\; (0.92274469)^2 \;=\; 0.85145677$$

$$(0.99)^{24} \;=\; (0.99)^{16} \cdot (0.99)^{8} \;=\; 0.85145677 \times 0.92274469 \;=\; 0.78570$$

$$(0.99)^{25} \;=\; 0.78570 \times 0.99 \;=\; 0.77784$$

Therefore:

$$P_e(25) \;=\; 1 - 0.77784 \;=\; 0.22216$$

$$\boxed{\;P_e \;\approx\; 22.2\%\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A "100-year wave" still has more than a 1-in-5 chance of striking a structure whose design life is a quarter-century. This is why offshore jackets in the North Sea are typically designed for 10,000-year events during the *survival* condition.

## 3.2 Derivation: Morison Equation Force on a Vertical Pile

**Goal:** Derive the total horizontal force on a vertical cylinder of diameter $D$, length below SWL $\ell$, subjected to a linear Airy wave of height $H$, period $T$, in depth $d$.

**Step 1 — Water-particle kinematics.** For linear theory, at a vertical position $z$ measured upward from the seabed ($-d \le z \le 0$):

$$u(z,t) \;=\; \dfrac{\pi H}{T}\,\dfrac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{\sinh(kd)}\,\cos(kx-\omega t)$$

$$\dot{u}(z,t) \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2}\,\dfrac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{\sinh(kd)}\,\sin(kx-\omega t)$$

(Here $u$ is in m/s, $\dot u = \partial u/\partial t$.)

**Step 2 — Force per unit length.** Substitute into Morison:

$$f(z,t) \;=\; \tfrac12\rho_w C_D D\,u\,\vert u\vert \;+\; \rho_w C_M \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dot{u}$$

**Step 3 — Total force on the pile.** Integrate from $z = -d$ to $z = 0$:

$$F(t) \;=\; \int_{-d}^{0} f(z,t)\,dz$$

The inertia part integrates analytically:

$$F_I(t) \;=\; \rho_w C_M \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2}\,\dfrac{1}{\sinh(kd)}\,\sin(kx-\omega t)\int_{-d}^{0}\cosh\!\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)\,dz$$

Compute the integral:

$$\int_{-d}^{0}\cosh\!\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)\,dz \;=\; \left[\dfrac{\sinh\!\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{k}\right]_{-d}^{0} \;=\; \dfrac{\sinh(kd) - \sinh(0)}{k} \;=\; \dfrac{\sinh(kd)}{k}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\;F_I(t) \;=\; \rho_w C_M \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2 k}\,\sin(kx-\omega t)\;}$$

The drag part **cannot be integrated in closed form** — it is evaluated numerically by quadrature or approximated by Fourier expansion for engineering work.

**Step 4 — Maximum inertia amplitude** (taking $\sin = 1$):

$$F_{I,\max} \;=\; \rho_w C_M \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2 k} \;=\; \rho_w C_M \dfrac{\pi^3 D^2 H}{2\,T^2\,k}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.** Take $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³, $C_M = 2.0$, $D = 2$ m, $H = 8$ m, $T = 10$ s, $k = 0.08$ rad/m:

$$F_{I,\max} \;=\; 1025 \times 2.0 \times \dfrac{\pi^3 \times (2)^2 \times 8}{2 \times (10)^2 \times 0.08}$$

Compute the numerator: $\pi^3 \approx 31.006$; $(2)^2 = 4$; $31.006 \times 4 \times 8 = 992.2$; denominator: $2 \times 100 \times 0.08 = 16$.

So $F_{I,\max} = 2050 \times 992.2 / 16 = 2050 \times 62.01 = 127{,}121$ N $\approx$ **127 kN**.

This matches typical jacket-pile inertia loads in moderate sea states.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Structure Type | $D/\lambda$ | Dominant Regime | Recommended Formula |
|----------------|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Small buoy, sensor | $< 0.05$ | Inertia-dominated | Morison inertia term only |
| Mooring line, riser | $0.05$ – $0.20$ | Drag + inertia | Full Morison equation |
| Jacket leg, monopile | $0.05$ – $0.20$ | Drag + inertia | Full Morison equation |
| Large caisson, FPSO hull | $0.20$ – $1.0$ | Diffraction | MacCamy-Fuchs / diffraction theory |
| Very large island (concrete) | $> 1.0$ | Slender hydrostatics | Integrated pressure distribution |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Environmental Load Hierarchy on a Ship / Offshore Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmosphere] --> B[Wind Load]
    A --> C[Precipitation and Temperature]
    D[Sea Surface] --> E[Wave Load - Dominant]
    D --> F[Current Load]
    G[Seabed] --> H[Hydrostatic Pressure]
    G --> I[Seismic Load if Active Zone]
    J[Polar Sea Ice] --> K[Ice Load]
    L[Biofouling] --> M[Marine Growth Load]
    B --> N[Total Environmental Action on Structure]
    C --> N
    E --> N
    F --> N
    H --> N
    I --> N
    K --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Limit State Verification]
    O --> P[Serviceability SLS]
    O --> Q[Ultimate ULS]
    O --> R[Fatigue FLS]
    O --> S[Accidental ALS]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Design Procedure for Environmental Loads

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Site Metocean Study] --> stepB[Derive Extreme Value Distribution]
    stepB --> stepC[Select Return Period based on Limit State]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute Characteristic Load]
    stepD --> stepE[Apply Partial Safety Factors]
    stepE --> stepF[Combine Load Cases]
    stepF --> stepG[Check Limit State Criteria]
    stepG --> stepH{Pass or Fail}
    stepH -->|Fail| stepI[Revise Geometry or Material]
    stepI --> stepD
    stepH -->|Pass| stepJ[Issue Construction Drawings]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Morison Force Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeU[Wave Kinematics Input: u and du/dt vs depth and time] --> nodeDrag[Drag Component: 0.5 rho Cd D u mod u]
    nodeU --> nodeInertia[Inertia Component: rho Cm pi D squared over 4 times du dt]
    nodeDrag --> nodeSum[Total Inline Force per Unit Length]
    nodeInertia --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeInt[Integrate Over Submerged Length]
    nodeInt --> nodeF[Total Horizontal Wave Force on Member]
    nodeF --> nodeS[Superpose with Wind and Current]
```

## 4.4 Decision Diagram — Selection of Wave-Force Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ[Compute D over Lambda Ratio] --> nodeR{Ratio Less Than 0.2}
    nodeR -->|Yes| nodeMor[Use Morison Equation]
    nodeR -->|No| nodeS{Ratio Between 0.2 and 1.0}
    nodeS -->|Yes| nodeMac[Use MacCamy-Fuchs Diffraction]
    nodeS -->|No| nodeT[Slender Hydrostatic Pressure Integration]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define "Significant Wave Height" and explain its physical significance in maritime structural design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *significant wave height* $H_s$ is defined as the mean of the highest one-third of wave heights recorded in a sea-state over a typical 20–30 minute observation window, originally proposed by Sverdrup and Munk in the 1940s. Mathematically it equals **$H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$**, where $m_0$ is the zeroth moment of the wave-energy spectrum, and is approximately equal to $4\sigma_\eta$ for narrow-band spectra. Physically, $H_s$ approximates the height that a *trained observer* would visually report — making it the primary descriptor used by mariners and designers alike. In design codes, $H_s$ is the input to spectral fatigue analysis and to long-term extreme-value statistics, so almost every offshore design begins with $H_s$. (3 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Distinguish between ULS, SLS, FLS, and ALS as applied to a marine structure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| State | Stands For | Purpose | Typical Return Period |
|-------|------------|---------|-----------------------|
| SLS | Serviceability Limit State | Ensure structure remains functional under operational conditions — deflection, vibration, motion comfort | 1–10 years |
| ULS | Ultimate Limit State | Prevent collapse under extreme environmental loads | 100 years |
| FLS | Fatigue Limit State | Prevent crack initiation/propagation under cyclic wave loading | Entire service life, spectral |
| ALS | Accidental Limit State | Ensure survival under fire, collision, dropped-object, blowout | Beyond design basis, risk-based |

These four states are verified separately; satisfying ULS does not guarantee FLS, and vice versa. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the various environmental loads acting on a marine structure with neat sketches. Discuss the metocean data required for design. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A vertical cylindrical pile of diameter **2.5 m** extends **30 m** below the still-water level in a depth of **35 m**. It is subjected to a regular Airy wave with height **6 m** and period **9 s**. Use the Morison equation to compute the **maximum horizontal inertia force** on the pile. Take $C_M = 2.0$, $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³, and assume deep-water kinematics simplification where $k(z+d) \approx 1$. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Types of Environmental Loads & Metocean Data**

The major environmental loads acting on a marine structure are:

1. **Wind load** — Acts on the above-water hull and topside. Computed using $F_W = \tfrac12 \rho_a C_D A V^2$.
2. **Wave load** — The dominant hydrodynamic load. Computed using Morison's equation (for slender members) or diffraction theory (for large bodies).
3. **Current load** — Steady drag from tidal/ocean currents; combined with wave drag through velocity addition.
4. **Ice load** — Relevant in polar/cold regions; ISO 19906 specifies global pressure.
5. **Seismic load** — Inertia forces from earthquake motion, derived from site response spectra.
6. **Temperature & moisture** — Thermal expansion, corrosion augmentation, marine growth.
7. **Hydrostatic load** — Pressure from the water column, $p = \rho_w g h$.

**Metocean data required for design:**
- Long-term wind, wave, current statistics at the site
- Joint probability distributions (wind–wave, wave–current)
- Extreme-value parameters (100-year, 1,000-year, 10,000-year contours)
- Water-level variation (tide, surge, sea-level rise)
- Sea-bed bathymetry and soil properties
- Air and water temperatures for thermal design
- Seismic zone classification, if applicable
- Marine-growth thickness profile

These data are obtained from buoy records, satellite altimetry, hindcast databases (e.g., ERA5, NCEP), and site-specific measurement campaigns. (7 marks)

**Part (b) — Maximum Inertia Force on the Pile**

Given: $D = 2.5$ m, submerged length $\ell = 30$ m, $d = 35$ m, $H = 6$ m, $T = 9$ s, $C_M = 2.0$, $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³.

The maximum inertia force amplitude on a vertical cylinder from a linear Airy wave is:

$$F_{I,\max} \;=\; \rho_w\,C_M\,\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2\,k}\,\dfrac{\sinh(kd)}{k\,?}$$

Working in *deep-water simplification* as instructed, with $k(z+d)\approx 1$, the velocity potential gives an effective depth-averaged reduction that we approximate as the kinematic-factor ratio $\cosh/\sinh \approx 1$ for the worst-case crest. We therefore use:

$$F_{I,\max} \;\approx\; \rho_w\,C_M\,\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2 k}$$

Step 1 — Compute $k$ using the linear dispersion relation in deep water ($\tanh(kd) \approx 1$):

$$k \;\approx\; \dfrac{\omega^2}{g} \;=\; \dfrac{(2\pi/T)^2}{g} \;=\; \dfrac{4\pi^2}{g\,T^2}$$

$$k \;=\; \dfrac{4\pi^2}{9.81 \times 81} \;=\; \dfrac{39.478}{794.61} \;\approx\; 0.04968 \;\text{rad/m}$$

Step 2 — Substitute:

$$F_{I,\max} \;=\; 1025 \times 2.0 \times \dfrac{\pi (2.5)^2}{4} \times \dfrac{2\pi^2 (6)}{(9)^2 \times 0.04968}$$

Compute the area term: $\pi (2.5)^2 / 4 = \pi \times 6.25 / 4 = 4.9087$ m².

Compute the wave term: $2 \pi^2 \times 6 / (81 \times 0.04968) = 19.739 \times 6 / 4.0241 = 118.435 / 4.0241 \approx 29.43$ m/s².

Therefore:

$$F_{I,\max} \;=\; 1025 \times 2.0 \times 4.9087 \times 29.43$$

$$= \; 2050 \times 4.9087 \times 29.43$$

$$= \; 2050 \times 144.46$$

$$\boxed{\;F_{I,\max} \;\approx\; 2.96 \times 10^{5} \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 296 \;\text{kN}\;}$$

This is the peak horizontal inertia load; total design force would add the drag contribution at the relevant Keulegan-Carpenter number. (7 marks)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled sketch, derive the **Morison equation** for wave forces on a slender vertical cylinder. Clearly state its **range of validity** and the typical values of the empirical coefficients. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** An offshore jacket structure is to be designed for a 100-year return period in a region where the design service life is **30 years**. Compute the **probability that the 100-year event will be exceeded at least once** during the service life. If the owner requires the exceedance probability to be **less than 5%** during the service life, what return period should be specified? **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Morison Equation Derivation**

For a slender vertical cylinder of diameter $D$ such that $D/\lambda < 0.2$, the cylinder does not significantly disturb the incident wave field. The inline horizontal force per unit length on the cylinder can be decomposed into:

1. A **drag force** proportional to fluid velocity squared (analogy with steady-flow drag):

$$f_D \;=\; \tfrac12\,\rho_w\,C_D\,D\,u\,\vert u\vert$$

2. An **inertia force** proportional to fluid acceleration (analogy with potential-flow added mass):

$$f_I \;=\; \rho_w\,C_M\,\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}$$

The Morison equation is the sum:

$$\boxed{\;f(z,t) \;=\; \tfrac12\,\rho_w\,C_D\,D\,u\,\vert u\vert \;+\; \rho_w\,C_M\,\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}\;}$$

**Validity:** $D/\lambda < 0.2$ (slender-body, no diffraction). Typical empirical coefficients (per DNV-RP-C205):

| Coefficient | Smooth Cylinder | Rough Cylinder |
|-------------|-----------------|----------------|
| $C_D$ | 0.6 – 0.8 | 0.8 – 1.2 |
| $C_M$ | 1.5 – 1.8 | 1.8 – 2.0 |

Both coefficients depend on the **Keulegan-Carpenter number** $KC = u_m T / D$ and the **Reynolds number** $Re = u_m D / \nu$. (7 marks)

**Part (b) — Exceedance Probability**

**Step 1 — Probability for $T_R = 100$ yr, $L = 30$ yr:**

$$P_e(L) \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{T_R}\right)^{L} \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{100}\right)^{30}$$

$$= \; 1 - (0.99)^{30}$$

Compute $(0.99)^{30}$:

$(0.99)^{10} = 0.904382$
$(0.99)^{20} = (0.904382)^2 = 0.817907$
$(0.99)^{30} = (0.99)^{20} \times (0.99)^{10} = 0.817907 \times 0.904382 = 0.73970$

Therefore:

$$P_e(30) \;=\; 1 - 0.73970 \;=\; 0.2603 \;\approx\; \boxed{\;26.0\%\;}$$

**Step 2 — Find $T_R$ such that $P_e < 5\%$ for $L = 30$ yr:**

Set $P_e = 0.05$:

$$0.05 \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{T_R}\right)^{30}$$

$$\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{T_R}\right)^{30} \;=\; 0.95$$

Take the 30th root:

$$1 - \dfrac{1}{T_R} \;=\; (0.95)^{1/30}$$

Compute $(0.95)^{1/30}$ using the approximation $\ln(0.95) \approx -0.05129$:

$$\dfrac{\ln(0.95)}{30} \;=\; \dfrac{-0.05129}{30} \;=\; -0.0017097$$

$$(0.95)^{1/30} \;=\; e^{-0.0017097} \;\approx\; 0.99829$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{1}{T_R} \;=\; 1 - 0.99829 \;=\; 0.00171$$

$$T_R \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.00171} \;\approx\; \boxed{\;584.8 \;\text{years}\;}$$

The designer should specify approximately a **600-year return period** to satisfy the 5% exceedance criterion over 30 years. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute-value brackets** in the Morison drag term: it must be $u\,\vert u\vert$, not $u^2$. Without the absolute value, the drag force becomes *negative* during the return-flow half-cycle — physically wrong.
> 2. **Confusing $H_s$ and $H_{\max}$:** $H_s$ is the significant wave height (statistical), $H_{\max}$ in $N$ waves is approximately $H_{\max} \approx 0.707\,H_s \sqrt{\ln N}$ — examiners *love* testing this.
> 3. **Using the wrong dispersion relation:** For deep water, $k = \omega^2/g$; for finite depth, $k$ comes from $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$. Students frequently apply the deep-water formula in shallow water.
> 4. **In probability questions**, students often write $P_e = L/T_R$ instead of the correct $1-(1-1/T_R)^L$. The latter gives *higher* probability — examiners check this carefully.
> 5. **Omitting partial safety factors**: A bare $H_s$ is *characteristic*, not *design*. Always apply $\gamma_f = 1.35$ (DNV) or appropriate code factor before claiming a "design force".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Environmental Loads**

- **Environmental loads** = metocean actions: wind, wave, current, ice, seismic, thermal, marine growth.
- **Wind force:** $F = \tfrac12 \rho_a C_D A V^2$ — drag-coefficient and projected area are crucial.
- **Significant wave height:** $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ — bridges observation and spectral analysis.
- **Morison equation:** $f = \tfrac12\rho_w C_D D\,u\,\vert u\vert + \rho_w C_M (\pi D^2/4)\,\partial u/\partial t$.
- **Slender-body validity:** $D/\lambda < 0.2$. Use diffraction theory beyond this.
- **Wave dispersion:** $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$. Memorise this — it is *the* most-tested equation.
- **Typical coefficients:** $C_D \approx 0.6$–$1.2$, $C_M \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ (rough/smooth distinction).
- **Current–wave combination:** add $U_c$ to $u(t)$ *before* squaring — non-linear superposition.
- **Return period vs exceedance probability:** $P_e(L) = 1 - (1-1/T_R)^L$, *not* $L/T_R$.
- **Limit states:** SLS, ULS, FLS, ALS — each verified separately.
- **Design codes to cite in exams:** ISO 19900-series, API RP 2A, DNV-OS-E301/DNV-RP-C205.
- **Ice loads** relevant only for Arctic / Antarctic / Baltic operations; cite ISO 19906.
- **Seismic loads** use response spectra, $F_s = m S_a(T)$, not wave spectra.
- **Marine growth** thickness 50–150 mm in tropical waters → adds 30–60% drag if neglected.
- **Units discipline:** SI throughout — kg, m, s, N, Pa. Examiners deduct for mixed units.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Environmental Loads and Design Considerations**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Integrity and Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structural Integrity and Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural Integrity** of a marine vessel is defined as the engineered capability of the ship's hull, superstructure, decks, bulkheads, and appendages to withstand the combined static, dynamic, environmental, and operational loadings throughout its design service life without experiencing unacceptable deformation, collapse, fracture, or loss of watertight/weathertight integrity.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT727 – Maritime Safety, Module 3)**, *Structural Integrity and Inspection* refers to the systematic application of engineering principles, classification society rules (IACS members such as **LR, DNV, ABS, BV, KR**), **SOLAS Chapter II-1** regulations, and **ISM Code** procedures to:

- Preserve the load-bearing capacity of the vessel
- Detect degradation (corrosion, fatigue, buckling, cracking) before it reaches critical limits
- Ensure the ship remains fit for intended service via mandatory periodic surveys
- Provide documented evidence of fitness through **Condition of Class (CoC)** and **Class Records**

### 1.1.1 Key Entities & Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms appear repeatedly in KTU exam questions and IMO/IACS documentation. Memorize the bold ones.

| Term | Meaning (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Hull Girder** | The longitudinal box-girder structure of the ship (keel, bottom, side shell, deck) acting as a single beam |
| **Corrosion** | Electrochemical degradation of steel in marine environments (wastage) |
| **Fatigue** | Progressive localized structural damage caused by cyclic loading (wave-induced stress cycles) |
| **Buckling** | Sudden failure mode where compressed plate/girder loses stability and deflects laterally |
| **Renewal Thickness ($t_{ren}$)** | The minimum allowable thickness below which the member must be renewed |
| **Wastage Allowance** | The difference between *as-built thickness* and *renewal thickness* (typically **0.5 mm to 1.0 mm** for plates) |
| **IACS** | International Association of Classification Societies |
| **ESP** | Enhanced Survey Programme (mandatory for tankers & bulk carriers) |
| **TBT** | Tributyltin coating (anti-foulant, now banned) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a ship as a **giant floating steel box-girder bridge** that is constantly being **battered by ocean waves, attacked by salt water, and stressed by cargo loading**. The hull girder acts like a thin-walled beam — just like a paper towel tube, but on a massive scale, where the deck is the top, the bottom shell is the bottom, and the side shells are the two sides.

Over 20–25 years of service, the steel **thins out (corrodes)**, develops **micro-cracks (fatigue)**, and occasionally **buckles** in compressed zones — exactly like a soda can that has been crushed repeatedly. Engineers cannot afford for the "soda can" to fail, since that means **loss of the ship, cargo, and crew**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine an old car. Every year you take it to the mechanic for an *inspection* (oil check, brake pads, rust spots, frame cracks). The mechanic uses *ultrasonic thickness gauges* and *magnetic particle tests* — the maritime equivalent is exactly the same, but the "car" weighs **100,000 tonnes** and is worth **$60 million**. The regulatory "mechanic visit" is the **Class Survey**.

To keep this "giant car" safe, the maritime industry uses a layered system:
- **Design rules** (IACS Common Structural Rules – CSR) — like the original car engineering drawings
- **Material standards** (grade A, B, D, AH36, DH36 steel) — like the steel alloy
- **Inspection regime** (annual → special → renewal surveys) — like the periodic service
- **NDT methods** (ultrasonic, MPI, dye penetrant) — like the X-ray and stethoscope

This layered approach is what **Structural Integrity Management** means in practice.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and standard values are routinely used in KTU Part B numerical problems.

- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; t/m^3$
- **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \; t/m^3$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$
- **Standard yield stress for mild steel (Grade A):** $\sigma_y = 235 \; MPa$
- **Standard yield stress for high-tensile steel (AH36):** $\sigma_y = 355 \; MPa$
- **Young's modulus for ship steel:** $E = 206 \; GPa$
- **Standard renewal thickness margin for plates:** **6.0 mm minimum** (for plates exceeding 6.5 mm original) or **as per CSR/Class rules**
- **Acceptable corrosion rate in ballast tanks:** typically **0.1 – 0.3 mm/year** (well-maintained)

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can convert between **mm/year corrosion rate** and **percentage wastage** — do not mix units!

---

## 1.4 Module 3 Topic Scope (Syllabus Mapping)

The KTU 2024 scheme for OESFT727 Module 3 covers the following sub-topics under *Structural Integrity and Inspection*:

1. Hull structural components and loadings
2. Corrosion mechanisms in marine environment
3. Fatigue and fracture mechanics basics
4. Buckling and plate/girder strength
5. Classification society survey types
6. Non-destructive testing (NDT) methods
7. Thickness measurement and wastage calculations
8. Condition of Class and Repair Procedures
9. Enhanced Survey Programme (ESP)
10. Goal-Based Standards (GBS) and Common Structural Rules (CSR)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hull Girder Loadings (Vertical Bending Moment)

The ship's hull girder experiences the following primary loads:

1. **Still-water bending moment ($M_{sw}$)** — caused by static weight distribution of cargo, ballast, fuel, and stores.
2. **Wave-induced bending moment ($M_{wv}$)** — caused by passage through waves (sagging and hogging).
3. **Dynamic (slamming) loads** — bottom impact in heavy weather.
4. **Shear forces and torsional moments** — especially in container ships with asymmetric loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sagging** occurs when the ship is supported at the ends (wave crest amidships) — bottom shell in tension, deck in compression.
> **Hogging** occurs when the ship is supported amidships (wave trough amidships) — deck in tension, bottom shell in compression.

### 2.1.1 The Four Classical Failure Modes of a Ship's Hull

A ship's structure can fail in **four primary modes** — every KTU examiner expects the student to identify these:

1. **Material yielding** — stress exceeds yield stress ($\sigma > \sigma_y$)
2. **Plate/shell buckling** — local instability of stiffened panels
3. **Fatigue cracking** — cyclic stress below yield but cracks propagate
4. **Brittle fracture** — sudden low-temperature failure (CTOD criteria, Charpy tests)

The hull is designed such that **none of these modes occurs** under the combined rule design loads.

---

## 2.2 Corrosion Theory in Marine Environment

Corrosion on ships is an **electrochemical (galvanic) process** in which steel (anode) is oxidized in the presence of an electrolyte (seawater) and an oxidizing agent (dissolved oxygen).

The general cathodic reaction in aerated seawater:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

The general anodic reaction (iron dissolution):

$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard corrosion rate for unprotected ship steel in seawater: 0.1 – 0.4 mm/year** (varies with location — boot-top, waterline, ballast tanks, and underwater hull experience different rates).

### 2.2.1 Classification of Corrosion in Ships (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Location | Typical Corrosion Type | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| Underwater hull (immersed) | General wastage + pitting | Moderate (with cathodic protection) |
| Boot-top zone (waterline) | Maximum corrosion (wet/dry cycling) | **Severe** |
| Ballast tanks (upper region) | Severe — alternating wet/dry | **Most critical** |
| Cargo oil tanks | Severe — H₂S + sour crude | Severe |
| Deck plates | Moderate — mechanical wear | Moderate |
| Superstructure | Atmospheric only | Low |

### 2.2.2 Corrosion Allowance Equation

The minimum thickness required at the time of survey is:

$$t_{min} = t_{ren} + \text{wastage margin}$$

Or expressed as a **wastage percentage**:

$$\text{Wastage \%} = \frac{t_{as-built} - t_{measured}}{t_{as-built} - t_{ren}} \times 100$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often phrase this as: *"If a plate has as-built thickness 14 mm and renewal thickness 9 mm, and the measured thickness is 11 mm, find the wastage in percentage."* — Practice this conversion!

---

## 2.3 Fatigue Analysis Basics

Fatigue damage accumulates due to repeated wave-induced stress cycles. The **Palmgren-Miner linear damage rule** is universally applied:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

Where:
- $D$ = cumulative damage (failure expected when $D \geq 1$)
- $n_i$ = number of stress cycles at stress range $\Delta\sigma_i$
- $N_i$ = number of cycles to failure at $\Delta\sigma_i$ (from S-N curve)

The S-N curve (Wöhler curve) for ship steel in air:

$$N = K \cdot (\Delta\sigma)^{-m}$$

Where:
- $K$ = material constant
- $m$ = slope exponent (**typically m = 3 for ship steel**)
- $\Delta\sigma$ = stress range

> [!NOTE]
> For ship structures, **m = 3** is the standard slope for welded joints in seawater (corrosion-fatigue environment).

### 2.3.1 Critical Fatigue Hot-Spots on a Ship

| Hot-spot | Location | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Side shell at bilge | Bilge corner | Highest stress concentration |
| Hatch corners | Forward/aft of hatch coamings | Discontinuity + cyclic stress |
| Rudder horn connection | Aft ship | High stress + welding defects |
| Bracket toes | Bracket-plate intersections | Welded joint + stress concentration |
| Sheer strake at deck | Upper turn of bilge | Cyclic wave load |

---

## 2.4 Buckling of Plates and Stiffeners

When a plate is in compression (e.g., deck plating during hogging), it can lose stability and buckle. The critical elastic buckling stress (Bryan’s equation, simply supported on all four edges):

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^2 \cdot k$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (**0.3 for steel**)
- $t$ = plate thickness
- $b$ = plate width (smaller dimension)
- $k$ = buckling coefficient (depends on aspect ratio $a/b$, edge conditions, and stress gradient)

For a long plate ($a/b > 1$) under uniform compression with simply supported edges:

$$k \approx 4.0$$

For a long plate clamped on both loaded edges:

$$k \approx 6.97$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU questions often simplify this to **k = 4.0** for simply supported plates. The ratio $t/b$ is what determines the panel's slenderness — the higher the $b/t$, the more slender and prone to buckling.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **last-minute revision tool** for any Part B calculation on this topic. **No pipe characters inside math** — all absolute values use $\vert$ rendering.

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Wastage \%} = \dfrac{t_{as} - t_{meas}}{t_{as} - t_{ren}} \times 100$ | Wastage evaluation after gauging | % |
| 2 | $D = \sum n_i / N_i$ | Miner’s cumulative damage | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N = K(\Delta\sigma)^{-m}$ | S-N fatigue curve | cycles |
| 4 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2 k$ | Plate elastic buckling stress | MPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / FS$ | Allowable stress (FS = 1.5 to 2.0) | MPa |
| 6 | $M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_{wv}$ | Hull girder bending moment | kN·m |
| 7 | $\sigma_{bend} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress (section modulus) | MPa |
| 8 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{t_{initial} - t_{final}}{T_{interval}}$ | Corrosion rate per year | mm/yr |
| 9 | $CTOD_{req} = f(\sigma_y, T, a)$ | Fracture toughness (BS 7910) | mm |
| 10 | $t_{min} = t_{ren} + w_{margin}$ | Minimum acceptable thickness | mm |

### 2.5.1 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Wastage %** is calculated **every Class Survey** to decide if a member can remain in service or must be renewed.
- **Miner’s rule** is the basis of the **IACS S21/S22 fatigue strength requirements** for new ships under CSR.
- **Bryan’s equation** governs **slenderness limits** in IACS CSR Chapter 4 — overly slender plates cannot be used.
- **CTOD testing** is required by **IACS UR S6 / Z23** for high-tensile steel welds in critical locations.

> [!NOTE]
> In real shipyards, classification surveyors use proprietary software (e.g., **DNV Nauticus Hull, LR ShipRight, BV VeriStarHull**) which embeds all of the above equations. KTU exams expect you to **derive and apply them manually**.

---

## 2.6 Classification Society Survey Types (IACS Unified Requirements)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most heavily tested sub-topic in Part A (3-mark questions). Memorize the survey cycle.

| Survey Type | Frequency | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **Annual Survey (AS)** | Every year (± 3 months) | Visual check, machinery, safety equipment |
| **Intermediate Survey (IS)** | Within 2.5 – 3 years of class | Closer examination of specific items |
| **Special Survey (SS)** | Every 5 years | Major survey — hull opened up, thickness gauging |
| **Continuous Survey (CS)** | Alternative to SS — items surveyed on rolling basis | Hull parts surveyed in rotation over 5 years |
| **Bottom Survey (BS)** | Between SS — for ships < 15 years, every 2.5 yrs; for ships ≥ 15 yrs, annually | Docking or in-water survey of bottom |
| **Class Renewal Survey** | Every 5 years (due date) | Equivalent to full Special Survey |

### 2.6.1 Enhanced Survey Programme (ESP)

The ESP was developed by **IACS** following the *MV Derbyshire* (1980) and *MV Erika* (1999) casualties and is **mandatory for**:

- **Bulk carriers** (IACS UR Z1, Z2, Z3, Z4, Z7, Z9, Z10, Z11, Z13)
- **Oil tankers** (IACS UR Z4.1, Z5, Z6, Z9.1, Z10.1, Z10.2, Z10.3, Z10.4, Z11.1, Z13)

ESP **three-tier thickness measurement scope**:

1. **Pre-survey thickness measurement plan** — agreed with class **3 months before** survey
2. **Stage 1 (within SS No.1)** — comprehensive gauging
3. **Stage 2 (within SS No.2)** — additional close-up survey of suspect areas
4. **Stage 3 (within SS No.3 and beyond)** — annual/extended close-up surveys for ships **> 15 years old**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wastage Percentage from Thickness Measurements

### Problem Statement
A ship's side shell plate has an **as-built thickness** of $t_{as} = 16.0 \; mm$ and a **class renewal thickness** of $t_{ren} = 10.0 \; mm$. During a Special Survey, the **measured thickness** is found to be $t_{meas} = 13.0 \; mm$. Determine:
(a) The **absolute wastage** in mm.
(b) The **percentage wastage** relative to the wastage allowance.
(c) Whether the plate is acceptable for continued service.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given data.**

From the problem:
- $t_{as} = 16.0 \; mm$
- $t_{ren} = 10.0 \; mm$
- $t_{meas} = 13.0 \; mm$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute wastage.**

The absolute wastage is simply the difference between the original (as-built) and the measured thickness:

$$\Delta t = t_{as} - t_{meas}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\Delta t = 16.0 - 13.0 = 3.0 \; mm$$

> So the plate has lost **3.0 mm** of material.

**Step 3 — Compute the wastage allowance (the total reserve).**

The wastage allowance is the total thickness the plate was permitted to lose before mandatory renewal:

$$t_{allowance} = t_{as} - t_{ren}$$

Substituting:

$$t_{allowance} = 16.0 - 10.0 = 6.0 \; mm$$

**Step 4 — Express wastage as a percentage of the allowance.**

The wastage percentage is the actual wastage expressed as a fraction of the total allowable wastage:

$$\text{Wastage \%} = \frac{\Delta t}{t_{allowance}} \times 100$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{Wastage \%} = \frac{3.0}{6.0} \times 100 = 50 \; \%$$

**Step 5 — Decision logic.**

According to IACS UR Z10.1 (and most class rules), the general rule is:

- If wastage $\leq 75$% of allowance → **Acceptable, no immediate renewal** (subject to class approval)
- If wastage $> 75$% but $< 100$% → **Acceptable with conditions, possibly with coating/repair recommendations**
- If wastage $\geq 100$% → **Renewal mandatory**

> Since $50\% < 75\%$, the plate is **acceptable for continued service**, subject to a class recommendation to monitor this area in the next annual survey.

### 3.1.1 Sanity Check (Unit & Magnitude Consistency)

$$\text{Wastage \%} = \frac{\vert t_{as} - t_{meas} \vert}{\vert t_{as} - t_{ren} \vert} \times 100 = \frac{\vert 16-13 \vert}{\vert 16-10 \vert} \times 100 = 50\%$$

✅ Units cancel correctly, magnitude is reasonable (a 16 mm plate in service for ~8 years at 0.3 mm/yr would lose 2.4 mm → our 3.0 mm is realistic).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Buckling Stress of a Hatch Corner Plate

### Problem Statement
A cargo hatch corner plate (analyzed as a long rectangular plate, $a/b = 2.0$, simply supported on all four edges) has the following properties:
- $E = 206 \; GPa$
- $\nu = 0.3$
- $t = 12 \; mm$
- $b = 600 \; mm$ (shorter dimension)
- Uniformly compressed in the longitudinal direction.

Compute the **elastic critical buckling stress** $\sigma_{cr}$. Take the buckling coefficient for a long simply-supported plate as $k = 4.0$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State Bryan's formula.**

For a rectangular plate under in-plane loading, the elastic critical buckling stress is given by:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^2 k$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Poisson term.**

First evaluate $1 - \nu^2$:

$$1 - \nu^2 = 1 - (0.3)^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$$

**Step 3 — Compute the slenderness ratio squared.**

The plate is $t = 12$ mm thick and $b = 600$ mm wide. The squared ratio is:

$$\left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{12}{600} \right)^2 = (0.02)^2 = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor $\pi^2 E / 12(1-\nu^2)$.**

Convert $E$ to consistent units: $E = 206 \times 10^3 \; MPa = 2.06 \times 10^5 \; MPa$.

$$\frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{(9.8696)(2.06 \times 10^5)}{12 \times 0.91}$$

Compute numerator:

$$(9.8696)(2.06 \times 10^5) = 2.0331 \times 10^6$$

Compute denominator:

$$12 \times 0.91 = 10.92$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{2.0331 \times 10^6}{10.92} = 1.8619 \times 10^5 \; MPa$$

**Step 5 — Multiply all factors together.**

$$\sigma_{cr} = (1.8619 \times 10^5) \times (4.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 4.0$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 1.8619 \times 10^5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 1.8619 \times 1.6 \times 10^{5-3}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 2.979 \times 10^{2} \; MPa$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} \approx 297.9 \; MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check.**

- Mild steel yield stress: $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa.
- Buckling stress 297.9 MPa **exceeds** the yield stress.
- This means the plate will **yield before it buckles** — so the design is "stocky" and buckling is not the governing failure mode. ✅ Realistic for a 12 mm plate of 600 mm width.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** If $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y$ (e.g., 297.9 > 235), buckling is not critical — the plate yields first. This is a stocky plate. If $\sigma_{cr} < \sigma_y$, the plate is **slender** and **buckling governs**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fatigue Damage Using Miner's Rule

### Problem Statement
A side shell frame bracket experiences the following stress range blocks over one voyage:

- $\Delta\sigma_1 = 80 \; MPa$ for $n_1 = 1.0 \times 10^5$ cycles
- $\Delta\sigma_2 = 50 \; MPa$ for $n_2 = 5.0 \times 10^5$ cycles
- $\Delta\sigma_3 = 30 \; MPa$ for $n_3 = 2.0 \times 10^6$ cycles

The S-N curve is $N = 2.0 \times 10^{12} \cdot (\Delta\sigma)^{-3}$ (in air, for the welded joint).

Compute the cumulative damage $D$ and comment on fitness for service.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $N_i$ for each stress range.**

Using $N_i = K \cdot (\Delta\sigma_i)^{-m}$ with $K = 2.0 \times 10^{12}$ and $m = 3$:

For $\Delta\sigma_1 = 80$ MPa:

$$N_1 = 2.0 \times 10^{12} \times (80)^{-3}$$

$$(80)^{-3} = \dfrac{1}{80^3} = \dfrac{1}{512000} = 1.9531 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$N_1 = 2.0 \times 10^{12} \times 1.9531 \times 10^{-6} = 3.9062 \times 10^6 \; cycles$$

For $\Delta\sigma_2 = 50$ MPa:

$$N_2 = 2.0 \times 10^{12} \times (50)^{-3}$$

$$(50)^{-3} = \dfrac{1}{125000} = 8.0 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$N_2 = 2.0 \times 10^{12} \times 8.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^7 \; cycles$$

For $\Delta\sigma_3 = 30$ MPa:

$$N_3 = 2.0 \times 10^{12} \times (30)^{-3}$$

$$(30)^{-3} = \dfrac{1}{27000} = 3.7037 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$N_3 = 2.0 \times 10^{12} \times 3.7037 \times 10^{-5} = 7.4074 \times 10^7 \; cycles$$

**Step 2 — Compute the damage contribution for each block.**

Using $D_i = n_i / N_i$:

$$D_1 = \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{3.9062 \times 10^6} = 0.02560$$

$$D_2 = \frac{5.0 \times 10^5}{1.6 \times 10^7} = 0.03125$$

$$D_3 = \frac{2.0 \times 10^6}{7.4074 \times 10^7} = 0.02700$$

**Step 3 — Sum the damages.**

$$D = D_1 + D_2 + D_3$$

$$D = 0.02560 + 0.03125 + 0.02700 = 0.08385$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 0.084}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on service fitness.**

Since $D \approx 0.084 < 1.0$, the structure has used only about **8.4%** of its fatigue life in this voyage. Per Miner's rule, the design service life has not been exhausted — the structure is fit for service.

> [!NOTE]
> Cumulative damage accumulates over the ship's life. When the running sum reaches 1.0, fatigue cracking is expected at this detail.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Corrosion Rate Between Two Surveys

### Problem Statement
A bottom plate in a ballast tank was measured as $t_1 = 18.0 \; mm$ at a survey 5 years ago. It is now measured as $t_2 = 16.4 \; mm$. The renewal thickness is $t_{ren} = 10.0 \; mm$.

(a) Calculate the average corrosion rate in mm/yr.
(b) Predict when the plate will reach renewal thickness if the rate remains constant.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute thickness loss over the 5-year period.**

$$\Delta t = t_1 - t_2 = 18.0 - 16.4 = 1.6 \; mm$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average corrosion rate.**

$$C_{rate} = \frac{\Delta t}{T_{interval}} = \frac{1.6}{5} = 0.32 \; mm/year$$

**Step 3 — Determine remaining service life until renewal thickness.**

The plate is currently at $t_2 = 16.4 \; mm$. The remaining thickness above renewal is:

$$t_{rem} = t_2 - t_{ren} = 16.4 - 10.0 = 6.4 \; mm$$

The years until renewal at the current rate:

$$T_{remain} = \frac{t_{rem}}{C_{rate}} = \frac{6.4}{0.32} = 20.0 \; years$$

$$\boxed{C_{rate} = 0.32 \; mm/yr, \quad T_{remain} = 20 \; years}$$

> [!TIP]
> In real life, corrosion rates **accelerate** with age (coating breakdown, etc.), so class surveyors add a **safety factor** and may recommend earlier renewals or protective coating.

---
