# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Differentiate between Annual Survey and Special Survey of a ship's hull under IACS classification rules. List any three items specifically covered in a Special Survey.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Aspect | Annual Survey | Special Survey |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | Every year (± 3 months) | Every 5 years |
| **Scope** | Visual examination, confirmation of class | Comprehensive, opened-up examination |
| **Thickness gauging** | Spot or random | Systematic, full TMCP plan |
| **Documentation** | Annual class endorsement | Full class renewal certificate |

**Three items in Special Survey:**
1. **Docking** (or in-water bottom survey) and complete examination of underwater hull
2. **Internal examination of all tanks** (ballast, cargo, void spaces) including thickness gauging per the TMCP
3. **Operational testing of all safety equipment, machinery, and emergency systems**

> Distribution: 1 mark for tabular differentiation, 1 mark for naming the survey scope difference, 1 mark for the three Special Survey items.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**List and briefly explain any three Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods used in ship structural inspection, stating one specific application of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

1. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — Uses high-frequency sound waves transmitted through a couplant gel into the steel. Measures **thickness** and detects **internal flaws, laminations, and sub-surface cracks**. Application: Hull plate thickness measurement in ballast tanks.

2. **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** — Magnetizes the component; iron particles accumulate at surface/near-surface cracks, making them visible. Application: Detection of **fatigue cracks at welded joints** of side shell frames (ferrous materials only).

3. **Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)** — A coloured dye is applied to a clean surface, penetrates any open surface cracks, then is removed and a developer reveals the cracks. Application: **Surface crack detection on non-magnetic materials** (e.g., stainless-steel cargo piping, austenitic welds).

> Distribution: 1 mark per NDT method (0.5 method + 0.5 application).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Wastage Analysis & Class Survey Interpretation

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 3]**

A bulk carrier's **port-side bilge strake plate** has the following survey data:
- As-built thickness: $t_{as} = 20.0 \; mm$
- Class renewal thickness: $t_{ren} = 11.0 \; mm$
- Latest Special Survey gauging: $t_{meas} = 14.5 \; mm$

#### (a) Compute the **wastage percentage** relative to the wastage allowance. Decide whether the plate is acceptable, needs repair recommendation, or requires mandatory renewal, citing the IACS criterion. (7 marks)

#### (b) Describe the **Enhanced Survey Programme (ESP)** applicable to a 17-year-old bulk carrier. Explain the **stages of thickness measurement** and the **scope of close-up survey** that would apply. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Wastage Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the given data (1 mark).**

$t_{as} = 20.0 \; mm$, $t_{ren} = 11.0 \; mm$, $t_{meas} = 14.5 \; mm$

**Step 2 — Compute absolute wastage (1 mark).**

$$\Delta t = t_{as} - t_{meas} = 20.0 - 14.5 = 5.5 \; mm$$

**Step 3 — Compute the wastage allowance (1 mark).**

$$t_{allow} = t_{as} - t_{ren} = 20.0 - 11.0 = 9.0 \; mm$$

**Step 4 — Compute percentage wastage (2 marks).**

$$\text{Wastage \%} = \frac{\Delta t}{t_{allow}} \times 100 = \frac{5.5}{9.0} \times 100 = 61.11 \; \%$$

**Step 5 — Apply IACS criterion and decision (2 marks).**

According to **IACS UR Z10.2 / Z11** and the general class rule:
- Wastage $\leq 75\%$ → **Acceptable, no immediate action** (eligible for continued service with possible annual spot checks)
- Wastage $> 75\%$ → **Acceptable with substantial corrosion / condition of class** (e.g., renewal within next SS or coating required)
- Wastage $\geq 100\%$ → **Mandatory renewal**

> Since $61.11\% < 75\%$, the plate is **acceptable for continued service** without immediate renewal. The surveyor may issue a recommendation for close monitoring and possibly protective coating in the bilge area.

$$\boxed{\text{Wastage} = 61.11\% \rightarrow \text{Acceptable, continued service with annual monitoring}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — ESP for 17-Year-Old Bulk Carrier (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define ESP and applicability (1 mark).**

The **Enhanced Survey Programme (ESP)** was developed by IACS as a comprehensive regime for bulk carriers (UR Z7–Z13) and oil tankers (UR Z4.1, Z10.1, etc.) following major casualties. For a **17-year-old bulk carrier**, ESP is **mandatory** and the third Special Survey or later is in progress.

**Step 2 — Thickness Measurement Stages (2 marks).**

ESP divides thickness measurement into **three progressive stages**:

- **Stage 1** (Special Survey No. 1, at 5 years): Comprehensive thickness measurement as per the **TMCP** (Thickness Measurement Compliance Plan) agreed with class.
- **Stage 2** (Special Survey No. 2, at 10 years): Additional and more extensive thickness measurement scope, including areas found suspect in Stage 1.
- **Stage 3** (Special Survey No. 3 and onwards, age > 15 years): The most comprehensive scope — every plate in critical areas must be gauged, and the survey scope is extended to cover all ESP-identified hot-spots.

**Step 3 — Close-up Survey Scope (2 marks).**

For a **17-year-old bulk carrier**, ESP requires **annual close-up surveys** in addition to the Special Survey close-up requirements. Close-up surveys cover:

- **Bilge hopper knuckle** (cargo hold lower turn of bilge)
- **Lower side shell strake** (adjacent to the bilge)
- **Forward and aft of cargo hold bulkheads** (transverse bulkhead stools)
- **Sheer strake at the upper turn of bilge**
- **Deck plate adjacent to hatch corners**
- **All cross-deck structures and brackets**

The surveyor physically enters these spaces and inspects for **cracks, severe corrosion, buckles, and coating breakdown**.

**Step 4 — Repair Recommendation Triggers (1 mark).**

If wastage exceeds the IACS trigger limits (typically 75% for general areas and **substantial corrosion** limits in ESP), **renewal or repair is required within a defined window** (e.g., before next sailing, within 3 months, or before next SS — depending on severity).

**Step 5 — Concluding Statement (1 mark).**

> Thus, the **ESP regime for a 17-year-old bulk carrier** is highly intrusive, with annual close-ups, full TMCP, and Stage 3 scope. The goal is **early detection of critical wastage** before catastrophic failure of the hull girder.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Fatigue & Plate Buckling

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3]**

A structural detail on a container ship experiences a **variable amplitude stress spectrum**:

- Block 1: $\Delta\sigma_1 = 90 \; MPa$, $n_1 = 2 \times 10^4$ cycles
- Block 2: $\Delta\sigma_2 = 70 \; MPa$, $n_2 = 1 \times 10^5$ cycles
- Block 3: $\Delta\sigma_3 = 50 \; MPa$, $n_3 = 5 \times 10^5$ cycles

The S-N curve for the welded detail in seawater (corrosion-fatigue) is:

$$N = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \cdot (\Delta\sigma)^{-3}$$

A **stiffened panel** on the deck has the following properties:
- Plate dimensions: $a = 1200 \; mm$, $b = 600 \; mm$ (longer side / shorter side)
- Plate thickness: $t = 10 \; mm$
- Material: $E = 206 \; GPa$, $\nu = 0.3$
- Boundary conditions: Simply supported on all four edges, uniform longitudinal compression
- Use $k = 4.0$ for long simply-supported plates.

#### (a) Compute the **cumulative fatigue damage** using **Miner's rule** and comment on fitness for service. (7 marks)

#### (b) Compute the **elastic critical buckling stress** $\sigma_{cr}$ for the stiffened panel and state whether buckling or yielding governs the failure. Assume $\sigma_y = 235 \; MPa$. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Fatigue Damage (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State Miner's rule (1 mark).**

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $N_i$ for each stress range (3 marks).**

Using $N = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \cdot (\Delta\sigma)^{-3}$:

For $\Delta\sigma_1 = 90$ MPa:

$$N_1 = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \times (90)^{-3} = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \times \frac{1}{729000} = 1.372 \times 10^6 \; cycles$$

For $\Delta\sigma_2 = 70$ MPa:

$$N_2 = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \times (70)^{-3} = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \times \frac{1}{343000} = 2.915 \times 10^6 \; cycles$$

For $\Delta\sigma_3 = 50$ MPa:

$$N_3 = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \times (50)^{-3} = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \times \frac{1}{125000} = 8.0 \times 10^6 \; cycles$$

**Step 3 — Compute damage per block (1 mark).**

$$D_1 = \frac{2 \times 10^4}{1.372 \times 10^6} = 0.01457$$

$$D_2 = \frac{1 \times 10^5}{2.915 \times 10^6} = 0.03431$$

$$D_3 = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{8.0 \times 10^6} = 0.06250$$

**Step 4 — Sum the damages and decide (2 marks).**

$$D = 0.01457 + 0.03431 + 0.06250 = 0.11138$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 0.111}$$

**Step 5 — Comment.**

Since $D = 0.111 < 1.0$, the detail has used approximately **11.1%** of its fatigue life. The structure is **fit for continued service** under this loading spectrum.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the spectrum would repeat across many voyages, and $D$ accumulates linearly. At $D = 1.0$, fatigue cracking is expected. KTU exams may ask the student to estimate **how many voyages** remain before $D = 1.0$: here, remaining fraction = $(1 - 0.111) = 0.889$, so approximately **8 more similar voyages**.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Plate Buckling (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State Bryan's equation (1 mark).**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^2 k$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values (1 mark).**

- $E = 206 \times 10^3 \; MPa$
- $\nu = 0.3$, so $1 - \nu^2 = 0.91$
- $t = 10 \; mm$, $b = 600 \; mm$
- $k = 4.0$

**Step 3 — Compute the slenderness ratio squared (1 mark).**

$$\left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{10}{600} \right)^2 = (0.01667)^2 = 2.778 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor (2 marks).**

$$\frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2.06 \times 10^5}{12 \times 0.91} = \frac{2.033 \times 10^6}{10.92} = 1.862 \times 10^5 \; MPa$$

**Step 5 — Multiply all factors (1 mark).**

$$\sigma_{cr} = 1.862 \times 10^5 \times 2.778 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.0$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 1.862 \times 10^5 \times 1.111 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 2.069 \times 10^2 \; MPa$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} \approx 206.9 \; MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with yield stress and conclude (1 mark).**

- $\sigma_{cr} = 206.9 \; MPa$
- $\sigma_y = 235 \; MPa$
- Since $\sigma_{cr} < \sigma_y$, the panel is **slender** and **buckling governs** the failure mode (the plate will buckle before it yields).

> [!TIP]
> If the panel were under $b = 400$ mm (with same $t = 10$ mm), the slenderness ratio $t/b$ would increase, $\sigma_{cr}$ would be larger, and yielding might govern instead. **Stiffer plates resist buckling better** — that's why thick plate is used in high-stress zones.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on this topic (KTU ESE pattern observed over multiple years):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up as-built vs. renewal thickness in the wastage formula.** Many students write $\frac{t_{meas}}{t_{ren}} \times 100$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct denominator is the *allowance* ($t_{as} - t_{ren}$), not $t_{ren}$ itself.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in corrosion rate problems.** Corrosion rate is **mm/year**, not mm only. Always divide thickness loss by the time interval in years.
>
> 3. **Wrong exponent in Miner's rule.** Some students confuse $m = 3$ with $m = 2$. For ship steel S-N curves, **$m = 3$ is standard** for welded joints in air, and the slope is sometimes shallower (e.g., $m = 5$ for some tubular joints) — read the problem.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Poisson's ratio in buckling problems.** Bryan's formula has the $12(1-\nu^2)$ denominator — students who write $12$ alone get **only 1 mark** in a 7-mark sub-part.
>
> 5. **Not stating the conclusion.** A bare numerical answer without a decision (acceptable / renewal / buckles / yields) **loses 1–2 marks** as KTU examiners award marks for engineering judgement.
>
> 6. **Confusing Sagging and Hogging.** Sagging = ends up, mid down (U-shape) — bottom in tension. Hogging = ends down, mid up (∩-shape) — deck in tension. **Mixing these up is a common Part A trap.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — memorize these bullets the night before the exam.**

### Core Definitions
- **Structural Integrity** = Hull's ability to withstand all design loads without unacceptable damage throughout its service life.
- **Hull Girder** = The longitudinal box-beam structure of the ship (keel + bottom + side shell + deck).
- **Renewal Thickness ($t_{ren}$)** = The minimum permissible thickness; below this, **mandatory renewal**.
- **Wastage Allowance** = $t_{as-built} - t_{ren}$.
- **Wastage %** = $\frac{t_{as} - t_{meas}}{t_{as} - t_{ren}} \times 100$.

### Critical Survey Triggers (IACS)
- Wastage $\leq 75\%$ → **Acceptable, no action**
- Wastage $> 75\%$ → **Acceptable with substantial corrosion, condition of class**
- Wastage $\geq 100\%$ → **Mandatory renewal**

### IACS Survey Cycle
- **Annual Survey (AS)** → every year
- **Intermediate Survey (IS)** → within 2.5–3 yrs of class
- **Special Survey (SS)** → every 5 years (renewal)
- **Continuous Survey (CS)** → alternative to SS, items on rolling basis
- **Bottom Survey (BS)** → 2.5 yrs (or annually for > 15 yrs)

### Failure Modes (the 4 classical modes)
1. **Yielding** — stress $>$ yield
2. **Buckling** — local instability of compressed plates
3. **Fatigue** — cyclic damage (Miner's rule)
4. **Brittle Fracture** — low temperature, high strain rate (CTOD)

### Key Formulas (must know)
- $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left( \dfrac{t}{b} \right)^2 k$
- $D = \sum n_i / N_i$
- $N = K(\Delta\sigma)^{-m}$, with $m = 3$ for ship steel
- $C_{rate} = \dfrac{t_1 - t_2}{T_{interval}}$ (mm/yr)

### NDT Method Selection
- **Surface crack on steel** → **MPI**
- **Surface crack on stainless** → **DPT**
- **Internal flaw / thickness** → **UT**
- **Permanent record of internal flaw** → **RT (X-ray)**
- **Coating thickness** → **Coating thickness gauge (eddy current)**

### Sagging vs. Hogging
- **Sagging (U-shape)**: ends up, middle down. Bottom shell **tension**, deck **compression** (risks deck buckling).
- **Hogging (∩-shape)**: ends down, middle up. Deck **tension**, bottom shell **compression** (risks bottom buckling).

### ESP Trigger Age
- **Bulk carriers and oil tankers > 15 years** must undergo **annual ESP close-up surveys** in addition to the Special Survey.
- ESP code reference for bulk carriers: **IACS UR Z7, Z9, Z10, Z11, Z13**.
- ESP code reference for tankers: **IACS UR Z4.1, Z5, Z10.1, Z10.2, Z10.3, Z10.4**.

### GBS & CSR
- **Goal-Based Standards (GBS)** — IMO Resolution MSC.296(87) — new ships must meet functional performance standards.
- **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** — IACS unified rules for **bulk carriers (CSR-B)** and **oil tankers (CSR-T)** — implemented from 1 July 2015 onwards.
- **CSR for Double Hull Oil Tankers (CSR-OT)** — separate code, applies to tankers $\geq 150$ m.

### Hot-spot Corrosion Locations (memorize for Part A)
1. **Boot-top zone** (alternating wet/dry)
2. **Ballast tank upper regions**
3. **Cargo oil tank tops** (sour crude + H₂S)
4. **Bilge keels and their connection**
5. **Sheer strake and gunwale connections**
6. **Rudder and propeller aperture area**

### Common KTU Pitfalls (re-emphasized)
- Always include **units** in numerical answers.
- Always end with a **decision/conclusion** (acceptable/renewal/buckles/yields).
- For fatigue, **sum all damage contributions** before comparing to 1.0.
- For buckling, **compare $\sigma_{cr}$ with $\sigma_y$** explicitly.
- For NDT, **match the method to the defect type AND the material** (e.g., MPI works only on ferromagnetic steel).

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — STRUCTURAL INTEGRITY AND INSPECTION**

topics.title: Marine Pollution and Environmental Protection
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marine Pollution and Environmental Protection

# Marine Pollution and Environmental Protection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Learning Outcome (CO Mapping):** Upon completing this module, the student must be able to identify, classify, and evaluate the various forms of marine pollution, interpret the international regulatory frameworks (primarily **MARPOL 73/78** and **IMO conventions**), and apply operational control measures onboard ships to ensure environmental compliance.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Marine Pollution** is defined under **UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea), Article 1, Paragraph 1(4)** as:

> *"The introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) resulting in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources and marine life, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities (including fishing), impairment of quality for use of sea water, and reduction of amenities."*

**Environmental Protection in the Maritime Domain** refers to the structured, multi-layered regulatory, engineering, and operational framework instituted globally (centrally by the **International Maritime Organization – IMO**) to prevent, control, and mitigate the release of harmful substances from ships into the marine and atmospheric environment.

The foundational international treaty governing this is the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978 (MARPOL 73/78)**. MARPOL is the single most important legal instrument covering the prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Technical Annexes of MARPOL 73/78:**
> - **Annex I** – Oil (Operational and Accidental)
> - **Annex II** – Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in Bulk
> - **Annex III** – Harmful Substances Carried in Packaged Form
> - **Annex IV** – Sewage
> - **Annex V** – Garbage
> - **Annex VI** – Air Pollution (entered into force 19 May 2005)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a massive, shared apartment building (the **ocean**) inhabited by billions of tenants (marine life) — fish, corals, plankton, mammals. Every resident depends on the air, water, and cleanliness of the building. Now imagine thousands of **moving trucks** (ships) constantly entering this building, dumping fuel, chemicals, plastic, sewage, and exhaust fumes as they travel through.

Without rules, the building would become unlivable. **MARPOL is essentially the "house rules"** established by the **United Nations of the Ocean (the IMO)** to keep the building clean. Each **Annex** is a separate set of rules for a different type of trash — one for oil, one for chemicals, one for plastic, and so on. **Environmental Protection** is the act of enforcing those rules through ship design, equipment, and operational discipline.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid for exams:** *“MARPOL = 6 Annexes covering OIL, CHEMICALS, PACKAGED GOODS, SEWAGE, GARBAGE, AIR.”*

### 1.3 Key Maritime Pollution Metrics and Constants

The following standardized metrics appear repeatedly in KTU examination questions and IMO circulars:

- **MARPOL Annex VI SOx Limit in ECAs (Emission Control Areas):** **0.10 % m/m (mass by mass) sulphur content** in fuel oil.
- **MARPOL Annex VI SOx Limit (Global outside ECAs):** **0.50 % m/m** (as of 1 Jan 2020).
- **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index):** mandatory baseline for newbuild vessels.
- **Standard Oil Discharge Limit:** **15 ppm** (parts per million) of oil in water from machinery space bilges via OWS (Oil-Water Separator).
- **Sewage Discharge Standard:** generally ≤ **5 miles from nearest land only through approved sewage treatment plant**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categories of Marine Pollution

Marine pollution can be classified into the following structured taxonomy:

1. **Pollution by Oil** — Operational discharge of oily bilge water, cargo oil residues, bunker fuel spills, ballast water contaminated with oil, accidental tanker spills (e.g., Torrey Canyon, Exxon Valdez, Erika, Prestige).
2. **Pollution by Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS)** — Discharge of chemical cargo residues from chemical/product tankers.
3. **Pollution by Packaged Harmful Substances** — Improper jettisoning of containers in storms or accidents (e.g., MSC Napoli 2007).
4. **Pollution by Sewage** — Black and grey water from toilets, hospitals, living quarters.
5. **Pollage by Garbage** — Plastics, food waste, operational waste; the **“All plastics are prohibited from disposal at sea”** rule (Annex V).
6. **Air Pollution** — SOx, NOx, CO₂, PM (Particulate Matter), ODS (Ozone-Depleting Substances), VOC from tankers during loading.
7. **Pollution by Ballast Water** — Invasive species transfer (biological pollution).
8. **Pollution by Antifouling Systems** — TBT (Tributyltin) and other biocide leaching.

### 2.2 MARPOL Annex-wise Detailed Analysis

| **Annex** | **Pollutant Type** | **Key Onboard Equipment / Method** | **Critical Discharge Rule** | **Documentation** |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| **I – Oil** | Oil / Oily Mixtures | OWS (Oil-Water Separator), Sludge Tank, 15 ppm Bilge Alarm, IOPP Cert. | Discharging ≤ **15 ppm** oil-in-water, away from special zones | **IOPPC**, Oil Record Book (ORB) |
| **II – NLS** | Liquid Chemicals | P / A / B / C / D categorization, stripping pumps | Pre-wash, discharge to reception facility | **NLS Certificate**, Cargo Record Book |
| **III – Packaged** | IMDG cargo | Marking & labelling per IMDG Code | Stowage to minimize accidental loss | Dangerous Goods Manifest |
| **IV – Sewage** | Black & grey water | Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) or holding tank | ≥ **12 nm** from nearest land without STP; ≥ **3 nm** if STP fitted | **ISPP** certificate (Sewage) |
| **V – Garbage** | Plastics, food, cargo residues | Incinerator, compactor, garbage record book | **ALL plastics** prohibited at sea | Garbage Management Plan, Garbage Record Book |
| **VI – Air** | SOx, NOx, ODS, CO₂ | Scrubbers, EGR, SCR, Vapour Recovery, low-sulphur fuel | SOx ≤ **0.10 % m/m** in ECA | **IAPP**, **IEE**, Bunker Delivery Notes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Areas under MARPOL:** Mediterranean Sea, Baltic Sea, Black Sea, Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, Persian Gulf, Antarctic (Annex I & V), North Sea (Annex V), North American ECA, US Caribbean ECA, Chinese ECA (Annex VI).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are frequently tested in numerical problems.** Avoid using the vertical pipe symbol inside the table — use LaTeX `\vert` instead.

| **S.No** | **Formula / Concept** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Unit** |
|---------|-----------------------|-------------------|----------|
| 1 | $C_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oil}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 10^{6}$ | Oil-in-water concentration; $V_{\text{oil}}$ oil volume, $V_{\text{total}}$ total bilge volume | **ppm** |
| 2 | $V_{\text{discharge}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{max allowable}}}{C_{\text{actual}}} \cdot V_{\text{total}}$ | Dilution-to-comply calculation for bilge discharge | $\text{m}^{3}$ |
| 3 | $\text{ECA SOx Limit} = 0.10 \,\%\,\text{m/m}$ | Sulphur limit in ECAs | **% m/m** |
| 4 | $\text{Global SOx Limit} = 0.50 \,\%\,\text{m/m}$ | Sulphur limit globally | **% m/m** |
| 5 | $\text{SOx}_{\text{output}} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times C_{S} \times 2 \times \dfrac{64}{32}$ | SOx emission; $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}$ fuel rate, $C_{S}$ sulphur content | **kg/hr** |
| 6 | $\text{EEDI} = \dfrac{CO_{2}\,[\text{tonnes}]}{\text{Capacity} \times \text{V}_{\text{ref}}\,[\text{tons} \times \text{knots}]}$ | Energy Efficiency Design Index baseline | $\text{g CO}_{2} / \text{ton·nm}$ |
| 7 | $\text{EEOI} = \dfrac{\sum_{j} C_{fj} \times C_{CO_{2}j}}{\sum_{i} m_{\text{cargo}i} \cdot D_{i}}$ | Energy Efficiency Operational Indicator | $\text{g CO}_{2} / \text{ton·nm}$ |
| 8 | $V_{\text{ballast}} = A_{\text{tank}} \times L_{\text{tank}}$ | Ballast tank capacity (prismatic) | $\text{m}^{3}$ |
| 9 | $\text{TBT Leaching Rate} = k \cdot C_{\text{TBT}}^{\text{surface}}$ | Pseudo-first-order antifouling release (illustrative) | $\mu\text{g/cm}^{2}\text{/day}$ |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{scrubber}} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{SO}_2\,\text{out}}}{C_{\text{SO}_2\,\text{in}}}$ | Scrubber efficiency | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering Practice

- The **15 ppm oil-in-water equation** is the foundation for every **Oil Record Book** entry and every **Type Approved OWS** certification.
- The **SOx emission equation** is what port state control inspectors and air quality authorities use to verify compliance during **Fuel Oil Sampling Points (FOSPs)** and **Bunker Delivery Note (BDN)** audits.
- **EEDI** determines whether a newly-built vessel can be **flagged and registered** under MARPOL Annex VI Chapter 4; failure means the ship cannot legally operate.
- **Ballast water capacity calculations** are essential for designers and for **BWM (Ballast Water Management) Plan** compliance.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative 1 — Oil Concentration in Bilge Discharge (ppm Calculation)

**Problem Scenario:** A vessel has a bilge holding tank of capacity $200 \text{ m}^{3}$. During cargo operations, $0.6 \text{ m}^{3}$ of oil leaked into the bilge. The Chief Engineer intends to discharge the bilge overboard. Verify compliance with MARPOL Annex I.

**Step 1 — Identify variables and knowns.**

$$V_{\text{total}} = 200 \text{ m}^{3} \,,\qquad V_{\text{oil}} = 0.6 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the oil-in-water concentration formula.**

$$C_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oil}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$C_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{0.6}{200} \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{\text{oil}} = 0.003 \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{\text{oil}} = 3000 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with MARPOL limit.**

The discharge limit is **15 ppm**. Since:

$$3000 \text{ ppm} \gg 15 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

> The bilge water must be processed through the **Oil-Water Separator (OWS)** until the 15 ppm bilge alarm shows compliance, or discharged to a **port reception facility**. Direct overboard discharge is **illegal**.

**Verification:** If the OWS discharges at exactly 15 ppm:

$$V_{\text{discharge legal}} = \dfrac{200 \times 15}{10^{6}} = 0.003 \text{ m}^{3} \text{ of oil permitted}$$

So a **maximum of only 3 litres of oil** is legally allowable in $200 \text{ m}^{3}$ of bilge water.

---

### 3.2 Derivative 2 — SOx Emission from a Marine Engine

**Problem Scenario:** An auxiliary diesel generator consumes **$1.2 \text{ tonnes/hr}$** of HFO with **$2.8 \,\%\,\text{m/m}$** sulphur. Compute the SOx emission rate in $\text{kg/hr}$.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation (IMO standard approximation).**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times C_{S} \times 2 \times \dfrac{M_{\text{SO}_2}}{M_{S}}$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}$ = fuel mass flow rate
- $C_{S}$ = sulphur mass fraction
- $2$ = stoichiometric ratio (each sulphur atom binds one O atom to form SO₂, but actual conversion uses 2 because of conversion of $\text{SO}_{3}$ etc.)
- $\dfrac{M_{\text{SO}_2}}{M_{S}} = \dfrac{64}{32} = 2$ (molecular weight ratio)

**Step 2 — Express values in consistent SI units.**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = 1.2 \, \text{tonnes/hr} = 1200 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$C_{S} = 2.8 \,\%\,\text{m/m} = 0.028$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 1200 \times 0.028 \times 2 \times 2$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 1200 \times 0.028 \times 4$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 134.4 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Verify dimensional consistency.**

- Input: $\text{kg/hr} \times (\text{kg S/kg fuel}) \times (\text{dimensionless}) \times (\text{dimensionless})$
- Output: $\text{kg SO}_{2}\text{/hr}$ ✓

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

This vessel emits **134.4 kg of SO₂ every hour**. Switching to **VLSFO (0.5 % sulphur)** would reduce emission by a factor of $\dfrac{2.8}{0.5} = 5.6$, giving:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2}^{\text{VLSFO}} = \dfrac{134.4}{5.6} = 24 \text{ kg/hr}$$

A **marine scrubber** with $\eta = 95 \,\%$ would achieve:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2}^{\text{scrubbed}} = 134.4 \times (1 - 0.95) = 6.72 \text{ kg/hr}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivative 3 — Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI) Reduction Calculation

**Problem Scenario:** A newbuild bulk carrier has an **EEDI attained value of 14 g CO₂/ton·nm**. The IMO reference line value for its category is **17 g CO₂/ton·nm**, and the reduction factor for the phase is **20 %**.

**Step 1 — Required EEDI formula.**

$$\text{EEDI}_{\text{required}} = (1 - R) \cdot \text{EEDI}_{\text{reference}}$$

Where $R = 0.20$.

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$\text{EEDI}_{\text{required}} = (1 - 0.20) \times 17 = 0.80 \times 17 = 13.6 \text{ g CO}_{2}/\text{ton·nm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare attained vs required.**

$$\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} = 14 \text{ vs } \text{EEDI}_{\text{required}} = 13.6$$

Since $14 > 13.6$, the vessel **fails** the EEDI requirement.

**Step 4 — Compliance shortfall.**

$$\Delta\text{EEDI} = 14 - 13.6 = 0.4 \text{ g CO}_{2}/\text{ton·nm}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering solution.**

The designer must reduce emissions by:

$$\text{Reduction Needed} = \dfrac{0.4}{14} \times 100 \,\% \approx 2.86 \,\%$$

Methods: install **waste heat recovery**, **shaft generator**, **air lubrication system**, optimize hull form, or upgrade propeller.

---

### 3.4 Derivative 4 — Ballast Water Exchange Efficiency

**Problem Scenario:** A tanker has a ballast tank of $5000 \text{ m}^{3}$. It performs **sequential ballast exchange**, replacing $92 \,\%$ of its ballast water with open-ocean water (3.5 % pumping efficiency gain per cycle). Determine the volume of original coastal water remaining after **3 sequential cycles**.

**Step 1 — General formula for sequential exchange.**

$$V_{n} = V_{0} \cdot (1 - f)^{n}$$

Where:
- $V_{0} = 5000 \text{ m}^{3}$ (initial volume)
- $f = 0.92$ (exchange efficiency)
- $n = 3$ (number of cycles)

**Step 2 — Substitute and solve step-by-step.**

After cycle 1:

$$V_{1} = 5000 \times (1 - 0.92)^{1} = 5000 \times 0.08 = 400 \text{ m}^{3}$$

After cycle 2:

$$V_{2} = 5000 \times (0.08)^{2} = 5000 \times 0.0064 = 32 \text{ m}^{3}$$

After cycle 3:

$$V_{3} = 5000 \times (0.08)^{3} = 5000 \times 0.000512 = 2.56 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Overall exchange efficiency.**

$$\eta_{\text{total}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{3}}{V_{0}}\right) \times 100 \,\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{2.56}{5000}\right) \times 100 \,\%$$

$$\eta_{\text{total}} = 99.9488 \,\% \approx 99.95 \,\%$$

**Step 4 — Compliance with D-1 Standard (IMO BWM Convention).**

The D-1 performance standard requires **≥ 95 % volumetric exchange**. Since $\eta_{\text{total}} = 99.95 \,\%$, the vessel **passes** the D-1 standard comfortably.

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** IMO Ballast Water Management (BWM) Convention has two standards: **D-1 (volumetric exchange)** and **D-2 (biological discharge standard)**. By **8 September 2024**, all vessels must comply with **D-2**.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MARPOL 73/78 — Annex Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[MARPOL 73 78 Master Convention] --> A1[Annex I Oil]
    A0 --> A2[Annex II NLS]
    A0 --> A3[Annex III Packaged]
    A0 --> A4[Annex IV Sewage]
    A0 --> A5[Annex V Garbage]
    A0 --> A6[Annex VI Air Pollution]

    A1 --> B1[Oil Record Book ORB]
    A1 --> B2[IOPPC Certificate]
    A1 --> B3[SOPEP]
    A1 --> B4[15 ppm Bilge Alarm and OWS]

    A2 --> C1[NLS Certificate]
    A2 --> C2[Cargo Record Book]
    A2 --> C3[Stripping and Pre Wash]

    A4 --> D1[Sewage Treatment Plant]
    A4 --> D2[Holding Tank]
    A4 --> D3[ISPP Certificate]

    A5 --> E1[Garbage Management Plan]
    A5 --> E2[Garbage Record Book]
    A5 --> E3[Plastic Discharge Banned]

    A6 --> F1[IAPP Certificate]
    A6 --> F2[IEE Certificate]
    A6 --> F3[SOx ECA Rule]
    A6 --> F4[NOx Tier I II III]
    A6 --> F5[EEDI EEOI EEXI CII]
```

### 4.2 Onboard Pollution Prevention Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Pollution Source] --> P1{Source Type}
    P1 --> P1A[Operational]
    P1 --> P1B[Accidental]

    P1A --> Q1[Prevention Engineering Controls]
    Q1 --> Q1A[Double Hull Construction]
    Q1 --> Q1B[Segregated Ballast Tanks]
    Q1 --> Q1C[CODAG and Inert Gas System]

    Q1 --> R1[Operational Controls]
    R1 --> R1A[SOPEP Drill]
    R1 --> R1B[Oil Record Book]
    R1 --> R1C[Crew Training STCW]

    R1 --> S1[Detection and Monitoring]
    S1 --> S1A[15 ppm Bilge Monitor]
    S1 --> S1B[Sloshing and Level Sensors]
    S1 --> S1C[Cargo Tank Monitoring]

    S1 --> T1[Response Measures]
    T1 --> T1A[Oil Spill Containment Booms]
    T1 --> T1B[Skimmers]
    T1 --> T1C[Dispersants and Sorbents]

    T1 --> U1[Reporting and Documentation]
    U1 --> U1A[Port State Control]
    U1 --> U1B[Flag State Audit]
    U1 --> U1C[IMO Reporting]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Ballast Water Management System (BWMS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    X1[Sea Chest Sea Inlet] --> X2[Ballast Pump]
    X2 --> X3[Filter Stage 1 40 micron]
    X3 --> X4[Treatment Unit Selection]
    X4 --> X4A[UV Reactor]
    X4 --> X4B[Electrochemical Chlorination]
    X4 --> X4C[Ozone Generation]
    X4 --> X4D[Deoxygenation with Nitrogen]

    X4A --> X5[Ballast Tank]
    X4B --> X5
    X4C --> X5
    X4D --> X5

    X5 --> X6[Neutralization Unit]
    X6 --> X7[Overboard Discharge]

    Y1[Sediment Sludge] --> Y2[Holding and Dispose at Port]
    X4B --> Y1
    X4C --> Y1

    Z1[BWMP Plan] --> Z2[BWMS Operation Control]
    Z2 --> X4
    Z2 --> Z3[Log Book and Sampling]
```

### 4.4 Air Pollution Control Architecture (MARPOL Annex VI)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K0[Marine Diesel Engine] --> K1[Combustion Process]
    K1 --> K1A[NOx Formation]
    K1 --> K1B[SOx from Fuel Sulphur]
    K1 --> K1C[PM Particulate Matter]
    K1 --> K1D[CO2 GHG]

    K1A --> K2[NOx Reduction]
    K2 --> K2A[Selective Catalytic Reduction SCR]
    K2 --> K2B[Exhaust Gas Recirculation EGR]
    K2 --> K2C[Engine Tuning and Tier I II III]

    K1B --> K3[SOx Reduction]
    K3 --> K3A[Low Sulphur Fuel 0.10 percent in ECA]
    K3 --> K3B[EGCS Scrubber Open Loop Closed Loop Hybrid]

    K1C --> K4[PM Control]
    K4 --> K4A[Diesel Particulate Filter]
    K4 --> K4B[Engine Optimization]

    K1D --> K5[CO2 Reduction]
    K5 --> K5A[EEDI Newbuild Compliance]
    K5 --> K5B[EEXI Existing Ships]
    K5 --> K5C[CII Operational Carbon Intensity]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For diagram questions in the 14-mark Part B, always label each block clearly and annotate the controlling IMO regulation / Annex number beside it.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**Define MARPOL 73/78. List all its Annexes with one example of pollutant each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **MARPOL 73/78** is the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978**, adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** to minimize pollution of the oceans and seas from ships.
>
> **The Six Annexes:**
> | Annex | Subject | Example |
> |-------|---------|---------|
> | I | Oil | Bilge water |
> | II | Noxious Liquid Substances | Chemical tanker cargo residues |
> | III | Packaged Harmful Substances | IMDG containers |
> | IV | Sewage | Black water from toilets |
> | V | Garbage | Plastics, food waste |
> | VI | Air Pollution | SOx, NOx, CO₂ |
>
> *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for listing)*

---

#### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**State the IMO regulation regarding plastic disposal at sea. What is the prescribed document to record garbage disposal?**

**Model Answer:**

> Under **MARPOL Annex V**, the discharge of **all plastics into the sea is strictly prohibited** at all latitudes — this includes synthetic ropes, fishing gear, plastic bags, and even incinerator ash containing plastic residues.
>
> The two mandatory documents are:
> 1. **Garbage Management Plan (GMP)** — required for all ships ≥ 100 GT and every ship certified to carry 15 or more persons.
> 2. **Garbage Record Book (GRB)** — required for ships ≥ 400 GT.
>
> *(3 marks — 1 for plastic rule + 1 for documentation type + 1 for threshold)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often write vague answers like *"MARPOL is for pollution"* — this **loses 2–3 marks**. Examiners expect **specific Annex numbers, threshold limits (ppm, % m/m), certification names (IOPPC, IAPP, IEE), and equipment names (OWS, STP, Scrubber)**. Memorize them.

---

#### **Question B-A (Choice 1):** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **MARPOL Annex VI regulations for air pollution from ships**. Discuss the **SOx Emission Control Areas (ECAs)** and **NOx Tier limits** with current thresholds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vessel's auxiliary boiler consumes **$800 \text{ kg/hr}$** of fuel oil with **$3.5 \,\%\,\text{m/m}$** sulphur content. The vessel is operating inside the **Baltic Sea ECA**. Determine:
- (i) The SO₂ emission rate in $\text{kg/hr}$.
- (ii) Whether the fuel complies with the ECA limit.
- (iii) The emission reduction if a **scrubber with 96 % efficiency** is fitted. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to B-A (a):**

MARPOL Annex VI was adopted in **1997** and entered into force on **19 May 2005**. It addresses air pollution from ships and includes four chapters:

**Chapter I – General** (definitions and application).

**Chapter II – Survey, Certification and Means of Control.**
- Every ship of **≥ 400 GT** must hold an **International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP)**.
- Bunker Delivery Notes (BDN) and Fuel Oil Sampling Point (FOSP) compliance is mandatory.

**Chapter III – Control of Emissions from Ships (SOx, NOx, PM, ODS).**

*SOx Regulation (Reg. 14):*
- **Global limit:** ≤ **0.50 % m/m** sulphur (since 1 Jan 2020).
- **ECA limit:** ≤ **0.10 % m/m** sulphur (since 1 Jan 2015).
- Methods of compliance: low-sulphur fuel, **LNG**, **Exhaust Gas Cleaning System (Scrubber)**, or equivalent approved means.

*NOx Regulation (Reg. 13):*
- **Tier I** (2000): baseline – heavy-duty diesel engine.
- **Tier II** (2011): **80 %** reduction from Tier I for new engines.
- **Tier III** (2016, in ECA only): **80 %** reduction below Tier I, applicable to engines **> 130 kW** installed on ships constructed on or after **1 January 2016** operating in designated **NOx ECAs** (Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA).

**Chapter IV – Energy Efficiency for Ships.**
- **EEDI** for newbuilds.
- **SEEMP** (Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan).
- **EEXI** (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index) – from 2023.
- **CII** (Carbon Intensity Indicator) – operational, from 2023.

> **Conclusion:** MARPOL Annex VI is the central regulatory pillar for **sulphur, nitrogen oxides, particulate matter, and greenhouse gas** emissions, with progressive tightening aligned to IMO's 2050 net-zero ambition.

---

##### **Model Solution to B-A (b):**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = 800 \text{ kg/hr}$
- $C_{S} = 3.5 \,\%\,\text{m/m} = 0.035$
- Operating area: **Baltic Sea ECA**
- Scrubber efficiency: $\eta = 96 \,\%$

**(i) SO₂ emission rate (using standard formula):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times C_{S} \times 2 \times \dfrac{M_{\text{SO}_2}}{M_{S}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 800 \times 0.035 \times 2 \times \dfrac{64}{32}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 800 \times 0.035 \times 2 \times 2$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 800 \times 0.14$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 112 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**(ii) Compliance check:**

Baltic Sea ECA requires $C_{S} \leq 0.10 \,\%\,\text{m/m}$.

Since $C_{S} = 3.5 \,\%\,\text{m/m} \gg 0.10 \,\%\,\text{m/m}$, the fuel **does NOT comply**. The vessel must either:
- Switch to **ULSFO (Ultra-Low Sulphur Fuel Oil)** with ≤ 0.10 % sulphur.
- Switch to **LNG / methanol / ammonia**.
- Install an **EGCS scrubber** certified to **Reg. 4 of Annex VI**.

**(iii) Emission with scrubber fitted:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2}^{\text{scrubbed}} = \dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} \times (1 - \eta)$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2}^{\text{scrubbed}} = 112 \times (1 - 0.96) = 112 \times 0.04$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2}^{\text{scrubbed}} = 4.48 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**Reduction achieved:**

$$\Delta \dot{m} = 112 - 4.48 = 107.52 \text{ kg/hr (saved)}$$

$$\text{Reduction fraction} = 96 \,\%$$

**Equivalent sulphur content after scrubbing for compliance verification:**

$$C_{S}^{\text{effective}} = C_{S} \times (1 - \eta) = 3.5 \times 0.04 = 0.14 \,\%\,\text{m/m}$$

> Note: An EGCS is considered compliant if it **meets $C_{S,\text{eff}} \leq 0.10 \,\%\,\text{m/m}$**. Here the scrubber alone yields $0.14 \,\%$, which is **borderline non-compliant** — a hybrid scrubber with $\eta = 97.2 \,\%$ would be required. This nuance often attracts **bonus marks** from KTU examiners.

---

#### **Question B-B (Choice 2):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]

**(a)** Explain the **MARPOL Annex I requirements for oil pollution** from ships. Discuss the function of the **Oil Record Book (ORB)**, **IOPPC**, and the **15 ppm bilge alarm standard**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vessel's bilge well has collected $180 \text{ m}^{3}$ of oily water contaminated with **$1.5 \text{ m}^{3}$ of oil**. The vessel is in the **North Sea (Annex V special area)**. Calculate:
- (i) The oil concentration in ppm.
- (ii) Whether direct overboard discharge is permitted.
- (iii) Volume of additional clean water required to be added (assume perfect mixing) to dilute bilge water to **exactly 15 ppm** for legal discharge using OWS. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to B-B (a):**

MARPOL **Annex I** (entered into force **2 January 1978**, originally 1973) governs **oil pollution** from machinery spaces and cargo areas of all ships.

**Scope:** Applies to all **oil tankers** and **non-oil tankers** of **≥ 400 GT**.

**Key Requirements:**

1. **Discharge Prohibition Outside Special Areas:**
   - For **oil tankers**: Discharge of oil or oily mixtures is prohibited, except when:
     - The tanker is **> 50 nautical miles** from the nearest land,
     - The tanker is proceeding en route,
     - The instantaneous rate of discharge does not exceed **60 litres per nautical mile**,
     - The total quantity discharged does not exceed **1/15,000** of the total cargo carried,
     - The OWS and filtering equipment are in operation, and 15 ppm monitor shows compliance.

2. **Discharge Inside Special Areas (Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, Persian Gulf, Gulf of Aden, Antarctic):**
   - Stricter: **> 12 nm** from nearest land, discharge not exceeding **60 L/nm**, and additional regulations.

3. **Mandatory Onboard Equipment (Non-Tanker):**
   - **Oil-Water Separator (OWS)** — Type Approved to **15 ppm**.
   - **15 ppm Bilge Alarm** — IMO MEPC.107(49) standard.
   - **Sludge Tank** with capacity determined by fuel and lube oil consumption.
   - **Standard Discharge Connection** for reception facilities.

4. **International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate (IOPPC):**
   - Mandatory for all ships ≥ 400 GT.
   - Issued after initial and renewal surveys.

5. **Oil Record Book (ORB) – Part I (Machinery) and Part II (Cargo/Ballast):**
   - Every operation — **discharge overboard, transfer to another tank, disposal to reception facility, boiler/incinerator burn, accidental discharge** — must be recorded.
   - Each entry must be signed by the **Master** and the **Chief Engineer / Officer in Charge**.

6. **SOPEP (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan):**
   - Required for **every oil tanker ≥ 150 GT** and **non-tanker ≥ 400 GT**.
   - Provides procedures for accidental discharge reporting.

> **15 ppm Bilge Alarm Function:** Continuously samples the effluent from OWS; if oil-in-water exceeds 15 ppm, it triggers an **automatic shut-off** preventing overboard discharge and routes flow back to the bilge tank. This is the **last-line regulatory safeguard**.

---

##### **Model Solution to B-B (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{total}} = 180 \text{ m}^{3}$ (bilge holding tank)
- $V_{\text{oil}} = 1.5 \text{ m}^{3}$
- Target limit: $C_{\text{target}} = 15 \text{ ppm}$
- Operating area: **North Sea — Annex V Special Area** (note: oil limits also apply because of Annex I integration)

**(i) Oil concentration:**

$$C_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oil}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{1.5}{180} \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{\text{oil}} = 0.008333 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{oil}} = 8333.33 \text{ ppm}}$$

**(ii) Discharge permission?**

$$\because 8333 \text{ ppm} \gg 15 \text{ ppm}$$

> **Direct overboard discharge is strictly PROHIBITED.** The bilge must be processed through the **OWS** with continuous 15 ppm monitoring, or disposed of at a **port reception facility**.

**(iii) Volume of clean water required to dilute to exactly 15 ppm:**

We need the oil concentration in the **final total volume** ($V_{\text{total}} + V_{\text{added}}$) to equal 15 ppm.

**Oil remains constant:** $V_{\text{oil}} = 1.5 \text{ m}^{3}$ (assuming perfect mixing and no oil removal).

$$15 = \dfrac{1.5}{V_{\text{total}} + V_{\text{added}}} \times 10^{6}$$

$$V_{\text{total}} + V_{\text{added}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{6}}{15}$$

$$V_{\text{total}} + V_{\text{added}} = 100{,}000 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$V_{\text{added}} = 100{,}000 - 180 = 99{,}820 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{added}} \approx 99{,}820 \text{ m}^{3}}$$

> **Engineering Reality:** It is **physically and economically impossible** to dilute to 15 ppm by adding clean water. This is precisely why **MARPOL mandates an OWS with separation, not dilution.** A real OWS removes oil from water mechanically or via coalescers, achieving 15 ppm with no need for dilution. **This conceptual point is often asked in KTU viva.**

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] MARPOL 73/78 = **6 Annexes** (Oil, NLS, Packaged, Sewage, Garbage, Air). Know all 6.
- [x] **15 ppm** oil-in-water is the magic number for OWS compliance.
- [x] **0.10 % m/m** sulphur in ECA; **0.50 % m/m** global (since 1 Jan 2020).
- [x] **All plastics prohibited** from disposal at sea (Annex V).
- [x] **IOPPC, IAPP, IEE, ISPP** = the four key MARPOL certificates.
- [x] **ORB (Parts I & II)**, **Garbage Record Book**, **Bunker Delivery Notes** = mandatory records.
- [x] **EEDI (newbuilds)** vs **EEXI (existing ships)** vs **CII (operational)** — distinguish carefully.
- [x] **SOPEP** mandatory for tankers ≥ 150 GT.
- [x] **Ballast Water Management Convention D-2** standard is in force from **8 Sept 2024**.
- [x] The conversion factor for SO₂ emission: $\dfrac{M_{\text{SO}_2}}{M_{S}} = \dfrac{64}{32} = 2$, combined with stoichiometric factor 2.
- [x] Special Areas = **Med, Baltic, Black, Red, Persian Gulf, Antarctic** + North Sea (garbage).
- [x] NOx Tier III applies **only in NOₓ ECAs and only to newbuilds ≥ 1 Jan 2016**.
- [x] Dilution is **never** the solution for oily bilge — separation is.
- [x] **Port State Control (PSC)** can inspect ships anywhere; **Flag State** issues certificates.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute 30-second summary:** *"Six annexes, six pollutions, six rules. 15 ppm oil, 0.10 % SOx in ECA, no plastic ever, STP for sewage, ORB & GRB for records, IOPPC & IAPP for certificates, SOPEP for emergencies, EEDI/EEXI/CII for energy, BWM for invasive species."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Pollution Sources and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marine Pollution Sources and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (UNCLOS, 1982 — United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea)**
> *"Marine pollution means the introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) resulting in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources and marine life, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities (including fishing), impairment of quality for use of sea water, and reduction of amenities."*

Under the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL 73/78)** — administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** — marine pollution is categorized into **six operational annexes**, each addressing a specific pollutant type from vessels.

### Key Standardized Metrics (Must Remember for KTU Exam)
- **1 nautical mile (nm)** = **1.852 km** = **1852 m**
- **1 barrel of oil (bbl)** ≈ **0.159 m³** ≈ **159 litres**
- **1 Gross Tonnage (GT)** = 100 cubic feet = **2.831 m³** of enclosed vessel volume
- **MARPOL Annex VI limit for SOₓ emission in Emission Control Areas (ECAs):** **0.10% m/m** (mass by mass)
- **Global cap on sulphur content of marine fuel (post-2020):** **0.50% m/m**
- **Oil Spill Threshold of "Major Spill" (IMO/ITOPF):** **> 700 tonnes**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Ocean as a Giant Bathtub"**
> Imagine the world's oceans as a single, enormous **bathtub with no plug and no drain** (it does drain very slowly via evaporation and currents). Every day, humans *pour into this bathtub* a mixture of:
> - Used motor oil (oil pollution),
> - Plastic bottles and microbeads (plastic pollution),
> - Leftover antibiotics and pesticides (chemical pollution),
> - Hot water from power plant cooling (thermal pollution),
> - Even loud underwater rock concerts (noise pollution).
>
> Since the bathtub is so huge, people assume *"it will dilute and disappear."* But just like a real bathtub, **the waste accumulates faster than the water can refresh itself**, especially in semi-enclosed seas (e.g., the Mediterranean, Baltic Sea, Red Sea). Marine pollution is the **uncontrolled addition of harmful substances or energy** into this finite, slow-flushing bathtub.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for Maritime Safety Officers

Marine pollution is not merely an *environmental* problem — it is a **maritime safety, legal liability, and economic catastrophe** for any shipping company. A single major oil spill can:

- Render a vessel's **Protection and Indemnity (P&I) insurance** void if MARPOL was breached,
- Lead to criminal prosecution of the **Master** under **Section 356–361 of the Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958**,
- Cause **port state detentions** and **flag state sanctions**,
- Trigger **civil liability** under conventions like **CLC 1992** and **Fund Convention 1992**.

Hence, the **"polluter pays" principle** is enshrined in international maritime law.

---

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Box (Kerala Coast Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala-Specific Reference (Often asked in KTU internal exams):**
> The Kerala coast (≈ 590 km long, Arabian Sea) is particularly vulnerable to:
> - **Ballast water discharge** at Cochin Port (Kochi) — one of India's busiest transshipment hubs.
> - **Oil spills** from the *Kochi–Mangalore pipeline terminal* and tanker traffic in the Laccadive Sea.
> - **Plastic and biomedical waste** from coastal municipalities via 41 west-flowing rivers.
> - **Operational discharges** from the fishing trawler fleet (> 50,000 vessels registered in Kerala).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Marine Pollution — The Six Categories Framework

Marine pollution is academically classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — By **Source of Origin** (where the pollutant came from)
### Axis B — By **Type of Pollutant** (what the pollutant is)

KTU questions typically frame the question using **Axis A**, while MARPOL/IMO regulations operate primarily on **Axis B**.

---

## 2.2 Axis A — Classification by Source (GESAMP / UNEP Framework)

The **Group of Experts on the Scientific Aspects of Marine Environmental Protection (GESAMP)** identifies **nine major source categories**. For KTU Module 4, the following six are highest-yield:

### Source Category 1: Land-Based Sources (≈ 80% of marine pollution globally)
**Sources include:**
- Municipal sewage and wastewater outfalls (e.g., Cochin urban drainage),
- Riverine discharge carrying agricultural runoff (fertilizers, pesticides),
- Industrial effluents (textile, tannery, pharmaceutical),
- Coastal aquaculture discharge,
- Urban storm-water runoff (oil, heavy metals, litter),
- Coastal mining and quarrying.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UNEP Estimate:** Approximately **80% of marine pollution originates from land-based activities**, making this the single largest contributor — a high-yield KTU fact.

---

### Source Category 2: Sea-Based Sources (Vessel-Originated)
**Sub-categories:**
- **Operational discharges** — bilge water, ballast water, sewage, gray water, cargo residues.
- **Accidental discharges** — collisions, groundings, structural failures.
- **Intentional discharges** — illegal tank cleaning at sea (called *"Slop disposal"* or *MARPOL violation*).
- **Dumping** — wastes deliberately loaded and discarded at sea.
- **Offshore installations** — oil & gas platforms (e.g., Bombay High in India).

---

### Source Category 3: Atmospheric Deposition
- Airborne particulates from power plants (coal ash, fly ash).
- SOₓ and NOₓ from shipping exhaust (acid rain → ocean acidification).
- **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)** transported via air currents (e.g., DDT, PCBs).
- **Black carbon** and **soot** deposition.
- Radionuclide fallout (legacy of nuclear testing).

---

### Source Category 4: Dumping at Sea
- **Dredged material** from port maintenance.
- **Sewage sludge** (largely banned now).
- **Industrial waste** (historical practice, banned by **London Convention 1972** and **London Protocol 1996**).
- **Inert geological material**.
- **Fish processing waste** (regulated under MARPOL Annex V).

---

### Source Category 5: Offshore Mining and Resource Extraction
- Deep-sea mining discharge plumes.
- Oil & gas exploration drilling fluids and cuttings.
- Sand and aggregate extraction.

---

### Source Category 6: Recreational and Tourism Sources
- Cruise ship sewage, gray water, and food waste.
- Anchor damage to seabed ecosystems (Posidonia seagrass in Mediterranean).
- Marine litter from coastal tourism.

---

## 2.3 Axis B — Classification by Type of Pollutant (MARPOL Annex Mapping)

The **MARPOL Convention** structures the entire regulatory framework around **six pollutant types**, each covered by a dedicated **Annex**:

| Annex | Pollutant Type | Regulated From | Year Added |
|:-----:|:--------------|:--------------:|:----------:|
| **Annex I** | Oil / Oily mixtures | Ships (oilers, tankers) | 1973 |
| **Annex II** | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in bulk | Chemical tankers | 1973 |
| **Annex III** | Harmful Substances in packaged form | All ships | 1973 |
| **Annex IV** | Sewage | All ships ≥ 400 GT or carrying > 15 persons | 1973 (entered force 2003) |
| **Annex V** | Garbage (plastics, food waste, cargo residues) | All ships | 1973 |
| **Annex VI** | Air Pollution (SOₓ, NOₓ, ODS, CO₂) | All ships | 1997 (revised 2008) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** Annex IV and Annex V apply to **ALL ships**, not just tankers. Annex VI's sulphur cap was tightened from **3.50% → 0.50%** globally from **1 January 2020**.

---

## 2.4 Deep Dive — The Five Major Types of Marine Pollution

### Type 1: Oil Pollution (≈ 12% of marine pollution by volume but **highest impact**)

**Mechanism of damage:**
- Spreads into a thin **slick** on the water surface (thickness ≈ 0.1 µm to several mm).
- Reaches coastlines as **tar balls** and **emulsified mousse** ("chocolate mousse").
- Smothers seabirds, blocks sunlight → kills phytoplankton → **biological magnification** up food chain.

**Sub-types:**
- **Crude oil** (heavy vs. light — light is more toxic but evaporates faster).
- **Refined products** (diesel, gasoline — high volatility, fire risk).
- **Bunker fuel** (heavy fuel oil / HFO used in ship engines — persistent).
- **Persistent oils** (asphalt, crude oils with high asphaltene content).

**Behavioural stages of an oil spill:**
1. **Spreading** (gravity–inertia–viscous–surface tension regimes).
2. **Drift** (wind + current, deflection angle ≈ 3–4° to the right of wind in Northern Hemisphere).
3. **Evaporation** (light fractions lost in first 24–48 hours).
4. **Emulsification** ("mousse" formation, water-in-oil).
5. **Dissolution** (water-soluble toxic fractions).
6. **Photo-oxidation** (sunlight breaks down hydrocarbons).
7. **Sedimentation** (oil-coated particles sink).
8. **Biodegradation** (bacteria consume hydrocarbons, slow in cold water).

---

### Type 2: Chemical Pollution (Noxious Liquid Substances, Pesticides, PCBs)

**Source MARPOL Annex:** Annex II.
**Cargo categories under MARPOL Annex II (the "ABC" system):**
- **Category X** — Highest hazard, prohibited from discharge (e.g., halogenated solvents).
- **Category Y** — High hazard, severe restrictions.
- **Category Z** — Moderate hazard, moderate restrictions.
- **Category OS** — "Other Substances" — low hazard, minor restrictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Discharge of Category X substances is **prohibited under any circumstances** within 12 nm of the nearest land. Memorize the **X → Y → Z → OS** hazard hierarchy.

---

### Type 3: Plastic Pollution (Marine Litter / Macro & Microplastics)

**The "Great Pacific Garbage Patch"** — an accumulation zone in the North Pacific Gyre — is approximately **1.6 million km²** (≈ 3× the area of France), containing an estimated **1.8 trillion plastic pieces**.

**Particle size classification:**
- **Macroplastic:** > 25 mm (bottles, bags, fishing nets — **"ghost nets"**).
- **Mesoplastic:** 5–25 mm.
- **Microplastic:** < 5 mm (microbeads, degraded fragments).
- **Nanoplastic:** < 1 µm (newest research area).

**MARPOL Annex V rule:** **Discharge of ALL plastics into the sea is PROHIBITED** — no exceptions, no distance limits.

---

### Type 4: Sewage and Wastewater Pollution

**MARPOL Annex IV standard:**
- **Sewage holding tank** mandatory on ships ≥ 400 GT or carrying > 15 persons.
- Discharge allowed only at **> 12 nm from nearest land**, at **> 4 knots**, with **Comminuted and Disinfected Sewage Treatment Plant (SDTP)** operational.
- Untreated discharge allowed at **> 12 nm** without rate restriction.

**Pollutants in sewage:** pathogens (*E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae*), nutrients (N, P → eutrophication), pharmaceuticals, microplastics from grey water.

---

### Type 5: Heavy Metal and Radioactive Pollution

**Heavy metals of concern:** Mercury (Hg), Lead (Pb), Cadmium (Cd), Arsenic (As), Copper (Cu), Zinc (Zn).

**Mechanism of damage:** **Bioaccumulation** and **biomagnification** — concentration increases **10⁶ to 10⁷ times** up the food chain (phytoplankton → zooplankton → small fish → large fish → human).

**Notable incidents:** Minamata disease (Japan, 1956 — methylmercury), Itai-itai disease (Japan — cadmium).

**Radioactive pollution sources:** nuclear weapon tests, nuclear reprocessing discharges (Sellafield UK, La Hague France), nuclear accidents (Fukushima 2011), lost nuclear submarines (K-27, K-159).

---

### Type 6: Thermal and Noise Pollution (Energy-Based Pollution)

**Thermal pollution:**
- Source: Power plant cooling water, industrial effluent.
- Effect: Reduces dissolved oxygen capacity of water; disrupts breeding cycles of marine life.
- Formula of key concern: **Henry's Law — gas solubility decreases as temperature rises.**

**Noise pollution:**
- Source: Commercial shipping (low-frequency 10–1000 Hz), naval sonar, seismic airgun surveys, pile-driving.
- Effect: Stranding of cetaceans (whales, dolphins), interference with marine mammal communication, masking of fish acoustic signals.
- **International Whaling Commission (IWC)** and **UN General Assembly Resolution 46/215** regulate anthropogenic ocean noise.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:---------------|:--------------|:--------------|
| 1 | $v_{drift} = v_{current} + 0.03 \cdot v_{wind}$ | Oil slick drift velocity | m/s; empirical rule (0°–4° deflection) |
| 2 | $Q_{evap} = k \cdot A \cdot P_{vap}$ | Evaporation rate of oil | kg/s; $k$ = mass-transfer coefficient |
| 3 | $BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Bioconcentration Factor | Dimensionless; BCF > 1000 → "bioaccumulative" |
| 4 | $BMF = \dfrac{C_{predator}}{C_{prey}}$ | Biomagnification Factor | Dimensionless; often 5–10 per trophic step |
| 5 | $C_{SO_x} \leq 0.50\%$ (global) | MARPOL Annex VI sulphur cap | % m/m since 1 Jan 2020 |
| 6 | $C_{SO_x} \leq 0.10\%$ (ECA) | ECA sulphur cap (Baltic, North Sea, N. America) | % m/m since 1 Jan 2015 |
| 7 | $d_{min}^{sewage} = 12 \text{ nm}$ | Min. distance for sewage discharge | nautical miles (MARPOL Annex IV) |
| 8 | $d_{min}^{garbage} = 3 \text{ nm}$ / 12 nm / 25 nm | Min. distance for garbage (by type) | nautical miles (MARPOL Annex V) |
| 9 | $V_{spill} = A \cdot h_{avg}$ | Oil spill volume estimate | m³; $A$ = slick area, $h_{avg}$ = avg. thickness |
| 10 | $S_{oil} = \rho_{oil} \cdot V_{spill}$ | Mass of oil spilled | kg; $\rho_{oil} \approx 800\text{–}980$ kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical formatting note:** All vertical separators in the table are alignment colons (`:-----:`). For absolute-value notation in formulas, the LaTeX expression `$\vert x \vert$` is used instead of the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil spill response planning:** Operators use drift formulas ($v_{drift}$) to pre-position booms and skimmers.
- **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs):** Designed to meet MARPOL Annex IV effluent standards (≤ 200 faecal coliforms/100 mL, suspended solids ≤ 25 mg/L).
- **Sulphur scrubbers / EGCS:** New shipboard systems use seawater scrubbing or hybrid technology to comply with **0.10% ECA sulphur limit**.
- **Ballast water management systems (BWMS):** Use UV, electro-chlorination, or filtration to comply with **BWM Convention (in force 8 Sept 2017)** — directly linked to invasive species and pollution control.
- **Plastics and recycling onboard:** Under MARPOL Annex V, every ship > 100 GT must carry a **Garbage Management Plan** and a **Garbage Record Book**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Oil Slick Drift Velocity (Fay's Equations Simplified)

The **Fay (1969, 1971)** classical theory decomposes oil spreading into four gravity–viscosity–surface tension regimes. The KTU-simplified version focuses on the **quasi-steady state drift** under combined wind and current.

### Given
- Current velocity: $v_{current}$ in m/s
- Wind velocity at 10 m height: $v_{wind,10}$ in m/s
- Empirical deflection coefficient: $C_{d} \approx 0.03$
- Empirical deflection angle: $\theta \approx 3°$ (Northern Hemisphere)

### Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Vector decomposition of wind drag on slick surface.**

The wind exerts tangential stress on the oil slick. By empirical correlation (Stolzenbach, 1977), the slick moves at approximately **3% of the wind speed**, offset by a small angle.

$$
\vec{v}_{wind,drift} = C_{d} \cdot \vec{v}_{wind,10}
$$

**Step 2 — Combine linearly with surface current.**

The slick moves with the surface current plus the wind-coupled drift:

$$
\vec{v}_{slick} = \vec{v}_{current} + \vec{v}_{wind,drift}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude (assuming aligned vectors in the downwind direction).**

$$
v_{slick} = v_{current} + 0.03 \cdot v_{wind,10}
$$

**Step 4 — Direction (accounting for Coriolis deflection).**

In the Northern Hemisphere, the slick deviates to the **right** of the wind:

$$
\theta_{deflect} = +3° \text{ (N. Hemisphere)}, \quad \theta_{deflect} = -3° \text{ (S. Hemisphere)}
$$

### Sample Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** $v_{current} = 0.30$ m/s (eastward), $v_{wind,10} = 10$ m/s (northward), Northern Hemisphere.

**Step 5 — Compute wind drift component.**

$$
v_{wind,drift} = 0.03 \times 10 = 0.30 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Net slick speed (vector sum).**

$$
v_{slick} = \sqrt{(0.30)^2 + (0.30)^2} = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.09} = \sqrt{0.18} \approx 0.424 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to nautical miles per day for SAR planning.**

$$
v_{slick} = 0.424 \text{ m/s} \times \frac{86400 \text{ s/day}}{1852 \text{ m/nm}} \approx 19.8 \text{ nm/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Real slick drift is observed in the range **3–5% of wind speed** plus current. Our result of 19.8 nm/day aligns with operational ITOPF and NOAA reports.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Oil Spill Volume from Slick Area and Thickness

### Given
- Slick area observed via satellite: $A$ in m²
- Average slick thickness: $h_{avg}$ in m (typically 0.1 µm to 1 mm for thin sheens)
- Oil density: $\rho_{oil}$ in kg/m³

### Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Volumetric definition of spilled oil.**

The spilled oil is a film of area $A$ and mean thickness $h_{avg}$:

$$
V_{spill} = A \cdot h_{avg}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume to mass using density.**

$$
m_{spill} = \rho_{oil} \cdot V_{spill} = \rho_{oil} \cdot A \cdot h_{avg}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass to barrels (oil industry standard).**

$$
V_{spill,bbl} = \dfrac{V_{spill}}{0.158987} \approx 6.29 \cdot V_{spill}
$$

### Sample Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** $A = 10 \text{ km}^2 = 10^7 \text{ m}^2$, $h_{avg} = 0.5 \text{ µm} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$, $\rho_{oil} = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 4 — Compute volume.**

$$
V_{spill} = 10^7 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 5 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Compute mass.**

$$
m_{spill} = 920 \times 5 = 4600 \text{ kg} = 4.6 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to barrels.**

$$
V_{spill,bbl} = 6.29 \times 5 = 31.45 \text{ bbl}
$$

**Step 7 — Classify severity (IMO/ITOPF scale).**

$$
\text{Result: } 4.6 \text{ tonnes} < 7 \text{ tonnes} \Rightarrow \textbf{Minor Spill}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Severity Classification (ITOPF):**
> - **Small** : < 7 tonnes
> - **Medium** : 7 – 700 tonnes
> - **Major** : > 700 tonnes

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bioconcentration Factor (BCF) Determination

### Given
- A pollutant with octanol–water partition coefficient: $\log K_{ow} = 5.2$ (a moderately lipophilic compound, e.g., DDT-like)
- Water concentration: $C_{water} = 0.001$ mg/L
- Empirical regression (Veith et al., 1979): $\log BCF = 0.76 \cdot \log K_{ow} - 0.23$

### Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Substitute $\log K_{ow}$ into the regression.**

$$
\log BCF = 0.76 \times 5.2 - 0.23
$$

**Step 2 — Compute intermediate.**

$$
\log BCF = 3.952 - 0.23 = 3.722
$$

**Step 3 — Take antilog to obtain BCF.**

$$
BCF = 10^{3.722} \approx 5275
$$

**Step 4 — Compute organism concentration.**

$$
C_{organism} = BCF \cdot C_{water} = 5275 \times 0.001 = 5.275 \text{ mg/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

$$
BCF \approx 5275 > 1000 \Rightarrow \textbf{Bioaccumulative substance} \Rightarrow \textbf{Banned under MARPOL Annex I/II protocols}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Sulphur Emission Compliance via Mass Balance

A ship burns fuel with sulphur content $C_S = 3.5\%$ m/m. To comply with ECA limit of 0.10% m/m, the Master may use an **Exhaust Gas Cleaning System (EGCS / scrubber)** in **open-loop mode** using seawater scrubbing.

**Given:**
- Fuel flow: $\dot{m}_{fuel} = 1000$ kg/h
- $C_{S,before} = 3.5\%$
- $C_{S,after} = 0.10\%$ (target)
- Sea water alkalinity: assume neutralization complete.

**Step 1 — Sulphur mass flow entering system.**

$$
\dot{m}_{S,in} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times C_{S,before} = 1000 \times 0.035 = 35 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Sulphur mass flow leaving stack (after scrubbing).**

$$
\dot{m}_{S,out} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times C_{S,after} = 1000 \times 0.001 = 1 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Sulphur removed per hour.**

$$
\Delta \dot{m}_{S} = 35 - 1 = 34 \text{ kg/h} \rightarrow \text{washed into sea as SO}_4^{2-}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent SO₂ mass (molecular weight ratio).**

$$
\dot{m}_{SO_2,removed} = 34 \times \frac{64}{32} = 34 \times 2 = 68 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Open-loop scrubbers transfer pollution from **air to water** — they are controversial under the **HELCOM** and **US EPA Vessel General Permit (VGP)** rules. Closed-loop scrubbers capture washwater for shore disposal.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Oil Spill Behavioural Stages (Fay Clock)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start0[Spill Event]
    start0 --> stage1[Stage 1 Spreading Gravity Inertia Viscous Surface Tension]
    stage1 --> stage2[Stage 2 Drift Wind plus Current 3 to 4 percent of wind]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3 Evaporation Light Fractions 0 to 48 hours]
    stage3 --> stage4[Stage 4 Emulsification Mousse Formation]
    stage4 --> stage5[Stage 5 Dissolution Water Soluble Toxic Fractions]
    stage5 --> stage6[Stage 6 Photo Oxidation Sunlight Driven]
    stage6 --> stage7[Stage 7 Sedimentation Oil Coated Particles Sink]
    stage7 --> stage8[Stage 8 Biodegradation Bacterial Action Slow in Cold Water]

    stage1 --> recovery1[Response Skimmers and Booms]
    stage2 --> recovery1
    stage3 --> recovery1
    stage4 --> recovery2[Recovery Difficult Mousse Hard to Recover]
    stage5 --> recovery2
    stage6 --> recovery2
    stage7 --> recovery3[Recovery Requires Shoreline Cleanup]
    stage8 --> recovery4[Recovery Natural Attenuation Long Term]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Response Timing:** Recovery is most effective in **stages 1–3** (first 24–48 hours). After emulsification, recovery efficiency drops dramatically. This drives the **Tier 1, 2, 3 response levels** in the *OSRO (Oil Spill Response Organization)* framework.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define marine pollution as per UNCLOS, 1982. List any four major sources of marine pollution.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** As per Article 1(1)(4) of UNCLOS 1982, *"Marine pollution means the introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment resulting in deleterious effects such as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities, impairment of sea water quality, and reduction of amenities."*

**Four major sources (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Land-based sources** (municipal sewage, industrial effluents, agricultural runoff).
2. **Sea-based / vessel-originated sources** (operational discharges, accidental spills).
3. **Atmospheric deposition** (SOₓ, NOₓ, POPs, black carbon).
4. **Dumping at sea** (dredged material, industrial waste).
