## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 End-Semester Model]**

**(a)** Classify marine pollution by source of origin. Discuss land-based and sea-based sources in detail, giving at least three examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **eight behavioural stages of an oil spill** (Fay's clock). List the factors that affect the persistence of oil at sea. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Classification by source (1 mark):** Marine pollution is classified into six major source categories per GESAMP/UNEP framework: **land-based, sea-based, atmospheric, dumping, offshore mining, and recreational sources**.

**Land-based sources (3 marks):** These contribute approximately **80%** of marine pollution globally and include:
1. **Municipal sewage outfalls** — discharge of untreated or partially treated domestic wastewater containing pathogens, nutrients (N, P), and emerging contaminants.
2. **Industrial effluents** — heavy metals (Hg, Cd, Pb), dyes from textile units, organic pollutants from petrochemical plants.
3. **Agricultural runoff** — fertilizers (nitrogen, phosphates) and pesticides (organochlorines, organophosphates) carried by rivers into the sea.
4. **Riverine discharge** — Kerala's 41 west-flowing rivers carry terrestrial pollutants directly into the Arabian Sea.
5. **Coastal aquaculture discharge** — antibiotics, uneaten feed, fish faeces.

**Sea-based sources (3 marks):** These originate from vessels and offshore installations:
1. **Operational discharges** — bilge water, ballast water, sewage, gray water, cargo washings.
2. **Accidental discharges** — collisions, groundings, structural failures (e.g., *MV Wakashio*, Mauritius 2020; *MV Prestige*, Spain 2002).
3. **Intentional illegal discharges** — deliberate tank cleaning, slop disposal in violation of MARPOL (e.g., *Probo Koala* incident, 2006).
4. **Offshore oil and gas platforms** — drilling muds, produced water, wellhead accidents (e.g., *Deepwater Horizon*, 2010).

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Eight behavioural stages (5 marks — 1 mark each with diagram):**

1. **Spreading:** Slick spreads under gravity, inertia, viscosity, and surface tension forces (Fay's four regimes). Area grows as $A \propto t^{0.5}$ to $t^{1.0}$ depending on regime.
2. **Drift:** Combined action of surface current and wind (≈ 3% of wind speed at 10 m height), deflected ≈ 3° to the right of wind in Northern Hemisphere.
3. **Evaporation:** Light hydrocarbon fractions evaporate within 24–48 hours. Evaporation rate $Q_{evap} = k \cdot A \cdot P_{vap}$.
4. **Emulsification:** Water-in-oil "mousse" forms, doubling or tripling the slick volume; viscosity rises sharply.
5. **Dissolution:** Water-soluble toxic fractions (BTEX — benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, xylene) enter the water column.
6. **Photo-oxidation:** Sunlight-driven chemical breakdown of hydrocarbons into oxygenated compounds.
7. **Sedimentation:** Oil-coated mineral particles aggregate and sink, contaminating the seabed.
8. **Biodegradation:** Marine bacteria (e.g., *Alcanivorax borkumensis*) slowly metabolize hydrocarbons; very slow in cold Arctic waters.

**Factors affecting persistence (2 marks — any four):**
- Type and viscosity of oil (light vs. heavy; HFO persists longest).
- Sea state and water temperature (cold = slow weathering).
- Wind speed and direction.
- Solar radiation intensity.
- Biological activity in the water column.
- Proximity to coast and sediment type.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 End-Semester Model — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **MARPOL Convention** and its **six annexes** in detail. State the regulatory distance limits for sewage and garbage discharge as per Annex IV and Annex V. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **bioaccumulation** and **biomagnification** in marine pollution. With a suitable example, explain the **Minamata disaster** and its relevance to MARPOL regulations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks

**MARPOL overview (1 mark):** The **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978 (MARPOL 73/78)** is the main international convention covering the prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes. It is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.

**Six Annexes (4 marks — 1 mark each for two annexes, partial for others):**
- **Annex I (Oil):** Regulates discharge of oil and oily mixtures. Oil record book mandatory. Tankers must have **SOPEP** and **IOPP certificate**.
- **Annex II (NLS):** Categorizes noxious liquid substances into X, Y, Z, OS. Category X prohibited from any discharge.
- **Annex III (Packaged form):** Standards for packaging, marking, labelling, and documentation.
- **Annex IV (Sewage):** Applies to ships ≥ 400 GT or carrying > 15 persons. Sewage holding tank and STP mandatory.
- **Annex V (Garbage):** **All plastics prohibited** from discharge anywhere. Other garbage subject to distance and processing rules.
- **Annex VI (Air):** SOₓ cap (0.50% global, 0.10% ECA), NOₓ Tier I/II/III, ODS ban, EEDI/EEXI/CII for CO₂.

**Distance limits (2 marks):**

| Discharge Type | MARPOL Annex | Minimum Distance from Nearest Land | Additional Conditions |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:--|
| Sewage (untreated) | IV | ≥ 12 nm | None — but no floating solids |
| Sewage (comminuted + disinfected) | IV | ≥ 12 nm | Mod rate, ship moving ≥ 4 knots |
| Sewage (from approved STP) | IV | Any distance | Effluent standards must be met |
| Plastics (all types) | V | **PROHIBITED at any distance** | No exceptions |
| Food waste (comminuted) | V | ≥ 3 nm | Ship moving en route |
| Food waste (not comminuted) | V | ≥ 12 nm | Ship moving en route |
| Cargo residues | V | ≥ 12 nm | Not harmful to marine environment |
| Animal carcasses | V | As far as possible from land | — |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Bioaccumulation:** The progressive accumulation of a substance (e.g., heavy metal, POP) in an organism's body over time because the rate of intake exceeds the rate of excretion. Quantified by the **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF)**: $BCF = C_{organism} / C_{water}$.
- **Biomagnification:** The progressive increase in concentration of a substance as it moves up successive trophic levels of a food chain. Quantified by **Biomagnification Factor (BMF)**: $BMF = C_{predator} / C_{prey}$.

**Minamata disaster (4 marks):**
- **Location:** Minamata Bay, Kumamoto Prefecture, Japan.
- **Time period:** 1932 – 1968 (officially recognized 1956).
- **Source:** The Chisso Corporation's chemical plant discharged **methylmercury**-laden wastewater into Minamata Bay.
- **Mechanism:** Methylmercury was **bioaccumulated** by phytoplankton and zooplankton, then **biomagnified** up the food chain. Local residents who consumed fish and shellfish suffered severe neurological damage: numbness, vision loss, paralysis, coma, and death. Officially, **1,784 victims** were certified, with **> 10,000** receiving compensation.
- **Symptoms:** Called **"Minamata disease"** — sensory disturbance, ataxia, tremors, mental disorder, congenital deformities in children born to affected mothers.
- **Relevance to MARPOL:** The disaster was instrumental in shaping:
  - **MARPOL Annex I** restrictions on industrial waste from vessels.
  - **MARPOL Annex III** controls on packaged harmful substances.
  - **The "polluter pays" principle** later codified in the **Stockholm Convention (2001)** on Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs).
  - **IMO's special area designations** under MARPOL to protect vulnerable seas.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Minamata stands as the **textbook example** of bioaccumulation-driven marine pollution and underlines why **strict effluent standards** and **Pollution Prevention Certificates (PPC)** are mandatory for all commercial vessels under the MARPOL framework.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps (As per KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Sources" with "Types"** — Students often write *oil, plastic, sewage* under "sources of pollution." These are *types*. Sources are *land-based, sea-based, atmospheric, etc.* Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting UNCLOS definition** — KTU examiners specifically ask for UNCLOS 1982 wording; writing only MARPOL definition loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong MARPOL distance limits** — Writing "3 nm" or "5 nm" for untreated sewage instead of the correct **12 nm** is a frequent error. Memorize the **3 / 12 / 25 nm** hierarchy.
> 4. **Missing "all plastics prohibited"** — Many students forget that under Annex V, **NO distance allows plastic discharge**. This is a 1-mark snap question.
> 5. **Spelling "biomagnification" wrongly** — Examiners do deduct marks for incorrect technical spelling. Always use: *bio-ac-cu-mu-la-tion* and *bio-mag-ni-fi-ca-tion*.
> 6. **Confusing BCF with BMF** — Bioaccumulation is **within one organism over time**; Biomagnification is **across trophic levels**. The distinction is a classic 2-mark trap.
> 7. **Not mentioning "polluter pays" principle** — In any question on MARPOL, adding the phrase *"polluter pays principle of UNCLOS"* fetches an extra ½ mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Marine Pollution Sources and Types**
>
> - **Definition anchor:** UNCLOS 1982 — *"introduction of substances or energy into the marine environment"* — words to memorize verbatim.
> - **Land-based sources = 80%** of global marine pollution (UNEP statistic).
> - **Six MARPOL Annexes:** I (Oil) → II (NLS) → III (Packaged) → IV (Sewage) → V (Garbage) → VI (Air).
> - **Annex VI sulphur cap:** **0.50%** global (since 1 Jan 2020) and **0.10%** in ECAs (since 1 Jan 2015).
> - **Annex V rule of thumb:** **Plastics = 0 nm**; Food waste = 3 nm (comminuted) / 12 nm (whole); Cargo residues = 12 nm.
> - **Annex IV sewage rule:** ≥ **12 nm** for untreated discharge, with STP-approved discharge allowed at any distance.
> - **NLS hazard hierarchy:** **X (prohibited) → Y → Z → OS** (least hazardous).
> - **Oil spill severity (ITOPF):** < 7 t = small; 7–700 t = medium; > 700 t = major.
> - **Drift formula:** $v_{slick} = v_{current} + 0.03 \cdot v_{wind,10}$, deflected ≈ 3° to the right of wind in N. Hemisphere.
> - **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF) > 1000** ⇒ bioaccumulative substance (e.g., DDT, PCBs, methylmercury).
> - **Minamata disaster (1956, Japan):** Methylmercury poisoning via fish — landmark case for bioaccumulation regulation.
> - **Great Pacific Garbage Patch:** ≈ 1.6 million km² — 3× area of France, dominated by fishing gear and microplastics.
> - **Fay's Clock (8 stages):** Spreading → Drift → Evaporation → Emulsification → Dissolution → Photo-oxidation → Sedimentation → Biodegradation.
> - **Polluter Pays Principle:** Codified in UNCLOS; reinforced by **CLC 1992**, **Fund Convention 1992**, and **Bunker Convention 2001**.
> - **Indian Law:** Indian Merchant Shipping Act 1958 (Sections 356–361) — domestic enforcement of MARPOL.
> - **"Ghost nets":** Derelict fishing gear — deadliest form of plastic pollution for marine life.
> - **BWM Convention:** In force since 8 Sept 2017 — controls invasive species via ballast water management.
> - **MEPC 76 amendments (2021):** Introduced EEXI and CII alongside existing EEDI for CO₂ control.
> - **Kerala context:** Cochin Port is a major ballast water exchange point; oil spill contingency plan 2020 mandates SOPEP for all vessels calling at Indian ports.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 — Marine Pollution Sources and Types. Aligns with KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome CO4: Identify and analyse sources, types, and impacts of marine pollution to ensure compliance with international maritime environmental regulations.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oil Spills, Chemical Pollution, and Solid Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oil Spills, Chemical Pollution, and Solid Waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Oil Spills in the Maritime Environment

An **oil spill** is the unauthorized or accidental release of liquid petroleum hydrocarbons into the marine environment, occurring from tankers, offshore platforms, drilling rigs, pipelines, bunkering operations, or vessel collisions. The International Maritime Organization (IMO) defines an oil spill under **MARPOL Annex I** as any discharge of oil or oily mixtures that results from a maritime casualty, operational error, or deliberate discharge exceeding the statutory limit of **15 parts per million (ppm)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Oil spills account for approximately **12% of marine pollution** by volume, but represent the most acutely damaging form due to their rapid ecological, economic, and social impact.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine spilling a bottle of cooking oil onto a fish tank. The oil floats on top because it is **less dense** than water (oil density typically **0.85–0.98 g/cm³** vs water at **1.00 g/cm³**). It spreads rapidly, blocking oxygen exchange, coating fish gills, and poisoning the water. A marine oil spill is the same phenomenon on a scale of **thousands to millions of liters**, made worse by waves, wind, and salt water. The oil doesn't just stay as oil — it weathers (evaporates, emulsifies, disperses, biodegrades) over days, weeks, and years.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — One Tonne of Crude Oil:** Can cover approximately **12 km²** of sea surface in a **microscopic sheen** (0.0001 mm thickness) or contaminate **250 million liters** of seawater to a level toxic to marine life.

---

## 1.2 Chemical Pollution from Ships

**Chemical pollution** refers to the discharge of noxious liquid substances (NLS), harmful substances in packaged form, sewage, anti-fouling paint residues, cargo residues, and operational chemicals into the marine environment. This is regulated primarily under **MARPOL Annex II** (Noxious Liquid Substances) and **MARPOL Annex IV** (Sewage).

The IMO categorizes NLS into **four categories (X, Y, Z, and OS)** based on their hazard profile:
- **Category X**: Highest hazard — *bioaccumulative, persistent, toxic* (e.g., tributyltin, certain pesticides).
- **Category Y**: Moderate hazard.
- **Category Z**: Minor hazard.
- **Category OS**: Other Substances with minimal environmental impact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tributyltin (TBT)** — once a common anti-fouling biocide — is now globally banned under the **IMO AFS Convention (2001)** due to severe endocrine disruption in marine gastropods, causing **imposex** (imposition of male sexual organs on females).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of chemical pollution as "invisible oil" — unlike oil, you cannot see it floating on the surface, but it dissolves, precipitates, or accumulates in marine food chains. Imagine pouring paint thinner, pesticides, or pharmaceutical waste into a river that flows to the sea. The chemicals travel, concentrate in fish and shellfish, and end up on your dinner plate via **bioaccumulation** and **biomagnification**.

---

## 1.3 Solid Waste from Ships

**Ship-generated solid waste** includes all plastics, food waste, domestic waste, cooking oil, operational waste, cargo residues, fishing gear, and incinerator ash. Regulation falls under **MARPOL Annex V** (Garbage), which enforces a strict **discharge prohibition** for plastics anywhere in the marine environment.

The estimated **6.5 million tonnes** of fishing gear lost or abandoned annually ("ghost gear") constitutes nearly **46% of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch** by weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plastic Persistence:** A single plastic bottle persists in the marine environment for approximately **450 years**, fragmenting into **microplastics (<5 mm)** and **nanoplastics (<1 µm)**, both now detected in human blood and breast milk.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a cruise ship carrying **3,000 passengers** for a 7-day voyage. Each passenger generates roughly **3.5 kg of waste per day**, including food, packaging, bottles, and personal items. Multiply that by **2,000+ cruise ships worldwide**, and you have a **floating city producing waste continuously**. Now imagine that, until 1988, all this was legally dumped into the ocean.

---

## 1.4 Interconnection of the Three Pollutant Streams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three pollution types are interconnected through **vessel operations**: an oil tanker casualty can simultaneously release oil (Annex I), chemical cargo (Annex II), and solid debris (Annex V). Effective maritime safety requires a **holistic, multi-annex compliance framework**.

| Pollutant Type | MARPOL Annex | Primary Vessel Source | Persistence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oil | I | Tankers, machinery spaces | Weeks to decades |
| Chemicals | II, IV | Chemical tankers, bilge | Months to centuries |
| Solid Waste | V | All vessels | Decades to millennia |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Oil Spill Classification & Behavior

Oil spills are classified by **bulk type**, which determines behavior at sea:

- **Group I — Light Oils & Gasolines** (API > 35): Evaporate rapidly (50–90% in 24 hours). Limited environmental persistence.
- **Group II — Crude & Fuel Oils** (API 17–35): Undergo **photo-oxidation, emulsification**, and dispersion. Form **"chocolate mousse" emulsions** (3–4× original volume).
- **Group III — Heavy Crude & Bunker C** (API < 17): Highly persistent, form tar balls, may sink in sediment-laden water.
- **Group IV — Non-Floating Oils**: Dense oils (e.g., Orimulsion) that **submerge** and contaminate the seabed.

### 2.1.1 The Four Phases of Oil Weathering

When oil enters the marine environment, it undergoes sequential physical and chemical transformations:

1. **Spreading & Drifting** (Hours): Oil film thickness decreases as it spreads. Governed by gravity, viscosity, surface tension, and **friction velocity** of wind.
2. **Evaporation** (Hours to Days): Volatile fractions (light hydrocarbons) evaporate. Approximately **30–50%** of crude oil evaporates within 24 hours.
3. **Emulsification** (Days): Water-in-oil emulsions form under wave action, dramatically increasing volume.
4. **Oxidation & Biodegradation** (Weeks to Years): Photochemical and microbial processes convert hydrocarbons to CO₂ and water.

---

## 2.2 Chemical Pollution: Behaviour Categories

The **GHS (Globally Harmonized System)** and **MARPOL Annex II** categorize chemical behavior in the marine environment:

- **Floating (Fp < 0°C)**: Spread on surface, evaporate, may dissolve.
- **Evaporating**: High volatility, lost to atmosphere.
- **Dissolving**: Soluble in water column, enter marine food web.
- **Sinking**: Dense substances that reach benthic environments.
- **Solidifying**: React with seawater to form solid masses.

---

## 2.3 Solid Waste: Categorization under MARPOL Annex V

Annex V categorizes ship-generated garbage into:

- **A — Plastics** (Including synthetic ropes, fishing nets, plastic bottles).
- **B — Food waste**.
- **C — Domestic waste** (Paper, rags, glass, metal, etc.).
- **D — Cooking oil**.
- **E — Incinerator ash**.
- **F — Operational waste**.
- **G — Cargo residues** (non-HME – harmful to the marine environment).
- **H — Animal carcasses**.
- **I — Fishing gear**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plastics are NEVER permitted to be discharged at sea under any circumstances** — even ground to microplastics (a 2021 KTU-aligned clarification).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are essential for solving numerical problems on oil spill behavior, dispersion, and solid waste generation in KTU examinations.

> **Note on notation:** Vertical bars (e.g., $ \vert x \vert $) are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_o = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h$ | Volume of an oil slick (cylindrical model) | $m^3$ |
| 2 | $r = \left( \dfrac{V_o \cdot g}{\pi \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot \nu_w^2} \right)^{1/4}$ | Fay spreading radius (gravity–viscous phase) | m |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{V_o}{\pi r^2}$ | Mean slick thickness | m |
| 4 | $E = A \cdot e^{-k_e t}$ | Evaporative mass loss (first-order decay) | kg |
| 5 | $F = 0.025 \cdot U_{10}^{3.5}$ | Oil drift factor (wind-driven, 3.5% rule) | — |
| 6 | $W_{gen} = P \cdot D \cdot w_d$ | Solid waste generation per voyage | kg |
| 7 | $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_d t}$ | Chemical concentration decay (dissolution/dispersion) | mg/L |
| 8 | $K_{ow} = \dfrac{C_{oct}}{C_{w}}$ | Octanol-water partition coefficient (bioaccumulation indicator) | — |
| 9 | $BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Bioconcentration Factor | — |
| 10 | $V_{disp} = k \cdot C \cdot A \cdot t$ | Chemical dispersion rate | kg |

**Where:**
- $V_o$ = oil volume, $r$ = slick radius, $h$ = thickness.
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**).
- $\Delta \rho$ = density difference between water and oil.
- $\nu_w$ = kinematic viscosity of water (**≈ 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**).
- $E$ = evaporated mass, $A$ = initial mass, $k_e$ = evaporation rate constant (**0.05–0.15 h⁻¹**).
- $U_{10}$ = wind speed at 10 m height (m/s).
- $W_{gen}$ = waste generated, $P$ = number of persons, $D$ = voyage days, $w_d$ = daily waste rate per person (**≈ 3.5 kg**).
- $K_{ow}$: if $\log K_{ow} > 3$, high bioaccumulation potential.
- $BCF > 1000$ indicates significant bioaccumulation.

### Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Fay Equation (Eq. 2)**: Used in **NOAA's GNOME spill trajectory model** and **OSCAR** (Oil Spill Contingency and Response) for predicting slick extent within the first hours — critical for activating containment booms.
- **3.5% Wind Rule (Eq. 5)**: Allows field responders to estimate oil movement with only a wind measurement — used by **ITOPF (International Tanker Owners Pollution Federation)**.
- **Solid Waste Estimation (Eq. 6)**: Required under **MARPOL Annex V Port Reception Facility (PRF) planning** to size onshore waste reception capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fay Spreading Equation (Gravity–Viscous Phase)

This derivation models how a spilled oil slick spreads radially under the balance of **gravitational forces** and **viscous resistance** of seawater during the early hours of a spill.

### Step 1: Define the Governing Forces

For a circular slick of radius $r$ and uniform thickness $h$, the **gravitational driving force** arises from the buoyancy of oil over water:

$$
F_g = \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot h^2
$$

where $\Delta \rho$ is the density difference between seawater and oil, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $h^2$ arises from the **hydrostatic pressure head** integrated over the slick depth.

The **viscous resistive force** of the underlying water column, parameterized by its kinematic viscosity $\nu_w$, opposes the spread:

$$
F_v = \rho_w \cdot \nu_w \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Conservation of Volume

Since oil is assumed **incompressible and non-evaporating** during the gravity–viscous phase:

$$
V_o = \pi r^2 h \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \dfrac{V_o}{\pi r^2}
$$

Differentiate with respect to time $t$:

$$
\dfrac{dh}{dt} = -\dfrac{2V_o}{\pi r^3} \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt} = -\dfrac{2h}{r} \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt}
$$

### Step 3: Equate Driving and Resistive Forces

Setting the buoyancy force proportional to the viscous shear:

$$
\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot h^2 \propto \rho_w \cdot \nu_w \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt}
$$

Insert $h = V_o / (\pi r^2)$:

$$
\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot \left( \dfrac{V_o}{\pi r^2} \right)^2 = C \cdot \rho_w \cdot \nu_w \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt}
$$

where $C$ is a dimensionless constant. Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{dr}{dt} = \dfrac{\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot V_o^{2}}{C \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot \rho_w \cdot \nu_w} \cdot \dfrac{1}{r^{4}}
$$

### Step 4: Integrate to Obtain the Fay Spreading Law

Assume constant volume and material properties. Integrate with initial condition $r(t=0) = 0$:

$$
\int_0^{r} r^{4} \, dr = \dfrac{\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot V_o^{2}}{C \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot \rho_w \cdot \nu_w} \int_0^{t} dt
$$

$$
\dfrac{r^{5}}{5} = \dfrac{\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot V_o^{2}}{C \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot \rho_w \cdot \nu_w} \cdot t
$$

Solving for $r$:

$$
r = \left( \dfrac{5 \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot V_o^{2}}{C \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot \rho_w \cdot \nu_w} \right)^{1/5} \cdot t^{1/5}
$$

### Step 5: Apply Empirical Calibration

Field experiments (Fay, 1971) calibrate the constant to yield the working engineering form:

$$
r = K \left( \dfrac{\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot V_o^{2}}{\nu_w^{2}} \right)^{1/4} \cdot t^{1/4}
$$

where $K$ is an empirical constant (typically **1.5–2.0**). Substituting practical units yields the **operational formula** for early-phase spill response:

$$
r = \left( \dfrac{V_o \cdot g}{\pi \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot \nu_w^{2}} \right)^{1/4}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $t^{1/4}$ time dependence means the slick radius **grows slowly** after the initial rapid spread — a key insight for **boom deployment timing**. Containment is most effective within the first 6–12 hours.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Oil Slick Geometry

> **Problem:** A bulk carrier suffers a bunker oil spill releasing **$V_o = 200 \, m^3$** of HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil) into calm seawater at **$20°C$**. Given:
> - $\Delta \rho = 1000 - 970 = 30 \, kg/m^{3}$ (oil density **970 kg/m³**)
> - $g = 9.81 \, m/s^{2}$
> - $\nu_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \, m^{2}/s$
>
> **Calculate:** (a) the slick radius, (b) the mean film thickness, (c) the time to reach 500 m radius.

### Part (a) — Slick Radius

$$
r = \left( \dfrac{200 \times 9.81}{\pi \times 30 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-6})^{2}} \right)^{1/4}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 200 \times 9.81 = 1962
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = \pi \times 30 \times 10^{-12} = 9.4248 \times 10^{-11}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1962}{9.4248 \times 10^{-11}} = 2.082 \times 10^{13}
$$

$$
r = (2.082 \times 10^{13})^{0.25} = 2.137 \times 10^{3} \, m
$$

$$
\boxed{r \approx 2137 \, m \,\, (\approx 2.14 \, km)}
$$

### Part (b) — Mean Film Thickness

$$
h = \dfrac{V_o}{\pi r^{2}} = \dfrac{200}{\pi \times (2137)^{2}}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{200}{\pi \times 4.567 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{200}{1.435 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
\boxed{h \approx 1.39 \times 10^{-5} \, m \,\, (\approx 14 \, \mu m)}
$$

This corresponds to a **silver-grey sheen** visible from the air.

### Part (c) — Time to Reach 500 m Radius

Using $r = (C)^{1/4} \cdot t^{1/4}$ implies $r \propto t^{1/4}$, so $t \propto r^{4}$.

Compute the constant $C$:

$$
C = \dfrac{1962}{9.4248 \times 10^{-11}} = 2.082 \times 10^{13}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{r^{4}}{C} = \dfrac{(500)^{4}}{2.082 \times 10^{13}} = \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{10}}{2.082 \times 10^{13}}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 3.00 \times 10^{-3} \, s}
$$

> **Note:** This extremely short time is unphysical — it indicates the **gravity–viscous phase ends very quickly**, and the slick transitions to the **gravity–surface-tension phase** within minutes, after which spreading slows dramatically.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Evaporative Loss of Oil

The **Stiver–Mackay evaporation model** (1984) is the KTU-recommended approach. The fractional evaporative loss is given by:

$$
F(t) = \ln \left[ 1 + P \cdot k_e \cdot t \cdot \dfrac{R \cdot T}{M_v} \right] \cdot \dfrac{M_v}{P_v \cdot R \cdot T}
$$

For a 1000-tonne crude oil spill with **kerosene-like volatility**:

| Time (h) | Fraction Evaporated (%) | Mass Remaining (tonnes) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8.2 | 918 |
| 6 | 22.4 | 776 |
| 12 | 31.0 | 690 |
| 24 | 41.5 | 585 |
| 48 | 52.0 | 480 |
| 72 | 57.3 | 427 |

---

## 3.4 Solid Waste Generation Calculation

### Worked Example

A **30-day voyage** with a crew of **25** generates solid waste at **$w_d = 3.5 \, kg/person/day$** (IMO standard estimate).

$$
W_{gen} = 25 \times 30 \times 3.5
$$

$$
W_{gen} = 750 \times 3.5 = 2625 \, kg
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{gen} = 2.625 \, tonnes \text{ of solid waste}}
$$

Categorized per **MARPOL Annex V**:

| Category | % of Total | Mass (kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Plastics | 15% | 394 |
| Food waste | 30% | 788 |
| Domestic waste | 35% | 919 |
| Cooking oil | 10% | 263 |
| Operational waste | 10% | 263 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Of the **394 kg of plastics**, **none** may be discharged at sea — all must be transferred to a **Port Reception Facility (PRF)**. Failure to comply can result in fines up to **USD 25,000** per offence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Oil Spill Response Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spill Occurs] --> B[Initial Assessment]
    B --> C[Notify Coast Guard / ITOPF]
    C --> D{Slick Type?}
    D -- Crude / Fuel Oil --> E[Deploy Booms]
    D -- Heavy Oil --> F[Skimmers + Vacuum]
    D -- Chemical --> G[Specialized HAZMAT Team]
    E --> H[Skimmer Vessels]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Decanting and Storage]
    I --> J[Temporary Onshore Storage]
    J --> K[Waste Classification]
    K --> L[Recycler or Disposal Site]
    L --> M[Documentation and Lessons Learned]
    M --> N[Update Spill Contingency Plan]

    step1[Step 1 Detection]:::phaseNode
    step2[Step 2 Containment]:::phaseNode
    step3[Step 3 Recovery]:::phaseNode
    step4[Step 4 Disposal]:::phaseNode
    step5[Step 5 Review]:::phaseNode

    A -.- step1
    E -.- step2
    H -.- step3
    J -.- step4
    M -.- step5

    classDef phaseNode fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Chemical Pollution Decision Tree (Annex II Compliance)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NLS[Noxious Liquid Substance Onboard] --> CAT{Category?}
    CAT -- X --> PROHIB[No Discharge Allowed]
    CAT -- Y --> STRICT[Discharge Only to PRF]
    CAT -- Z --> LIMITED[Limited Discharge After 12 nm]
    CAT -- OS --> RELAXED[Standard Discharge Allowed]

    PROHIB --> P1[Full Pre-Wash Required]
    STRICT --> S1[Strip to MARPOL Standards]
    LIMITED --> L1[Underway at 7 knots]
    RELAXED --> R1[Log in Garbage Record Book]

    P1 --> LOG[Update Ship Log]
    S1 --> LOG
    L1 --> LOG
    R1 --> LOG

    LOG --> AUDIT[PSC Inspection Ready]

    style PROHIB fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style STRICT fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800
    style LIMITED fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#0088cc
    style RELAXED fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#00cc66
```

## 4.3 Solid Waste Management Hierarchy (Annex V)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE[Onboard Generation]
        A1[Crew Galley]
        A2[Cabin Areas]
        A3[Cargo Holds]
        A4[Machinery Spaces]
    end

    subgraph SORT[Source Segregation]
        B1[Plastics Bins]
        B2[Food Waste Bins]
        B3[Domestic Waste Bins]
        B4[Hazardous Waste Containers]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Onboard Processing]
        C1[Compactor]
        C2[Incinerator]
        C3[Shredder]
    end

    subgraph TRANSFER[Shore Transfer]
        D1[Port Reception Facility]
    end

    subgraph DISPOSAL[Final Disposal]
        E1[Recycling]
        E2[Landfill]
        E3[Waste-to-Energy]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B4

    B1 --> C3
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> D1

    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> E3
    C3 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    D1 --> E2
    D1 --> E3

    style C2 fill:#ffe0b3
    style E3 fill:#b3ffb3
```

## 4.4 MARPOL Annex Cross-Reference Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SPILL[Marine Pollution Event] --> A1[MARPOL Annex I - Oil]
    SPILL --> A2[MARPOL Annex II - NLS]
    SPILL --> A3[MARPOL Annex III - Packaged Goods]
    SPILL --> A4[MARPOL Annex IV - Sewage]
    SPILL --> A5[MARPOL Annex V - Garbage]
    SPILL --> A6[MARPOL Annex VI - Air]

    A1 --> I1[Oily Water Separator]
    A1 --> I2[SOPEP]

    A2 --> I3[Cargo Stripping]
    A2 --> I4[Pre-Wash Systems]

    A5 --> I5[Garbage Management Plan]
    A5 --> I6[PRF Contract]

    I1 --> COMP[Compliance Verification]
    I3 --> COMP
    I5 --> COMP

    COMP --> PSC[Port State Control]

    style SPILL fill:#ffd6d6
    style PSC fill:#d6ffd6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "oil spill" as per MARPOL Annex I. What is the maximum permissible oil content in bilge water discharged from a ship?**

**Model Answer:**

> An **oil spill** under MARPOL Annex I refers to the unauthorized release of petroleum hydrocarbons or oily mixtures into the marine environment. The discharge of bilge water through the **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** must not exceed **15 ppm** of oil content, monitored continuously by an **Oil Content Monitor (OCM)** with a 15 ppm alarm setpoint. The IMO 15 ppm limit is enforced under **Regulation 14 of MARPOL Annex I**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for 15 ppm limit with monitoring requirement)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six categories of ship-generated solid waste under MARPOL Annex V.**

**Model Answer:**

The six categories under MARPOL Annex V are:

1. **Plastics** (including ropes, fishing gear, bottles).
2. **Food waste**.
3. **Domestic waste** (paper, glass, metal, rags).
4. **Cooking oil**.
5. **Operational waste**.
6. **Cargo residues** (non-HME substances).

*(3 marks: 0.5 mark per correct category)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list only "plastics, food, and domestic waste" — earning **2 marks** for 4 items, but losing **1 mark** for missing operational waste and cargo residues. Memorize all **9 official categories (A–I)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.A. (a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **functional components of a Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan (SOPEP)** as required under MARPOL Annex I. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **SOPEP** is a mandatory contingency plan carried on all oil tankers of **150 GT and above** and all other vessels of **400 GT and above** under MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 37. Its functional components are:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Introduction & Policy** | Master and crew responsibilities, reporting chain |
| **Reporting Procedure** | Initial contact to coastal State, port State, flag State |
| **Operational Spill Scenarios** | Pre-planned response for bunker spill, cargo tank rupture, collision |
| **Containment Equipment List** | Sorbents, booms, skimmers, pumps, dispersants |
| **Communication Directory** | Coastal radio stations, ITOPF, regional OSRL |
| **Training & Drills** | Quarterly OPA drills, SOPEP familiarization |
| **Annexes** | Vessel particulars, oil types, MSDS, port contacts |

**Key elements of the reporting procedure:**
- Master must notify **nearest coastal State** within **15 minutes** of spill detection.
- Report to **flag State administration** within **24 hours**.
- Follow **ITOPF notification** template including: vessel position, spill size, oil type, weather, sea state, resources deployed.

**Block Diagram (Text Form):**

```
Spill Detection → Master Notified → SOPEP Activated
        ↓
Internal Team (Engineer/OOW) → Containment Equipment
        ↓
External Notify: Coastal State → Flag State → ITOPF → OSRL
        ↓
Containment + Recovery → Documentation → P&I Club Report
```

*(7 marks: 2 marks diagram, 3 marks for 5+ components, 2 marks for reporting time and authority)*

---

**Q.A. (b)** An oil tanker spills **500 m³ of crude oil** ($\Delta \rho = 50 \, kg/m^3$, $\nu_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$) into calm sea. Calculate (i) the radius of the slick at equilibrium during the gravity-viscous phase and (ii) the mean film thickness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_o = 500 \, m^3$
- $\Delta \rho = 50 \, kg/m^3$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
- $\nu_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$

### (i) Slick Radius

$$
r = \left( \dfrac{V_o \cdot g}{\pi \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot \nu_w^{2}} \right)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute numerator:
$$
V_o \cdot g = 500 \times 9.81 = 4905
$$

**Step 2:** Compute denominator:
$$
\pi \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot \nu_w^{2} = \pi \times 50 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = \pi \times 50 \times 10^{-12} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide:
$$
\dfrac{4905}{1.5708 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.123 \times 10^{13}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply fourth root:
$$
r = (3.123 \times 10^{13})^{0.25}
$$

Computing: $(3.123)^{0.25} = 1.328$ and $(10^{13})^{0.25} = 10^{3.25} = 1778$.

$$
r = 1.328 \times 1778 = 2361 \, m
$$

$$
\boxed{r \approx 2.36 \, km}
$$

### (ii) Mean Film Thickness

$$
h = \dfrac{V_o}{\pi r^{2}} = \dfrac{500}{\pi \times (2361)^{2}}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{500}{\pi \times 5.574 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{500}{1.751 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
\boxed{h \approx 2.86 \times 10^{-5} \, m \,\, (\approx 28.6 \, \mu m)}
$$

This corresponds to a **dark brown to black** oil film.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for radius calculation showing all 4 steps, 2 marks for correct unit handling, 2 marks for thickness calculation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Common mistake is forgetting to apply the **fourth root** ($^{1/4}$) — students frequently write $r = (...) $ without exponent, losing **2 marks**. Always show the exponent transition explicitly: $r = (X)^{0.25}$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.B. (a)** Discuss the **four main categories of chemical pollution from ships** under MARPOL Annex II. Explain the special regulatory requirements for **Category X substances**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

MARPOL Annex II categorizes Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) shipped in bulk into four hazard categories:

1. **Category X** — *Highest hazard*
   - Substances that are **bioaccumulative, persistent, and toxic**.
   - Examples: **Tributyltin (TBT) compounds**, certain pesticides, mercury compounds.
   - **No discharge permitted** into the marine environment under any operational circumstances.

2. **Category Y** — *Moderate hazard*
   - Examples: toluene, xylene, certain solvents.
   - Discharge permitted only via **approved pre-wash procedures** and to **Port Reception Facilities (PRF)**.

3. **Category Z** — *Minor hazard*
   - Examples: low-toxicity esters, certain vegetable oils.
   - Limited discharge allowed beyond **12 nautical miles** from the nearest land, at minimum **7 knots** vessel speed, with **continuous water flow**.

4. **Category OS** — *Other Substances*
   - Substances with minimal environmental impact.
   - Standard operational discharge rules apply.

### Special Requirements for Category X

- **Pre-wash mandatory** for all cargo tanks after unloading.
- **Stripping to MARPOL standards** — the tank residue must be reduced to a near-zero level.
- **No discharge of Category X substances** under any operational condition, regardless of distance from shore.
- Mandatory reporting to **flag State** and **coastal State** within **1 hour** of any incident.
- **P&I insurance** must cover pollution damage up to **SDR 100 million** (Special Drawing Rights).

*(7 marks: 3 marks for 4 categories, 2 marks for 2 examples per category, 2 marks for X-category special rules)*

---

**Q.B. (b)** A passenger ship with **1500 persons** is on a **14-day** voyage. Calculate the **total solid waste generated** and the **mass of plastics** (assumed 15% of total) that must be transferred to a Port Reception Facility, given daily waste rate of **3.5 kg/person/day**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Total Solid Waste

$$
W_{gen} = P \times D \times w_d
$$

$$
W_{gen} = 1500 \times 14 \times 3.5
$$

$$
W_{gen} = 21{,}000 \times 3.5 = 73{,}500 \, kg
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{gen} = 73.5 \, tonnes}
$$

### Plastic Mass (15% of Total)

$$
W_{plastic} = 0.15 \times 73{,}500
$$

$$
W_{plastic} = 11{,}025 \, kg
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{plastic} = 11.025 \, tonnes}
$$

### Discussion

- All 11.025 tonnes of plastic must be **transferred to PRF** — discharge at sea is **strictly prohibited** under MARPOL Annex V.
- The ship must maintain a **Garbage Management Plan** and **Garbage Record Book** documenting each transfer.
- The Port State may levy **fines of up to USD 25,000** per offence for non-compliance.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for total, 2 marks for plastic mass, 1 mark for regulatory discussion)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often use **wrong units** — the rate of **3.5 kg/person/day** is **per day per person**, not per day total. The marks depend on correctly multiplying all **three variables** (P × D × w_d). A common error is $W = 1500 \times 3.5 \times 14 = 73{,}500$ ✓ — but writing $W = 1500 \times 3.5$ only (forgetting D) loses **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Oil Spills, Chemical Pollution, and Solid Waste**

- ✅ **MARPOL Annex I** governs oil; **Annex II** chemicals; **Annex V** garbage.
- ✅ The **15 ppm** bilge water discharge limit is a **mandatory KTU exam fact**.
- ✅ **Plastics are NEVER discharged at sea** — under any circumstances.
- ✅ **Fay's spreading law** yields $r \propto t^{1/4}$ in the gravity–viscous phase.
- ✅ The **3.5% wind drift rule**: oil moves at **3.5% of the 10 m wind speed**.
- ✅ **Category X substances** = **NO discharge allowed** under operational conditions.
- ✅ **TBT** is banned under the **AFS Convention (2008)** — causes **imposex** in marine snails.
- ✅ **SOPEP** is mandatory for tankers **≥150 GT** and non-tankers **≥400 GT**.
- ✅ Solid waste generation formula: $W_{gen} = P \times D \times w_d$, with $w_d \approx 3.5 \, kg/person/day$.
- ✅ **ITOPF** is the global technical body for oil spill response and contingency planning.
- ✅ **NOAA, EMSA, REMPEC** are key regional response centres.
- ✅ A **microplastic** is defined as **<5 mm**; **nanoplastic** is **<1 µm**.
- ✅ The **Great Pacific Garbage Patch** spans **1.6 million km²** — three times the size of France.
- ✅ **Fishing gear** accounts for **~46%** of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch by weight ("ghost gear").
- ✅ **Marpol Annex VI** (not covered here) addresses **air pollution** — SOx, NOx, ODS.
- ✅ **Logarithmic $K_{ow}$** of **>3** indicates **high bioaccumulation potential**.
- ✅ **BCF > 1000** = significant bioaccumulation; **BCF > 5000** = very high concern.
- ✅ **Pre-wash** is mandatory for **Category X and Y** cargo tanks after unloading.
- ✅ The **Master** is legally responsible for reporting spills; the **Chief Engineer** handles technical response.
- ✅ **Port Reception Facilities (PRF)** must be made available by all signatory States under **MARPOL**.
- ✅ **Photo-oxidation** contributes to **15–30%** of oil mass loss in tropical waters.
- ✅ **Stiver–Mackay** is the KTU-recommended **evaporation model**.
- ✅ **Emulsification** can increase oil volume by **3–4×**, forming "**chocolate mousse**".
- ✅ **Oil spill trajectory models** include **GNOME (NOAA)** and **OSCAR (Environment Canada)**.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — OESFT727 Maritime Safety**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme NEP 2020 guidelines.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Maritime Pollution Treaties
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** International Maritime Pollution Treaties

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

International Maritime Pollution Treaties constitute the **codified body of legally binding international conventions, protocols, and annexes** formulated primarily under the auspices of the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** — a specialized agency of the United Nations headquartered in **London, United Kingdom**. These treaties establish uniform global standards governing the prevention, control, and compensation of pollution originating from commercial shipping activities across all maritime jurisdictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - Module 4):** The course outcome for this module demands that students critically appraise the structural architecture of MARPOL 73/78, identify the regulatory obligations imposed on flag states and port states, and evaluate the response mechanisms for oil and chemical spills at sea.

Mathematically expressed, the **Pollution Risk Index (PRI)** for any commercial vessel can be conceptualized as:

$$PRI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( V_i \cdot C_i \cdot T_i \right)$$

Where:
- $V_i$ = Volume/mass of pollutant *i* (in metric tonnes)
- $C_i$ = Concentration/toxicity coefficient of pollutant *i*
- $T_i$ = Time of exposure / persistence in marine environment (in hours)

The fundamental objective of every maritime pollution treaty is therefore to minimize **PRI → 0** through legally enforceable design, operational, and reporting requirements.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Traffic Code" of the Oceans

Imagine the world's oceans as a massive **six-lane global highway** with **no traffic police** on the open stretches. Just as road traffic laws (speed limits, vehicle emission standards, accident liability rules) prevent chaos on land, **maritime pollution treaties act as the "Highway Traffic Code" for ships**.

| Analogy Element | Maritime Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Highway = Road | Ocean = MARPOL-regulated waterway |
| Vehicle emissions test | Air Pollution Annex (Annex VI) |
| Truck carrying hazardous cargo | Noxious Liquid Substances (Annex II) |
| Illegal dumping of trash | Garbage discharge (Annex V) |
| Speed limits / checkpoints | Special Area restrictions / Port State Control |
| Traffic court | IMO Legal Committee / ITLOS Tribunal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike national laws, maritime treaties operate under the principle of **"flag state jurisdiction"** — meaning a ship is legally bound by the rules of the country whose flag it flies, regardless of which ocean it is sailing in. Port states (countries whose ports the ship visits) also have enforcement powers.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Maritime Pollution Governance

The entire edifice of international maritime pollution law rests upon **three foundational pillars**:

1. **Prevention Pillar (MARPOL 73/78, AFS, BWM)** — Stops pollution *before* it occurs through design and operational rules.
2. **Response Pillar (OPRC, Intervention Convention)** — Mandates *immediate action* once a spill occurs.
3. **Liability & Compensation Pillar (CLC, FUND, HNS, Bunker Convention)** — Ensures *polluters pay* for cleanup and damages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Governing Body:** The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** — established in 1948, operational since 1958 — is the only international body authorized to adopt globally binding maritime safety and pollution regulations under Article 1 of the IMO Convention.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Across All Treaties

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oil spill threshold | $Q_{oil}$ | parts per million (ppm) | MARPOL Annex I limit = **15 ppm** |
| Garbage classification | $G_c$ | Categories A–K | MARPOL Annex V |
| Sewage discharge distance | $D_s$ | Nautical miles (nm) | **3 nm** (treated) / **12 nm** (untreated) |
| SO$_x$ emission cap (ECAs) | $E_{SO_x}$ | % m/m | **0.10%** since 2015 |
| NOx Tier limit | $E_{NO_x}$ | g/kWh | **Tier III = 3.4 g/kWh** in ECAs |

---

## 1.5 Hierarchy of Norms in Maritime Pollution Law

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   UNITED NATIONS CONVENTION ON THE          │
│   LAW OF THE SEA (UNCLOS), 1982             │  ← Umbrella Convention
│   (Articles 192–237: Marine Pollution)       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                    │
        ┌───────────┼───────────┐
        ▼           ▼           ▼
┌──────────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌──────────────┐
│ IMO Sectoral │ │Regional │ │Customary     │
│ Conventions  │ │Agreements│ │International │
│ (MARPOL,etc.)│ │(HELCOM, │ │Law           │
│              │ │REMPEC)  │ │              │
└──────────────┘ └─────────┘ └──────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **UNCLOS Article 211(1)** explicitly empowers States to adopt *stricter* measures than the global minimum, which is why regions like the **North Sea**, **Baltic Sea**, and **Antarctic waters** have developed their own stringent pollution control regimes.

---

## 1.6 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

The OESFT727 syllabus treats International Maritime Pollution Treaties as a **high-weightage Module 4 topic** because:

- It forms the **legal backbone** of every ship officer's daily compliance duties.
- It is repeatedly tested in **Part A (3 marks)** as definition-type questions and in **Part B (14 marks)** as descriptive/analytical questions.
- It interlinks with **MARPOL**, **STCW**, and **MLC** conventions — meaning students must know cross-references.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU examiners expect students to quote **Article numbers**, **Annex designations**, and **entry-into-force dates** wherever applicable. Vague answers like *"MARPOL prevents pollution"* fetch partial credit only.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MARPOL Convention — The Mother of All Maritime Pollution Treaties

### 2.1.1 Origin and Evolution

The **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL 73/78)** was adopted in two stages:
- **1973** — Original MARPOL Convention adopted at the IMO Conference in London
- **1978** — MARPOL Protocol adopted (following the *Amoco Cadiz*, *Torrey Canyon* disasters)
- **2 November 1983** — Entered into force with Annexes I & II

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naming Convention:** Although officially titled "MARPOL 73/78," it is referred to as a single instrument because the 1978 Protocol absorbed and amended the 1973 Convention. The combined text is treated as one integrated treaty.

### 2.1.2 Structural Architecture — The Six Annexes

MARPOL's compliance mechanism is built on **six technical Annexes**, each addressing a specific pollution category:

#### **Annex I — Prevention of Pollution by Oil**

- **Entry into Force:** 2 October 1983
- **Scope:** Discharges of oily bilge water, cargo oil residues, and oil tank washings.
- **Key Requirements:**
  - Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT) for crude oil tankers ≥ 20,000 DWT
  - Crude Oil Washing (COW) system for product carriers
  - Oil Discharge Monitoring and Control System (ODMCS) — **15 ppm** ceiling
  - Double Hull / Double Bottom requirements (post-*Exxon Valdez* amendments)
  - **IOPP Certificate** (International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate)

#### **Annex II — Control of Pollution by Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS)**

- **Entry into Force:** 6 April 1987
- **Scope:** Carriage of noxious liquid substances in bulk (chemical tankers).
- **Categorization:**
  - **Category X** — Most severe (high environmental hazard)
  - **Category Y** — Substantial hazard
  - **Category Z** — Minor hazard
  - **Other Substances (OS)** — Low hazard
- Prewash, stripping, and discharge procedures regulated by **P&A Manual** (Procedures and Arrangements Manual).

#### **Annex III — Prevention of Pollution by Harmful Substances in Packaged Form**

- **Entry into Force:** 1 July 1992
- **Scope:** Materials classified under the **IMDG Code** (International Maritime Dangerous Goods Code).
- **Key Principle:** Marking, labeling, stowage, and documentation (no discharge to sea permitted).

#### **Annex IV — Prevention of Pollution by Sewage from Ships**

- **Entry into Force:** 27 September 2003
- **Key Discharge Standards:**
  - **3 nm from nearest land:** Treated sewage via approved sewage treatment plant
  - **12 nm from nearest land:** Untreated sewage (disintegrated/commuted)
- **Sewage Holding Tank** mandatory for ships ≥ 400 GT or carrying > 15 persons.

#### **Annex V — Prevention of Pollution by Garbage from Ships**

- **Entry into Force:** 31 December 1988
- **Garbage Categories (A–K):**
  - Category A: Plastics (including synthetic ropes, fishing nets)
  - Category B: Food waste
  - Category C: Domestic waste
  - Categories D–K: Cargo residues, cooking oil, operational waste, etc.
- **Special Areas:** Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, North, Antarctic, Wider Caribbean — *zero* discharge of plastics permitted.

#### **Annex VI — Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships**

- **Entry into Force:** 19 May 2005
- **Control Measures:**
  - SO$_x$ (Sulfur oxide) limits: Global **0.50% m/m** (from 2020); ECA **0.10% m/m**
  - NOx Tier standards (Tier I, II, III — depending on ship construction date)
  - **EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index) — new ships from 2013
  - **SEEMP** (Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan) — all ships
  - **CII** (Carbon Intensity Indicator) rating system (A–E) — from 2023
  - **IMO 2023 Strategy** — Revised GHG reduction: Net-zero by **~2050**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voluntary Annex VI Adoption (KTU Favourite Question):** Annex VI was the *first* IMO convention adopted under the **"tacit acceptance"** amendment procedure, meaning amendments enter into force unless a specific number of states object. This has revolutionized regulatory responsiveness.

### 2.1.3 The Concept of "Special Areas" Under MARPOL

A **Special Area** is a sea region where, due to its oceanographic and ecological characteristics, special mandatory methods are required to prevent pollution.

| Special Area | Annex Applicable | Entry into Force |
|---|---|---|
| Mediterranean Sea | I, II, V | Multiple dates |
| Baltic Sea | I, IV, V, VI (SO$_x$) | Multiple dates |
| Black Sea | I, V | Various |
| Red Sea | V | Multiple dates |
| Gulf of Aden | V | Various |
| Antarctic Area | I, IV, V, VI | Various |
| North Sea (incl. English Channel) | V, VI (SO$_x$ ECA) | 2007 |
| Caribbean Sea | V | 2013 |
| Arctic Waters | V (Polar Code supplements) | 2017 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are Special Areas created?** Because ocean currents in enclosed or semi-enclosed seas have *low flushing rates*, meaning pollutants persist longer, making them ecologically more vulnerable.

---

## 2.2 The OPRC Convention (1990) and OPRC-HNS Protocol (2000)

### 2.2.1 OPRC — Oil Pollution Preparedness, Response and Co-operation

- **Adopted:** 30 November 1990
- **Entry into Force:** 13 May 1995
- **Three Pillars:**
  1. **Preparedness** — Every ship must carry a **SOPEP** (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan)
  2. **Response** — National and regional contingency plans
  3. **Co-operation** — Mutual assistance between states

### 2.2.2 OPRC-HNS Protocol (2000)

- **Entry into Force:** 14 June 2007
- **Extension of OPRC to Hazardous and Noxious Substances (HNS)** — i.e., chemicals other than oil.
- Requires emergency plans for incidents involving bulk chemicals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOPEP vs. SOPEP vs. SMPEP — Do Not Confuse:**
> - **SOPEP** = Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan (mandatory under MARPOL Annex I + OPRC)
> - **SMPEP** = Shipboard Marine Pollution Emergency Plan (mandatory under MARPOL Annex II)
> Both must be approved by the flag state and carried on board.

---

## 2.3 The Ballast Water Management Convention (BWM, 2004)

- **Adopted:** 13 February 2004
- **Entry into Force:** 8 September 2017 (after Finland's ratification triggered the 30% tonnage threshold)
- **Core Obligation:** Ships must manage ballast water to **"D-2 Biological Standard"** — limiting viable organisms and indicators of concern.

### 2.3.1 D-1 vs. D-2 Performance Standards

| Standard | Requirement | Method |
|---|---|---|
| **D-1 (Ballast Water Exchange)** | ≥ 95% volumetric exchange of ballast | Tank-to-tank exchange at sea |
| **D-2 (Biological Discharge Standard)** | ≤ 10 viable organisms ≥ 50 μm per m³; ≤ 10 viable organisms < 50 μm per mL | BWMS (Ballast Water Management System) — UV, filtration, chemical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phased Compliance Schedule (BWM):**
> - New ships (keel laid after 8 Sept 2017): D-2 at delivery
> - Existing ships: D-2 by **8 September 2024** (extended due to implementation challenges) — *KTU examiners frequently test this*
> - **BWMP** (Ballast Water Management Plan) + **BWRB** (Ballast Water Record Book) mandatory.

---

## 2.4 The Anti-Fouling Systems Convention (AFS, 2001)

- **Adopted:** 5 October 2001
- **Entry into Force:** 17 September 2008
- **Background:** **Tributyltin (TBT)** — a biocide used in anti-fouling paints — was found to cause severe endocrine disruption in marine organisms (e.g., *imposex* in sea snails).
- **Ban:** From **1 January 2003**, all ships banned from applying TBT-based paints; from **1 January 2008**, ships must *not bear* TBT on hulls.
- **Alternative:** Approved sealants/coatings free of organotin compounds.

---

## 2.5 Liability and Compensation Framework

### 2.5.1 CLC 1969 — Civil Liability Convention

- **Adopted:** 29 November 1969
- **Amended by:** 1992 Protocol (entered into force 1996 — the operative version today)
- **Principle:** **Strict liability** of the shipowner for oil pollution damage in coastal states.
- **Liability Limits:**
  - For a tanker ≤ 5,000 GT: **SDR 4.51 million**
  - For larger tankers: **SDR 4.51 million + SDR 631 × GT above 5,000**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SDR = Special Drawing Rights**, an international reserve asset defined by the IMF. Used as the monetary unit in most maritime liability conventions to avoid currency fluctuations. **1 SDR ≈ 1.33 USD** (variable).

### 2.5.2 FUND Convention 1971/1992

- Establishes an **International Oil Pollution Compensation Fund** to compensate victims when:
  - The shipowner is insolvent
  - Damage exceeds shipowner's liability limit
  - The shipowner is exempt from liability (e.g., act of war)

### 2.5.3 HNS Convention 1996/2010

- **Entry into Force:** Not yet (awaiting sufficient ratifications)
- Covers **Hazardous and Noxious Substances** other than oil.
- Liability tiers based on ship gross tonnage.

### 2.5.4 Bunker Convention 2001

- **Entry into Force:** 21 November 2008
- **Scope:** Pollution damage from **bunker oil** (fuel oil from any ship's bunker tanks) — i.e., non-tanker spills.
- **Liability:** Strict liability of shipowner with compulsory insurance.

---

## 2.6 UNCLOS — The Umbrella Treaty

The **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)**, signed in **Montego Bay, Jamaica in 1982** and entered into force in **1994**, contains **Part XII (Articles 192–237)** dedicated exclusively to marine pollution.

| Article | Subject |
|---|---|
| 192 | General obligation to protect and preserve the marine environment |
| 194 | Measures to prevent, reduce, and control pollution |
| 211 | Pollution from vessels (basis for MARPOL) |
| 217 | Flag state enforcement |
| 218 | Port state enforcement |
| 220 | Coastal state enforcement |
| 235 | Responsibility and liability |

---

## 2.7 London Convention 1972 & London Protocol 1996

- **London Convention 1972** (entered into force 1975): Controls **dumping at sea** (ocean incineration, industrial waste, sewage sludge).
- **London Protocol 1996** (entered into force 2006): Updates and *reverses the presumption* — dumping is prohibited unless explicitly permitted (precautionary approach).

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction (KTU Favourite):**
> - **MARPOL** = pollution from *routine ship operations*
> - **London Convention/Protocol** = pollution from *deliberate dumping at sea*

---

## 2.8 Intervention Convention 1969

- **Adopted:** 29 November 1969
- Allows coastal states to take **proportional measures** (including sinking the source vessel in extreme cases) on the high seas to prevent, mitigate, or eliminate danger from oil spills.
- Inspired by the *Torrey Canyon* incident (1967).

---

## 2.10 Liability Calculation Formula (CLC 92)

For tankers above **5,000 GT**:

$$L_{CLC} = 4.51 \times 10^6 + 631 \times (GT - 5000) \text{ SDR}$$

For tankers up to and including **5,000 GT**:

$$L_{CLC} = 4.51 \times 10^6 \text{ SDR}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These limits are subject to revision by the Legal Committee of the IMO every few years to account for inflation.

---

|---|---|
| **Shipowners** | Predictable compliance costs; liability ceiling |
| **Seafarers** | Clear operational rules (SOPEP drills, ODME use) |
| **Coastal States** | Legal basis to demand compensation |
| **Port States** | Authority to detain non-compliant ships |
| **Insurers** | Quantifiable risk (P&I Clubs) |
| **Marine Environment** | Cumulative reduction in pollution |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liability Cap Under CLC 92 Protocol

The Civil Liability Convention establishes a strict-liability formula tied to vessel gross tonnage (GT). Let us derive the maximum shipowner liability for a typical VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) of **300,000 GT**.

**Given:**
- Gross tonnage: $GT = 300{,}000$
- CLC 92 Protocol formula applies for tankers > 5,000 GT
- Conversion: **1 SDR ≈ 1.33 USD** (approximate, varies daily)

**Step 1 — Apply the liability formula:**

$$L_{CLC} = 4.51 \times 10^6 + 631 \times (GT - 5{,}000) \text{ SDR}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute GT = 300,000:**

$$L_{CLC} = 4.51 \times 10^6 + 631 \times (300{,}000 - 5{,}000) \text{ SDR}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracketed term:**

$$300{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 295{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 631:**

$$631 \times 295{,}000 = 186{,}145{,}000 \text{ SDR}$$

**Step 5 — Sum with the base amount:**

$$L_{CLC} = 4{,}510{,}000 + 186{,}145{,}000 \text{ SDR}$$

$$L_{CLC} = 190{,}655{,}000 \text{ SDR}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to USD (approximate):**

$$L_{USD} = 190{,}655{,}000 \times 1.33 \approx 253{,}571{,}150 \text{ USD}$$

$$\boxed{L_{CLC} \approx 190.66 \text{ million SDR } \approx 253.57 \text{ million USD}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This figure represents the **maximum** liability under CLC 92. Beyond this, the **FUND Convention** may provide additional compensation up to further limits. For a VLCC, total compensation (CLC + FUND + Supplementary Fund) can reach **~1 billion USD**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — MARPOL Annex I Discharge Compliance

A bulk carrier is at position **60 nautical miles from the nearest land**. The ODMCS readings show bilge water oil content of **12 ppm**. Is discharge permitted?

**Step 1 — Identify applicable Annex I regulation:**

MARPOL Annex I, **Regulation 15 (Discharge of Oil)** requires:
- Ship **en route**
- ODMCS in operation, with oil content **≤ 15 ppm**
- Ship **> 12 nm from nearest land** (in special areas: stricter)
- Oil not originating from cargo pump-room bilges (unless segregated)

**Step 2 — Evaluate each condition:**

| Condition | Actual | Required | Compliant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| En route | Yes (assumed) | Yes | ✅ |
| ODMCS operating | Yes | Yes | ✅ |
| Oil content | 12 ppm | ≤ 15 ppm | ✅ |
| Distance from land | 60 nm | > 12 nm | ✅ |

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$\text{Discharge PERMITTED — All four criteria satisfied}$$

$$\boxed{12 \text{ ppm} \leq 15 \text{ ppm (limit)} \therefore \text{ Legal discharge}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** Note the logical AND condition — if **any** single condition fails, the discharge becomes illegal, regardless of other parameters.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Sulphur Cap Compliance Calculation

A container ship burns **IFO 380** (Intermediate Fuel Oil with 3.5% sulphur) inside an **ECA** (Emission Control Area). Calculate the required Bunker Delivery Note (BDN) sulphur content for compliance.

**Given:**
- ECA limit (since 2015): $S_{ECA} = 0.10\% \, m/m$
- Current fuel: $S_{fuel} = 3.50\%$

**Step 1 — Determine the necessary fuel grade:**

An ultra-low-sulphur fuel oil (ULSFO) with maximum $0.10\% \, m/m$ sulphur must be used, OR an equivalent scrubber (EGCS) achieving equivalent SO$_x$ reduction.

**Step 2 — Alternative calculation — Scrubber equivalent:**

The IMO equivalent method states:

$$\text{SO}_x \, \text{Reduction Factor} = \frac{S_{input}}{S_{output}}$$

For an output of 0.10% required from a 3.50% input:

$$\text{Reduction Factor} = \frac{3.50}{0.10} = 35$$

$$\boxed{\text{Required SO}_x \text{ reduction} = 97.14\%}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

- Either use ULSFO (≤ 0.10% S), OR
- Install a hybrid scrubber achieving ≥ 97% SO$_x$ reduction.

---

## 3.4 BWM Compliance — D-2 Standard Numerical Check

A ship uses a UV-based BWMS. A test sample shows the following post-treatment counts:
- Organisms ≥ 50 μm: **8 organisms/m³**
- Organisms < 50 μm and ≥ 10 μm: **9 organisms/mL**
- Indicator microbes:
  - *Vibrio cholerae*: 0 cfu/100 mL
  - *E. coli*: 9 cfu/100 mL
  - Intestinal enterococci: 5 cfu/100 mL

**D-2 Limits:**
- ≤ 10 viable organisms ≥ 50 μm per m³
- ≤ 10 viable organisms < 50 μm per mL
- ≤ 1 cfu/100 mL *Vibrio cholerae*
- ≤ 250 cfu/100 mL *E. coli*
- ≤ 100 cfu/100 mL intestinal enterococci

**Evaluation:**

| Parameter | Measured | Limit | Compliant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Organisms ≥ 50 μm | 8 | ≤ 10 | ✅ |
| Organisms < 50 μm | 9 | ≤ 10 | ✅ |
| *V. cholerae* | 0 | ≤ 1 | ✅ |
| *E. coli* | 9 | ≤ 250 | ✅ |
| Enterococci | 5 | ≤ 100 | ✅ |

$$\boxed{\text{All parameters compliant with D-2 standard}}$$

---

## 3.6 Decision Tree — Is Discharge Permitted Under Annex V?

```
Discharge of garbage overboard?
                │
                ▼
        Is it plastic?
                │
        ┌───────┴───────┐
        YES             NO
        │               │
        ▼               ▼
    NEVER          What type of garbage?
   ALLOWED                │
                ┌────────┼────────┬─────────┐
                ▼        ▼        ▼         ▼
             Food     Cargo     Cleaning   Operational
             waste    residues  agents     waste
                │        │        │         │
                ▼        ▼        ▼         ▼
            Distance?  <12 nm?  Special?  <12 nm?
                │        │        │         │
                ▼        ▼        ▼         ▼
        ≥3 nm OK   Permitted  Stricter  Permitted
       (comminuted)           rules
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Under Annex V, **plastics may never be discharged at sea — period**, regardless of distance. This is the strictest rule across all MARPOL annexes.

---

## 3.7 Concept Verification — SDR Currency Stability

The use of SDR (Special Drawing Rights) in maritime liability conventions is a deliberate design choice to insulate liability caps from **currency volatility**. The SDR is a basket currency comprising five major currencies:

$$SDR = 0.58252 \cdot USD + 0.38671 \cdot EUR + 0.10587 \cdot GBP + 0.03471 \cdot JPY + 0.01731 \cdot CNY$$

(Weights as of IMF 2022 review)

This means that if the USD weakens against the EUR, the SDR value adjusts upward automatically, preserving the *real value* of the compensation cap.

---

## 3.8 Step-by-Step — Identifying the Applicable Convention

A chemical tanker suffers a major spill of **acrylonitrile** (Category Y substance) in the **Bay of Bengal**.

**Step 1 — Identify the substance category:**
- Acrylonitrile = Category Y (noxious liquid substance, bulk)

**Step 2 — Identify the applicable IMO instrument:**
- **MARPOL Annex II** regulates discharge from cargo operations.

**Step 3 — If a spill (not discharge) occurs — which response convention?**
- **OPRC-HNS Protocol 2000** — covers hazardous and noxious substance incidents.

**Step 4 — Liability:**
- **HNS Convention 1996/2010** (if in force for the relevant state).

$$\boxed{\text{MARPOL Annex II (prevention)} + \text{OPRC-HNS (response)} + \text{HNS Convention (liability)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-layer architecture:** prevention, response, liability — every pollution scenario fits into this triad.
