<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define an Environmental Management System (EMS) and name the international standard governing it.**

**Model Answer:**
An **Environmental Management System (EMS)** is a structured framework of policies, procedures, processes, and resources that an organization uses to manage and reduce its environmental impacts, comply with applicable legal requirements, and continually improve its environmental performance. It is governed internationally by the **ISO 14001:2015** standard. In the maritime industry, EMS forms the environmental pillar of the **Safety Management System (SMS)** required by the **ISM Code**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for standard, 1 for maritime link.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between an Environmental Aspect and an Environmental Impact with one maritime example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Environmental Aspect | Environmental Impact |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Definition | Element of an activity that can interact with the environment | Any change to the environment resulting from an aspect |
| Level | Cause (Activity-level) | Effect (Consequence-level) |
| Maritime Example | Bunkering of HFO from barge | Oil spill causing marine water pollution |

*(3 marks: 1 each for distinction + 1 for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** as applied to ISO 14001:2015 EMS implementation on board a merchant ship. Explain each phase with reference to relevant MARPOL clauses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **process of identifying and evaluating environmental aspects and impacts** on board a ship. Using the formula $S = M \times F \times R$, calculate the significance score for the following three aspects and classify them into priority categories: **(7 marks)**

| Aspect | M | F | R |
|--------|---|---|---|
| A1: Oil spill during bunkering | 5 | 3 | 5 |
| A2: Sewage discharge without treatment | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| A3: Disposal of food waste at sea | 2 | 5 | 3 |

*(Threshold: Low 1–25, Medium 26–60, High 61–125.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) PDCA Cycle in ISO 14001:2015 (7 marks):**

1. **PLAN (Clause 6):** The company establishes an environmental policy, prepares a register of environmental aspects (e.g., oil spills under **MARPOL Annex I**, garbage disposal under **Annex V**, SOx emissions under **Annex VI**), identifies applicable legal requirements, and sets SMART environmental objectives.
2. **DO (Clause 7):** Resources, roles, and competence are ensured. Operational controls like SOPs for bilge pumping, garbage segregation, and bunker operations are documented. Emergency preparedness includes SOPEP (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan).
3. **CHECK (Clause 8):** Continuous monitoring — e.g., **oil content meter** readings (< 15 ppm per MARPOL Annex I), SOx fuel sampling (< 0.10% in ECA), internal audits of the EMS, evaluation of compliance with legal obligations.
4. **ACT (Clause 9):** Top management reviews audit findings, non-conformities, and corrective actions. Continual improvement may involve retrofitting scrubbers, switching to LNG, or improving crew training frequency.

**(b) Aspect Significance Calculations (7 marks):**

For A1 — Oil spill during bunkering:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{A1} &= M \times F \times R \\
&= 5 \times 3 \times 5 \\
&= 75 \rightarrow \text{High Priority}
\end{aligned}
$$

For A2 — Sewage discharge without treatment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{A2} &= 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64 \rightarrow \text{High Priority}
\end{aligned}
$$

For A3 — Food waste disposal at sea:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{A3} &= 2 \times 5 \times 3 = 30 \rightarrow \text{Medium Priority}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Aspects A1 and A2 demand stringent operational controls, frequent audits, and dedicated crew drills. A3 requires standard segregation and record-keeping under MARPOL Annex V.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **benefits and challenges** of implementing an EMS on board merchant vessels. How does EMS integration with the **ISM Code's Safety Management System** strengthen environmental performance? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of internal audits and management review** in an EMS. Outline the steps involved in handling a **Non-Conformity (NC)** raised during an internal audit on board a ship. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Benefits and Challenges (7 marks):**

**Benefits:**
1. Regulatory compliance with **MARPOL 73/78** and **flag state** requirements → avoids port-state control detentions.
2. Reduced fuel and waste-handling costs via improved energy efficiency (links to **EEDI/EEXI/CII**).
3. Lower insurance premiums with **P&I clubs**.
4. Improved crew environmental awareness and corporate CSR reputation.
5. Better preparedness for **oil spill emergencies** (SOPEP drills).

**Challenges:**
1. High initial cost of certification, training, and documentation.
2. Resistance to change among crew and shore staff.
3. Continuous data collection burden (Oil Record Book, Garbage Record Book, Bunker Delivery Notes).
4. Maintaining auditor-calibrated equipment (e.g., oil content meters, exhaust gas sensors).
5. Variability across fleets and flag administrations.

**Integration with ISM Code:** The ISM Code's pollution-prevention clauses merge seamlessly with EMS. The **Designated Person Ashore (DPA)**, **Master**, and **Chief Engineer** roles overlap with EMS management representatives. Safety and environmental objectives become unified — creating a single compliance culture rather than siloed systems.

**(b) Internal Audit and Management Review (7 marks):**

**Internal Audits (Clause 9.2):**
- Conducted at planned intervals by **trained auditors** (independent of the audited function).
- Verify conformance with ISO 14001, MARPOL, and internal SOPs.
- Output: **Audit Report** with findings, observations, and **Non-Conformities (NCs)**.

**Management Review (Clause 9.3):**
- Conducted by top management (e.g., Fleet Manager, DPA) at planned intervals.
- Inputs: Audit results, compliance status, NC trends, stakeholder feedback, opportunities for improvement.
- Outputs: Decisions on resource allocation, revised objectives, and improvement actions.

**Steps for Handling a Non-Conformity (NC):**
1. **Identify** the NC (e.g., oil content meter showing > 15 ppm during discharge).
2. **Contain** immediate risk (stop the discharge immediately).
3. **Investigate Root Cause** using tools like the **5-Why analysis** or **Ishikawa fishbone**.
4. **Implement Corrective Action** (calibrate the meter, retest, modify SOP).
5. **Verify Effectiveness** of corrective action in a follow-up audit.
6. **Document and Close** the NC in the CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) log.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Aspect with Impact** — This is the #1 mistake in KTU EMS answers. Memorize: *"Aspect = Cause (Activity); Impact = Effect (Environment)."*
> 2. **Forgetting MARPOL Annex references** — A 14-mark answer without MARPOL loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Skipping numerical justification** — In Part B, always plug values into the $S = M \times F \times R$ formula rather than leaving the table blank.
> 4. **Ignoring the cyclical nature of PDCA** — PDCA is **not linear**; mention that ACT feeds back into PLAN.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EMS = ISO 14001:2015** backbone framework for environmental governance.
- **PDCA Cycle** is the **non-negotiable core** — Plan (Clause 6), Do (7), Check (8), Act (9).
- **Aspect = Activity-level (cause)**; **Impact = Environment-level (effect)**.
- **Significance Score** $S = M \times F \times R$ determines aspect prioritization.
- **MARPOL Annexes I, IV, V, VI** are the maritime regulatory anchors of EMS.
- **EMS integrates with ISM Code** — together they form the vessel's compliance ecosystem.
- **Internal audits + Management Review + CAPA** drive continual improvement.
- **Documentation pyramid**: Policy → Procedures → Records.
- **Key numerical limits to memorize**:
  - Bilge oil discharge: $\leq 15 \text{ ppm}$
  - ECA sulphur: $\leq 0.10\%$
  - Sewage BOD: $\leq 50 \text{ mg/L}$
- **Always use `\vert` or `\mid`** instead of literal `|` in LaTeX within markdown tables.
- **NC handling sequence**: Identify → Contain → Root Cause → Corrective Action → Verify → Close.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maritime Security and Counter-Terrorism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maritime Security and Counter-Terrorism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maritime Security** is the umbrella discipline encompassing all preventive, protective, and responsive measures adopted by the international community, flag states, coastal states, port authorities, and shipping operators to safeguard the global maritime domain from unlawful interference, hostile acts, criminal exploitation, and terrorist threats. It operates under a multilayered legal-policy architecture anchored in the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS, 1982)**, the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** Chapter XI-2, and the **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code (2002)**.

**Maritime Counter-Terrorism (MCT)** is the specialized subset of maritime security that specifically addresses the prevention, interdiction, mitigation, and response to terrorist acts conducted in, on, or against vessels, offshore installations, ports, and critical maritime infrastructure. Its operational legal bedrock is the **Convention for the Suppression of Unlawful Acts against the Safety of Maritime Navigation (SUA Convention, 1988, as amended by the 2005 Protocols)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Examiners expect students to distinguish *general maritime security* (broad policing of crimes at sea) from *maritime counter-terrorism* (a narrower, threat-specific doctrine aimed at ideologically/religiously/politically motivated violence against shipping and ports).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a massive apartment complex (the **world's shipping fleet**) where thousands of residents (cargo containers, vessels, crew, port workers) live, transit, and interact daily. The complex has multiple entry gates (ports), corridors (sea lanes), and shared amenities (anchorages, canals). 

- **Maritime Security** is the **overall security apparatus of the building**: CCTV cameras (AIS, LRIT), access cards (ISPS Code certifications), guard patrols (naval escorts), and fire alarms (Ship Security Alert Systems).
- **Counter-Terrorism** is the **elite counter-assault unit** specifically trained to neutralize a *deliberate, coordinated violent attack* (a bomb-laden small boat ramming a tanker, a hijacking for propaganda, a dirty-bomb container) — distinct from a routine burglary (piracy, smuggling).

**The Standard Security Posture (Three Security Levels):**

| Level | Code Word | Analogy in the Building | Threat Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Security Level 1** | *Green* | Normal day, guards at lobby | Normal — minimum baseline |
| **Security Level 2** | *Yellow* | Heightened awareness, extra patrol | Heightened risk of incident |
| **Security Level 3** | *Red* | Full lockdown, K-9 units deployed | Imminent/probable attack |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** Per UNODC and IMO reports, approximately **80–90% of global trade by volume** moves through the maritime domain, making maritime counter-terrorism a critical pillar of international economic and geopolitical stability.

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

The following baseline figures are mandated by IMO and frequently cited in KTU valuation:

- **ISPS Code applicability:** Applies to **passenger ships ≥ 12 passengers**, **cargo ships ≥ 500 GT**, and **port facilities serving such ships**.
- **SSAS (Ship Security Alert System) transmission:** Silent alert sent to flag-state administration via **two separate communication channels**.
- **LRIT data polling interval:** Every **6 hours** for ships underway.
- **PR24 (Personnel Recovery) window under SUA:** Vessel must be boarded within **2 hours** of flag-state authorization.
- **ISPS Code entry into force date:** **1 July 2004**.
- **BMP (Best Management Practices) latest version:** BMP5 (released 2018, updated 2024 guidance).
- **High Risk Area (HRA) for piracy:** Gulf of Aden, Somali Basin, Arabian Sea — geographically defined corridor.
- **Critical Maritime Infrastructure (CMI):** Refineries, LNG terminals, undersea cables, offshore rigs.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love asking for **acronym expansions**. Memorize: ISPS, SSAS, AIS, LRIT, CSR, SSO, CSO, PFSO, PFSO, PCASP, BMP, HRA, CMI, MDA, SUA, CTF, C-TPAT, CSI, PSI.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Maritime Security Doctrine

Maritime security and counter-terrorism are built on three interlocking pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Legal & Treaty Framework**
- **UNCLOS (1982):** Defines territorial sea (12 nm), EEZ (200 nm), high seas, and right of *innocent passage* and *transit passage*.
- **SOLAS Chapter XI-2:** The "Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety and Security" — the legal gateway that brought the ISPS Code into force.
- **ISPS Code (2002, in force 2004):** The operational heartbeat of maritime security — divided into Part A (mandatory) and Part B (guidance/recommended).
- **SUA Convention (1988) + 2005 Protocols:** Specifically criminalizes unlawful acts against navigation, expanded post-9/11 to include terrorism as a distinct offense, WMD transport, and forced use of vessels as weapons.
- **UN Security Council Resolutions 1816, 1838, 1846 (2008):** Authorized international counter-piracy operations off Somalia.

**Pillar 2 — Operational & Technical Architecture**

The layered defense follows the **Deter → Detect → Deny → Defend → Defeat** continuum:

1. **Deter:** Visible naval presence, citadel hardening, BMP compliance, citadel design (double-skin bulkheads).
2. **Detect:** AIS (Automatic Identification System), LRIT (Long Range Identification & Tracking), SSAS (Ship Security Alert System), coastal radar chains, satellite AIS (S-AIS).
3. **Deny:** Access control at ports, TWIC cards (Transportation Worker Identification Credential), biometric crew vetting.
4. **Defend:** Ship Security Plan (SSP) activation, muster, citadel refuge, evasive maneuvering.
5. **Defeat:** Privately Contracted Armed Security Personnel (PCASP), Vessel Protection Detachments (VPDs), naval boarding action.

**Pillar 3 — Intelligence, Cooperation & Capacity Building**
- **Maritime Domain Awareness (MDA):** The effective understanding of anything associated with the global maritime domain that could impact security, safety, economy, or environment.
- **Regional Cooperation Agreements:** ReCAAP (Asia), Djibouti Code of Conduct, Yaoundé Code of Conduct (W. Africa).
- **Industry Self-Regulation:** BIMCO, OCIMF, INTERTANKO, INTERCARGO, WLP&I Clubs — issue the **BMP (Best Management Practices)** guides.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Framework Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic is **conceptual-policy-oriented**, not numerical. Therefore the "Formula Sheet" below is a **Structured Framework Matrix** mapping the *core operational concepts* examiners test.

| # | Framework Element | Core Definition | Key Actors | Trigger Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** | Documented plan detailing security measures for a specific ship | Company Security Officer (CSO) / Ship Security Officer (SSO) | Mandatory under ISPS for every applicable ship |
| 2 | **Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP)** | Plan for each port facility interfacing with ISPS ships | Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO) | Approved by Contracting Government |
| 3 | **Declaration of Security (DoS)** | Agreement between ship and port facility on shared security responsibilities | Master ↔ PFSO | When Security Level changes or interface risk warrants |
| 4 | **Ship Security Assessment (SSA)** | Risk assessment underpinning the SSP | Recognized Security Organization (RSO) | Required once and after major changes |
| 5 | **Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)** | Onboard record of ship's security history | Master | Updated continuously through ship's life |
| 6 | **SSAS Activation** | Silent alert to flag state of imminent security threat | SSO / Master | Piracy/terror attack in progress |
| 7 | **SUA Article 8 bis** | Right of a state party to board a vessel suspected of terrorism offenses | Naval / coast guard boarding party | Reasonable grounds; flag state consent (or PR24 if flag unreachable) |
| 8 | **BMP5 Principles** | Industry best practice for self-protection in piracy HRA | Master, CSO, crew | Transit through Gulf of Aden / Indian Ocean HRA |
| 9 | **Proliferation Security Initiative (PSI)** | US-led (2003) voluntary interdiction of WMD-related shipments | 100+ participating states | Suspected WMD cargo in breach of UNSC resolutions |
| 10 | **Container Security Initiative (CSI)** | Pre-screening of containers at foreign ports before US arrival | US CBP + partner customs | Containers bound for US ports |
| 11 | **ReCAAP Information Sharing Centre** | Regional agreement (2006) against piracy/armed robbery | 21 Asian states + 9 dialogue partners | Armed robbery/piracy in Asian waters |
| 12 | **Critical Maritime Infrastructure (CMI)** | Assets essential to maritime operations whose loss would severely impact security/economy | Govt. + private operators | National risk register designation |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Policy Utility

Maritime security design integrates with:
- **Naval Architecture:** Citadel design, citadel strength, bridge access control, retractable citadel lighting, ballistic plating.
- **Marine Electrical Engineering:** SSAS hardware integration, AIS transponder tampering protection, LRIT conformance test equipment, secure VHF (GMDSS).
- **Port & Harbor Engineering:** PIDS (Perimeter Intrusion Detection Systems), CCTV analytics, hydrosweep acoustic sensors, fiber-optic fence sensors.
- **Cyber Security:** AIS spoofing countermeasures, ECDIS hardening, ransomware mitigation in port logistics (Maersk *NotPetya* 2017 case study).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A single shipping disruption (e.g., Suez Canal blockage by *Ever Given* in 2021) can ripple into **~$9.6 billion/day** in global trade losses. Security is therefore inseparable from logistics economics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Note:** This topic does not involve closed-form mathematical derivation. Instead, the KTU 2024 scheme assesses *analytical reasoning* through structured threat-modeling, security-level escalation logic, and legal-procedural flow. The following exhaustive analytical matrix replicates the step-by-step rigor of a derivation by mapping every logical transition.

### 3.1 Exhaustive Analytical Procedure: ISPS Code Security Level Escalation

**Scenario Premise:** A vessel (*MV "Kerala Voyager"*, IMO 9876543, 30,000 DWT product tanker, flag India) is transiting the Gulf of Aden. Intelligence indicates a credible terrorist threat against tankers carrying petroleum products in this region.

**Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation (Logical Walkthrough):**

**Step 1 — Baseline Identification (Level 1 — Green)**
- At all times, the vessel operates at **Security Level 1** as a default under ISPS Code Part A, Section 7.
- Routine measures: access control, monitoring of deck areas, restricted access to bridge/machinery spaces, monitoring of persons and baggage.

**Step 2 — Threat Intelligence Receipt**
- Intelligence input may arrive from: (a) flag state administration, (b) coastal state (Yemen/Somalia), (c) MSCHOA (Maritime Security Centre Horn of Africa), (d) UKMTO (United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations), (e) industry alerts.
- The CSO/SSO evaluates credibility. **Decision criterion:** Does the intelligence establish a *heightened risk of a security incident*?

**Step 3 — Escalation Decision: Security Level 2 (Yellow)**
- Trigger: credible (but not imminent) threat.
- Additional measures under ISPS Annex:
  - Increased deck patrols.
  - Activation of SSAS readiness check.
  - Pre-positioning of fire hoses against small-boat attacks.
  - Mustering crew to non-essential stations cleared.
  - Lockdown of accommodation ladders.
  - Coordination with embarked PCASP (if any).

**Step 4 — Further Threat Confirmation: Security Level 3 (Red)**
- Trigger: *imminent or probable security incident* (e.g., specific threat naming the vessel or convoy).
- Maximum measures:
  - Restriction of access to a single controlled point.
  - Cessation of cargo operations.
  - Preparation for SSAS activation.
  - Implementation of *citadel refuge protocol* if boarding threat is imminent.
  - Coordination with naval CTF-151 (Combined Task Force) escorting vessels.

**Step 5 — Attack Execution & SSAS Activation**
- If the threat materializes into an actual attack, the **Master** or **SSO** activates the SSAS.
- The alert is transmitted via the ship's GMDSS terminal to the flag-state designated authority (in India: the Directorate General of Shipping, Mumbai) and simultaneously to the company CSO.
- The alert is **silent** — it does NOT sound an alarm on board. This prevents attackers from learning that help has been requested.

**Step 6 — Flag-State Coordination & SUA Boarding**
- Flag-state authorizes a responding naval vessel to board under SUA Article 8 bis procedures.
- **PR24 Protocol:** If the flag state does not respond within 2 hours and the vessel is suspected of certain terrorism offenses, other states parties may board with flag-state consent presumed.
- Naval action: approach, hailing, warning shots if necessary, boarding by VBSS (Visit, Board, Search, and Seizure) team.

**Step 7 — De-escalation & Post-Incident Reporting**
- Following neutralization, vessel transits to a safe port for **SSO investigation report**.
- **CSR (Continuous Synopsis Record)** is updated to reflect the security incident.
- **Lessons learned** are fed back to update the SSA (Ship Security Assessment).

### 3.2 Exhaustive Threat Categorization Matrix

| Threat Category | Modus Operandi | Primary Target | Mitigation Countermeasure | Reference Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Maritime Piracy** | Armed boarding for theft/ransom | Merchant vessels in transit | BMP5, citadel, PCASP, naval CTFs | UNCLOS Art. 100, SUA |
| **Maritime Terrorism** | Bombing, hijacking for propaganda, WMD use | Tankers, LNG carriers, cruise ships | ISPS, SSAS, citadel, VPDs | SUA 1988 + 2005 Protocols |
| **Smuggling (drugs, arms)** | Concealment in containers, vessels | Container ports, yachts | CSI, C-TPAT, scanning (X-ray) | UN Drug Conventions, SOLAS Ch. VI |
| **Human Trafficking** | Hidden persons in cargo holds | Ro-Ro, container ships | MLC 2006, flag-state inspection | Palermo Protocol 2000 |
| **WMD Proliferation** | Shipment of nuclear/radiological/chem material | Dual-use cargo, ports | PSI, Proliferation Screening | UNSC 1540 (2004) |
| **Cyber Attack** | Ransomware, AIS spoofing, ECDIS manipulation | Port OS, vessel nav systems | IMO 2021 cyber regs, network segregation | MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 |
| **Sabotage of CMI** | Underwater attack on cables/pipelines | Subsea telecoms cables, oil pipelines | Patrols, AIS monitoring of vessels | National law, UNCLOS Art. 113 |
| **Anchor Drag / Collision** | Forced grounding/collision | Busy sea lanes | TSS adherence, AIS, VTS | COLREGs 1972 |

### 3.3 Counter-Terrorism Action Loop (CBTAL) — Conceptual Derivation

The KTU 2024 cognitive level expected at the Apply/Analyze stage is captured in this CBTAL derivation:

$$
\text{Effective MCT} = f(\text{Intelligence}, \text{Legal Authority}, \text{Operational Capability}, \text{International Cooperation})
$$

**Analytical breakdown of each component:**

- **Intelligence (I):** Fusion of HUMINT, SIGINT, OSINT, AIS-data lakes. *Without I, no threat specificity.*
- **Legal Authority (L):** SUA, UNCLOS, UNSC Resolutions. *Without L, no right to board/interdict.*
- **Operational Capability (O):** Navies, coast guards, PCASP, VTMS. *Without O, no kinetic option.*
- **International Cooperation (C):** ReCAAP, Djibouti Code, CSI, PSI. *Without C, response is fragmented.*

**Causal Flow (Per Iterative Loop):**

1. **Sense:** AIS/LRIT/Satellite SAR identifies anomaly (vessel loitering, AIS-dark).
2. **Understand:** Pattern-of-life analysis confirms terrorism indicators.
3. **Decide:** Flag state authorizes boarding under SUA Art. 8 bis.
4. **Act:** VBSS team interdicts, WMD-cargo (if any) is secured, crew is rescued.
5. **Recover:** Legal proceedings, vessel release, lessons fed back to intelligence cycle.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Insight:** When a question asks "explain the legal basis for boarding a suspect vessel," always cite **SUA 1988 + 2005 Protocols first**, then UNCLOS, then UNSC Resolutions. Layered citation scores maximum marks.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**

> Define the **ISPS Code**. List its **three Security Levels** and briefly state the conditions under which each level is declared.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code** is the IMO framework adopted under SOLAS Chapter XI-2 (effective 1 July 2004) to detect security threats and take preventive measures against security incidents affecting ships and port facilities engaged in international trade.

The **three Security Levels** are:

1. **Security Level 1 (Normal):** Baseline level maintained at all times. Routine security measures apply, including restricted access, monitoring of persons and baggage, and supervision of cargo handling.
2. **Security Level 2 (Heightened):** Declared when there is a *heightened risk of a security incident*. Additional protective measures are activated, such as increased patrols, restricted access to fewer points, and coordination with port facilities via the Declaration of Security.
3. **Security Level 3 (Exceptional):** Declared when a security incident or imminent threat is probable. Maximum protective measures are imposed, including possible cessation of ship/port operations, full lockdown, and activation of the SSAS if attack materializes.

Levels are set by the **Contracting Government** based on threat intelligence. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing levels, 1 mark for conditions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

> What is the **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)**? Why is it called a "silent" alert?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)** is a mandatory GMDSS-integrated security device fitted on all ISPS-Code-applicable vessels, capable of transmitting a covert alert signal to the ship's **flag-state administration** and the **company security officer (CSO)** when a security threat (piracy, terrorism, armed robbery) is encountered.

It is termed a **"silent" alert** because:
- The transmission does **not produce any audible or visual alarm on the ship**, thereby preventing the attackers from realizing that assistance has been requested.
- It avoids provoking escalation of violence against the crew.
- It enables discreet flag-state coordination with naval forces for boarding under SUA Article 8 bis procedures.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for who receives, 1 mark for silent rationale.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU ESE – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **SUA Convention 1988 and its 2005 Protocols** as the principal legal instrument for maritime counter-terrorism. Explain the significance of the **2005 amendments**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **key elements of the ISPS Code** with reference to the Ship Security Plan (SSP), Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP), and the role of the Recognized Security Organization (RSO).

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question A

**(a) SUA Convention 1988 + 2005 Protocols (7 Marks)**

The **Convention for the Suppression of Unlawful Acts against the Safety of Maritime Navigation (SUA Convention)** was adopted in **Rome on 10 March 1988** at a conference convened in response to the **1985 Achille Lauro hijacking**. It entered into force on **1 March 1992**.

**Original 1988 Framework:**
- Article 3 criminalized specific acts: seizing a ship by force, committing acts of violence against persons on board, placing devices aboard likely to destroy the ship, and seriously endangering safe navigation.
- Article 6 established **universal jurisdiction** — any state party could prosecute offenders regardless of nationality or location of the act.
- Article 7 obliged parties to either extradite or prosecute (aut dedere aut judicare principle).

**Significance of the 2005 Protocols (adopted 14 October 2005, in force 28 July 2010):**

1. **Expanded Offense Catalog (New Article 3 bis):** Added offenses relating to the use of ships as weapons, transport of WMD-related materials, and acts intended to intimidate a population. *(1 mark)*
2. **Boarding Procedures (New Article 8 bis):** Established a formal regime permitting one state party to board a vessel flying the flag of another state party, when suspected of terrorism-related offenses, with the flag state's consent or via the **PR24 procedure** if no response within 2 hours. *(2 marks)*
3. **Jurisdictional Enhancements:** Clarified the rights of states to exercise jurisdiction and detailed procedures for the seizure of vessels suspected of offenses under Article 3, 3 bis, 3 ter. *(1 mark)*
4. **Fixed Platform Protocol (2005):** A separate protocol extended coverage to **offshore fixed platforms** (oil rigs, FSOs) — previously a gap. *(1 mark)*
5. **Liability and Compensation Provisions (New Annex):** Added an Annex establishing a compensation regime for innocent parties whose vessels were seized in error. *(1 mark)*
6. **Closing the WMD Loophole:** Pre-2005, transport of WMD by ship was not specifically criminalized. The 2005 Protocols made this a discrete offense, enabling **Proliferation Security Initiative (PSI)** interdictions to rest on a firmer legal footing. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently state that SUA "applies only to piracy." This is **incorrect** — SUA applies to *terrorism/unlawful acts*; piracy is governed by UNCLOS Articles 100–107 and customary international law. Mixing up the two frameworks loses 1–2 marks.

---

**(b) ISPS Code — SSP, PFSP, and RSO (7 Marks)**

The **ISPS Code (2002, in force 1 July 2004)** is the operational core of SOLAS Chapter XI-2 and applies to:
- Passenger ships engaged in international voyages,
- Cargo ships ≥ 500 GT engaged in international voyages,
- Port facilities serving such ships.

**Ship Security Plan (SSP) — (2 marks):**
The SSP is a comprehensive, ship-specific document developed by the Company Security Officer (CSO) following a **Ship Security Assessment (SSA)**. It addresses:
- Identification of security threats and vulnerabilities.
- Operational and physical security measures for each ISPS Security Level (1, 2, 3).
- Procedures for access control, restricted areas, monitoring, weapons/dangerous substances handling.
- Procedures for SSAS activation, muster, and coordination with PFSO.
- Maintenance and updating protocols, including change management after any major modification.

**Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP) — (2 marks):**
The PFSP is the port-side equivalent of the SSP. It is developed by the **Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO)** and approved by the **Contracting Government (Port State)**. It addresses:
- Perimeter security, surveillance systems, lighting, fencing, PIDS.
- Personnel vetting (TWIC cards in US, equivalent in other jurisdictions).
- Coordination with ships via the **Declaration of Security (DoS)**.
- Emergency response protocols (fire, bomb, CBRN, mass casualty).
- Training and drills (frequency: at least once every 3 months for exercises, annually for full drill).

**Recognized Security Organization (RSO) — (2 marks):**
An RSO is an entity authorized by a Contracting Government to perform specific security-related services. Functions include:
- Conducting or validating Ship Security Assessments (SSAs).
- Approving and certifying SSPs and PFSPs.
- Conducting audits and verifications on behalf of the flag state.
- Examples of recognized RSOs: classification societies (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, ClassNK), and approved private maritime security firms.

**Interlinkage (1 mark):** The SSP and PFSP are bound by the **Declaration of Security (DoS)** — a written agreement between the master and the PFSO confirming mutual understanding of each side's responsibilities during the ship-port interface.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall 2:** Students often confuse **CSO (Company Security Officer)** with **SSO (Ship Security Officer)**. CSO = shore-based, oversees multiple ships in a fleet; SSO = onboard, implements the SSP on that specific vessel. Confusing these roles costs 0.5–1 mark.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU ESE – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Maritime Domain Awareness (MDA)** and describe the **four pillars of an effective MDA framework**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Proliferation Security Initiative (PSI)** and the **Container Security Initiative (CSI)** as counter-terrorism instruments targeting WMD and cargo threats respectively.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question B

**(a) Maritime Domain Awareness — Concept and Four Pillars (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** **Maritime Domain Awareness (MDA)** is defined by the IMO and allied navies as the *effective understanding of any activity associated with the global maritime domain that could impact security, safety, the economy, or the environment*. MDA is the cognitive foundation upon which all counter-terrorism and counter-piracy decisions rest.

**The Four Pillars of Effective MDA:**

1. **Information Sharing and Knowledge Management (2 marks):** Real-time fusion of data from multiple sources — AIS, LRIT, satellite SAR imagery, coastal VTS, customs manifests, crew biometric records, port logistics data — into a Common Operational Picture (COP). Standardization of data formats (e.g., NATO APP-11) and inter-agency MOUs (e.g., between Navy, Coast Guard, Customs, Intelligence) are essential.

2. **Maritime Intelligence and Threat Assessment (2 marks):** Continuous analytical assessment of *pattern-of-life anomalies* — e.g., vessels going "dark" by switching off AIS, ships operating outside established sea lanes, repeated rendezvous at sea, falsified flags or port-of-call histories. Outputs are threat advisories, BOLO ("Be On LookOut") alerts, and risk-ratings per vessel.

3. **Surveillance, Reconnaissance and Detection (2 marks):** The technical means of observing the maritime domain: coastal radar chains, maritime patrol aircraft (MPA), unmanned aerial systems (UAS), satellite constellations (S-AIS, optical, SAR), underwater acoustic sensors (SOSUS-class for CMI protection), and human intelligence networks.

4. **International and Interagency Cooperation (1 mark):** No single state can achieve MDA over the global maritime domain. Regional fusion centres (e.g., ReCAAP ISC, MARSIC), bilateral ship-rider agreements, information exchange with industry (BIMCO, OCIMF), and participation in multinational task forces are essential.

> [!TIP]
> For a 7-mark question, examiners expect **each pillar to carry ~1.5–2 marks** of detail. Listing them without elaboration scores poorly.

---

**(b) PSI and CSI as Counter-Terrorism Instruments (7 Marks)**

**Proliferation Security Initiative (PSI) — (3.5 marks):**
Launched in **Krakow, Poland on 31 May 2003** under US President George W. Bush, the PSI is a **voluntary, non-treaty cooperation framework** among more than **100 states** designed to interdict the transfer of **weapons of mass destruction (WMD)**, their delivery systems, and related materials to and from states and non-state actors of proliferation concern (notably Iran and North Korea historically).

- **Operational Principle:** Participants commit to: (i) interdict WMD-related shipments on the high seas consistent with international law (UNCLOS, SUA), (ii) cooperate with boarding requests, (iii) share intelligence, (iv) tighten export controls.
- **Air, Land, Sea Interdiction:** PSI operates across all transport modes; the **Proliferation Security Exercise** series rehearses combined interdiction scenarios.
- **Relationship to SUA 2005:** Post-2005 SUA Protocols provided firmer legal grounding for PSI interdictions; SUA Article 8 bis boarding procedures underpin PSI sea interdictions.
- **Limitations:** Being a *political initiative* and not a treaty, PSI relies on consensus and is sensitive to flag-state consent, making it operationally complex.

**Container Security Initiative (CSI) — (3.5 marks):**
Launched in **January 2002** by US Customs (now CBP), CSI is a **pre-screening programme** that targets the maritime container supply chain — approximately **90% of global trade by volume moves in containers**, with millions of containers entering US ports annually.

- **Operational Mechanism:** US Customs personnel are stationed at **foreign mega-ports** (initially the top 20 — Singapore, Hong Kong, Rotterdam, etc.) to identify and pre-screen high-risk containers **before they are loaded onto vessels** bound for the US.
- **Risk Targeting:** Uses Automated Targeting System (ATS), Advance Cargo Declarations (24-hour rule), and intelligence to flag containers matching risk indicators (origin, shipper, commodity, routing).
- **Outcome:** Containers cleared pre-loading receive a *green light* (fast-track); flagged containers are physically inspected or held at the foreign port before shipment, preventing the arrival of compromised containers on US soil.
- **Complementary Programme:** **Customs-Trade Partnership Against Terrorism (C-TPAT, 2001)** is the private-sector twin — a voluntary partnership where importers commit to supply-chain security best practices in exchange for expedited processing.
- **Counter-Terrorism Relevance:** CSI directly addresses the maritime counter-terrorism concern that a terrorist weapon or operative could be concealed in a container and detonated upon arrival at a port, as feared in the post-9/11 environment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall 3:** Students frequently write that "PSI is a UN treaty." This is **factually incorrect** — PSI is a *political initiative*, not legally binding under a treaty. SUA 2005 is the underlying treaty; PSI is the operational framework. This distinction is a frequent 1-mark trap.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Twice Before Exam**

- **ISPS Code** = mandatory under SOLAS Ch. XI-2; in force **1 July 2004**; applies to passenger ships (≥12 pax) and cargo ships ≥ **500 GT**.
- **Three Security Levels:** 1 = Normal (Green), 2 = Heightened (Yellow), 3 = Exceptional (Red).
- **SUA Convention 1988** criminalized piracy-of-hijacking type acts; **2005 Protocols** added WMD offenses, expanded boarding rights, and offshore platform coverage.
- **PR24 Protocol:** Flag-state silence for 2 hours permits boarding by another state party under SUA 2005.
- **SSAS:** Silent alert; dual transmission; goes to flag state + CSO; no audible/visible alarm on board.
- **LRIT:** Polled every 6 hours for ships underway; complements AIS for global tracking.
- **AIS:** Mandatory under SOLAS Ch. V; transmits every 2–10 seconds depending on speed; vulnerability = spoofing/jamming.
- **CSR (Continuous Synopsis Record):** Onboard security history document; updated throughout the ship's life.
- **DoS (Declaration of Security):** Master ↔ PFSO written agreement during ship-port interface.
- **MDA:** Real-time understanding of maritime domain = foundation for all counter-terrorism decisions.
- **Four MDA Pillars:** Information sharing, intelligence/threat assessment, surveillance/detection, international cooperation.
- **BMP5:** Industry best practice for self-protection in piracy HRA; covers Gulf of Aden and Indian Ocean corridor.
- **PCASP / VPD:** Privately Contracted Armed Security Personnel / Vessel Protection Detachments — kinetic last-resort.
- **Citadel:** Central refuge area on a ship with independent communications and supplies for use during a pirate/terror boarding.
- **ReCAAP ISC:** Asia's first regional government-to-government agreement on piracy/armed robbery (2006, Tokyo).
- **Djibouti Code (2009)** & **Yaoundé Code (2013):** Capacity-building frameworks for African maritime security.
- **CTF-151:** NATO-led combined naval task force countering piracy in the Gulf of Aden and Somali Basin.
- **PSI (2003):** Political (not treaty) initiative for WMD interdiction; > 100 participants; legal basis = UNCLOS + SUA 2005.
- **CSI (2002):** US-led container pre-screening at foreign mega-ports; partnered with C-TPAT for supply-chain security.
- **Critical Maritime Infrastructure (CMI):** Ports, LNG terminals, undersea cables, offshore platforms — designated under national risk registers.
- **Cyber Risk:** IMO Resolution MSC.428(98) mandates cyber-risk management in Safety Management Systems (SMS) from **2021 onwards**.
- **Theoretical Underpinning:** UNCLOS provides the jurisdictional matrix; SOLAS Ch. XI-2 is the legal gateway; ISPS Code is the operational doctrine; SUA 2005 is the specific counter-terrorism instrument.
- **Common Examiner Traps:** (i) SUA ≠ Piracy Law, (ii) PSI ≠ Treaty, (iii) CSO ≠ SSO, (iv) SSAS ≠ AIS, (v) Level 2 ≠ Level 3, (vi) PFSP ≠ SSP — these distinctions are tested every cycle.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for ISPS Officers — "C²S³P":** **C**ompany Security Officer (CSO), **C**rew/Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO), **S**hip Security Officer (SSO), **S**SA, **S**SP, **P**FSP. The two Cs and three Ss and a P cover the entire officer cadre and plan inventory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maritime Security Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Maritime Security Threats** refer to any deliberate act, attempt, or conspiracy intended to cause harm, damage, or disruption to the safety of navigation, the security of ships, port facilities, offshore installations, and the maritime transportation system as a whole. These threats encompass criminal acts (piracy, armed robbery, smuggling), terrorist activities, cyber-attacks, and proliferation of weapons of mass destruction (WMD) that endanger vessels, crew, cargo, and maritime infrastructure.

According to the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, maritime security is defined under the **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code** as the combination of preventive measures and response actions intended to protect shipping and port facilities against intentional unlawful acts.

The official classifications adopted by IMO and the **United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC)** include:

| Term | Formal Definition | KTU Reference |
|------|------------------|---------------|
| **Piracy** | Acts committed within the High Seas or EEZ outside national jurisdiction under UNCLOS Article 101 | SOLAS Chapter XI-2 |
| **Armed Robbery** | Similar unlawful acts occurring **inside territorial waters** or port areas under national jurisdiction | National Maritime Law |
| **Maritime Terrorism** | Use or threat of violence against ships/platforms to intimidate a population or compel a government | ISPS Code Section A/2.1.7 |
| **Cyber Threat** | Malicious intrusion into shipboard networks, ECDIS, AIS, or OT systems | IMO Resolution MSC.428(98) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a vast, interconnected "floating highway system" — the oceans carry about **80% of global trade by volume** and approximately **70% by value**. Maritime Security Threats are the "highway robbers, hackers, and vandals" of this system. Just like a truck carrying gold on a remote highway faces threats from bandits, a supertanker carrying crude oil through the Gulf of Aden faces threats from pirates. The **International Maritime Security Framework** acts as the "global highway patrol" — a layered network of regulations, patrol forces, technology (AIS, LRIT, satellites), and international cooperation designed to deter, detect, and defeat these threats.

### 1.3 Why Maritime Security Matters — The Critical Numbers

> [!NOTE]
> - **$14 trillion** worth of goods transported by sea annually
> - Approximately **50,000+ merchant vessels** operate globally
> - Over **1,800 incidents** of piracy and armed robbery reported in recent years (IMB Piracy Reporting Centre)
> - The **Strait of Hormuz, Strait of Malacca, Gulf of Aden, and Gulf of Guinea** are recognized as the world's most strategically vulnerable maritime chokepoints
> - **95% of cyber incidents** in maritime go unreported or undetected (BIMCO/ABS survey)

The maritime domain is often referred to as the **"invisible supply chain"** because disruptions rarely affect the average consumer directly until cascading economic consequences emerge.

### 1.4 Classification Framework of Maritime Security Threats

Maritime Security Threats can be broadly classified into **six primary categories**:

1. **Traditional Criminal Threats** — Piracy, Armed Robbery, Smuggling
2. **Terrorist Threats** — Maritime Terrorism, WMD Proliferation, Use of Ships as Weapons
3. **Irregular Threats** — Human Trafficking, Smuggling of Migrants, Stowaways
4. **Cyber Threats** — Ransomware, GPS Spoofing, ECDIS Manipulation
5. **Geopolitical Threats** — Maritime Boundary Disputes, Blockades, Grey-Zone Warfare
6. **Environmental/Criminogenic Threats** — Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated (IUU) Fishing, MARPOL Violations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The ISPS Code specifically uses three **Security Levels** to define threat intensity:
> - **Security Level 1 (Normal):** Baseline — minimum protective measures apply
> - **Security Level 2 (Heightened):** Heightened risk — additional precautions
> - **Security Level 3 (Exceptional):** Imminent threat — maximum protective measures

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Threat-by-Threat Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Piracy and Armed Robbery Against Ships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Definition under UNCLOS Article 101:**
> Piracy consists of **any of the following acts**:
> (a) Any illegal acts of violence or detention, or any act of depredation, committed for private ends by the crew or passengers of a private ship or aircraft, and directed:
>     (i) Against another ship or aircraft, or against persons or property on board such ship or aircraft
>     (ii) Against a ship, aircraft, persons or property in a place outside the jurisdiction of any State
> (b) Any act of voluntary participation in the operation of a ship or aircraft with knowledge of facts making it a pirate ship or aircraft
> (c) Any act of inciting or intentionally facilitating an act described in (a) or (b)

**Critical Distinction (HIGH-YIELD for KTU Exams):**

| Feature | Piracy | Armed Robbery |
|---------|--------|---------------|
| **Location** | High Seas / EEZ (international waters) | Territorial Waters / Ports (national jurisdiction) |
| **Legal Jurisdiction** | Universal — any State may seize pirate vessel | Coastal State jurisdiction |
| **UNCLOS Article** | Article 101–107 | Not defined in UNCLOS; national law applies |
| **Reporting Body** | IMB PRC, IMO | National authorities, IMB PRC |
| **Conviction Authority** | Any flag State or seizing State | Coastal State only |

**Global Hotspot Analysis (IMB Annual Reports):**

- **Gulf of Aden / Somalia:** Historically the world's most dangerous region; reduced sharply after 2012 due to international naval patrols (Combined Task Force 151, EU NAVFOR Atalanta)
- **Gulf of Guinea (West Africa):** Currently the **most dangerous region**; accounts for over **80% of crew kidnappings globally**
- **Strait of Malacca / Singapore Strait:** High volume of petty armed robbery incidents due to traffic density
- **Sulu Sea / Celebes Sea:** Kidnapping-for-ransom threats
- **Caribbean / Venezuelan Coast:** Robbery and boarding incidents rising

#### 2.1.2 Maritime Terrorism

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Maritime terrorism is the use or threat of violence to advance political, religious, or ideological objectives through the maritime domain, targeting ships, ports, offshore platforms, passengers, crew, or coastal populations.

**Threat Vectors:**
- **Ship as a Weapon** — Using a vessel to ram critical infrastructure (e.g., USS Cole attack, 2000)
- **Ship as a Bomb** — Detonation aboard a vessel in port (e.g., MV Limburg, 2002)
- **Attacks on Ports** — Striking high-value port facilities
- **Hijacking for Propaganda** — Public spectacle (e.g., MV Sewol, Achille Lauro)
- **Offshore Platform Attacks** — Targeting oil/gas infrastructure

**Theoretical Framework — The "4C" Threat Model:**
1. **Capability** — Possession of weapons, explosives, marine craft
2. **Criminology** — Willingness to use violence
3. **Connection** — Links to terrorist networks
4. **Context** — Operating environment (political, economic)

#### 2.1.3 Cyber Threats in Maritime Domain

> [!WARNING]
> Since **1 January 2021**, IMO Resolution **MSC.428(98)** mandates that cyber risks must be addressed in Safety Management Systems (SMS) under the ISM Code. Failure to comply can lead to **port state control detentions**.

**Vulnerable Shipboard Systems:**

- **ECDIS** (Electronic Chart Display and Information System)
- **AIS** (Automatic Identification System) — can be spoofed
- **GPS / GNSS Receivers** — vulnerable to jamming and spoofing
- **Engine Control / OT Systems** — programmable logic controllers (PLCs)
- **VSAT / Communication Systems** — satellite uplinks
- **Cargo Management Systems** — manifest tampering
- **VDR** (Voyage Data Recorder) — data integrity attacks

**Cyber Threat Categories:**
1. **GPS Spoofing** — Transmitting false GPS signals to misroute vessels
2. **AIS Manipulation** — Fake vessel identities ("dark ships")
3. **Ransomware** — Encrypting shipboard systems for extortion (e.g., Maersk NotPetya incident, 2017)
4. **Man-in-the-Middle Attacks** — Interception of satellite communications
5. **Supply Chain Attacks** — Compromised ECDIS chart data

#### 2.1.4 Smuggling and Illicit Trade

**Common Cargo Categories:**
- **Drugs** (cocaine, heroin, methamphetamine) — narco-submarines increasingly sophisticated
- **Weapons** — small arms, MANPADS
- **Cigarettes & Counterfeit Goods** — high profit, low penalty
- **Wildlife** — illegal ivory, pangolin scales
- **People** — human trafficking and smuggling of migrants

**Estimating Smuggling Profit (KTU-Relevant):**
The economic scale of maritime smuggling can be modeled as:

$$
P_{\text{illicit}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( V_i \cdot Q_i \right) - C_{\text{operation}}
$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{illicit}}$ = Net profit from illicit operation
- $V_i$ = Black-market value per unit of contraband type $i$
- $Q_i$ = Quantity of contraband type $i$ successfully smuggled
- $C_{\text{operation}}$ = Total cost of operation (vessel, bribes, fuel, risk premium)
- $n$ = Number of contraband categories

#### 2.1.5 Human Trafficking and Smuggling of Migrants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Trafficking in Persons (TIP):** Involves **coercion, fraud, or force**; victim is considered a victim of a crime
> - **Smuggling of Migrants:** Migrants **consent** to be smuggled; considered an administrative/civil offence at destination

The **Protocol against the Smuggling of Migrants by Land, Sea and Air** (UN Palermo Protocol, 2000) governs maritime migration responses.

#### 2.1.6 Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated (IUU) Fishing

IUU fishing is a **criminogenic threat** that depletes fish stocks, undermines legal fisheries, and is often linked to transnational organized crime including **forced labour at sea** (a recognized form of modern slavery).

### 2.2 International Legal & Regulatory Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Maritime Security Regime Pyramid:**
>
> **Tier 1 — Universal Instruments:**
> - **UNCLOS (1982)** — Articles 100–107 define piracy and universal jurisdiction
> - **SOLAS Convention (1974)** — Chapter XI-2 & ISPS Code
> - **SUA Convention (1988) and SUA Protocols (2005)** — Suppression of Unlawful Acts against Maritime Navigation
> - **MARPOL Convention** — environmental threats
> - **STCW Convention** — crew competence including security awareness
>
> **Tier 2 — Specialized Frameworks:**
> - **ISPS Code** — Ship and Port Facility Security
> - **IMO Resolution MSC.428(98)** — Cyber risk management in SMS
> - **Ballast Water Management Convention**
> - **ILO Maritime Labour Convention (MLC 2006)**
>
> **Tier 3 — Regional Cooperative Mechanisms:**
> - **ReCAAP ISC** — Regional Cooperation Agreement on Combating Piracy and Armed Robbery against Ships in Asia
> - **Djibouti Code of Conduct** — Western Indian Ocean / Gulf of Aden
> - **Yaoundé Code of Conduct** — Gulf of Guinea
> - **Naval Combined Task Forces** — CTF 151, EU NAVFOR, Combined Maritime Forces (CMF)

### 2.3 ISPS Code — Three Security Levels Deep Dive

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The ISPS Code defines **three Security Levels** which are the basis of all shipboard security planning:

| Security Level | Threat Description | Required Measures |
|----------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Level 1 — Normal** | Baseline, no known heightened threat | Access control, monitoring, routine drills |
| **Level 2 — Heightened** | Heightened risk of security incident | Increased surveillance, restricted access, additional personnel |
| **Level 3 — Exceptional** | Imminent or actual security incident | Full lockdown, suspension of operations, armed escort if required |

**Ship Security Plan (SSP)** must contain:
1. Identification of **Ship Security Officer (SSO)**
2. Procedures for **continuous monitoring** of restricted areas
3. **Communication protocols** with CSO and flag State
4. **Drills and exercises** schedule (minimum quarterly)
5. **Declaration of Security (DoS)** procedures

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Principle | Application |
|---------|--------------------|-----------|
| Piracy Jurisdiction Rule | UNCLOS Art. 101: Universal jurisdiction in High Seas | Any state may seize pirate vessel |
| Hotspot Risk Index (qualitative) | $R = (T \times V \times C) / M$ | Risk = Threat $\times$ Vulnerability $\times$ Consequence / Mitigation |
| Response Time Model | $t_{\text{response}} = t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{decide}} + t_{\text{deploy}}$ | Naval patrol planning |
| Probability of Successful Attack | $P_{\text{attack}} = 1 - (1 - p_i)^n$ | $p_i$ = single attempt prob., $n$ = attempts |
| ISPS Security Levels | L1 < L2 < L3 in measure intensity | Escalation framework |
| Cyber Risk Rating | $R_{\text{cyber}} = P_{\text{event}} \times I_{\text{impact}}$ | Per IMO MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 |
| Stowaway Detection Rate | $\eta = N_{\text{detected}} / N_{\text{total}}$ | Effective screening metric |
| Piracy Economic Cost | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{ransom}} + C_{\text{insurance}} + C_{\text{detour}} + C_{\text{security}}$ | Industry-wide annual losses |
| Armed Robbery vs Piracy Boundary | Inside 12 nm = Armed Robbery; Outside = Piracy | UNCLOS territorial limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Watch:** Students often confuse **territorial sea (12 nm)** with **contiguous zone (24 nm)**. The boundary between **armed robbery and piracy** is the **outer limit of the territorial sea (12 nm)**, NOT the contiguous zone.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Piracy Risk Index for a Voyage Route

**Problem Statement:** A vessel must transit from Port A to Port B through a high-risk region. Compute the **quantitative piracy risk index** over the entire route.

**Given:**
- Number of transits per year: $N = 12$
- Length of voyage through high-risk zone: $L = 800$ nautical miles
- Historical attack rate per nautical mile: $\lambda = 0.0005$ attacks/nm
- Vessel defensive capability factor: $k = 0.7$ (0 = no defense, 1 = full defense)

**Step 1 — Compute the raw (undefended) probability of attack for one transit**

$$
P_{\text{raw}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda L}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

$$
P_{\text{raw}} = 1 - e^{-(0.0005)(800)}
$$

$$
P_{\text{raw}} = 1 - e^{-0.4}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential term**

$$
e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703
$$

$$
P_{\text{raw}} = 1 - 0.6703 = 0.3297
$$

**Step 4 — Adjust for vessel defensive capability**

The effective probability after defense is:

$$
P_{\text{effective}} = P_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - k)
$$

$$
P_{\text{effective}} = 0.3297 \times (1 - 0.7) = 0.3297 \times 0.3 = 0.0989
$$

**Step 5 — Compute annual risk (12 transits)**

$$
P_{\text{annual}} = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{effective}})^N
$$

$$
P_{\text{annual}} = 1 - (1 - 0.0989)^{12}
$$

$$
P_{\text{annual}} = 1 - (0.9011)^{12}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the power term**

$$
(0.9011)^{12} \approx 0.2911
$$

$$
P_{\text{annual}} = 1 - 0.2911 = 0.7089
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation**

$$
P_{\text{annual}} \approx 70.9\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Even with strong onboard defenses, the cumulative annual risk of attack is approximately **70.9%**. This justifies the use of **Best Management Practices (BMP)** combined with **convoy escort** by naval forces, which is the basis for the current industry standard.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cost-Benefit Analysis of Armed Guards

**Problem:** A shipping company must decide whether to hire **private maritime security companies (PMSC)** with armed guards for transits through the Gulf of Guinea.

**Given data per transit:**

- Voyage revenue at risk: $V = \$ 4,000,000$
- Probability of attack without guards: $P_{\text{no guard}} = 0.20$
- Probability of attack with guards: $P_{\text{guard}} = 0.03$
- Cost of PMSC service per transit: $C_{\text{PMSC}} = \$ 250,000$
- Insurance premium reduction with guards: $S_{\text{insurance}} = \$ 90,000$

**Step 1 — Expected loss without guards**

$$
E[L_{\text{no guard}}] = V \times P_{\text{no guard}}
$$

$$
E[L_{\text{no guard}}] = 4{,}000{,}000 \times 0.20 = \$ 800{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Expected loss with guards (gross)**

$$
E[L_{\text{guard, gross}}] = V \times P_{\text{guard}}
$$

$$
E[L_{\text{guard, gross}}] = 4{,}000{,}000 \times 0.03 = \$ 120{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Net cost with guards**

$$
C_{\text{net, guard}} = C_{\text{PMSC}} + E[L_{\text{guard, gross}}] - S_{\text{insurance}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{net, guard}} = 250{,}000 + 120{,}000 - 90{,}000 = \$ 280{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Comparative decision**

$$
\Delta C = E[L_{\text{no guard}}] - C_{\text{net, guard}}
$$

$$
\Delta C = 800{,}000 - 280{,}000 = \$ 520{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

$$
\Delta C > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Hire armed guards.}
$$

The decision criterion is satisfied; the shipping company should engage PMSC services, saving approximately **\$ 520,000 per transit** on an expected-value basis.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Threat-Vulnerability-Consequence (TVC)

$$
R_{\text{asset}} = T \times V \times C
$$

| Threat ($T$) | Vulnerability ($V$) | Consequence ($C$) | Risk ($R$) |
|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------|
| Somali Piracy (2010) | High (slow cargo) | Very High (cargo + crew) | Extreme |
| GPS Spoofing (Persian Gulf) | High (civilian GPS) | Medium (navigation error) | High |
| Ransomware on Engine | Medium (patch lag) | Very High (loss of propulsion) | High |
| Stowaway (Container) | Medium (port screening) | Medium (humanitarian, legal) | Medium |
| Smuggling (Drug Submarine) | Low (open waters) | Medium (legal, image) | Medium |
| Maritime Terrorism (Port) | Low (ISPS) | Catastrophic | High |
| Cyber Spoofing (AIS) | High (unencrypted) | Medium (collision, sanctions) | Medium-High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Analytical Insight:** Notice that **low probability but catastrophic consequence** events (like terrorism) still rank as **high-risk**. Risk matrices must always consider both axes — this is a frequent KTU conceptual trap.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Security Level Determination

A vessel is operating in the Gulf of Aden. The flag State has issued an advisory stating that there is credible intelligence of an imminent pirate attack within 72 hours.

**Determination of Security Level under ISPS Code:**

$$
\text{SL} = \text{Credible imminent threat} \Rightarrow \text{Security Level 3 (Exceptional)}
$$

**Required Actions:**

1. Initiate **SSAS (Ship Security Alert System)** standby
2. Implement **full restricted area lockdown**
3. Suspend non-essential cargo operations
4. Activate **citadel refuge** if so equipped
5. Maintain continuous communication with **naval forces (Combined Task Force 151, EU NAVFOR)**
6. Comply with **BMP5 (Best Management Practices) West Africa / Indian Ocean**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing **Security Level 2 (Heightened)** with **Security Level 3 (Exceptional)**. Remember: Level 3 requires **imminent or actual attack**, not merely elevated risk. Level 2 is for **heightened** risk only.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
Define **piracy** under UNCLOS Article 101. How is it legally distinguished from **armed robbery against ships**?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Under **UNCLOS Article 101**, piracy consists of any illegal acts of violence, detention, or depredation committed for **private ends** by the crew or passengers of a private ship against another ship, persons, or property on the high seas or outside the jurisdiction of any State.

**Legal Distinction:**

| Criterion | Piracy | Armed Robbery |
|-----------|--------|---------------|
| Location | High seas / outside national jurisdiction | Inside territorial waters (within 12 nm) |
| Jurisdiction | Universal — any State can act | Only the coastal State has jurisdiction |

Therefore, the geographic location — specifically whether the act occurs **beyond or within the 12 nm territorial sea limit** — is the legal dividing line. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
What are the **three Security Levels** prescribed by the ISPS Code? State one operational measure required at each level.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **ISPS Code** prescribes three security levels:

1. **Security Level 1 — Normal:** Minimum protective measures such as routine access control and surveillance of restricted areas.
2. **Security Level 2 — Heightened:** Additional precautions including enhanced monitoring, restricted access, and deployment of additional security personnel.
3. **Security Level 3 — Exceptional:** Maximum protective measures including full lockdown, suspension of non-essential operations, and possible armed escort or citadel activation.

The intensity of measures increases progressively from Level 1 to Level 3. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically divided as **Part (a) for 7 marks** and **Part (b) for 7 marks**. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Equivalent Model Question]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **various types of maritime security threats** faced by the global shipping industry. Provide specific examples for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a suitable diagram, explain the **three Security Levels of the ISPS Code**. Discuss the role of the **Ship Security Officer (SSO)** and **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** in maintaining maritime security. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Question A - Model Solution

**(a) Types of Maritime Security Threats (7 Marks):**

**1. Piracy and Armed Robbery (2 marks)**
Piracy occurs on the high seas; armed robbery occurs within territorial waters. Examples: Somali piracy in the Gulf of Aden (peaked 2011), Gulf of Guinea piracy, Strait of Malacca armed robbery. Pirates typically use small fast craft (skiffs), RPGs, and ladders to board vessels.

**2. Maritime Terrorism (2 marks)**
Use of ships as weapons or bombs against ports, offshore platforms, or coastal targets. Examples: USS Cole attack (2000) in Aden, MV Limburg attack (2002). Threat vectors include suicide attack vessels, hijacking for propaganda, and WMD transport.

**3. Cyber Threats (1.5 marks)**
Modern vessels are highly digitized. Threats include GPS spoofing (Persian Gulf incidents), AIS manipulation creating "ghost ships," ransomware attacks on engine systems (e.g., Maersk/NotPetya 2017), and ECDIS chart tampering. **IMO Resolution MSC.428(98)** mandates cyber risk management in Safety Management Systems since 1 January 2021.

**4. Smuggling and Illicit Trade (1 mark)**
Maritime routes are exploited for drug trafficking (cocaine via narco-submarines), weapons smuggling, and contraband. Increasingly sophisticated methods include semi-submersible vessels and hidden compartments.

**5. Irregular Migration (0.5 marks)**
Stowaways on merchant vessels, human trafficking via small craft, and migrant smuggling across the Mediterranean and Bay of Bengal.

**(b) ISPS Code Security Levels and SSO/SSP Role (7 Marks):**

**Three Security Levels (3 marks):**

The ISPS Code establishes three progressive security levels:
- **Level 1 (Normal):** Baseline security with routine access control, perimeter monitoring, and standard SSP implementation.
- **Level 2 (Heightened):** When there is a heightened risk of security incident; requires enhanced monitoring, restricted access to certain areas, and increased security personnel.
- **Level 3 (Exceptional):** When an attack is imminent or actually occurring; requires maximum protection including full lockdown, possible suspension of operations, and armed escort or citadel refuge.

**SSO Role (2 marks):**

The **Ship Security Officer (SSO)** is responsible for:
1. Implementing and maintaining the Ship Security Plan (SSP)
2. Conducting regular security inspections and audits
3. Coordinating with the Company Security Officer (CSO) and Port Facility Security Officers (PFSO)
4. Conducting security drills (minimum quarterly)
5. Reporting security incidents
6. Advising the Master on security matters

**SSP Role (2 marks):**

The **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** is the document that defines:
1. Security policies, organizational structure, and duties
2. Access control procedures and restricted area identification
3. Communication protocols with CSO, PFSO, and flag State
4. Procedures for raising the security level
5. Declaration of Security (DoS) procedures with port facilities
6. Drill and exercise schedule
7. SSAS (Ship Security Alert System) procedures

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often lose **2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Failing to distinguish between **Piracy and Armed Robbery** with the correct legal basis
> 2. Not mentioning **IMO MSC.428(98)** for cyber threats (post-2021 syllabus critical)
> 3. Confusing **SSO** with **CSO** — SSO is ship-based; CSO is company-based
> 4. Omitting the **Declaration of Security (DoS)** requirement between ship and port facility
> 5. Not mentioning the **12 nm territorial sea boundary** in piracy/armed robbery distinction

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Equivalent Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **International Legal Framework** for combating maritime security threats. Discuss the role of UNCLOS, SOLAS Chapter XI-2, ISPS Code, and SUA Convention. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze **cyber threats in the maritime sector**. List at least five vulnerable shipboard systems and recommend a layered cyber-defense strategy in compliance with IMO Resolution MSC.428(98). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Question B - Model Solution

**(a) International Legal Framework (7 Marks):**

**1. UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea), 1982 (2 marks)**
- Articles **100–107** define piracy and grant **universal jurisdiction** — any State may seize pirate ships on the high seas.
- Article 98 establishes the duty to render assistance at sea.
- Article 99 prohibits the transport of slaves.

**2. SOLAS Convention (Safety of Life at Sea), 1974 — Chapter XI-2 (2 marks)**
- Introduced the special measures to enhance maritime security post-9/11.
- Established the **Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code** as mandatory.
- Mandates Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) and Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR).

**3. ISPS Code (International Ship and Port Facility Security Code) (1.5 marks)**
- Provides operational framework for ship and port security.
- Defines three Security Levels (1, 2, 3).
- Mandates Ship Security Plan (SSP), Ship Security Officer (SSO), Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP), and Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO).

**4. SUA Convention (Convention for the Suppression of Unlawful Acts against Maritime Navigation), 1988 + 2005 Protocol (1.5 marks)**
- Establishes criminal offences for unlawful acts against ships.
- 2005 Protocol extends scope to include terrorism, WMD transport, and persons aboard.
- Establishes **universal jurisdiction** for prosecution.

**(b) Cyber Threats Analysis and Defense Strategy (7 Marks):**

**Vulnerable Shipboard Systems (3.5 marks):**

1. **GPS / GNSS Receivers** — Vulnerable to spoofing and jamming; can misroute vessels into hostile waters.
2. **AIS (Automatic Identification System)** — Can be spoofed to create "ghost ships" or hide vessel identities.
3. **ECDIS** — Chart data tampering can cause groundings; chart updates may carry malware.
4. **Engine Control Systems (OT/ICS)** — Ransomware or PLC manipulation can disable propulsion.
5. **VSAT/Satellite Communications** — Man-in-the-middle attacks can intercept sensitive data.
6. **Cargo Management Systems** — Manifest tampering, smuggling facilitation.
7. **VDR (Voyage Data Recorder)** — Data integrity attacks undermining accident investigation.

**Layered Cyber-Defense Strategy (3.5 marks):**

**Layer 1 — Governance and Risk Management:**
- Integrate cyber risk management into SMS per **IMO MSC.428(98)** and **MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3** guidelines.
- Appoint a designated cyber security officer.
- Conduct annual risk assessments.

**Layer 2 — Technical Controls:**
- Network segmentation between OT and IT networks.
- Multi-factor authentication for critical systems.
- Regular patching and vulnerability scans.
- Endpoint detection and response (EDR).

**Layer 3 — Operational Controls:**
- Crew cyber awareness training (phishing, social engineering).
- Strong password policies and USB device controls.
- Backup and disaster recovery procedures.

**Layer 4 — Monitoring and Response:**
- 24/7 SOC (Security Operations Center) monitoring.
- Incident response plan with defined escalation.
- Coordination with coast guard and cyber emergency response teams.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not list more than 3 systems** if the question asks "at least 5" — exceeding requirements wastes time.
> - **Always cite IMO MSC.428(98)** for any cyber-related question in 2024 Scheme.
> - **Distinguish OT (Operational Technology) from IT (Information Technology)** — many students conflate the two.
> - Mention **BIMCO Cyber Security Guidelines** and **NIST Framework** as recognized industry standards.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maritime Security Threats**

**Core Definitions:**
- Piracy = High Seas / Universal Jurisdiction (UNCLOS Art. 101)
- Armed Robbery = Territorial Waters / National Jurisdiction (within 12 nm)
- Maritime Terrorism = Politically / ideologically motivated unlawful acts
- Cyber Threats = Risks to digital shipboard systems

**Key Frameworks (Memorize Acronyms):**
- **UNCLOS** — Piracy jurisdiction
- **SOLAS XI-2** — Special measures, ISPS Code
- **ISPS Code** — Ship + Port Facility Security
- **SUA Convention + 2005 Protocol** — Suppression of Unlawful Acts
- **MSC.428(98)** — Cyber risk in SMS (effective 1 Jan 2021)
- **BMP5** — Best Management Practices against piracy

**ISPS Security Levels:**
- L1 (Normal) → L2 (Heightened) → L3 (Exceptional)
- Progression based on credible threat intelligence

**Hotspot Regions (Current):**
- **Gulf of Guinea** — Kidnapping for ransom (currently #1)
- **Strait of Hormuz** — Geopolitical / tanker seizures
- **South China Sea** — Geopolitical tensions
- **Strait of Malacca / Singapore Strait** — Petty armed robbery
- **Gulf of Aden** — Reduced since 2012 due to naval patrols

**Cyber Vulnerable Systems (5+):**
GPS, AIS, ECDIS, Engine OT, VSAT, Cargo, VDR

**Personnel Roles:**
- **SSO** = Ship Security Officer (ship-based)
- **CSO** = Company Security Officer (company-based)
- **PFSO** = Port Facility Security Officer (port-based)

**Mandatory Documents (must be on board):**
1. Ship Security Plan (SSP)
2. Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)
3. Declaration of Security (DoS)
4. International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)

**Two Universal Jurisdictions in Maritime Law:**
1. Piracy under UNCLOS
2. SUA Convention offences

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** When answering any 14-mark question on Maritime Security Threats, structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition** (1–2 lines with statutory reference)
> 2. **Classification** (bullet points with examples)
> 3. **Legal Framework** (cite specific article/section)
> 4. **Operational Implementation** (roles, plans, equipment)
> 5. **Diagram** (mandatory for full marks on design questions)
> 6. **Conclusion / Current Scenario** (e.g., Gulf of Guinea hotspots, cyber trends)
>
> A well-structured 14-mark answer with **one diagram** and **2–3 statutory references** typically scores **12–14 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piracy, Armed Robbery, and Terrorism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piracy, Armed Robbery, and Terrorism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Maritime Security Threats

Maritime security threats are unlawful acts directed against ships, ports, offshore installations, and seafarers. Among the gravest threats facing global shipping today are **piracy**, **armed robbery**, and **maritime terrorism**. These crimes disrupt trade, endanger lives, threaten energy supplies, and undermine the stability of coastal states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between **piracy** (international crime on the high seas) and **armed robbery** (similar act within territorial waters), as this distinction has direct legal implications under **UNCLOS** and the **SUA Convention**.

---

## 1.2 Piracy – Formal Definition (UNCLOS Article 101)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Piracy (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, Article 101):**
> "Piracy consists of any of the following acts:
> (a) Any illegal acts of violence or detention, or any act of depredation, committed for **private ends** by the crew or passengers of a private ship or aircraft, and directed:
>     (i) against another ship or aircraft, or
>     (ii) against persons or property on board such ship or aircraft;
> (b) Any act of voluntary participation in the operation of a ship or aircraft with knowledge of facts making it a pirate ship or aircraft;
> (c) Any act of **inciting** or of **intentionally facilitating** an act described in subparagraph (a) or (b)."

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine two taxis speeding on a deserted highway (the **high seas**, beyond any country's jurisdiction). One taxi is hijacked by criminals who use it to rob the other taxi. Because the highway belongs to no single nation, every country in the world has a legal right to chase and arrest the criminals. This is exactly what makes **piracy a universal-jurisdiction crime** — any state can capture pirate ships, arrest the crew, and prosecute them under its own laws.

### Key Legal Thresholds

- **Location:** Must occur on the **high seas** or **outside the jurisdiction** of any State (i.e., beyond the 12-nautical-mile territorial sea).
- **Motive:** Acts committed for **private ends** (NOT political/ideological — that would be terrorism).
- **Two-Vessel Rule:** There must be **two ships involved** — a pirate ship attacking another ship.
- **Universal Jurisdiction:** Any State may seize a pirate ship, arrest the persons, and seize the property on board (**UNCLOS Article 105**).

---

## 1.3 Armed Robbery at Sea – Formal Definition (SUA Convention, 1988)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Armed Robbery Against Ships (IMO Resolution A.1025(26)):**
> "Armed robbery against ships means any **unlawful act** of violence or detention, or any act of depredation, or threat thereof, **other than a piracy act**, directed against a ship or against persons or property on board such a ship, **within waters falling under the jurisdiction of a State**."

### Conceptual Analogy

If piracy is a crime on an open highway, then **armed robbery is the same type of crime committed in a city street** — the crime is identical in nature, but jurisdiction belongs to the **local coastal State**, not to the world at large. The local police (coast guard) have the authority to investigate and prosecute.

### Key Legal Thresholds

- **Location:** Inside a State's **territorial sea** or **internal waters** (within 12 nautical miles from baselines).
- **Jurisdiction:** Lies with the **coastal State** — foreign navies generally cannot intervene without permission.
- **Same Acts:** Includes violence, detention, depredation, and threats — very similar in nature to piracy.

---

## 1.4 Maritime Terrorism – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition – Maritime Terrorism (per IMO Maritime Safety Committee, MSC/Circ.1113):**
> "Maritime terrorism consists of acts of terrorism that involve **ships or ports**, or that target **maritime infrastructure**, passengers, crew, or cargo, with the intent to intimidate a population, compel a government or international organization to act or refrain from acting, or further political, religious, or ideological goals."

### Conceptual Analogy
