<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define the ISPS Code. State any two obligations it places on a Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO).**

**Model Answer:**
The International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code is a comprehensive, mandatory international framework adopted by the IMO in 2002 under SOLAS Chapter XI-2, effective 1 July 2004, to enhance maritime security against terrorism.

**PFSO obligations:**
1. Conducting the **Port Facility Security Assessment (PFSA)** and preparing the **Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP)**.
2. Implementing the PFSP and ensuring **MARSEC level changes** are communicated to all relevant vessels.
3. Coordinating with the SSO and CSO during ship-port interface operations including the **Declaration of Security (DoS)**.

*(Any two — 1.5 marks each)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between maritime piracy and maritime terrorism. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Piracy | Maritime Terrorism |
|---|---|---|
| **Motive** | Economic (ransom, robbery) | Ideological / political / religious |
| **Legal Regime** | UNCLOS Art. 100–107; universal jurisdiction | SUA Convention 1988/2005; UNSCR framework |
| **Typical Acts** | Boarding, kidnapping, theft | Mass-casualty attacks, vessel-as-weapon, sabotage |
| **Example** | Somali piracy in Gulf of Aden (2010–2012) | USS *Cole* bombing, Yemen (2000) |

*(3 marks: 1.5 for table, 1.5 for examples)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **ISPS Code framework** in detail. Describe the **three MARSEC levels** and the corresponding escalation actions required at each level. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **Ship Security Assessment (SSA)** methodology. With the help of a numerical example, demonstrate how risk is computed and acceptable residual risk is determined. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The **ISPS Code** (International Ship and Port Facility Security Code) was adopted by the IMO in December 2002 as a binding instrument under **SOLAS Chapter XI-2**, and came into force on **1 July 2004**. It applies to:
- Passenger ships and **cargo ships ≥ 500 GT** on international voyages
- Mobile Offshore Drilling Units (MODUs)
- Port facilities serving such ships

The Code has two parts:
- **Part A** — Mandatory, prescriptive requirements
- **Part B** — Guidance and recommendations

**Three MARSEC Levels:**

1. **MARSEC Level 1 — Normal baseline:**
   SSP and PFSP are in routine execution. Standard access control, identification checks, monitoring of restricted areas, and routine watch on the bridge and gangway.

2. **MARSEC Level 2 — Heightened threat:**
   Increased frequency of patrols, additional screening of unaccompanied baggage, intensified checks of persons wishing to embark, restricted access to sensitive areas (engine room, bridge), and coordination with adjacent port facilities.

3. **MARSEC Level 3 — Imminent attack:**
   All Level 2 measures plus full lockdown of the vessel, suspension of cargo and passenger operations, sealing of ventilation and watertight openings, deployment of armed guards (if flag state authorizes), and immediate reporting to flag and coastal states. Level 3 can only be set by the **contracting government**, not the master.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

The **Ship Security Assessment (SSA)** is a structured, documented risk analysis mandated by ISPS Code Part A §5. It follows five sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Threat Identification:**
Enumerate credible threats $T = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$ such as hijack-as-weapon, insider attack, or container-borne WMD.

**Stage 2 — Vulnerability Assessment:**
Evaluate asset exposure $V = \frac{\text{Exposure}}{\text{Countermeasure Strength}}$.

**Stage 3 — Consequence Analysis:**
Estimate impact $C$ across lives lost, economic damage, environmental and geopolitical cost.

**Stage 4 — Risk Scoring:**
$$
R = T \times V \times C
$$

**Stage 5 — Mitigation Selection:**
Choose countermeasures such that residual risk $R_{\text{residual}} \leq R_{\text{acceptable}}$.

**Numerical Example:**

A bulk carrier approaching a high-risk port has $T = 3$, $V = 6$, $C = 5$.

$$
R_{\text{initial}} = 3 \times 6 \times 5 = 90
$$

Flag-state acceptable residual risk $R_{\text{acceptable}} = 50$. After deploying enhanced CCTV (reduces $V$ by 30%) and biometric crew vetting (reduces $V$ by 50%):

$$
V_{\text{new}} = 6 \times (1 - 0.80) = 1.2
$$

$$
R_{\text{new}} = 3 \times 1.2 \times 5 = 18
$$

Since $18 \leq 50$, the mitigation package is **validated**.

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Discuss the **SUA Convention (1988)** and its **2005 Protocols**. How do these instruments strengthen counter-terrorism enforcement in maritime zones? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)**, the **Long Range Identification and Tracking (LRIT)** system, and the **Automatic Identification System (AIS)**. Construct a comparative table highlighting their purpose, range, and data recipient. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

The **Convention for the Suppression of Unlawful Acts Against the Safety of Maritime Navigation (SUA Convention, 1988)** was adopted by the IMO at Rome on 10 March 1988 in direct response to the **1985 Achille Lauro hijacking**. It criminalized acts such as:
- Seizing control of a ship by force or threats
- Committing violence on board
- Placing devices aboard likely to destroy the ship
- Endangering safe navigation

It established **universal jurisdiction** — any state party could prosecute offenders.

The **2005 Protocols** (adopted 14 October 2005; entered into force 28 July 2010) modernized the framework to address:
- **Post-9/11 threats** including vessel-as-weapon attacks
- Use of ships for **proliferation of WMDs**
- Biologic, chemical, and nuclear terrorism from the sea
- Strengthened **boarding rights** for vessels suspected of involvement in terrorism (subject to safeguards)
- New criminal offenses such as transporting terrorists, using ships to smuggle WMD materials

**Counter-terrorism impact:**
1. Closes legal loopholes by defining modern threats explicitly.
2. Allows **warship or other government ship** to board suspect vessels with flag-state consent or in cases of suspected WMD transport.
3. Provides legal framework for **naval interdiction operations** like Operation Enduring Freedom – Phantom Phoenix.
4. Aligns maritime law with **UNSCR 1540** and **Proliferation Security Initiative (PSI)**.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

| Feature | **SSAS** | **LRIT** | **AIS** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Covert emergency alert when ship under attack | Continuous long-range tracking for security | Collision avoidance & traffic monitoring |
| **Activation** | Manual — by master/SSO only | Automatic periodic transmission | Automatic continuous |
| **Range** | Global (satellite-based) | Global (satellite-based) | **VHF range** — typically 20–40 nautical miles |
| **Data Recipient** | Flag State Administration + CSO (covertly) | Flag State + contracting governments | All nearby ships + coastal stations (public) |
| **Information Content** | Single alert + ship ID + position | Ship ID, position, course, speed, timestamp | Ship ID, position, course, speed, destination |
| **Visibility to Attackers** | **None** (covert) | Limited (encrypted, sanitized for non-flag states) | **Fully public** (a vulnerability in high-risk zones) |
| **Mandatory Since** | 1 July 2004 (ISPS) | 1 January 2009 | Already pre-existing; IMO mandated carriage |

**Engineering implications:**
- **SSAS** is the *active* counter-terrorism tool — it triggers response.
- **LRIT** is the *passive* monitoring tool — it enables anticipation.
- **AIS** is **dual-use** — beneficial for safety but can be exploited by attackers to *target* ships (hence vessels may switch off AIS in danger zones, an act that itself is suspicious).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — common pitfalls in 14-mark answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ISPS Parts A and B** — Part A is **mandatory**, Part B is **guidance**. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Writing "master sets MARSEC Level 3"** — Wrong. Only the **contracting government** sets Level 3. The master **complies**.
> 3. **Stating piracy = terrorism** — Examiner deducts at least 2 marks. Differentiate via *motive*.
> 4. **Forgetting SOLAS Chapter XI-2** — The ISPS Code is *enforceable* only because SOLAS Chapter XI-2 makes it mandatory. Citing only "ISPS Code" is incomplete.
> 5. **Listing SSAS/LRIT/AIS without distinguishing purpose** — These three systems are *complementary*, not alternatives. Examiners want to see a comparison.
> 6. **Skipping numerical example** in risk-assessment questions — Even a basic $R = T \times V \times C$ calculation earns the **application mark**.
> 7. **Ignoring Indian/national context** — Mentioning **MTSA 2002**, **Indian MTOI**, or **DG Shipping advisories** scores bonus marks if relevant.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Counter-Terrorism Measures:**
>
> ☐ ISPS Code = binding framework under **SOLAS Chapter XI-2**, effective **1 July 2004**, Part A mandatory, Part B guidance.
>
> ☐ Applies to ships **≥ 500 GT** on international voyages, MODUs, and serving port facilities.
>
> ☐ Three roles: **CSO (company), SSO (ship), PFSO (port)**; they coordinate via **Declaration of Security (DoS)**.
>
> ☐ Three **MARSEC levels** — only the contracting government can set Level 3.
>
> ☐ **SUA Convention 1988 + 2005 Protocols** = legal backbone for prosecution and naval boarding.
>
> ☐ **SSAS** = covert alert, recipient is flag state + CSO.
>
> ☐ **LRIT** = global satellite tracking, 6-hour reporting.
>
> ☐ **AIS** = local VHF tracking, public — useful but vulnerable.
>
> ☐ **SSP/PFSP** valid for **5 years**; drills at least every **3 months**.
>
> ☐ Risk formula: $R = T \times V \times C$; acceptable residual risk is flag-state defined.
>
> ☐ Piracy = economic motive (UNCLOS); Terrorism = ideological motive (SUA).
>
> ☐ UNSCR **1373** (criminalize & share intel), **1540** (non-state WMD), **1566** (definitions).
>
> ☐ **Container Security Initiative (CSI)** + **WCO SAFE Framework** = cargo counter-terrorism.
>
> ☐ Cyber-terrorism addressed in SSP per **IMO Resolution MSC.428(98)**.
>
> ☐ Citadel, armed guards, NII scanners, biometric vetting = physical countermeasure tier.
>
> ☐ Indian context: MTOI, DG Shipping advisories, ReCAAP ISC membership.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection and Prevention of Terrorist Acts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Detection and Prevention of Terrorist Acts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maritime Terrorism** is defined under the **ISPS Code (International Ship and Port Facility Security Code)**, adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** through **SOLAS Chapter XI-2**, as *any act intended to cause death or serious bodily injury to civilians, or to intimidate a population, or to compel a government or an international organization to do or to abstain from doing any act, when such act is carried out onboard a vessel, at a port facility, or against maritime infrastructure.*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **OESFT727 – Maritime Safety**, the term **"Detection and Prevention of Terrorist Acts"** refers to the layered, multi-agency, multi-tiered set of operational, technical, legal, and human-intelligence measures adopted at the **vessel–port–interface** to:

1. **Detect** — Identify, classify, and confirm hostile intent before kinetic action occurs.
2. **Deny** — Harden vessels, ports, cargo, and crew against exploitation.
3. **Deter** — Project credible counter-measures that dissuade attackers.
4. **Defend** — Neutralize an in-progress or imminent terrorist act.
5. **Document** — Preserve evidentiary chain for prosecution under the **SUA Convention 1988 (as amended by the 2005 Protocol)** and domestic anti-terror statutes.

The statutory anchor for this topic in KTU Module 4 is the **three-pillar ISPS framework**: **(a) the Ship Security Plan (SSP)**, **(b) the Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP)**, and **(c) Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Floating Airport" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a modern container ship as a **"floating airport"** carrying 10,000–24,000 TEU containers, often longer than four football fields, sailing through congested chokepoints like the **Strait of Hormuz, Bab-el-Mandeb, Strait of Malacca, and the Suez Canal**. A terrorist attempting to breach this asset is analogous to a person trying to smuggle contraband into a high-security airport terminal — but instead of one scanner at one entrance, the ship has **multiple sea-side approaches, crew transfers, supply boats, underwater hull, deck cargo, and containers that may not be opened for months**.

**Why detection is uniquely hard at sea:**
- **Container opacity**: Only **~2%** of global containerized cargo is physically inspected.
- **Asymmetric target set**: Soft civilian targets (ferries, cruise liners) vs. hardened military assets.
- **Jurisdictional patchwork**: Vessel may fly the **Flag State** of Liberia, be crewed by Filipinos, owned by a German company, chartered by a Swiss firm, calling at a Chinese port — each triggering different laws.
- **Underwater approach vector**: Hull, sea chest, and rudder are invisible from deck.

**The Three-Layer "Onion" Defense Model (Intuitive View):**

| Layer | Real-World Equivalent | Maritime Example |
|-------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Outer (Deter)** | Airport perimeter fence + signage | MARSEC Level advisories, armed naval escort in HRA |
| **Middle (Detect)** | X-ray scanners, CCTV, AI face-recognition | AIS, LRIT, SSAS alerts, underwater hull sonar |
| **Inner (Defend)** | Armed air marshals, reinforced cockpit door | Citadel, Ship Security Officers, non-lethal weapons |

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 4 outcomes typically map to **CO4** of OESFT727: *"Identify and mitigate security threats in maritime operations, applying ISPS Code and SOLAS Chapter XI-2 provisions."* This is assessed at Bloom's levels **Apply, Analyse, and Evaluate** in the End Semester Examination.

The 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus emphasizes **outcome-based competency**, meaning students must be able to:
- Sketch the ISPS threat-assessment methodology.
- Compare the **three MARSEC security levels** and the trigger conditions for each.
- Identify the role-players (**CSO, SSO, PFSO, RSOs**) and their statutory duties.
- Evaluate the legal reach of the **SUA 2005 Protocol** over terrorist offences at sea.
- Recommend preventive hardening for **High-Risk Areas (HRAs)** as designated by the IMO.

**Key Statutory Reference Numbers (must memorize):**
- **SOLAS Chapter XI-2** — Special measures to enhance maritime safety and security.
- **ISPS Code Part A** — Mandatory (verifiable) requirements.
- **ISPS Code Part B** — Recommendatory (guidance) provisions.
- **SUA Convention 1988 + 2005 Protocol** — Universal jurisdiction over maritime terrorist offences.
- **MSC/Circ.1188** — Guidance on LRIT (Long-Range Identification and Tracking).
- **MSC.1/Circ.1638** — Guidelines on cyber security onboard ships (2017+).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The ISPS Code Threat Assessment Framework

The ISPS Code prescribes a **formalized threat assessment methodology** that Contracting Governments must apply to vessels and port facilities. The framework decomposes into five sequential logical steps.

### Step 1 — Asset Identification
Enumerate every maritime asset that has **residual value, symbolic importance, or criticality to supply chains**. Examples:
- Passenger terminals at cruise hubs (Mumbai, Cochin, Singapore).
- LNG/LPG carriers calling at energy terminals.
- Strategic chokepoints (Suez, Hormuz, Malacca, Bab-el-Mandeb).
- Offshore platforms and FPSOs.

### Step 2 — Threat Identification
Catalogue known threat actors and their typical **Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs)**:
- State-sponsored naval irregulars (Iranian IRGCN fast boats in Hormuz).
- Non-state jihadi groups (AQAP, Abu Sayyaf, ASWJ in Sulu Sea).
- Piracy-terror nexus off Somalia & Gulf of Guinea.
- Lone-actor insider threats (crew radicalization).

### Step 3 — Vulnerability Assessment
Identify **structural, procedural, and human weaknesses** in:
- Access control at gangway, sea-side doors, and hull penetrations.
- Cargo sealing and ISF (Importer Security Filing) compliance.
- Crew vetting and background screening (especially for ratings from high-risk ports).
- Cyber-physical interfaces (ECDIS, AIS, engine monitoring).

### Step 4 — Consequence Analysis
Quantify the potential **human, economic, environmental, and political** damage:
- Loss of life on a RoPax ferry (e.g., 2014 Sewol sinking, 850 deaths, non-terrorist but instructive).
- Closure of the Suez Canal (Ever Given 2021 = **\$9.6 billion/day** global trade impact; a deliberate terrorist blockage would be even worse).
- Oil spill from a tanker strike in a sensitive ecological zone.

### Step 5 — Risk Treatment Decision
Apply the **classic risk equation** and rank countermeasures:

$$R \;=\; T \times V \times C$$

Where:
- $R$ = Inherent Risk Score (numeric 1–125)
- $T$ = Threat Likelihood (1–5)
- $V$ = Vulnerability (1–5)
- $C$ = Consequence Severity (1–5)

Acceptable residual risk threshold for ISPS is typically **$R \le 27$**. Above that, mandatory countermeasures are triggered.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat-sheet** for KTU ESE preparation on this topic. Memorize every cell.

| **S.No.** | **Element** | **Statutory Reference** | **Mandatory/Recommendatory** | **Responsible Party** | **Activation Trigger** |
|:---------:|-------------|-------------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Ship Security Plan (SSP) | ISPS Code Part A §9 | **Mandatory** | Company + Master | Before vessel enters service |
| 2 | Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP) | ISPS Code Part A §16 | **Mandatory** | PFSO | Upon PFSP approval by DCA |
| 3 | Ship Security Assessment (SSA) | ISPS Code Part A §8 | **Mandatory** | Recognized Security Org (RSO) | Every 5 years or after major change |
| 4 | Port Facility Security Assessment (PFSA) | ISPS Code Part A §15 | **Mandatory** | RSO | Every 5 years |
| 5 | Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR) | SOLAS XI-1/5 | **Mandatory** | Master/Company | At every change of Flag, Owner, ISPS Mgr |
| 6 | Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) | SOLAS XI-2/6 | **Mandatory** | Master (covert activation) | On credible terrorist threat |
| 7 | Long-Range Identification & Tracking (LRIT) | SOLAS V/19-1 & MSC/Circ.1188 | **Mandatory** | Flag State + coastal State | Continuous, polling every 6 hrs |
| 8 | Automatic Identification System (AIS) | SOLAS V/19 | **Mandatory** | Master/OOW | Continuous, except in security-sensitive zones |
| 9 | Declaration of Security (DoS) | ISPS Code Part A §5 | **Mandatory** | Vessel–PF interface | When MARSEC Level ≥ 2 OR on interface |
| 10 | MARSEC Level 1 | ISPS Code Part A §7.1 | **Mandatory baseline** | SSO/CSO | Routine peacetime operations |
| 11 | MARSEC Level 2 | ISPS Code Part A §7.2 | **Heightened** | SSO/CSO | Credible intel of attack within region |
| 12 | MARSEC Level 3 | ISPS Code Part A §7.3 | **Maximum** | SSO/CSO + Naval assets | Imminent attack on specific vessel/PF |
| 13 | SUA Convention 2005 Protocol | IMO Legal Committee | **Mandatory for State Parties** | Coastal/Flag States | Post-incident prosecution |
| 14 | International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC) | SOLAS XI-2/9 | **Mandatory** | Flag State Administration | Validity 5 years, intermediate at 2.5 yrs |
| 15 | Recognized Security Organization (RSO) | ISPS Code Part A §4 | **Authorized by Flag/DCA** | Flag State / DCA | Continuous oversight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction to memorize for KTU exam:** *Mandatory* items are legally enforceable under SOLAS XI-2 and failure attracts **detention of the vessel** at the next port of call. *Recommendatory* items (Part B) are advisory but industry practice treats them as effectively mandatory to avoid liability.

## 2.3 MARSEC Security Levels — Detailed Comparison

| Feature | MARSEC Level 1 (Green) | MARSEC Level 2 (Yellow) | MARSEC Level 3 (Red) |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Threat Posture** | Routine; baseline vigilance | Heightened intel of nearby attack | Imminent or actual attack |
| **Declaration Authority** | Standing baseline | Contracting Govt / CSO / SSO | Contracting Govt / Coastal State |
| **Access Control** | Single-point gangway watch, ID check | Multi-zone perimeter, photo ID, log of all visitors | Total lockdown; only cleared personnel; armed escort of cargo |
| **CCTV Monitoring** | Continuous recording, 31-day retention | Live-monitored by SSO; AI-aided anomaly detection | All feeds simultaneously watched by SSO + shore SOC |
| **Crew Drill Frequency** | Quarterly security drills | Monthly drills; pre-arrival briefing | Continuous readiness; abandon-ship + citadel drills |
| **AIS Broadcast** | Standard transmission | Still on (compulsory) but route may be deviated | MAY be switched OFF in coordination with coastal state (rare, exceptional) |
| **Naval Coordination** | Routine MSCHOA/UKMTO advisories | Naval task force alert; convoy possible | Naval escort, helicopter over-watch, ROE authorization |
| **SSAS Posture** | Tested monthly | Tested weekly, batteries at 100% | Covert button on standby; one press transmits to Flag + Coastal State |
| **Cargo Handling** | Standard ISPS perimeter | Increased hold & seal checks; X-ray screening if available | No open holds at sea; armed guard at hatch covers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example (MARSEC 3):** In **July 2021**, after the drone attack on the Mercer Street tanker in the Gulf of Oman, the UK and Israel jointly raised MARSEC Level 3 for Israeli-owned vessels transiting the region. Combined with naval escort, this is the textbook Level 3 response.

## 2.4 The Detection Stack — Multi-Sensor, Multi-Domain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern maritime counter-terrorism does **not** rely on any single sensor. It is a **layered detection architecture** (analogous to defence-in-depth in nuclear engineering).

**Tier 1 — Space-based Detection**
- Satellite AIS (exactEarth, Spire Global) — detects "dark" vessels that switch off Class A AIS.
- Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery from Capella, ICEYE — can spot vessels of ≥10 m even in cloud/night.
- Electro-optical/IR imagery (Planet Labs, Maxar) — confirms vessel identity and behaviour.

**Tier 2 — Long-Range RF Detection**
- LRIT (Long-Range Identification and Tracking) — polls vessel transponders via Inmarsat C/Iridium.
- SSAS covert alert — one-way burst to Flag Administration + Coastal State.
- VHF channel monitoring — intelligence from coastal maritime radio nets.

**Tier 3 — Cooperative Surface Detection**
- AIS Class A (SOLAS-mandated, 12.5 W, dynamic + static + voyage data).
- AIS Class B (voluntary, smaller craft, 2 W).
- Blue Force Tracking (NATO-friendly forces only).

**Tier 4 — Non-Cooperative Surface Detection**
- Surface search radar (X-band, S-band) — picks up small fast boats at 6–24 NM.
- Thermal/IR cameras on bridges — night detection of fast inshore attack craft (FIAC).
- Acoustic hull-mounted sonar — detects divers, UUVs approaching underwater.

**Tier 5 — Internal/Human Detection**
- SSO continuous monitoring of CCTV, deck rounds, gangway log.
- Crew vigilance training (STCW ISPS familiarization).
- Insider-threat behavioural indicators (radicalization, financial stress, social-media activity).

**Tier 6 — Cyber Detection**
- Network Intrusion Detection (NIDS) on ECDIS, AMS, engine systems.
- AIS spoofing detection (compare GPS position vs. reported position, time-jump anomalies).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Risk Matrices & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Risk Assessment — Worked Example for a VLCC in the Strait of Hormuz

This is the type of analytical problem KTU ESE examiners set at the **Apply / Analyse** level. We walk through it exhaustively.

### Step A — Identify the Asset
The asset is a **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier), 320,000 DWT**, carrying 2 million barrels of crude oil, valued at approximately **\$180 million**, sailing from the Persian Gulf to the Strait of Hormuz.

### Step B — Identify the Threat Actors

| Actor | Capability | Intent | Historical Frequency |
|-------|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| IRGCN fast boats (Boghammar-type) | High | Moderate to High | 2–6 incidents/year (2019–2024) |
| Limpet mine / IRGCN diving teams | Medium | Moderate | 1 incident/year baseline |
| Houthi anti-ship missile (less likely in Hormuz proper) | High | High | 0–1/year in Hormuz |
| Lone-actor insider | Low | Variable | Rare but high-impact |

### Step C — Score Threat Likelihood (T) on 1–5 scale

For an IRGCN fast-boat boarding attempt in Hormuz, $T = 4$ (frequent).

### Step D — Score Vulnerability (V) on 1–5 scale

Assume the vessel has:
- SSAS installed and tested.
- LRIT active.
- Bridge watch doubled.
- AIS on.

But lacks:
- Private armed security team (PAST).
- Citadel (citadel spaces absent on most VLCCs).
- Underwater hull inspection at every port.

Hence $V = 3$ (moderate).

### Step E — Score Consequence (C) on 1–5 scale

Successful boarding and ignition: oil spill, 30+ crew dead, route closure, $\pm 100$ million dollar claims.

Hence $C = 5$ (catastrophic).

### Step F — Compute Inherent Risk

$$R_{\text{inherent}} \;=\; T \times V \times C \;=\; 4 \times 3 \times 5 \;=\; 60$$

Since $R_{\text{inherent}} = 60$ exceeds the **acceptable threshold of $R \le 27$**, the ISPS Code mandates implementation of additional countermeasures.

### Step G — Apply Countermeasures and Re-score

Add: Private Armed Security Team (PAST), citadel hardening, daily hull inspection, naval escort request via UKMTO.

New scores: $T' = 4$ (unchanged, threat is external), $V' = 2$ (hardened), $C' = 5$ (consequence unchanged by defences, only by prevention).

$$R_{\text{residual}} \;=\; 4 \times 2 \times 5 \;=\; 40$$

Still above 27. Critical risk persists. Additional measure:

**Reduce Threat by routing**: sail in MSCHOA-registered group transit, coordinate with naval Combined Task Force 153 (Red Sea) / 152 (Gulf).

Re-route adds 36 hours but drops $T$ from 4 to 2:

$$R_{\text{residual, rerouted}} \;=\; 2 \times 2 \times 5 \;=\; 20 \;<\; 27 \; \checkmark$$

**Verdict**: Combined hardening + threat suppression + convoy transit brings residual risk within ISPS-acceptable envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exam:** Always show the **inherent risk → mitigation → residual risk** trajectory, not just the final number. Examiners award marks for the *process*, not the arithmetic.

## 3.2 Decision Matrix — When is SSAS Activated?

> [!NOTE]
> SSAS is a covert, one-way alert system that transmits a burst to the **Flag State Administration** and the **Coastal State(s)** in the vessel's vicinity. It does **NOT** broadcast publicly.

| Trigger Condition | SSAS Activation? | Reasoning |
|-------------------|:---:|-----------|
| Routine ISPS drill | NO (test only) | Real alert reserved for genuine threat |
| Suspicious FIAC approaching at 25 kn | **YES** | Imminent threat to vessel |
| Master receives credible phone threat | **YES** | Pre-attack intelligence indicator |
| Cyber attack disables ECDIS | NO (use ship-shore radio) | SSAS is for physical/terror threats |
| Hull vibration + diver sighting | **YES** | Underwater attack in progress |
| Routine AIS jamming | NO (inform authorities by voice) | SSAS reserves capacity |
| Pirates climbing hull in Gulf of Aden | **YES** | Universal jurisdiction under SUA 2005 |
| Cargo manifest discrepancy (insider theft) | NO (internal report) | Not a terrorist act per ISPS definition |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students often confuse SSAS with EPIRB. **EPIRB** is for *distress at sea* (GMDSS). **SSAS** is for *security alert*. They use different Inmarsat codes and different shore stations.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Detection Probability of a FIAC Attack

A FIAC (Fast Inshore Attack Craft) is detected by surface radar at a range $R_d = 12$ nautical miles. The FIAC closes at speed $V_a = 40$ knots. The vessel's SSO needs to complete a hardening protocol (raise citadel, lock doors, muster crew) that takes time $t_p = 8$ minutes from detection to "ready-to-defend" state.

**Time to impact if no countermeasures taken:**
$$t_{\text{impact}} \;=\; \frac{R_d}{V_a} \;=\; \frac{12 \text{ NM}}{40 \text{ kn}} \;=\; 0.30 \text{ h} \;=\; 18 \text{ min}$$

**Net time margin (Margin of Safety, MoS):**
$$\text{MoS} \;=\; t_{\text{impact}} - t_p \;=\; 18 - 8 \;=\; 10 \text{ min}$$

Since $\text{MoS} = +10 \text{ min} > 0$, the defensive protocol can be completed before impact. **Threat successfully neutralized by pre-emptive action.**

**Now evaluate degraded scenario** — FIAC closes at 60 knots (super-cavitating), radar detection limited to 8 NM by sea clutter:

$$t_{\text{impact}} \;=\; \frac{8 \text{ NM}}{60 \text{ kn}} \;=\; \frac{8}{60} \times 60 \;=\; 8.0 \text{ min}$$

$$\text{MoS} \;=\; 8.0 - 8 \;=\; 0.0 \text{ min}$$

**Marginally zero.** The defensive protocol is *just* feasible. Recommendation: increase $R_d$ by mounting higher-power X-band radar on masthead, or reduce $t_p$ via pre-staged crew drills.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** This is the same **time-budget safety analysis** used in aerospace (TCAS resolution) and rail signalling (braking distance). KTU examiners love it because it links a security topic to classical safety-engineering methodology.

## 3.4 The SUA 2005 Protocol — Universal Jurisdiction Analysis

The **Convention for the Suppression of Unlawful Acts against the Safety of Maritime Navigation, 1988**, as amended by the **2005 Protocol**, is the principal anti-terrorism treaty at sea.

**Offences criminalized under SUA 2005 (Article 3bis):**
1. Committing violence against a person on board a ship that is likely to endanger its safety.
2. Destroying or seriously damaging the ship or its cargo.
3. Placing a destructive device or substance on board.
4. **Hijacking by any means of communication, threats, or intimidation.**
5. Threatening to commit any of the above.

**Jurisdictional reach (Article 6bis):** Any State Party may prosecute if the offence is committed:
- Against or on board a ship flying its flag.
- In its territorial sea / EEZ.
- By one of its nationals.
- By a person whose habitual residence is in its territory.
- Against any of its nationals.
- If the ship is stateless.
- **Against any ship in the territory of that State** — universal jurisdiction principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exam:** The SUA 2005 Protocol is the only universal-jurisdiction counter-terrorism instrument specific to maritime. The 2005 amendment (post-9/11, post-Limburg 2002) closed the loophole that allowed the *Achille Lauro* hijackers (1985) to be charged only with murder and kidnapping, not terrorism.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Define the ISPS Code. List any four mandatory requirements under it for ocean-going vessels.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The *International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code* is a comprehensive set of measures adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** in **December 2002**, in force from **1 July 2004**, under **SOLAS Chapter XI-2**, to enhance the security of ships and port facilities against terrorist acts.

**Four mandatory requirements for vessels:**

1. **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** — prepared, implemented, and maintained onboard (ISPS Part A §9).
2. **Ship Security Assessment (SSA)** — risk-based evaluation carried out by an RSO before the SSP is drafted.
3. **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)** — covert alarm that transmits to Flag State and coastal authorities.
4. **International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)** — issued by Flag Administration, valid 5 years.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for any four valid points)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Differentiate between SSAS and EPIRB in maritime safety and security context.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SSAS | EPIRB |
|-----------|------|-------|
| **Purpose** | Security alert against terrorism/piracy | Distress alert for maritime emergency |
| **Statutory Reference** | SOLAS XI-2/6 | SOLAS IV/7.1, GMDSS |
| **Activation** | Covert, manual, by Master/SSO only | Automatic on water immersion OR manual |
| **Recipient** | Flag State + Coastal State shore stations | Global search-and-rescue satellites (COSPAS-SARSAT) |
| **Signal Type** | One-way security burst (no public broadcast) | 406 MHz distress beacon with GPS coordinates |
| **Publicity** | Not publicly broadcast | Publicly coordinated globally |

*(3 marks: tabulated differentiation with at least four crisp distinctions)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **three MARSEC security levels** prescribed under the ISPS Code. State the declaration authority and the minimum security measures applicable at each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **SSAS architecture** and the **flow of alert from the vessel to the security authorities**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution A(a) — MARSEC Levels

The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under the ISPS Code defines three Maritime Security (MARSEC) levels that govern the counter-terrorism posture of vessels and port facilities:

**MARSEC Level 1 — Normal (Green)**
- **Declaration Authority:** Standing baseline; does not require formal declaration but must be maintained as the default.
- **Minimum Measures:**
  - Single-watch gangway access with ID verification.
  - CCTV continuous recording, 31-day retention.
  - Quarterly security drills.
  - Standard AIS transmission.
  - Locked sea-side doors (engine room, accommodation).
  - Restricted access to bridge, ECR, and cargo control room.

**MARSEC Level 2 — Heightened (Yellow)**
- **Declaration Authority:** Set by **Contracting Government** (e.g., DG Shipping for India), or by CSO/SSO if vessel-specific intel justifies.
- **Minimum Measures:**
  - All Level 1 measures PLUS:
  - Doubled watch-keeping and additional security personnel.
  - Live-monitored CCTV (not just recorded).
  - Pre-arrival briefing for all crew.
  - Increased frequency of hold and seal checks.
  - Declaration of Security (DoS) signed with every port facility interface.
  - Increased SSAS testing frequency (weekly vs monthly).

**MARSEC Level 3 — Maximum (Red)**
- **Declaration Authority:** Set by **Contracting Government** or **Coastal State** when an imminent attack is expected on a specific vessel or facility.
- **Minimum Measures:**
  - All Level 2 measures PLUS:
  - Full lockdown; only cleared personnel permitted.
  - Armed escort of all cargo operations.
  - Citadel activation; crew mustered in protected space.
  - Naval escort and helicopter over-watch if available.
  - Coordination with task force (e.g., Combined Maritime Forces).
  - Possible suspension of cargo operations entirely.

> **Conclusion:** The MARSEC framework is a graduated escalation model. Each level adds layers of detection, hardening, and response capability without abandoning the underlying baseline.

*(7 marks: 2 for explaining the level concept + 2 each for Levels 2 and 3, with declaration authority & measures)*

---

### Model Solution A(b) — SSAS Architecture

**Architecture of the Ship Security Alert System:**

The SSAS consists of three principal physical subsystems:

1. **Activation Device** — A covert push-button or foot-switch located in the bridge (or other protected location). It is intentionally hidden from crew and visitors to prevent accidental or malicious activation.
2. **Transmission Module** — An Inmarsat C or Iridium terminal dedicated to security alerts. It transmits a pre-formatted message containing the vessel's identity (IMO number, MMSI), position (from GPS), and timestamp.
3. **Receiving Shore Endpoints** — The Flag State Administration's designated shore station (e.g., DG Shipping in Mumbai) and any coastal State within whose waters the vessel is operating.

**Alert Flow Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MASTER["MASTER DETECTS THREAT"]
    SSAS["COVERT SSAS BUTTON ACTIVATED"]
    SAT["INMARSAT/IRIDIUM UPLINK"]
    FLAG["FLAG STATE SHORE STATION"]
    COASTAL["COASTAL STATE AUTHORITY"]
    NAVAL["NAVAL TASK FORCE"]
    RESPONSE["EMERGENCY RESPONSE"]

    MASTER --> SSAS --> SAT --> FLAG
    SAT --> COASTAL
    FLAG --> NAVAL
    COASTAL --> NAVAL
    NAVAL --> RESPONSE
    COASTAL --> RESPONSE
    FLAG --> RESPONSE
```

**Working Principle:**
- Activation is **silent and one-way** — the alert is **not** broadcast over open VHF or AIS, denying the attacker the knowledge that the alert has been raised.
- The position report is derived from **GPS** (or GLONASS/Galileo) — accurate to within 10 m.
- The Flag State shore station receives the alert and dispatches naval or coast-guard assets as per pre-agreed protocols.
- SSAS is tested monthly during MARSEC 1 (and weekly during MARSEC 2) to ensure functional readiness, but **never** during approach to a High-Risk Area to avoid detection by terrorists monitoring RF emissions.

> **Conclusion:** SSAS is a **non-duplicative, covert last-resort system** that complements — but does not replace — AIS, LRIT, and human surveillance. Its effectiveness rests on the speed and decisiveness of the Flag State's response apparatus.

*(7 marks: 2 for components + 2 for alert flow + 1 for working principle + 2 for design philosophy)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **role and responsibilities of the Company Security Officer (CSO)** and the **Ship Security Officer (SSO)** under the ISPS Code. How do they coordinate during a MARSEC Level 2 escalation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **SUA Convention 2005 Protocol** and its relevance to prosecution of maritime terrorist acts. Illustrate any **two offences** it criminalizes. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution B(a) — CSO and SSO Coordination

**Company Security Officer (CSO):** Designated by the **Company** (owner/operator). The CSO is the shore-based focal point for maritime security across the entire fleet. Statutory duties (ISPS Code Part A §11):

1. Advising the company on security policy.
2. Conducting **security surveys** of vessels under company management.
3. Ensuring the SSP is properly drafted, submitted for approval, and maintained.
4. Coordinating security drills and exercises fleet-wide.
5. Acting as the **single point of contact** between vessel, port facility, and shore authorities.
6. Maintaining security records for each vessel (drills, incidents, training).

**Ship Security Officer (SSO):** Designated onboard each vessel. Statutory duties (ISPS Code Part A §12):

1. Implementing the SSP onboard.
2. Conducting **regular security inspections** of the ship.
3. Maintaining 24/7 watch on SSAS, CCTV, and access control.
4. Briefing Master and crew on security matters.
5. Reporting security incidents immediately to the CSO.
6. Coordinating with **Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO)** at every port call.

**Coordination during MARSEC Level 2 Escalation:**

When the CSO (or Contracting Government) escalates the fleet to MARSEC 2:

| Step | CSO Action | SSO Action |
|:----:|------------|------------|
| 1 | Issues MARSEC 2 advisory to all fleet vessels via email/SSAS-test | Acknowledges receipt, briefs Master |
| 2 | Activates **fleet-wide security operations centre** watch | Doubles gangway watch, initiates live CCTV monitoring |
| 3 | Coordinates with Flag State security cell, naval liaison | Ensures LRIT polling on schedule, tests SSAS covertly |
| 4 | Approves any **deviation from planned route** for the vessel | Executes route deviation, logs it in bridge logbook |
| 5 | Maintains 24/7 hotline with SSO during transit | Maintains watch on Inmarsat voice channel to CSO |
| 6 | Post-voyage: reviews incident report, updates SSP | Submits written security incident report within 24 hrs |

> **Conclusion:** The CSO–SSO relationship is **hierarchical, complementary, and information-symmetric**. The CSO provides strategic-level coordination and external liaison; the SSO provides tactical-level execution onboard. Effective security depends on the integrity of this two-way communication loop.

*(7 marks: 2 for CSO role + 2 for SSO role + 3 for coordination table)*

---

### Model Solution B(b) — SUA Convention 2005 Protocol

**Background:** The **Convention for the Suppression of Unlawful Acts against the Safety of Maritime Navigation (SUA Convention)** was adopted at **Rome in 1988** in response to the 1985 *Achille Lauro* hijacking. It entered into force in 1992.

**Why the 2005 Protocol?** Following the **9/11 attacks (2001)** and the **bombing of the MV Limburg (2002)** off Yemen, the IMO Legal Committee determined that the 1988 text was inadequate — it criminalized acts of violence but did not adequately cover modern terrorism (suicide bombers, WMDs, etc.). The **2005 Protocol** thus amends the original convention to:

1. Criminalize **broader spectrum of terrorist acts** at sea.
2. Establish **expanded universal jurisdiction** so any State Party can prosecute.
3. Include new offences such as **transportation of BCNR weapons (Biological, Chemical, Nuclear, Radiological)** at sea.
4. Cover **fixed platforms** in a separate but parallel 2005 Protocol.

**Two Key Offences Criminalized:**

**Offence 1 — Seizure of a Ship by Force or Intimidation (Article 3.1.a):**
Any person who unlawfully and intentionally **seizes or exercises control over a ship by force, threat of force, or by any other form of intimidation**. 
- *Example:* The 2009 pirates who boarded the *Maersk Alabama* and took Captain Phillips hostage.
- *Penalty under SUA:* Extraditable offence; minimum 10 years' imprisonment in State Party jurisdictions.

**Offence 2 — Acts of Violence Likely to Endanger Ship's Safety (Article 3.1.b):**
Any person who commits **an act of violence against a person on board a ship**, if that act is likely to endanger the safe navigation of the ship.
- *Example:* The 2004 attack on the *MV Limburg*, where a small craft laden with explosives rammed and detonated alongside the tanker, killing one crewman and polluting the Gulf of Aden with 90,000 barrels of crude.
- *Penalty under SUA 2005:* Universal jurisdiction allows prosecution by any State Party, even if the act occurred in international waters far from the offender's nationality or flag.

**Relevance to Maritime Counter-Terrorism:**
- Allows **flag-neutral prosecution** — even if attacker is stateless and vessel flies flag of non-cooperative State, any coastal State can prosecute.
- Enables **extradition cooperation** between signatories under aut dedere aut judicare (extradite or prosecute) principle.
- Bridges the gap between **maritime law enforcement** and **counter-terrorism statutes** in national legal systems.

> **Conclusion:** The SUA 2005 Protocol is the **legal backbone of international maritime counter-terrorism prosecution**. Without it, many terrorists captured at sea would have to be charged only with piracy (a centuries-old but narrow offence) or domestic criminal law (which may have no extraterritorial reach).

*(7 marks: 2 for background + 2 for amendments + 3 for offences + relevance)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SSAS with AIS:** SSAS is *covert*; AIS is *public*. Examiners deduct marks when students describe SSAS as a "broadcast system."
> 2. **Treating ISPS Part B as binding:** Part B is *recommendatory*, not mandatory. Saying "Part B requirements must be met" loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping declaration authority for MARSEC levels:** Students routinely forget to mention *who* sets the level (Contracting Government / Coastal State / CSO). This is a recurring 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting the Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR):** The CSR is mandatory and often overlooked in answers on ISPS documentation.
> 5. **Mis-spelling SUA:** Common errors include "USC", "SUC", "SUAC". Spell it correctly as **SUA** (Convention for the Suppression of Unlawful Acts).
> 6. **Treating SSAS as equivalent to distress beacon:** SSAS is *security*; EPIRB is *distress*. Mixing these up costs marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 Detection & Prevention of Terrorist Acts**

### 📌 Statutory Framework
- ☐ **SOLAS Chapter XI-2** — Special measures to enhance maritime safety and security (in force 1 July 2004).
- ☐ **ISPS Code Part A** — Mandatory, verifiable requirements.
- ☐ **ISPS Code Part B** — Recommendatory guidance provisions.
- ☐ **SUA Convention 1988 + 2005 Protocol** — Universal jurisdiction over maritime terrorism.
- ☐ **MSC/Circ.1188** — LRIT performance and functional requirements.
- ☐ **MSC.1/Circ.1638** — Cyber security onboard ships.

### 📌 Mandatory Documents & Systems Onboard
- ☐ **SSP** — Ship Security Plan (must be approved, classified, and tested).
- ☐ **SSA** — Ship Security Assessment (every 5 years).
- ☐ **ISSC** — International Ship Security Certificate (5 years, intermediate at 2.5).
- ☐ **CSR** — Continuous Synopsis Record (kept throughout vessel's life).
- ☐ **SSAS** — Ship Security Alert System (covert, monthly test).
- ☐ **LRIT** — Long-Range Identification and Tracking (every 6 hours polling).
- ☐ **AIS** — Automatic Identification System (continuous unless security-justified switch-off).
- ☐ **DoS** — Declaration of Security (vessel–port interface).

### 📌 Key Roles
- ☐ **CSO** — Company Security Officer (shore).
- ☐ **SSO** — Ship Security Officer (vessel).
- ☐ **PFSO** — Port Facility Security Officer.
- ☐ **RSO** — Recognized Security Organization (independent auditor).
- ☐ **DCA** — Designated Contracting Authority (national maritime regulator).

### 📌 Three MARSEC Levels
- ☐ **Level 1 (Green):** Routine baseline — single watch, recorded CCTV, quarterly drills.
- ☐ **Level 2 (Yellow):** Heightened threat — doubled watch, live CCTV, weekly SSAS test, DoS required.
- ☐ **Level 3 (Red):** Imminent attack — lockdown, naval escort, citadel, armed cargo escort.

### 📌 Risk Equation (KTU Favourite)
$$R \;=\; T \times V \times C$$
- $R > 27$ → mandatory mitigation required.
- Always show *inherent* → *residual* trajectory.

### 📌 Detection Architecture (6 Tiers)
1. ☐ Space-based (satellite AIS, SAR, EO/IR).
2. ☐ Long-range RF (LRIT, SSAS, VHF intercept).
3. ☐ Cooperative surface (AIS, Blue Force Tracking).
4. ☐ Non-cooperative surface (radar, IR, sonar).
5. ☐ Human (CCTV, SSO patrol, crew vigilance, insider monitoring).
6. ☐ Cyber (NIDS, AIS spoofing detection, ECDIS integrity).

### 📌 SSAS vs EPIRB vs AIS
- ☐ **SSAS** = security alert, covert, one-way, to Flag + Coastal State.
- ☐ **EPIRB** = distress alert, automatic on immersion, global COSPAS-SARSAT.
- ☐ **AIS** = public identification, continuous, transponder-based.

### 📌 SUA 2005 Protocol Key Offences
- ☐ Seizure of ship by force/intimidation.
- ☐ Acts of violence against persons onboard.
- ☐ Destruction/damage to ship or cargo.
- ☐ Placing destructive device onboard.
- ☐ **Transporting BCNR weapons** at sea (new in 2005).
- ☐ **Universal jurisdiction** — any State Party may prosecute.

### 📌 Time-Budget Safety Analysis (Engineering Edge)
$$\text{Margin of Safety} \;=\; \frac{R_d}{V_a} - t_p$$
- Must be **positive** for hardening protocol to be effective.
- Applied to FIAC attacks, UUV approach, diver threats.

### 📌 India-Specific Anchor Points
- ☐ **DG Shipping** (Directorate General of Shipping, Mumbai) — Indian DCA.
- ☐ **Indian Naval Hydrographic Office** — issues security advisories.
- ☐ **Adani, JNPT, Cochin, Chennai, Mormugao** — major ISPS-certified port facilities.
- ☐ **STCW compliance** for Indian seafarers — ISPS familiarization mandatory.

### 📌 High-Risk Areas (HRAs) — IMO/MSCHOA Designations
- ☐ **Gulf of Aden / Western Indian Ocean** (HRA still formally listed).
- ☐ **Strait of Hormuz** (heightened advisory).
- ☐ **Bab-el-Mandeb / Southern Red Sea** (since 2023 Houthi attacks).
- ☐ **Sulu / Celebes Seas** (Abu Sayyaf / ASWJ).
- ☐ **Gulf of Guinea** (piracy declining, kidnapping still notable).

### 📌 One-Line Definitions (Rapid Recall)
- ☐ **Piracy** = "any illegal act of violence or detention... committed for private ends by the crew or passengers of a private ship" (UNCLOS Art. 101). *NB: political motives = terrorism, not piracy.*
- ☐ **Terrorism** = politically or ideologically motivated violence against civilians or infrastructure to coerce government (UN/IMO consensus).
- ☐ **Risk** = product of Threat × Vulnerability × Consequence.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — DETECTION AND PREVENTION OF TERRORIST ACTS**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol. Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, ISPS Code, SOLAS Chapter XI-2, and SUA 2005 Protocol.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Drills and Exercises
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT727 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Drills and Exercises

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Security Drills and Exercises** in the maritime domain are systematically planned, scheduled, and documented operational activities conducted aboard a vessel (and where applicable, jointly with a Port Facility) to test, validate, and continuously improve the **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** under the regulatory framework of the **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code**, Chapter XI-2 of **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Convention**, and the **Maritime Security (MARSEC) regime** administered by flag State Administrations and Recognized Security Organizations (RSOs).

A **drill** is a controlled, scenario-based simulation of a specific security threat or contingency, designed to verify crew competence, equipment readiness, and procedural compliance at a defined security level (Security Level 1, 2, or 3).

An **exercise** is a broader, multi-element, multi-agency, or multi-vessel operation that integrates several drills into a comprehensive test of the entire security management system, often involving external agencies such as coast guards, naval forces, or port state control inspectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Must-Know Definition**
> As per **ISPS Code Section 13 (Drills and Exercises)** and **SOLAS Chapter XI-2 Regulation 3**, every ship to which the ISPS Code applies must conduct **security drills at appropriate intervals**, and **at least one security exercise per year**, ensuring the SSO (Ship Security Officer) maintains continuous operational readiness.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your school conducts **fire drills** every few months. The fire alarm rings, students walk out in a pre-defined order, assemble at the muster point, and teachers verify attendance. Nobody expects a real fire every time, but the drill ensures that *if* a real fire breaks out, nobody panics because the muscle memory is already trained.

Maritime **Security Drills** work exactly the same way — but instead of fire, the threats being practiced are:
- A suspicious package found in the engine room (bomb threat),
- An unidentified small craft approaching the ship at high speed (piracy/armed robbery),
- A person found hiding in a cargo hold (stowaway),
- An unrecognized person trying to enter the bridge (unauthorized access).

A **drill** is like practicing one specific scenario (e.g., only the fire exit). An **exercise** is like the **annual mock evacuation** that combines multiple emergencies, involves the fire department, and tests the entire emergency response plan end-to-end.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Reference Documents

The following regulatory anchors govern all security drills and exercises:

| Reference | Issuing Body | Mandatory Force |
|---|---|---|
| **ISPS Code, Part A, Section 13** | **IMO (International Maritime Organization)** | International treaty law |
| **SOLAS Chapter XI-2, Reg. 3** | IMO | Mandatory for all SOLAS ships |
| **SSP (Ship Security Plan)** | Flag State / RSO | Vessel-specific binding document |
| **STCW Code, Section A-VI/5** | IMO | Crew training and certification basis |
| **MSC.1/Circ.1188** | IMO | Guidance on drills and exercises |
| **BIMCO ISPS Code Guide** | Industry guidance | Best-practice reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Personnel Recap (Cross-Reference with Module 3):**
> - **CSO** — Company Security Officer (shore-based, appointed by the company).
> - **SSO** — Ship Security Officer (assigned to each ship, reports to Master).
> - **PFSO** — Port Facility Security Officer (shore-side counterpart).
> - **Master** — Has **overriding authority** and the final decision on ship security matters.

The **Master** retains ultimate authority over the ship and its security — a fundamental, non-delegable responsibility that must be reflected in every drill scenario.

---

## 1.4 Three Security Levels (Drill Intensity Depends on These)

The ISPS Code defines **three security levels**, and the type/intensity of drills executed must correspond to them:

$$ \text{SL 1} < \text{SL 2} < \text{SL 3} $$

- **Security Level 1 (SL 1) — Normal:** Baseline level; minimum protective measures are maintained and **routine drills** are conducted.
- **Security Level 2 (SL 2) — Heightened:** Heightened risk; additional measures activated, drills may be more frequent.
- **Security Level 3 (SL 3) — Exceptional:** Imminent threat; specific protective measures applied, drills typically suspended in favour of real operational response.

The defining equation for **Drill Frequency** is:

$$ f_{\text{drill}} \geq \frac{1 \text{ drill}}{3 \text{ months}} \quad \text{(under SL 1)} $$

At least one drill must be conducted **within any 3-month period** under normal security conditions, while exercises are conducted **at least once annually**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — ISPS Code Section 13 Logic Chain

The ISPS Code Section 13 lays out the following structured logic chain that every KTU answer on this topic should reproduce:

1. **The Company** (via CSO) ensures the **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** is in place.
2. The **Master** is responsible for the overall safety and security of the ship.
3. The **SSO** is responsible for implementing the SSP and conducting drills.
4. Drills verify that crew members know their duties, can operate security equipment, and identify weaknesses.
5. Exercises test the **integration** of the SSP with other emergency procedures and external agencies.
6. After each drill/exercise, an **after-action review** is performed, lessons identified, and corrective actions fed back into the SSP revision cycle.

## 2.2 Types of Security Drills (Mandatory Scenarios)

The ISPS Code (and KTU examiners) expect candidates to enumerate the following canonical drill categories:

| # | Drill Type | Threat Simulated | Equipment Tested |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bomb Threat Drill** | Improvised Explosive Device (IED) / suspicious package | Bomb search kit, radio comms, muster procedure |
| 2 | **Piracy / Armed Robbery Drill** | Small boat attack, boarders | LRAD, citadel, hose pipes, barbed wire, bridge locks |
| 3 | **Stowaway Search Drill** | Hidden persons in cargo/void spaces | Mirrors, thermal imagers, gas detectors, dog units |
| 4 | **Unauthorized Access Drill** | Persons trying to enter restricted zones | Access cards, CCTV, deck patrols |
| 5 | **Suspicious Package Drill** | Unattended luggage or parcels | X-ray units, bomb blankets |
| 6 | **CBRN (Chemical/Biological/Radiological/Nuclear) Drill** | Weapons of Mass Destruction scenarios | Hazmat suits, decontamination showers, dosimeters |
| 7 | **Cyber Security Drill** | IT/OT system breach | Network isolation, password reset, satellite fallback |
| 8 | **Access Control Failure Drill** | Door/card system compromise | Manual locking, watchmen, key registers |

## 2.3 Distinction: Drill vs. Exercise vs. Real Incident

The following analytical distinction is **very high-yield** for KTU 14-mark questions:

$$ \text{Drill} \subset \text{Exercise} \subset \text{Real Incident Response} $$

- A **Drill** = Single scenario, internal crew only, no external agencies, time-bound.
- An **Exercise** = Multiple integrated scenarios, may include CSO/PFSO/Coast Guard, tabletop or full-scale.
- A **Real Incident** = Unplanned, no warning, actual threat present.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Use the word **"frequencies"** — drills are quarterly, exercises are annual, real incidents are uncontrolled. Examiners reward correct usage of regulatory cadences.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Item | Symbol / Formula | Value / Unit | KTU Yield |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum drill frequency | $f_d \geq \dfrac{1}{3 \text{ months}}$ | dimensionless | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | Minimum exercise frequency | $f_e \geq \dfrac{1}{1 \text{ year}}$ | dimensionless | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | Security Level escalation | $SL_1 \rightarrow SL_2 \rightarrow SL_3$ | stepwise | ★★★★ |
| 4 | Crew participation ratio (target) | $\dfrac{N_{\text{participating}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \geq 90\%$ | percent | ★★★ |
| 5 | Drill duration (typical) | $t_d \approx 30 \text{ min} - 2 \text{ h}$ | hours | ★★ |
| 6 | Exercise duration (typical) | $t_e \approx 4 \text{ h} - 2 \text{ days}$ | hours/days | ★★ |
| 7 | Post-drill report submission | $T_{\text{report}} \leq 7 \text{ days}$ | days | ★★★ |
| 8 | SSP review after incident | $\Delta t_{\text{SSP review}} \leq 30 \text{ days}$ | days | ★★★ |
| 9 | Total drills per year (minimum) | $N_d = 12 / 3 = 4$ | drills/yr | ★★★★ |
| 10 | Total exercises per year (minimum) | $N_e = 1$ | exercise/yr | ★★★★★ |

## 2.5 Engineering / Real-World Utility

Why does the maritime industry spend millions of dollars annually on security drills?

1. **Loss Prevention:** Piracy and armed robbery cost the global shipping industry an estimated **USD 1.5–2 billion per year** (ICC IMB data). Effective drills reduce successful boardings.
2. **Crew Safety:** Trained crews can shelter in the **citadel** (a hardened safe room) within 90 seconds during a piracy attack, dramatically reducing hostage casualties.
3. **Insurance Premiums:** Classification societies and P&I clubs offer reduced premiums to vessels with documented, audit-quality drill records.
4. **PSC (Port State Control) Compliance:** Failure to maintain drill records leads to **detentions**, which cost an operator **USD 30,000–100,000 per day**.
5. **Crew Competence Decay Mitigation:** Skills fade over time; the formula $\text{Skill Retention} = e^{-t/\tau}$ (where $\tau \approx 6 \text{ months}$) means drills are essential to prevent decay.

The last point is mathematically critical — human memory decay follows the Ebbinghaus curve, and security procedures left unpracticed degrade exponentially, not linearly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Annual Drill Count

**Problem:** How many minimum security drills must a ship conduct per calendar year under ISPS Code Section 13?

**Step 1 — Identify the regulatory constraint from ISPS Code, Part A, Paragraph 13.6:**
> "Ship security drills shall be conducted at appropriate intervals, taking into account the ship type, the personnel changes, the ports of call, and other relevant factors. The drills shall test individual elements of the Ship Security Plan."

**Step 2 — Identify the explicit numerical requirement from MSC.1/Circ.1188 guidance:**
> "Drills should be conducted at least once every three months."

**Step 3 — Translate the constraint into mathematical form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\max} &= 3 \text{ months} \\
f_{\min} &= \frac{1}{T_{\max}} = \frac{1}{3 \text{ months}} \\
\therefore f_{\min} &= 0.333 \text{ drills/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to annual frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{annual}} &= f_{\min} \times 12 \text{ months/year} \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \times 12 \\
&= 4 \text{ drills/year (minimum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

A ship subject to the ISPS Code **must conduct at least 4 security drills per year**, with **at least one exercise** in addition.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Crew Participation Percentage

**Problem:** A ship has 24 crew members. During a security drill, 21 actually participated. Calculate the participation ratio.

**Step 1 — Write the participation formula:**

$$
P = \frac{N_{\text{participating}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{21}{24} \times 100\% \\
P &= 0.875 \times 100\% \\
P &= 87.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The participation is **87.5%**, which is **below** the KTU-recommended benchmark of **≥90%**. The shortfall of **2.5 percentage points** (6 crew equivalents) should trigger a corrective action in the after-action review.

## 3.3 Derivation: Drill Reaction Time vs. Skill Retention

The **Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve** governs how fast crew competence decays:

$$
R(t) = e^{-t / \tau}
$$

Where:
- $R(t)$ = retained skill fraction,
- $t$ = time elapsed since last drill,
- $\tau$ = skill half-life (approximately 6 months for procedural maritime tasks).

**Example Calculation:**

A drill was conducted on 1 January. What is the retained competence by 1 July?

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 6 \text{ months} \\
\tau &= 6 \text{ months} \\
R(6) &= e^{-6/6} = e^{-1} \\
R(6) &= 0.3679 \approx 36.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Only about **37%** of the skill is retained after 6 months without re-training. This is *why* ISPS mandates quarterly drills — they prevent skill decay below the 37% threshold.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drill vs. Exercise Decision Logic

| Decision Parameter | Conduct a Drill | Conduct an Exercise | Real Operational Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Quarterly | Annually | As needed |
| Scope | Single threat scenario | Multi-scenario integrated | Actual threat |
| Personnel Involved | Ship's crew | Crew + CSO + PFSO + external agencies | All available + law enforcement |
| Duration | 30 min – 2 h | 4 h – 2 days | Until threat neutralized |
| Regulatory Driver | ISPS 13.6 | ISPS 13.7 / MSC.1/Circ.1188 | Master discretion |
| Documentation | Drill log entry | Formal exercise report | Incident report + SSP review |
| Cost | Low | Medium–High | Variable, often very high |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Conducting a Drill (Procedure)

A canonical drill follows this sequence:

1. **SSO plans the drill scenario** (in coordination with Master).
2. **SSO briefs crew** at minimum 24 hours in advance (for planned drills; unannounced drills also permitted).
3. **Crew stations are manned** according to SSP muster list.
4. **Scenario is initiated** (e.g., "suspicious package in forward store").
5. **Crew executes response** per SSP — search, isolate, muster, account for personnel.
6. **SSO observes, times, and records** each action with timestamps.
7. **Debrief** conducted immediately after.
8. **After-action report** filed within **7 days**.
9. **Corrective actions** logged in SSP and assigned to responsible persons.
10. **Records retained** for minimum **5 years** (and made available to PSC inspectors on demand).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Drill vs. Exercise Hierarchy (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[ISPS Code Compliance Requirement] --> nodeB[Security Drill]
    nodeA[ISPS Code Compliance Requirement] --> nodeC[Security Exercise]
    nodeA[ISPS Code Compliance Requirement] --> nodeD[Real Incident Response]

    nodeB[Security Drill] --> nodeE[Quarterly Minimum]
    nodeB[Security Drill] --> nodeF[Single Scenario]
    nodeB[Security Drill] --> nodeG[Crew Only]
    nodeB[Security Drill] --> nodeH[SSO Led]

    nodeC[Security Exercise] --> nodeI[Annual Minimum]
    nodeC[Security Exercise] --> nodeJ[Multi-Scenario Integrated]
    nodeC[Security Exercise] --> nodeK[Crew + CSO + PFSO + External Agencies]
    nodeC[Security Exercise] --> nodeL[SSO + CSO + Master Co Led]

    nodeD[Real Incident Response] --> nodeM[Unplanned]
    nodeD[Real Incident Response] --> nodeN[Actual Threat]
    nodeD[Real Incident Response] --> nodeO[Master Directed]
    nodeD[Real Incident Response] --> nodeP[Post Incident SSP Review Triggered]
```

## 4.2 Drill Execution Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Master Authority and Override] --> node2[SSO Plans Drill]
    node2 --> node3[Scenario Brief]
    node3 --> node4[Crew Mustering]
    node4 --> node5[Equipment Verification]
    node5 --> node6[Scenario Execution]
    node6 --> node7[SSO Observation and Timing]
    node7 --> node8[Debrief Session]
    node8 --> node9[After Action Report within 7 Days]
    node9 --> node10[Corrective Action Plan]
    node10 --> node11[SSP Revision if Required]
    node11 --> node12[Records Retained 5 Years]
    node1[Master Authority and Override] -.Overrides.- node6[Scenario Execution]
```

## 4.3 Security Level Escalation and Drill Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Security Level 1 Normal] -->|Threat Increases| node2[Security Level 2 Heightened]
    node2 -->|Imminent Threat| node3[Security Level 3 Exceptional]

    node1[Security Level 1 Normal] --> node1a[Routine Quarterly Drills]
    node2[Security Level 2 Heightened] --> node2a[Increased Drill Frequency]
    node3[Security Level 3 Exceptional] --> node3a[Real Response Drills Suspended]

    node1a[Routine Quarterly Drills] --> node1b[Bomb Drill]
    node1a[Routine Quarterly Drills] --> node1c[Piracy Drill]
    node1a[Routine Quarterly Drills] --> node1d[Stowaway Drill]

    node2a[Increased Drill Frequency] --> node2b[Weekly Access Control Checks]
    node2a[Increased Drill Frequency] --> node2c[Enhanced Deck Patrols]
```

## 4.4 Reporting and Documentation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Drill or Exercise Conducted] --> nodeB[SSO Records Events with Timestamps]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Debrief Held Immediately]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Within 7 Days After Action Report Filed]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Master Reviews and Signs Off]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Corrective Actions Assigned]
    nodeF --> nodeG[SSP Amended if Required]
    nodeG --> nodeH[5 Year Record Retention]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Available for PSC Inspection on Demand]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Continuous Improvement Loop Back to Drill Planning]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Define a Security Drill under the ISPS Code. State the minimum frequency of drills.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Security Drill** under the ISPS Code (Section 13.6) is a controlled, scenario-based activity conducted to test individual elements of the Ship Security Plan (SSP), verify crew competence, and validate security equipment readiness.

The **minimum frequency** is **one drill every three months**, i.e., **at least four drills per year** under Security Level 1.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]

**Differentiate between a Security Drill and a Security Exercise.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Drill | Exercise |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Quarterly | Annually |
| Scope | Single threat scenario | Multi-scenario, integrated |
| Participation | Ship's crew only | Crew + CSO + PFSO + external agencies |
| Duration | 30 min – 2 h | 4 h – 2 days |
| Purpose | Test individual SSP element | Test full SSP integration |
| Regulatory Anchor | ISPS 13.6 | ISPS 13.7 / MSC.1/Circ.1188 |

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **types of security drills** that must be conducted aboard an ISPS-compliant vessel. Discuss at least **six categories** with the threat each simulates and the equipment tested.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **flowchart**, describe the **complete procedure for conducting a security drill** from planning to record retention.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Types of Security Drills

A ship subject to the ISPS Code must conduct drills covering multiple threat categories. The principal types are:

**1. Bomb Threat Drill:**
- **Threat:** Suspicious package or Improvised Explosive Device (IED).
- **Action:** Crew isolates the package, evacuates the area, notifies Master and SSO, awaits bomb disposal experts.
- **Equipment Tested:** Radio communications, bomb search kit, muster list, public address (PA) system.

**2. Piracy / Armed Robbery Drill:**
- **Threat:** Suspicious approach by small high-speed boats.
- **Action:** Crew is mustered, non-essential personnel retreat to the citadel, water hoses and LRAD (Long Range Acoustic Device) activated.
- **Equipment Tested:** Citadel readiness, LRAD, deck lighting, fire hoses used as water cannons, razor wire deployment.

**3. Stowaway Search Drill:**
- **Threat:** Persons hidden in cargo holds, chain lockers, or void spaces.
- **Action:** Comprehensive sweep of all spaces using mirrors, thermal imagers, gas detectors.
- **Equipment Tested:** Search equipment, dog units (where available), biometric tools.

**4. Unauthorized Access Drill:**
- **Threat:** Persons attempting to enter the bridge, engine room, or other restricted zones.
- **Action:** Access control verification, identification challenge, lockdown procedures.
- **Equipment Tested:** Access cards, biometric readers, CCTV, door locks.

**5. CBRN Drill:**
- **Threat:** Chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear contamination.
- **Action:** Donning of hazmat suits, decontamination showers activation, isolation of affected zones.
- **Equipment Tested:** Hazmat suits, dosimeters, decontamination showers, breathing apparatus.

**6. Suspicious Package Drill:**
- **Threat:** Unattended luggage or parcels in passenger or crew areas.
- **Action:** Isolation, X-ray scanning, controlled detonation procedures (where required).
- **Equipment Tested:** X-ray units, bomb blankets, isolation protocols.

**7. Cyber Security Drill:**
- **Threat:** IT or Operational Technology (OT) system breach.
- **Action:** Network isolation, password rotation, switch to satellite communications.
- **Equipment Tested:** Backup servers, satellite phones, manual bridge controls.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Drill Procedure Flowchart

The complete procedure is described in the flowchart below (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the Mermaid diagram).

**Key textual description of each step:**

1. **Master authorizes and SSO plans** the drill scenario.
2. **SSO briefs the crew** (typically 24 hours prior for planned drills).
3. **Muster stations are activated** according to the SSP muster list.
4. **Equipment verification** (radio, fire hoses, LRAD, access cards, etc.) is performed.
5. **Scenario is initiated** with a realistic trigger (e.g., "unidentified boat approaching at 12 knots").
6. **SSO observes and times** every crew action using a stopwatch and logbook.
7. **Debrief** is held immediately after, with the Master, SSO, and all participating crew.
8. **After-action report** is filed within **7 days**, signed by Master.
9. **Corrective actions** are documented and assigned with deadlines.
10. **SSP is amended** if the drill reveals procedural gaps.
11. **Records are retained for 5 years** and made available for PSC inspections.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **regulatory framework** governing security drills and exercises. Cite ISPS Code sections, SOLAS chapters, and key IMO circulars.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the following for a ship with **24 crew members**: If **21 crew participated** in a drill, calculate the **participation ratio**. If the previous drill was conducted **6 months ago**, calculate the **retained skill percentage** using the Ebbinghaus curve with $\tau = 6$ months. Comment on whether the drill schedule meets ISPS minimum requirements.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Regulatory Framework

The regulatory framework governing maritime security drills and exercises is layered and complementary:

**1. SOLAS Convention, Chapter XI-2:**
- Adopted in **December 2002**, entered into force on **1 July 2004**.
- Establishes the mandatory link between ship and port facility security.
- Regulation 3 mandates that ships comply with the ISPS Code.

**2. ISPS Code (International Ship and Port Facility Security Code):**
- **Part A — Mandatory** provisions.
- **Part B — Recommendatory** guidance.
- **Section 13** is the dedicated section on **Drills and Exercises**.
- **Section 13.6** — Drills must be conducted at appropriate intervals.
- **Section 13.7** — Exercises must test communications, coordination, resource availability, and responses.
- **Section 13.8** — Records must be retained and made available for inspection.

**3. MSC.1/Circ.1188:**
- Provides detailed guidance on the conduct of drills and exercises.
- Recommends that **at least one exercise per year** include **multiple SSO/PFSO coordination**, **multi-threat scenarios**, and **external agency involvement**.

**4. STCW Convention and Code (Section A-VI/5):**
- Specifies competence requirements for seafarers with designated security duties.
- Requires periodic refresher training, which is tested through drills.

**5. Flag State and Port State Legislation:**
- National administrations (e.g., USCG NVIC 04-03, MCA MSN 1862, Indian DGS Orders) translate the ISPS Code into domestic law.

**6. Recognized Security Organizations (RSOs):**
- Approved by flag States to audit and certify ship security plans, including drill records.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1 — Participation Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{N_{\text{participating}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\
P &= \frac{21}{24} \times 100\% \\
P &= 87.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The participation ratio is **87.5%**.

**Step 2 — Skill Retention Calculation (Ebbinghaus Curve):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(t) &= e^{-t/\tau} \\
R(6) &= e^{-6/6} \\
R(6) &= e^{-1} \\
R(6) &= 0.3679 \\
R(6) &\approx 36.79\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Only **36.79%** of the procedural skill is retained.

**Step 3 — Compliance Assessment:**

ISPS requires at least **one drill every 3 months** to keep skill retention above the 50% threshold. The interval of 6 months between drills has driven retention down to **36.79%**, which is **below the 50% minimum competence threshold**.

**Conclusion:** The drill schedule **does not meet ISPS minimum requirements** if 6 months have elapsed since the last drill. The vessel must conduct an **immediate catch-up drill** and report the compliance gap in the next SSP audit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Do NOT confuse "drill" with "exercise"** in 14-mark answers — examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this conflation.
> - **Always cite specific sections** (e.g., ISPS Code Section 13.6, SOLAS Reg. XI-2/3) — vague references lose **1–2 marks**.
> - **For numerical questions**, show all working steps; even with a correct final answer, missing intermediate steps cost **1–2 marks**.
> - **Remember the 7-day rule** for after-action report submission — this is a common pitfall.
> - **Use the word "minimum"** when stating drill/exercise frequencies — examiners specifically look for this qualifier.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✔ **Security Drill** = single scenario, internal crew, **quarterly** minimum.
- ✔ **Security Exercise** = multi-scenario, may involve external agencies, **annual** minimum.
- ✔ **Minimum drill frequency:** one drill every **3 months** → **4 drills/year**.
- ✔ **Minimum exercise frequency:** **1 per year**.
- ✔ **After-action report:** within **7 days** of the drill/exercise.
- ✔ **Record retention:** minimum **5 years**.
- ✔ **SSP review after incident:** within **30 days**.
- ✔ **Three Security Levels:** **SL 1 (Normal), SL 2 (Heightened), SL 3 (Exceptional)**.
- ✔ **Master retains overriding authority** — non-delegable.
- ✔ **SSO** plans and executes drills; **CSO** coordinates from shore; **PFSO** is the port-side counterpart.
- ✔ **Regulatory anchors:** ISPS Code Section 13, SOLAS Chapter XI-2, MSC.1/Circ.1188, STCW Section A-VI/5.
- ✔ **Common drill types:** Bomb, Piracy, Stowaway, Unauthorized Access, CBRN, Cyber, Suspicious Package.
- ✔ **Skill retention** follows $R(t) = e^{-t/\tau}$ with $\tau \approx 6$ months; quarterly drills prevent decay below ~50%.
- ✔ **Participation benchmark:** ≥ **90%** of crew.
- ✔ **Drill duration:** typically **30 min – 2 h**; **Exercise duration:** **4 h – 2 days**.
- ✔ **Documentation hierarchy:** Drill Log → After-Action Report → Corrective Actions → SSP Revision → 5-Year Retention.
